### [1] [COD10K-C: Benchmarking Robustness of Camouflaged Object Detection Under Natural Image Corruptions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02603)
*Arafat Hossain Sayem*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces COD10K-C, a corruption-robustness benchmark for camouflaged object detection based on COD10K with 40 conditions (8 corruptions × 5 severity). Evaluates SINet-v2, PFNet, ZoomNet, and RobustCODLite; corruption degrades all models, with motion/Gaussian blur most harmful. RobustCODLite, using augmentation, a frequency-prior branch, and an uncertainty-consistency loss, shows strongest robustness (≈92.3% of clean Dice) and favorable performance on hard corruptions; code repo to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world COD images are often corrupted by blur, noise, weather, and compression. Current COD benchmarks on clean images inadequately reflect deployment conditions; a robustness benchmark is needed to evaluate and improve models under distortion.

Method: Create COD10K-C with 8 corruption types across 5 severity levels (40 conditions) and 81,040 evaluation pairs. Evaluate four COD models (SINet-v2, PFNet, ZoomNet, RobustCODLite). Propose RobustCODLite with corruption augmentation, a frequency-prior branch, and an uncertainty-consistency loss to improve robustness.

Result: All models suffer performance drops under corruption; motion blur and Gaussian blur cause the largest Dice score declines (e.g., SINet-v2 down 18.5 points under motion blur). Brightness and fog are less harmful. RobustCODLite retains 92.3% of its clean Dice score under corruption, outperforming SINet-v2 (87.7%), ZoomNet (84.8%), and PFNet (84.1%). On the hardest corruptions, RobustCODLite matches or surpasses models that excel on clean data.

Conclusion: COD10K-C is a valuable benchmark for evaluating and improving robustness of COD models; the authors will release a GitHub repository to support future research.

Abstract: Camouflaged object detection has improved substantially, but most standard benchmarks evaluate models only on clean images. This is not realistic because real cameras often capture blur, sensor noise, weather effects, and compression artifacts. We present COD10K-C, a corruption robustness benchmark based on COD10K. It includes 8 corruption types and 5 severity levels, giving 40 conditions and 81,040 evaluation pairs in total. We evaluate three popular camouflaged object detection models, SINet-v2, PFNet, and ZoomNet, as well as a lightweight model called RobustCODLite. All models show clear performance drops on corrupted images. Motion blur and Gaussian blur cause the largest drops, with SINet-v2 losing 18.5 Dice points under motion blur. Brightness and fog are less harmful. RobustCODLite uses corruption augmentation, a frequency-prior branch, and an uncertainty-consistency loss. It retains 92.3% of its clean Dice score under corruption, compared with 87.7% for SINet-v2, 84.8% for ZoomNet, and 84.1% for PFNet. On the hardest corruptions, RobustCODLite matches or outperforms models that perform better on clean data. We will release the COD10K-C GitHub repository to support future research in robust camouflaged object detection.

</details>


### [2] [AVTrack: Audio-Visual Tracking in Human-centric Complex Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02724)
*Yaoting Wang,Yun Zhou,Zipei Zhang,Henghui Ding*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AVTrack introduces a challenging audio-visual instance segmentation (AVIS) dataset for dynamic real-world scenes to evaluate speaker tracking; current AVIS methods suffer substantial performance drop; a simple baseline is provided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AVIS datasets are limited to simple or homogeneous scenes with coarse annotations, which biases evaluation and fails to test robust spatiotemporal modeling and cross-modal reasoning in dynamic environments.

Method: Curate AVTrack with dynamic conditions (camera motion, occlusions, position changes); evaluate representative AVIS methods on AVTrack; provide a simple yet effective baseline to facilitate future work.

Result: AVIS methods show substantial performance degradation on AVTrack, confirming its hardness and suitability as a benchmark; the baseline demonstrates feasibility for progress.

Conclusion: AVTrack serves as a challenging, human-centric benchmark for robust audio-visual scene understanding in dynamic environments, and is expected to drive advances in cross-modal reasoning and spatiotemporal modeling; project website provided.

Abstract: Audio-visual speaker tracking aims to localize and track active speakers by leveraging auditory and visual cues, enabling fine-grained, human-centric scene understanding. This capability is essential for real-world applications such as intelligent video editing, surveillance, and human-computer interaction. However, existing datasets are largely limited to simple or homogeneous audio-visual scenes with coarse annotations. Such oversimplified settings bias evaluation toward static audio-visual co-occurrence, rather than rigorously assessing robust spatiotemporal modeling and cross-modal reasoning in complex, dynamic scenes. To address these limitations, we introduce AVTrack, a human-centric audio-visual instance segmentation (AVIS) dataset designed for dynamic real-world scenarios. AVTrack features diverse and challenging conditions, including camera motion, visual occlusions, and position changes. Evaluations of representative AVIS methods on AVTrack reveal substantial performance degradation, establishing AVTrack as a challenging benchmark for robust human-centric audio-visual scene understanding in complex environments. We further provide a simple yet effective baseline to facilitate future research. Project website: https://FudanCVL.github.io/AVTrack/

</details>


### [3] [Consistent Yet Wrong: Evidence Insensitivity in Spatial Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02742)
*S Divakar Bhat,Toshihiko Yamasaki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ViewDiag reveals that cross-view consistency in vision-language models often accompanies incorrect answers, challenging the use of consistency as a measure of geometric grounding; it introduces a multi-view benchmark to diagnose spatial reasoning along metric accuracy, distributional concentration, and latent feature collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether cross-view predictions reflect true geometric understanding in VLMs and to challenge the assumption that view-consistency indicates geometry-grounded reasoning; provide a controlled benchmark to separate actual evidence-sensitive reasoning from degenerate prediction patterns.

Method: Build a controlled multi-view evaluation protocol (ViewDiag) using Hypersim, ScanNet, and KITTI360; 176 object-pair tracks across 80 scenes, with 2–10 views per track; evaluate models on metric accuracy, distributional concentration, and a latent feature probe for internal collapse to distinguish decision collapse from representation collapse.

Result: Across diverse models, observe high prediction stability coupled with substantial error; a regime of strong consistency but low accuracy, indicating weak coupling between predictions and view-specific visual evidence.

Conclusion: Cross-view consistency is not a reliable proxy for geometric understanding; stable predictions may arise from priors rather than evidence-sensitive reasoning. ViewDiag provides a benchmark and diagnostic framework for evaluating spatial VLMs beyond accuracy, with code/data available at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: Spatial reasoning is fundamental to robotics, autonomy, and embodied AI, yet modern vision-language models (VLMs) remain unreliable on metric distance queries. A common assumption is that consistent predictions across viewpoints reflect geometric grounding. We test this assumption and find the opposite: leading VLMs often produce view-invariant and consistent answers even when those answers are incorrect, indicating weak coupling between predictions and viewpoint-specific visual evidence.
  We introduce \textbf{ViewDiag}, a controlled multi-view evaluation protocol built from Hypersim, ScanNet, and KITTI360, comprising 176 object-pair tracks across 80 scenes with 2--10 views per track. The protocol evaluates models along three axes: metric accuracy, distributional concentration, and a latent feature probe for internal collapse that distinguishes decision collapse from representation collapse. Across diverse models, we observe a consistent pattern of high prediction stability paired with substantial error, clustering in a regime characterized by strong consistency but low accuracy.
  \noindent These results challenge the common use of cross-view consistency as a proxy for geometric understanding. Instead, we show that stable predictions may reflect prior-driven collapse rather than evidence-sensitive reasoning. ViewDiag provides a controlled benchmark and diagnostic framework for evaluating spatial VLMs beyond accuracy alone. The code and data can be found \href{https://github.com/SDivakarBhat/Consistent_Yet_Wrong.git}{here}

</details>


### [4] [Plan2Map: A Multimodal Benchmark for Document-Grounded Geospatial Boundary Reconstruction from Planning Records](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02747)
*Fabian Degen,Oishi Deb,Jindong Gu,Junchi Yu,Samuele Marro,Philip Torr,Jialin Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Plan2Map introduces a 208-case multimodal benchmark for reconstructing geospatial boundaries from UK planning documents and presents GeoPlanAgent, a tool-augmented pipeline achieving strong IoU-based performance, outperforming direct VLM baselines and highlighting localization/registration as key error sources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable automatic conversion of non-machine-readable planning records into precise GIS boundaries, addressing the gap where source documents provide indirect spatial evidence.

Method: Plan2Map collects multimodal planning records (notice text, schedules, map plates/labels, boundary annotations) and builds GeoPlanAgent, a document-grounded, geospatial-tool-in-the-loop pipeline with stages: evidence extraction, localisation, map registration, boundary segmentation, projection, and verification.

Result: Mean IoU 0.736 and median IoU 0.904 on Plan2Map; 67.8% of predictions ≥0.8 IoU; direct VLM-to-GeoJSON baselines underperform; diagnostic analysis shows localisation/registration challenges; supervised boundary segmentation improves pixel-level masks.

Conclusion: Plan2Map provides a concrete, multimodal testbed for geospatial boundary reconstruction from public planning documents and demonstrates the efficacy of a tool-augmented approach over end-to-end VLM baselines.

Abstract: Planning records define restrictions over geographic areas, but their source documents often provide only indirect spatial evidence rather than machine-readable boundaries. We introduce Plan2Map, a 208-case multimodal benchmark for document-grounded geospatial boundary reconstruction from UK planning records. Given only a source planning document, systems must reconstruct a valid geospatial boundary from notice text, schedules, map plates, map labels, and boundary annotations; the reference GeoJSON is held out for scoring. We propose GeoPlanAgent, a document-grounded, geospatial-tool-in-the-loop system that decomposes the task into evidence extraction, localisation, map registration, boundary segmentation, projection, and verification. On Plan2Map, GeoPlanAgent achieves 0.736 mean IoU and 0.904 median IoU, with 67.8\% of predictions at or above 0.8 IoU, substantially outperforming direct VLM-to-GeoJSON baselines. Diagnostic analysis shows that direct VLM prediction remains unreliable, while remaining errors are concentrated in localisation and map registration, and supervised boundary segmentation substantially improves pixel-level mask quality. Plan2Map provides a concrete testbed for multimodal geospatial reconstruction from public planning records. Project page: https://odeb1.github.io/Plan2Map_Project_Page/.

</details>


### [5] [MetaWorld: Scaling Multi-Agent Video World Model from Single-view Video Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02753)
*Teng Hu,Mingchun Lu,Yating Wang,Jiangning Zhang,Jinkun Hao,Ye Pan,Ran Yi,Lizhuang Ma,Dacheng Tao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MetaWorld enables scalable multi-agent video world modeling from single-view videos by (1) extracting ego-motion and subject trajectories (MWSU), (2) conditioning visual generation on per-agent identities (Subject-Aware World Generator), and (3) enforcing cross-view world-state consistency via per-layer cross-attention (World-State Alignment).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two core issues in multi-agent video world models: data scarcity (coordinated multi-view data is expensive) and world state alignment (independent streams must reflect a shared reality).

Method: 1) Monocular World-State Unrolling (MWSU): decompose monocular footage into camera ego-motion and subject trajectories to obtain synchronized multi-agent motion data in 3D space without multi-camera setups. 2) Subject-Aware World Generator: appearance-driven simulation conditioned on per-agent identity images. 3) World-State Alignment: per-frame inter-branch cross-attention inserted at every transformer layer of the video DiT to enforce static and dynamic consistency between views.

Result: Extensive experiments show improved cross-view consistency and identity fidelity, demonstrating a scalable, physics-driven approach for multi-agent video world modeling.

Conclusion: MetaWorld offers a scalable framework that leverages monocular cues and cross-view alignment to realize open-domain, multi-agent video world models from single-view data, reducing data requirements while maintaining coherent physical reality across views.

Abstract: Video world models are a foundational generative technology for embodied AI and the Metaverse, yet existing approaches are inherently limited to a single agent observing from a single perspective. Extending these models to multi-agent settings introduces two critical challenges: data scarcity (coordinated multi-view recordings are prohibitively expensive to collect for general open-domain scenarios) and world state alignment (independently generated video streams cannot ensure that shared physical environments and events evolve consistently across views). To address these challenges, we propose MetaWorld, a novel framework that scales multi-agent video world models to open-domain environments directly from single-view videos. First, we introduce Monocular World-State Unrolling (MWSU) to explicitly decompose monocular footage into the camera operator's ego-motion and the visible subject's spatial trajectory. This camera-trajectory decomposition naturally extracts synchronized multi-agent motion data within a shared 3D space, completely bypassing the need for multi-camera setups. Second, for precise visual control, we develop the Subject-Aware World Generator to enable appearance-driven simulation conditioned on per-agent identity images. Finally, to ensure both views are grounded in the identical physical reality, we propose World-State Alignment, a per-frame inter-branch cross-attention mechanism inserted at every transformer layer of the video DiT. By jointly synchronizing the denoising process, WSA enforces both static geometric consistency and dynamic motion consistency, encouraging that the shared 3D environment and physical events remain well-aligned across both egocentric views. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MetaWorld achieves superior cross-view consistency and identity fidelity, establishing a highly scalable, physics-driven paradigm for multi-agent video world modeling.

</details>


### [6] [From Local Training to Large-Scale Mapping: A Comparative Assessment of Machine Learning and Deep Learning for Transferable Satellite-Derived Bathymetry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02764)
*Hsiao-Jou Hsu,Joachim Moortgat*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Transferable satellite bathymetry (SDB) models are evaluated across regions using Sentinel-2 data; preserving spatial continuity and a SWF-weighted RMSE loss improve depth accuracy and cross-regional generalization, with deep CNNs outperforming RF; multi-temporal imagery and inference-time median aggregation further enhance robustness; results on MagicBathyNet show strong accuracy and pretrained weights are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SDB from multispectral imagery is cost-effective but struggles to generalize across diverse coastal environments; need scalable, transferable models that perform well in optically complex waters and across regions.

Method: Baseline RF and four CNNs (ResNet-50/101, EfficientNet-B4, ConvNeXt-Large) trained on Pratas Island and selected GBR regions; evaluated on intra- and cross-regional test areas with spatially contiguous training blocks to preserve reef structure; introduced a SWF-weighted RMSE loss emphasizing near-surface depths; explored multi-temporal repeat imagery and median aggregation at inference; tested on MagicBathyNet benchmark and released pretrained weights.

Result: Intra-regional RMSE: 1.15–1.92 m (0–20 m); near-surface depths (≤3 m) RMSE as low as 0.26 m. RF drops in cross-region transfer (1.53 m to 2.99–3.78 m); deep models remain more robust (2.46–2.98 m). On MagicBathyNet (0–16 m): 0.19–0.22 m RMSE, outperforming U-Net and a task-specific transformer with fewer parameters. Multi-temporal training broadens diversity; median-averaging across passes reduces noise. Optimized architectures and pretrained weights released for scalable transfer.

Conclusion: Contiguous-region training blocks and the SWF-weighted loss substantially improve transferability of SDB models; deep architectures generalize better across regions than RF; multi-temporal imagery and inference-time fusion further boost robustness; results support scalable deployment and open-access pretrained weights.

Abstract: Satellite-derived bathymetry (SDB) from multispectral imagery is cost-effective but scales poorly across regions, especially in optically complex coastal environments. We evaluate machine learning and deep learning for transferable SDB over the 0-20 m depth range using Sentinel-2 imagery. A Random Forest baseline and four CNNs (ResNet-50, ResNet-101, EfficientNet-B4, ConvNeXt-Large) are trained on Pratas Island and selected Great Barrier Reef regions, then evaluated on spatially independent intra- and cross-regional test areas. Preserving spatial continuity during training, by keeping contiguous reef blocks rather than random patches, is the single most impactful design choice; we further introduce a Smooth Weight Function (SWF)-weighted RMSE loss that emphasizes near-surface depths. With these choices, intra-regional RMSE ranges from 1.15 to 1.92 m over 0-20 m and is as low as 0.26 m for depths <= 3 m. Random Forest degrades sharply under cross-regional transfer (RMSE 1.53 m -> 2.99-3.78 m), while the deep models stay more robust (2.46-2.98 m). On the public MagicBathyNet aerial-RGB benchmark (0-16 m) the proposed networks reach 0.19-0.22 m RMSE, outperforming a U-Net baseline and a task-specific transformer architecture with substantially fewer parameters. We further exploit multi-temporal repeat imagery: training on it broadens diversity, and median-aggregating predictions across passes at inference reduces noise from changing sun angles, atmospheric conditions, water properties, and tides. We release optimized architectures and pretrained weights to enable scalable transfer to new sites.

</details>


### [7] [GeoDrive-Bench: Benchmarking Region-Specific Multimodal Reasoning in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02774)
*Yingzi Ma,Chaowei Xiao,Ming Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoDrive-Bench evaluates geo-cultural driving reasoning of vision-language models (VLMs) for autonomous driving. It comprises 5,053 QA pairs across six countries, covers perception/prediction/planning/region reasoning, uses country-agnostic cues, and introduces a distillation method to inject region-specific traffic-rule knowledge into VLMs. Experiments on nine VLMs show cross-cultural performance variation; baselines improve geo-cultural reasoning. The work positions GeoDrive-Bench as both a diagnostic and training testbed for deployable autonomous driving foundation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of geo-cultural grounding in VLMs for autonomous driving and to systematically assess and improve region-specific driving intelligence across diverse global settings.

Method: Curate 5,053 human-validated MCQA pairs across six countries focusing on perception, prediction, planning, and region reasoning; questions rely on visual evidence and local traffic conventions without country labels. Evaluate nine state-of-the-art VLMs. Propose a distillation algorithm that injects region-specific traffic-rule knowledge into internal representations to align scene understanding with local policies.

Result: Significant performance variations across geo-driving cultures for all tasks. The proposed baseline models show improved geo-cultural reasoning across regions. Demonstrates that current VLMs lack robust region-aware driving intelligence and that GeoDrive-Bench effectively diagnoses and trains geo-cultural driving capabilities.

Conclusion: GeoDrive-Bench serves as a diagnostic and training-oriented testbed for deployable autonomous driving foundation models, highlighting the need for region-aware reasoning and offering a concrete pathway (data + distillation) to enhance VLMs across diverse driving cultures.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) for autonomous driving have shown promising performance, but their ability to handle region-specific traffic rules remains underexplored, raising uncertainties about their deployment across diverse global settings. We therefore introduce GeoDrive-Bench, a novel benchmark that enables the systematic investigation of VLMs' geo-culturally grounded driving reasoning. We curated 5,053 human-validated multiple-choice QA pairs across six countries covering diverse driving cultures. Specifically, we emphasize four driving tasks: perception, prediction, planning, and region reasoning. Each question requires models to infer the correct driving behavior from visual evidence and local traffic conventions without explicit country labels. Beyond evaluation, we further design a distillation algorithm that injects region-specific traffic-rule knowledge into the internal representations of VLMs, enabling models to better align visual scene understanding with local driving policies. Experiments on nine state-of-the-art VLMs show substantial performance variations across geo-driving cultures for each task, while our proposed baseline models exhibit improved geo-cultural reasoning across regions. These results suggest that current VLMs still lack robust region-aware driving intelligence and highlight GeoDrive-Bench as a diagnostic and training-oriented testbed for deployable autonomous driving foundation models.

</details>


### [8] [Diagnosis of Human Object Interaction Detectors for Real World Educational Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02789)
*Divya Mereddy,Ashwin Tudur Sadashiva,Marcos Quinones-Grueiro,Gautam Biswas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diagnosis-driven HOI adaptation with error taxonomy boosts macro-F1 from 48.6 to 90.2 on CCATT data


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SOTA HOI detectors falter in real-world educational settings due to domain-specific objects, occlusion and challenging visuals; need a diagnostic framework to guide adaptation.

Method: Propose triplet-level HOI error taxonomy plus error-factor attribution; integrate into diagnosis-informed refinement to adapt pretrained HOI models to target CCATT domain; evaluate with CDN model

Result: Macro-F1 improved from 48.6 to 90.2 on CCATT dataset with targeted refinement guided by diagnosed error factors

Conclusion: Detailed diagnostic analysis enables effective targeted adaptation of HOI models for real-world educational/medical training environments

Abstract: Human-object interaction (HOI) recognition is critical for automatically analyzing student behavior in complex educational environments. Although state-of-the-art (SOTA) HOI detectors perform well on benchmark datasets, their performance often degrades when deployed in real-world training environments due to domain-specific objects, occlusions, and complex visual conditions. In this paper, we introduce a diagnosis-driven framework that integrates a triplet-level HOI error taxonomy with error-factor attribution analysis for real-world educational video data. We study this problem in the context of Critical Care Air Transport Team (CCATT) mixed-reality medical training. Based on an analysis of HOI failure modes and their causes, we develop a diagnosis-informed refinement strategy for adapting pretrained HOI models to the target domain. Experiments on the CCATT dataset show that this approach improves the macro-F1 score of a pretrained CDN model from 48.6 to 90.2 through targeted refinement guided by diagnosed error factors. These results highlight the value of detailed diagnostic analysis for informing targeted adaptation of HOI models in real-world educational environments.

</details>

*Aditi,Niket Agarwal,Arslan Ali,Jon Allen,Martin Antolini,Adeline Aubame,Alisson Azzolini,Junjie Bai,Maciej Bala,Yogesh Balaji,Josh Bapst,Aarti Basant,Mukesh Beladiya,Mohammad Qazim Bhat,Zaid Pervaiz Tang,Shitao Tang,Yangyang Tang,Yue Tang,Tolou Tavakkoli,Kayley Ting,Krzysztof Tomala,Wei-Cheng Tseng,Jibin Varghese,Sergei Vasilev,Thomas Volk,Raju Wagwani,Roger Waleffe,Andrew Z. Wang,Boxiang Wang,Haoxiang Wang,Qiao Wang,Shihao Wang,Shijie Wang,Ting-Chun Wang,Yan Wang,Yu Wang,David Wehr,Fangyin Wei,Xinshuo Weng,Jay Zhangjie Wu,Kedi Wu,Hongchi Xia,Summer Xiao,Tianjun Xiao,Kevin Xie,Daguang Xu,Jiashu Xu,Mengyao Xu,Ruqing Xu,Xingqian Xu,Yao Xu,Dinghao Yang,Dong Yang,Hans Yang,Xiaodong Yang,Xuning Yang,Yichu Yang,Yurong You,Zhiding Yu,Hao Yuan,Simon Yuen,Xiaohui Zeng,Pengcuo Zeren,Cindy Zha,Haotian Zhang,Jenny Zhang,Jing Zhang,Liangkai Zhang,Paris Zhang,Shun Zhang,Xuanmeng Zhang,Zhizheng Zhang,Ann Zhao,Yilin Zhao,Yuliya Zhautouskaya,Charles Zhou,Fengzhe Zhou,Shilin Zhu,Yuke Zhu,Dima Zhylko,Artur Zolkowski*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Cosmos 3 is a unified omnimodal world-model framework built on a mixture-of-transformers that handles language, image, video, audio, and actions in flexible IO configurations, achieving state-of-the-art results across understanding and generation tasks and released openly to accelerate research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a general-purpose, scalable backbone for embodied AI that unifies critical modalities (vision, language, video, audio, actions) into a single architecture, enabling seamless input-output configurations and improving performance on diverse tasks.

Method: A family of omnimodal world models (Cosmos 3) based on a mixture-of-transformers architecture with highly flexible input-output configurations to jointly process and generate multiple modalities (language, image, video, audio, actions). Post-training and evaluation on diverse tasks to demonstrate broad capabilities and open-source release.

Result: Achieves new state-of-the-art across a diverse suite of understanding and generation tasks; post-trained Cosmos 3 models ranked as the best open-source Text-to-Image and Image-to-Video models by Artificial Analysis, and best policy model by RoboArena; open release of code, checkpoints, synthetic data, and benchmarks.

Conclusion: Cosmos 3 demonstrates that omnimodal world models can serve as scalable, general-purpose backbones for embodied agents, unifying multiple modalities in a single framework and enabling strong performance across understanding, generation, and control tasks.

Abstract: We introduce Cosmos 3, a family of omnimodal world models designed to jointly process and generate language, image, video, audio, and action sequences within a unified mixture-of-transformers architecture. By supporting highly flexible input-output configurations, Cosmos 3 seamlessly unifies critical modalities for Physical AI -- effectively subsuming vision-language models, video generators, world simulators, and world-action models into a single framework. Our evaluation demonstrates that Cosmos 3 establishes a new state-of-the-art across a diverse suite of understanding and generation tasks, demonstrating omnimodal world models as scalable, general-purpose backbones for embodied agents. Our post-trained Cosmos 3 models were ranked as the best open-source Text-to-Image and Image-to-Video models by Artificial Analysis, and the best policy model by RoboArena at the time the technical report was written. To accelerate open research and deployment in Physical AI, we make our code, model checkpoints, curated synthetic datasets, and evaluation benchmark available under the Linux Foundation's OpenMDW-1.1 https://openmdw.ai/license/1-1/ License at https://github.com/nvidia/cosmos}{github.com/nvidia/cosmos and https://huggingface.co/collections/nvidia/cosmos3 . The project website is available at https://research.nvidia.com/labs/cosmos-lab/cosmos3 .

</details>


### [10] [Automated Report-Derived Oncology VQA Benchmark for Evaluating Vision-Language Models on 3D Medical Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02809)
*Bo Liu,Hanxue Gu,Xiangru Li,Zheren Zhu,Jacob Ellison,Kang Wang,Janine M. Lupo,Yang Yang,Hui Lin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Automates VQA benchmarking from private radiology reports and 3D imaging using RADS-style and report-derived questions; reveals zero-shot VLMs show no clear leader and dataset-specific reliance on vision; pipeline releases as open agent skill with contamination control limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide clinically grounded, scalable, and contamination-controlled benchmarks for vision-language models on medical imaging, addressing limitations of public datasets and potential pretraining leakage.

Method: An automated agent-driven pipeline that creates multiple-choice VQA datasets from paired private radiology reports and 3D oncology imaging. It generates two question types: (1) RADS-style questions derived deterministically from clinician reporting schemas; (2) radiology report-derived questions produced by an LLM from radiologist findings and validated against source reports. Applied to four in-house cancer cohorts, yielding an instance-contamination-controlled benchmark without per-question human annotation.

Result: Zero-shot evaluation on six VLMs shows no dominant model and substantial headroom. A blind ablation shows visual reliance is dataset-specific: liver report-derived questions require the image, while Lung CT can be solved without it; the leading closed model even outperforms its sighted accuracy on Lung CT when blinded, highlighting contamination concerns with private data.

Conclusion: The pipeline is released as an open agent skill for in-house redeployment and provides a scalable, controlled benchmark for medical VLM evaluation, while highlighting limitations in guaranteeing visual capability assessments due to dataset-specific dependencies and potential leakage.

Abstract: Evaluating vision-language models (VLMs) on medical images requires benchmarks that are clinically grounded, scalable, and controlled for evaluation confounds. Existing public benchmarks are limited in scale, manually annotated, or potentially leaked into VLM pretraining corpora. We present an automated agent-driven pipeline that generates multiple-choice VQA datasets directly from paired private radiology reports and 3D oncology imaging, producing two complementary question types: RADS-style questions deterministically derived from clinician-defined reporting schemas, and radiology report-derived questions generated by an LLM from radiologist findings and verified against the source report. Applied to four in-house cancer cohorts, the pipeline yields an instance-contamination-controlled benchmark without per-question human annotation. Zero-shot evaluation of six VLMs reveals no dominant model and substantial headroom across all cells. A blind ablation reveals that visual reliance is highly dataset-specific: liver Report-derived questions genuinely require the image, while Lung CT is essentially solvable without it - the leading closed model exceeds its sighted accuracy on Lung CT when blinded - indicating that even private clinical data does not guarantee a contamination-controlled read of visual capability. The pipeline is released as an open agent skill for in-house redeployment.

</details>


### [11] [Principled Reflection Separation via Nonlinear Superposition and Feature Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02831)
*Qiming Hu,Mingjia Li,Yuntong Li,Xiaojie Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a learnable nonlinear superposition model and a dual-stream interactive framework for single-image reflection separation, addressing nonlinear coupling in real pipelines; demonstrates improved generalization and insights into decomposition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing linear models in the sRGB domain fail to capture the nonlinear coupling introduced by real-world image signal processing, limiting reflection separation performance; a unified approach is needed to model layer interactions.

Method: Introduce a learnable nonlinear superposition model to characterize layer interactions; develop a generalized dual-stream interactive framework with bidirectional feature exchange, unifying activation-, gating-, and attention-based interactions; compatible with CNN and Transformer backbones; provide code and models.

Result: Extensive experiments on diverse real-world benchmarks show superior performance and strong generalization; reveals that reflection separation arises from learning nonlinear formation and interaction rather than undoing a linear mixture.

Conclusion: Reflection separation should center on nonlinear formation and interaction modeling; the proposed framework offers a principled, versatile approach and generalizable insights for image decomposition, with publicly available code.

Abstract: Single-image reflection separation is fundamentally challenged by the entanglement of transmission and reflection layers under complex image formation processes. Existing approaches largely rely on simplified assumptions or independent modeling, limiting their ability to handle real-world scenarios. In this work, we revisit the problem from a unified perspective and identify a key issue of existing approaches, i.e., the widely adopted linear composition model in the sRGB domain fails to capture the nonlinear coupling introduced by real-world image signal processing pipelines. To address this, we introduce a learnable nonlinear superposition model that more faithfully characterizes layer interactions and improves decomposition fidelity. Building upon this formulation, we propose a generalized dual-stream interactive framework that explicitly models bidirectional dependencies between transmission and reflection through feature exchange. This framework unifies activation-, gating-, and attention-based interaction mechanisms, and is compatible with both CNN and Transformer backbones. Extensive experiments on diverse real-world benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves superior performance with strong generalization capability. More importantly, our study reveals that reflection separation is not about undoing a linear mixture, but about learning nonlinear formation and interaction}, offering new insights into the design of principled image decomposition models. Code and models are publicly available at https://mingcv.github.io/DIRS-Page.

</details>


### [12] [Pathway-Structured Privileged Distillation for Deployable Computational Pathology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02877)
*Yongxin Guo,Hao Lu,Onur Koyun,Zhengjie Zhu,Muhammet Demir,Metin Gurcan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MoPE uses pathway-guided distillation to train on RNA data but perform histology-only inference, improving WSI performance for breast cancer across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RNA profiling is often unavailable in routine settings; histology captures morphology linked to molecular programs but cannot fully reconstruct the transcriptome, necessitating learning strategies that leverage RNA data during training while enabling RNA-free inference.

Method: A knowledge-distillation framework named Mixture of Pathway Experts (MoPE). It uses privileged distillation where RNA-derived pathways are encoded and transferred to pathway-indexed pathology experts via memory-usage alignment. This enables RNA-informed supervision to guide training without requiring RNA at inference time.

Result: MoPE consistently improves whole-slide image (WSI)–only inference performance compared with baselines across diverse public benchmarks and two independent breast cancer cohorts. Pathway-usage analyses and human-audited visual inspections provide interpretable readouts of the model’s behavior.

Conclusion: Pathway-structured privileged distillation is a promising approach to incorporate molecular information during training while preserving RNA-free inference, suggesting broader applicability for multimodal learning under partial observability.

Abstract: Integrating transcriptomics and histopathology can improve cancer risk modelling, yet practical use is constrained by the limited availability of RNA profiling in routine settings. Here we introduce Mixture of Pathway Experts (MoPE), a knowledge-distillation framework that reframes multimodal learning as privileged distillation for histology-only inference. MoPE is motivated by the partial observability between RNA profiles and whole-slide images: histology can capture morphology-linked consequences of certain molecular programmes, but cannot be expected to reconstruct the full transcriptomic state. MoPE encodes RNA-derived pathways and transfers the molecular supervision to pathway-indexed pathology experts through memory-usage alignment. Across diverse public benchmarks and two independent breast cancer cohorts, MoPE consistently improved WSI-only inference performance relative to baseline methods. Pathway-usage analyses and human-audited visual inspection provide bounded inspection of model behaviour and candidate morphology-linked readouts. These results support pathway-structured privileged distillation as a promising route to using molecular information during training while preserving RNA-free inference.

</details>


### [13] [Tiny Collaborative Inference for Occlusion-Robust Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02894)
*Chieh-Tung Cheng,Mustafa Aslanov,Eiman Kanjo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Decision-level fusion using Weighted Boxes Fusion (WBF) improves occlusion-robust object detection on ultra-low-end hardware (sub-1 MB SRAM) with MCUNet+YOLOv2 and TensorFlow Lite quantisation. Across two and three viewpoints, WBF outperforms feature-level fusion, achieving up to ~0.38 mAP gains; energy and bandwidth costs are modest, enabling on-device multi-board operation; federated learning is explored but not central due to non-iid data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable locally run object detection on resource-constrained edge devices amid occlusion and limited communication, by comparing fusion strategies and validating hardware deployment.

Method: Evaluate two collaboration strategies: 1) feature-level fusion by concatenating intermediate features, 2) decision-level fusion using Weighted Boxes Fusion on regional detections. Use MCUNet backbone + YOLOv2 head with TensorFlow Lite quantisation; test with two and three views; measure mAP; implement on-device WBF; deploy on Coral Dev Board Micro with USB-relay baseline and Wi-Fi P2P.

Result: WBF yields higher mAP under occlusion (up to +0.2736 mAP in asymmetric occlusion); three-view fusion improves to +0.3827 mAP. Frame coverage improved by 29.8% in a ~302s run (61 fused frames vs 47). Communication overhead ~1.3 KB per fusion exchange. Energy usage for communication small relative to inference; host-free multi-board operation demonstrated. Exploratory decentralized federated learning note included but results limited under non-iid data.

Conclusion: Decision-level fusion is a viable strategy to enhance occlusion robustness for tiny edge detectors, enabling efficient multi-board collaboration on ultra-low-end hardware without centralized fusion.

Abstract: Small edge devices such as IoT surveillance nodes and search-and-rescue (SAR) platforms are increasingly expected to run computer vision locally. On ultra-low-end hardware, however, object detection is limited by available memory and compute, by communication costs when several devices cooperate, and by the loss of accuracy caused by occlusion. The work evaluates occlusion-robust object detection on devices with less than 1 MB SRAM by combining an MCUNet backbone, a YOLOv2 detection head, and TensorFlow Lite quantisation. We evaluate two collaborative inference strategies: feature-level fusion, which concatenates intermediate feature maps, and decision-level fusion via Weighted Boxes Fusion (WBF). Under the tested occlusion settings, WBF outperforms feature-level fusion and gives gains of up to +0.2736 mAP in asymmetric occlusion scenarios. Extending fusion to three views improves accuracy further (up to +0.3827 mAP) while adding communication overhead (approximately 1.3 KB per exchange). The hardware experiments start with a host-assisted USB-relay baseline and then move to a Wi-Fi peer-to-peer deployment on two Coral Dev Board Micro units, where WBF runs on-device and communication energy remains small relative to inference. In a representative 301.9 s autonomous session comprising 108 frames, fused output is observed on 61 frames compared with 47 for Board 2 alone, a frame-level coverage gain of +29.8%. We also include a small exploratory decentralised federated learning (DFL) feasibility note, but do not treat it as a main result because performance remains limited under non-iid local data. The results support decision-level fusion as a viable option for improving occlusion robustness in small-scale edge object detection, including host-free multi-board operation on ultra-low-end hardware.

</details>


### [14] [Any2Poster: Any-Source Poster Generation Across Modalities and Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02915)
*Amogh Vinaykumar,Aiden Li,Suozhi Huang,Shilong Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A new benchmark suite Any2Poster Bench enables cross-source poster generation across eight input modalities and five content domains; paired with Any2Poster Agent, it yields strong cross-modal/per-domain performance and a higher PaperQuiz-style score, offering a standard for multimodal poster generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluations are narrow (paper-only inputs, domain-specific) and rely on surface visual similarity. There is a need for a unified, reproducible benchmark to assess information fidelity and visual communication across diverse sources and domains.

Method: Two components: Any2Poster Bench for evaluation, combining quiz probes and VLM-based judgments; Any2Poster Agent as end-to-end reference agent that parses sources, extracts salient content, plans layouts, renders posters, and refines via visual feedback. Bench covers eight input modalities (PDF, URL, PPTX, DOCX, Markdown, LaTeX, notebooks, videos) and five content domains.

Result: Agent achieved 87.25% average accuracy across input modalities and 87.28% across content domains on the bench. In PaperQuiz-style evaluation, Agent improved from PosterAgent-4o's 51.06-51.33% to 72.58% overall accuracy; density-augmented score rose from 116-121 to 145.16.

Conclusion: The benchmark and agent provide a reusable evaluation resource and a competitive baseline for multimodal, domain-general poster generation.

Abstract: Visual posters are a compact medium for communicating dense information, yet progress on automatic poster generation remains difficult to measure because existing evaluations are often restricted to paper-only inputs, narrow domains, or surface-level visual similarity. We introduce Any2Poster Bench, a benchmark for any-source poster generation that evaluates systems across eight input modalities--PDFs, URLs, PPTX, DOCX, Markdown, LaTeX, notebooks, and videos--and five content domains. Any2Poster Bench pairs each source with quiz-based probes of verbatim factual retention and interpretive understanding, together with VLM-based judgments of visual quality, layout, readability, content completeness, and logical flow, enabling reproducible assessment of both information fidelity and visual communication. To instantiate and validate this benchmark, we further present Any2Poster Agent, an end-to-end reference agent that parses heterogeneous sources, organizes salient content, plans poster layouts, renders posters, and iteratively refines them using visual feedback. On Any2Poster Bench, Any2Poster Agent achieves 87.25% average accuracy across input modalities and 87.28% across content domains. On PaperQuiz-style evaluation, where prior paper-to-poster agents are directly comparable, Any2Poster Agent improves over PosterAgent-4o from 51.06-51.33% to 72.58% overall accuracy and from 116-121 to 145.16 in density-augmented score. Together, Any2Poster Bench and Any2Poster Agent provide a reusable evaluation resource and a competitive baseline for studying multimodal, domain-general poster generation.

</details>


### [15] [Pixel Cube: Diffusion-based Portrait Video Relighting Through Realistic Lighting Reproduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02919)
*Yufan Zhang,Yu Ji,Ayo Ajiboye,Rundi Wu,Yu Guo,Changxi Zheng,Jinwei Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion-based method for photorealistic, temporally consistent relighting of dynamic portrait videos using hybrid real and rendered data, HDR lighting per frame, and background synthesis to control exposure and color. Achieves identity preservation and state-of-the-art photorealism and temporal coherence under new lighting and unseen data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable photorealistic, temporally coherent relighting of dynamic portraits under novel lighting while preserving facial identity and fine details, leveraging diffusion priors and rich training data.

Method: Train on a hybrid dataset of real-captured and rendered dynamic portraits. Use an LED-based lighting system for realistic lighting and fast data capture. Employ per-frame HDR environment maps as lighting controls. Leverage pre-trained video diffusion priors and synthesize a background image to control exposure and color tone. Train a high-performance generative model to produce temporally consistent relit portraits that preserve expressions and fine facial features.

Result: Extensive experiments show temporally consistent, photorealistic relighting with various environment maps, generalizing to unseen subjects, motions, and lighting. Practical portrait photography applications with state-of-the-art performance in photorealism, lighting harmony, and temporal consistency.

Conclusion: The approach enables realistic, identity-preserving relighting of dynamic portraits under new environments with strong temporal coherence, and generalizes well to unseen data, supporting practical deployment in portrait photography.

Abstract: We present a diffusion-based method for relighting dynamic portrait videos with photorealism and temporal consistency. Our method is fueled by a hybrid training dataset that consists of real-captured and rendered dynamic portrait videos with diverse subject appearances, facial motions, head poses, and known lighting conditions. Specifically, we construct an LED-based lighting system for realistic lighting emulation and high-speed video relighting data acquisition. By leveraging the image priors embedded in pre-trained video diffusion models, and using per-frame high dynamic range (HDR) environment map as lighting control, we train a high-performance generative model for realistic and identity-preserving dynamic portrait video relighting. In addition to the environment map control, our model uses a synthesized background image to enable control on the camera's exposure level and color tone. Our model can produce temporally consistent relit portrait video that looks realistic and harmonious under a provided new environment and faithfully preserve the subject's expression and fine facial features, including skin tone, wrinkles, and facial hair. Our model generalizes well to unseen data, in terms of the subject appearance, motion, and lighting condition. We perform extensive experiments on relighting in-the-wild videos with various environment maps and demonstrate practical applications on portrait photography. Results show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in photorealism, lighting harmony, and temporal consistency.

</details>


### [16] [ATLAS: A Large-Scale Evaluation Benchmark for Adversarial LiDAR Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02924)
*Mellon M. Zhang,Siddhant Panse,Zimo Fan,Akshal Dhal,Rishit Sarkar,Glen Chou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ATLAS is a large-scale, physically grounded benchmark that evaluates LiDAR perception under black-box sensor attacks (point injection and removal) across real driving sequences. It reveals an asymmetry: stronger models resist removal but are more vulnerable to injection, links vulnerability to object database sampling augmentations, and provides open-source code to enable reproducible evaluations and future defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world autonomous driving requires robustness to structured, adversarial, and potentially black-box sensor perturbations, especially for LiDAR. Existing benchmarks inadequately probe these failure modes, hindering development of robust perception systems.

Method: Introduce ATLAS: a large-scale, physically grounded evaluation suite that simulates two attack modes—point injection and point removal—across real driving sequences. Evaluate a broad set of state-of-the-art LiDAR perception models; analyze failure modes; trace robustness gaps to augmentation practices; propose initial mitigation directions; release the generation code for reproducibility.

Result: Discovery of robustness asymmetry: models with higher standard benchmark performance are more robust to removal attacks but more vulnerable to injection attacks. Attribution of this vulnerability to object database sampling augmentations, indicating architecture-agnostic robustness failures induced by training practices.

Conclusion: ATLAS provides a concrete, reproducible benchmark for assessing black-box LiDAR sensor robustness, highlighting practical attack vulnerabilities and guiding future defense strategies; code release supports ongoing evaluation as attack methods evolve.

Abstract: Autonomous driving perception is typically evaluated on clean benchmark data, yet real-world deployment requires robustness to rare, structured, and potentially adversarial sensor anomalies. This gap is especially critical for LiDAR, where external actors can physically manipulate the sensing process to induce black-box perception failures without accessing the model. Existing LiDAR benchmarks provide little visibility into this failure mode. Prior adversarial LiDAR studies have largely centered on attack hardware, geometric and algorithmic defenses, and early-generation detectors, leaving the robustness of modern perception systems unexplored. To address this evaluation gap, we introduce ATLAS (Adversarial Temporal LiDAR Attack Suite), the first large-scale, physically grounded evaluation benchmark for LiDAR perception models under black-box sensor attacks, simulating the two primary attack modes -- point injection and point removal -- across real driving sequences. Evaluating a broad cross-section of current state-of-the-art LiDAR perception models, ATLAS reveals a surprising robustness asymmetry: models with stronger performance on standard benchmarks tend to better withstand removal attacks, yet are actually more vulnerable to injection attacks than weaker models. We trace this vulnerability to standard object database sampling augmentations, revealing how current training practices can induce architecture-agnostic robustness failures, and study initial directions for mitigating both attack modes. We release the ATLAS generation code to support extensible, reproducible evaluations as attack capabilities evolve, helping make black-box sensor robustness an explicit consideration in future LiDAR perception development.

</details>


### [17] [SaluNet: Enabling Total Plasticity in Normalization-Free Deep Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02927)
*Mourad Zaied*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SALU replaces normalization with a single learnable activation, enabling normalization-free networks (SaluNet) that reportedly match or exceed normalization-based baselines on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet, with transformers also benefiting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Normalization layers (BatchNorm/LayerNorm) are observed to suppress plasticity when paired with learnable activation parameters. The authors hypothesize that standard normalization erodes the adaptability of activations, motivating a normalization-free paradigm that preserves plasticity through a bounded, learnable activation.

Method: Introduce SALU, a saturating adaptive linear unit SALU(x;a,b)= a x / sqrt(1+ a b x^2) with positive learnable parameters a,b. Build SaluNet where SALU replaces normalization (SaluNet-C), and variants SWALU/GALU replace standard activations. Evaluate on ResNet-18/ResNet-50 (ImageNet) and transformers, comparing against normalized baselines (BatchNorm/LayerNorm-GELU).

Result: SaluNet-C-18 achieves 97.35% on CIFAR-10 and 83.25% on CIFAR-100 without normalization, maintaining 93.44% and 76.23% at batch size 1. For transformers, SaluNet-T outperforms LayerNorm-GELU from 90.92% to 91.01% (CIFAR-10) and 66.54% to 68.10% (CIFAR-100). SaluNet-C-50 reaches 78.67% Top-1 on ImageNet-1K at 224x224 and 79.23% at 288x288. The results suggest normalization suppresses total plasticity, which the paper argues is a natural property of biological neurons.

Conclusion: Normalization layers may be unnecessary for stable training if a proper bounded, learnable activation (SALU) provides intrinsic stabilization. The SaluNet family claims competitive or superior performance across CNNs and transformers without normalization, highlighting a new direction toward learning-only plasticity in deep networks.

Abstract: Normalization layers such as BatchNorm and LayerNorm have long been considered essential for stable training in deep networks. This work demonstrates that they can be fully replaced by a single learnable activation mechanism. We identify a plasticity suppression effect induced by standard normalization: learnable activation parameters rapidly lose adaptability when paired with normalization layers. Motivated by this observation, we introduce SALU (Saturated Adaptive Linear Unit), \[ \operatorname{SALU}(x;a,b) = \frac{a x}{\sqrt{1 + a b x^2}},\quad a>0,\; b>0 \] a bounded, learnable activation that provides intrinsic signal stabilization without relying on batch statistics or external affine parameters. Building on SALU, we propose SaluNet, a paradigm grounded in total plasticity: SALU replaces normalization layers, while SWALU and GALU replace standard activations. With ResNet-18, SaluNet-C-18 achieves 97.35\% on CIFAR-10 and 83.25\% on CIFAR-100 without normalization, maintaining 93.44\% and 76.23\% at batch size 1 where normalized architectures fail. For transformers, SaluNet-T improves over LayerNorm-GELU from 90.92\% to 91.01\% on CIFAR-10 and from 66.54\% to 68.10\% on CIFAR-100. SaluNet-C-50 reaches 78.67\% Top-1 on ImageNet-1K at $224\times224$, and $79.23\%$ at $288\times288$. These results suggest normalization layers suppress total plasticity, a property biological neurons inherently possess, enabling deep networks to learn effectively.

</details>


### [18] [CAD-to-CT Registration of Cylindrical Objects via Ellipse-Based Axis Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02935)
*Aleksander Ogonowski,Mikołaj Mrozowski,Daniel Więcek,Arkadiusz Ćwiek,Konrad Klimaszewski,Rafał Możdżonek,Adam Padee,Lech Raczyński,Piotr Wasiuk,Wojciech Wiślicki,Michał Matusiak,Sławomir Wronka*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-stage registration for cylindrical CAD-to-CT without intensity calibration: detect the 3D rotation axis from elliptical cross-sections in CT slices, then align a voxelized CAD model by maximizing volumetric overlap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable ground-truth geometry and object masks are crucial for volumetric imaging and ML datasets. Traditional intensity-calibrated methods may fail when CT values lack references; point-based methods need correspondences that are absent between ideal CAD geometry and noisy CT data.

Method: Stage 1 detects the rotation axis by fitting ellipses to edge-detected cross-sections across CT slices and applying PCA on ellipse centers after RANSAC outlier removal. Stage 2 voxelizes the CAD model, orients it along the detected axis, and translationally maximizes volumetric overlap with the CT scan.

Result: Registration achieves tilt and orientation errors below 0.1 degrees without intensity calibration or feature matching, enabling the CAD model to serve as ground truth geometry for ML localization and automated industrial CT workflows.

Conclusion: The approach provides robust CAD-to-CT registration for cylindrical objects, facilitating ground-truth generation in ML pipelines and industrial CT analysis without relying on calibrated intensities or explicit feature correspondences.

Abstract: Accurate registration of CAD models to CT scans is essential for establishing ground truth geometry in volumetric imaging. Obtaining reliable object masks is of growing importance in machine learning settings; as recent architectures grow more capable, huge datasets are required to fully utilise their capabilities. Traditional intensity-based methods fail when CT grayscale values lack calibration references, while point-based algorithms (e.g., ICP, RANSAC) require feature correspondence unavailable between idealized CAD geometry and noisy volumetric CT data.
  We propose a two-stage geometric registration method for cylindrical objects (ionization chambers) that takes advantage of the distinctive geometric features of the objects. First, we estimate the 3D rotation axis by detecting elliptical cross-sections across CT slices, fitting ellipses to edge-detected contours, and performing PCA on the fitted ellipse centers after RANSAC outlier removal. Second, we voxelize the CAD model, orient it along the detected axis, and maximize volumetric overlap with the CT scan through translational adjustment.
  This approach achieves robust registration with tilt and orientation errors below $0.1^\circ$ without intensity calibration or feature matching. Once registered, the aligned CAD model provides ground truth geometry for applications including machine learning-based object localization and automated analysis in industrial CT workflows.

</details>


### [19] [The Road Ahead in Autonomous Driving: The KITScenes Multimodal Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02956)
*Richard Schwarzkopf,Fabian Immel,Alexander Blumberg,Jonas Merkert,Nils Rack,Kaiwen Wang,Fabian Konstantinidis,Julian Truetsch,Carlos Fernandez,Annika Bätz,Kevin Rösch,Marlon Steiner,Willi Poh,Yinzhe Shen,Royden Wagner,Felix Hauser,Dominik Strutz,Jaime Villa,Gleb Stepanov,Holger Caesar,Ömer Şahin Taş,Frank Bieder,Jan-Hendrik Pauls,Christoph Stiller*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: KITScenes Multimodal introduces a European autonomous driving dataset with high-fidelity synchronized sensors (global-shutter cameras, long-range >400m lidar, 4D radar, GNSS/INS) and the most complete HD maps to date, including 3D, reprojection-accurate traffic elements with full topology. It also adds four benchmarks—online HD map construction, long-range depth estimation, novel view synthesis, and end-to-end driving—to advance spatial learning for embodied AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address gaps in existing datasets regarding sensor fidelity, map completeness, and geographic diversity, enabling more realistic and scalable autonomous driving research and embodied AI applications.

Method: Assembles a fully synchronized multimodal sensor suite (high-resolution global-shutter cameras, long-range lidar (>400 m), 4D imaging radar) with redundant GNSS/INS, and develops HD maps that include 3D traffic elements with topological connectivity. Data collected in European cities with irregular street layouts and mixed traffic modes. Introduces four benchmarks: online HD map construction, long-range depth estimation, novel view synthesis, and end-to-end driving.

Result: Dataset provides highly complete HD maps validated through autonomous driving trials on open-source software. It records from diverse European cities and offers four benchmarks that push spatial learning for embodied AI.

Conclusion: KITScenes Multimodal advances autonomous-driving research by delivering high-fidelity sensor data, highly complete 3D HD maps with topology, and four benchmarks that target key embodied AI tasks, thereby broadening geographic diversity and enabling robust evaluation on open-source stacks.

Abstract: Existing autonomous driving datasets have enabled major progress, but fall short in sensor fidelity, map completeness, or geographic diversity. We present KITScenes Multimodal, a European dataset built around high-fidelity sensors and maps. Our fully synchronized sensor suite combines high-resolution global-shutter cameras, long-range lidar beyond 400m, 4D imaging radar, and redundant GNSS/INS localization. Our HD maps are, to our knowledge, the most complete of any sensor dataset, validated through autonomous driving trials on open-source software. For the first time in a public dataset, all driving-relevant traffic elements, such as traffic lights, are mapped in 3D to a reprojection-accurate level with full topological connectivity. Recorded in cities with irregular street layouts and mixed traffic modes, our dataset complements existing datasets by broadening the available geographic diversity. We also introduce four benchmarks, each advancing spatial learning for embodied AI: online HD map construction, long-range depth estimation, novel view synthesis, and end-to-end driving. Project page: https://kitscenes.com/

</details>


### [20] [Hand Trajectory Fusion for Egocentric Natural Language Query Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02962)
*Enmin Zhong,Carlos R. del-Blanco,Fernando Jaureguizar,Narciso García*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces a hand-trajectory encoder to incorporate hand motion into NLQ grounding in egocentric video, boosting grounding for hand-centric queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hand motion is a strong, underutilized cue in egocentric NLQ grounding; about 41% of NLQ queries relate to hand-object manipulation or its outcomes, suggesting potential performance gains by explicitly modeling hand dynamics.

Method: Develop a hand-trajectory encoder that converts a sequence of hand skeletons into semantic hand kinematic features. Fuse these features with pretrained video-text representations using cross-attention with adaptive gating..

Result: On Ego4D NLQ v2 validation, notable improvements for Hand-Object Interaction queries (+2.54 R1@IoU=0.3) and Quantity/State queries (+4.32 R1@IoU=0.3), indicating hand trajectory provides additional grounding cues beyond appearance.

Conclusion: Explicit hand-trajectory modeling yields measurable gains in NLQ grounding, particularly for queries centered on hand-object interactions and state/quantity information, validating the cue's utility beyond appearance-based cues.

Abstract: Egocentric Natural Language Query (NLQ) grounding asks a model to localize, in a long first-person video, the temporal interval that answers a free-form text query. Existing methods fuse video appearance with the query but ignore hand motion, despite the fact that roughly 41% of Ego4D NLQ queries are answered at a moment of hand--object manipulation or their immediate outcomes.We propose a hand-trajectory encoder for converting a sequence of hand skeletons into highly-semantic hand kinematic features, which are then aligned and combined with pretrained video--text features through a cross-attention fusion strategy with adaptive gating. On the Ego4D NLQ v2 validation split, the clearest gains appear for Hand-Object Interaction queries (+2.54 R1@IoU=0.3) and Quantity/State queries (+4.32 R1@IoU=0.3), indicating that hand trajectory provides grounding cues beyond appearance alone.

</details>


### [21] [Towards Compact Autonomous Driving Perception with Balanced Learning and Multi-sensor Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02979)
*Oskar Natan,Jun Miura*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A compact single-pass multi-task perception model for autonomous driving that fuses RGB, DVS, and LiDAR data to perform semantic segmentation, depth estimation, LiDAR segmentation, and BEV projection, with adaptive loss weighting; achieves strong results with fewer parameters and faster inference across CARLA and nuScenes-lidarseg; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve real-time, multi-task perception in autonomous driving with fewer parameters, leveraging multi-modal data and adaptive balancing for many tasks.

Method: Proposed compact deep multi-task framework with shared encoder and task-specific heads for semantic segmentation, depth, LiDAR segmentation, and BEV; intermediate sensor fusion of RGB, DVS, and LiDAR from multiple ego positions; data preprocessing; adaptive loss weighting to manage task imbalance.

Result: Ablation study shows improved performance with the proposed method. Competitive against combinations of recent models. Maintains better performance with fewer parameters, enabling faster inference and lower GPU memory usage. Consistent results across 3 CARLA datasets and 1 nuScenes-lidarseg dataset.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates efficient, accurate multi-task perception in autonomous driving and is suitable for real-time deployment; code and files are publicly released.

Abstract: We present a novel compact deep multi-task learning model to handle various autonomous driving perception tasks in one forward pass. The model performs multiple views of semantic segmentation, depth estimation, light detection and ranging (LiDAR) segmentation, and bird's eye view projection simultaneously without being supported by other models. We also provide an adaptive loss weighting algorithm to tackle the imbalanced learning issue that occurred due to plenty of given tasks. Through data pre-processing and intermediate sensor fusion techniques, the model can process and combine multiple input modalities retrieved from RGB cameras, dynamic vision sensors (DVS), and LiDAR placed at several positions on the ego vehicle. Therefore, a better understanding of a dynamically changing environment can be achieved. Based on the ablation study, the model variant trained with our proposed method achieves a better performance. Furthermore, a comparative study is also conducted to clarify its performance and effectiveness against the combination of some recent models. As a result, our model maintains better performance even with much fewer parameters. Hence, the model can inference faster with less GPU memory utilization. Moreover, the result tends to be consistent in 3 different CARLA simulation datasets and 1 real-world nuScenes-lidarseg dataset. To support future research, we share codes and other files publicly at https://github.com/oskarnatan/compact-perception.

</details>


### [22] [MUSE: A Unified Agentic Harness for MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03005)
*Jianglin Lu,Hailing Wang,Xu Ma,Qihua Dong,Mingyuan Zhang,Yizhou Wang,Yun Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Wrapping frozen multimodal LLMs with a modular, verifier-guided execution harness (MUSE) yields robust, model-agnostic gains across visual-spatial, perception, and multimodal tasks without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome persistent failures of MLLMs by improving the surrounding execution scaffold rather than modifying the model itself; explore how far capabilities can be elicited from a frozen MLLM.

Method: Introduce MUSE: a multimodal unified structured execution harness that wraps any off-the-shelf MLLM with composable modules for task representation, visual processing, perception tool use, structured parsing, deterministic verification, and verifier-guided repair; no model training or fine-tuning. Evaluate on diverse benchmarks with multiple state-of-the-art MLLMs.

Result: MUSE delivers consistent performance gains over the bare MLLM across visual spatial planning, visual perception, multimodal reasoning, and fine-grained discrimination; the largest improvements occur on challenging instances; harness-level shortcomings, not model deficits, account for many failures and can be addressed via verifier-guided repair without retraining.

Conclusion: Agentic multimodal harnesses represent a critical, orthogonal design dimension for enhancing MLLMs beyond model-centric optimization.

Abstract: Despite rapid progress, multimodal large language models (MLLMs) still fail on tasks that humans solve effortlessly, such as navigating a grid maze from a screenshot or selecting the correct puzzle piece. Rather than retraining the model, we ask a complementary question: how much capability can be elicited from a frozen MLLM purely by improving the execution scaffold around it? We introduce MUSE, a multimodal unified structured execution harness that wraps any off-the-shelf MLLM with composable modules for task representation, visual processing, perception tool use, structured parsing, deterministic verification, and verifier-guided repair, without any model retraining. We evaluate MUSE across diverse benchmarks spanning visual spatial planning, visual perception, multimodal reasoning, and fine-grained visual discrimination, using multiple state-of-the-art MLLMs. MUSE delivers consistent gains over the bare model in all settings, with the largest jumps on challenging instances. Further analysis reveals that many MLLM failures arise from harness-level shortcomings rather than fundamental model deficits, and can be addressed through verifier-guided repair without touching the model. These findings highlight the agentic multimodal harness as a critical yet underexplored design dimension, offering an orthogonal avenue for improving MLLMs beyond model-centric optimization.

</details>


### [23] [FCUS-rPPG: A Fast-Converging Unsupervised Framework for Remote Photoplethysmography via Gradient Oscillation Suppression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03050)
*Jiajie Li,Yu Liu,Rencheng Song,Xun Chen,Juan Cheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FCUS-rPPG introduces a fast-converging unsupervised rPPG framework with a spectrally shared backbone and a unified optimization strategy across gradients, loss landscapes, and feature representations, achieving one-epoch training and strong cross-dataset generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unsupervised rPPG methods suffer from noisy/unstable gradients, slow convergence, and limited cross-domain generalization due to weak supervision and noisy signals.

Method: A spectrally shared backbone enforces BVP feature disentanglement by leveraging multi-spectral covariation and a low-dimensional manifold structure. The optimization framework includes: (1) post-verification masking to filter misleading gradients using a weak-amplitude physiological prior of BVP; (2) perturbation-based loss landscape smoothing to steer optimization toward flat minima; (3) noise-aware null-space regularization constraining updates to the orthogonal complement of the noise subspace to reduce noise-induced drift.

Result: Evaluations on five datasets show FCUS-rPPG can be trained in a single epoch, substantially faster than prior methods that require tens to hundreds of epochs, while delivering state-of-the-art cross-dataset performance.

Conclusion: The proposed framework delivers an efficient and robust unsupervised rPPG solution with strong generalization, facilitating practical deployment. Source code will be released publicly.

Abstract: Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) enables non-contact extraction of blood volume pulse (BVP) signals using consumer-grade cameras. Recent unsupervised rPPG methods learn BVP representations without requiring ground-truth physiological annotations, yet their optimization is often hindered by noisy and unstable gradients, resulting in slow convergence and limited cross-domain generalization. In this paper, we propose FCUS-rPPG, a fast-converging unsupervised rPPG framework with strong generalization capability. Motivated by the observation that BVP representations exhibit both multi-spectral covariation and low-dimensional manifold structure, we design a spectrally shared backbone that facilitates BVP feature disentanglement while improving optimization efficiency. To jointly enhance convergence stability and generalization performance, we further develop a unified optimization framework operating at the gradient, loss-landscape, and feature-representation levels. Specifically, a post-verification masking mechanism filters out misleading gradients according to the weak-amplitude physiological prior of BVP signals; a perturbation-based loss landscape smoothing strategy steers optimization toward more generalizable flat minima; and a noise-aware null-space regularization constrains feature updates to the orthogonal complement of the noise subspace, thereby mitigating noise-induced representation drift. Extensive experiments on five datasets demonstrate that FCUS-rPPG requires only one training epoch, whereas existing methods typically require tens to hundreds of epochs. Notably, FCUS-rPPG consistently achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in cross-dataset evaluations. This study provides an efficient and robust solution to the real-world deployment of unsupervised rPPG. The source code will be publicly available at https://github.com/JiaJieLee/FCUS-rPPG.

</details>


### [24] [ROBUST-WT: Robust Uncertainty-aware Segmentation Transform via Whitening and Training Enhancements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03069)
*Aqsa Naseer,Maryam Bibi,Syeda Samiya Urooj,Muhammad Khurram Shahzad*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Enhances WT-PSE for cross-domain medical image segmentation with four training-time improvements (domain-adaptive augmentations, hybrid BCE-Dice loss, curriculum-based Dice weighting, and ablation-capable controls). On fundus optic disc segmentation, final-epoch Dice reaches 0.956 and ASD 13.31, outperforming the baseline epoch-5 Dice of 0.939, suggesting gains from training-level tweaks without architectural changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalized segmentation across multiple imaging devices and protocols remains challenging due to domain shift. WT-PSE addresses this via feature decorrelation and Wasserstein-distance-based distillation, but initial training setup had limits that could reduce robustness.

Method: Four enhancements: (1) domain-adaptive augmentation (random erasing, gamma correction, salt-and-pepper noise); (2) hybrid loss (BCE + Dice) to improve edge sensitivity under noise; (3) curriculum-based Dice weight scheduling; (4) command-line flags enabling systematic ablations for controlled comparisons.

Result: Empirical evaluation on a fundus optic disc benchmark shows improved performance: final-epoch optic-disc Dice = 0.956; ASD = 13.31; baseline epoch-5 Dice = 0.939. Indicates training-time improvements yield consistent gains without modifying WT-PSE architecture.

Conclusion: Training-level improvements are effective for cross-domain segmentation, delivering performance gains while preserving the WT-PSE framework. The findings support incorporating augmentations, loss shaping, progressive weighting, and ablation-centric experimentation to enhance robustness across domains.

Abstract: Generalized segmentation of medical images prevents performance degradation when different imaging devices and clinical protocols are used across multiple domains. The Whitening Transform-based Probabilistic Shape Regularization Extractor (WT-PSE), published in IEEE Transactions on Medical Imaging in 2024, addresses this challenge by employing feature decorrelation and Wasserstein distance-based knowledge distillation to achieve robust cross-domain segmentation. This study systematically examines improvements to the WT-PSE learning framework. Four limitations in the original implementation are identified: limited training augmentations that fail to simulate real scanner variations, reliance on per-pixel binary cross-entropy loss that is sensitive to edge noise, the absence of a scheduled loss weighting strategy that may destabilize early training, and the lack of ablation switches for controlled scientific comparison. To address these issues, we propose four enhancements: (1) domain-adaptive augmentation including random erasing, gamma correction, and salt-and-pepper noise; (2) a hybrid BCE and Dice loss function for improved edge-aware segmentation under noisy conditions; (3) a curriculum-based Dice weight scheduling strategy; and (4) command-line control flags for systematic ablation studies. Experiments on the fundus optic disc segmentation benchmark demonstrate that the improved pipeline achieves a final epoch optic-disc Dice score of 0.956 and an ASD score of 13.31, outperforming the baseline epoch-5 Dice score of 0.939. These results indicate that training-level improvements can provide consistent performance gains without modifying the underlying WT-PSE architecture.

</details>


### [25] [TGV-KV: Text-Grounded KV Eviction for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03075)
*Jizhihui Liu,Ruizi Han,Miao Zhang,Rui Shao,Xuebo Liu,Weili Guan,Yaowei Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Text-Grounded KV Eviction (TGV-KV) for vision-language models reduces inference memory while preserving accuracy by grounding visual drop-in information in textual guidance, using three modules: TVB, TWR, and TPR. It achieves 99.2% full-KV accuracy on VizWiz-VQA with LLaVA-NeXT and up to 52.6% throughput gain at a small retention budget (5%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV caching in auto-regressive VLMs scales linearly with context length, with substantial visual redundancy. Existing KV eviction methods are LM-centric and degrade VLM performance due to the text-vision gap. A method that accounts for cross-modal interaction is needed to maintain accuracy while reducing memory.
