Method: Propose TGV-KV with three submodules: (1) Text-Vision Budgeting (TVB) allocates per-layer KV budgets guided by mutual information between text and vision; (2) Text-Weighted Ranking (TWR) ranks and prioritizes text and assigns importance to vision based on weighted text-image attention; (3) Text-Prioritised Retention (TPR) preserves text KV to prevent loss of textual information. Evaluated across five models and architectures on VizWiz-VQA tasks.

Result: TGV-KV preserves 99.2% of full-KV accuracy on VizWiz-VQA when using LLaVA-NeXT, and increases end-to-end throughput by 52.6% under an extreme retention budget of 5%. Code available at the provided GitHub repository.

Conclusion: Text-grounded KV eviction effectively mitigates cross-modal information loss and yields substantial inference-time efficiency gains in VLMs. The approach demonstrates strong robustness across multiple models and tasks, suggesting broad applicability and potential generalization beyond VizWiz-VQA.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) inherit the auto-regressive generation paradigm and cache the keys and values (KV) of all previous tokens to accelerate inference, resulting in memory consumption that scales linearly with context length. This issue is particularly pronounced in VLMs due to substantial redundancy in the visual modality. Although KV cache eviction approaches can effectively reduce inference memory, they often incur significant performance degradation in VLMs, as most are designed for language models and overlook the inherent gap between text and vision. By systematically analyzing the modality gap in VLMs in this work, we argue that the importance of visual information should be grounded in textual guidance and accordingly propose a Text-Grounded KV Eviction method for VLMs (TGV-KV). TGV-KV comprises three submodules: (1) Text-Vision Budgeting (TVB) assigns budget to each layer based on the mutual information interaction. (2) Text-Weighted Ranking (TWR) assesses the priority of text and ranks vision importance based on weighted text-image attention. (3) Text-Prioritised Retention (TPR) policy strategically preserves text KV to avoid acute information loss. We evaluate TGV-KV across five models with different sizes and architectures, showing that TGV-KV preserves 99.2% full-KV accuracy on the VizWiz-VQA task with LLaVA-NeXT and boosts end-to-end throughput by 52.6% with an extreme retention budget of 5%. Code is available at https://github.com/Danielement321/TGV-KV.

</details>


### [26] [Hierarchical Federated Learning with Dynamic Clustering and Adaptive Regularization for Robust Infrastructure Inspection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03084)
*Yuhu Feng,Keisuke Maeda,Takahiro Ogawa,Miki Haseyama*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hierarchical federated learning framework for structural health monitoring that addresses dual heterogeneity (macro-structural type divergence and micro-local data skew) via a two-tier strategy: macro-level gradient-based clustering into expert groups without geographic metadata, and micro-level intra-cluster DRAPR (Dynamic Region-Adaptive Proximal Regularization) to compute a Non-IID Intensity Score and adapt proximal penalties, yielding robust, specialized diagnostic models on real-world SHM data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data privacy regulations create data silos in SHM, and dual heterogeneity across structures and datasets hampers standard FL. A hierarchical approach aims to preserve privacy while producing specialized, robust models across diverse infrastructure.

Method: 1) Macro-level: dynamic gradient-based clustering that groups clients into experts based on structural degradation trajectories without requiring prior geographic metadata. 2) Micro-level: within each cluster, DRAPR computes a real-time Non-IID Intensity Score and modulates a proximal penalty based on label skewness and gradient divergence to reduce client drift and prevent forgetting minority damage classes.

Result: Large-scale real-world structural inspection dataset evaluations show that combining macro-clustering with micro-regularization neutralizes dual-level heterogeneity, yielding robust, specialized diagnostic models for complex infrastructure inspection.

Conclusion: Hierarchical FL with macro-level clustering and micro-level DRAPR effectively handles privacy-constrained, heterogeneous SHM data, enabling accurate, specialized diagnostics across diverse infrastructure.

Abstract: The deployment of data-driven computer vision models for structural health monitoring (SHM) is heavily constrained by the data silo dilemma due to stringent privacy and security regulations. While federated learning (FL) offers a privacy-preserving collaborative alternative, its application to nationwide infrastructure networks is severely hindered by the challenge of ``double heterogeneity'': macro-level physical divergence across disparate structural types and micro-level statistical imbalances within local datasets. To overcome this challenge, this paper proposes a novel hierarchical federated learning framework. The framework orchestrates a synergistic two-tier optimization strategy. At the macro-level, a dynamic gradient-based clustering mechanism autonomously aggregates distributed clients into specialized expert groups based on their structural degradation trajectories, circumventing the need for prior geographical metadata. Concurrently, at the micro-level, an intra-cluster Dynamic Region-Adaptive Proximal Regularization (DRAPR) module computes a real-time statistical Non-IID Intensity Score for each client. By adaptively modulating a proximal penalty based on local label skewness and gradient divergence, DRAPR effectively calibrates local updates, mitigates client drift, and prevents the catastrophic forgetting of minority damage classes. Comprehensive evaluations on a large-scale, real-world structural inspection dataset demonstrate that the hierarchical integration of macro-clustering and micro-regularization successfully neutralizes dual-level heterogeneity, yielding highly robust and specialized diagnostic models for complex infrastructure inspection.

</details>


### [27] [Zero-Shot 3D Question Answering via Hierarchical View-to-Token Transportation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03100)
*Dongsheng Wang,Dawei Su,Hui Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: KeyVT introduces a two-level context selection for zero-shot 3D scene understanding with 2D VLMs: (i) view-level scoring that fuses pixel features with camera parameters to pick spatially coherent, task-relevant views, and (ii) token-level pruning via optimal transport to select representative tokens across views, reducing redundancy. It achieves strong gains over tuning-free baselines and is competitive with training-based methods across three benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To maximize task-relevant 3D detail under limited input budgets when using 2D VLMs for zero-shot 3D scene understanding, addressing the loss of informative cues and redundancy across multiple views.

Method: A hierarchical input-context strategy: (1) view-level: combine pixel features with camera parameters and score view importance using semantic content and geometric position to select informative views; (2) token-level: apply Optimal Transport to identify representative tokens, formulating view tokens and key tokens as distributions in embedding space and minimizing OT distance to cover all view features.

Result: The approach yields significant improvements over existing tuning-free methods and achieves performance on par with training-based approaches across three standard benchmarks.

Conclusion: Hierarchical context collection with OT-based token selection enhances zero-shot 3D understanding from 2D VLMs, delivering efficient, robust input representations that bridge tuning-free and supervised training-based performance.

Abstract: Recently, zero-shot 3D scene understanding via 2D Vision-Language Models (VLMs) has gained increasing research interest due to their promising spatial reasoning capabilities. Typically, multiple 2D views are sampled from a 3D point cloud and fed into pre-trained VLMs to answer a given question. This paradigm highlights the critical role of input context quality and raises the challenge of retaining as many task-relevant 3D details as possible under a limited input budget. We propose \texttt{KeyVT}, a hierarchical approach for input context collection at both the view and token levels. Specifically, we combine pixel features with camera parameters and assess view importance based on both semantic content and geometric position, resulting in spatially consistent and task-relevant views. Furthermore, we address redundancy among patches across selected views by identifying representative tokens under the optimal transport (OT) framework, where view tokens and key tokens are formulated as two discrete distributions in the embedding space. These key tokens are expected to cover all view features by minimizing the OT distance. We evaluate our framework on three widely used benchmarks, demonstrating significant improvements over existing tuning-free methods and performance comparable to training-based approaches.

</details>


### [28] [Inverting the Generation Process of Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models: Empirical Evaluation and a Novel Method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03111)
*Yan Zeng,Masanori Suganuma,Takayuki Okatani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hybrid DDIM inversion method improves initial latent recovery; gradient descent for the first step plus a fixed-point method for later steps; introduces a self-interpolation test to evaluate latent interpolation quality; shows superior reconstruction and latent prediction across three datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Invert the DDIM generation process to recover the initial noise (latent) map from a produced image; existing inversion methods struggle to accurately recover the initial latent, limiting editing and analysis.

Method: Estimate the initial latent via direct gradient-descent inversion for the first step, then apply a fixed-point iteration for subsequent steps in the inversion process; introduce a self-interpolation test to assess image quality when interpolating between true and predicted latent maps; evaluate across three datasets.

Result: Significant improvements in predicting the initial latent; superior reconstruction accuracy; better performance on the self-interpolation test; outperforms existing methods across all assessed metrics.

Conclusion: The proposed hybrid inversion approach provides deeper insights into diffusion models, enhances image generation/editing via more accurate latent recovery, and establishes self-interpolation as a valuable evaluation metric for latent inversion.

Abstract: This paper studies the problem of inverting the DDIM image generation process to recover latent variables, particularly the initial noise map, from a generated image. Existing methods often struggle with accuracy in this task. We propose a novel hybrid approach that combines direct inversion via gradient descent for the first step, followed by a fixed-point method for subsequent steps. Empirical evaluations across three datasets demonstrate that our method significantly improves the prediction of initial latent variables while achieving superior reconstruction accuracy. Additionally, we introduce a new evaluation, called the self-interpolation test, which assesses the quality of images generated from interpolated points between the true and predicted latent maps, offering deeper insights into performance. Our results reveal that while existing methods perform reasonably well in reconstruction, they consistently fail to accurately predict the initial latent variables, resulting in poor performance on the self-interpolation test. In contrast, our method outperforms all others across all metrics, providing valuable insights into diffusion models and enhancing their applications in image generation and editing.

</details>


### [29] [FAF-CD: Frequency-Aware Fusion for Change Detection under Imperfect Multimodal Remote Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03114)
*Yufan Wang,Sokratis Makrogiannis,Chandra Kambhamettu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FAF-CD is a frequency-aware, cross-domain change detection framework using a DINOv3-pretrained ConvNeXt encoder and a linear-complexity VMamba decoder, featuring a tri-branch fusion that fuses deformable alignment with Fourier and Haar-wavelet cues via adaptive gating; it shows strong robustness across heterogeneous EO-SAR and optical CD with reduced compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To robustly detect changes under imperfect, heterogeneous observations (asynchrony, cross-sensor shifts, and nuisance variations) common in real-world monitoring, especially for EO-SAR disaster mapping.

Method: Introduce FAF-CD with a rectification-aware tri-branch fusion module. The three branches compare/align changes via deformable spatial alignment, Fourier-domain cues, and Haar-wavelet cues, fused adaptively across scales. Uses a DINOv3-pretrained ConvNeXt encoder and a linear-complexity VMamba-based decoder. Evaluated on BRIGHT (EO-SAR adaptation), LEVIR-CD, and WHU-CD with pseudo-change-aligned stress tests.

Result: On BRIGHT validation, achieved improved clean and perturbed tc-mIoU/tc-mAP over NeXt2Former-CD with heterogeneous EO-SAR adaptation. Generalizes to binary optical CD, achieving 0.924 cF1 on LEVIR-CD and 0.955 cF1 on WHU-CD. Attains best average perturbed cIoU/cF1 on both binary datasets among M-CD and NeXt2Former-CD under stress tests. Reduces compute by ~24 GFLOPs compared to NeXt2Former-CD while maintaining or improving accuracy.

Conclusion: FAF-CD demonstrates robust cross-domain change detection across EO-SAR and optical data, with improved accuracy under nuisance/distortion conditions and lower computational cost, indicating strong generalization and practical efficiency.

Abstract: Remote sensing change detection for real-world monitoring often relies on imperfect heterogeneous observations, where pre- and post-event images may be asynchronous, cross-sensor, or affected by illumination, seasonal, and modality shifts. This setting is especially challenging for EO-SAR disaster mapping, where nuisance variation can resemble structural damage. We propose FAF-CD, a frequency-aware hybrid framework with a DINOv3-pretrained ConvNeXt encoder and a linear-complexity VMamba-based decoder. Its rectification-aware tri-branch fusion module combines deformable spatial alignment with Fourier and Haar-wavelet comparisons, using adaptive gating to aggregate complementary cues across scales. On BRIGHT validation, a matched heterogeneous EO-SAR adaptation improves clean and perturbed tc-mIoU/tc-mAP over NeXt2Former-CD. FAF-CD also generalizes to binary optical CD, achieving 0.924 cF1 on LEVIR-CD and 0.955 cF1 on WHU-CD, and obtains the best average perturbed cIoU/cF1 on both binary datasets among M-CD and NeXt2Former-CD under pseudo-change-aligned stress tests. It further reduces cost by approximately 24 GFLOPs relative to NeXt2Former-CD while maintaining or improving accuracy.

</details>


### [30] [GuidedBridge: Training-freely Improving Bridge Models with Prior Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03119)
*Zehua Chen,Yucheng Yang,Binjie Yuan,Kaiwen Zheng,Jun S. Liu,Jun Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free Prior Guidance (PG) for bridge models in diffusion-based image translation, introducing a weak prior to suppress exploitation and a seen prior with a scaling factor, plus frequency-modulated prior guidance (FMPG) to tailor guidance by frequency. Extends to a cascaded CFG-FMPG for image inpainting. Demonstrated consistent gains across diverse tasks without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge models enable data-to-data generation and can leverage a clean prior to steer outputs. Existing methods create quality differences between denoising steps for guidance, but there is a need to exploit priors more effectively without retraining or heavy computational cost.

Method: 1) Propose PG: use a weak prior unseen during bridge pre-training to hinder prior exploitation; contrast with a seen prior and apply a scaling factor to enhance exploitation. 2) Analyze the mechanism of prior exploitation in the bridge process. 3) Design frequency-modulated prior guidance (FMPG) that adjusts guidance scales across low- and high-frequency bands in alignment with bridge dynamics. 4) For image in-painting, develop a cascaded CFG-FMPG: generate a noisy hidden representation via CFG and then use it as a generative prior with FMPG, preserving inference efficiency. 5) Validate on multiple image translation tasks with pre-trained bridge models.

Result: PG methods consistently improve the performance of pre-trained bridge models across diverse image translation tasks, indicating enhanced utilization of priors without additional training or retraining of models. FMPG provides frequency-aware guidance, and CFG-FMPG extends benefits to image inpainting via a cascaded pipeline.

Conclusion: PG offers a training-free pathway to boost prior exploitation in bridge-based diffusion models. By employing a weak vs. seen prior with scaling and incorporating frequency-aware guidance, the method yields consistent gains across tasks while preserving inference efficiency. The cascaded CFG-FMPG framework further leverages complementary strengths for image inpainting.

Abstract: Guidance methods, such as classifier-free guidance (CFG) and auto-guidance (AG), have advanced noise-to-data generation in diffusion models. Recently, bridge models have introduced a data-to-data generative process that can exploit an instructive clean prior. In this work, inspired by previous methods creating quality difference between denoising results as guidance, we propose a training-free bridge guidance method, termed Prior Guidance (PG). Specifically, we introduce a weak prior, which is unseen during bridge pre-training, hindering prior exploitation and thereby degrading denoising result. Then, we contrast it with the seen prior to highlight and enhance prior exploitation via a scaling factor. Moreover, we analyze the underlying mechanism of prior exploitation in the bridge process and design frequency-modulated prior guidance (FMPG), which tailors the guidance scale to low- and high-frequency bands coherent with bridge generative dynamics. To address prior exploitation in image in-painting, we develop a cascaded framework, CFG-FMPG, which first generates a noisy hidden representation via CFG and then exploits it as a generative prior with FMPG, fulfilling their complementary strengths without compromising inference efficiency. Experiments demonstrate that our PG methods consistently improve pre-trained bridge models across diverse image translation tasks.

</details>


### [31] [KC-3DGS: Kurtosis-Constrained Gaussian Splatting for High-Fidelity View Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03120)
*Vivekjyoti Banerjee,Abhay Yadav,Rama Chellappa,Aniket Roy*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: KC-3DGS enhances 3D Gaussian Splatting with wavelet-domain supervision to preserve high-frequency content and enforce natural image statistics, tackling oversmoothing in sparse views.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pixel-space losses (L1/SSIM) distribute error across frequencies, enabling frequency redistribution that degrades fine detail and structure, especially with limited viewpoints.

Method: Three components: (1) multi-scale wavelet coefficient alignment loss to penalize missing high-frequency detail; (2) supervised kurtosis concentration loss to match heavy-tailed frequency statistics; (3) cross-band covariance penalty to promote frequency specialization. The paper provides theoretical analysis showing pixel-space losses admit wavelet redistribution perturbations and that the joint objective eliminates degenerate solutions; the approach is plug-and-play in 3DGS frameworks.

Result: Consistent perceptual quality improvements across MipNeRF360, Tanks&Temples, MVImgNet, DeepBlending, WRIVA-ULTRRA; 9.48% DreamSim improvement on WRIVA-ULTRRA; PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS gains; in sparse-view (12 training images) PSNR up to +0.5 dB on MipNeRF360; maintains perceptual quality.

Conclusion: Wavelet-domain supervision provides effective regularization for 3DGS, delivering better high-frequency fidelity without sacrificing stability, and can be integrated as a plug-and-play regularizer in existing pipelines.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) enables real-time novel view synthesis by representing scenes as collections of anisotropic Gaussians optimized via differentiable rasterization. However, standard pixel-space losses (L1, SSIM) constrain only aggregate reconstruction error, permitting the optimization to redistribute error across frequency scales. This leads to oversmoothing and structural artifacts, particularly in sparse-view settings where supervision is limited. We propose KC-3DGS, which augments 3DGS training with wavelet-domain supervision based on natural image statistics. Our method combines three components: (1) a multi-scale wavelet coefficient alignment loss that explicitly penalizes missing high-frequency detail, (2) a supervised kurtosis concentration loss that encourages rendered images to match the heavy-tailed frequency statistics of ground-truth images, and (3) a cross-band covariance penalty that promotes frequency specialization. We provide theoretical analysis showing that pixel-space losses admit a family of indistinguishable perturbations under wavelet redistribution, and that our joint objective excludes degenerate solutions. Experiments across MipNeRF360, Tanks&Temples, MVImgNet, DeepBlending, and WRIVA-ULTRRA demonstrate consistent improvements in perceptual quality. On the challenging WRIVA-ULTRRA outdoor dataset, KC-3DGS achieves a 9.48% improvement in DreamSim while also improving PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS. In sparse-view settings with only 12 training images, our method improves PSNR by up to 0.5 dB on MipNeRF360 while maintaining perceptual quality. The approach integrates seamlessly into existing 3DGS pipelines as a plug-and-play regularization strategy.

</details>


### [32] [Disentangling Visual and Factual Correctness in LVLMs' Visualization Literacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03142)
*Soohyun Lee,Jaeyoung Kim,Seokhyeon Park,Sihyeon Lee,Jiwon Song,Bohyoung Kim,Hyunjoo Song,Jinwook Seo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes CVLAT, a counterfactual framework to separate visual reasoning from factual priors in LVLMs, revealing that many models rely on facts rather than genuine visual interpretation; introduces VFRI-based arbitration metrics and human baselines; finds model heterogeneity and asymmetric impact of prompts on visual vs. factual reliance; emphasizes robust evaluation for visual literacy beyond standard benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the conflation of visual interpretation and memorized factual priors in LVLM evaluation by isolating visual correctness and factual correctness to assess true visual reasoning capabilities.

Method: Three experiments (VLAT, reVLAT, CVLAT) across 15 LVLMs; introduce Counterfactual Visualization Literacy Assessment Test (CVLAT) and arbitration metrics; compute Visual-Factual Reliance Index (VFRI); compare with human baseline (N=30); test prompt-based intervention; release benchmark and code.

Result: Standard tests often reflect factual recall rather than visual understanding; CVLAT uncovers a majority visualization-oriented models and minority factual, with several near-zero cases; humans prefer chart-following under conflict; prompt interventions are model-dependent and not universally effective; high chart-reading ability does not predict prompt controllability.

Conclusion: Reliable visual analytics require evaluating both visualization literacy and how models arbitrate between visual evidence and factual priors when they diverge. The framework reveals limitations of current benchmarks and provides a path toward more faithful visual reasoning evaluation.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) show strong visualization interpretation, yet it is unclear whether their responses reflect genuine reasoning over visual evidence or factual priors learned during training. Current evaluations mix these two sources, obscuring when correct visual interpretation is overridden by memorized facts. We present a framework that isolates visual correctness from factual correctness, revealing validity limitations in existing visualization literacy assessments. Across three experiments with 15 state-of-the-art LVLMs: (1) several models reach human-level performance on standard tests (VLAT), but this may reflect factual recall rather than visual understanding, while randomized-data tests (reVLAT) underestimate literacy when correct visual interpretation is superseded by factual priors. (2) Using our Counterfactual Visualization Literacy Assessment Test (CVLAT) with capability-normalized arbitration metrics, we classify models by the sign of their visual-factual reliance index (VFRI), revealing a visualization-oriented majority and a factual knowledge-oriented minority, though several near-zero cases warrant caution. A human baseline (N=30) on the same counterfactual items confirms that people overwhelmingly follow the chart under conflict, providing a human reference point. (3) Prompt-based intervention can shift prioritization, but its effectiveness is highly model-dependent and direction-asymmetric, and high chart-reading capability does not predict prompt-controllability. Overall, high visualization accuracy is not sufficient evidence of faithful visual reasoning: reliable integration into visual analytics requires evaluating not only visualization literacy but also how models arbitrate between visual evidence and factual priors when the two diverge. Benchmark and code: https://github.com/JaeyoungKim-HCIL/CVLAT

</details>


### [33] [$A^2$: Smaller Self-Supervised ViTs Localize Better than Larger Ones](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03148)
*Sreehari Rammohan,Huy Ha,Carl Vondrick*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A^2 decouples localization from representation by using a small attention model to localize foreground objects and a large embedding model to extract rich features from crops around attention peaks; it uses only pretrained components with no group/dataset-specific supervision and shows competitive/robust performance across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve robust visual classification by focusing on foreground localization while retaining rich representations, leveraging the observation that smaller self-supervised ViTs localize better than larger ones.

Method: Identify attention peaks with a small model, crop around these peaks, and embed the crops with a larger pretrained model. No per-dataset attention or backbone training is needed; uses entirely pretrained features and no group labels.

Result: On 5 benchmarks, A^2 is competitive with backbone-matched loss-level methods (e.g., DFR) and outperforms end-to-end attention training under stronger distribution shifts.

Conclusion: Decoupling localization and representation via inverse-scaling insight yields strong robustness and simplicity, suggesting pretrained, decoupled components can achieve competitive performance without targeted supervision or retraining.

Abstract: Robust visual classification often depends on localizing the main foreground objects in an image while ignoring contextual distractors. Surprisingly, we find that the attention maps of smaller self-supervised ViTs localize foreground objects better than those of larger ViTs. However, we still need large ViTs, because they extract richer representations from each patch. To get the best of both worlds, good localization and rich representations, we propose $A^2$, a simple method that leverages this inverse scaling finding by decoupling where to look (a small attention model) from what to extract (a large embedding model): we crop around the attention peaks of a small model and embed the crops with a larger model. $A^2$ uses entirely pretrained features, requires no group labels, and does not require per-dataset attention or backbone training. Across 5 benchmarks, $A^2$ is competitive with backbone-matched loss-level methods like DFR, and outperforms end-to-end attention training under stronger distribution shifts.

</details>


### [34] [NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03159)
*NVIDIA,:,Aarti Basant,Amlan Kar,Despoina Paschalidou,Fangyin Wei,Francesco Ferroni,Guillermo Garcia Cobo,Haithem Turki,Huan Ling,Jaewoo Seo,James Lucas,Jay Zhangjie Wu,Jialiang Wang,Jonathan Lorraine,Jun Gao,Kai He,Katarina Tothova,Kevin Xie,Michał Tyszkiewicz,Qi Wu,Riccardo de Lutio,Ruilong Li,Sanja Fidler,Seung Wook Kim,Tianchang Shen,Tianshi Cao,Tobias Pfaff,William Lew,Xindi Wu,Xuanchi Ren,Yifan Lu,Yuxuan Zhang,Zan Gojcic,Zian Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OmniDreams is a real-time, action-conditioned world model for closed-loop autonomous driving simulation, leveraging Cosmos priors and mid-/post-training to synthesize photorealistic, dynamic driving scenes; it improves policy evaluation and training, with a preliminary WAM post-trained from OmniDreams achieving strong results on NuRec using only 1/5 the parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The safe evaluation of driving policies in long-tail, dynamic scenarios is bottlenecked by simulators that lack generalization and by limited photorealism. A scalable, realistic world model capable of autoregressively generating sensor data conditioned on actions can enable better policy training and assessment.

Method: Mid-/post-training OmniDreams from the Cosmos diffusion model to autoregressively generate action-conditioned, photorealistic videos in real time. It conditions on past frames, current simulator state, and immediate driving actions. Deployed in a closed-loop with Alpamayo 1 policy and AlpaSim orchestrator; also introduces a WAM post-trained from OmniDreams.

Result: OmniDreams can synthesize complex phenomena (e.g., extreme weather, unpredictable dynamic agents) beyond traditional simulators. It operates in real time within closed-loop driving, and a WAM post-trained from OmniDreams achieves strong performance on the NuRec Autonomous Vehicles dataset, surpassing the VLA-based Alpamayo 1.5 with about one-fifth the parameters.

Conclusion: A real-time, world-model backbone like OmniDreams promises scalable, comprehensive policy training and evaluation for autonomous driving, with potential for broad applicability as a foundation for future policy architectures.

Abstract: As autonomous vehicle capabilities advance, the safe evaluation of driving policies in long-tail scenarios remains a critical bottleneck. In closed-loop simulation, the driving policy model actively interacts with the environment, where its actions dynamically update the simulator state and directly influence the next set of generated sensor observations. While recent reconstruction-based neural simulators offer photorealism, they are fundamentally constrained by their initial captured data and struggle to generalize to highly dynamic or novel scenes. To overcome these limitations, we introduce OmniDreams, a foundation generative world model mid- and post-trained from the Cosmos diffusion model to autoregressively generate action-conditioned videos in real time. By leveraging the rich visual priors of Cosmos and mid- and post-training on 21k hours of driving scenarios, OmniDreams synthesizes complex, unobserved phenomena that are hard for traditional simulators to capture, such as extreme weather and unpredictable dynamic agent behaviors. Crucially, it autoregressively conditions its photorealistic sensor generation on past frames, the current simulator state, and immediate driving actions. Deployed in a closed-loop system with the Alpamayo 1 policy model and AlpaSim orchestrator, OmniDreams acts as a highly responsive, reactive environment, providing a scalable and comprehensive solution for training and evaluating next-generation autonomous driving policies. We additionally show preliminary results indicating that a world-action model (WAM) post-trained from OmniDreams achieves strong performance on the Physical AI Autonomous Vehicles NuRec dataset, surpassing the VLA-based Alpamayo 1.5 research policy model while using only 1/5 the total parameters. These results highlight the potential for a real-time world model like OmniDreams to also serve as a backbone for policy architectures.

</details>


### [35] [SRENet: Spectral Re-Entry Network for Point Cloud Action Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03160)
*Qiuxia Wu,Jiarui Lan,Wenxiong Kang,Zhiyong Wang,Kun Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SRENet introduces spectral decomposition for point-cloud action recognition, using SDeBlock and SReBlock to model low- and high-frequency motion components and a curriculum- and contrastive-based spectral-learning strategy, achieving state-of-the-art results on MSR-Action3D, NTU-RGBD, and NTU-RGBD120.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Point-cloud sequences present irregular structure and temporal inconsistency, making spatio-temporal representation learning challenging. A frequency-domain approach can disentangle global motion context from fine-grained temporal dynamics to improve action recognition.

Method: SRENet employs a Spectral Decomposition Block (SDeBlock) that uses wavelet-based analysis along temporal and spatial axes to separate low- and high-frequency features with frequency-specific attention. A Spectral Re-entry Block (SReBlock) performs secondary temporal decomposition to recover residual dynamics and re-align distorted temporal frequencies. A spectral-aware learning strategy uses contrastive loss and a curriculum schedule that shifts focus from low- to high-frequency spaces.

Result: Extensive experiments on MSR-Action3D, NTU-RGBD, and NTU-RGBD120 demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, validating the effectiveness of frequency modeling for 3D action recognition from point clouds.

Conclusion: Frequency-aware spectral modeling in point-cloud action recognition yields more discriminative spatio-temporal representations, enabling superior performance and offering a principled approach to capture both global motion and fine-grained temporal dynamics; potential applicability to other 3D sequence tasks.

Abstract: Recognizing human actions from point cloud sequences is critical for 3D perception driven applications such as autonomous driving and human-computer interaction. However, the irregular structure and temporal inconsistency of point clouds pose unique challenges for spatio-temporal representation learning, especially in capturing both global motion context and fine-grained temporal dynamics. We propose SRENet, a spectral-aware framework designed to explicitly learn both global context and fine-grained temporal dynamics of motion from a frequency perspective for action recognition. SRENet introduces a Spectral Decomposition Block (SDeBlock) that performs wavelet-based analysis along temporal and spatial axes, disentangling features into low- and high-frequency components with frequency-specific attention. To recover residual dynamics and re-align temporal frequency structures distorted during semantic fusion, a Spectral Re-entry Block (SReBlock) performs secondary temporal decomposition. Furthermore, a spectral-aware learning strategy is devised to enhance discriminability in both frequency subspaces via contrastive loss and a curriculum schedule that gradually shifts focus from low- to high-frequency spaces in line with coarse to detailed motion patterns. Extensive experiments on MSR-Action3D, NTU-RGBD and NTU-RGBD120 demonstrate that SRENet achieves state-of-the-art performance, validating the effectiveness of frequency modeling in point cloud-based action understanding.

</details>


### [36] [JAVEDIT: Joint Audio-Visual Instruction-Guided Video Editing with Agentic Data Curation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03168)
*Yinan Chen,Chuming Lin,Zhennan Chen,Yuxiang Zeng,Junwei Zhu,Yali Bi,Xijie Huang,Chengming Xu,Donghao Luo,Zhucun Xue,Xiaobin Hu,Chengjie Wang,Yong Liu,Jiangning Zhang,Shuicheng Yan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces JAVEdit-100k dataset, JAVEditBench benchmark, and JAVEdit baseline for instruction-guided joint audio-visual editing; addresses dataset/benchmark gap; claims strong performance across metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In instruction-based video editing, joint audio-visual editing lacks standardized data and evaluation; need large-scale, high-quality data and benchmarks to enable progress and fair comparisons.

Method: Construct five-category, ~100k editing triplets via four generation pipelines with agent-in-the-loop quality control; focus on human-centric videos; create JAVEditBench with curated sources and human-aligned instructions; propose JAVEdit baseline model for joint editing.

Result: JAVEdit outperforms all baselines on five of six evaluation metrics.

Conclusion: Dataset, benchmark, and baseline establish resources and evaluation protocol for joint audio-visual editing and pave the way for future research; further work may expand categories and metrics.

Abstract: While instruction-based video editing has seen significant progress, joint audio-visual editing remains constrained by the absence of dedicated datasets and benchmarks. To bridge this gap, we present JAVEdit-100k, the first large-scale, high-quality dataset tailored for instruction-guided joint audio-visual editing. Focusing on human-centric videos, JAVEdit-100k comprises approximately 100K editing triplets spanning five distinct categories, including subject editing and speech editing. This dataset is rigorously constructed via four meticulously designed generation pipelines, seamlessly paired with an agent-in-the-loop quality control mechanism. Furthermore, to address the lack of standardized evaluation within the field, we introduce JAVEditBench, a comprehensive benchmark featuring curated source videos and human-aligned instructions across all editing categories. Finally, we propose JAVEdit, a pioneering baseline model for instruction-guided joint audio-visual editing. Experiments show that \model\ outperforms all baselines on five of six evaluation metrics.

</details>


### [37] [Ask When It Pays: Cost-Aware Open-Ended Interaction for Instance Goal Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03175)
*Xunyi Zhao,Sihao Lin,Gengze Zhou,Zerui Li,Shijie Li,Wei Tao,Jiajun Liu,Qi Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Reframes interactive instance-goal navigation as a cost-sensitive uncertainty-reduction problem, introducing a question-typing taxonomy, data-driven weights, a Weighted Success Rate benchmark, and a zero-shot MLLM navigator that queries only when expected information gain justifies interaction cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ambiguity in IGN due to underspecified natural-language descriptions; existing interactive methods waste effort by pursuing high-information questions rather than efficiently resolving underlying ambiguity; need cost-aware interaction and a benchmark to study efficiency.

Method: Apply information-gain analysis on navigation corpora to identify cues that reduce navigation uncertainty; derive a compact set of question types with data-derived costs/weights; construct a benchmark for interaction behavior using a Weighted Success Rate metric; propose a zero-shot MLLM-based navigator that selectively queries at each step based on expected uncertainty reduction vs. interaction cost.

Result: Proposes a new benchmark and Weighted Success Rate metric for evaluating cost-sensitive interaction in IGN; introduces a zero-shot MLLM navigator that queries selectively; quantitative results are not provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: A cost-sensitive interaction framework for IGN is proposed, enabling more efficient decision making by prioritizing low-cost, high-information questions; accompanied by a practical benchmark and a scalable zero-shot navigator.

Abstract: Instance Goal Navigation (IGN) requires an embodied agent to find a specific object instance among distractors from an underspecified natural-language description. Such ambiguity often cannot be resolved from perception and language alone, making interaction with an oracle a natural mechanism for disambiguation. Prior interactive methods allow oracle queries but treat lightweight clarification and route-level guidance alike, letting agents boost success rate through repeated high-information questions rather than by resolving the underlying ambiguity efficiently. We recast interactive IGN as a cost-sensitive uncertainty-reduction problem, where the agent should ask the question whose answer provides the largest reduction in navigation uncertainty relative to its penalty. To this end, we apply an information-gain analysis on existing navigation corpora to identify which cues reduce navigation uncertainty, yielding a compact set of question types and data-derived weights.However, existing interactive navigation benchmarks do not model the cost of different question types or evaluate how efficiently agents use interaction, making them unsuitable for studying cost-sensitive interaction. Based on this taxonomy, we construct a benchmark for diagnosing interaction behavior and efficiency, together with a Weighted Success Rate metric that penalizes each query by its derived cost. We further propose a zero-shot MLLM navigator that selectively queries at each decision step only when the expected uncertainty reduction justifies the interaction cost.

</details>


### [38] [GLINT: Sparsely Gated Vision-Language Alignment for Fine-Grained Radiology Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03180)
*Jonggwon Park,Seongeun Lee,Junhyun Park,Hannah Yun,Hyunwoong Kim,Sohyun Jeong,Hyewon Kang,Byungmu Yoon,Kyoyun Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GLINT introduces sparsely gated alignment and dense feature regularization to enable sparse, query-specific localization in radiology VLMs, achieving zero-shot classification, grounding, and segmentation (including 3D CT) and outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In image-report paired radiology data, supervision is global; findings are small regions; existing methods spread attention everywhere. There is a need for sparse, query-driven alignment and stable patch-feature representations.

Method: Two components: Sparsely Gated Alignment with a gate over a separate embedding space to activate patches relevant to textual queries; Dense Feature Regularization aligning encoder features to a frozen SSL teacher (DINOv3 for 2D CXR, V-JEPA 2.1 for 3D CT). Applied to 2D CXR and 3D CT.

Result: GLINT enables zero-shot classification, grounding, and segmentation from free text; first zero-shot segmentation on 3D CT without masks; strongest gains in grounding/segmentation; outperforms SSL encoders and medical VLMs on classification, report generation, and segmentation.

Conclusion: Sparse, query-specific localization via gated alignment plus feature regularization yields improved radiology VLM performance, enabling zero-shot localization tasks and robust segmentation without fine-grained mask supervision.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) for radiology have emerged as a scalable paradigm by leveraging image-report pairs naturally produced in clinical workflows. However, this pairing reveals a mismatch in scale: each finding occupies only a small region of the image, yet supervision is provided only at the global image-report level. This poses a central challenge: prior approaches spread weight densely across all patches rather than concentrating on the sparse subset relevant to a given query. To address this, we present GLINT (Gated Language-Image alignmeNT), a framework that explicitly models this sparse correspondence. On the alignment side, we introduce Sparsely Gated Alignment, a novel architecture in which a sigmoid gate over a separate gate embedding space activates only the patches relevant to each textual query, enforcing explicit sparsity. On the representation side, we add Dense Feature Regularization, which anchors the trainable encoder's intermediate features to a frozen self-supervised learning (SSL) teacher, preserving the fine-grained patch features that the gate relies on. The same recipe applies to both 2D chest X-ray (CXR) and 3D chest computed tomography (CT), built with DINOv3 and V-JEPA 2.1, respectively. GLINT enables zero-shot classification, grounding, and segmentation from free-text queries, and to our knowledge is the first to demonstrate zero-shot segmentation on 3D CT volumes without mask supervision. Notably, the most pronounced gains arise on zero-shot grounding and segmentation, where sparse, query-specific localization is required, consistent with our design intent. In downstream evaluation, GLINT outperforms both SSL encoders and medical VLMs on classification, report generation, and segmentation.

</details>


### [39] [Reinforcement Learning from Cross-domain Videos with Video Prediction Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03201)
*Zhao Yang,Xinrui Zu,Jacob E. Kooi,Thomas Delliaux,He Liu,Shujian Yu,Kevin Sebastian Luck,Vincent François-Lavet*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: XIPER introduces a cross-domain video prediction reward model that enables reinforcement learning from expert videos across visually distinct domains by mapping observations into the expert domain and using the prediction likelihood as a reward signal; demonstrated improvements on DMC Color and Body suites and a sim-to-real transfer dataset, with resources available online.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning from expert videos is challenging when there is no explicit reward and when there are domain gaps between the agent and expert (e.g., color, morphology, sim-to-real). A reward signal robust to visual and embodiment differences is needed to leverage expert demonstrations across domains.

Method: Train a cross-domain video predictor that maps agent observations into the expert domain. Use the likelihood of expert-domain video predictions as a reward signal to guide reinforcement learning, enabling cross-domain imitation without explicit state alignment.

Result: XIPER outperforms baselines on the DMC Color Suite (8 tasks) and DMC Body Suite (3 tasks) despite domain gaps in color and morphology. In a sim-to-real transfer setting, it provides meaningful reward signals for real-robot observations using only simulated expert videos.

Conclusion: XIPER provides a viable cross-domain reward mechanism for RL from expert videos, effectively bridging appearance and embodiment gaps. It enables learning from visually different experts and supports real-to-sim and sim-to-real transfer scenarios; code and data are available on the project webpage.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from expert videos across visually distinct domains is challenging due to the absence of reward signals and the presence of domain gaps. We introduce XIPER (Cross-domain Video Prediction Reward), a reward model for learning from expert videos collected in a visually different domain, where the agent's appearance differs due to factors such as color, morphology, or the sim-to-real gap. More specifically, XIPER trains a cross-domain video prediction model that maps agent observations into the expert domain and uses the prediction likelihood as a reward signal. Experiments on the DMC Color Suite (8 tasks) and DMC Body Suite (3 tasks) show that XIPER consistently outperforms baselines despite domain gaps such as differences in agent color and morphology. We further analyze XIPER on a sim-to-real transfer dataset, demonstrating that it produces meaningful reward signals for real-robot observations given only simulated expert videos. Code, pretrained models, datasets and video demonstrations can be found on our project webpage: https://sites.google.com/view/xiper

</details>


### [40] [Follow-Your-Preference++: Rethinking Preference Alignment for Image Inpainting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03216)
*Junkun Yuan,Yutao Shen,Toru Aonishi,Hideki Nakayama,Yue Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A concise synthesis of the paper: studying preference alignment for image inpainting through direct preference optimization across many reward models, revealing biases and the mitigating effect of ensembling, with transfer to object removal and strong performance without architectural/dataset changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question the core challenges of preference-based alignment in image inpainting, assess the reliability of reward-model signals, and understand robustness and biases under scaling, rather than proposing a new architecture.

Method: Direct preference optimization setup using nine reward models, two benchmarks, and two baseline inpainting models with differing architectures/generative mechanisms; construct preference data from reward signals; analyze biases (brightness, composition, color); propose and evaluate a simple ensemble of reward models; test transfer to object removal task; conduct calibration analyses; release code at GitHub.

Result: Most reward models provide valid signals for constructing preference data, but some are unreliable evaluators. Preference data trends persist under candidate and sample scaling. Reward models exhibit biases in brightness, composition, color, which can cause reward hacking; an ensemble mitigates these biases and yields robust, generalizable performance. Preference alignment transfers to object removal; calibrated ensemble further reduces hacking and improves robustness. Without changing architectures or extra data, the approach achieves strong performance on standard metrics, large vision-language model evaluations, and human assessments. Code available at the provided URL.

Conclusion: Ensembling reward-model signals within direct preference optimization improves robustness to biases and hacking, with transferability to related tasks like object removal. The results suggest preference alignment can be made robust without architectural/dataset changes, aided by calibration and multi-model ensembles.

Abstract: We study preference alignment for image inpainting. Rather than proposing yet another method, we revisit the problem from first principles and reassess its core challenges. We adopt the widely used direct preference optimization framework and construct preference training data with publicly available reward models. Our empirical study spans nine reward models, two benchmarks, and two baseline inpainting models that differ in architecture and generative mechanism. Our main findings are: (1) Most reward models provide valid signals for preference data construction, although some are unreliable as evaluators. (2) Across models and benchmarks, preference data exhibits consistent trends under both candidate and sample scaling. (3) Reward models display pronounced biases--particularly in brightness, composition, and color scheme--that make them prone to inducing reward hacking. (4) A simple ensemble of reward models mitigates such biases and yields robust, generalizable performance. {\color{rebuttal_blue}(5) Preference alignment is transferable to the object removal task, where the goal shifts from open-ended creative generation to coherent background completion. (6) Further analysis reveals that a calibrated ensemble method further mitigates hacking and improves robustness.} Without modifying model architectures or introducing additional datasets, our models substantially outperform prior state-of-the-art models on standard metrics, large vision-language model evaluations, and human assessments. Our code is available at: https://github.com/shenytzzz/Follow-Your-Preference.

</details>


### [41] [MemoGen: Can Past Experience Improve Future Text-to-Image Generation?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03243)
*Wenshuo Chen,Kuimou Yu,Bowen Tian,Jianfei Song,Shaofeng Liang,Haozhe Jia,Kan Cheng,Haosen Li,Kaishen Yuan,Lei Wang,Jiemin Wu,Songning Lai,Yutao Yue*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MemoGen is a training-free framework that augments an existing text-to-image generator with an agentic evolution layer and an explicit experience memory. It learns from its own generations across evolution rounds, retrieving past experiences to refine future outputs, without updating the generator parameters. After two rounds on Qwen-Image, it surpasses strong baselines on WISE and Mind-Bench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-image models struggle with implicit visual constraints, relational reasoning, and external knowledge. Existing retrieval-augmented or agentic systems treat each generation as an isolated episode and fail to systematically preserve past successes and failures for future use, limiting continual improvement without retraining.

Method: MemoGen explicitly identifies visual requirements, retrieves external evidence and references when needed, converts them into executable generation constraints, evaluates outputs, and stores rich experience memory (task understanding, reference choices, visual feedback, success strategies, failure lessons). Across evolution rounds, it retrieves relevant past experiences to guide future generations, selectively repairs failed cases while preserving successes, all without updating the underlying generator parameters.

Result: Empirical evaluation on knowledge-intensive and reasoning benchmarks shows MemoGen's effectiveness: after two evolution rounds on the open-source Qwen-Image backbone, it outperforms proprietary systems (Nano Banana Pro, GPT-Image-1) on WISE and Mind-Bench tasks, validating experience memory as a strong continual learning signal for reliable text-to-image generation.

Conclusion: Explicit, test-time experience memory enables self-evolution of text-to-image systems without retraining, yielding improved reliability and performance across challenging tasks.

Abstract: Modern text-to-image models have achieved strong visual synthesis, yet remain unreliable when prompts require implicit visual constraints, relational reasoning, or external knowledge. Existing retrieval-augmented and agentic generation methods mitigate this issue by acquiring external knowledge, references, or refined prompts for the current request, yet they typically treat each generation as an isolated episode and do not systematically preserve past successes or failures for future use. In this work, we ask whether a text-to-image system can continually improve from its own generation experience without updating the underlying generator. We propose MemoGen, a training-free framework that augments existing image generators with an agentic evolution layer. For each task, MemoGen explicitly infers visual requirements, retrieves external evidence and references when necessary, translates them into executable generation constraints, evaluates the generated result, and stores task understanding, reference choices, visual feedback, successful strategies, and failure lessons as reusable experience memory. Across evolution rounds, the agent retrieves relevant experience to improve similar future generations, selectively repairing previously failed cases while preserving successful ones, thereby enabling test-time self-evolution without parameter updates. Extensive experiments on knowledge-intensive and reasoning-oriented benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of this paradigm: after only two evolution rounds, MemoGen built upon the open-source Qwen-Image backbone surpasses strong proprietary systems such as Nano Banana Pro and GPT-Image-1 on WISE and Mind-Bench, showing that explicit experience memory can serve as a powerful continual learning signal for reliable text-to-image generation.

</details>


### [42] [MariData: One-Step Unpaired Image Translation for Maritime Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03246)
*Santeri Henriksson,Mehdi Asadi,Amin Majd,Juha Kalliovaara*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes CycleGAN-turbo with zero-convolution skip connections to bypass VAE bottlenecks in unpaired image translation for maritime data; aims to preserve small object details during Day-to-Foggy, Day-to-Sunset, and Day-to-Night translations; uses a 7,000-image maritime dataset; reports improved structural retention, with Night domain showing semantic hallucination due to data imbalance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data scarcity for robust perception in Maritime Autonomous Surface Ships (MASS) under adverse weather and low-light; unpaired image-to-image translation as a data augmentation solution; conventional generative models struggle to preserve fine details due to latent compression. 

Method: CycleGAN-turbo, a one-step unpaired translation framework augmented with zero-convolution skip connections that bypass the Variational Autoencoder (VAE) bottleneck to preserve small-scale details like distant vessels and sea marks during translation.

Result: Qualitative evaluations and variable-strength inference indicate realistic atmospheric synthesis with maintained semantic structure; Day-to-Foggy and Day-to-Sunset show strong structural retention; Day-to-Night reveals semantic hallucination risks (e.g., artificial coastal lights) due to unbalanced training distributions.

Conclusion: Presents an efficient, structure-aware data synthesis pipeline to mitigate data scarcity in autonomous maritime navigation, while noting domain-specific challenges (notably Night domain) and the need for balanced datasets or targeted training to mitigate hallucinations.

Abstract: The development on robust perception systems for Maritime Autonomous Surface Ships (MASS) is heavily constrained by the scarcity of diverse training data, particularly for adverse weather and low-light conditions. Because collecting paired images in dynamic maritime environments is physically impossible, synthetic data generation via unpaired image-to-image translation offers a critical solution. However, existing generative models suffer from failing to preserve the fine structural details of small navigational objects due to latent compression bottlenecks. In this paper, we introduce a framework for generating synthetic maritime data using CycleGAN-turbo, a one-step unpaired translation architecture. By incorporating zero-convolution skip connections to bypass the Variational Autoencoder (VAE) bottleneck, our approach explicitly preserves small object details (e.g., distant vessels and sea marks) during translation. We compiled a dataset of 7,000 maritime images to train and evaluate models for Day-to-Foggy, Day-to-Sunset, and Day-to-Night domain translations. Qualitative evaluations and variable-strength inference studies demonstrate that our method effectively synthesizes realistic atmospheric conditions while maintaining the underlying semantic structure of the scene. The Day-to-Foggy and Day-to-Sunset models exhibit great structural retention, whereas the Day-to-Night model highlights the challenge of semantic hallucination, such as generating artificial coastal lights, induced by unbalanced training distributions. Ultimately, this work establishes an efficient, structure-aware data synthesis pipeline that directly addresses the data scarcity bottleneck in autonomous maritime navigation.

</details>


### [43] [FreeStreamGS: Online Feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting from Unposed Streaming Inputs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03254)
*Ruiyang Chen,Feiran Li,Chu Zhou,Zonglin Li,Zhanyu Ma,Heng Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces FreeStreamGS, an online feed-forward framework for robust high-quality NVS from streaming unposed images, achieving performance close to offline methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online NVS from streaming, unposed inputs suffers from cumulative camera intrinsic biases, scale jitter, and pose-depth drift, causing artifacts; existing online geometry approaches fail to meet NVS constraints.

Method: Two key components: (1) Decoupled Intrinsic Recovery Head to remove intrinsic bias and prevent scene scale jitter over long streams; (2) Dynamic Point Refinement Offset to relax rigid unprojection, correcting coupled pose-depth drift; integrated into an online feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting pipeline.

Result: Extensive experiments show rendering quality competitive with state-of-the-art offline feed-forward 3DGS methods, despite not having access to future frames.

Conclusion: FreeStreamGS enables robust online NVS from streaming inputs, mitigating intrinsic/scale and pose-depth drift issues and narrowing the gap to offline 3DGS methods.

Abstract: Feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) allows efficient and high-fidelity novel view synthesis (NVS) from an offline recorded image sequence. However, achieving online NVS from streaming and unposed image inputs remains challenging. Although online feed-forward geometric estimation methods have been proposed for streaming depth and point cloud recovery, they cannot be adapted to NVS due to severe rendering artifacts. This is because NVS demands stricter multi-view consistency in Gaussian scales and pose-geometry alignment; even minor deviations would accumulate over time and visibly degrade rendering quality. To this end, we propose FreeStreamGS, a robust online feed-forward framework for efficient and high-quality NVS. We introduce two key mechanisms: a Decoupled Intrinsic Recovery Head that removes cumulative camera intrinsic bias and prevents scene scale jitter during long-term streaming, and a Dynamic Point Refinement Offset strategy that relaxes rigid unprojection to correct coupled pose-depth drift. Extensive experiments show that FreeStreamGS achieves rendering quality competitive with state-of-the-art offline feed-forward 3DGS methods, despite operating without access to future frames.

</details>


### [44] [PaddleOCR-VL-1.6: Expanding the Frontier of Document Parsing with Under-Optimized Region Refinement and Progressive Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03264)
*Zelun Zhang,Hongen Liu,Suyin Liang,Yubo Zhang,Yiqing Xiang,Jiaxuan Liu,Ting Sun,Manhui Lin,Yue Zhang,Changda Zhou,Tingquan Gao,Cheng Cui,Yi Liu,Dianhai Yu,Yanjun Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PaddleOCR-VL-1.6 enhances PaddleOCR-VL-1.5 with region-aware data optimization and staged post-training, achieving 96.33% on OmniDocBench v1.6 and competitive with top VLMs while avoiding indiscriminate data expansion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Even with a strong 0.9B baseline, errors cluster in under-optimized regions due to data sparsity and unreliable supervision; targeted improvements and robust supervision signals are needed without bloating the training data.

Method: 1) Region-aware data optimization: identify weak regions from the prior model, apply targeted enhancement to those regions and improve supervision signals. 2) Progressive post-training: data-curation and reinforcement learning to guide staged optimization; provides a practical post-training recipe for the PaddleOCR-VL series.

Result: State-of-the-art score 96.33% on OmniDocBench v1.6; strong competitiveness against top-tier VLMs; provides a practical post-training recipe.

Conclusion: The approach shows that targeted region-focused augmentation plus staged RL-based post-training can yield substantial gains for document-grounded VLMs, offering a scalable path beyond naive data expansion.

Abstract: We introduce PaddleOCR-VL-1.6, an upgraded compact document parsing model built upon PaddleOCR-VL-1.5. Although PaddleOCR-VL-1.5 establishes a strong 0.9B baseline, its remaining errors concentrate in under-optimized regions where model behavior is unstable, data coverage is sparse, or supervision is unreliable. Rather than expanding the training corpus indiscriminately, PaddleOCR-VL-1.6 introduces a region-aware data optimization framework that identifies weak regions from the previous model, applies targeted enhancement to these regions, and improves the reliability of supervision signals. It further adopts a progressive post-training recipe based on curated data selection and reinforcement learning, pushing model performance to a higher level through staged optimization. PaddleOCR-VL-1.6 achieves a new state-of-the-art score of 96.33% on OmniDocBench v1.6, demonstrates strong competitiveness against top-tier VLMs, and provides a practical post-training recipe for the PaddleOCR-VL series.

</details>


### [45] [VistaHop: Benchmarking Multi-hop Visual Reasoning for Visual DeepSearch](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03273)
*Hang He,Chuhuai Yue,Chengqi Dong,Chengcheng Wan,Ting Su,Haiying Sun,Jiajun Chai,Xiaohan Wang,Guojun Yin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VistaHop is a new benchmark and VistaArena unified evaluation environment for vision-centric search and multi-hop visual reasoning, revealing significant gaps in current multimodal reasoning models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations of existing benchmarks that emphasize single-step visual understanding and static VQA, by evaluating iterative image inspection, visual-grounding fidelity, evidence revisiting, long-chain reasoning, and multi-anchor fusion.

Method: Create VistaHop with 300 high-resolution images, 25 visual search scenarios, 350 multi-hop QA tasks; provide VistaArena environment with tool-enabled reasoning including text search, image search, image cropping, and evidence-based validation; evaluate seven state-of-the-art MLRMs; report Pass@1 metrics.

Result: Current models perform poorly; best model SenseNova-MARS-32B achieves 24.31% Pass@1, indicating substantial room for improvement in grounding, evidence revisiting, and multi-anchor reasoning.

Conclusion: VistaHop exposes persistent challenges in Visual DeepSearch; need stronger training methods and benchmarks; future work should enhance visual grounding, chain-of-thought reasoning, and integration of multiple information sources.

Abstract: Visual DeepSearch requires multimodal large reasoning model (MLRM) agents to answer complex visual queries by repeatedly inspecting image regions, grounding intermediate reasoning in visual evidence, and connecting fine-grained clues across long reasoning chains. However, existing benchmarks mainly focus on single-step visual understanding or static image-question answering, offering limited evaluation of iterative image inspection, visual-anchor grounding, and multi-hop evidence integration. In this work, we introduce VistaHop, a benchmark for evaluating vision-centric search and multi-hop visual reasoning in Visual DeepSearch. VistaHop contains 300 high-resolution images, 25 visual search scenarios, and 350 multi-hop QA tasks that require models to follow evidence chains from visual anchors or fuse information across multiple image-grounded reasoning paths. We further develop VistaArena, a unified evaluation environment that supports tool-augmented reasoning with text search, image search, image cropping, and evidence-based answer validation. Experiments on seven representative MLRMs show that current models remain far from solving VistaHop: the best model, SenseNova-MARS-32B, achieves only 24.31% Pass@1. These results reveal persistent limitations in visual grounding, evidence revisiting, long-chain reasoning, and multi-anchor information fusion, highlighting the need for stronger benchmarks and training methods for Visual DeepSearch.

</details>


### [46] [BA-T: An Iterative Transformer for Two-View Bundle Adjustment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03287)
*Ganlin Zhang,Weirong Chen,Daniel Cremers,Xi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BA-T is an iterative Transformer that mimics bundle adjustment to refine 3D pose and geometry through structured, BA-style updates in latent space, achieving high efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current feed-forward, cross-view attention-based 3D reconstruction models rely on deep decoder stacks and lack a geometry-refinement mechanism, leading to poor multi-view consistency.

Method: Introduce BA-T: an iterative Transformer that implements BA-like updates as repeatable layers operating on latent tokens, refining predictions via latent residuals with a lightweight layer, avoiding deep attention stacks.

Result: BA-T progressively improves pose and reconstruction accuracy across iterations, achieves stronger cross-view consistency than conventional decoders, and matches or surpasses much larger models while using only ~16% of their decoder parameters.

Conclusion: BA-T offers a compact, efficient, and structurally principled alternative to depth-heavy attention for accurate 3D reconstruction, enabling effective performance in a lightweight architecture; code will be released.

Abstract: Feed-forward models for 3D reconstruction have achieved strong performance using deep cross-view attention to exchange information across images. However, these approaches often depend on heavy decoder stacks and lack a structured mechanism for geometry refinement, resulting in poor multi-view consistency. We address this by drawing inspiration from classical bundle adjustment (BA), which can be viewed as an iterative information propagation process between poses and local geometry. Inspired by BA, we propose BA-T, an iterative Transformer that implements BA-style structured updates as a repeatable layer in implicit token space. Instead of relying on deep attention stacks, BA-T refines predictions based on latent residual by a single lightweight layer. Experiments demonstrate that BA-T progressively improves pose and reconstruction accuracy across iterations, achieves stronger cross-view consistency than conventional decoders, and matches or surpasses substantially larger models while using only 16% of their decoder parameters. BA-T provides a compact, efficient, and structural alternative to depth-heavy attention, enabling accurate 3D reconstruction within a lightweight architecture. The code will be made publicly at https://github.com/zhangganlin/BA-T.

</details>


### [47] [TASE: Truncation-Aware Semantic Embeddings for 3D Scene Understanding and Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03314)
*Tim-Felix Faasch,Jochen Kall,Lucas Nunes,Jens Behley,Cyrill Stachniss*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TASE enables flexible, text-driven editing of high-fidelity 3D scenes by projecting pretrained 2D semantic features into a truncation-aware embedding space, with a channel-controlled abstraction mechanism, improved multi-view consistency via a scale- and translation-equivariance loss, and a finetuning stage for the editing diffusion model to handle geometric edits; it achieves strong performance, especially for large geometry changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-fidelity 3D semantic scene representations are essential for robotics, autonomous driving, and simulation, and editable representations are needed to adapt scenes to target scenarios. Existing methods offer limited controllable editing.

Method: Project pretrained 2D semantic features into a truncation-aware embedding space where progressive channel reduction yields abstract semantics while preserving detail with more channels. Improve multi-view consistency using a scale- and translation-equivariance loss. Enable text-driven edits with explicit control over adherence to content. Finetune the editing diffusion model to mitigate artifacts from geometric changes.

Result: Experimental results show competitive performance in 3D scene editing, with substantial improvements over prior methods for edits involving large geometric modifications.

Conclusion: The proposed truncation-aware embedding and editing-finetuning approach deliver controllable, robust 3D scene editing with better content preservation and cross-view consistency, particularly excelling in substantial geometric changes.

Abstract: High-fidelity semantic 3D scene representations are crucial for numerous applications, including robotics, autonomous driving, and simulation. Beyond this, the ability to edit such representations enables developers to adapt these applications more easily to specific target scenarios. Current approaches provide limited support for controllable editing. We introduce TASE, a method that projects pretrained 2D semantic features into a truncation-aware embedding space to enable flexible 3D scene editing. Our method explicitly optimizes a feature space in which progressively reducing feature channels yields increasingly abstract semantic representations, while retaining more channels preserves fine-grained detail. Additionally, we improve multi-view consistency of the features using a scale- and translation-equivariance loss. The resulting truncation-aware embedding space enables text-driven edits to 3D scenes, providing explicit control over how strongly edits adhere to the original scene content and allowing more substantial modifications than prior methods. Moreover, we propose a finetuning stage for the editing diffusion model to mitigate artifacts caused by geometric changes. Experimental results demonstrate competitive performance in 3D scene editing, substantially outperforming prior methods on edits involving large geometric modifications.

</details>


### [48] [Cross-Modality Feature Fusion Based on Structured State Space Duality for Multimodal Image Registration Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03341)
*Zhikang Li,Yan Wu,Xin Hu,Yi Dai,Ming Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RegNetMamba-2 introduces an SSD-based cross-modality, multi-scale coarse-to-fine registration framework (CMI and MSF) for multi-modal image alignment, achieving strong performance and efficiency on VIS-SAR, VIS-IR, and VIS-NIR datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The task of multi-modal image registration is challenged by extracting shared structural information across modalities. SSD provides global structural feature extraction with improved efficiency, motivating its integration into a coarse-to-fine registration pipeline to enhance cross-modality feature fusion and matching.

Method: Apply Structured State Space Duality (SSD) at three scales for multi-modal feature extraction, with foreground edge emphasis via an SSD-based feature scaling function. Introduce a cross-modality feature fusion architecture consisting of: (1) Cross-Modality Interaction (CMI) module for cross-form SRC feature extraction per scale, and (2) Multi-Scale Fusion (MSF) module for progressive feature fusion across scales. Use coarse-to-fine strategy, combining 1/8 scale from CMI and 1/2 scale from MSF to compute pixel-wise matching probabilities and establish correspondences.

Result: Extensive experiments show RegNetMamba-2 outperforms state-of-the-art deep learning methods in both performance and efficiency for multi-modal image registration on VIS-SAR (OSDataset), VIS-IR (LGHD/RoadSence), and VIS-NIR (RGB-NIR sense).

Conclusion: The SSD-based multi-scale, cross-modality registration framework RegNetMamba-2 effectively extracts local and global structural features and fuses them across scales to achieve accurate and efficient multi-modal image registration across diverse datasets.

Abstract: In multi-modal image registration, the primary challenge lies in shared structural information extraction. Compared to Transformers, Structured State Space Duality (SSD) offers greater global structural feature extraction with higher efficiency during training and inference. Inspired by these advantages, we propose a novel algorithm for multi-modal image registration, named RegNetMamba-2. Our algorithm incorporates SSD into coarse-to-fine matching process to extract local and global structural features effectively. Firstly, SSD is applied in three different scales for multi-modal feature extraction in our network. To strengthen local representation, we pay more attention on foreground edge and structural information by feature scaling function of SSD. Secondly, for shared feature extraction of input images and multi-modal feature fusion in all scales, we propose cross-modality feature fusion model based on SSD, consisting of Cross-Modality feature Interaction (CMI) module and Multi-Scale feature Fusion (MSF) module. CMI module is designed for cross-modality feature extraction of each scale by SSD in cross form. MSF module is designed to employ a progressive upward fusion in feature-level to obtain fine features, consisting of multi-modal features in all scales. Following coarse-to-fine, the features in 1/8 scale from CMI and 1/2 scale from MSF are collected to calculate matching probability scores. Then we respectively establish matching process by correspondences of pixel-wise. Extensive experiments demonstrate that comparing with state-of-the-art deep-learning based algorithms, RegNetMamba-2 has achieved good effects in both performance and efficiency for multi-modal image registration on the following datasets: VIS-SAR (OSDataset), VIS-IR (LGHD/RoadSence) and VIS-NIR (RGB-NIR sense).

</details>


### [49] [Beyond Semantics: Modeling Factual and Affective Perceptual Experiences from Vision-Language Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03345)
*Youssef Mohamed,Kenneth Ward Church,Mohamed Elhoseiny*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: P-Topics presents a two-stage PercepT framework to model perception experiences in images as visual–textual clusters and map images to these clusters, achieving strong clustering and mapping metrics on ArtELingo and supported by human evaluation; code will be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand affective and cross‑cultural image perception by jointly modeling objective facts and subjective affect, addressing the gap where perceptual experiences are underrepresented in existing methods; aims to discover perceptual topics unsupervisedly and map images to them.

Method: Two-stage architecture: (1) formation stage discovers P-Topics as visual–textual clusters via an unsupervised objective with dynamic cluster count to match dataset richness; (2) mapping stage learns P-Topic mapping functions through attention pooling to associate images with clusters.

Result: On ArtELingo, PercepT achieves a silhouette score of 0.97 (vs. 0.37 baseline) and an AUC of 0.94 (vs. 0.77 baseline) for perceptual clustering/mapping; human evaluation confirms semantic meaningfulness and superiority over existing methods; implementation will be public.

Conclusion: PercepT advances unsupervised discovery of perception experiences, improving cross-cultural perceptual modeling by separating topic discovery from image-to-topic mapping; dynamic clustering and attention-based mapping yield strong empirical gains and set the stage for broader applicability and future cross-cultural validation.

Abstract: We present P-Topics (Perception Topics) modeling, a novel problem for understanding how images are perceived affectively and across cultures.
