The goal is to (1) discover and model the different perception experiences in a dataset of images and captions, where each experience is defined by an objective factual and a subjective affective aspect, and (2) associate images to their relevant perception experiences. We introduce **PercepT** (**Percep**tion topic **T**ransformer), a two-stage architecture that tackles P-Topics modeling. In the formation stage, percepT discovers *P-Topics* as visual-textual clusters using an unsupervised training objective, and dynamically selects the number of clusters to match the perceptual richness of the dataset. In the mapping stage, it learns *P-Topic mapping functions* via attention pooling to associate images to their respective clusters. On ArtELingo, PercepT achieves a silhouette score of **0.97** compared to **0.37** from the closest baseline reflecting better perceptual clusters. PercepT also achieves an AUC score of **0.94** compared to **0.77** showing better mapping to perceptual clusters. Human evaluation confirms that PercepT captures semantically meaningful perception experiences and significantly outperforms existing methods. Our implementation will be made public.

</details>


### [50] [SynCred-Bench: Benchmarking Synthetic Credibility in AI-Generated Visual Misinformation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03348)
*Junxiao Yang,Minghao Zhang,Xiaoce Wang,Haoran Liu,Shiyao Cui,Hongning Wang,Minlie Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SYNCRED-Bench: a 600-image synthetic credibility benchmark with FP450 negatives; evaluation shows current detectors struggle under 5% FPR, highlighting synthetic credibility as a critical visual misinformation threat.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address rising synthetic credibility in AI-generated visuals and the need for robust detection beyond superficial cues; create a benchmark to measure progress and guide detector development.

Method: Construct SYNCRED-Bench with six credible-form categories and seven circulation styles; include FP450 real-image negatives; evaluate multiple detector families (15 MLLMs, open-source detectors, commercial APIs) and human annotators under a 5% false-positive-rate constraint.

Result: Under 5% FPR, 15 MLLMs achieve 10.5% TPR; open-source AIGC detectors <5% TPR; commercial APIs 57.6% TPR; human annotators 63% TPR.

Conclusion: Synthetic credibility represents a severe, underexplored visual misinformation threat. The benchmark provides a platform for developing detectors that reason beyond superficial credibility cues.

Abstract: Recent generative models can now produce visual artifacts with realistic embedded text and layouts, creating a new misinformation threat: synthetic credibility. We introduce SYNCRED-Bench, a benchmark of 600 AI-generated misinformation images balanced across six credible-form categories and seven fine-grained circulation styles, together with FP450, a real-image negative set for measuring false positives. Extensive evaluation shows that existing systems remain unreliable: under a 5% false-positive-rate constraint, 15 MLLMs achieve only 10.5% true positive rate (TPR), open-source AIGC detectors achieve less than 5%, and commercial APIs reach 57.6%. Human annotators also struggled to identify synthetic credibility, reaching only 63% TPR. These findings establish synthetic credibility as a severe and underexplored visual misinformation challenge, and provide a benchmark for developing detectors that reason beyond superficial credibility cues.

</details>


### [51] [P\textsuperscript{2}-DPO: Grounding Hallucination in Perceptual Processing via Calibration Direct Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03376)
*Ruipeng Zhang,Zhihao Li,Haozhang Yuan,C. L. Philip Chen,Tong Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: P2-DPO trains LVLMs with on-policy, self-generated preference pairs and a Calibration Loss to address perceptual bottlenecks in attended regions and improve Visual Robustness, outperforming human-feedback baselines on ARF and degraded-image tests with similar data and cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucination in large vision-language models is linked to perceptual bottlenecks in attended regions and insufficient robustness to image degradation. Existing Direct Preference Optimization relies on vision-agnostic, off-policy human preferences, which are costly and misaligned with visual-grounding requirements.

Method: Introduce Perceptual Processing Direct Preference Optimization (P2-DPO): the model generates and learns from its own on-policy preference pairs focusing on Focus-and-Enhance perception and Visual Robustness. Includes a Calibration Loss to align visual signals with causal text generation, and an on-policy preference construction strategy.

Result: Empirical results show P2-DPO outperforms strong baselines that rely on costly human feedback on standard benchmarks, achieving higher Attention Region Fidelity and better performance under degraded image conditions, with comparable training data and cost.

Conclusion: P2-DPO effectively addresses the perceptual bottleneck in attended regions and enhances Visual Robustness by leveraging on-policy, self-generated preferences and a calibration objective, offering a data-efficient alternative to human-feedback-based DPO in LVLMs.

Abstract: Hallucination has recently garnered significant research attention in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) aims to learn directly from the corrected preferences provided by humans, thereby addressing the hallucination issue. Despite its success, this paradigm has yet to specifically target the perceptual bottleneck in attended regions or address insufficient Visual Robustness against image degradation. Furthermore, existing preference pairs are often vision-agnostic and their inherently off-policy nature limits their effectiveness in guiding model learning. To address these challenges, we propose Perceptual Processing Direct Preference Optimization (P\textsuperscript{2}-DPO), a novel training paradigm in which the model generates and learns from its own preference pairs, thereby directly addressing the identified visual bottlenecks while inherently avoiding the issues of vision-agnostic and off-policy data. It introduces: (1) an on-policy preference pairs construction method targeting Focus-and-Enhance perception and Visual Robustness, and (2) a well-designed Calibration Loss to precisely align visual signals with the causal generation of text. Experimental results demonstrate that with a comparable amount of training data and cost, P\textsuperscript{2}-DPO outperforms strong baselines that rely on costly human feedback on benchmarks. Furthermore, evaluations on Attention Region Fidelity (ARF) and image degradation scenarios validate the effectiveness of P\textsuperscript{2}-DPO in addressing perceptual bottleneck in attended regions and improving Visual Robustness against degraded inputs.

</details>


### [52] [Towards Characterizing Scientific Image Utility and Upgradability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03401)
*WenZhe Li,Qihang Yan,Liang Chen,Junying Wang,Farong Wen,Yijin Guo,Chunyi Li,Zicheng Zhang,Guangtao Zhai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces SIU^2A: a two-dimension framework (Utility and Upgradability) to evaluate scientific image integrity, addressing AI-induced distortions. Proposes a four-type corruption taxonomy, a corresponding SIU^2A-Benchmark with expert annotations, and a two-stage evaluation protocol showing current multimodal systems struggle with scientific error detection and faithful correction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To close the gap between image perceptual quality metrics and scientific validity amid AI-generated artifacts in scientific imagery; existing LMs lack domain-specific verification for scientific content.

Method: Define a four-type corruption taxonomy (Detail Distortion, Incompleteness, False Content, Entity Confusion); build SIU^2A-Benchmark with expert annotations for error identification and repair; implement a two-stage protocol: Utility (error detection and repair instruction) and Upgradability (faithful correction quality); evaluate multimodal systems on the benchmark.

Result: Demonstrated that current multimodal models have significant limitations in detecting scientific errors and in producing faithful, valid corrections; highlights a gap between visual perception and scientific usability.

Conclusion: SIU^2A provides a structured, dual-dimension framework and benchmark to assess and guide improvements in scientific image integrity, facilitating systematic evaluation of error detection and repair capabilities.

Abstract: Scientific images function as critical evidence in research communication, yet their integrity faces unprecedented threats from AI-generated content that introduces subtle but consequential errors. Existing evaluation paradigms prove inadequate: perceptual quality metrics poorly correlate with scientific validity, while language models lack domain-specific verification capabilities. To address this gap, we propose the \textbf{S}cientific \textbf{I}mage \textbf{U}tility and \textbf{U}pgradability \textbf{A}ssessment (\textbf{SIU$^2$A}) framework, which introduces two complementary dimensions for scientific image evaluation. \textbf{Utility} encompasses \textit{error detection} (identifying scientific inaccuracies) and \textit{correction feasibility} (assessing whether errors can be reliably repaired). \textbf{Upgradability} measures the quality of correction. We categorize scientific image corruption into four fundamental types: Detail Distortion, Incompleteness, False Content, and Entity Confusion. Based on this taxonomy, we construct SIU$^2$A-Benchmark, a dataset with expert annotations for error identification and repair. The framework implements a two-stage evaluation protocol: the \textit{Utility} stage evaluates error detection capability and repair instruction generation, while the \textit{Upgradability} stage assesses whether corrections faithfully restore scientific validity without compromising existing accurate information. Experiments reveal that current multimodal systems exhibit significant limitations in both scientific error assessment and faithful correction, exposing a fundamental gap between visual perception and scientific usability.

</details>


### [53] [Mamba-Enhanced Implicit Motion Learning for Audio-Driven Portrait Animation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03402)
*Xuan Wei,Jiahui Chen,Kaiheng Li,Mingyu Shao,Qingqi Hong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-stage implicit-motion framework for audio-driven talking-head video generation from a single image; decoupling motion prediction from rendering; region-aware attention with appearance priors and depth cues; Mamba-enhanced diffusion to predict motion features from audio; trained on a 380-hour dataset; achieves state-of-the-art in accuracy, naturalness, and temporal coherence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional keypoint-based methods often fail to capture subtle motion dynamics; need realistic, temporally coherent motion from a single image driven by audio; decoupling motion prediction from rendering to improve flexibility and efficiency.

Method: Stage 1 integrates appearance priors and hierarchical depth cues into a region-aware attention mechanism to model latent motion features. Stage 2 employs a Mamba-enhanced diffusion model to directly predict these features from audio and the source image, enabling unsupervised learning of fine-grained motion patterns; a two-stage, decoupled pipeline.

Result: Outperforms prior work across public benchmarks and the authors’ 380-hour dataset in accuracy, naturalness, and temporal coherence; establishes a new state-of-the-art.

Conclusion: The proposed implicit-motion, decoupled framework yields realistic, temporally coherent audio-driven motion from a single image with improved flexibility and efficiency.

Abstract: Audio-driven human motion video generation aims to synthesize realistic and temporally coherent human animations from a single static image, with applications in talking-head synthesis, co-speech gesture generation, and dynamic presentations. Moving beyond conventional keypoint-based methods that often struggle to capture subtle motion dynamics, We propose a novel implicit-motion framework for generating realistic and temporally coherent human motion videos from a single static image and audio. Our approach uses a two-stage pipeline that decouples motion prediction from rendering. The first stage integrates appearance priors and hierarchical depth cues into a region-aware attention mechanism to model latent motion features. The second stage employs a Mamba-enhanced diffusion model to directly predict these features from audio and the source image, enabling unsupervised learning of fine-grained motion patterns. This decoupled architecture enhances flexibility and efficiency. Trained on a new 380-hour high-quality dataset, our method outperforms prior work across multiple public benchmarks and our collected data in accuracy, naturalness, and temporal coherence, setting a new state-of-the-art.

</details>


### [54] [SAMatcher: Co-Visibility Modeling with Segment Anything for Robust Feature Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03406)
*Xu Pan,Qiyuan Ma,Mingyue Dong,He Chen,Wei Ji,Xianwei Zheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAMatcher reframes feature correspondence as co-visibility modeling: it predicts co-visible region masks and bounding boxes using the Segment Anything Model (SAM), then performs symmetric cross-view interactions for bidirectional feature exchange, with a unified supervision scheme (mask learning, box regression, mask-box consistency). It yields substantial improvements on challenging benchmarks, suggesting monocular segmentation models can be extended to multi-view matching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current learning-based matching often operates at pixel/patch level and lacks explicit region-level co-visibility priors across views. Incorporating co-visible regions provides structured priors that can improve robustness to viewpoint and scale changes.

Method: Use SAM to predict co-visible region masks and bounding boxes as priors. Introduce symmetric cross-view interaction for bidirectional feature exchange and cross-view semantic alignment. Train with unified supervision: mask learning, box regression, and mask-box consistency constraints, enabling joint optimization of segmentation and localization with matching.

Result: Extensive experiments on challenging benchmarks show substantial improvements over existing matching pipelines, particularly under large viewpoint and scale variations. Demonstrates that foundation models designed for monocular segmentation can be extended to multi-view correspondence reasoning via explicit co-visibility modeling.

Conclusion: Co-visibility priors and region-level reasoning can enhance multi-view correspondence estimation. The work provides a new perspective on structured representation learning for image matching and suggests broader applicability of segmentation-focused foundation models to multi-view tasks.

Abstract: Reliable correspondence estimation is a fundamental problem in image processing, underpinning applications such as Structure from Motion, visual localization, and image registration. Existing learning-based methods have significantly improved local feature representations, yet most still operate at the pixel or patch level and lack explicit modeling of regions that are jointly visible across views. We propose SAMatcher, a feature matching framework that formulates correspondence estimation through co-visibility modeling. Instead of directly matching local features, SAMatcher first predicts co-visible region masks and bounding boxes as structured priors for correspondence estimation. Built upon the Segment Anything Model (SAM), it introduces a symmetric cross-view interaction mechanism that enables bidirectional feature exchange and cross-view semantic alignment. We further develop a unified supervision scheme that jointly optimizes mask prediction and box localization through mask learning, box regression, and mask-box consistency constraints. Extensive experiments on challenging benchmarks demonstrate substantial improvements over existing matching pipelines, particularly under large viewpoint and scale variations. Our results show that foundation models originally designed for monocular segmentation can be effectively extended to multi-view correspondence reasoning through explicit co-visibility modeling, offering a new perspective on structured representation learning for image matching. Code and project page: https://xupan.top/Projects/samatcher

</details>


### [55] [Enginuity: A Dataset and Benchmark for Vision-Language Understanding of Engineering Diagrams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03410)
*Abhishek Kumar,Isha Motiyani,Tilak Kasturi,Ethan Seefried,Prahitha Movva,Tirthankar Ghosal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Enginuity introduces the first open benchmark for evaluating VLMs on engineering diagrams, with two tasks: structured parts-table extraction and free-form visual diagram VQA, using military manuals; evaluates four frontier VLMs under zero-shot and chain-of-thought prompting; reveals gaps in token-based metrics vs semantic/factual understanding; releases dataset and tools for reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Engineering diagrams convey dense, structured information through spatial layouts and domain-specific symbols, which current VLM benchmarks inadequately cover. There is a need for a public benchmark to assess VLM capability in understanding complex engineering visuals crucial for service, repair, and design workflows.

Method: Create Enginuity dataset from U.S. military service and repair manuals. Define Task 1: structured parts-table extraction; Task 2: free-form visual diagram VQA. Evaluate four frontier VLMs (GPT-5.2 Chat, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemma 4, Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct) under zero-shot and chain-of-thought prompting. Provide evaluation harness and per-sample model outputs.

Result: Task 1: Recall@all 0.61–0.87; Token F1pen 0.03–0.18, indicating a gap between part identification and description fidelity. Task 2 shows a consistent factual-reasoning gap across models. Token-overlap metrics under-report capability by 2–6x compared to semantic similarity, motivating LLM-as-judge calibration for domain-specific evaluation.

Conclusion: The authors release the dataset, annotations, evaluation harness, and per-sample outputs to enable reproducible study of VLMs on engineering content.

Abstract: Engineering diagrams pose a distinct challenge for vision-language models: unlike natural images or general documents, they encode information through dense spatial layouts, domain-specific symbols, and cross-references between visual callouts and structured parts tables. Despite their centrality to service, repair, and design workflows, there is no public benchmark for measuring VLM capabilities in this domain; existing datasets primarily focus on flowcharts, scientific figures, or business documents. To address this gap, we introduce Enginuity, the first open dataset and benchmark for evaluating VLMs on complex engineering diagrams. We define two tasks over a corpus of U.S. military service and repair manuals: structured parts-table extraction (Task 1) and free-form visual diagram question answering (VQA)(Task 2) for benchmarking. We evaluate four frontier VLMs (GPT-5.2 Chat, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemma 4, Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct) under zero-shot and chain-of-thought prompting. On Task 1, models reach Recall@all of 0.61-0.87 but Token F1pen of only 0.03-0.18, exposing a systematic gap between part identification and description fidelity. Task 2 reveals a consistent factual-reasoning gap across all models. A supporting analysis shows that token-overlap metrics under-report model capability on technical descriptions by 2-6x relative to semantic similarity, motivating LLM-as-judge calibration for domain-specific evaluation. We release the dataset, annotations, evaluation harness, and per-sample model outputs to support a reproducible study of VLM capability on engineering content.

</details>


### [56] [A unified multi-task framework enables interpretable chest radiograph analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03417)
*Lijian Xu,Ziyu Ni,Xinglong Liu,Xiaosong Wang,Hongsheng Li,Shaoting Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Presents IMT-CXR, an interpretable, multi-task transformer for chest X-ray analysis that mimics radiologists' workflow through disease recognition, attribute characterization, and evidence-integrated report generation; shows competitive benchmarks and radiologist-rated reports, enabling traceable, clinically useful AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address trust, interpretability, and clinical utility gaps in multimodal medical imaging AI by modeling the radiologist's multi-task workflow and providing traceable evidence from findings to conclusions.

Method: A unified transformer architecture optimized with medical-domain instruction tuning to perform four tasks in sequence: (1) multi-label disease classification, (2) lesion localization, (3) anatomical segmentation, (4) radiology report generation, organized into three evidence-driven stages: disease recognition, attribute characterization, and evidence-integrated reporting.

Result: Competitive performance on ten chest X-ray benchmarks in both direct inference and fine-tuning. In a blinded evaluation of 160 historical reports from four centers, three radiologists judged 66% of AI-generated reports as comparable to or better than the originals in diagnostic clarity, indicating notable translational potential.

Conclusion: By enabling traceable diagnostic pathways from anatomical findings to conclusions, the approach aligns AI metrics with clinical utility and advances trustworthy AI in medical imaging.

Abstract: While multimodal deep learning has advanced medical imaging analysis, existing black-box systems \textcolor{black}{may remain confined to isolated tasks, often overlooking} the trust-sensitive nature of clinical diagnosis as a multi-task process. We propose IMT-CXR (Interpretable Multi-task Transformer for Chest X-ray Analysis), a framework that emulates radiologists' diagnostic workflow through three evidence-driven stages: 1) Disease recognition; 2) Attribute characterization (e.g., size, location, severity quantification); 3) Evidence-integrated report generation with traceable decision pathways. The framework employs a unified transformer architecture optimized via medical-domain instruction tuning, sequentially executing four clinical tasks: multi-label disease classification, lesion localization, anatomical segmentation, and radiology report generation. Experimental validation demonstrates competitive performance on ten CXR benchmarks under direct inference and fine-tuning settings. In a blinded evaluation of 160 historical reports from four medical centers, three radiologists rated 66\% of AI-generated reports as comparable to or surpassing original clinical reports in diagnostic clarity, highlighting the framework's translational potential. By establishing traceable diagnostic pathways from anatomical findings to conclusions, this work bridges the gap between AI technical metrics and clinical utility, advancing trustworthy AI systems in medical imaging.

</details>


### [57] [IDO: Incongruity-aware Distribution Optimization for Multimodal Fake News Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03418)
*Hengyang Zhou,Rongman Hong,Yuxuan Zhou,Jing Wang,Zhaoyan Pan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IDO proposes incongruity-aware distribution optimization for multimodal fake news detection, modeling factual and modality incongruity via channel-wise reweighting with Gaussian modeling and incongruity contrastive learning, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal fake news detection emphasizes cross-modal consistency but largely ignores semantic incongruity that often characterizes deceptive content; misinformation frequently contains information incongruent with the facts.

Method: IDO comprises (1) a channel-wise reweighting strategy to yield semantically discriminative embeddings and (2) Gaussian distribution modeling to capture uncertain correlations caused by factual incongruity; for modality incongruity, it employs incongruity contrastive learning to learn cross-modal semantic information.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows IDO achieves state-of-the-art performance on multimodal fake news detection benchmarks.

Conclusion: IDO effectively addresses both factual and modality incongruity in multimodal fake news detection and outperforms baselines.

Abstract: Multimodal fake news detection aims to identify the authenticity of news. Existing multimodal fake news detection methods mainly focus on cross-modal consistency, but often fail to explicitly model the semantic incongruity that characterizes deceptive multimodal content. However, misinformation often contains semantic information incongruity with the facts. To address these challenges, we propose Incongruity-aware Distribution Optimization (IDO) to improve the performance of fake news detection from the perspectives of factual incongruity and modality incongruity. For factual incongruity, we introduce a channel-wise reweighting strategy to obtain semantically discriminative embeddings and utilize gaussian distribution to model the uncertain correlation caused by factual incongruity. For modality incongruity, we utilize incongruity contrastive learning to learn cross-modal semantic information. Experiments demonstrate that IDO achieves state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [58] [PHAF-Personalized Hand Avatars in a Flash](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03420)
*Meghana Shankar,Akanxit Upadhyay,Anmol Namdev,Green Rosh KS,Pawan Prasad BH*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PHAF creates personalized hand avatars from two images with fast texture generation for edge devices, achieving comparable fidelity with 30x faster texture generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable photo-realistic, personalized hand avatars for AR/VR that can be produced quickly on-device without expensive optimization.

Method: Semantic guided mesh alignment; densified texture extraction to transfer high-frequency details; view-based inpainting network for texture refinement; uses a parametric hand model for articulation; designed for real-time edge deployment.

Result: Achieves visual fidelity comparable to existing methods while reducing texture generation time by 30x; generalizes to novel viewpoints; compatible with standard graphics engines.

Conclusion: PHAF provides fast, edge-friendly, high-quality hand avatars suitable for practical AR/VR applications, bridging realism and real-time performance.

Abstract: We present PHAF-Personalized Hand Avatars in a Flash, a personalized photo-realistic hand avatar which provides high quality multi-view renders from just two images (dorsal and palmar views).Unlike slow optimization-based techniques, PHAF generates fast personalized textures for real-time deployment on edge devices. Our approach combines semantic guided mesh alignment and densified texture extraction to transfer high-frequency details efficiently. A view-based inpainting network refines textures ensuring smooth, continuous appearance. PHAF generalizes to novel viewpoints and leverages a parametric hand model for accurate articulations, making it compatible with standard graphics engines. Experiments show it is comparable to existing methods in visual fidelity while drastically reducing texture generation time by 30 times, enabling practical AR/VR applications.

</details>


### [59] [PRISM: Synergizing Vision Foundation Models via Self-organized Expert Specialization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03444)
*Ying Tang,Dong Li,Youjia Zhang,Zikai Song,Junqing Yu,Wei Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PRISM is a dual-stream Mixture-of-Experts framework that unifies diverse Vision Foundation Models via expert specialization and dynamic recomposition, addressing negative transfer in monolithic distillation; it achieves SOTA on PASCAL-Context and NYUD-v2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To efficiently combine diverse VFMs without negative transfer, enabling scalable integration of rich visual knowledge.

Method: Two-stage: (1) expertise deconstruction with a teacher-conditional router guiding experts to specialize in distinct representational subspaces; (2) dynamic recomposition where the router assembles experts into task-specific computational pathways within a dual-stream MoE.

Result: Empirical evaluation on PASCAL-Context and NYUD-v2 shows state-of-the-art performance, validating sparse emergent specialization as a scalable integration approach.

Conclusion: Sparse, emergent specialization via modular MoE is an effective and scalable strategy to unify diverse VFMs without adverse interference.

Abstract: Unifying the complementary strengths of diverse Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) into a single efficient model is highly desirable but challenged by the negative transfer inherent in monolithic distillation. To address these feature conflicts, we introduce \textbf{PRISM}, a novel dual-stream Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework that synergizes VFMs via modular specialization. We propose a two-stage paradigm: (1) expertise deconstruction, where a teacher-conditional router guides experts to specialize in distinct representational subspaces to mitigate interference, followed by (2) dynamic recomposition, where the router learns to assemble these experts into tailored computational pathways for downstream tasks. Experiments on PASCAL-Context and NYUD-v2 show that \textbf{PRISM} establishes a new state of the art, validating that sparse, emergent specialization is a scalable approach for integrating diverse visual knowledge.

</details>


### [60] [From 3D Perception to Safety Reasoning: A Graph-Based Framework for Real-Time Underground Mine Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03460)
*Pasindu Ranasinghe,Simit Raval,Dibyayan Patra,Bikram Banerjee,Ismet Canbulat*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A 3D perception and graph-based reasoning framework for underground mine safety that fuses semantic 3D perception, anomaly detection, rule checks, on-device LLM reasoning, and GraphRAG memory to detect immediate hazards and patterns; achieves 92.7% accuracy at 30 FPS and hazard-coverage improvements up to 93% with memory-based reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Underground mining combines confined spaces, obscured hazards, and scarce labeled data, requiring richer 3D scene understanding and memory beyond fixed cameras and rule-based alerts.

Method: Integrates 3D semantic perception, uncertainty-based anomaly detection, rule-based hazard checks, on-device LLM reasoning, and GraphRAG-based memory analysis. Uses scene and temporal graphs as explicit knowledge structures; employs self-supervised pretraining; generates hazard scenarios via real roadway scans, controlled object placement, and high-fidelity longwall simulation.

Result: Perception model achieves 92.7% accuracy at 30 FPS with low memory usage; hazard coverage across 115 scenarios: 57% baseline, 76% with contextual LLM reasoning, 93% with memory-based reasoning using historical records; qualitative anomaly signals support interpretation of out-of-distribution hazards.

Conclusion: Graph-based knowledge representation combined with 3D perception and layered safety reasoning provides a practical foundation for intelligent decision support in underground mine monitoring.

Abstract: Underground coal mining requires personnel and heavy equipment to operate within shared, confined, and poorly illuminated spaces where hazards such as equipment proximity violations, structural instabilities, and occluded blind spots are difficult to anticipate. Conventional monitoring systems, including fixed cameras and rule-based proximity alerts, can detect predefined events but lack the 3D scene understanding and contextual memory needed to identify complex or evolving hazards. This paper presents a continuous monitoring framework that converts colourised 3D point clouds into structured and traceable safety reasoning outputs. The framework combines 3D semantic perception, uncertainty-based anomaly detection, rule-based hazard checks, on-device LLM reasoning, and GraphRAG -based memory analysis to identify immediate hazards and interpret longer-term safety patterns. Scene and temporal graphs serve as the explicit knowledge structure, linking perception outputs across reasoning stages. To overcome the scarcity of labeled underground data, real roadway scans, controlled object placement, and high-fidelity longwall simulation were combined to generate diverse hazard scenarios, while self-supervised pretraining improved segmentation from limited annotations. The perception model achieved 92.7% accuracy at 30 FPS with low memory usage. Across 115 hazard scenarios, rule-based checks achieved 57% coverage, increasing to 76% with contextual LLM reasoning and 93% with memory-based reasoning using historical records. Qualitative results show uncertainty-derived anomaly signals support the interpretation of out-of-distribution hazards beyond predefined classes. Overall, graph-based knowledge representation combined with 3D perception and layered safety reasoning provides a practical foundation for intelligent decision support in underground mine monitoring.

</details>


### [61] [Mixed-Modality Dual Face-Hair Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03470)
*Quoc-Anh Bui-Huynh,Mai-Tuyen Lam,Dai-Anh-Tuan Nguyen,Thanh Duc Ngo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: We propose Dual Face-Hair Retrieval (DFHR), a mixed-modality dual-reference retrieval task where a query specifies identity via a face image and hairstyle via an image or text. This requires cross-component reasoning, disentangled representations, and cross-modal alignment in a unified embedding space. A large DFHR benchmark (DFHR-Bench) with ~180k annotated triplets across dual-image and image-text settings is introduced, built via a multi-stage annotation protocol. We also present MFHC (Multimodal Face-Hair Combiner), a unified framework that fuses disentangled identity and hairstyle embeddings through token injection and multi-view supervision. Together, DFHR and DFHR-Bench establish a new paradigm for identity-aware, attribute-controllable visual retrieval across modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior retrieval settings focus on single modalities or single attributes, lacking the ability to perform cross-component reasoning between identity and hairstyle from heterogeneous modalities. There is a need for localized feature disentanglement, cross-modal semantic alignment, and mixed-modality composition in a unified embedding space to enable controllable face-related retrieval across modalities.

Method: Define the DFHR task and construct DFHR-Bench with over 180k annotated triplets in dual-image and image-text settings using a staged annotation protocol to preserve semantic and identity integrity. Propose MFHC to fuse disentangled identity and hairstyle embeddings via token injection and multi-view supervision, enabling unified embedding and cross-modal retrieval.

Result: DFHR-Bench provides a large-scale, challenging benchmark for mixed-modality face-hair retrieval, enabling evaluation of cross-modal and cross-component reasoning. MFHC achieves competitive retrieval performance by effectively disentangling and re-combining identity and hairstyle cues across modalities, demonstrating the practicality of the proposed framework.

Conclusion: DFHR and DFHR-Bench establish a new paradigm for identity-aware, attribute-controllable visual retrieval across modalities, highlighting the importance of cross-component reasoning, disentanglement, and unified embedding space design for mixed-modality tasks.

Abstract: We introduce Dual Face-Hair Retrieval (DFHR), a new mixed-modality dual-reference task in image retrieval where a query consists of a face image specifying identity and a hairstyle reference expressed as either an image or text. Unlike prior retrieval settings, DFHR requires cross-component reasoning between two semantically independent attributes -- identity and hairstyle -- originating from heterogeneous modalities. This formulation demands localized feature disentanglement, cross-modal semantic alignment, and mixed-modality composition within a unified embedding space. We construct DFHR-Bench, the first benchmark for mixed-modality face-hair retrieval, comprising over 180K annotated triplets across dual-image and image-text settings, built via a multi-stage annotation protocol ensuring semantic and identity integrity. We further propose MFHC (Multimodal Face-Hair Combiner), a unified framework that fuses disentangled identity and hairstyle embeddings through token injection and multi-view supervision. DFHR and DFHR-Bench together establish a new paradigm for identity-aware, attribute-controllable visual retrieval across modalities.

</details>


### [62] [PersistGS: Differentiable Physics for Object Permanence in 4D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03479)
*Adrian Ramlal,John S. Zelek*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PersistGS integrates differentiable rigid-body dynamics with dynamic 3D Gaussian splatting to preserve object persistence during occlusion in dynamic scenes; it uses per-object Gaussians and collision meshes, infers SE(3) trajectories from pre-occlusion motion, and propagates them through occlusion, aided by a centroid silhouette loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Photometric supervision in dynamic 3D Gaussian Splatting fails when objects are fully occluded, leading to degraded or absent object representations. Existing methods rely on learned priors that emphasize visual plausibility over physical correctness.

Method: Decompose scene into per-object Gaussians and collision meshes; estimate friction and velocity from observed pre-occlusion trajectories via differentiable simulation; compute SE(3) trajectories and position object Gaussians during occlusion; introduce a centroid silhouette loss to separate positional gradients from appearance noise.

Result: Trajectory error reduced by ~40% relative to photometric supervision; persists better than constant-velocity extrapolation, achieving +2.46 dB PSNR over photometric baseline and within 0.19 dB of a ground-truth trajectory upper bound in synthetic scenes; evaluation on unseen cameras observes occluded objects.

Conclusion: Enforcing physics via differentiable rigid-body dynamics yields physically plausible occlusion handling, improving object permanence in dynamic 3D Gaussian Splatting and reducing reliance on purely generative priors.

Abstract: Dynamic 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) methods reconstruct time-varying scenes from synchronized multi-camera video using photometric supervision. When a moving object becomes fully occluded from all training cameras, this supervision vanishes: the Gaussians representing it receive no gradient signal and degrade. Existing approaches to incomplete observations in neural reconstruction rely on learned generative priors that prioritize visual plausibility over physical correctness.
  We propose $\textbf{PersistGS}$, a method that restores object permanence during occlusion by coupling differentiable rigid body simulation with 3D Gaussian Splatting. Our approach decomposes the scene into per-object Gaussians and collision meshes, estimates friction and velocity from the observed pre-occlusion trajectory via differentiable simulation, and uses the resulting SE(3) trajectory to position object Gaussians throughout the occlusion period. Because the predicted trajectory satisfies the governing equations of rigid body dynamics, it faithfully captures contact events (bounces, friction-based deceleration, direction changes) that kinematic extrapolation cannot model. We introduce a centroid silhouette loss that isolates positional gradients from appearance noise, yielding 40% lower trajectory error than photometric supervision. We evaluate using cameras withheld from training that observe the object during its occlusion. Experiments on synthetic scenes show that PersistGS outperforms constant velocity extrapolation by +2.46dB PSNR and comes within 0.19dB of a ground-truth trajectory upper bound.

</details>


### [63] [TrAction: Action Recognition with Sparse Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03490)
*Jan F. Meier,Felix B. Mueller,Alexander Ecker,Timo Lüddecke*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Sparse 2.5D trajectory-based transformer with masked-trajectory pretraining delivers competitive action recognition using sparse input, achieving strong results and complementing appearance cues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense RGB approaches are memory/use-intensive and prone to exploiting appearance/background biases; sparse point trajectories offer an efficient, less biased input modality for action recognition.

Method: Develop a simple transformer architecture for 2.5D trajectory-based recognition and train with masked-trajectory pretraining to learn robust trajectory representations from sparse inputs.

Result: achieves 45% top-1 on Something-Something V2 and 54% on EPIC-Kitchens-100; outperforms V-JEPA on time-reversal sensitivity; fusion with DINOv2 and V-JEPA2 yields +8.7 and +1.6 top-1 points on Something-Something V2.

Conclusion: Trajectory features provide complementary information to appearance-based features; the sparse trajectory input offers efficiency and robustness, and combining it with existing appearance models yields improved performance; code released.

Abstract: Modern action recognition models operate on memory- and compute-intensive dense RGB video volumes and frequently exploit appearance and background shortcuts, for example, predicting actions from objects or scenes instead of characteristic motion. We investigate an efficient alternative input modality that is largely free of such biases by construction: sparse point trajectories. To this end, we develop a simple transformer architecture for 2.5D trajectory-based recognition together with a masked-trajectory pretraining, which we show to substantially improve downstream action recognition accuracy. Despite using only a fraction of the dense RGB input, our method reaches 45% top-1 on Something-Something V2 and 54% on EPIC-Kitchens-100, and surpasses V-JEPA on time-reversal sensitivity. More importantly, we find trajectory features to be complementary to state-of-the-art appearance-based features. Fusing our pretrained model with DINOv2 and V-JEPA 2 improves top-1 accuracy on Something-Something V2 by 8.7 and 1.6 points, respectively. Code: https://github.com/ecker-lab/TrAction

</details>


### [64] [Low-Frequency Shortcuts in Texture-Driven Visual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03493)
*Utku Şirin,Cathy Hou,David Alvarez-Melis,Stratos Idreos*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Texture-driven datasets rely on low-frequency shortcuts; pruning low-frequency components from data reduces shortcuts, improving in-distribution accuracy and robustness to low-frequency corruptions, but introduces a trade-off with high-frequency information and affects OOD performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend shortcut-learning analysis to texture-driven domains and compare with standard benchmarks to understand spectral shortcuts and their impact on ID and OOD performance.

Method: Perform spectral analysis to identify low-frequency shortcuts (LFCs) in texture-driven domains, prune LFCs from training and test data, and assess effects on ID accuracy and robustness to corruptions; compare with a standard benchmark.

Result: Texture-driven domains rely on LFCs with skewed spectral behavior, even though their discriminative information lies in higher-frequency details. Pruning LFCs eliminates the shortcut, increasing ID accuracy by up to 8%. It also substantially improves robustness to low-frequency corruptions (up to 40%), but introduces a trade-off by increasing dependence on high-frequency features, which can hurt high-frequency generalization and, overall, OOD performance depends on the interaction between low- and high-frequency factors. Without pruning, OOD accuracy can suffer up to ~70% relative drop from ID accuracy.

Conclusion: Spectral shortcuts drive shortcut learning in texture-driven domains; mitigating low-frequency shortcuts yields better generalization and resilience to low-frequency corruptions, but balanced spectral behavior is crucial to manage high-frequency information and OOD performance.

Abstract: Neural networks suffer from shortcut learning, where learned features generalize well to the training set but not to in-distribution (ID) or out-of-distribution (OOD) test sets. Existing studies are all based on a few standard benchmarks, which are shape-driven. Numerous application domains, however, are texture-driven. In this work, we present shortcut learning analysis for texture-driven domains, and compare it with that of a standard benchmark. We show that texture-driven domains suffer from low-frequency shortcuts. They make the majority of their decisions based on a few low-frequency components (LFCs) with a skewed spectral behavior, despite that their classification information is in higher-frequency, fine-grained details. Pruning LFCs from training and test sets eliminates the shortcut and provides a more balanced spectral behavior, improving the ID accuracy by up to 8%. We show that low-frequency shortcuts make the models highly vulnerable to OOD corruptions, leading up to 70% accuracy drop compared to the ID accuracy. Pruning LFCs significantly improves robustness to low-frequency corruptions, by up to 40%, and introduces a trade-off for high-frequency corruptions; the balanced spectral behavior provides a better generalization performance, whereas the increased dependence on high-frequency features reduces it. OOD accuracy depends on the interaction between these two factors.

</details>


### [65] [Learned Non-Maximum Suppression for 3D Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03568)
*Timo Osterburg,Stefan Schütte,Torsten Bertram*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two learned post-processing modules replace heuristic NMS in LiDAR 3D detection: D2D-Rescore with transformer-based detection-to-detection attention, and GossipNet3D with BEV-localized message passing. A metric-aware matching strategy aligned to nuScenes training/validation improves mAP, NDS, and true positive quality, especially for small/rare classes, with minimal overhead. Code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of heuristic NMS (e.g., CircleNMS) in 3D LiDAR detection by introducing learnable, detection-level filtering that leverages inter-detection relations to improve reliability and accuracy without changing the base detector.

Method: - D2D-Rescore: transformer-based attention across detections (detection-to-detection) to re-score and filter proposals. 
- GossipNet3D: adapts 2D GossipNet to 3D using localized message passing in BEV to refine detections. 
- Metric-aware matching: training/validation aligned with nuScenes metrics, ensuring consistent optimization for evaluation.

Result: Both modules outperform CircleNMS on mAP, NDS, and true positive quality, particularly for small and infrequent classes, while adding minimal computational overhead. They serve as learning-based alternatives to heuristic suppression and maintain compatibility with existing detectors.

Conclusion: Learned, detection-level filtering can enhance 3D detector reliability without modifying the base network, providing a principled alternative to heuristic NMS. Code is available for reproduction and integration.

Abstract: Post-processing is a critical stage in LiDAR-based 3D object detection, where dense and overlapping proposals must be filtered for compact and reliable perception. This work introduces two learned filtering modules that replace heuristic non-maximum suppression (NMS) by leveraging relations among detections. D2D-Rescore employs transformer-based detection-to-detection (D2D) attention, while GossipNet3D adapts the 2D GossipNet concept to 3D through localized message passing in bird's-eye view. A metric-aware matching strategy aligned with the nuScenes evaluation protocol ensures consistent training and validation behavior, improving overall detection performance. Both approaches improve mean average precision (mAP), nuScenes detection score (NDS), and true positive quality compared to CircleNMS, particularly for small and infrequent classes, while adding minimal computational overhead. These results demonstrate that learned, detection-level filtering can enhance 3D detector reliability without modifying the base network, offering a principled alternative to heuristic suppression. Code is available at https://github.com/rst-tu-dortmund/learned-3d-nms .

</details>


### [66] [Characterizing Detectability in 3DGS Poisoning: A Stage-wise Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03499)
*Quoc-Anh Bui-Huynh,Thanh Duc Ngo,Xue Geng,Kaixin Xu,Wang Zhe,Xulei Yang,Ngai-Man Cheung*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Poison-3DGS: a stage-aware benchmark for detecting poisoning in 3D Gaussian Splatting across pipeline stages; reveals stage-dependent detectability and signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3DGS is vulnerable to diverse poisoning attacks; existing work focuses on attack success; detection remains underexplored, particularly given the multi-stage, heterogeneous intermediate representations of the reconstruction pipeline.

Method: Introduce Poison-3DGS benchmark to characterize poisoning detectability in a stage-wise fashion, exposing stage-specific forensic signals (multi-view images, geometry, training dynamics, Gaussian parameters) across varied scenes and attacks; perform systematic evaluation of detectability per stage.

Result: Detectability varies significantly across stages; no single stage dominates across attack types; different attacks produce stage-specific forensic signals; later-stage signals (training dynamics, Gaussian parameter statistics) provide strong cues not seen at early stages; establishes a principled benchmark and first comprehensive stage-wise characterization in 3DGS.

Conclusion: Offers a foundational benchmark and insights for developing robust, stage-aware defenses and detectors for 3DGS, enabling more reliable real-time 3D synthesis.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has rapidly emerged as a leading representation for real-time novel view synthesis, but recent work shows it is vulnerable to diverse poisoning attacks, including illusory object injection, computation cost amplification, and post hoc model watermarking. Despite this expanding threat surface, existing studies focus mainly on attack success, while defense and detection remain underexplored. From a detection perspective, a key challenge and opportunity arise from the multi-stage nature of the 3DGS reconstruction pipeline, which produces heterogeneous intermediate representations. Forensic signals for detecting poisoning are inherently stage dependent: an attack introduced at one stage may produce signals that emerge only at later stages. This motivates a stage-wise view of detectability that goes beyond single-stage evaluation. We introduce Poison-3DGS, a benchmark for stage-wise characterization of poisoning detection in 3DGS. It exposes stage-specific artifacts, including multi-view images, geometry, training dynamics, and Gaussian parameters, across a diverse set of scenes and attacks. Using it, we conduct a systematic study of detectability across pipeline stages. Our analysis reveals several insights. First, detectability varies significantly across stages, and no single stage consistently dominates across attack types. Second, different attacks exhibit distinct stage-specific forensic signals, so detection effectiveness depends critically on where signals are observed. Third, later-stage signals such as training dynamics and Gaussian parameter statistics provide strong cues not observable at earlier stages. Overall, our work provides a principled benchmark and the first systematic characterization of stage-dependent detectability in 3DGS, offering a foundation for future research on robust and reliable 3DGS systems.

</details>


### [67] [UnsOcc: 3D Semantic Occupancy Prediction in Unstructured Scene via Rendering Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03581)
*Ye Wu,Ruiqi Song,Baiyong Ding,Nanxin Zeng,Junjie Cheng,Yunfeng Ai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-modal 3D semantic occupancy framework for unstructured scenes (UnsOcc) that uses rendering-based fusion (RenderFusion) and a Gaussian Splatting-based refinement (GSRefinement) to handle sparse data and long-tail distributions; validated on open-pit mine data and nuScenes, outperforming SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unstructured scenes in autonomous driving feature irregular obstacles and sparse scene layouts that hinder traditional 3D perception methods. 3D semantic occupancy offers dense spatial labeling but faces severe sparsity and long-tail challenges in such environments. A dedicated dataset from open-pit mines is used to evaluate robustness.

Method: Propose UnsOcc, a multi-modal 3D semantic occupancy framework. Introduce RenderFusion, a rendering-based fusion module with bidirectional rendering supervision to improve cross-modal feature alignment. Propose GSRefinement, a detail-aware supervision method using Gaussian Splatting to project sparse 3D occupancy into dense 2D semantic maps, enabling supervision for long-tail categories. Build a dedicated open-pit mine dataset for validation and test on nuScenes.

Result: Extensive experiments on the open-pit mine dataset and nuScenes demonstrate that UnsOcc significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches in 3D semantic occupancy prediction for unstructured scenes.

Conclusion: UnsOcc with RenderFusion and GSRefinement improves robustness of 3D semantic occupancy in unstructured environments, addressing cross-modal alignment and long-tail supervision, and is validated across real-world unstructured data and standard benchmarks.

Abstract: Unstructured scenes present unique challenges for autonomous driving, as irregular obstacles and sparse scene layouts undermine the effectiveness of traditional perception methods such as 3D object detection. 3D semantic occupancy prediction has emerged as a prominent focus due to its ability to provide dense spatial representations by assigning semantic labels to individual voxels in 3D space. However, directly applying 3D semantic occupancy prediction to unstructured scenes remains challenging because scene sparsity hinders effective cross-modal fusion and the more severe long-tail distribution in these scenarios further degrades prediction performance. To validate the effectiveness of our approach, we construct a dedicated dataset of unstructured scenes collected from open-pit mines. Based on this, we propose UnsOcc, a multi-modal 3D semantic occupancy prediction framework that improves robustness in unstructured environments. At its core, we introduce a rendering-based fusion module, RenderFusion, which enhances cross-modal feature alignment through bidirectional rendering supervision. Furthermore, we propose GSRefinement, a detail-aware auxiliary supervision method based on Gaussian Splatting that projects sparse 3D occupancy predictions into dense 2D semantic segmentation maps, enabling effective supervision for long-tail categories. Extensive experiments on both the open-pit mine dataset and the nuScenes dataset demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [68] [AvatarMix: Identity-Preserving Cross-Avatar Composition for Outfit Personalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03506)
*Zhaorong Wang,Yoshihiro Kanamori,Yuki Endo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AvatarMix introduces a compositional 3D avatar workflow that merges two high-fidelity Gaussian avatars (head and body) to preserve outfit quality and identity while avoiding cross-layer artifacts. It uses a two-tier refinement (SeamFix and FullbodyFix) and mesh-based retargeting for robust body reshaping, achieving state-of-the-art results in outfit fidelity and identity preservation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current 3D outfit transfer methods struggle with quality degradation when lifting 2D edits to 3D, or with intersection artifacts when modeling body and clothing separately. A seamless, identity-preserving, artifact-free pipeline is needed for realistic 3D outfit personalization.

Method: AvatarMix directly composes head and body from two high-fidelity Gaussian avatars. It uses SeamFix, a localized diffusion module, to refine hair and neck for a seamless join, and an optional FullbodyFix to restore clothing appearance after body retargeting. All operations act on renders from an already 3D-consistent Gaussian avatar, reducing multi-view artifacts. A mesh-based Gaussian representation enables robust mesh retargeting to reshape the clothed body to the user’s physique.

Result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance in outfit fidelity and identity preservation, suggesting AvatarMix provides a new perspective for realistic 3D outfit personalization.

Conclusion: AvatarMix offers artifact-free joining of head and body while maintaining outfit fidelity and user identity; the two-tier refinement plus robust mesh retargeting enable accurate, 3D-consistent personalization with reduced multi-view artifacts.

Abstract: Existing 3D avatar outfit transfer methods face distinct challenges: approaches that lift 2D edits to 3D often suffer from outfit or identity quality degradation, while those that separately model body and clothing layers are prone to intersection artifacts. We introduce AvatarMix, a compositional paradigm that bypasses these issues by directly composing the head and body from two high-fidelity Gaussian avatars. While this paradigm inherently preserves outfit quality and avoids intersections, it introduces challenges in creating a seamless join and maintaining appearance fidelity after body reshaping. To this end, we propose a two-tier refinement strategy: SeamFix, a localized diffusion module that refines hair and neck to ensure an artifact-free join, and an optional full-body refinement, FullbodyFix, that restores garment appearance when retargeting degrades the clothed body. Both operate on renders from an already 3D-consistent Gaussian avatar, which limits multi-view artifacts compared to 2D-to-3D lifting. To preserve the user's body identity, our mesh-based Gaussian representation enables the adaptation of a robust mesh retargeting technique, precisely reshaping the clothed body to the user's physique and robustly handling diverse body shapes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art results in outfit fidelity and identity preservation, providing a new perspective for realistic 3D outfit personalization. Project page: https://larsph.github.io/avatarmix/

</details>


### [69] [DyaPlex: Full-Duplex Speech-Motion Model for Dyadic Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03874)
*Koki Nagano,Hongyu Liu,Seonwook Park,Tianye Li,Amrita Mazumdar,Christian Jacobsen,Shengze Wang,Michael Stengel,Rajarshi Roy,Ka Chun Cheung,Simon See,Shalini De Mello*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DyaPlex is a streaming, full-duplex dyadic model that jointly generates speech and motion in real time using a dual-tower Transformer, dyadic token interleaving, and time-aligned RoPE, achieving state-of-the-art results on monadic and dyadic interaction benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To model the continuous, reciprocal nature of human interaction by enabling simultaneous perception and generation of speech and motion in a streaming, fully synchronized fashion.

Method: A dual-tower Transformer architecture preserves a frozen base speech model for zero-shot conversational reasoning while adding a streaming motion pathway. A unified dyadic token interleaving mechanism and time-aligned speech-motion RoPE guide cross-attention, aligning autoregressive motion with latent speech features. The model is trained end-to-end on a 4,000-hour Seamless Interaction dataset.

Result: The approach captures cross-speaker dependencies and achieves state-of-the-art performance on both monadic and dyadic human interaction benchmarks.

Conclusion: DyaPlex demonstrates effective synchronization of autoregressive motion with rich latent speech representations, enabling streaming full-duplex multi-modal interaction and scalable dyadic communication.

Abstract: We present DyaPlex, a streaming, full-duplex speech-and-motion model designed for dyadic interaction. To capture the continuous and reciprocal nature of human communication, this full-duplex capability empowers the agent to simultaneously perceive and generate both speech and physical motion in a streaming fashion. At its core, our method leverages the strong priors of a foundational full-duplex speech model and integrates a novel motion pathway, thereby achieving fully synchronized multi-modal interaction. Specifically, we design a dual-tower Transformer architecture that preserves the zero-shot conversational reasoning of a frozen base speech model while constructing a deeply coupled, streaming motion pathway. By introducing a unified dyadic token interleaving mechanism and guiding cross-attention via a time-aligned speech-motion RoPE, our model effectively aligns autoregressive motions with rich latent speech features. Trained on the 4,000-hour Seamless Interaction dataset, our model effectively captures cross-speaker dependencies and establishes new state-of-the-art performance across both monadic and dyadic human interaction benchmarks.

</details>


### [70] [Structure-Guided Mixed Masked Pretraining and Spatial Continuity Regularization for Printed Circuit Board Defect Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03508)
*Peitong Wang,Nuo Wang,Enxin Qin,Chengjin Yu,Hanyu Xuan,Yuanting Yan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-phase PCB defect detector using structure-guided mixed masking pretraining and spatial continuity regularization, achieving state-of-the-art results on DsPCBSD+ (85.5% mAP@0.5 and 52.3% mAP@0.5:0.95).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PCB defects are tiny, low-contrast, and densely embedded in backgrounds. Unlabeled PCB data can be leveraged via pretraining to learn structural priors; fine-tuning with a regularization term enforces spatial coherence for defect localization.

Method: 1) Sparse convolutional masked pretraining with structure-guided mixed masking on unlabeled PCB images to learn PCB structure priors; 2) Fine-tuning on defect detection with a spatial continuity regularization that reduces dispersed predictions for the same defect and promotes compact localization on elongated regions.

Result: The approach yields 85.5% mAP@0.5 and 52.3% mAP@0.5:0.95 on the DsPCBSD+ dataset, outperforming strong baselines. Ablation studies validate the contributions of the pretraining strategy and the regularization term.

Conclusion: A two-phase framework combining structure-guided masked pretraining and spatial continuity regularization effectively enhances PCB defect detection under challenging AOI conditions; the method leverages unlabeled data to learn structural priors and enforces coherent defect localization during fine-tuning.

Abstract: Printed circuit board (PCB) defect detection is an essential part of automated optical inspection (AOI); yet it remains challenging in practice because many defects are tiny, low-contrast, and embedded in dense circuit backgrounds. To address these issues, this paper presents a two-phase PCB defect detection framework that combines structure-guided mixed masked pretraining with spatial continuity regularization. In the pretraining stage, we design a sparse convolutional masked pretraining scheme to exploit unlabeled PCB images, where structure-guided mixed masking is used to construct informative masked inputs. The sparse convolutional reconstruction pipeline suppresses invalid responses from masked regions and enables the detector backbone to infer missing PCB structures from visible conductive patterns, thereby learning PCB structural priors. In the fine-tuning stage, the pretrained backbone is transferred to the downstream defect detection task. For the task, a spatial continuity regularization term is introduced during fine-tuning. This term constrains dispersed positive predictions assigned to the same defect instance and promotes more compact localization on elongated defect regions. Experiments on the DsPCBSD+ dataset show that the proposed method achieves 85.5% mAP0.5 and 52.3% mAP0.5:0.95, outperforming several strong baseline detectors. Ablation studies and qualitative results further confirm the effectiveness of the proposed framework for robust PCB defect detection in industrial AOI scenarios.

</details>


### [71] [EvoMemNav: Efficient Self-Evolving Fine-Grained Memory for Zero-Shot Embodied Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03509)
*Zuhao Ge,Xiaosong Jia,Chao Wu,Yuchen Zhou,Zuxuan Wu,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EvoMemNav proposes a self-evolving, fine-grained memory framework for zero-shot embodied navigation that preserves raw views in a Visual-Semantic Memory Graph (VSMGraph), uses a budgeted coarse-to-fine search for scalable planning, and updates memory priors via reflection after subtasks. It achieves consistent SR/SPL gains and better zero-shot generalization on GOAT-Bench and HM3D across multiple modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detector-centric scene graphs compress observations into sparse nodes, discarding fine-grained visual evidence and accumulating noise, while 3D reconstruction-based methods are computationally prohibitive. There is a need for rich, scalable memory that preserves details to support long-horizon zero-shot navigation.

Method: Construct a Visual-Semantic Memory Graph (VSMGraph) that stores raw views as first-class memory and organizes them with lightweight semantic cues and topological relations into a room-view-object hierarchy. Implement a budgeted coarse-to-fine policy: a coarse stage narrows the search space to promising regions, followed by a fine stage that uses a Vision-Language Model (VLM) for targeted verification and decision. After each subtask, perform reflection-driven write-back to update graph-attached priors encoding accumulated environmental knowledge, enabling better future decisions without retraining.

Result: Empirical evaluation on GOAT-Bench and HM3D across object, text-description, and image-goal tasks shows consistent gains in SR and SPL, improved multi-instance disambiguation, fewer premature stops, and stronger zero-shot generalization.

Conclusion: EvoMemNav delivers an efficient, self-evolving memory framework that preserves fine-grained evidence, scales with memory via a coarse-to-fine policy, and enhances zero-shot embodied navigation performance without retraining.

Abstract: Building memory is essential for long-horizon planning in zero-shot embodied navigation. Detector-centric scene graphs often compress observations into sparse nodes, discarding fine-grained visual evidence and accumulating noise, while 3D reconstruction-based methods remain computationally prohibitive. We present EvoMemNav, an efficient, self-evolving, fine-grained memory framework for zero-shot embodied navigation. EvoMemNav constructs a Visual-Semantic Memory Graph (VSMGraph) that keeps raw views as first-class memory and organizes them with lightweight semantic cues and topological relations into a room-view-object hierarchy, preserving fine-grained details for disambiguation and Stop verification. To scale to growing memory, we introduce a budgeted coarse-to-fine policy: a coarse stage compresses the search space into promising regions, and a fine stage invokes a VLM only for targeted verification and decision. Beyond static memories, EvoMemNav performs reflection-driven write-back after each subtask, updating graph-attached priors that encode accumulated environmental knowledge to refine future decisions without retraining. Experiments on GOAT-Bench and HM3D across object, text-description, and image-goal modalities show consistent gains in SR/SPL, with better multi-instance disambiguation, fewer premature stops, and stronger zero-shot generalization.

</details>


### [72] [Knowledge-Preserved Model Tuning in Null-Space for Robust Spatio-Temporal Video Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03539)
*Haoxuan Chen,Xianqin Liu,Jian-Fang Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Null-Space Tuning (NST) injects learnable residuals into inputs that are selectively invisible to frozen backbones, enabling robust spatio-temporal video grounding across mixed-quality videos while preserving pre-trained knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world videos are often low-quality, and fine-tuning methods can erode pre-trained knowledge. A mechanism is needed to adapt degraded inputs without altering the backbone.

Method: NST uses the geometric property of null-space: residuals added to layer inputs that lie in the null-space do not affect outputs of frozen weights. It combines a Quality-Adaptive Unit and Dual-Space Reparameterization to synthesize residuals that place HQ-specific components in the null-space and LQ restoration components in the non-null space, leaving HQ performance unchanged while correcting LQ inputs.

Result: NST outperforms state-of-the-art methods on a mixed-quality benchmark for spatio-temporal video grounding.

Conclusion: A principled, non-destructive adaptation framework that leverages null-space to rectify degraded inputs while preserving pre-trained knowledge, enhancing performance across HQ and LQ inputs.

Abstract: Spatio-Temporal Video Grounding aims to localize object tubes based on textual queries. While recent methods have achieved remarkable success, they mainly focus on high-quality(HQ) inputs, neglecting the widespread presence of low-quality(LQ) videos in real-world scenarios. Although tuning methods like LoRA can adapt to degraded inputs, they inevitably disrupt pre-trained knowledge. To address this, we propose Null-Space Tuning (NST). This framework exploits the geometric property that adding vectors within the null-space of frozen weights to the layer input does not affect the output. Leveraging this, NST injects learnable residuals into input features that can be selectively invisible to the pre-trained backbone. Specifically, NST combines the Quality-Adaptive Unit and Dual-Space Reparameterization to synthesize these residuals by confining components for HQ inputs to the null-space, while directing restoration components for LQ inputs to the non-null space. As the frozen weights eliminate null-space components, we effectively rectify degraded inputs while preserving pre-trained knowledge for HQ inputs. Extensive experiments show that NST outperforms state-of-the-art methods on our Mixed-Quality benchmark.

</details>


### [73] [Attend to Anything: Foundation Model for Unified Human Attention Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03540)
*Wenzhuo Zhao,Ronghao Xian,Keren Fu,Qijun Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Attend to Anything Model (AAM), a multi-modal foundation model for attention modeling across images, video, and audio-visual tasks, unifying attention via cognitive entailment prompts in hyperbolic space; uses a fluid-dynamics perspective with Fokker-Planck equation for video diffusion; achieves ~6% average gains on 16 benchmarks and ~4x faster video inference; releases dataset and code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current saliency/attention models are fragmented across modalities and tasks, limiting generalization and real-world applicability. A unified, scalable attention model across image, video, and audio-visual data is needed.

Method: Introduce Attend to Anything Model (AAM) that treats attention as a cognitive entailment relationship organized in a general-to-specific hierarchy, implemented via language prompts with hierarchical hyperbolic embeddings. Unify static image and dynamic video attention using a fluid-dynamics perspective, modeling video-frame attention as diffusive temporal evolution governed by the Fokker–Planck equation. Train/validate on multi-modal data to serve as a foundation model for attention tasks; code and dataset will be released.

Result: AAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods by ~6% on average across 16 benchmarks and achieves about a 4× speedup in video inference, demonstrating robust cross-modal generalization and efficiency.

Conclusion: AAM provides a principled foundation for future attention and saliency research, enabling cross-modal generalization and scalable, efficient attention modeling across image, video, and audio-visual tasks; public release will facilitate broader adoption.

Abstract: Existing human attention (saliency) modeling methods persist as highly fragmented across modalities, scenes, and task formulations. Consequently, even with increasing model capacity and data scale, current models predominantly remain scene-dependent and task-specific, failing to practically generalize in real-world applications. To address the fundamental limitations, we present the Attend to Anything Model (AAM), a multi-modal foundation model that unifies attention modeling across various image, video, and audio-visual tasks and scenes. AAM reformulates attention as a cognitive entailment relationship organized in a general-to-specific hierarchy, implemented through language prompts with hierarchical embeddings in hyperbolic space. Furthermore, to unify static image and dynamic video attention, we adopt a fluid-dynamics perspective, formulating video-frame attention as a diffusive temporal evolution governed by the Fokker--Planck equation. Extensive experiments on 16 benchmarks demonstrate that AAM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods by an average of 6\% across various scenarios, while achieving approximately a 4$\times$ speedup in video inference. Overall, these results demonstrate that AAM provides a principled foundation for future research on attention and saliency-related tasks. The dataset and code will be available at https://github.com/wz-zhao/Attend-to-Anything.

</details>


### [74] [\textsc{CR-Seg}: Attention-Guided and CoT-Enhanced Coarse-to-Refined Reasoning Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03564)
*Yifan Cao,Xiaocui Yang,Faxian Wan,Shi Feng,Daling Wang,Yifei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CR-Seg introduces a two-stage coarse-to-refined reasoning segmentation framework. It uses an Extract Attention Maps and Points (EAP) module to localize targets and refine masks via SAM, and a Global-to-Local Chain-of-Thought (GLCoT) to ensure consistent reasoning from global scene context to local details, validated on reasoning segmentation benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle cross-modal alignment between Multimodal LLMs and segmentation models and avoid loss of holistic semantics from explicit spatial prompts, by enabling better integration of visual attention and reasoning.

Method: Two-stage framework: (1) EAP extracts attention maps and informative points to guide SAM-based mask refinement; (2) GLCoT guides reasoning from global scene context to local target details to reduce reasoning-answer inconsistency.

Result: Extensive experiments on reasoning segmentation benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of CR-Seg.

Conclusion: CR-Seg effectively combines attention-guided localization with global-to-local reasoning to improve coarse-to-refined segmentation in vision-language scenarios.

Abstract: Reasoning segmentation aims to segment target objects described by complex language through joint visual-textual reasoning. Existing methods typically rely on either learned semantic tokens to bridge Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) and segmentation models, suffering from difficult cross-modal alignment, or explicit spatial prompts such as bounding boxes, which may lose holistic response semantics. To address these limitations, we propose Attention-Guided and CoT-Enhanced Coarse-to-Refined Reasoning Segmentation, termed CR-Seg, a two-stage framework for coarse-to-refined reasoning segmentation. Specifically, we design an Extract Attention Maps and Points (EAP) module to extract attention maps for coarse target localization and select informative points, both of which are fed into SAM for mask refinement. To alleviate reasoning--answer inconsistency, we further introduce Global-to-Local Chain-of-Thought (GLCoT), which guides the model to reason progressively from global scene context to local target details. Extensive experiments on reasoning segmentation benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of CR-Seg.

</details>


### [75] [Efficient Transformer-Based Localized Patch Sampling for Choroid Plexus Segmentation in Multiple Sclerosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03566)
*Po-Jui Lu,Alessandro Cagol,Mario Ocampo-Pineda,Federico Spagnolo,Marina Mastantuono,Andreea-Alexandra Aldea,Jannis Müller,Özgür Yaldizli,Matthias Weigel,Lester Melie-Garcia,Roberta Magliozzi,Maria Pia Sormani,Ludwig Kappos,Jens Kuhle,Cristina Granziera*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SwinUNETR-based LVCP segmentation with targeted patch sampling achieves higher DSC and far lower computation than UXNET, using multi-modal (MPRAGE+FLAIR) and standalone FLAIR on MS data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual LVCP segmentation is time-consuming; LVCP is a promising imaging biomarker for MS; a robust, efficient automated method across modalities and cohorts is needed for trials and longitudinal studies.

Method: SwinUNETR architecture trained on 32×32×32 voxel patches with targeted intra-/peri-ventricular sampling; compared to 3D UXNET; evaluated on three MS-dominant datasets (Dataset1 n=177; Dataset2 n=177; expanded test set n=388); inputs include MPRAGE+FLAIR and standalone FLAIR; metrics: DSC, HD95, GFLOPs.

Result: On the expanded test set, mean DSC = 0.868 (95% CI: 0.863–0.872) with MPRAGE+FLAIR, vs UXNET DSC = 0.858 (95% CI: 0.853–0.862); p<0.0001. Standalone FLAIR: DSC = 0.863; UXNET HD95 worsened (1.86 vs. 3.00 mm) in spatial localization. Computational load reduced by ~99% (91.8 vs. 22,080 GFLOPs).

Conclusion: Integrating localized patch sampling with SwinUNETR yields accurate, robust LVCP segmentation with substantial computational savings, enabling broad clinical and research deployment.

Abstract: Background: The lateral ventricle choroid plexus (LVCP) is gaining recognition as a key imaging biomarker for multiple sclerosis (MS) related to physical disability and neuroinflammation. Yet, manual segmentation of the LVCP is highly tedious, restricting its use in broad clinical trials and longitudinal assessments.
