This research aims to develop a SwinUNETR-driven pipeline that leverages targeted intra- and peri-ventricular small patch sampling to automatically segment the LVCP in MS from both standalone and multi-modal MRI inputs. Methods: We retrospectively assessed 3T MRI scans across three sets of data stemming from two separate MS-dominant cohorts (Dataset 1: n=177; Dataset 2: n=177; expanded test set: n=388). Our method employed a SwinUNETR architecture trained on 32x32x32 voxel patches, benchmarking it against the 3D UXNET model. The primary metric for evaluation was the Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC), supplemented by computational demand (GFLOPs) and the 95th percentile Hausdorff Distance (HD95). Results: On the extended test set, the SwinUNETR model secured a mean DSC of 0.868 (95% CI: 0.863-0.872) with MPRAGE and FLAIR combined, showing a statistically significant gain over UXNET (DSC: 0.858 [95% CI: 0.853-0.862], p<0.0001). When restricted to standalone FLAIR inputs, the transformer-based approach sustained a high DSC of 0.863, while the spatial localization of UXNET worsened considerably (HD95: 1.86 vs. 3.00 mm). Importantly, the proposed framework lowered computational load by 99% (91.8 vs. 22,080 GFLOPs). By integrating localized patch sampling with a SwinUNETR architecture, this methodology offers an accurate, robust, and statistically superior alternative to current leading models for LVCP segmentation. Its vast reduction in computational cost makes it ideal for widespread implementation in clinical and research environments.

</details>


### [76] [When Attention Collapses: Stage-Aware Visual Token Pruning from Structure to Semantics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03569)
*Jiahui Wang,Kai Zhang,Mai Han,Huanghe Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-stage visual token pruning (Structure-to-Semantics) decouples geometry and semantics: (1) a repulsion-based sampling to maximize spatial/structural diversity, and (2) instruction-aware cross-attention to filter tokens by prompt relevance, reducing redundancy and improving fine-grained alignment while lowering inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLM token pruning largely relies on a single metric (attention scores) that tends to collapse onto semantically similar regions, causing redundancy and loss of contextual diversity. There is a need to preserve geometric coverage while maintaining semantic relevance to the prompt.

Method: Stage 1 uses repulsion-based sampling to maximize spatial/structural diversity among retained tokens. Stage 2 uses instruction-aware cross-attention to filter tokens based on semantic relevance to the prompt, effectively decoupling pruning from pure attention magnitude.

Result: The two-stage framework reduces redundancy from attention-based token selection, improves structural diversity among preserved tokens, and enhances alignment with fine-grained task cues, leading to better performance with lower computational overhead.

Conclusion: Decoupling structure (geometry) and semantics in token pruning via a two-stage process yields richer token sets and better prompt alignment, addressing the limitations of single-metric attention-based pruning.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities but suffer from significant computational overhead during inference. While visual token pruning offers a promising solution, existing methods predominantly rely on initial attention scores. This single-metric paradigm presents a critical flaw: high attention scores inherently collapse onto semantically similar regions, thereby severely reducing feature diversity and discarding vital contextual details. To address this, we introduce Structure-to-Semantics (STS), a novel two-stage visual token pruning framework that explicitly decouples the pruning process. The first stage employs a repulsion-based sampling mechanism to maximize spatial and structural diversity. The second stage leverages instruction-aware cross-attention to precisely filter out prompt-irrelevant tokens. This two-stage synergy constitutes the core of STS, first ensuring geometric coverage and then refining the retained tokens according to semantic relevance. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that STS mitigates the redundancy caused by attention-based selection, improving both structural diversity and fine-grained task alignment of the preserved visual tokens.

</details>


### [77] [Eliciting Complex Spatial Reasoning in MLLMs through Wide-Baseline Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03577)
*Hao Zhong,Muzhi Zhu,Shenyan Zeng,Anzhou Li,Cong Chen,Hua Geng,Duochao Shi,Wentao Ye,Tao Lin,Hao Chen,Chunhua Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReasonMatch-Bench provides a stratified benchmark for wide-baseline matching in multimodal LLMs, paired with a scalable data-generation pipeline from video-3D sources and Dynamic Correspondence Reinforcement Learning (DCRL). It reveals a large human-vs-baseline gap and reports strong gains from DCRL with transfer to related spatial tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of systematic evaluation and training frameworks for wide-baseline matching (WBM) in multimodal LLMs deployed in physical environments, and to push spatial reasoning capabilities under viewpoint changes and occlusions.

Method: (1) ReasonMatch-Bench: a benchmark stratified by viewpoint displacement and matching granularity across indoor, outdoor, and object-centric scenarios. (2) Data-generation pipeline: automatically extracts wide-baseline view pairs from large-scale video-3D corpora (RGB-D videos and SfM reconstructions) to provide diverse and verifiable supervision. (3) Dynamic Correspondence Reinforcement Learning (DCRL): combines Image-Level Viewpoint Progression and Point-Level Correspondence Curriculum to train WBM with verifiable rewards, without explicit chain-of-thought supervision.

Result: On a difficult 90-sample subset, human annotators achieve 84.0 F1, while the best existing baseline achieves 37.2. DCRL substantially improves ReasonMatch-Bench performance and transfers to related spatial benchmarks, while preserving general visual understanding with modest gains on several benchmarks.

Conclusion: The work offers a scalable evaluation and training framework for WBM in multimodal LLMs. The proposed ReasonMatch-Bench and DCRL reduce the gap to human performance, enable cross-task transfer, and maintain general perception capabilities.

Abstract: Wide-baseline matching (WBM) requires integrating geometric understanding, viewpoint changes, fine-grained perception, and occlusion reasoning, making it a challenging testbed for spatial reasoning in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) deployed in physical environments. However, current MLLMs lack systematic evaluation and training frameworks for these capabilities. We introduce ReasonMatch-Bench, a benchmark stratified by viewpoint displacement and matching granularity across indoor, outdoor, and object-centric scenarios, and show that current MLLMs still struggle with fine-grained wide-baseline correspondence: on a difficult 90-sample subset, human annotators achieve 84.0 F1, while the best existing baseline reaches 37.2. To bridge this gap, we build a scalable data-generation pipeline that automatically extracts wide-baseline view pairs from large-scale video-3D corpora, including RGB-D videos and SfM reconstructions, yielding diverse and verifiable supervision. We further propose Dynamic Correspondence Reinforcement Learning (DCRL), which combines Image-Level Viewpoint Progression and Point-Level Correspondence Curriculum to improve WBM training through verifiable rewards without explicit CoT supervision. Extensive experiments show that DCRL substantially improves ReasonMatch-Bench and transfers to related spatial benchmarks, while maintaining general visual understanding performance with modest gains on several benchmarks.

</details>


### [78] [Diffusing in the Right Space: A Systematic Study of Latent Diffusability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03578)
*Tianxiong Zhong,Xingye Tian,Xuebo Wang,Xin Tao,Pengfei Wan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Systematically studies diffusion-friendly latent spaces in latent diffusion models, identifying properties that predict generation quality and introducing Velocity Irreducible Variance (VIV) as a robust predictor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tokenizer reconstruction quality does not reliably indicate downstream generation quality; there is a need to understand latent properties that promote diffusion compatibility across diverse tokenizers and backbones.

Method: Train a large collection of tokenizers with diverse regularization strategies, architectures, and latent configurations. Evaluate them with multiple downstream diffusion backbones. Analyze correlations between latent properties and generation quality. Introduce Velocity Irreducible Variance (VIV) as a measure of velocity ambiguity from trajectory crossings.

Result: Several latent properties consistently correlate with generation quality across settings. VIV emerges as one of the most stable predictors of downstream diffusion quality.

Conclusion: VIV and related latent properties generalize beyond specific tokenizers/backbones, providing a robust metric to forecast diffusion-generation quality and guiding tokenizer design and evaluation.

Abstract: Latent diffusion models leverage visual tokenizers to compress images into latent spaces for efficient generative modeling. However, better reconstruction quality of a tokenizer does not necessarily translate into better generation quality, suggesting that latent representations should be evaluated not only by fidelity but also by their diffusability. Recent studies have proposed diverse explanations for diffusion-friendly latent spaces, including semantic separability, affine equivariance, distribution uniformity, spatial structure, spectral smoothness, and manifold continuity. Yet these properties are often validated on a limited set of tokenizers, leaving it unclear which factors are most predictive of downstream generation quality and whether such conclusions hold beyond the specific settings in which they are introduced. In this work, we conduct a systematic study of latent diffusability by training a large collection of tokenizers with diverse regularization strategies, architectures, and latent configurations, and evaluating them with multiple downstream diffusion backbones. Our analysis identifies several latent properties that consistently correlate with generation quality and exhibit strong generalization across experimental settings. Beyond existing metrics, we introduce Velocity Irreducible Variance (VIV), a measure of velocity ambiguity induced by trajectory crossings. Extensive experiments show that VIV is one of the most stable predictors of generation quality.

</details>


### [79] [World Models Meet Language Models: On the Complementarity of Concrete and Abstract Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03603)
*Yucheng Zhou,Wei Tao,Yiwen Guo,Jianbing Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PF-OPSD enables controlled use of visual future simulations in multimodal LLMs by privileged, on-policy distillation, yielding ~10% gains on two benchmarks; introduces VRQABench and OpenWorldQA; code at GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inherent stochasticity and potential task-irrelevance of visual rollouts from world models when combined with abstract reasoning in MLLMs; seeks to learn when to invoke visual simulations and how to verify/integrate them.

Method: Train a student model with privileged future context using ground-truth future videos and answers as teachers; on-policy self-distillation (PF-OPSD) guided by privileged futures; student deployed without access to true futures; evaluate on two human-verified benchmarks for controllable spatial lookahead and open-domain physical prediction.

Result: PF-OPSD achieves 10.6% and 10.9% improvements on VRQABench and OpenWorldQA respectively over baselines; improved robustness to noisy or conflicting rollouts.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that controlled, privileged supervision can effectively harness visual future simulations for concrete reasoning within MLLMs, enabling more accurate and robust open-world prediction; resources available online.

Abstract: World models and multimodal large language models (MLLMs) provide complementary capabilities for predicting future outcomes from static visual observations. World models can generate concrete visual rollouts of possible futures, while MLLMs can reason abstractly over questions, goals, and rules. However, generated rollouts are stochastic and may be visually plausible but task-incorrect, making it necessary to determine when visual simulation is useful, whether a rollout is credible, and how it should influence the final answer. We formulate this problem as controlled concrete reasoning, where a model learns to invoke, verify, and integrate visual future simulation alongside abstract reasoning. To study this setting, we construct two human-verified benchmarks, VRQABench for controllable spatial lookahead and OpenWorldQA for open-domain physical prediction, and propose Privileged-Future On-Policy Self-Distillation (PF-OPSD). During training, PF-OPSD uses ground-truth future videos and answers only as teacher-side privileged context to evaluate on-policy concrete-reasoning trajectories, while the deployable student never observes true futures at test time. Experimental results show that PF-OPSD outperforms baseline by 10.6% and 10.9% on VRQABench and OpenWorldQA, respectively, while increasing robustness to noisy or conflicting rollouts. Our code and dataset are available at https://github.com/yczhou001/PF-OPSD.

</details>


### [80] [SkelHCC: A Hyperbolic CLIP-Driven Cache Adaptation Framework for Skeleton-based One-Shot Action Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03610)
*Yanan Liu,Anqi Zhu,Jingmin Zhu,Jun Liu,Hossein Rahmani,Mohammed Bennamoun,Farid Boussaid,Dan Xu,Qiuhong Ke*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hyperbolic CLIP-driven skeleton action recognition framework (SkelHCC) for one-shot learning that embeds skeleton sequences and language in a shared hyperbolic space and uses a training-free LM-guided cache for context-aware inference, achieving state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address one-shot skeleton-based action recognition where data is scarce and existing Euclidean embeddings fail to capture the hierarchical, tree-like structure of human skeletons, hindering cross-modal alignment with semantics.

Method: Introduce EH-HCLIP: Explicitly Hierarchical Hyperbolic CLIP that maps skeleton sequences and action-language into a shared hyperbolic space to leverage negative curvature and exponential volume growth for hierarchy encoding; integrate LMV-Cache: training-free, LLM-guided Multi-granularity Voting Cache for context-aware inference to enable rapid adaptation.

Result: Empirical evaluation on NTU RGB+D 60/120 and PKU-MMD shows SkelHCC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in one-shot skeleton-based action recognition.

Conclusion: Hyperbolic geometry effectively models the hierarchical joint-part-body structure and improves cross-modal alignment; the combination with a training-free LM-guided cache enables efficient adaptation in data-scarce regimes.

Abstract: Skeleton-based action recognition aims to understand human behaviors from body joint sequences and is especially challenging in the one-shot setting, where only a single labeled exemplar is available for each novel action. A key challenge is learning representations that capture the hierarchical and compositional structure of human motion while aligning effectively with high-level action semantics under extreme data scarcity. Existing approaches, largely based on Euclidean embeddings and low-level motion cues, struggle to model the tree-like organization of skeleton data, limiting cross-modal alignment and generalization to unseen action categories. We propose SkelHCC, a unified skeleton hyperbolic CLIP-driven cache adaptation framework for one-shot skeleton-based action recognition. SkelHCC introduces an Explicitly Hierarchical Hyperbolic CLIP (EH-HCLIP) module that embeds skeleton sequences and action language into a shared hyperbolic space. By leveraging the negative curvature and exponential volume growth of hyperbolic geometry, EH-HCLIP naturally encodes the joint-part-body hierarchy of human anatomy and yields structurally consistent cross-modal representations. To support efficient one-shot adaptation, SkelHCC further integrates a training-free LLM-guided Multi-granularity Voting Cache (LMV-Cache) for context-aware inference. Experiments on NTU RGB+D 60, NTU RGB+D 120, and PKU-MMD demonstrate that SkelHCC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [81] [TurtleAI: Benchmarking Multimodal Models for Visual Programming in Turtle Graphics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03626)
*Chao Wen,Jacqueline Staub,Adish Singla*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TurtleAI benchmark for education-oriented visual programming with Turtle Graphics; 823 tasks; 20+ VLMs tested; performance below 30%; synthetic data + fine-tuning improves real-world tasks by ~20%; analysis reveals spatial reasoning issues and alignment improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill the gap in education-focused visual programming. Assess vision-language models' ability to perceive geometric patterns, reason about spatial relations, and generate faithful Python code for Turtle Graphics; identify bottlenecks and factors limiting performance.

Method: Create TurtleAI benchmark of 823 tasks; develop a seed-based synthetic data generation technique requiring minimal seed samples; evaluate 20+ VLMs (including GPT-5, GPT-4o, Qwen2-VL-72B); perform failure analysis; fine-tune Qwen2-VL-72B on synthetic data and evaluate on real tasks.

Result: Most models achieve success rates below 30%; fine-tuning Qwen2-VL-72B with synthetic data yields about a 20% absolute improvement on real-world tasks.

Conclusion: Spatial reasoning and precise visual replication are major bottlenecks for VLMs in education-oriented visual programming; fine-tuning improves alignment between visual reasoning and code generation. Data augmentation shows promise for bridging the gap, but substantial improvements in geometric reasoning are needed for broader competence.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have been explored for visual programming, where they generate code to solve visual tasks. However, most prior work focuses on visual programming for productivity; it remains unclear how well current VLMs perform on education-oriented visual programming and what factors limit their performance. To bridge this gap, we introduce TurtleAI, a benchmark containing 823 tasks curated based on real-world visual programming tasks in the Turtle Graphics domain. Solving these tasks requires models to perceive geometric patterns, reason about spatial relationships, and synthesize Python code that faithfully reproduces geometric patterns. We evaluate 20+ VLMs, including GPT-5, GPT-4o, and Qwen2-VL-72B, and find that they struggle significantly, with most achieving success rates below 30%. To address these limitations, we propose a data generation technique that requires only a small set of seed samples. Fine-tuning Qwen2-VL-72B on the resulting synthetic data yields an improvement of about 20% on real-world tasks. Our failure analysis reveals that GPT-4o struggles with spatial reasoning and precise visual replication, whereas fine-tuning primarily improves the alignment between visual reasoning and code implementation.

</details>


### [82] [VidMsg: A Benchmark for Implicit Message Inference in Short Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03635)
*Issar Tzachor,Michael Green,Rami Ben-Ari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VidMsg is a new benchmark for implicit message understanding in short videos. It provides 400 YouTube clips across 9 topics and 52 target messages, focusing on message-oriented retrieval and a diagnostic QA task; baseline VidVec-Msg shown to help but strong models still struggle.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video-language models excel at object/action recognition but often miss underlying messages; there is a need for benchmarks that evaluate pragmatic inference and holistic video understanding for scalable retrieval and recommendation.

Method: A message-first pipeline: LLM translates target messages into indirect search cues to collect candidate clips; human annotators curate clips conveying the intended message non-explicitly; dataset supports bidirectional retrieval and a diagnostic MCQA; includes VidVec-Msg baseline to improve message-oriented retrieval.

Result: State-of-the-art models underperform on VidMsg due to requiring pragmatic reasoning and disambiguation among semantically close messages; VidVec-Msg provides improved retrieval but substantial headroom remains.

Conclusion: VidMsg offers a challenging, practical benchmark for holistic video understanding with potential to enhance video search/recommendation and conversational QA; future work should advance baselines and models capable of nuanced, message-level inference.

Abstract: Understanding short online videos involves more than identifying visible objects and actions; video makers often include an underlying message or purpose in the clip. We introduce VidMsg, a benchmark for evaluating implicit message understanding in short, internet-native video clips. VidMsg contains 400 YouTube-derived clips across 9 practical topic areas and 52 fine-grained target messages, covering domains such as career and finance, education, health and well-being, culture, safety, sustainability, and lifestyle. VidMsg is constructed through a message-first pipeline: an LLM first translates target messages into indirect search scenarios, which are used to retrieve candidate clips. Human annotators then retain clips that convey the intended message without being overly explicit. VidMsg is designed primarily for bidirectional message-clip retrieval for scalable applications such as video search and recommendation, where systems must capture holistic video understanding. In addition to retrieval, VidMsg includes a diagnostic multiple-choice QA benchmark, where models select the intended message of a clip from semantically related alternatives. Experiments with contemporary video-language and retrieval models show that strong models often fail on VidMsg, because the task requires pragmatic inference, integration of contextual cues, and discrimination among semantically close messages. We also introduce VidVec-Msg, a baseline method that improves message-oriented retrieval while leaving substantial headroom for future work.

</details>


### [83] [A Benchmark for Semi-supervised Multi-modal Crowd Counting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03646)
*Haoliang Meng,Xiaopeng Hong,Yabin Wang,Wangmeng Zuo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces the first benchmark for semi-supervised multi-modal crowd counting, with a standardized protocol, baselines, and plans to release code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the lack of a benchmark for semi-supervised multi-modal crowd counting and establish a fair, reproducible evaluation foundation for the task.

Method: Formulates the semi-supervised multi-modal setting, defines a standardized protocol with labeled/unlabeled data partitions across labeled ratios, curates representative baselines (fully supervised multi-modal and semi-supervised single-modal), and evaluates them under the proposed benchmark.

Result: The benchmark and protocol are established with baseline evaluations; the abstract does not report numerical results. Code and data partitions will be released on GitHub.

Conclusion: Provides a foundational benchmark for the task and resources for community adoption.

Abstract: This paper constructs the first benchmark on semi-supervised multi-modal crowd counting. To lay the foundation for this unexplored task, we first formulate the semi-supervised multi-modal setting and a standardized protocol that specifies the labeled-unlabeled data partition across different labeled ratios. Next, to establish solid reference points, we carefully tailor a diverse set of representative baselines, including existing fully supervised multi-modal methods and semi-supervised single-modal methods. Then, we carefully evaluate their performance under our proposed benchmark. Codes and the data partition will be released on https://github.com/HenryCilence/Semi-supervised-Multimodal-Crowd-Counting.

</details>


### [84] [Graph Regularized Non-negative Reduced Biquaternion Matrix Factorization for Color Image Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03654)
*Hailang Wu,Yonghe Liu,Bingxuan Yu,Chaoqian Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GNRBMF extends NRBMF by adding a graph Laplacian regularizer to the reduced biquaternion factorization, improving discriminative power in color image recognition while preserving non-negativity; solved via a component-wise alternating projected gradient method with convergence analysis; shows competitive/superior results in experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NRBMF focuses on reconstruction quality but does not exploit the local geometric (manifold) structure of image data, potentially limiting discriminative feature learning for recognition tasks.

Method: Introduce a graph Laplacian regularizer on the reduced biquaternion coefficient matrix within NRBMF, enforcing that nearby samples in the input space have similar representations in the latent space while maintaining non-negativity in the RB domain; solve the resulting constrained optimization with a component-wise alternating projected gradient algorithm; analyze convergence.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate competitive or superior recognition performance under several tested settings.

Conclusion: Incorporating graph-based local geometry into NRBMF via the regularized coefficient matrix yields improved discriminative capability for color image recognition while preserving non-negativity in the reduced biquaternion framework; the proposed optimization method converges.

Abstract: Non-negative reduced biquaternion matrix factorization (NRBMF) uses the product of reduced biquaternion (RB) matrices to incorporate the non-negativity constraints of color image pixels into the factorization process. However, NRBMF mainly focuses on reconstruction accuracy and does not exploit the local geometric structure of image data, which may limit the discriminative ability of the learned low-dimensional features. To address this issue, we propose a graph regularized non-negative reduced biquaternion matrix factorization (GNRBMF) model for color image recognition. The proposed model incorporates a graph Laplacian regularizer into the reduced biquaternion coefficient matrix, encouraging nearby samples in the original space to have similar representations in the learned feature space. Meanwhile, GNRBMF retains the non-negativity-preserving property of NRBMF in the reduced biquaternion domain. To solve the optimization problem, a component-wise alternating projected gradient algorithm is derived, and its convergence properties are analyzed. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed GNRBMF model achieves competitive or superior recognition performance in some tested settings.

</details>


### [85] [Beyond Single Solution: Multi-Hypothesis Collaborative Deep Unfolding Network for Image Compressive Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03666)
*Wenxue Cui,Hualin Li,Yuhang Qin,Yifu Xu,Xiaopeng Fan,Debin Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Multi-Hypothesis Collaborative Deep Unfolding (MHC-DUN) for compressive sensing, which models multiple plausible solutions jointly via gradient-descent and proximal steps across hypotheses; includes AlphaNet for adaptive step sizes and a multi-hypothesis proximal mapper; uses a composite loss to balance fidelity, diversity, and accuracy; reports improved performance over existing CS networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CS is inherently ill-posed with multiple plausible solutions. Traditional single-solution deep unfolding networks can miss complementary hypotheses. Exploiting multiple hypotheses can improve reconstruction fidelity and robustness.

Method: Extend proximal gradient descent-based unrolling to a multi-hypothesis setting. Gradient descent across multiple hypotheses is guided by AlphaNet, which predicts spatially varying step sizes. Proximal mapping is implemented as a multi-hypothesis collaborative module leveraging intra- and inter-hypothesis correlations. End-to-end training uses a composite loss that balances measurement fidelity, hypothesis diversity, and reconstruction accuracy.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate that MHC-DUN outperforms existing CS networks, indicating the benefit of explicit multi-hypothesis modeling and collaboration between hypotheses.

Conclusion: Modeling and collaborating among multiple hypotheses within a deep unfolding CS framework effectively addresses ill-posedness, yielding improved reconstructions and robustness; the approach is end-to-end trainable and emphasizes diversity alongside fidelity.

Abstract: Recent deep unfolding networks (DUNs) have advanced Compressive Sensing (CS) by effectively integrating iterative optimization with deep learning architectures. However, most CS approaches predominantly confine their inference to a single solution space, neglecting the inherent ill-posedness of CS problems that intrinsically permits multiple plausible candidate hypotheses. In this paper, a novel Multi-Hypothesis Collaborative Deep Unfolding CS Network (MHC-DUN) is proposed, which explicitly models and leverages multiple hypotheses by jointly optimizing across diverse solution spaces. Specifically, following the Proximal Gradient Descent algorithm, MHC-DUN jointly performs gradient descent and proximal mapping within this multi-hypothesis paradigm. i) For gradient descent, a well-designed AlphaNet is introduced to dynamically predict spatially varying step sizes for all hypotheses, enabling collaborative gradient updates across multiple solutions. ii) For proximal operator, a sophisticated multi-hypothesis collaborative proximal mapping module is designed, which leverages both intra-hypothesis and inter-hypothesis correlation priors to jointly refine multiple solutions. To enable end-to-end training, a novel composite loss function is designed, which balances measurement fidelity, hypothesis diversity, and reconstruction accuracy, encouraging exploration of complementary solutions while maintaining reconstruction fidelity. Experimental results reveal that the proposed CS method outperforms existing CS networks.

</details>


### [86] [A Fast Methane Detection Pipeline on Board Satellites Based on Mag1c-SAS and LinkNet](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03675)
*Jonáš Herec,Vít Růžička,Rado Pitoňák,Jan Sedmidubsky*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Ultra-fast onboard methane detection for hyperspectral imagery using fast ACE/CEM detectors and a faster Mag1c-SAS variant, integrated with lightweight ML (U-Net/LinkNet); evaluated on STARCOP and the newly introduced EMIT-MSeg; achieves ~80x speedup, improved detection metrics, and low-power hardware profiling; code and data released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the bottleneck of downlink rates and onboard compute by enabling real-time methane leak detection on resource-limited satellites, reducing missed events and enabling targeted data capture.

Method: Adapt fast target detection methods ACE and CEM for methane, develop Mag1c-SAS as a faster variant of Mag1c, and fuse these with lightweight segmentation networks (U-Net, LinkNet). Evaluate on STARCOP and the open EMIT-MSeg dataset, with two novel band selection strategies. Validate onboard feasibility via hardware profiling and release code/data as open source (PyPI library and GitHub).

Result: Mag1c-SAS achieves ~80x speedup over Mag1c; outputs are visually similar but noisier. When paired with LinkNet, AUPRC on EMIT-MSeg improves by >30 percentage points; F1 on STARCOP improves by ~4 percentage points. Band selection strategies validated. Hardware profiling indicates marginal power usage and efficient CPU/RAM usage. All experimental code, models, and data are released; a PyPI package for onboard-methane-detection is provided.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of fast, low-power onboard methane detection using a hybrid detection plus lightweight ML pipeline, with reproducible open datasets and software to facilitate further research and deployment.

Abstract: Methane is a potent greenhouse gas, and detecting leaks early via hyperspectral satellite imagery can help climate change mitigation efforts. Meanwhile, many existing hyperspectral missions only capture areas manually targeted by operators, thus missing potential events of interest. To overcome slow downlink rates cost-effectively, onboard detection is a viable solution. However, traditional methane detection methods are too computationally demanding for resource-limited onboard hardware. This work accelerates methane detection by focusing on efficient, low-power algorithms. In particular, we test fast target detection ACE and CEM methods that have not been previously used for methane detection and propose Mag1c-SAS -- a significantly faster variant of the current state-of-the-art Mag1c algorithm. To explore their detection potential, we integrate them with a machine learning model based on U-Net and LinkNet. We evaluate our methods on the STARCOP dataset and a novel EMIT-MSeg dataset, which we introduce and open-source alongside a high-quality annotation strategy. The proposed Mag1c-SAS approach proves highly effective by operating ~80x faster than the original Mag1c approach, providing a visually similar, but noisier result. When additionally paired with the lightweight LinkNet approach, it effectively reduces noise, achieving AUPRC score improvements of over 30 pp on EMIT-MSeg compared to the baseline Mag1c approach, and an F1 score on STARCOP ~4 pp higher. We evaluate two novel band selection strategies and confirm the system's onboard viability through hardware profiling, demonstrating marginal power consumption and efficient CPU/RAM utilization. We release the final system in a user-friendly and lightweight PyPI library at: https://pypi.org/project/onboard-methane-detection/, alongside all experimental code, models, and data at: https://github.com/zaitra/methane-filters-benchmark.

</details>


### [87] [Investigating Adversarial Robustness of Multi-modal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03713)
*Hashmat Shadab Malik,Muzammal Naseer,Salman Khan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Systematic study of robustness in multi-modal LLMs; end-to-end multimodal training with robust vision encoders yields substantial gains under adversarial attacks; introduces a diagnostic protocol to predict transferability; test-time transformations and robust backbones further improve robustness; non-robust training can harm; code/weights to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs are vulnerable to visual adversarial perturbations; existing defenses that enforce CLIP-space alignment limit robustness; understand what factors enable robust transfer from vision encoders to multimodal models.

Method: Introduce a diagnostic CLIP-alignment protocol to predict which robust vision encoders transfer well to multimodal settings; train MLLMs end-to-end with robust backbones; apply adversarial training; evaluate with strong visual attacks on captioning (CIDEr) and VQA; test-time stochastic visual transformations; assess toxicity under white-box visual jailbreak.

Result: End-to-end multimodal training with robust encoders yields ~28 CIDEr-point gain on captioning and 11.7% higher VQA accuracy under strong attacks vs constrained baselines; adversarial training on non-robust MLLMs degrades performance; lightweight test-time transformations raise adversarial robustness from near-zero to competitive levels; robust models also reduce toxic generation under visual jailbreak.

Conclusion: Robust visual representations are a prerequisite for strong MLLM robustness; end-to-end robust training provides additional gains; test-time defenses are effective black-box strategies for non-robust models; code and pretrained weights will be released.

Abstract: Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) achieve strong performance on vision-language tasks, but incorporating visual inputs through a vision encoder (e.g., CLIP) substantially expands the attack surface, making these models vulnerable to visual adversarial perturbations. Prior defenses typically preserve compatibility with pretrained MLLMs by enforcing strict alignment to CLIP's original embedding space during adversarial fine-tuning; while practical, this constraint fundamentally limits achievable robustness. We present a systematic investigation of adversarial robustness in MLLMs. We first introduce a diagnostic CLIP-alignment protocol that predicts, prior to full MLLM training, which robust vision encoders will transfer effectively to the multimodal setting, revealing that large-scale multimodal adversarial pretraining, rather than unimodal scale alone, is the critical factor for strong robustness transfer. Integrating such encoders into MLLMs via end-to-end multimodal training yields average gains of 28 CIDEr points on captioning and 11.7% VQA accuracy under strong adversarial attacks compared to constrained plug-and-play baselines. We further show that adversarial training applied directly to a standard non-robust MLLM degrades both clean and adversarial performance, establishing robust visual representations as a strict prerequisite, while end-to-end adversarial training from a robust backbone delivers additional gains of 1.9 CIDEr points and 4.3% VQA accuracy. Beyond training-time defenses, lightweight test-time visual stochastic transformations serve as an effective black-box defense for non-robust MLLMs, elevating adversarial performance from near-zero to levels comparable with robust models. Finally, we show that our robust models substantially reduce toxic generation under white-box visual jailbreak attacks. Code and pretrained weights will be released publicly.

</details>


### [88] [Text-to-Image Models Need Less from Text Encoders Than You Think](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03715)
*Nurit Spingarn,Noa Cohen,Tamar Rott Shaham,Tomer Michaeli*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two key text aspects drive image generation: word meaning and word order. A minimal embedding that preserves word meanings and token positions (but omits full context) yields image outputs as good as full embeddings, suggesting image models perform complex linguistic composition themselves rather than relying on rich prompt context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate which elements of text representations are actually used by text-to-image generation, challenging the assumption that full contextual information in prompts is leveraged by diffusion models.

Method: Develop a text embedding that preserves only individual word meanings and token order (with position tags), removing contextual information across the prompt. Compare this 'bag of position-tagged words' embedding to standard full-text embeddings in diffusion-based image generation, evaluating visual quality and text fidelity.

Result: The restricted embedding achieves image quality and textual fidelity on par with full embeddings, implying that complex linguistic structure is decoded by the image model rather than encoded in the text embedding itself.

Conclusion: Current text encoders may not exploit rich contextual information in prompts; the essential factors are word meaning and word order. This challenges assumptions about prompt handling and suggests rethinking how text representations contribute to generation, with implications for model design and interpretation.

Abstract: Text-to-image models rely on text prompts as their primary interface to human intent. Prompts are encoded by a text encoder into embeddings that condition the image generation process. Beyond individual token meanings, text embeddings encode contextual information across the full prompt, such as compositionality and attribute binding. However, whether image models actually exploit this richer information remains underexplored. Here, we address the question: Which aspects of text representation are essential for image generation? We show that text-to-image diffusion transformer-based models commonly rely only on two relatively straightforward aspects of text representations: (i) the merging of adjacent tokens into a word representation, for words spanning multiple tokens, and (ii) word order, which is imprinted by the positional embedding of the text-encoder. To show this, we construct a new text embedding that encodes only individual word meanings and order but lacks any contextual information about the full prompt. We find that this bag of position-tagged words representation is sufficient to successfully guide image generation, achieving visual quality and text fidelity that are on par with full text embedding-guided generation. This demonstrates that, contrary to common belief, text-to-image models often do not use the rich information encoded in the text embedding beyond individual word meanings and word order. Instead, the decoding of complex linguistic structures is performed by the image model itself. Project webpage: https://nsping13.github.io/contextless-TTI/

</details>


### [89] [Beyond False Stability: High-Noise Drift Gating for Test-Time Adversarial Defenses in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03730)
*Hashmat Shadab Malik,Muzammal Naseer,Salman Khan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A drift-gated, test-time defense for CLIP-like VLMs leverages a noise-regime transition in representations to trigger defenses only when adversarial-like instability is detected, improving the clean-robust trade-off without additional training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the trade-off gap in stochastic test-time defenses for VLMs by exploiting the observed shift from false stability in the weak-noise regime to pronounced instability in the strong-noise regime of adversarial representations.

Method: 1) Characterize CLIP representation behavior under uniform, Gaussian, photometric, and geometric perturbations across datasets; 2) Identify a noise-regime transition where high-noise drift distinguishes adversarial from clean representations; 3) Propose a plug-in drift-gated mechanism that uses high-noise feature drift as a gating signal to selectively trigger existing test-time defenses; 4) Evaluate on 13 datasets, including eight fine-grained datasets and ImageNet variants, comparing against counterattack and noise-anchoring defenses.

Result: The gating approach yields consistent improvements in the clean-robust trade-off: across eight fine-grained datasets, mean clean+adversarial accuracy rises from 65.7% to 71.4% (counterattack) and 68.4% to 73.2% (noise-anchoring); on ImageNet and four shifted variants, from 56.1% to 66.2% (counterattack) and 62.1% to 67.6% (noise-anchoring).

Conclusion: A training-free drift-gated mechanism leveraging high-noise feature drift provides a practical, scalable way to improve robustness with minimal impact on clean accuracy, and the phenomenon is less pronounced in adversarially trained models, suggesting it stems from fragile local-basin geometry in non-robust CLIP representations.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) such as CLIP show strong zero-shot generalization but remain highly vulnerable to adversarial attacks. Adversarial training improves robustness but is computationally expensive, motivating test-time defenses. Recent approaches exploit how CLIP's visual representations respond to stochastic perturbations: aggregating predictions across noisy views, constructing Gaussian noise-averaged anchors and interpolating features toward them, or applying counter-perturbations. These strategies improve robustness but often degrade clean accuracy, yielding an unfavorable clean-robust trade-off. We revisit stochastic test-time defenses and identify an underexplored noise-regime transition in CLIP's representation space. Prior work explored perturbations mainly in the weak-noise regime, where adversarial examples can appear unusually stable (false stability). Our analysis shows this reverses as perturbation strength grows: beyond the weak-noise regime, adversarial representations become markedly more unstable than clean ones, giving a clearer separation signal. The transition is consistent across uniform and Gaussian noise, photometric and geometric transforms, datasets, and diverse attacks. It largely disappears in adversarially trained models, suggesting it is tied to the fragile local-basin geometry of adversarial representations in non-robust CLIP. We propose a training-free, plug-in drift-gated mechanism that uses high-noise feature drift as a lightweight gating signal to trigger existing test-time defenses only when adversarial-like instability is detected. Across 13 datasets it consistently improves the clean-robust trade-off. On eight fine-grained datasets, mean clean+adversarial accuracy rises from 65.7% to 71.4% for counterattack defenses and 68.4% to 73.2% for noise-anchoring; on ImageNet and four shifted variants, from 56.1% to 66.2% and 62.1% to 67.6%.

</details>


### [90] [Qwen-Image-Flash: Beyond Objective Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03746)
*Tianhe Wu,Kun Yan,Zikai Zhou,Lihan Jiang,Jiahao Li,Jie Zhang,Kaiyuan Gao,Ningyuan Tang,Shengming Yin,Xiaoyue Chen,Xiao Xu,Yilei Chen,Yuxiang Chen,Yan Shu,Yixian Xu,Yanran Zhang,Zihao Liu,Zhendong Wang,Zekai Zhang,Deqing Li,Liang Peng,Yi Wang,Jingren Zhou,Chenfei Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Few-step distillation performance is shaped by training recipe factors (data composition, teacher guidance, task mixture); proposed Qwen-Image-Flash; emphasizes holistic pipeline design beyond objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in which prior work emphasized distillation objectives; understand how broader training pipeline factors influence student performance in unified text-to-image generation and instruction-guided image editing, using Qwen-Image-2.0 as a representative case.

Method: Systematic empirical study of three factors in distillation (data composition, teacher guidance, task mixture) applied to unified T2I generation and instruction-guided editing; use of Qwen-Image-2.0 as a representative model; development of Qwen-Image-Flash; analysis of non-obvious behaviors.

Result: Identification of non-obvious behaviors; evidence that training pipeline factors significantly affect few-step distillation; demonstration of effective distillation when data, guidance, and tasks are organized thoughtfully, motivating Qwen-Image-Flash.

Conclusion: Beyond objective design, holistic training pipeline organization is crucial for few-step distillation effectiveness; the approach yields practical improvements and highlights directions for future work in efficient distillation pipelines.

Abstract: Few-step distillation has become an effective strategy for accelerating advanced visual generative models, yet prior work has largely focused on distillation objectives. In this work, we revisit few-step distillation from a complementary perspective, focusing on the training recipe that critically shapes student performance. Using Qwen-Image-2.0 as a representative case, we systematically investigate three factors in unified text-to-image generation and instruction-guided image editing distillation: data composition, teacher guidance, and task mixture. Our empirical analysis reveals several non-obvious behaviors, which motivate the development of Qwen-Image-Flash. Overall, our results suggest that effective few-step distillation requires not only carefully designed objectives, but also principled organization of the broader training pipeline.

</details>


### [91] [Ultralytics YOLO26: Unified Real-Time End-to-End Vision Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03748)
*Glenn Jocher,Jing Qiu,Mengyu Liu,Shuai Lyu,Fatih Cagatay Akyon,Muhammet Esat Kalfaoglu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A unified real-time vision model family, Ultralytics YOLO26, that delivers NMS-free end-to-end inference, lighter heads by removing Distribution Focal Loss, and a coordinated training pipeline (MuSGD, Progressive Loss, STAL) across five scales for detection, segmentation, pose, and oriented detection, achieving leading COCO and LVIS results with fast latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To meet real-time vision demands by improving accuracy, efficiency, and deployability. It addresses limitations of YOLO detectors that rely on non-maximum suppression, heavy detection heads (e.g., Distribution Focal Loss), long training schedules, and poor small-object labeling.

Method: A dual-head architecture enabling NMS-free end-to-end inference; removal of Distribution Focal Loss; training pipeline combining MuSGD optimizer (Muon-SGD hybrid), Progressive Loss (supervision shifts toward the inference-time head), and STAL (label assignment ensuring positive coverage for small objects). Extends with task-specific heads/losses for instance segmentation, pose estimation, and oriented detection; five scales (n/s/m/l/x); single pipeline supporting detection, instance segmentation, pose, classification, and oriented detection; open-vocabulary extension YOLOE-26.

Result: Achieves 40.9–57.5 mAP on COCO at 1.7–11.8 ms T4 TensorRT latency; YOLOE-26x reaches 40.6 AP on LVIS minival under text prompting; demonstrates advancement on accuracy-latency Pareto front for real-time detectors.

Conclusion: YOLO26 provides a unified, deployment-efficient real-time vision family with end-to-end NMS-free inference, lighter heads, and cross-task capabilities, maintaining strong accuracy across scales and enabling open-vocabulary inference.

Abstract: Real-time vision demands models that are accurate, efficient, and simple to deploy across diverse hardware. The YOLO family has become widely deployed for this reason, yet most YOLO detectors still rely on non-maximum suppression at inference, carry heavy detection heads due to Distribution Focal Loss, require long training schedules, and can leave the smallest objects without positive label assignments. We present Ultralytics YOLO26, a unified real-time vision model family that addresses these limitations through coordinated architecture and training advances. YOLO26 uses a dual-head design for native NMS-free end-to-end inference and removes DFL entirely, yielding a lighter head with unconstrained regression range. Its training pipeline combines MuSGD, a hybrid Muon-SGD optimizer adapted from large language model training; Progressive Loss, which shifts supervision toward the inference-time head; and STAL, a label assignment strategy that guarantees positive coverage for small objects. Beyond detection, YOLO26 introduces task-specific head and loss designs for instance segmentation, pose estimation, and oriented detection, producing consistent gains across tasks and scales. The family spans five scales (n/s/m/l/x) and supports detection, instance segmentation, pose estimation, classification, and oriented detection in a single pipeline, with an open-vocabulary extension, YOLOE-26, for text-, visual-, and prompt-free inference. Across all scales, YOLO26 achieves 40.9-57.5 mAP on COCO at 1.7-11.8 ms T4 TensorRT latency, advancing the accuracy-latency Pareto front over prior real-time detectors, while YOLOE-26x reaches 40.6 AP on LVIS minival under text prompting. Code and models are available at https://github.com/ultralytics/ultralytics.

</details>


### [92] [AmbientEye: A Dataset for Pupil Segmentation under Natural Ambient Infrared Illumination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03774)
*Mingyu Han,Hyunyoung Han,Nitheekulawatn Thommakoon,Gangtae Park,Jieun Han,Xucong Zhang,Ian Oakley*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Passive IR eye tracking in outdoor ambient light is viable only with strong performance gaps; AmbientEye introduces a large-scale benchmark showing substantial drop in pupil segmentation accuracy under ambient sunlight (0.767) vs controlled IR (0.928).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the practicality barrier of active IR illumination for outdoor eye tracking and evaluate whether passive IR with sunlight can support reliable pupil detection; provide a large-scale dataset to enable research in ambient-light conditions.

Method: Collect a large outdoor dataset (AmbientEye) with 2,606,225 eye images from 35 participants across 19 countries, using two off-axis cameras under natural sunlight with two sun-orientation conditions; produce high-quality pupil annotations via SAM2 automatic segmentation refined by humans; benchmark a state-of-the-art pupil segmentation algorithm and compare with IR-illuminated controlled baselines.

Result: Pupil segmentation performance drops from 0.928 on controlled IR datasets to 0.767 on AmbientEye, indicating a significant challenge in ambient-light conditions; AmbientEye constitutes a first benchmark for outdoor, ambient-light eye-tracking.

Conclusion: Passive, ambient-light eye tracking is markedly harder than IR-based systems; AmbientEye provides a critical benchmark and dataset to drive development of robust algorithms for outdoor smart-glasses and ambient-intelligence applications.

Abstract: Eye tracking is essential for smart glasses, as it provides insight into user attention for ambient intelligence applications. However, most existing eye-tracking systems rely on active infrared (IR) illumination, creating practical barriers to all-day outdoor use due to power consumption. In this paper, we investigate whether passive IR cameras alone, without any active IR light source, can enable reliable pupil detection in unconstrained outdoor environments, where ambient sunlight serves as the sole illumination source. To support this investigation, we introduce AmbientEye, a large-scale dataset of 2,606,225 eye images collected from 35 participants from 19 countries. It is captured outdoors under natural sunlight with two off-axis camera configurations and two sun-orientation conditions. We provide high-quality pupil annotation through SAM2 automatic segmentation, followed by refinement by human annotators. We benchmark a state-of-the-art pupil segmentation algorithm on our dataset and compare its performance with that on existing datasets under controlled IR illumination. Results reveal a substantial drop in pupil segmentation performance from 0.928 on controlled IR datasets to 0.767 on AmbientEye. This performance gap highlights the challenge of the ambient-light setting. This positions AmbientEye as a first benchmark for an unexplored and highly practical eye-tracking scenario.

</details>


### [93] [SLU-2K: A Question-Based Benchmark for Semantic Evaluation of Sign Language Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03788)
*Zeno Testa,Antonino Furnari,Lorenzo Baraldi,Natalia Díaz-Rodríguez*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes SLU-2K to evaluate semantic understanding in sign-language processing; multimodal models show substantial semantic gaps despite good surface metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current SLT evaluation relies on surface-form metrics (BLEU/ROUGE) that do not measure meaning preservation; the work argues for semantic understanding aligned with assistive tech.

Method: Introduces SLU-2K, a dataset of 2,350 closed-ended video QA pairs derived from PHOENIX-2014T and CSL-Daily, generated via an automated pipeline across 7 semantic categories (actions, locations, numbers, objects, people, time, weather); evaluates Multimodal LLMs and two SOTA SL systems (MMSTL, SpaMo).

Result: MLLMs perform near random; even state-of-the-art SLT systems show a substantial semantic gap (56.7%–75.2% depending on setup); traditional metrics overestimate understanding.

Conclusion: Semantic correctness should be measured alongside fluency and n-gram overlap; SLU-2K and similar benchmarks are needed to drive progress toward true understanding in sign-language systems; code and prompts are released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Sign Language Translation (SLT) is typically evaluated with surface-form metrics such as BLEU and ROUGE, which reward lexical overlap but do not directly measure whether a translation preserves the meaning of the source sign sequence. This is in contrast with the final objective of integrating SLT in assistive technology. In this work, we shift the focus from Sign Language Translation (SLT) to Sign Language Understanding (SLU), with particular emphasis on semantic understanding. Specifically, we evaluate systems based on their ability to correctly recover, from the input video, key semantic aspects of the original sentence, such as actions taking place and facts about people and objects. To enable this evaluation systematically, we propose SLU-2K, a dataset of 2,350 closed-ended video question-answer pairs based on the popular PHOENIX-2014T and CSL-Daily datasets. To obtain SLU-2K, we propose and extensively evaluate an automated data generation pipeline which produces questions across 7 categories, namely actions, locations, numbers, objects, people, time, and weather conditions. We show the potential of SLU-2K by evaluating popular Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) and two representative state-of-the-art systems, MMSTL and SpaMo. Our results show that MLLMs reach near-random performance, highlighting the need for a more systematic integration of SLU in current AI systems. Furthermore, state-of-the-art translation systems carefully fine-tuned on in-domain data still exhibit a substantial semantic gap, with results ranging from 56.7% to 75.2%. These findings suggest that current SLT evaluation protocols overestimate true understanding and that future progress should be measured not only by fluency and n-gram overlap, but also by semantic correctness. Code, prompts, and benchmark files are available at https://github.com/ZenoTsT/SLU-2K

</details>


### [94] [Training-Free Multi-Concept LoRA Composition with Prompt-Aware Weighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03792)
*Georgios Tsoumplekas,Stella Bounareli,Vasileios Argyriou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A prompt-aware, multi-LoRA composition method (W-Switch, W-Composite) that combines multiple LoRA outputs based on prompt-token importance to enable multi-concept customization, plus an image-based evaluation framework; demonstrated improvements on ComposLoRA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Naive multi-LoRA fusion causes concept interference degrading quality and fidelity; need robust method to weight concept influence according to prompt semantics.

Method: Introduce prompt-aware weighting to combine LoRA modules. Two strategies: W-Switch and W-Composite, weighting each LoRA by semantic influence of its trigger words; also propose an image-based similarity evaluation using segmented regions and real-world references; evaluate on ComposLoRA; provide code.

Result: Consistent improvements in visual quality, identity preservation, and compositionality over state-of-the-art; qualitative LLM-based and user studies validate; metrics correlate with subjective assessments.

Conclusion: Promotes simple yet effective multi-concept LoRA composition via prompt-aware weighting; extends evaluation with image-based fidelity metrics; publicly released code; validated on ComposLoRA.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) successfully enables personalization in text-to-image generation by adapting pre-trained diffusion models to specific visual concepts and styles. However, extending such models to multi-concept customization remains challenging. Naively combining multiple LoRA weights or their outputs often leads to interference among concepts, resulting in degraded visual quality and reduced fidelity to the reference images of individual concepts. This paper proposes a simple yet effective approach for multi-concept customization by optimally combining the outputs of multiple LoRA modules. We leverage the relative importance of each concept during generation, as inferred from its corresponding prompt tokens and introduce two methods, W-Switch and W-Composite, that employ a prompt-aware importance weighting strategy in which each LoRA is weighted according to the semantic influence of its trigger words in the target prompt. In addition, we extend existing quantitative evaluation metrics by proposing a new image-based similarity evaluation framework that assesses image fidelity and identity preservation through comparisons between real-world reference images and automatically segmented concept regions from generated images. We evaluate our approach on the ComposLoRA testbed and demonstrate consistent improvements over existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of visual quality, identity preservation and compositionality. Qualitative evaluations, including a Large Language Model (LLM) based assessment and a user study, further validate the effectiveness of the proposed methods and align with the newly introduced quantitative image-based metrics. Our code is available at https://github.com/GeorgeTsoumplekas/Prompt-Aware-Multi-LoRA-Composition.

</details>


### [95] [Beyond Compression: Quantifying Spectral Accessibility in Vision Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03795)
*Akayou A. Kitessa,Yijun Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Examines how vision-language model projections reshape spectral structure of visual representations; introduces Residual Spectral Loss (RSL) to isolate spectral changes from dimensionality reduction; finds non-monotonic spectral accessibility with depth; CLIP is spectrally neutral, DINOv2 shows structured spectral loss via [CLS] pooling; intermediate layers and pooling drive spectral transformation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how learned projection layers in vision-language models alter the frequency-domain structure of visual features, beyond simple dimensionality reduction.

Method: Measure linear recoverability of band-limited Fourier energy from frozen pretrained encoders (CLIP and DINOv2) across ImageNet and MS-COCO; compare to dimension-matched random projection baseline; introduce Residual Spectral Loss (RSL) to quantify changes relative to the baseline; use frozen parameters to avoid optimization confounds.

Result: Spectral accessibility changes non-monotonically with network depth, peaking at intermediate layers. Across architectures and datasets, spectral transformations occur; CLIP’s learned projection is spectrally neutral with changes explained by compression, while DINOv2’s CLS pooling induces a structured spectral loss across the spectrum.

Conclusion: The main drivers of spectral transformation are intermediate layers and pooling mechanisms rather than final embeddings alone, highlighting architecture-specific spectral shaping in modern vision encoders.

Abstract: Vision-language models map visual features into a shared embedding space through learned projection layers, yet it remains unclear how these transformations alter the structure of visual information. This study examines changes in representation through spatial-frequency accessibility, measured by the linear recoverability of band-limited Fourier energy from model representations. To isolate effects beyond dimensionality reduction, we introduce Residual Spectral Loss (RSL), which evaluates changes relative to a dimension-matched random projection baseline. To reduce confounding effects from optimization, the analysis uses pretrained models with all parameters frozen. The experimental results show consistent frequency-dependent changes in accessibility across CLIP and DINOv2 on ImageNet and MS-COCO datasets. Spectral accessibility follows a non-monotonic trajectory across depth, peaking at intermediate layers before decreasing toward the output representation. The final transformation differs across architectures: CLIP's learned projection is spectrally neutral, with changes explained by compression, whereas DINOv2's [CLS] pooling induces a structured loss across the spectrum. These findings identify intermediate layers and pooling mechanisms as primary drivers of spectral transformation in modern vision encoders.

</details>


### [96] [Template Collapse and Information-Theoretic Limits in Camera rPPG Pulse Morphology Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03802)
*Achraf Ben Ahmed*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Single-cycle consumer-rPPG cannot recover individual arterial morphology; cross-subject collapse dominates; SupCon shows no discriminative morphology extraction; VAEs introduce population-level content and partial hallucination; generalization to UBFC indicates limited signal reading.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess recoverability of arterial stiffness biomarkers from consumer-face rPPG and differentiate subject-specific recovery from template collapse.

Method: Evaluate 16 architectures (six families) on 153 subjects across three datasets; use cross-subject Pearson r to separate subject-specific recovery from collapse; evaluate VAEs and anti-collapse objectives; test cross-domain generalization to UBFC.

Result: No architecture recovered subject-specific morphology (cross-subject r range 0.773–0.9999; ground-truth ceiling 0.601). SupCon converged to log N = 4.844, indicating no discriminative morphological structure from single-cycle rPPG by tested encoders. The VAE decoder restores population-level harmonic content absent from rPPG input (H2/H1: 0.310 output vs. 0.275 input); generalises zero-shot to UBFC (r = +0.708); a directional hallucination gap (p = 0.150) suggests partial signal reading. Anti-collapse objectives fail when input carries no discriminative structure.

Conclusion: Consumer cameras cannot encode individual arterial morphology; cross-subject r is a necessary collapse diagnostic for waveform reconstruction benchmarks.

Abstract: Objective: Consumer face camera remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) enables passive cardiovascular monitoring, but whether single-cycle waveform morphology encoding arterial stiffness biomarkers is recoverable from this measurement has not been characterised.
  Methods: We evaluated 16 architectures spanning six families on 153 subjects across three datasets, introducing cross-subject Pearson r to distinguish subject-specific recovery from template collapse.
  Results: No architecture recovered subject-specific morphology (cross-subject r range 0.773--0.9999; ground-truth ceiling 0.601). Supervised Contrastive (SupCon) converged to log N = 4.844, constituting the strongest available empirical evidence that no discriminative morphological structure is extractable from single-cycle rPPG by the encoder families tested. The VAE decoder restores population-level harmonic content absent from the rPPG input (H2/H1: 0.310 output vs. 0.275 input), generalising zero-shot to UBFC (r = +0.708); a directional hallucination gap (p = 0.150) suggests partial signal reading. Anti-collapse objectives fail when input carries no discriminative structure.
  Significance: Consumer cameras cannot encode individual arterial morphology; cross-subject r is a necessary collapse diagnostic for waveform reconstruction benchmarks.

</details>


### [97] [TeX-1500: A Paired Real-World LWIR Hyperspectral Dataset and Benchmark for Temperature-Emissivity-Texture Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03806)
*Cheng Dai,Jiale Lin,Hongyi Xu,Bingxuan Song,Ziyang Xie,Fanglin Bao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TeX-1500: a large-scale paired LWIR HSI-TeX dataset and TeX-UNet baseline for supervised HSI-to-TeX decomposition; enables data-driven, physically grounded thermal property recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of paired LWIR HSI-TeX supervision and the scene-specific nature of existing TeX pipelines, hindering learning-based decomposition across diverse scenes and sensors.

Method: Dataset construction: 1,522 calibrated real-scene pairs from DARPA IH pushbroom imagery and FTIR acquisitions; five locations, four seasons; diverse acquisition times, heterogeneous wavelength layouts, two sensor families. Each sample includes calibrated valid-band radiance cube, calibrated wavelength positions, and aligned temperature, emissivity, and texture supervision via a consistent restoration and TeX-construction protocol. Baseline model TeX-UNet: a simple wavelength-aware network mapping HSI bands and wavelength positions to TeX fields.

Result: Demonstrates that TeX-1500 provides usable paired supervision and a measurable benchmark for data-driven, physics-centered thermal perception. Zero-/few-shot transfer to FTIR scenes shows cross-sensor generalization potential.

Conclusion: TeX-1500 enables supervised HSI-to-TeX decomposition with real-world data across multiple scenes and sensors, offering a practical benchmark and a baseline that paves the way for learning-based, physically interpretable TeX methods.

Abstract: Temperature-emissivity-texture (TeX) decomposition seeks to recover object heat state, material spectral response, and visible-like geometric texture from long-wave infrared hyperspectral imaging (LWIR HSI). Existing TeX pipelines are mainly scene-specific inverse solvers, and the lack of paired LWIR HSI-TeX supervision has limited learning-based decomposition. To address this gap, we introduce TeX-1500, a large-scale paired LWIR HSI-TeX dataset and benchmark for supervised HSI-to-TeX decomposition. TeX-1500 contains 1,522 calibrated real-scene pairs from DARPA Invisible Headlights (DARPA IH) pushbroom imagery and our FTIR acquisitions, covering five locations, four seasons, diverse acquisition times, heterogeneous wavelength layouts, and two sensor families. Each sample stores a calibrated valid-band radiance cube, calibrated wavelength positions, and aligned temperature, emissivity, and texture supervision constructed through a consistent restoration and TeX-construction protocol. We further provide TeX-UNet, a simple wavelength-aware baseline that maps calibrated HSI bands and wavelength positions to TeX fields. Experiments on the held-out DARPA IH pushbroom scenes and zero-/few-shot transfer to FTIR scenes show that TeX-1500 provides usable paired supervision and a measurable benchmark for data-driven physical-property-centered thermal perception.

</details>


### [98] [Conditional Latent Diffusion Model with Fourier-based Motion Modelling for Virtual Population Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03827)
*Shaokun Lan,Haoran Dou,Jinghan Huang,Arezoo Zakeri,Fengming Lin,Zherui Zhou,Jinming Duan,Alejandro F. Frangi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-assisted, 4D mesh generator (4D F-MeshLDM) for dynamic cardiac anatomies that encodes 3D+t meshes via a VAE, models motion with a truncated Fourier series in a diffusion latent space, and conditions synthesis on clinical covariates for controllable, cycle-consistent 3D+t outputs. Evaluated on 5k UK Biobank subjects, it surpasses baselines in anatomy fidelity and preserves functional indices, enabling reliable in-silico cardiac trials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In-silico medical-device trials require realistic, temporally coherent virtual anatomies. Existing mesh generators are mostly static, and sequence models often lack explicit periodicity, hindering faithful 4D cardiac simulations. A method that jointly encodes anatomy, parameterizes motion with a periodic latent representation, and allows covariate-conditioned diffusion-based generation promises improved fidelity, cycle consistency, and controllable synthesis for trials.

Method: A conditional generative framework comprising: (1) a convolutional mesh VAE to encode 3D+t meshes into latent representations; (2) a structural latent space parameterized by a truncated Fourier series to capture motion, with coefficients tokenized for diffusion; (3) a diffusion prior learning the distribution over Fourier coefficient tokens; (4) affine modulation to condition the diffusion process on clinical covariates; sampling tokens and inverse Fourier synthesis yield cycle-consistent latent trajectories, decodable into 3D+t cardiac mesh sequences.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 5,000 UK Biobank subjects shows 4D F-MeshLDM achieves superior anatomical fidelity compared with state-of-the-art baselines and attains near-zero cycle closure error. Generated cohorts preserve clinical functional indices, indicating reliable representation of functional cardiac metrics and potential applicability to in-silico trials.

Conclusion: The framework enables controllable, cycle-consistent 4D cardiac mesh synthesis with preserved functional indices, offering a promising tool for realistic in-silico cardiac trials and the generation of diverse, covariate-conditioned virtual populations.

Abstract: In-silico trials of medical devices require the generation of virtual populations of anatomies. In cardiovascular applications, virtual anatomy is typically represented as a 3D+t mesh sampled from a generative model. However, most existing mesh generators focus on static anatomy, while sequence models often lack explicit periodicity. To this end, we propose 4D F-MeshLDM, a conditional generative framework comprising a convolutional mesh VAE to encode meshes, a structural latent space that parameterises motion using a truncated Fourier series, and a diffusion prior that learns the latent distribution over Fourier coefficient tokens. By conditioning the diffusion process on clinical covariates via affine modulation, we enable controllable synthesis. Sampling tokens and performing inverse Fourier synthesis yield cycle-consistent latent trajectories, which can be decoded into 3D+t cardiac mesh sequences. Experiments on 5,000 UK Biobank subjects demonstrate that 4D F-MeshLDM outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in anatomical fidelity and achieves near-zero cycle closure error. Furthermore, the generated cohorts accurately preserve clinical functional indices, highlighting the potential of our framework for reliable in-silico cardiac trials.

</details>


### [99] [Where Do We (Not) Need Temporal Context in Low-Resource Video Task Adaptation?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03837)
*Luc P. J. Sträter,Hazel Doughty*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Systematic study of how to allocate temporal context across backbone, PEFT modules, and probes for video understanding, showing that temporal distribution matters for performance, especially under data-limited regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video adaptation often confines temporal reasoning to a single component; PEFT and probing offer parameter-efficient routes, but their interaction in video settings and across different task types is underexplored.

Method: Evaluate appearance-focused, motion-focused, and spatially dense settings; test various configurations of distributing temporal context among backbone, PEFT, and probe; emphasize limited-data scenarios; compare PEFT and probing strategies.

Result: Findings indicate that temporal context allocation materially affects performance across settings; PEFT and probing yield gains when temporal reasoning is distributed rather than localized, with strong benefits under data scarcity; the study offers insights and practical guidance on where to allocate temporal context.

Conclusion: Effective video adaptation requires careful temporal-context distribution across model components; the results provide guidance for practitioners on selecting PEFT/probing configurations depending on data regime and task setting.

Abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) and probing enable adaptation of foundation models using only a small number of trainable parameters, making it attractive for video understanding where annotation and computation are expensive. However, video PEFT has focused on adapting image-pretrained models, while standard PEFT methods can also be applied to video representations. These settings are rarely compared and both confine temporal reasoning to a single component of the model, leaving open how temporal context should be distributed across backbone, PEFT and probe. In this work we provide a systematic study of model adaptation strategies for video understanding. We evaluate methods across appearance-focused, motion-focused and spatially dense settings, with a particular focus on scenarios with limited data where parameter-efficiency is most beneficial. Our results provide new insights into PEFT and probing across settings and demonstrate the importance of temporal context allocation for effective video adaptation

</details>


### [100] [Unified Video-Action Joint Denoising for Dexterous Action and Data Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03868)
*Dingrui Wang,YuAn Wang,Jinkun Liu,Yue Zhang,Mattia Piccinini,Yu Sun,Johannes Betz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Donk is a unified video-action denoising model that samples future videos and hand trajectories from language, image, or text inputs, broadening priors from action-conditioned policies to joint video-action modeling and improving dexterous trajectory accuracy and video fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reframe alignment of visual-dynamics priors with executable actions from a distributional perspective, expanding beyond observation-conditioned action policies to model the joint space of interaction videos and hand trajectories under multiple conditioning regimes.

Method: Donk uses a unified video-action denoising architecture. With language, an initial image, and the initial hand state, it samples future videos and bimanual MANO trajectories as actions. Without the image, it samples paired video-action rollouts from a text-conditioned distribution. The same training recipe applies across action, video, and text modalities.

Result: Across action, video, and text-only generation tasks, Donk achieves higher dexterous trajectory accuracy, maintains strong video fidelity, and produces smooth text-conditioned action rollouts.

Conclusion: A unified training framework enables joint video-action generation across modalities, effectively turning the aligned video prior into a versatile data engine for dexterous manipulation policies.

Abstract: Recent world action models leverage video foundation models by aligning broad visual-dynamics priors with executable robot actions. We revisit this alignment from a distributional perspective. Existing formulations typically narrow the aligned prior into an observation-conditioned policy distribution over future actions. In contrast, we keep the distribution broader by modeling the joint space of interaction videos and executable hand trajectories under multiple conditioning regimes. We propose Donk, a unified video-action denoising model for dexterous hands.
