With language, an initial image, and the initial hand state, Donk samples future videos and bimanual MANO trajectories as an action policy. Without the image condition, the same denoising architecture samples paired video-action rollouts from a text-conditioned distribution, turning the aligned video prior into a data engine. Across action, video, and text-only generation evaluations, Donk improves dexterous trajectory accuracy, preserves strong video fidelity, and produces smooth text-conditioned action rollouts under the same unified training recipe.

</details>


### [101] [Visual Instruction Tuning Aligns Modalities through Abstraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03871)
*Luis Palacios,Lorenzo Basile,Diego Doimo,Alberto Cazzaniga*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Visual instruction tuning embeds visual features primarily into the LLM's intermediate layers, treating them as the semantic core; early layers remain devoted to unimodal processing, and intermediate-layer fine-tuning aligns visual and textual semantics efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand where and how visual information is integrated in LLMs during vision-language fine-tuning and how this affects performance and representations across architectures.

Method: Probe layer-wise representations across diverse vision-language models, perform causal interventions (ablation and layer-wise finetuning), compare geometry of visual vs textual representations, and evaluate on a broad set of multimodal benchmarks; test restricting fine-tuning to intermediate layers.

Result: Instruction-tuning behavior centers on embedding visual features into intermediate semantic layers; these layers serve as the semantic core for multimodal processing; fine-tuning extends and strengthens existing abstraction, aligning visual with textual representations; restricting fine-tuning to these layers preserves vision-centric performance and reduces training time.

Conclusion: Multimodal integration is a localized phenomenon that repurposes the LLM’s internal abstraction engine, with intermediate layers sufficing for effective vision-language alignment and efficiency gains.

Abstract: Visual instruction tuning effectively adapts a pre-trained Large Language Model (LLM) to process image information alongside text. Yet, it remains unclear how visual features are embedded into the layer-wise hierarchy of abstractions of the LLM backbone. Across a diverse set of vision-language architectures, we show that instruction tuning primarily serves as a bridge, embedding visual features directly into the intermediate semantic layers of the LLM, bypassing the early layers devoted to unimodal processing. With probing analyses and causal interventions, we show that these intermediate layers are the semantic core of vision-language processing and play a critical role in the performance on a broad set of multimodal benchmarks. In addition, by comparing the geometry of semantically equivalent visual and textual representations, we find that fine-tuning extends and strengthens the existing abstraction phase, aligning visual features with pre-existing textual ones. Finally, we confirm the functional role of this localized alignment by restricting fine-tuning to intermediate layers alone: this strategy preserves the performance of full fine-tuning on vision-centric benchmarks while reducing training time. Our results suggest that multimodal integration is a localized phenomenon driven by the repurposing of the internal abstraction engine of the LLM.

</details>


### [102] [Seg2Track++: Probabilistic Track Validation and Data Association for Multi-Object Tracking and Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03875)
*Diogo Mendonça,Tiago Barros,Cristiano Premebida,Urbano J. Nunes*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Zero-shot MOTS framework Seg2Track++ that fuses SAM2-based segmentation with a new track-management module, using Mask Centroid Distance (MCD) and Confidence-Aware Cost Modulation (CCM) for association and a Bernoulli-filter-based Probabilistic Track Validation (PTV) to suppress ghosts; evaluated on KITTI MOTS with improved identity preservation and reduced false positives without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address unreliable track association and ghost-positive propagation when applying foundation segmentation models (like SAM2) to MOTS, by enabling zero-shot MOTS with stronger temporal consistency.

Method: Combines instance segmentation with SAM2; introduces a track-management module. Tracks are associated via Mask Centroid Distance (MCD) and Confidence-Aware Cost Modulation (CCM). Probabilistic Track Validation (PTV) uses a Bernoulli filter to validate track existence and suppress ghost tracks, enabling zero-shot MOTS without fine-tuning.

Result: On KITTI MOTS, Seg2Track++ yields improved identity preservation, reduced false-positive propagation, and robust track management without fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Seg2Track++ demonstrates that integrating SAM2-based instance segmentation with probabilistic track validation and novel association metrics can achieve robust zero-shot MOTS with enhanced temporal consistency and reduced ghosting.

Abstract: Autonomous systems require robust Multi-Object Tracking and Segmentation (MOTS) to operate reliably in dynamic environments, ensuring consistent object identities and precise mask-level delineation. Foundation models such as SAM2 have shown strong zero-shot generalization for segmentation, but their direct application to MOTS is limited by unreliable track association and false-positive propagation. This work introduces Seg2Track++, a framework that integrates instance segmentation with SAM2 and a novel track management module to perform zero-shot MOTS with enhanced temporal consistency. Tracks are associated using Mask Centroid Distance (MCD) and Confidence-Aware Cost Modulation (CCM), while Probabilistic Track Validation (PTV) employs a Bernoulli filter to validate track existence and suppress ghost tracks. Experimental results on KITTI MOTS demonstrate improved identity preservation, reduced false-positive propagation, and robust track management without fine-tuning.

</details>


### [103] [MLP Splatting: Object-Centric Neural Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03877)
*Shinjeong Kim,Yuzhou Cheng,Xin Kong,Paul H. J. Kelly,Andrew J. Davison*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MLP-Splatting introduces localized MLP-based neural primitives for scene decomposition, enabling photorealistic novel-view synthesis with object-level editing without segmentation masks; optionally supports open-vocabulary interaction via semantic distillation, and delivers significant efficiency gains (memory ~1/15x, rendering ~3x faster) over semantic 3DGS baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable simple, expressive decomposition of scenes into a small set of interpretable primitives for object-level manipulation, addressing the limitations of existing 3D representations (e.g., 3D Gaussian Splatting, NeRF) that lack clean, edit-friendly decomposition or require segmentation.

Method: Represent each primitive as an independent compact MLP with localized spatial support that predicts radiance and opacity. Render via sparse volumetric compositing over ray-primitive interactions. Train using RGB supervision only; optionally apply semantic feature distillation for open-vocabulary scene interaction and open-set instant segmentation.

Result: Primitives align with local scene regions (often corresponding to objects/parts), enabling interactive object-level editing without segmentation masks. Empirical results show substantial efficiency: memory usage ~1/15x and rendering ~3x faster than semantic 3DGS baselines, with open-vocabulary interaction enabled by semantic distillation.

Conclusion: MLP-Splatting provides a scalable, expressive, and efficient neural-primitive representation for 3D scenes that supports easy decomposition and interactive editing, while maintaining photorealism in novel-view synthesis and offering optional semantic augmentation.

Abstract: 3D representations are fundamental to scene rendering, understanding, and interaction. Recent approaches, such as 3D Gaussian Splatting and Neural Radiance Fields, achieve impressive photorealistic novel-view synthesis, but lack the ability to easily decompose scene elements into a few primitives, requiring additional segmentation or grouping for object-level manipulation. We present MLP-Splatting, a method that enables scene decomposition via a few expressive light-field primitives while providing photorealistic novel-view synthesis.
  MLP-Splatting models each primitive as an independent compact MLP with localized spatial support that predicts radiance and opacity. In contrast to low-level Gaussian primitives or a single global radiance field, our neural primitives provide greater expressive capacity while remaining spatially localized. Rendering is performed through efficient sparse volumetric compositing over ray-primitive interactions.
  Our primitives are supervised using RGB supervision alone, which yields primitives that represent local scene regions often corresponding to objects or object parts, enabling interactive object-level editing without segmentation masks by selecting a handful of primitives. Our method, augmented with optional semantic feature distillation, enables open-vocabulary scene interaction and open-set instant segmentation. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, we achieve substantially lower memory usage (1/15$\times$) and faster rendering (3$\times$), as we show in our experiments compared to semantic 3DGS methods. Project Page: https://shinjeongkim.com/mlp-splatting

</details>


### [104] [Beyond Encoder Accumulation: Measuring Encoder Roles in Multi-Encoder VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03879)
*Wei Ding,Yudong Zhang,Ruobing Xie,Xingwu Sun,Jiansheng Chen,Yu Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Systematic retraining of all encoder subsets on Cambrian-1 reveals how encoder capacity and necessity shape joint-training performance, showing that top-Capacity pairings are not always optimal, identifying an anchor-complement strategy, and introducing a pre-projector rank signal for efficiency with five encoders.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how heterogeneous encoders interact under joint training in large vision-language models (LVLMs) and to provide principled design guidance for multi-encoder configurations, addressing methodological gaps in current evaluation that rely on fixed checkpoints or masking.

Method: Retrain and evaluate all 31 non-empty subsets of five common vision encoders on the 16-task Cambrian-1 benchmark using a unified training/evaluation pipeline (~20k GPU-hours). Analyze encoder contributions by (i) full retraining vs fixed-checkpoint masking, (ii) decomposing each encoder’s impact into Capacity (self-score) and Necessity (drop when removed), (iii) assessing per-encoder pre-projector effective rank at fixed parameter counts, and (iv) examining encoder pairings and gains.

Result: Three findings: (1) Encoder rankings under joint training differ from those inferred by masking a fixed checkpoint, including which encoder is top-ranked. (2) Capacity and Necessity are distinct axes; pairing the two highest-Capacity encoders is suboptimal, while pairing a high-Capacity anchor with an adaptive complement matches the full five-encoder model; adding more encoders yields marginal gains. (3) At fixed parameter count, per-encoder pre-projector effective rank explains residual score variation; strongest gains come from an anchor whose rank persists under joint training combined with a complement whose rank expands under it, suggesting higher-rank, less-collapsed projector inputs improve optimization at the encoder-projector interface.

Conclusion: The Capacity–Necessity framework and pre-projector rank analysis reveal a clear design gap for multi-encoder LVLMs and offer actionable primitives: move beyond naive top-Capacity pairings, adopt anchor–complement configurations, and monitor pre-projector rank to predict efficiency and performance gains in joint-training settings.

Abstract: As foundation models scale toward fusing more heterogeneous visual streams, understanding how diverse encoders interact under joint training becomes a prerequisite for principled design. Yet large vision-language models (LVLMs) currently lack the tools to do so, and parameter-efficient encoder configurations remain hard to identify before training. To re-examine encoder roles under joint training, on the 16-benchmark Cambrian-1 suite we retrain and evaluate all 31 non-empty subsets of five common vision encoders under a unified pipeline (~20k GPU-hours total), and report three findings. First, retraining each subset from scratch reveals encoder rankings that differ from those obtained by masking encoders on a fixed checkpoint, including which encoder ranks first overall. Second, we decompose each encoder's contribution into two axes, Capacity, the score an encoder reaches on its own, and Necessity, the drop when it is removed from the full pool. The two axes are not interchangeable. Pairing the two highest-Capacity encoders is suboptimal, while pairing a high-Capacity anchor with an adaptive complement matches the full five-encoder model. Adding further encoders beyond this pair yields only marginal gains. Third, at fixed parameter count, per-encoder pre-projector effective rank explains the residual score variation. The strongest pairs combine an anchor whose rank survives joint training with a complement whose rank expands under it, suggesting that higher-rank, less-collapsed projector inputs correspond to a more favorable optimization regime at the encoder-projector interface. Together, the Capacity-Necessity decomposition and the pre-projector rank analysis, along with comprehensive evaluation through retraining, expose a methodological gap in multi-encoder LVLM design, and offer concrete primitives for closing it.

</details>


### [105] [CoralBay: A Self-Supervised CT Foundation Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03888)
*Ioannis Gatopoulos,Nicolas Känzig,Sebastian Otálora,Fei Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoralBay introduces a 3D self-supervised learning approach for volumetric radiology data, extending DINO with a hierarchical 3D Swin backbone and self-distillation on multi-scale features to learn rich spatial representations; it also provides an open-source 3D radiology leaderboard for standardized benchmarking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 2D pretraining on natural images inadequately models volumetric medical data, which require capturing spatial continuity, anatomy, and CT-specific Hounsfield Unit properties. A volumetric, data-efficient representation learning framework is needed to bridge this gap and improve transfer to radiology tasks.

Method: Extend DINO with a hierarchical 3D Swin Transformer backbone. Apply self-distillation to concatenated multi-scale features to learn representations that encode global semantics and local structure. Train in a self-supervised, data-efficient manner on volumetric CT data. Release an open-source 3D radiology leaderboard within the eva framework to standardize benchmarking across datasets.

Result: CoralBay transfers effectively to a broad set of downstream radiological tasks, exhibiting strong and consistent performance across diverse anatomical targets.

Conclusion: The approach advances 3D representation learning for medical imaging by combining hierarchical 3D transformers with self-distillation, and it contributes a reproducible benchmark to unify evaluation of volumetric representation learning methods.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning has enabled large-scale pre-training on 2D natural images, producing general-purpose visual representations that transfer effectively across tasks. However, many medical imaging modalities, such as CT scans, are inherently three-dimensional and differ fundamentally from natural images in both structure and semantics. Volumetric modalities capture spatial continuity, organ anatomy, and intensity-based tissue properties (e.g., Hounsfield Units), which are not adequately modeled by 2D pre-training. To bridge this gap, we introduce CoralBay, a self-distillation framework that extends DINO by using a hierarchical 3D Swin backbone and applying self-distillation to concatenated multi-scale features, enabling data-efficient self-supervised learning of rich spatial representations that encode both global semantics and fine-grained local structure. As a result, CoralBay transfers effectively to a wide range of downstream radiological tasks, demonstrating strong and consistent performance across diverse anatomical targets. In addition, we contribute to the open-source \eva framework by introducing a public, reproducible 3D radiology leaderboard that unifies multiple datasets and establishes a standardized benchmark for evaluating volumetric representation learning methods.

</details>


### [106] [OVO-S-Bench: A Hierarchical Benchmark for Streaming Spatial Intelligence in Multimodal LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03890)
*Yifei Li,Pengyiang Liu,Yuhang Zang,Zhongyue Shi,Qi Fu,Hongye Hao,Jiwen Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OVO-S-Bench is a fully human-annotated benchmark for streaming spatial intelligence, with 1,680 questions over 348 source videos, designed to evaluate egocentric-to-allocentric spatial reasoning in real-time and diagnose bottlenecks in current models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for benchmarks that assess streaming, spatial reasoning from egocentric streams (not offline full videos or event-level tasks) across multiple abstraction levels, to drive the next generation of streaming spatial MLLMs.

Method: Constructed a fully human-annotated dataset (12 annotators acting also as blind cross-reviewers) totaling ~804 person-hours of QA. Each question has a query timestamp and an evidence interval; evaluation uses only the prefix before the query. Questions span four abstraction levels. Benchmark tested across 38 MLLMs (open-source and proprietary).

Result: Models (e.g., Gemini-3.1-Pro) score 59.2 vs human 86.6 (27-point gap). Allocentric mapping is the dominant bottleneck. Streaming and spatially fine-tuned MLLMs underperform their backbones. Chain-of-thought reasoning worsens spatial errors when not grounded in stream.

Conclusion: OVO-S-Bench provides a challenging, human-annotated testbed that reveals current limitations in streaming spatial intelligence, especially in allocentric mapping and grounded reasoning, guiding future development of next-generation streaming spatial MLLMs.

Abstract: Multimodal agents in robotics, AR, and autonomous driving must reason about places and layouts from continuous egocentric streams, often using evidence outside the current view. Existing benchmarks either evaluate offline over full videos or target events rather than spatial structure. We introduce OVO-S-Bench, a fully human-annotated benchmark for streaming spatial intelligence, comprising 1,680 questions over 348 source videos. Annotation involves 12 trained annotators, each also serving as a blind cross-reviewer, across roughly 804 person-hours of multi-round quality assurance. Each question carries a query timestamp and an evidence interval, and at evaluation, the model sees only the prefix preceding the query. Questions span four levels of increasing abstraction: instantaneous egocentric perception, spatiotemporal context tracking, spatial simulation and reasoning, and allocentric mapping. Across 38 proprietary and open-source MLLMs, Gemini-3.1-Pro trails human experts by 27 points, 59.2 vs. 86.6, with allocentric mapping as the dominant bottleneck. Notably, streaming and spatially fine-tuned MLLMs underperform their own backbones. We further find that chain-of-thought reasoning amplifies spatial errors when ungrounded in the stream. By exposing these limitations, OVO-S-Bench establishes a demanding testbed for next-generation streaming spatial MLLMs.

</details>


### [107] [Electromagnetic Navigation for Femoral Osteotomy Using High-Accuracy X-ray-to-CT Registration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03893)
*Roman Flepp,Arend Nieuwland,Bastian Sigrist,Philipp Fürnstahl,Lilian Calvet,Thomas Dreher*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EMT-based navigation for femoral osteotomies enables real-time, fluoroscopy-free guidance that matches PSI accuracy and reduces radiation exposure compared to free-hand methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate execution of preoperative plans in corrective femoral osteotomies is challenging; free-hand and PSI approaches have high fluoroscopy and invasiveness; a low-exposure, accurate navigation system is needed.

Method: An integrated EMT navigation system that uses CT-based preoperative planning, a one-time intraoperative C-arm calibration, and X-ray-to-CT registration from two fluoroscopic images to enable real-time, fluoroscopy-free navigation of the saw blade and bone fragments for uniplanar and biplanar osteotomies.

Result: In 18 synthetic femora, EMT guidance reduced total angular error to 3.05±0.75° vs 6.32±2.36° for free-hand (p=0.031); no EMT trials exceeded 5°; four outliers among free-hand; EMT was statistically equivalent to PSI for angular (±2°) and translational (±2 mm) errors (p≤0.02, p=0.048); similar user satisfaction.

Conclusion: The EMT system can transfer preoperative plans using only two fluoroscopic images while achieving PSI-equivalent accuracy without extra surgical exposure, supporting further cadaveric and clinical validation.

Abstract: Accurate execution of preoperative plans in corrective femoral osteotomies remains challenging. Current techniques are limited by variable accuracy, invasiveness, and radiation exposure, with free-hand methods and patient-specific instrumentation (PSI) often requiring >30 and >6 fluoroscopic images, respectively. We present an integrated, electromagnetic tracking (EMT)-based navigation system for femoral osteotomies that minimizes dissection and intraoperative fluoroscopy. The system couples CT-based preoperative planning with one-time intraoperative C-arm calibration and accurate X-ray-to-CT registration from two fluoroscopic images acquired at initialization. This enables real-time, fluoroscopy-free EMT navigation of the saw blade and bone fragments relative to the preoperative plan, and is compatible with uniplanar and biplanar osteotomies. In a feasibility study using 18 synthetic femora, EMT guidance significantly outperformed free-hand execution in total angular error ($(3.05 \pm 0.75)^\circ$ vs.\ $(6.32 \pm 2.36)^\circ$, $p=0.031$), assuming the same minimal surgical exposure for both. No EMT-guided trials exceeded the >5° clinical threshold, whereas free-hand produced 4 outliers of 6 trials. The system achieved statistical equivalence ($\pm 2^\circ$, $\pm 2,\text{mm}$) to PSI for total angular ($p \le 0.02$) and total translational ($p=0.048$) errors, with no significant differences in user questionnaire scores. By transferring preoperative plans using only two fluoroscopic images while matching PSI accuracy without additional surgical exposure, the proposed system motivates subsequent cadaveric and clinical validation.

</details>


### [108] [An Attention-Based Denoising Model for Diffusion Weighted Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03903)
*Prithviraj Verma,Pawan Kumar,Chandan Deshani,Prasun Chandra Tripathi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A noise-aware attention-driven denoising framework for diffusion-weighted MRI using hierarchical Swin Transformer with noise-level conditioning and residual reconstruction. It addresses Rician noise from magnitude DWI, achieving PSNR 33.69 dB and SSIM 0.8539 across 1–15% noise, with stable performance under severe noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce scan time in whole-body DWI while preserving image quality; standard CNN denoising struggles with signal-dependent, heteroscedastic Rician noise in magnitude-reconstructed DWI.

Method: Introduce noise-aware attention-driven denoising that combines hierarchical Swin Transformer window attention with transformer-based multi-dimensional gated refinement. Incorporates explicit noise-level conditioning and residual reconstruction to adaptively suppress noise across varying corruption levels.

Result: Experimental evaluation on corrupted DWI scans shows strong restoration performance, with mean PSNR 33.69 dB and SSIM 0.8539 across 1%–15% noise levels; model remains stable under severe noise.

Conclusion: Attention-guided contextual modeling plus channel-adaptive refinement provides a robust, generalizable solution for denoising magnitude-reconstructed DWI under heterogeneous noise conditions.

Abstract: Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) is used for whole-body cancer screening, but it typically requires a long acquisition time. When the scan time is reduced, the image quality often suffers, leading to increased noise in the scans. Magnitude reconstruction in DWI introduces signal-dependent Rician noise, which makes denoising more challenging for conventional convolution-based methods. To address this limitation, we propose a noise-aware attention-driven denoising framework that integrates hierarchical Swin Transformer window attention with transformer-based multi-dimensional gated refinement for DWI restoration. The model incorporates explicit noise-level conditioning and residual reconstruction to enable adaptive suppression of heteroscedastic noise across a wide range of corruption levels. Experimental evaluation on corrupted DWI scans demonstrates strong restoration performance. Our model achieves a mean PSNR of 33.69~dB and SSIM of 0.8539 across noise levels from 1\% to 15\%, while maintaining stable behavior under severe noise conditions. These results indicate that attention-guided contextual modeling combined with channel-adaptive refinement provides a robust and generalizable solution for DWI denoising.

</details>


### [109] [SparseStreet: Sparse Gaussian Splatting for Real-Time Street Scene Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03909)
*Qingpo Wuwu,Xiaobao Wei,Peng Chen,Nan Huang,Zhongyu Zhao,Hao Wang,Ming Lu,Ningning Ma,Shanghang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SparseStreet compresses 3D Gaussian Splatting for street scenes by pruning low-contribution primitives and applying background compression after stabilization; achieves up to 80% compression on Waymo/nuScenes with minimal quality loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting requires many primitives to capture fine details, leading to large storage and slow rendering. Dynamic objects demand high fidelity; static background contains redundancy. A compression framework tailored to street scenes can reduce resources while preserving dynamic accuracy.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) node-based learnable pruning that removes low-contributing Gaussian primitives while preserving visually critical regions; (2) after the scene representation stabilizes, apply background compression to static regions to further reduce redundancy.

Result: Experiments on Waymo and nuScenes show up to 80% compression ratio with minimal quality degradation, preserving dynamic object geometry/appearance and enabling resource-efficient high-fidelity dynamic scene reconstruction.

Conclusion: SparseStreet effectively reduces primitive count and storage without sacrificing dynamic object fidelity, enabling efficient street-scene reconstruction; potential for broader applicability and further improvements in dynamic/static separation and pruning strategies.

Abstract: While 3D Gaussian Splatting has shown promising results in street scene reconstruction, existing methods require massive numbers of Gaussian primitives to capture fine details, leading to prohibitive storage costs and slow rendering speeds. We observe that dynamic objects (e.g., vehicles and pedestrians) demand high-fidelity representations to maintain temporal consistency, while static background regions often contain substantial redundancy. Motivated by this, we propose SparseStreet, a general compression framework specifically designed for street scenes. First, we introduce a node-based learnable pruning strategy that systematically removes low-contributing Gaussian primitives while preserving visually critical regions. Second, after the scene representation stabilizes, we apply background compression, further reducing redundancy in static regions. Our method effectively preserves the geometry and appearance of dynamic objects while significantly reducing the total number of Gaussian primitives. Extensive experiments on the Waymo and nuScenes demonstrate that SparseStreet achieves up to 80% compression ratio with minimal quality degradation, enabling resource-efficient, high-fidelity dynamic scene reconstruction. Project website: https://sparsestreet.github.io/.

</details>


### [110] [Bootstrap Your Generator: Unpaired Visual Editing with Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03911)
*Yoad Tewel,Yuval Atzmon,Gal Chechik,Lior Wolf*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Bootstrap Your Generator (ByG) enables unpaired training of image/video editing models by leveraging a frozen base model to provide instruction cues and using cycle-consistency, with gradient routing from clean to noisy states. It achieves state-of-the-art results in data-scarce settings and generalizes to unseen domains without external reward models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Paired data for image/video editing is expensive and scarce, especially for video; there is a need for scalable, unpaired training methods that leverage existing models.

Method: ByG pairs instruction-following cues extracted from a frozen base model with cycle-consistency losses for structure preservation. It routes gradients from downstream losses over clean predictions to noisy training states to manage the train-inference gap, avoiding reliance on external reward models.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on challenging data-scarce image and video editing tasks; strong generalization to unseen domains; user studies and extensive evaluations show superiority over supervised baselines trained on large datasets.

Conclusion: Gradient routing bridges the train–inference gap; semantic cues from a base model provide robust supervision without external rewards, enabling effective unpaired training of editing models.

Abstract: Modern generative models possess a deep understanding of visual content, yet training them for image editing typically requires massive datasets of paired examples. This limits scalability, especially for video editing where collecting paired data is prohibitively expensive. We propose Bootstrap Your Generator (ByG), a general framework for unpaired training of flow matching editing models. It leverages the base model's knowledge without any external signal. Our approach pairs instruction-following cues extracted from the frozen model with cycle-consistency for structure preservation. To make this tractable, we propose to route gradients from downstream losses over clean predictions to noisy training states. We demonstrate state-of-the-art results on challenging data-scarce image and video editing scenarios. Extensive evaluations and user studies show that our method effectively generalizes to unseen domains and outperforms supervised baselines trained on millions of samples. Analysis reveals that our gradient routing bridges the train-inference gap, and extracting semantic cues from a base model provides a robust training signal that obviates the need for external reward models.

</details>


### [111] [PatchScene: Patch-based Voxel Diffusion for Large-Scale Scene Completion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03915)
*Qingdong Xu,Jiajun Zhu,Shilin Zhu,Xinjing He,Chao Lu,Huanran Wang,Jiyao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PatchScene introduces a patch-based diffusion framework for large-scale LiDAR completion, outperforming prior global-grid methods by modeling local geometry with overlapping patches and a radial Annular-Flow to propagate details from near to far ranges, achieving state-of-the-art results and strong generalization (20m to 50m) on SemanticKITTI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LiDAR completion methods rely on global latent representations or dense voxel grids, which can hinder fine-grained geometry and scalability. There is a need for localized, coherent, temporally-consistent reconstruction that scales to large scenes and varying sensing ranges.

Method: PatchScene uses a patch-based voxel diffusion paradigm to generate fine-grained geometry within localized 3D regions. A confidence-guided spatio-temporal fusion integrates overlapping patches and adjacent frames in a unified generative process. An Annular-Flow diffusion strategy leverages the radial density pattern of LiDAR scans to progressively propagate high-fidelity information from near-range to far-range regions, enabling spatially unbounded scene completion.

Result: Experiments on SemanticKITTI show PatchScene achieves state-of-the-art performance across standard metrics, with improvements in geometric accuracy and temporal consistency. A model trained on 20 m LiDAR ranges generalizes effectively to 50 m scenes without retraining, indicating strong scalability and generalization for real-world autonomous driving.

Conclusion: PatchScene demonstrates scalable, high-fidelity large-scale LiDAR completion with strong generalization and temporal coherence. Patch-based diffusion and Annular-Flow enable spatially unbounded completion, making it practical for real-world autonomous driving applications.

Abstract: We propose PatchScene, a novel diffusion-based framework for large-scale LiDAR scene completion. Unlike existing methods that rely on global latent representations or dense voxel grids, PatchScene adopts a patch-based voxel diffusion paradigm that explicitly generates fine-grained geometry within localized 3D regions. To ensure coherent reconstruction at both spatial and temporal scales, we introduce a confidence-guided spatio-temporal fusion mechanism that integrates overlapping patches and adjacent frames in a unified generative process. Furthermore, we design an Annular-Flow diffusion strategy that leverages the radial density pattern of LiDAR scans to progressively propagate high-fidelity information from near-range to far-range regions, enabling spatially unbounded scene completion. Extensive experiments on the SemanticKITTI benchmark demonstrate that PatchScene achieves state-of-the-art performance across all standard metrics, surpassing previous approaches in both geometric accuracy and temporal consistency. Remarkably, the model trained on 20 m LiDAR ranges generalizes effectively to 50 m scenes without retraining, highlighting its strong scalability and generalization capability for real-world autonomous driving applications.

</details>


### [112] [Benchmarking Visual State Tracking in Multimodal Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03920)
*Sihyun Yu,Nanye Ma,Pinzhi Huang,Hyunseok Lee,Shusheng Yang,June Suk Choi,Ellis Brown,Oscar Michel,Boyang Zheng,Jinwoo Shin,Saining Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces VSTAT, a video benchmark for diagnosing visual state tracking in MLLMs. Models lag far behind humans, performing only slightly better than answer-prior baselines; text reasoning is sound but visual perception is weak; recent agentic approaches do not resolve the gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual state tracking (continuous perception of entities, states, and events over time) is central to video understanding but is underexplored in Multimodal Large Language Models. Existing benchmarks rely on single frames or short segments, failing to gauge continuous integration across entire video streams.

Method: Propose Visual STAte Tracking benchmark (VSTAT): 834 clips from synthetic and real-world videos with 1,500 questions that require across-video integration and cannot be answered from a single frame. Compare MLLMs to humans and answer-prior baselines; analyze 'thinking traces' by aligning model outputs with the actual video stream; evaluate whether agentic approaches (MLLM-based video agents, coding agents) alleviate the failures.

Result: Across the benchmark, current state-of-the-art MLLMs perform far below humans and only modestly above answer-prior baselines. They reason correctly in text but fail at visually perceiving events they need to track. Preliminary agentic approaches do not readily resolve these failures and still fall short on VSTAT.

Conclusion: VSTAT reveals a persistent gap in visual state tracking for MLLMs, highlighting a need to improve visual perception and grounding of temporal events. Agentic strategies alone do not close the gap, suggesting future work should focus on perceptual integration with reasoning and new evaluation paradigms.

Abstract: Understanding a video requires more than recognizing isolated moments, as humans continuously track entities, states, and events over time. This capacity for visual state tracking is fundamental to video understanding, yet remains underexplored in current evaluations of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). We introduce Visual STAte Tracking benchmark (VSTAT), a video-based benchmark designed to diagnose visual state tracking in MLLMs. VSTAT consists of 834 clips drawn from both synthetic and real-world videos, paired with 1,500 questions that cannot be answered from any single frame or short segment, requiring continuous perception and integration of events across the entire video stream. Despite their strong performance on existing video benchmarks, we find that state-of-the-art MLLMs perform far below humans and only modestly above answer-prior baselines. To analyze this gap, we compare MLLMs' thinking traces with the underlying video stream to understand why and when MLLMs fail on VSTAT. We find that MLLMs reason and track correctly in text, but fail at visually perceiving the events they need to track. Finally, our preliminary evaluation suggests that recent agentic approaches, including MLLM-based video agents and coding agents, do not readily resolve these failures, still falling short on VSTAT.

</details>


### [113] [GARDEN: Gravity-Aligned Reconstruction of Disentangled ENvironments from RGB images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03921)
*Jiahao Sun,Dingkun Wei,Zehong Shen,Hongyu Zhou,Yujun Shen,Liang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GARDEN is an RGB-only framework that converts multi-view reconstructions into a structured hybrid scene with explicit rigid bodies and a decoupled background by gravity-guided alignment and object-centric mesh recovery, enabling stable physics simulation without CAD retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RGB-based reconstruction pipelines yield monolithic, gauge-ambiguous scenes with entangled foreground/background, which hampers reliable physics interactions and simulation-ready accuracy.

Method: Align the reconstruction to a Gravity-View frame to resolve gauge ambiguity; extract object-centric rigid meshes with accurate 6-DoF placements; remove duplicate geometry from the background via conditional 3D point classification to produce a hybrid scene representation combining explicit rigid bodies with a decoupled background.

Result: Experiments on simulated and real multi-view scenes show improved object placement reliability, disentanglement quality, and rendering-simulation efficiency compared with retrieval-based baselines.

Conclusion: GARDEN enables direct physics simulation with explicit rigid bodies while preserving visual realism, addressing the fidelity gap in RGB-only reconstructions.

Abstract: Converting multi-view RGB observations into simulation-ready 3D environments remains challenging because current reconstruction pipelines produce monolithic scene representations without explicit physical structure. They are typically defined up to an arbitrary global rotation and entangle rigid foreground objects with background geometry, which hinders stable physical interaction. Existing solutions often recover interactivity by replacing reconstructed objects with retrieved CAD assets, but this introduces a slow retrieval-and-replacement stage and weakens scene-specific geometric fidelity. We propose GARDEN, an RGB-only framework that reformulates reconstruction as physically-grounded scene factorization and outputs a structured hybrid scene representation. The key idea is to use gravity as a universal physical prior: we first align the reconstruction to a unified Gravity-View frame to resolve gauge ambiguity, then recover object-centric rigid meshes with accurate 6-DoF placement, and finally remove duplicate object geometry from the background through conditional 3D point classification. The resulting representation combines explicit rigid bodies with a decoupled background, enabling direct physics simulation while preserving visual realism. Experiments on both simulated and real multi-view scenes show that GARDEN improves object placement reliability, disentanglement quality, and rendering-simulation efficiency compared with retrieval-based baselines.

</details>


### [114] [Adaptive Causal Alignment for High-Confidence Adversarial Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03925)
*Zhiming Luo,Kejia Zhang,Yingxin Lai,Junwei Wu,Juanjuan Weng,Shaozi Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HICAT proposes a semantic-aligned training framework to disentangle object semantics from contextual biases in high-confidence predictions. It introduces a Learnable Background-Bias Estimator (LBBE) within a Measure-Debias-Align pipeline, using adaptive logit rectification and a FLOE loss to improve robust generalization across CNNs and ViTs on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet-1K.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-confidence predictions in inverse adversarial training can reflect overfitting to non-causal background correlations rather than intrinsic object semantics. Visual context can be either a necessary prior or a spurious confounder, undermining existing suppression strategies and causing feature loss.

Method: Measure-Debias-Align pipeline with a Learnable Background-Bias Estimator (LBBE) to diagnose context utility; Adaptive Debiasing for logit rectification; Foreground Logit Orthogonal Enhancement (FLOE) loss for feature disentanglement; evaluated on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet-1K across CNNs and ViTs.

Result: HICAT consistently improves over matched baselines across architectures and reduces the robust generalization gap on the tested datasets.

Conclusion: Semantic Equilibrium between background context and object semantics can be achieved by adaptive diagnosis and selective debiasing, yielding better robust generalization while mitigating feature loss.

Abstract: Inverse adversarial training leverages high-confidence predictions to stabilize robust learning, yet we uncover a critical paradox: high confidence often stems from overfitting to non-causal background correlations rather than intrinsic object semantics. Our investigation reveals that visual context functions as a dual-natured signal, serving as either a necessary supportive prior or a spurious confounder. This insight renders existing blind suppression strategies flawed, as they inevitably lead to severe Feature Loss. To resolve this, we propose High-Confidence Causally Aligned Training (HICAT), a unified framework that establishes a Semantic Equilibrium. Operating on a ``Measure-Debias-Align'' pipeline, HICAT integrates a Learnable Background-Bias Estimator (LBBE) to adaptively diagnose context utility. Guided by this diagnosis, an Adaptive Debiasing mechanism performs surgical logit rectification, complemented by a geometrically grounded Foreground Logit Orthogonal Enhancement (FLOE) loss to enforce rigorous feature disentanglement. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet-1K demonstrate that HICAT consistently improves over matched baselines across diverse architectures (CNNs and ViTs) while significantly reducing the robust generalization gap.

</details>


### [115] [Demo2Tutorial: From Human Experience to Multimodal Software Tutorials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03951)
*Zechen Bai,Zhiheng Chen,Yiqi Lin,Kevin Qinghong Lin,Difei Gao,Xiangwu Guo,Xin Wang,Mike Zheng Shou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Demo2Tutorial converts authentic human-computer interaction data (screen records and logs) into structured, multimodal tutorials via a four-stage pipeline (Recorder, Action Parser, Step Planner, Tutorial Composer) to support both human learning and GUI agent learning; results show high-quality tutorials that outperform baselines and human-authored content, enabling faster task completion and better GUI planning; code and data will be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exploit rich, untrimmed human interaction traces as a source of procedural knowledge for teaching both humans and autonomous agents, addressing gaps in curated tutorial data.

Method: Collect human experience with a dedicated recorder; parse with a multimodal Action Parser to recover perception, action, and intent; abstract into hierarchical task graphs via a Step Planner; generate reusable image-text tutorials via a Tutorial Composer; evaluate on a benchmark derived from official software documentation; release code and data.

Result: High-quality tutorials that surpass human-authored and baseline methods; demonstrations of faster human task completion and improved GUI agent planning/generalization; empirical evidence on dual benefits for humans and agents.

Conclusion: Structured tutorials distilled from authentic human experience are effective knowledge representations that advance both human learning and agent capabilities in GUI tasks; potential for broad applicability and future improvements through richer modalities and benchmarks.

Abstract: Human experience in digital environments offers a vast, underexplored resource of authentic, untrimmed interactions that contain rich procedural knowledge. We introduce Demo2Tutorial, a framework that transforms this experience captured via screen recordings and interaction logs into structured, multimodal software tutorials for teaching both humans and agents. Demo2Tutorial first collects human experience via a dedicated recorder, then parses raw experience using a multimodal Action Parser to reconstruct perception, action, and intent. A Step Planner then abstracts these steps into hierarchical task graphs representing goals and steps. Finally, a Tutorial Composer transforms the parsed experience into structured, reusable image-text instructions. We evaluate the tutorial generation quality on a new benchmark derived from official software documentation. We further demonstrate that this distilled representation benefits (i) human learning, by automatically generating multimodal tutorials, and (ii) agent learning, by improving downstream GUI-agent planning and generalization. Experiments show Demo2Tutorial produces high-quality tutorials that surpass human-authored ones and significantly outperform baseline methods, while enabling both faster human task completion and improved GUI agent planning, demonstrating that structured tutorials distilled from human experience can serve as effective knowledge representations for advancing both human learning and agent capabilities. Code and data will be available at https://github.com/showlab/Demo2Tutorial.

</details>


### [116] [VLESA: Vision-Language Embodied Safety Agent for Human Activity Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03954)
*Hanjiang Hu,Yiyuan Pan,Jiaxing Li,Xusheng Luo,Alexander Robey,Na Li,Yebin Wang,Changliu Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A vision-language embodied safety agent (VLESA) monitors egocentric video to detect dangerous actions and intervene in real time by modeling intent-conditioned safety via a GRPO-trained Q-filter and an intent-action predictor, achieving state-of-the-art intervention accuracy on ASIMOV-2.0 and substantial safety gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety in AI-assisted physical tasks requires context-aware decisions; identical actions can be safe or dangerous depending on user intent and context. The work aims to provide real-time, goal-conditioned safety oversight using visual and language signals without retraining for each new intent.

Method: Propose VLESA framework: (1) dataset pairing egocentric frames with goal-conditioned safety annotations; (2) GRPO-based Q-filter to evaluate actions against inferred intent; (3) an intent-action prediction agent to jointly infer goals and forecast future actions from video; (4) real-time intervention at the precise ground-truth frame; (5) goal-conditioned constrained decoding to improve safety; (6) evaluation on ASIMOV-2.0; (7) release code.

Result: Interventions are more accurate at exact ground-truth frame than baselines; GRPO Q-filter improves action safety by >41 percentage points; code released.

Conclusion: VLESA demonstrates effective real-time, intent-aware safety monitoring for physical tasks; introducing an annotated dataset and a GRPO-based safety filter enables robust, goal-conditioned intervention; potential for deployment and extension to other safety-critical domains.

Abstract: As AI systems increasingly assist humans in physical tasks, ensuring safety becomes paramount -- physical actions carry immediate and irreversible consequences that digital errors do not. We introduce the Vision-Language Embodied Safety Agent (VLESA), a framework that monitors human activities from egocentric video and triggers real-time safety interventions when dangerous actions are predicted. VLESA addresses intent-dependent safety where identical actions can be safe or dangerous depending on context. A dataset pairing egocentric frames with goal-conditioned safety annotations is introduced, enabling a goal-conditioned safety Q-filter trained via GRPO that evaluates actions with respect to inferred intent without retraining. On top of that, an intent-action prediction agent is proposed to jointly infer goals and predict future actions from video. On the ASIMOV-2.0 benchmark, VLESA achieves higher intervention accuracy at the exact ground-truth frame compared to baselines, while the GRPO-trained Q-filter improves action safety by over 41 percentage points through goal-conditioned constrained decoding. Code is available at https://github.com/HanjiangHu/VLESA.

</details>


### [117] [Video-Mirai: Autoregressive Video Diffusion Models Need Foresight](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03971)
*Yonghao Yu,Lang Huang,Runyi Li,Zerun Wang,Toshihiko Yamasaki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Video-Mirai is a training-only method that injects foresight supervision into causal video diffusion, closing the representation-level planning gap without altering inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal video generators trained on past data risk learning representations that fit the present but discard cues needed for future consistency; a foresight-based supervisory signal is proposed to bridge this gap.

Method: During training, roll out causally, read the finished rollout with a frozen foresight encoder in a non-causal manner, and distill the resulting stopped-gradient targets into the causal states via a lightweight predictor; future frames supervise representations (not generator inputs); at inference, the encoder/predictor are discarded, leaving the original architecture and per-step computational behavior.

Result: On 5-second VBench, Total Score improves from 83.8 to 84.6. On 30-second rollouts, subject consistency improves from 84.9 to 88.5 and background consistency from 90.2 to 91.9. Ablations show future-conditioned targets are key; probes indicate future frames become more decodable from current features.

Conclusion: Causality should constrain inference, not representation supervision. Visual autoregressive models benefit from foresight during training to produce more consistent long-horizon video.

Abstract: Causal video generators must predict from the past, but they need not learn only from it. In streaming autoregressive video diffusion, each emitted segment becomes a commitment that future segments must preserve. Standard training, however, only asks each causal state to explain the present. This creates what we call a representation-level planning gap: states that fit the current segment may discard identity, layout, and motion information needed for a consistent future. We introduce Video-Mirai, a training-only method that closes this gap without changing causal inference: the generator rolls out causally, a frozen foresight encoder reads the completed rollout non-causally, and a lightweight predictor distills the resulting stopped-gradient targets into causal states. Future frames supervise representations, never generator inputs. At inference, the encoder and predictor are discarded, leaving the original architecture, per-step FLOPs, and KV-cache behavior unchanged. Video-Mirai improves a strong Causal-Forcing baseline on 5-second VBench from 83.8 to 84.6 in terms of Total Score. On 30-second rollouts beyond the training horizon, subject consistency improves from 84.9 to 88.5 and background consistency from 90.2 to 91.9. Ablations identify future-conditioned targets as the key ingredient, and probes show that future frames become more decodable from current features. Causality should constrain inference, not representation supervision. Our study highlights that visual autoregressive models need foresight. Project page: https://y0uroy.github.io/Video-Mirai.

</details>


### [118] [AAD-1: Asymmetric Adversarial Distillation for One-Step Autoregressive Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03972)
*Haobo Li,Yanhong Zeng,Yunhong Lu,Jiapeng Zhu,Hao Ouyang,Qiuyu Wang,Ka Leong Cheng,Yujun Shen,Zhipeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AAD-1 is an asymmetric adversarial distillation framework for one-step autoregressive video generation that uses a unidirectional generator and a bidirectional discriminator with a phased distillation training, achieving state-of-the-art results on VBench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing adversarial distillation methods for autoregressive video generation struggle with motion collapse and training instability, producing static videos; a more expressive, stable framework is needed.

Method: Introduce architectural asymmetry: a causal generator for autoregressive sampling and a bidirectional, full-context discriminator that produces a single realism score for the entire video. Employ a phased training strategy: first perform distribution matching to bootstrap a stable one-step generator, then start adversarial distillation.

Result: Empirical evaluation on VBench shows state-of-the-art performance for one-step autoregressive video generation.

Conclusion: Architectural asymmetry combined with a staged training regimen mitigates motion collapse and stabilizes training, enabling higher-quality one-step autoregressive video generation.

Abstract: We present AAD-1, an Asymmetric Adversarial Distillation framework for One-step autoregressive image-to-video generation. State-of-the-art methods adopt adversarial distillation but suffer from motion collapse and training instability, resulting in static videos. AAD-1 addresses these challenges through two key designs in architecture and training strategy. Our key architectural insight is to break the symmetry between generator and discriminator. While the generator remains causal to preserve autoregressive sampling capability, the discriminator attends bidirectionally over the full spatiotemporal context and produces a single holistic realism score for the entire video sequence. This asymmetric design enables the discriminator to effectively detect global temporal failures and long-range drift that cause motion collapse in autoregressive generation. To stabilize training, we introduce a phased strategy that first uses distribution matching to bootstrap a stable one-step generator, providing a warm-up phase that brings the student distribution closer to the teacher before adversarial distillation begins. Extensive experiments on VBench demonstrate that AAD-1 achieves state-of-the-art performance in one-step autoregressive video generation.

</details>


### [119] [Formalizing the Binding Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03976)
*Lianghuan Huang,Yihao Li,Saeed Salehi,Yingshan Chang,Ansh Soni,Konrad P. Kording*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes an information-theoretic framework and a probing method to quantify binding information in Vision Transformer representations, showing binding signals exist and vary across CLS and spatial tokens; binding information correlates with improved recognition in binding-challenged scenes, highlighting binding as essential for robust visual reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formalize and measure the binding problem in deep learning, specifically in ViTs, and to understand whether these models encode information about which features belong to the same object, addressing misbinding in feature-sharing and occlusion scenarios.

Method: Introduce an information-theoretic formalization of binding information; develop a probing method to quantify binding in model representations (CLS token vs spatial tokens); evaluate on ViTs with datasets designed to stress binding (feature sharing, occlusion, natural features); compare several pretrained ViTs.

Result: Binding information is detectable in ViT representations; CLS and spatial tokens encode binding signals with complementary roles; misbinding is more prevalent in challenging scenes; stronger binding signals correlate with better performance on binding-challenging tasks; the framework provides a diagnostic tool to compare models and guide improvements.

Conclusion: Binding information is a key ingredient for robust visual recognition and reasoning. The proposed information-theoretic framework and probes offer a principled way to quantify and study binding in ViTs, with implications for designing architectures and training methods that promote correct feature-object binding.

Abstract: Representations of the world, arguably, contain information about features (e.g. something is blue, something is a circle) but also information about which features are part of the same object (e.g. the circle is blue), which we call binding information. Any system with the ability to understand scenes with multiple objects must be able to solve the binding problem: it needs to know which features belong together. However, despite work showing that Vision Transformers (ViTs) know which patches belong together, it is not known whether current deep learning models learn to exhibit binding information, i.e., for features. We may believe that there is not much binding information, after all misattributing features to wrong objects is a common failure of ViT-based architectures, especially in scenes with objects sharing features. Here we formalize the binding problem with an information-theoretic approach, and introduce a probing method to measure binding information in model representations. We perform experiments on ViTs, measuring binding from different components of the architecture, such as the image summary token [CLS] or the spatial tokens. We use datasets with different binding challenges, such as feature sharing, occlusion, and natural features, while comparing the performance of several pre-trained ViTs. Overall, our research demonstrates binding as a key ingredient to strong visual recognition and reasoning.

</details>


### [120] [NewtPhys: Do Foundation Models Understand Newtonian Physics?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03986)
*Sebastian Cavada,Soumava Paul,Tuan-Hung Vu,Andrei Bursuc,Raoul de Charette*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NewtPhys is a 4D, physically annotated dataset from real-world multiview scenes with physics-grounded simulations, enabling fine-grained per-pixel and 3D-force annotations to evaluate low-level Newtonian reasoning in 56 VLMs and 10 VFMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of visual fidelity in existing physics benchmarks that use synthetic scenes and high-level events, by providing a realistic, physics-grounded evaluation dataset for true low-level Newtonian understanding.

Method: Build a multiview, real-world dataset with physics-grounded simulations; provide dense, fine-grained annotations across timesteps (3D forces and amodal per-pixel quantities) spanning physics, tracking, semantics, and geometry; benchmark a broad set of VLMs and VFMs; release code and data.

Result: Systematically evaluate 56 VLMs (54 open-weight and 2 closed-source frontier models) and 10 VFMs, revealing limitations in low-level physics reasoning; dataset enables future physics-grounded vision research; code and data publicly available.

Conclusion: NewtPhys bridges the realism gap for physics reasoning in vision-language systems and enables next-generation physics-aware evaluations and research.

Abstract: Previous work has evaluated physics reasoning in foundation models using synthetic or semi-synthetic scenes and visual question-answering tasks. However, these benchmarks emphasize high-level events and lack the visual fidelity required to assess true low-level Newtonian understanding. We introduce NewtPhys, a 4D physically annotated dataset built from multiview images of real-world scenes with physics-grounded simulations. The dataset provides dense, fine-grained annotations across timesteps -- including 3D forces and amodal per-pixel quantities covering physics, tracking, semantics and geometry -- bridging the gap between simplistic synthetic setups and realistic visual complexity. Using NewtPhys, we systematically evaluate 56 VLMs, including 54 open-weight models and 2 closed-source frontier models, and 10 VFMs and reveal limitations in low-level physics reasoning. Beyond benchmarking, our dataset enables future research in physics-grounded vision and the development of next-generation physics-aware evaluations. Code and datasets are available at https://astra-vision.github.io/NewtPhys.

</details>


### [121] [PixVOD: Pixel-Distributed Direct Visual Odometry and Depth Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03989)
*Shinjeong Kim,Ignacio Alzugaray,Callum Rhodes,Paul H. J. Kelly,Andrew J. Davison*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Parallelized pixel-level visual odometry and depth estimation via Gaussian Belief Propagation (GBP) across a focal-plane sensor-processors network, with keyframe anchoring to stabilize motion and depth updates on-sensor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transmitting raw, redundant, and noisy pixel data off the sensor is inefficient. Shifting computation to focal-plane sensor-processors enables distributed, local synthesis of higher-level signals and reduces downstream load.

Method: Pixels function as sensor-processors exchanging information through Gaussian Belief Propagation to reach consensus on camera motion and depth from per-pixel photometric observations and a surface normal prior. A keyframe-like anchoring mechanism regulates the effective baseline between frames to maintain geometric stability during optimization, enabling consistent updates.

Result: Evaluated on realistic datasets, demonstrating the feasibility of GBP-based pixel-level distributed odometry and depth estimation with keyframe anchoring on-sensor.

Conclusion: The work validates the potential of on-sensor GBP-based distributed computation for VO and depth tasks, highlighting reduced data movement and richer per-pixel inputs; further work is needed on scalability, robustness to non-idealities, and hardware integration.

Abstract: Images composed of 2D pixel arrays are the standard input to computer vision algorithms, yet many underlying computations can be distributed across pixels. Transmitting raw, redundant, and noisy pixel data off the sensor remains inefficient, motivating a shift toward focal-plane sensor-processors that perform a significant part of the computation directly within each pixel. We envision pixels synthesizing higher-level signals locally, reducing downstream load, and providing richer inputs for higher-level vision tasks.
  We propose a fully parallelizable form of visual odometry and depth estimation across pixels, where sensor-processors exchange information through Gaussian Belief Propagation (GBP) to achieve consensus about camera motion and infer depth from per-pixel photometric observations and a surface normal prior. To maintain geometric stability during optimization, we introduce a keyframe-like anchoring mechanism that regulates the effective baseline between frames, enabling consistent motion and depth updates. Our method is evaluated on realistic datasets, demonstrating the feasibility of GBP-based pixel-level distributed odometry and depth estimation with keyframe anchoring on-sensor. Project Page: https://www.shinjeongkim.com/pixvod/

</details>


### [122] [Exploring Easy Boosts for Lidar Semantic Scene Completion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03992)
*Tetiana Martyniuk,Jonathan Seele,Alexandre Boulch,Gilles Puy,Renaud Marlet,Raoul de Charette*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Semantic priors from off-the-shelf segmentors and visibility cues act as 'free-lunch' boosts for lidar SSC, enabling older models to rival or beat SOTA without architectural changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Determine whether simple input-side enhancements (semantic priors and visibility information) can substantially improve SSC performance and explain gains relative to an oracle.

Method: Apply semantic pseudo-labels to input point clouds using off-the-shelf segmentors; augment inputs with visibility information that distinguishes empty vs unknown spaces; evaluate across several SSC architectures and compare to an oracle; provide code.

Result: Semantic priors are the primary driver of mIoU gains; visibility information provides a secondary boost; older architectures become competitive with, or even surpass, state-of-the-art models when using these cues.

Conclusion: Simple input-side enhancements can unlock substantial SSC gains without architectural redesigns; semantic priors and visibility cues are key factors in SSC performance; the approach sustains competitive results across architectures.

Abstract: This paper investigates "free lunch" strategies to boost the performance of lidar semantic scene completion (SSC) without requiring complex architectural redesigns. We first demonstrate that endowing input point clouds with semantic pseudo-labels from off-the-shelf segmentors significantly improves the performance of existing architectures. By evaluating these models against an oracle, we establish that high-quality semantic priors are a primary driver of mIoU gains. Furthermore, we equip the input lidar scan with visibility information that distinguishes between empty and unknown spaces, which provides a secondary performance boost across the tested architectures. Using these simple enhancements, we observe that older models remain competitive with state-of-the-art systems, and can even outperform them. Our code is available at https://github.com/astra-vision/SSC-Priors.

</details>


### [123] [SimuScene: Simulation-Ready Compositional 3D Scene Reconstruction from a Single Image](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03994)
*Inhee Lee,Sangwon Baik,Sungjoo Kim,Hyeonwoo Kim,Hyunsoo Cha,Hanbyul Joo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A physics-informed, single-image 3D reconstruction pipeline (SimuScene) integrates a physics engine in the generative loop to diagnose and correct geometric and layout errors, yielding stable, simulation-ready scenes for robotic tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-image reconstructions yield per-object shapes that often fail under physical simulation due to interpenetration, hovering, or sinking; existing methods treat physical plausibility as post-hoc corrections, not addressing underlying geometry.

Method: Use a physics engine as a diagnostic feedback tool during reconstruction. Simulate the assembled objects under gravity to detect penetration and support failures; translate these failures into correction signals that drive gravity-axis stretching and amodal shape resampling, forming a feedback loop that tightens geometry and layout while maintaining compositionality.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on physical stability and geometric alignment benchmarks. Demonstrates utility by enabling reconstructed environments to support humanoid control and robot-arm manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: A physics-informed loop mitigates accumulated geometric and layout errors in single-image scene reconstruction, producing stable, simulation-ready environments for robotics.

Abstract: Reconstructing interactive, simulation-ready 3D scenes from a single image is a critical bottleneck for robotic manipulation. While recent single-image lifters recover plausible per-object shapes, composing them yields scenes that collapse under physical simulation due to interpenetrating, hovering, or sinking objects. Existing physics-aware methods address this strictly as a post-hoc layout correction, leaving the underlying geometric errors unresolved. To address this, we introduce SimuScene, a compositional 3D reconstruction pipeline that puts physics in the loop of shape and layout estimation. Rather than using physics merely for layout cleanup, we utilize the physics engine as a diagnostic measurement tool during the generative process itself. By diagnostically simulating reconstructed objects under gravity, we convert penetration and support failures into quantitative correction signals that drive gravity-axis stretching and amodal shape resampling. This physics-informed feedback loop mitigates accumulated reconstruction errors and produces a stable, simulation-ready compositional 3D scene. Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on physical stability and geometric alignment benchmarks. We further highlight SimuScene's utility by deploying reconstructed environments in humanoid control and robot-arm manipulation tasks.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [124] [Too Much of a Good Thing: When sim2real Efforts Impede Policy Learning (And What to Do About It)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02636)
*Kyle Morgenstein,Bharath Masetty,Stephen Welch,Luis Sentis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Overreliance on sim2real realism can cause simulator lock‑in and poor policy exploration; a sim2sim2real approach using only kinematic constraints could improve policy learning and transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current sim2real practice creates misaligned incentives for policy learning, leads to simulator lock-in, and hinders exploration due to strict real-world constraints; a more flexible training paradigm is needed.

Method: Diagnostic analysis of existing sim2real limitations and a proposal for a sim2sim2real paradigm that uses the robot’s kinematics as the sole design constraint, promoting intermediate simulations before real deployment.

Result: Conceptual framework with argumentation; no empirical results yet; outlines the design of a sim2sim2real pipeline and potential benefits.

Conclusion: A kinematics‑only sim2sim2real approach could reduce simulator lock-in and improve policy exploration and transfer, pending empirical validation and practical implementation details.

Abstract: While sim2real efforts are necessary for effective policy transfer to hardware, there is such a thing as too much of a good thing. We argue that sim2real efforts have led to misaligned incentives with policy learning, resulting in simulator lock in and poor policy exploration due to the unreasonable constraints imposed by the real world. We offer a diagnosis and explanation of the current status of the problem, and propose a potential solution via a sim2sim2real paradigm that leverages the robot's kinematics as the sole design constraint.

</details>


### [125] [Fixed-Time Dynamic Landing of Quadrotors using Adaptive Unscented Kalman Filtering and Nonlinear Model Predictive Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02658)
*Mohammadreza Izadi,Zeinab Shayan,Steven Waslander,Reza Faieghi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Unified estimation and control framework for dynamic landing of multi-rotor UAVs on moving platforms using NMPC with a real-time minimum-jerk trajectory planner enforcing a fixed touchdown time, plus an adaptive UKF for online noise statistics; feasibility analysis shows bounded thrust/torque under standard tracking assumptions; validated in simulation and hardware with repeatable landings and improved platform velocity prediction over EKF/UKF methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable precise, time-bounded landings on moving platforms amid time-varying sensing quality and uncertain platform velocity, ensuring safety and repeatability.

Method: Integrate nonlinear model predictive control with a real-time minimum-jerk trajectory planner that enforces a prescribed touchdown time; use an adaptive unscented Kalman filter to update process and measurement noise statistics online; provide a theoretical feasibility analysis showing minimum-jerk references keep thrust/torque bounded under standard tracking assumptions.

Result: Simulation and hardware experiments demonstrate repeatable landings and improved platform velocity prediction accuracy compared with EKF/UKF-based methods.

Conclusion: The framework delivers consistent touchdown timing, robustness to sensing variability, and improved velocity estimation, with a feasible bound on control commands under typical tracking conditions; suitable for practical dynamic landing on moving platforms.

Abstract: This paper introduces an estimation and control framework for dynamic landing of multi-rotor uncrewed aerial vehicles on moving platforms. The proposed method integrates nonlinear model predictive control with a real-time minimum-jerk trajectory planner that enforces a prescribed touchdown time, enabling consistent timing during the terminal descent. To enhance robustness in the presence of time-varying sensing quality, we utilize an adaptive unscented kalman filter that updates the process and measurement noise statistics online. In addition, we provide a reference feasibility analysis showing that minimum-jerk references induce bounded thrust and torque commands under standard tracking hypotheses. The proposed framework is evaluated in simulation and hardware experiments, and it is shown to achieve repeatable landings and improved platform velocity prediction accuracy relative to EKF/UKF-based methods.

</details>


### [126] [Motion Planning in Dynamic Environments: A Survey from Classical to Modern Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02677)
*Zongyuan Shen,Yaming Ou,Shalabh Gupta,Shancheng Zhao,Dehua Zhou,Gao Wang,Zhongqiang Ren,Junfeng Fan,Long Cheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A comprehensive taxonomy and critique of motion planning in dynamic environments, surveying 138 works (2015–2025) across classical and learning-based methods, perception, and key challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic environments require continuous path adaptation; existing reviews largely focus on static settings, creating a gap for a systematic synthesis that organizes methods, compares strengths/limits, and guides future research.

Method: Literature survey of 138 publications, categorizing methods into five families (sampling-based, graph-search, model predictive control, learning, and classical local planners such as velocity obstacles/potential fields/dynamic windows), including dynamic perception approaches (cameras, LiDAR, event-based sensors) and learning techniques (supervised and reinforcement learning).

Result: Provides a structured taxonomy and critical analysis of methods, with insights into advantages, limitations, and applicability in dynamic settings, and highlights challenges unique to dynamic environments (prediction uncertainty, human-robot interaction, freezing robot problem).

Conclusion: Fills a key gap by offering a coherent synthesis that guides researchers in method selection and identifies open directions, especially regarding perception, uncertainty handling, and integration of learning with traditional planning.

Abstract: Motion planning in dynamic environments requires robots to continuously adapt their paths in response to environmental changes for safe and uninterrupted navigation. While many surveys have reviewed planning in static settings, systematic reviews focused on dynamic environments remain limited. This paper presents a comprehensive survey of 138 works, primarily published between 2015 and 2025, spanning both classical and learning-based approaches. The motion planning methods are grouped into five categories based on the concepts of sampling, graph search, model predictive control, learning, and additional classical local planning approaches, including velocity obstacles, potential fields and dynamic windows. The learning techniques include supervised learning and reinforcement learning. We also discuss the role of dynamic perception in motion planning, covering techniques for detecting and modeling moving obstacles using cameras, LiDAR, and event-based sensors. The survey analyzes the principles, strengths, and limitations of each method, with particular attention to challenges unique to dynamic environments, such as prediction uncertainty, human-robot interaction, and the freezing robot problem. The survey provides researchers with a structured understanding of motion planning methods in dynamic environments.

</details>


### [127] [See Less, Specify More: Visual Evidence Budgets for Generalizable VLAs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02735)
*Yueh-Hua Wu,Tatsuya Matsushima,Kei Ota*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: S2 improves VLA generalization by relabeling trajectories into refined subtask-level language and enforcing an explicit visual evidence budget, reducing reliance on broad visual context. It yields substantial gains on real-robot tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalization bottleneck in vision-language-action (VLA) models due to reliance on coarse instructions and unconstrained visual context; need for a cleaner interface that preserves high-level goals while guiding local execution.

Method: Train the executor under a cleaned interface: keep the original instruction as a stable high-level goal; relabel trajectories into refined trajectory- and subtask-level language; replace native attention with an explicit 'See Less' visual-evidence budget, avoiding region annotations; compatible with off-the-shelf VLM planners via in-context learning.

Result: Across eight real-robot tasks (TX-G2 and HSR), mean subtask success increases from 54.2% to 79.0% over pi0.5; improvements attributed to reduced supervision aliasing and guidance based on task-sufficient evidence rather than broad visual context.
