Conclusion: VLA generalization benefits when the executor is trained to act from informative local guidance and task-sufficient visual evidence, rather than recovering both from weak supervision.

Abstract: Generalization remains a central bottleneck for vision-language-action (VLA) models: under distractors, appearance shifts, and semantically similar tasks, the policy must often infer local execution details from coarse instructions while also deciding which parts of the image matter for control. We present S2 (See Less, Specify More), a framework for improving VLA generalization by training the executor under a cleaner interface.
  Specify More preserves the original instruction as a stable high-level goal while relabeling each trajectory into refined trajectory- and subtask-level language that disambiguates the current execution mode. Unlike native attention, See Less imposes an explicit visual evidence budget, training the executor to act from task-sufficient evidence rather than unconstrained visual context, without any region or mask annotation.
  This interface lets the executor follow detailed guidance without relying on distracting visual patches or resolving avoidable ambiguity on its own, and it remains compatible with off-the-shelf VLM planners through in-context learning. Across our main evaluation settings, S2 improves overall generalization metrics by changing the executor's learning problem: coarse instructions induce avoidable supervision aliasing, goal-preserving local guidance outperforms instruction replacement in our main ablations, and explicit evidence budgeting reduces dependence on broad visual context beyond efficiency considerations.
  Across eight real-robot tasks on TX-G2 (an AgiBot G2-compatible variant) and HSR, S2 raises mean subtask success from 54.2% to 79.0% over pi0.5. Together, these results suggest that VLA generalization improves when the executor is trained to act from informative local guidance and task-sufficient visual evidence, rather than recovering both from weak supervision.

</details>


### [128] [SeeTraceAct: Visibility-Aware Latent Planning from Cross-Embodiment Demonstration Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02745)
*Jaehyeon Son,Junhyun Kim,Kyle Kam,Jeremiah Coholich,Seok Joon Kim,Jinhoo Kim,Chris Dongjoo Kim,Jaemin Cho,Dieter Fox,Zsolt Kira*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SeeTraceAct improves one-shot demo-conditioned vision-language-action policies by predicting visibility-aware end-effector traces to achieve precise spatial grounding, evaluated on RoboCasa-DC and real world.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLAs are promising general robot policies but struggle when success hinges on precise localization of small target regions; one-shot demonstrations reduce data needs but end-to-end grounding often lacks spatial precision.

Method: Propose SeeTraceAct, a demo-conditioned VLA framework that encourages precise spatial grounding via visibility-aware prediction of future end-effector traces. Introduce RoboCasa-DC, a demo-conditioned extension of RoboCasa with episode-paired humanoid videos, enabling cross-embodiment evaluation. Demonstrate on a Franka Panda arm conditioned on human demonstrations.

Result: SeeTraceAct outperforms baselines, achieving the best success rate across all four RoboCasa-DC settings and improving real-world average success by 12.5 percentage points.

Conclusion: SeeTraceAct enhances spatial grounding in one-shot demo-conditioned VLAs and enables reproducible cross-embodiment evaluation; it shows practical improvements for real robot manipulation tasks and provides a valuable benchmark resource.

Abstract: Vision-language-action models (VLAs) are promising general-purpose robot policies, but adapting them to new tasks typically requires costly task-specific teleoperation data. As an alternative, we study one-shot demo-conditioned VLAs, where a robot policy is conditioned on a single demonstration video of an unseen task. We find that existing end-to-end approaches often struggle when successful execution requires precisely localizing small target regions. To address this limitation, we propose SeeTraceAct, a demo-conditioned VLA framework that encourages precise spatial grounding through visibility-aware prediction of future end-effector traces. To enable reproducible evaluation with cross-embodiment demonstrations, we introduce and release RoboCasa-DC, a demo-conditioned extension of RoboCasa with episode-paired humanoid videos. Experiments on RoboCasa-DC and a real-world benchmark, where a Franka Panda arm is conditioned on human demonstrations, show that SeeTraceAct outperforms baselines, achieving the best success rate across all four RoboCasa-DC settings and improving real-world average success by 12.5 percentage points.

</details>


### [129] [Hybrid Adaptive Kalman Filtering for Data-Efficient Joint Tracking and Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02767)
*Jiho Lee,Nisar R. Ahmed,Rebecca Russell*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Self-supervised Hybrid Adaptive Kalman Filter learns corrections to dynamics and process noise from measurements, preserving probabilistic structure and enabling model classification via innovation likelihood; improves accuracy, consistency, and classification across data regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Kalman filters are sensitive to model mismatch and noise covariance tuning; supervised learning requires large datasets and may not yield consistent uncertainty estimates. A self-supervised approach can adapt online while maintaining Bayesian uncertainty.

Method: Learn structured corrections to system dynamics and process noise covariance from measurements only, while preserving the Kalman filter framework; compute innovation likelihood and use generalized Bayesian inference for model classification.

Result: Experiments on real-world and simulated data show improved estimation accuracy and statistical consistency, along with robust classification performance in both low-data and large-data scenarios.

Conclusion: The approach combines self-supervised learning with Kalman filtering to adapt to mismatch while maintaining probabilistic structure, enabling reliable uncertainty quantification and model classification across data regimes.

Abstract: Kalman filtering performance is highly sensitive to model mismatch and noise covariance tuning. Learning-based approaches address these limitations but typically rely on supervised training with large datasets and do not produce consistent uncertainty estimates. In this paper, we propose a self-supervised Hybrid Adaptive Kalman Filter that learns structured corrections to system dynamics and process noise covariance from measurements alone while preserving the probabilistic structure of the filter. This allows the innovation likelihood to be computed and subsequently used for model classification via generalized Bayesian inference. Experimental results on real-world and simulated datasets demonstrate improved estimation accuracy and statistical consistency as well as robust classification performance across both low-data and large-data scenarios.

</details>


### [130] [A Measurement-Driven Digital Twin Architecture for Plant-Level Biomass Estimation and Growth Forecasting in Hydroponic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02796)
*Morgan Mayborne,Abhisesh Silwal,George Kantor*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A digital twin system predicts lettuce growth in hydroponics using RGB-D data and a neural network to estimate biomass and forecast near-term yield with low error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve monitoring and forecasting of crop growth in urban, soil-less horticulture to enhance food distribution efficiency and resource use.

Method: Integrate digital twin with hydroponic greenhouse hardware; train a neural network on RGB-D images (1300 images) to estimate plant mass; use streams of sensor data to update growth trajectories and yield forecasts 1–4 days ahead.

Result: Mass estimation error of 1.5 g; near-term yield forecasts with about 2 g error over 1–4 days.

Conclusion: Shows feasible end-to-end digital twin workflow for controlled-environment agriculture, enabling near-term yield forecasting and precise biomass estimation; future work needed on scalability and generalization.

Abstract: Alternatives to soil-based horticulture, such as hydroponics, have been developed to respond to food distribution concerns for dense urban centers. A new system was developed to track an individual lettuce plant's growth in a hydroponic environment, utilizing streams of measured information and available models to continuously update the growth trajectory estimates for a plant. These "digital twin" models were integrated into an operating hydroponic greenhouse, with custom horticultural and sensor hardware to grow and measure relevant information. To aid in updating model parameters, plant yield was continuously measured with a custom neural network, using RGB-D images of the plants as an input. The network, trained on a collected dataset of 1300 images, was able to estimate mass within 1.5 g of the ground-truth value. After integration into the custom system, digital twin growth projections could approximate future yield between one and four days in the future, maintaining around a 2 g forecasting error.

</details>


### [131] [Direct Informed Sampling on Riemannian Manifolds via Loewner Order Lower Bounds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02879)
*Phone Thiha Kyaw,Jonathan Kelly*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a matrix-valued, admissible heuristic for Riemannian metrics in motion planning, preserving directional structure and enabling rejection-free sampling by mapping to isotropic Euclidean space via Cholesky of the tight Loewner bound; yields faster convergence across multiple manipulators and planners.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inform informed-sampling relies on metric geometry; Euclidean heuristics become inadmissible with configuration-dependent Riemannian metrics and scalar eigenvalue bounds discard directionality, leading to conservative informed sets.

Method: Compute the tightest constant lower bound on the SPD metric tensor using the Loewner order, preserving its directional structure. Use the Cholesky factor of this bound to define a linear map to an isotropic Euclidean space, turning the Riemannian informed set into a prolate hyperspheroid and enabling direct, rejection-free sampling.

Result: Experiments on 6-DoF UR5, 7-DoF Franka, and 14-DoF PR2 under three Riemannian metrics show consistently tighter informed sets than Euclidean or scalar bounds, accelerating convergence for multiple state-of-the-art asymptotically optimal planners.

Conclusion: A matrix-valued Loewner-bound heuristic preserves metric structure and yields tighter informed sets, improving sampling efficiency in Riemannian-motion-planning problems across diverse manipulators and planners.

Abstract: Informed sampling techniques accelerate sampling-based motion planners by focusing the search on promising regions of the state space, yet most existing methods rely on Euclidean heuristics that become inadmissible under configuration-dependent Riemannian metrics. While scalar eigenvalue bounds restore admissibility by uniformly scaling the Euclidean distance, they discard the directional structure of the metric, producing overly conservative informed sets. We propose a matrix-valued admissible heuristic that exploits the Loewner order on symmetric positive definite matrices to compute the tightest constant lower bound on the metric tensor while preserving its full directional structure. The Cholesky factorization of this bound defines a linear map to an isotropic Euclidean space in which the Riemannian informed set reduces to a standard prolate hyperspheroid, enabling direct, rejection-free sampling using existing algorithms. Experiments on manipulation tasks with a 6-DoF UR5, 7-DoF Franka, and 14-DoF PR2 under three distinct Riemannian metrics show that our heuristic produces consistently tighter informed sets than both the Euclidean and scalar eigenvalue bounds, accelerating convergence across multiple state-of-the-art asymptotically optimal planners.

</details>


### [132] [Impact of a Soft Wearable Back-Support Device on Postural Stability during Trip-Like Perturbations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02888)
*Yuanhao Chen,Rohan Khatavkar,Soubhagya Nayak,Jiefeng Sun,Hyunglae Lee*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Soft wearable back-support with adjustable stiffness improves reactive balance under perturbations, increasing Margin of Stability (MOS) in both standing and walking, with greater gains in standing when stiffness increases; walking shows improvement for both device conditions but no stiffness difference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether a soft back-support with tunable stiffness can enhance postural stability and reduce fall risk under external perturbations.

Method: Two perturbation paradigms (perturbed standing and perturbed walking) with healthy subjects, testing three back-support conditions (no device, low stiffness, high stiffness). MOS at the point of maximal instability quantified overall stability.

Result: MOS increased with device use; standing MOS rose with higher stiffness; walking MOS improved relative to no device for both device conditions, but the stiffness levels did not differ significantly.

Conclusion: Soft wearable back-supports with adjustable stiffness show potential to enhance reactive balance and fall prevention; suggests further work on personalized stiffness optimization and evaluation in populations at elevated fall risk.

Abstract: The effectiveness of a soft wearable back-support device in enhancing postural stability was investigated under trip-like perturbations using two experimental paradigms: perturbed standing and perturbed walking. Healthy subjects completed trials under three different back-support conditions: no device, device worn with low stiffness, and device activated with high stiffness. Whole-body stability was quantified using the minimum Margin of Stability (MOS) at the point of maximal instability. Results demonstrated increased MOS during device use, indicating enhanced postural stability. In standing, MOS increased significantly with device stiffness, whereas in walking, both device conditions improved MOS relative to no device but did not differ significantly from each other. These findings highlight the potential of soft wearable back-support devices with adjustable stiffness to improve reactive balance control against external perturbations, with important implications for fall prevention. Future research should explore personalized stiffness optimization and evaluate efficacy in populations at elevated risk of falls.

</details>


### [133] [Improved Postural Stability Using a Lightweight Semi-Active Soft Back Support Device Under Standing Perturbations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02928)
*Rohan Khatavkar,Jiefeng Sun,Hyunglae Lee*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Lightweight semi-active soft back support with a pneumatic actuator in parallel with a passive elastic band reduces whole-body angular momentum and increases margin of stability after standing perturbations, demonstrated in five healthy adults.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Falls from perturbations during standing are common in older adults; heavy back supports impair stability due to added mass; passive devices offer limited benefits; a lightweight semi-active solution could enhance balance recovery without compromising stability.

Method: Prototype semi-active back support combining a pneumatic artificial muscle (active element) with a passive elastic band. The active element rapidly provides assistive force after perturbations. Experiments with five healthy individuals evaluated postural stability after standing perturbations, using metrics such as whole-body angular momentum and margin of stability.

Result: The device significantly reduced whole-body angular momentum and increased the margin of stability, indicating improved balance recovery.

Conclusion: Semi-active soft back supports are a promising, lightweight strategy for fall prevention during standing perturbations, warranting further research with larger cohorts and varied perturbations.

Abstract: Older adults are particularly susceptible to falls following perturbations during standing, such as forward loss of balance. Back support devices that assist trunk extension may help mitigate fall risk by preventing excessive trunk flexion. Previous studies have investigated heavy back support devices; however, these systems often introduced adverse effects on stability due to their added mass, which shifted the body's natural center of mass unfavorably. In contrast, lightweight passive devices have shown limited benefits, as they can generate only modest assistive forces during the relatively small trunk flexion associated with forward balance loss. In this study, we evaluated the effects of a lightweight semi-active soft back support device on postural stability following standing perturbations. Our device combines an active element (a pneumatic artificial muscle) in parallel with a passive elastic band. The active element rapidly provides assistive force following a perturbation, overcoming the limitations of passive devices. Experiments conducted with five healthy individuals demonstrated that the semi-active device significantly reduced whole-body angular momentum and increased the margin of stability, indicating improved balance recovery performance. These results highlight the promise of semi-active soft wearable robots as an effective and lightweight strategy for fall prevention during standing perturbations.

</details>


### [134] [SCOPE: Real-Time Natural Language Camera Agent at the Edge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02951)
*Nikolaj Hindsbo,Sina Ehsani,Pragyana Mishra*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SCOPE is an edge-deployable, language-driven PTZ camera agent evaluated on a large 536-task benchmark that blends Blender simulation with real hardware, showing that stronger LMs reduce hallucinations and routing errors, while perception remains the bottleneck; mixture-of-experts and quantization enable real-time edge performance with a robust evaluation framework using LM-as-Judge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable reliable deployment-ready evaluation of language-driven robotic agents that must interpret natural-language instructions and act via perceptual and control tools at the edge, with reproducible outcomes and deployment-critical metrics.

Method: Develop SCOPE for open-vocabulary PTZ control and scene understanding, run in Blender sim and on a real PTZ camera; create a 536-task benchmark spanning QA, multi-step commands, counting, spatial reasoning, descriptions, and OCR; use LM-as-Judge and evaluate 19 planner-perception model combos (Qwen3 SLMs with Moondream and Qwen VLMs); explore mixture-of-Experts and quantization.

Result: Stronger LMs reduce hallucinations and tool routing errors, improving closed-loop reliability; once a capable SLM is used, perception becomes the main bottleneck; Mixture-of-Experts on planning and perception match or beat dense models with similar latency and memory; quantization yields efficiency with minimal accuracy loss; sim-to-real validation demonstrates practical edge feasibility for real-time language-driven PTZ control.

Conclusion: The work provides a deployment-focused framework and dataset for evaluating edge-ready language-driven agents, identifies perception as the principal bottleneck after improving planning, and demonstrates that MoE and quantization enable scalable, real-time performance for PTZ control in real-world settings.

Abstract: Deploying language-driven agents in robotics requires evaluations that reflect real-world task demands: natural-language instructions with reproducible outcomes. Such agents must connect language models to callable perception and control tools, and be assessed using deployment-critical metrics including latency, accuracy, and error modes. We present SCOPE (Simulation and Camera Operations for Perception and Evaluation), a modular agent for natural-language, open-vocabulary pan-tilt-zoom (PTZ) camera control and visual scene understanding, designed explicitly for edge deployment. SCOPE operates both in a Blender-based simulation environment and on a physical PTZ camera, executing all perception, planning, and control locally at the deployment site using edge-accessible compute.
  We release a 536-task benchmark spanning QA, single- and multi-step commands, counting, spatial reasoning, descriptions, and optical character recognition in a Blender-based simulation environment that exposes realistic PTZ control affordances. Execution traces are combined with an LM-as-Judge to evaluate latency, accuracy, and error modes.
  We evaluate 19 planner-perception model combinations pairing Qwen3 small language models (SLMs) with Moondream and Qwen vision-language models (VLMs). Stronger SLMs substantially reduce hallucinations and improve tool routing, leading to more reliable closed-loop behavior. Once a sufficiently capable SLM is used, perception becomes the dominant performance bottleneck. Mixture-of-Experts models on both the planning and perception side consistently match or exceed dense alternatives at latencies and memory footprints comparable to much smaller networks. Quantization provides additional efficiency gains with minimal accuracy degradation, identifying a practical, sim-to-real validated design point for real-time, edge-feasible language-driven PTZ control.

</details>


### [135] [Hybrid Dynamics Modeling for a Flexible 2-DoF Robotic Arm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02969)
*Maciek Popik,Daniel Yang,Mahdis Bisheban*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Physics-informed RBD+GMM models and a kinematics-based regression baseline are evaluated on a 2-DoF flexible-link arm for torque prediction. Physics-based parameters perform worst; regularized and least-squares data-driven estimates align better with measured torques, highlighting the benefits of semi-parametric residual learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address unmodeled dynamics in flexible-link manipulators and improve torque prediction by combining physics-based models with data-driven residuals.

Method: Three approaches: (1) two physics-informed models combining rigid-body dynamics with Gaussian Mixture Models to capture residuals; (2) a purely data-driven baseline using kinematics-based regression; estimation steps include Ridge regression on kinematic features for torque prediction; physics-based baseline built from published specifications; ordinary least-squares to estimate the same parameter set directly from data.

Result: Physics-based parameters yield the poorest accuracy; regularized (Ridge) and least-squares estimators closely match measured torques; residual analysis shows limitations of purely parametric models; data-driven or semi-parametric approaches improve performance.

Conclusion: Findings support regularization and data-driven identification; advocate semi-parametric residual learning methods for flexible-link systems.

Abstract: This paper examines three approaches for modeling the dynamics of a flexible-link 2-DoF robotic arm to address unmodeled dynamics not captured by rigid-body models. Two physics informed models combine rigid-body dynamics (RBD) formulations with a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) to capture residual model errors and linkage flexibility. A kinematics-based regression model serves as a purely data-driven baseline. Using an open-source dataset, torque predictions are first estimated using Ridge regression on kinematic features, while the physicsbased baseline is constructed from published specifications, and ordinary least-squares regression is subsequently used to estimate the same parameter set directly from data. Results show that the physics-based parameters yield the poorest accuracy, while regularized and least-squares estimators align more closely with measured torques. Residual analysis and error metrics highlight the limitations of purely parametric models for flexible-link systems and underscore the value of regularization and data-driven identification, supporting developments of semi-parametric residual learning methods.

</details>


### [136] [MARIO: Motion-Augmented Real-Time Multi-Sensor Inertial Odometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02996)
*Yiquan Li,Taeyoung Yeon,Chenfeng Gao,Vasco Xu,Xuanyou Liu,Karan Ahuja*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Grounds inertial odometry in human kinematics via a learned IMU-inferred pose prior and multimodal sensor fusion, reducing drift on Nymeria and improving robustness for camera-less human tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inertial odometry from IMUs alone suffers drift and noise; although large-scale pretraining improves generalization, explicit motion dynamics are not captured, especially on daily activity datasets like Nymeria. A learned pose prior enforces physically plausible motion; multimodal signals from magnetometers, barometers, and secondary IMUs can further enhance long-term stability.

Method: Incorporate a learned IMU-inferred pose prior into existing IO architectures to enforce kinematic constraints. Integrate multimodal sensors via a sensor-fusion framework to utilize lightweight AR glasses signals. Evaluate on Nymeria (5x larger than prior datasets); compare ablations to quantify the prior and fusion contributions.

Result: Drift reduction up to 36% on Nymeria; Nymeria dataset is 5x larger than datasets used in prior work. With the proposed sensor fusion (magnetometer, barometer, secondary IMU), drift reduction increases to up to 42%. The approach improves long-term robustness and generalization across diverse motion conditions.

Conclusion: Raises the bar for camera-less human tracking by unifying human motion kinematics with multimodal sensing. Demonstrates a new paradigm for inertial and lightweight odometry with improved accuracy and robustness; establishes a new benchmark and provides a project website for reproducibility.

Abstract: Inertial odometry (IO) using only Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) provides a lightweight solution for human motion tracking in augmented reality (AR) and wearable devices. Recent learning-based IO methods have improved the generalizability of inertial localization through large-scale pretraining on human motion datasets. However, these approaches remain prone to drift and noise because they do not explicitly capture human motion dynamics, especially on daily activity datasets such as Nymeria. In this work, we propose to ground inertial odometry in human kinematics through a learned IMU-inferred pose prior, which promotes physically consistent motion constraints. We integrate this pose prior into existing IO architectures and reduce positional drift by up to 36% on the challenging Nymeria dataset, which is 5x larger than datasets used in prior work. We further improve long-term performance with a sensor-fusion framework that incorporates auxiliary signals from lightweight sensors already available on commercial AR glasses, including magnetometers, barometers, and secondary IMUs. With this fusion strategy, positional drift is reduced by up to 42%, improving robustness and generalization across diverse motion conditions. Together, our results introduce a new paradigm for inertial and lightweight odometry by unifying human motion kinematics with multimodal sensing, setting a new benchmark for accurate and robust camera-less human tracking. Our website is available at https://spice-lab.org/projects/MARIO/.

</details>


### [137] [ModuLoop : Low-Level Code Generation using Modular Synthesizer and Closed-Loop Debugger for Robotic Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03047)
*Gina Yoon,Sumin Lee,Joo Yong Sim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An LLM-driven, closed-loop, modular code synthesis framework enables autonomous robotic control without task-specific fine-tuning by planning, generating, and iteratively executing code with debugging probes; validated on RGB-D calibration and a pick-and-place task, demonstrating accuracy and scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs excel at software tasks but struggle with low-level, real-time robotic control. A closed-loop, modular code synthesis approach can leverage pre-trained LLMs to generate executable control programs, with runtime probing to ensure correct behavior in dynamic environments.

Method: Use a pre-trained LLM without task-specific fine-tuning to perform modular code planning and generation. Execute the generated code iteratively, inserting debugging probes to observe behavior, forming a closed loop for systematic debugging and refinement. Apply framework to RGB-D camera calibration and robotic arm pick-and-place tasks in real-world settings to validate effectiveness and extensibility.

Result: The framework achieves high execution accuracy and autonomy across calibration and manipulation tasks, demonstrating effective calibration outcomes and successful real-world execution with potential for extension to additional tasks.

Conclusion: Closed-loop modular code synthesis with an off-the-shelf LLM is a practical, scalable approach for LLM-based robotic control, enabling systematic debugging, refinement, and expansion to varied real-world manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive performance across various domains, including code generation and problem solving. However, their application in robotic control, particularly in low-level tasks that require precise manipulation, real-time feedback, and environment-dependent execution, remains limited. To address this challenge, we propose the Closed-Loop Modular Code Synthesizer framework. This framework leverages a pre-trained LLM without any task-specific fine-tuning to perform modular code planning and generation, and iteratively executes the generated code while inserting debugging probes to observe its behavior. This closed-loop structure facilitates systematic debugging and refinement, ultimately producing executable control programs. We apply the proposed framework to the calibration of an RGB-D camera and a robotic arm, validating its effectiveness in real-world settings. Furthermore, through a subsequent pick-and-place task, we demonstrate not only the accuracy of the calibration but also the potential extensibility of the framework. Across both tasks, the framework achieved high execution accuracy and autonomy, illustrating the practicality and scalability of LLM-based robotic control using our framework.

</details>


### [138] [TTT-VLA: Test-Time Latent Prompt Optimization for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03127)
*Wenbo Zhang,Jianxiong Li,Shuai Yang,Sijin Chen,Jiajun Liu,Lingqiao Liu,Xiao Ma*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A test-time training framework (TTT-VLA) optimizes a latent prompt via a proxy self-supervised task to adapt VLA policies at deployment without changing the policy, yielding improved performance and targeting critical decisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Distribution shifts impair Vision-Language-Action models. Prompt-based steering offers a control interface, but prior methods rely on external guidance; in-situ adaptation via test-time optimization of prompts can align behavior to the current environment.

Method: Train a latent prompt with an auxiliary proxy task during training; at test time, collect interaction data and optimize only the latent prompt using the proxy task's self-supervised signal, while keeping the policy fixed. Evaluated on SimplerEnv for single- and multi-embodiment scenarios.

Result: Consistent improvement in task success rates; gains arise from correcting a small number of critical decisions rather than globally altering policy behavior.

Conclusion: Latent Prompt Optimization provides a practical pathway for deployment-time improvement of foundation manipulation policies within VLA frameworks.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models trained on large-scale data have made remarkable progress, but they remain vulnerable to distribution shifts at deployment time. Recent VLA models suggest that prompts can serve as an efficient interface for steering policy behavior, but existing prompt-based steering typically relies on external guidance. This raises a natural question: can test-time training (TTT) for VLA be achieved by optimizing a prompt, so that the steering interface itself can be learned and adapted from interaction? We address this question with TTT-VLA, a test-time training framework based on Latent Prompt Optimization (LPO). During training, the latent prompt is learned with an additional proxy task, providing an extra learned conditioning signal for policy learning. At test time, TTT is performed by collecting interaction data from the current environment and optimizing only the latent prompt on those data using the proxy task's self-supervised signal, without modifying the policy itself. Experiments on SimplerEnv demonstrate that the proposed method consistently improves task success rates in both single- and multi-embodiment settings. Further analysis shows that the gains arise primarily from correcting a small number of critical decisions rather than globally altering policy behavior. These results suggest that LPO provides an effective and practical pathway for deployment-time improvement of foundation manipulation policies.

</details>


### [139] [How Visible Are Silent Manipulation Failures? An Observability Study of False-Success Detection in Simulated Robot Episodes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03134)
*Aarav Bedi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This work analyzes how much information is needed to overturn false “success” labels in imitation-learning robot manipulation, comparing proprioceptive data to vision. Using simulated two‑hand ALOHA tasks with perturbations, the authors label episodes by privileged simulator state and retain only robot‑labeled successes. They find that overturnability of false successes varies by task: proprioception alone nearly recovers all false successes in cube transfer, but only partially in peg insertion, where a vision detector recovers most of the remaining cases. They show that the separability hinges on velocity differences below realistic sensor noise, making the result an optimistic bound from a noiseless simulator. The authors also release their generation/evaluation pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address reliability of success labels in imitation learning and quantify how much proprioceptive versus visual information is required to overturn false positives, with practical implications for improving policy quality and evaluation pipelines.

Method: Two bimanual ALOHA tasks in simulation; induce failures via environment perturbations; label episodes by privileged simulator state; keep only episodes the robot flagged as successful; compare detectors using proprioception (joint data) versus vision; analyze recoverability and sensitivity to sensor noise.

Result: Recoverability of false successes varies by task: cube transfer allows near-complete overturning from proprioception; peg insertion yields partial overturning with proprioception, with vision recovering most remaining cases; separability relies on velocity differences below sensor-noise floors, suggesting the bound is optimistic; pipeline and data are released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: In imitation-learning evaluation, false-success labels may be overturnable to varying degrees depending on task modality; visual information can be essential to close gaps where proprioception alone is insufficient, and the analysis reflects an optimistic, noiseless-simulator bound rather than a real-world bound.

Abstract: Imitation-learning policies for robot manipulation inherit the quality of the success labels attached to their training episodes, and those labels are usually produced by the robot's own success check. A particularly damaging error is the false success: an episode the robot logs as a success when the task outcome was actually wrong. We ask a narrow but practical question about these episodes. Once an episode has already been flagged as a success, how much of the information needed to overturn that label is present in proprioception, and how much requires vision? We build a simulated testbed on two bimanual ALOHA tasks, induce failures through environment perturbations rather than label edits, label every episode by privileged simulator state that the detector never sees, and keep only episodes the robot flagged as successful. We then compare detectors restricted to proprioception against a vision-based detector. We find that recoverability spans a wide range: in cube transfer the false successes are almost fully recoverable from joint data alone, while in peg insertion proprioception recovers only part of them and a vision detector closes most of the gap. We also show that the proprioceptive separability we measure rests on velocity differences far below any realistic sensor noise floor, so it is best read as an optimistic upper bound that a noiseless simulator inflates. We release the generation and evaluation pipeline.

</details>


### [140] [ConTrack: Constrained Hand Motion Tracking with Adaptive Trade-off Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03177)
*Yutong Liang,Quanquan Peng,Ri-Zhao Qiu,Xiaolong Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ConTrack is an RL framework for dexterous manipulation that treats object tracking as a constraint and allocates remaining control to motion fidelity, enabling online trade-off adjustments via a dual-variable update; it stabilizes learning with an adaptive mid-trajectory reset library, achieving improved tracking, pose accuracy, and fidelity on both simulation and real robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transferring human demonstrations to real robot manipulation is hampered by kinematic gaps, especially for long-horizon, contact-rich tasks. Existing RL policies often require per-sequence reward tuning and struggle under limited interaction budgets. There is a need for scalable, data-efficient methods that can preserve demonstrated joint motion and contact timing while achieving high task performance.

Method: ConTrack frames object tracking as a constraint within reinforcement learning and uses a dual-variable update to allocate remaining control authority to motion fidelity, enabling online task-style trade-offs. It also introduces an adaptive mid-trajectory reset library that reuses policy-reachable simulator states to stabilize long-horizon learning.

Result: In both simulation tracking and real-robot experiments, ConTrack achieves higher success rates and more accurate object poses than prior approaches, while preserving joint and contact fidelity.

Conclusion: ConTrack demonstrates scalable use of tracking data for dexterous manipulation, enabling online balance between tracking and motion fidelity and improving long-horizon learning stability through adaptive resets; it shows promise for bridging sim-to-real gaps in contact-rich manipulation.

Abstract: Human demonstrations provide strong priors for robot manipulation, yet it is non-trivial to transfer them to execute on real robots due to the kinematic gap. In dexterous manipulation, it remains challenging to track long-horizon, contact-rich sequences even in simulators: a reference-tracking policy must keep objects on their target trajectories while preserving demonstrated joint motion and contact timing. Existing approaches often rely on hand-crafted reward tuning that require per-sequence tuning and break under limited interaction budgets. We introduce ConTrack, a reinforcement learning (RL) framework that scales with tracking data. ConTrack treats object tracking as a constraint and allocates remaining control authority to motion fidelity, which allows it to adapt task--style trade-offs online using a dual-variable update. In addition, ConTrack also stabilizes long-horizon learning with an adaptive mid-trajectory reset library that reuses policy-reachable simulator states. Our qualitative and quantitative results in simulation tracking and real robot demonstrate that ConTrack improves success and object pose accuracy significantly over prior arts while preserving joint and contact fidelity. Website: https://www.lyt0112.com/projects/ConTrack.

</details>


### [141] [GeoSem-WAM: Geometry- and Semantic-Aware World Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03188)
*Fulong Ma,Daojie Peng,Wenjun Yue,Jiahang Cao,Bintao Wang,Qiang Zhang,Jun Ma*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Structured world modeling with geometry and semantic supervision enhances latent representations in WAMs, achieving better action prediction and robustness without test-time video generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of RGB-only future prediction in World Action Models (WAMs) and the need for richer latent representations that capture structure (geometry, semantics) to improve decision-making and robustness while avoiding test-time rollout overhead.

Method: Introduce two auxiliary branches predicting future geometry and semantic representations in addition to future RGB prediction. Jointly learn a unified latent space that encodes scene dynamics, spatial geometry, and semantic context, while preserving efficient inference by not performing explicit future rollout at test time.

Result: Experimental results show consistent improvements in action prediction accuracy, scene understanding, and robustness across challenging embodied scenarios when incorporating structured supervision.

Conclusion: Structured world supervision that targets geometry and semantics strengthens WAMs by producing richer latent representations and maintaining test-time efficiency, advancing scalable, robust embodied decision-making.

Abstract: Recent World Action Models (WAMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in embodied decision-making. However, whether their effectiveness stems from explicit future imagination during inference or representation learning induced by predictive training remains an open question. Emerging evidence suggests the primary advantage lies in learning robust latent representations rather than generating future observations at test time. Nevertheless, existing WAMs mainly rely on RGB-based future prediction, which provides limited structural and spatial understanding of complex environments. To address this, we propose a structured world modeling framework that enhances latent representations through geometric and semantic supervision. Alongside future RGB prediction, our model introduces two auxiliary prediction branches for future geometry and semantic representations, enabling it to jointly capture scene dynamics, spatial geometry, and semantic context within a unified latent space. Crucially, our approach preserves efficient inference by avoiding explicit future rollout or video generation at test time. Extensive experiments show that incorporating structured world supervision consistently improves action prediction accuracy, scene understanding, and robustness under challenging embodied scenarios, highlighting its potential for advancing scalable and efficient WAMs.

</details>


### [142] [Toward Gripper-Integrated Active Electrosense for Pre-Contact Sensing in Underwater Soft Grippers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03204)
*Ahsan Tanveer,Muhammad Hamza,Waqar Hussain Afridi,Chen Wang,Guangming Xie*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Active electrosense enables a proximity-like pre-contact cue for underwater soft grippers by detecting object-induced perturbations in an applied electric field; demonstrated in simulation and tank experiments with a multi-electrode octopus-inspired gripper.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable reliable approach and grasp under degraded visibility; soft grippers lack onboard pre-contact cues; electrosense could provide a lightweight, onboard proximity signal in conductive water.

Method: Instrument octopus-inspired gripper with discrete electrodes; collect multi-channel voltages with off-the-shelf hardware; perform simulations and tank experiments using a suspended conductive sphere; vary excitation 5-20 V and frequency from 1 mHz to 1 kHz; compare readouts to empty-water baselines.

Result: Readouts show structured, object-dependent changes; detectability depends on excitation amplitude and frequency; suggests electrosense can serve as proximal cue prior to contact.

Conclusion: Supports systematic exploration of gripper-integrated electrosense as complementary pre-contact cue; potential to improve robustness of underwater soft manipulation when vision is unreliable.

Abstract: Underwater manipulation often occurs under degraded visibility due to turbidity, glare, and gripper occlusion, limiting the reliability of vision-based perception during approach and grasping. In such settings, soft grippers are well suited for compliant interaction, but they typically lack an onboard pre-contact cue that can guide approach and closure when vision is unreliable. This extended abstract explores active electrosense as a lightweight sensing modality that can provide a proximity-like signal prior to contact by measuring perturbations of an applied electric field in conductive media. We instrument an octopus-inspired gripper with a discrete electrode layout and record multi-channel sensing voltages using off-the-shelf hardware. Simulation and tank experiments with a suspended conductive sphere show structured, object-dependent changes in the multi-electrode voltage readout relative to empty-water baselines, with detectability varying across excitation of 5 to 20 V and frequencies from 1 mHz to 1 kHz. These findings motivate systematic investigation of gripper-integrated electrosense as a complementary pre-contact cue for underwater soft manipulation.

</details>


### [143] [BotDirector: Robot Storytelling Across the Symmetrical Reality with Multi-modal Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03223)
*Zhe Sun,Meng Wang,Lei Wang,Yuxi Wang,Wanxin Li,Yujia Peng,Zhenliang Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A tangible, language-driven robot storytelling system enabling children to create narratives with everyday objects, transformed into robot swarm motions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the technical barrier of robot storytelling for children by combining tangible interaction, large-language models, and swarm robotics to enable flexible, kid-driven narratives.

Method: Children arrange a play area with personal objects; an LLM agent generates narratives; narratives are mapped to motion sequences based on a scene map and character constraints; a swarm of self‑navigating robots executes the motions.

Result: Enhanced robot storytelling with flexible, child-created scenarios; supports creation of robot dramas using everyday objects; demonstrates an end-to-end pipeline from narrative generation to embodied robot performance.

Conclusion: The approach democratizes robot storytelling for children and highlights how tangible interfaces and LLMs can render embodied, adaptable narratives through swarm robotics.

Abstract: Robot storytelling offers a unique blend of technological innovation and creative expression that engages children in unprecedented ways. However, the technical aspects are often too complicated for children. We propose an interactive system that facilitates robot storytelling with tangible and natural language interactions. Children arrange the playground with their own stuff and create narratives with an LLM agent. The created narratives are transformed into a motion sequence based on the map and characters, and the motions are executed by self-navigating swarm robots. This system enhances robot storytelling with flexible scenarios, enabling young children to create robot dramas with everyday objects.

</details>


### [144] [GeoAlign: Beyond Semantics with State-Guided Spatial Alignment in VLA Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03240)
*Yizhi Chen,Zhanxiang Cao,Xinyi Peng,Yixiao Zheng,Xiaxi Si,Yiheng Li,Liyun Yan,Keqi Zhu,Xueyun Chen,Shengcheng Fu,Tianyue Zhan,Yufei Jia,Jinming Yao,Yan Xie,Kun Wang,Cewu Lu,Yue Gao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GeoAlign introduces a geometry-aware post-training framework for Vision–Language–Action models by learning an RGB geometry branch with robot RGB-D supervision to produce Geometry-Enhanced Post-Trained (GEP) features; a proprioception-guided querying mechanism retrieves geometry tokens for action prediction, enabling robust spatial alignment and dynamic affordance selection in manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap where VLA models optimize semantic grounding but neglect geometry-aware spatial alignment and dynamic affordance required for executable manipulation.

Method: Post-train an RGB geometry branch using robot-domain RGB-D supervision to convert RGB inputs into GEP features. A robot proprioceptive state queries the GEP feature grid to produce compact, phase-dependent geometry tokens that guide the policy during action rollout.

Result: Significant performance gains across diverse benchmarks: 99.0% on LIBERO; 85.3% across three SimplerEnv-Fractal tasks; 78.8% on eight geometry-critical real-world ALOHA tasks; ablations confirm the contributions of geometry post-training and proprioceptive-state-guided querying.

Conclusion: Geometry-guided post-training effectively aligns perception with geometry-aware control in VLA policies, improving real-world manipulation performance and generalization across simulated and real tasks.

Abstract: Current Vision--Language--Action (VLA) models often optimize for semantic grounding, whereas executable manipulation requires geometry-aware spatial alignment and dynamic affordance selection. We introduce GeoAlign, a state-guided spatial alignment architecture for VLA policy learning. GeoAlign post-trains an RGB geometry branch with robot-domain RGB-D supervision, yielding RGB-derived Geometry-Enhanced Post-Trained (GEP) features for policy rollout. The robot's proprioceptive state queries the GEP feature grid, producing compact, phase-dependent geometry tokens for action prediction. GeoAlign achieves 99.0% on LIBERO, 85.3% across three SimplerEnv-Fractal tasks, and 78.8% on eight geometry-critical real-world ALOHA tasks, with ablations confirming the value of geometry post-training and proprioceptive-state-guided querying.

</details>


### [145] [AirDreamer: Generalist Drone Navigation with World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03252)
*Zian Liu,Andong Yang,Chunkai Yang,Ruidong An,Chao Gao,Guyue Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A reinforcement-learning navigation framework that leverages a world-model for environmental understanding to generalize to unseen, cluttered drone environments, using sparse rewards and emergent yaw control; demonstrates improved performance and sim-to-real transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address poor generalization in drone navigation caused by environment-specific perception pipelines and hand-crafted rules; inspired by animal navigation to achieve robust behavior in unseen layouts.

Method: A hierarchical approach where a reinforcement-learning policy operates on top of a world-model-based environment understanding. Utilizes a sparse reward signal without shaping terms to discourage local minima and encourage yaw control; evaluated in simulation and on real drones with emergent obstacle-escaping behaviors.

Result: Outperforms baselines by 5.3% in challenging maps, demonstrates effective sim-to-real transfer without deployment tuning, and shows emergent capabilities to escape local optima; code will be publicly available.

Conclusion: The framework improves generalization to unseen layouts and robustness to local minima by integrating world-model understanding with RL, enabling better real-world transfer and reducing reliance on handcrafted perception pipelines.

Abstract: Navigating a drone in unseen and cluttered environments requires reliable generalization to unseen scene layouts and understanding of environmental structure relative to the robot's capabilities. Previous methods, which assume the same environment configuration, often rely heavily on human-designed perception pipelines and predefined rules to guide the robot toward the target. This process is environment-dependent and generalizes poorly across environments. Inspired by animal navigation behavior, we design a navigation framework that navigates with a reinforcement-learning-based policy on top of a world-model-based environment understanding to overcome these issues. In addition, a sparse reward function without hand-crafted shaping terms is designed to avoid local minima traps and encourage yaw control behaviors. In simulation and on real drones, our method exhibits emergent capabilities for navigating complex, unseen environments and escaping local optima where other methods fail. In challenging maps, it achieves a 5.3% higher navigation success rate than best baseline. Furthermore, the proposed framework achieves effective sim-to-real transfer without any tuning during deployment. The code will be publicly available.

</details>


### [146] [Wheel-Mounted/GNSS Fusion with AI-Aided Position Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03265)
*Gal Versano,Itzik Klein*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hybrid neural inertial navigation approach using wheel-mounted IMUs, periodic trajectories, and an NN regressor in an error-state EKF, achieving ~46% RMSE reduction with GNSS updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses localization challenges for autonomous ground vehicles by reducing inertial drift and leveraging GNSS, using a lightweight NN and periodic trajectories to enhance inertial signal quality.

Method: Fuse wheel-mounted inertial sensors with enforced periodic trajectories; train a simple neural network to regress displacement from inertial readings; integrate within an error-state EKF with GNSS position updates; validate via real-world experiments with multiple sensors.

Result: Position RMSE reduced by ~46% compared to standard wheel-mounted inertial sensor fusion with GNSS updates; demonstrated in real-world tests.

Conclusion: The hybrid framework improves localization accuracy and robustness by increasing inertial SNR through periodic trajectories and efficient neural regression, enabling reliable GNSS-aided wheel-IMU navigation.

Abstract: Accurate and robust localization remains a fundamental challenge for autonomous ground vehicles. In this work, we propose a hybrid neural inertial navigation framework that integrates a wheel-mounted inertial sensors, enforced periodic trajectories, and a simple, efficient neural network capable of regressing vehicle displacement with GNSS position updates in an error-state extended Kalman filter. The periodic trajectories increase the inertial signal-to-noise ratio, allowing the network to use only inertial readings to estimate displacement. The approach is validated through real-world experiments using multiple wheel-mounted inertial sensors. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a significant improvement in positioning accuracy, reducing the position root mean squared error by approximately 46 % compared to standard wheel-mounted inertial sensor fusion with GNSS updates.

</details>


### [147] [EaDex: A Cross-Embodiment Dexterous Manipulation Framework from Low-Cost Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03268)
*Qian Zhao,Xin Tong,Chengdong Wu,Yang Yang,Yingtian Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EaDex presents a low-cost, multi-embodiment dexterous manipulation framework that uses a simple RGB-D demo pipeline and a dynamic demonstration annealing strategy, achieving substantial gains over non-annealed baselines across cross-embodiment tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dexterous manipulation learning is data- and training-intensive: reinforcement learning requires extensive interaction, while imitation learning depends on costly, high-quality demonstrations. A low-cost, scalable demonstration pipeline is needed to enable rapid training and cross-embodiment generalization.

Method: Data layer: capture human hand motions with a single RGB-D camera; construct structured demonstration data via MANO-based hand modeling, data normalization, and motion retargeting to accommodate multiple hands. Learning layer: employ a contact-reward-based dynamic demonstration annealing mechanism that leverages demonstrations in early exploration and gradually shifts to autonomous optimization using accumulating contact rewards. Evaluation on a custom dataset across three dexterous hands and three articulated objects, totaling nine cross-embodiment settings.

Result: The approach achieves a 55.3% relative improvement over the baseline without demonstration annealing on the nine settings, validating the effectiveness of the low-cost demonstration pipeline and the dynamic annealing strategy for faster, cross-embodiment dexterous manipulation learning.

Conclusion: EaDex demonstrates that low-cost demonstration data combined with a contact-reward dynamic annealing schedule can substantially accelerate dexterous manipulation learning across multiple hands and objects, enabling rapid data generation and improved training efficiency.

Abstract: Dexterous manipulation learning has long been hindered by the high costs of data and training, as pure reinforcement learning typically requires large-scale interactive exploration and imitation learning depends on high-quality demonstrations that are expensive to collect. To address this problem, we propose EaDex, a multi-embodiment dexterous manipulation learning framework under low-cost demonstration conditions, which enables rapid generation of demonstration data and consequently reduces training time for efficient dexterous manipulation. At the data level, EaDex captures human hand motions using only a single RGB-D camera and constructs structured demonstration data through MANO-based hand modeling, data normalization, and motion retargeting. At the learning level, we introduce a contact-reward-based dynamic demonstration annealing mechanism, which guides early-stage exploration under demonstration and gradually transitions to autonomous optimization with accumulating contact rewards. Using our custom dataset, we evaluate EaDex on three dexterous hands and three articulated object-opening tasks, covering nine cross-embodiment manipulation settings, achieving a 55.3% relative improvement over the baseline without demonstration annealing. These results validate the effectiveness of the proposed low-cost demonstration pipeline and the dynamic demonstration annealing strategy for dexterous manipulation learning.

</details>


### [148] [Bridging Predictive Uncertainty and Safe Action: Sample-Conditioned Differentiable Planning for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03296)
*Chengzhen Meng,Pei Liu,Zhiyu Huang,Chen Lv,Jun Ma*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A diffusion-based, sample-conditioned differentiable planning framework that uses CVaR tail-risk constraints and a directed graph scene representation to robustly plan trajectories under uncertainty, achieving state-of-the-art results on Waymo and Argoverse 2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty in surrounding traffic poses a major challenge for autonomous driving. There is a gap between expressive uncertainty modeling and interpretable, safe motion planning; existing approaches either overly deterministic or black-box.

Method: Use a conditional diffusion model to generate a diverse set of plausible future trajectories conditioned on observed context. Feed these samples into a differentiable planner that enforces an empirical CVaR tail-risk constraint to mitigate uncertainty. Introduce a directed graph representation for scene context to improve predictive effectiveness and computational efficiency.

Result: Empirically outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in safety, efficiency, and ride comfort on Waymo Open Motion and Argoverse 2. Demonstrated improvements in both predictive effectiveness and computational efficiency; validated through extensive open-loop and closed-loop evaluations.

Conclusion: The proposed sample-conditioned differentiable planning framework bridges expressive uncertainty modeling with interpretable, safe planning. Diffusion-generated futures combined with CVaR constraints yield robust trajectories, and the directed graph scene representation enhances performance and efficiency; suitable for deployment in complex driving environments.

Abstract: Complex, dynamic, and interactive driving environments pose significant challenges for autonomous driving, primarily due to the pervasive uncertainty of surrounding traffic. A fundamental bottleneck in current systems is the disconnect between highly expressive uncertainty modeling and interpretable, safe motion planning. In this paper, we propose a novel sample-conditioned differentiable planning framework that bridges this gap by explicitly incorporating diffusion-generated future trajectories into the optimization process. Rather than compressing predictions into a single deterministic future or relying on black-box end-to-end architectures, our approach leverages a conditional diffusion model to generate a diverse set of plausible future scenarios. Crucially, these samples are directly fed into a differentiable planner, which explicitly mitigates predictive uncertainty via an empirical Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR) tail-risk constraint. This allows the planner to optimize a physically interpretable trajectory that is robust to rare yet safety-critical interactions. Furthermore, we introduce a directed graph representation for scene context that yields substantial improvements in both predictive effectiveness and computational efficiency. Validated through extensive open-loop and closed-loop evaluations on the Waymo Open Motion and Argoverse 2 datasets, our framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in safety, efficiency, and ride comfort.

</details>


### [149] [SplitAdapter: Load-Aware Humanoid Loco-Manipulation via Factorized Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03297)
*Jeonguk Kang,Hanbyel Cho,Sanghyun Kang,Donghan Koo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SplitAdapter introduces factorized, load-aware adapters that freeze a pretrained box-pushing policy and use split world-model objectives, cross-adversarial regularization, and hierarchical FiLM to adapt to varying payload masses (2–6 kg) and pickup/placement heights (0–60 cm), improving full-task success in sim-to-sim and real-world tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humanoid loco-manipulation must remain stable across a range of payloads and contact dynamics, but sim-to-real transfer suffers when object-induced load variation and robot-side dynamics mismatch interact during contact. History-based adapters compress these factors into a single latent, reducing robustness under heavy loads.

Method: Freeze a pretrained box manipulation policy and extend it with object/load and dynamics-aware context encoders. Train using split world-model objectives, GRL-based cross-adversarial regularization, and hierarchical FiLM conditioning. Evaluate in sim-to-sim and real-world deployments across varying masses and heights.

Result: Full-task success improves over the base policy and world-model FiLM baselines across object masses of 2, 4, and 6 kg and pickup/placement heights of 0, 30, and 60 cm, with the largest gains under heavy-load conditions.

Conclusion: Factorized, load-aware adaptation with split world-model objectives and cross-adversarial regularization enhances robustness to load and height variations, improving sim-to-real transfer for humanoid loco-manipulation.

Abstract: Humanoid loco-manipulation requires stable whole-body control under varying object masses and pickup/placement heights. This becomes particularly challenging in sim-to-real transfer, where object-induced load variation and robot-side dynamics mismatch interact during physical contact. Existing history-based adapters often compress these factors into a single latent representation, which can weaken robustness under heavy-load manipulation. We propose \textbf{SplitAdapter: Load-Aware Humanoid Loco-Manipulation via Factorized Adaptation}, which freezes a pretrained box manipulation policy and extends it with object/load and dynamics-aware context encoders trained with split world-model objectives, GRL-based cross-adversarial regularization, and hierarchical Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM). In sim-to-sim experiments and real-world deployment, SplitAdapter improves Full-task success over the base policy and world-model FiLM baselines across object masses of $2$, $4$, and $6$ kg and pickup/placement heights of $0$, $30$, and $60$ cm, with the largest improvements under heavy-load conditions.

</details>


### [150] [RobotValues: Evaluating Household Robots When Human Values Conflict](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03312)
*Jongwook Han,Hyeongjin Kim,Yohan Jo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RobotValues: a 10k-sample benchmark to evaluate household robot planners on value-conflict decisions; models show default value preferences and struggle to override them when values conflict, underscoring need to evaluate value alignment beyond task success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domestic robots must navigate value conflicts (e.g., safety vs. privacy, efficiency, autonomy). Current benchmarks emphasize task completion; there is a gap for measuring value preferences.

Method: Generate 10K realistic household scenarios via LLM-assisted scenario generation; extract stakeholder-valued preferences; create images; ensure quality with automatic controls; evaluate vision-language models in robotics on action selection under value conflicts.

Result: VLMs reveal default preferences toward safety and accommodation, with underselection of privacy-prioritizing actions; when asked to prioritize conflicting values, models fail to override about 80% of the time, selecting incorrect actions.

Conclusion: Robot evaluation should assess ability to choose among plausible actions in value-conflict situations, not only task success or safety; promotes designing systems attuned to human values.

Abstract: While household robots are often evaluated based on task completion, everyday domestic environments involve value-conflicting situations in which robots are expected to choose actions that prioritize other values than task success, such as human autonomy, efficiency, or social appropriateness. Yet, there are no benchmarks for evaluating robots' value preferences in such scenarios. We introduce RobotValues, a benchmark to evaluate household robot planners in 10K value-conflict scenarios. Each instance consists of a realistic household image with multiple plausible robot actions that prioritize different human values. We construct RobotValues through LLM-assisted scenario generation, stakeholder-grounded value extraction, image generation and automatic quality control. Using RobotValues we evaluate VLMs used in robotics and find that models exhibit default value preferences, including safety and accommodation, while underselecting privacy-prioritizing actions. When the models are instructed to prioritize specific values that conflict with their own preferences, they often fail to override their default actions, choosing incorrect actions for 80% of the time. These findings suggest that household robot evaluation should measure not only task completion or safety compliance, but also whether robots can choose among plausible actions when human values conflict.

</details>


### [151] [GPU-Parallel Multi-Task Reinforcement Learning with Demonstration Guided Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03335)
*Rui Zhang,Qiwei Wu,Zhengyu Zhang,Tao Li,Yunrong Guo,Junjie Lai,Renjing Xu,Weihua Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A GPU-parallel, multi-task RL benchmark framework MT-Libero for structured manipulation task families; introduces DGPO—demonstration-guided PPO with adaptive behavior cloning; demonstrates improved performance and scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable scalable, multi-task reinforcement learning for structured manipulation tasks on GPUs, addressing the gap where most systems train a single specialist per task and leveraging sparse rewards and limited prior data.

Method: Provide a construction methodology to convert structured manipulation task families into GPU-parallel multi-task RL benchmarks and instantiate it as MT-Libero using LIBERO assets and Isaac Lab task predicates. Propose DGPO, an on-policy demonstration-guided method that combines importance-weighted PPO with adaptive behavior cloning on matched demonstration actions, enabling tunable preference toward demonstrated task distributions.

Result: MT-Libero supports simultaneous RL over heterogeneous task suites with parallel rendering, physics randomization, and state-input or visual-input policies. DGPO outperforms prior-free RL and existing demonstration-based methods while preserving the stability and online improvement of on-policy PPO.

Conclusion: The MT-Libero benchmark and DGPO method enable practical multi-task RL in structured manipulation tasks, offering tunable demonstration preference and improved performance while maintaining on-policy stability.

Abstract: Large scale GPU-parallel reinforcement learning has changed what can be trained in robot simulation, yet most systems still optimize one specialist policy per task. We propose a construction methodology for turning structured manipulation task families into GPU-parallel multi-task RL benchmarks, and instantiate it as MT-Libero using LIBERO assets and task predicates in Isaac Lab. The resulting benchmark supports simultaneous reinforcement learning over heterogeneous task suites with parallel rendering, physics randomization, and state-input or visual-input policies. To make such training practical under sparse success signals and limited prior data, we further propose DGPO, an on-policy demonstration guided method that combines importance weighted PPO with adaptive behavior cloning on matched demonstration actions. DGPO enables a tunable preference toward demonstrated task distributions, outperforming both prior-free RL and existing demonstration-based methods while preserving the stability and online improvement benefits of on-policy PPO.

</details>


### [152] [Autonomous Navigation System for Library Service Robot Based on Unitree Go2 Edu](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03340)
*Aoduo Li,Haoran Lv,Bingquan Ou,Jianfeng Li,Yingdong Li,Zimeng Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ROS 2 navigation for a quadruped library robot integrating RTAB-Map SLAM, AMCL EKF for localization, and Nav2 with A* and DWA; achieves high success rates in static/dynamic scenes and 3.7 cm map error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for quiet, safe autonomous traversal in libraries amid mobility discontinuities and clutter, where wheeled platforms struggle.

Method: Platform: Unitree Go2 Edu quad, sensors: 4D LiDAR, front depth camera, IMU. SLAM: RTAB-Map. Localization: AMCL + EKF sensor fusion. Planning: Nav2 with A* global planner and DWA local planner. Handles transitions, clutter, partial blockages; evaluated in real-world library settings.

Result: Static scene 100% success; low-density dynamic 96%; high-density dynamic 88%. Map validation mean error 3.7 cm against surveyed control distances.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasible ROS 2 navigation for legged platforms in library environments, successfully handling mobility discontinuities; results indicate robust performance; future work could explore generalization and efficiency improvements.

Abstract: Libraries require autonomous robots to move quietly through narrow aisles while remaining safe around readers, chairs, bags, and carts. This paper presents a ROS 2 navigation system for a Unitree Go2 Edu quadruped equipped with a 4D LiDAR, a front depth camera, and an IMU. Rather than assuming the library is rough terrain, we target the practical mobility discontinuities of real deployments, including floor transitions, temporary clutter, and partially blocked passages where low-clearance wheeled platforms are less tolerant. RTAB-Map is used for visual-LiDAR SLAM, AMCL and EKF-based sensor fusion provide localization, and a Nav2 stack with A* and DWA supports planning and local avoidance. In a real library, the system achieves 100%, 96%, and 88% success rates in static, low-density dynamic, and high-density dynamic scenes, while map validation against surveyed control distances yields a mean metric error of 3.7 cm.

</details>


### [153] [eMEM: A Hybrid Spatio-Temporal Memory System For Embodied Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03374)
*A. Haroon Rasheed,Maria Kabtoul*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: eMEM is a multi-index embodied-memory system combining SQL-based structured storage, approximate semantic search, and spatial indexing unified under a graph, with a hippocampal-inspired consolidation pipeline and 10 recall tools; paired with eMEM-Bench v1 benchmark evaluating eight cognitive-psychology memory paradigms in ProcTHOR-10K, achieving strong performance and enabling diagnostic analysis against baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Embodied agents operate in physical environments and require memory that is searchable by meaning, space, and time. Existing architectures (text streams or knowledge graphs) fail to provide integrated multi-modal querying and in-process execution needed for real-time embodied tasks.

Method: Hybrid graph-based memory with a multi-index backend: SQL ITE for structured storage, hnswlib for semantic approximate nearest-neighbor search, and an R-tree for spatial queries, all unified behind a single graph model. A tiered consolidation pipeline converts percepts into compressed summaries, mirroring hippocampal-neocortical consolidation. Ten agent-facing recall tools expose memory retrieval primitives (concept-to-location, cross-layer recall) for LLM tool calls. Fully embedded and in-process. Introduction of eMEM-Bench v1: ProcTHOR-10K-based benchmark organized around eight cognitive-psychology paradigms (DRM lures, pattern separation, pattern completion, source monitoring, context-dependent retrieval, long-horizon interference, serial position, foil-augmented retention).

Result: eMEM achieves an 80.8 weighted mean over 988 probes with a flat retention curve from 1 hour to 1 year for room-unique items. Ablation with a pure RAG baseline (flat_rag) loses 30 points on context-dependent retrieval and 29 points on DRM lure rejection, isolating gains due to multi-layer storage and consolidation. Code for both system and benchmark released.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates a viable, integrated memory architecture for embodied agents that supports meaning, space, and time queries, and provides a diagnostic benchmark aligned with human memory literature. It outlines a path for evaluating and improving embodied memory systems beyond existing surface-task benchmarks.

Abstract: We present eMEM (Embodied Memory), a hybrid graph-based memory system for embodied agents operating in physical environments. Current agent memory architectures, such as Generative Agents, MemGPT, and A-MEM, treat memory as text streams or knowledge graphs, but embodied agents require memory that is simultaneously searchable by meaning, space, and time. eMEM fills this gap with a multi-index architecture (SQL ITE for structured storage, hnswlib for approximate nearest neighbour semantic search, and an R-tree for spatial queries) unified behind a single graph model. A tiered consolidation pipeline transforms raw perceptual observations into compressed summaries, mirroring hippocampal-neocortical consolidation in biological systems. Ten agent-facing recall tools expose memory retrieval primitives, including concept-to-location resolution and cross layer recall, as first-class operations for LLM tool calling. The system is fully embedded and runs in-process alongside the agent. In addition we introduce eMEM-Bench v1, a benchmark we construct over ProcTHOR-10K scenes for embodied memory evaluation. The benchmark is organised explicitly around eight cognitive-psychology paradigms (DRM lures, pattern separation, pattern completion, source monitoring, context-dependent retrieval, long-horizon interference, serial position, and a foil augmented retention curve), each chosen so that the result is interpretable against the broader memory-systems literature in humans and prior agent-memory systems; a level of diagnostic that surface-task benchmarks like LoCoMo or OpenEQA cannot provide. eMEM scores 80.8 weighted mean over 988 probes, with a flat retention curve at ceiling from 1 h to 1 yr of simulated delay on room-unique items. We show that a pure RAG baseline (the flat_rag ablation) loses 30 pt on context dependent retrieval and 29 pt on DRM lure rejection, isolating the contribution of multi-layer storage and consolidation respectively. We release both the system and the benchmark code.

</details>


### [154] [Grasp-Then-Plan with Failure Attribution: A Closed Two-Stage Framework for Precise and Generalizable Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03385)
*Jiahao Xu,Peiyuan Wang,Hanzhuo Zhang,Zihao Yu,Tianyu Fu,Hao Chen,Xuanhao Xiang,Jianbo Yu,Chenchen Fu,Wanyuan Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Two-stage grasp-then-plan framework (GTP-FA) uses failure attribution to diagnose and optimize grasps and planning, yielding higher success across RL/IL/diffusion/VLA settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decoupled grasping and motion planning often misattributes failures and leads to inefficient trial-and-error; long-horizon manipulation requires diagnosis-guided optimization focusing on failure modes and high-risk initial states.

Method: Train a failure attribution model from failed trajectories to produce a distribution over failure modes; diagnose-guided optimization: inject task-level priors and risk penalties into grasp candidate scoring; planning-side optimization targets high-risk initial states via data collection and fine-tuning; evaluate on simulation and real robots across multiple base learners (RL, IL, diffusion-policy, VLA).

Result: GTP-FA improves base learners' performance, achieving substantially higher overall task success rates in both simulation and real-robot experiments.

Conclusion: Diagnosis-driven, two-stage grasp-then-plan with failure attribution can effectively identify and suppress unstable or incompatible grasps and address genuine planning bottlenecks, generalizing across learning paradigms.

Abstract: In robotic manipulation, the tight coupling between grasping and motion planning often obscures the true source of failure, leading to inefficient trial-and-error.
