To enable efficient long-horizon manipulation, we propose GTP-FA (Grasp-Then-Plan with Failure Attribution), a task-oriented two-stage grasp-then-plan framework that generates grasp candidates and performs downstream motion planning conditioned on the selected grasp. Given a failed manipulation trajectory, we learn a failure attribution model that generalizes to unseen grasps and produces a stable distribution over failure modes for diagnosis-guided optimization. Based on these attribution results, we then optimize both modules in a diagnosis-driven manner: on the grasping side, we inject task-level priors and risk penalties into grasp candidate scoring and optimization to suppress unstable or task-incompatible grasps; on the planning side, we target high-risk initial states through data collection and fine-tuning to address genuine planning bottlenecks. We evaluate the proposed framework in both simulation and real-robot experiments, and show that GTP-FA improves the corresponding base learners across RL, IL, diffusion-policy, and VLA-based settings, achieving substantially higher overall task success rates.

</details>


### [155] [Extreme Motion Generation via Hybrid Null-Space Control for Straight-Line Path Following](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03390)
*Xinyi Yuan,Weiwei Wan,Kensuke Harada*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hybrid, long-horizon motion-generation framework that blends RL-based policy with a model-based controller to maximize Cartesian path length along predefined trajectories, using diffusion-based initialization for joint states. Demonstrates 27% average rollout-length gain on 10k straight-line tasks with a 7-DoF Franka FR3; some tasks show large extreme-motion gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To maximize exploitation of a manipulator's kinematic reach by extending the path length along a given trajectory, while safely handling near-boundary regions through a hybrid control policy.

Method: A step-level hybrid controller switches between an RL-based controller for long-horizon exploration and a model-based controller near safety boundaries, with switching based on normalized joint-limit distance. Initial configurations are drawn via conditional diffusion-based sampling to inject a learned motion prior.

Result: Evaluated on 10,000 straight-line path-following tasks with a 7-DoF Franka FR3; the approach extends the average rollout length by 27% over a model-based baseline, with some tasks achieving pronounced extension toward motion extremes.

Conclusion: Long-horizon decisions are effectively delegated to learning in the far-from-boundary region, while the near-boundary region is handled by a classical controller. The approach yields meaningful gains in extreme-motion tasks and can be extended to related manipulation problems.

Abstract: This work studies ``extreme motion generation'', which aims to maximize the Cartesian path length along a pre-defined trajectory within the manipulator's workspace. This objective is important in industry as long as path-following is fundamental to a large variety of tasks such as surface coating and welding. More critically, extreme motion enables a fixed-base manipulator to exploit the kinematic capability under limited reachability. However, such exploitation is challenging in practice, as the manipulator must actively avoid the safety boundary through execution, which is inherently a long-horizon problem. Accordingly, we claim that long-horizon decision-making should be delegated to a learning-based policy to maximize exploitation, while a classical model-based controller covers the near-boundary region, where the learning policy degrades sharply due to sparse data coverage. In detail, our proposed method is a step-level hybrid controller that switches between an RL-based and a model-based controller according to the normalized joint-limit distance. The initial joint configuration is sampled through conditional diffusion-based sampling, which improves the achievable path length based on the learned motion prior. We evaluate the proposed framework on 10,000 straight-line path-following tasks with a 7-DoF Franka FR3, extending the average rollout length by 27\% over the model-based baseline. Notably, certain tasks yield a pronounced extension toward the motion extreme, as reflected in the maximum improvement reported in the statistical results. The project website and related videos of this paper can be found at https://yuan-xinyi.github.io/extreme-motion-generation/.

</details>


### [156] [OpenEAI-Platform: An Open-source Embodied Artificial Intelligence Hardware-Software Unified Platform](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03392)
*Jinyuan Zhang,Luoyi Fan,Leiyu Wang,Yeqiang Wang,Yicheng Zhu,Cewu Lu,Nanyang Ye*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OpenEAI-Platform provides an open-source hardware–software stack for embodied AI: a low-cost 6+1-DOF arm and a VLA model trained with open data; it achieves strong real-world performance and will release assets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To lower cost and improve reproducibility in embodied AI by combining accessible hardware with a reproducible VLA model trained on open datasets.

Method: Hardware: 6+1 DOF arm (OpenEAI-Arm) with open mechanical designs and compliant control. Software: OpenEAI-VLA built on Qwen3-VL-4B with a Diffusion Transformer action head; two-stage training using only open-source data. Evaluation on four real-world manipulation tasks; comparison with two commercial arms; vs a pi0-like baseline for VLA.

Result: OpenEAI-Arm outperforms two commercial 6+1-dof arms under the same policy; OpenEAI-VLA attains success rates comparable to the pi0 baseline with limited pretraining; plan to release hardware, drivers, models, pipelines.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that open hardware plus reproducible multimodal policies can achieve competitive real-world performance; fosters reproducible research and scalable data collection; materials to be released post-acceptance.

Abstract: Embodied AI in the real world requires both accurate hardware and robust vision-language-action (VLA) policies. We present OpenEAI-Platform, a fully open-source platform that integrates a low-cost 6+1 degree-of-freedom (dof) robotic arm (OpenEAI-Arm) and a reproducible VLA model (OpenEAI-VLA). OpenEAI-Arm provides open-source mechanical designs for low manufacturing cost and compliant control methods for higher accuracy. OpenEAI-VLA builds on Qwen3-VL-4B and uses a Diffusion Transformer action head, and is trained in two stages with only open-source robot and multimodal datasets. Across four real-world manipulation tasks, OpenEAI-Arm outperforms two commercial 6+1-dof arms under the same policy, and OpenEAI-VLA achieves success rates comparable to the large-scale pretrained pi0 baseline with only limited pretraining data. We will release the full hardware designs, drivers, models, and training/data pipelines to support reproducible research and scalable data collection. Our codes, layouts, and models will be released after the paper is accepted.

</details>


### [157] [Reliability-Guided Depth Fusion for Glare-Resilient Navigation Costmaps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03421)
*Shang-En Tsai*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Glare-resilient occupancy mapping via per-pixel depth reliability with DRM-Net and RGf fusion for safe real-time indoor navigation under reflective glare.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Specular glare on reflective floors, glass boundaries, and glossy surfaces corrupts active-stereo RGB-D depth, creating holes/spikes and phantom obstacles in occupancy maps; there is a need to treat glare as a measurement-reliability issue to maintain safe navigation.

Method: Introduce DRM-Net, a lightweight Depth Reliability Map network that predicts per-pixel measurement trustworthiness under glare; use a reliability-guided weighted-and-gated fusion (RGF) to modulate occupancy updates before integrating measurements; support robust training with pose-aligned multi-view reference-depth to reduce circular supervision bias; evaluate with fusion-variant ablations, parameter sensitivity, cross-condition tests, paired navigation comparisons, reliability-map metrics, and runtime profiling.

Result: The method reduces false obstacle insertion, preserves free space, and maintains real-time throughput under glare from reflective floors, glass walls, and natural light; demonstrated on a real mobile platform with Intel RealSense D435 and Jetson Orin Nano.

Conclusion: Treat glare as a measurement-reliability problem rather than a dense depth-completion problem, enabling safer indoor navigation by robustly fusing depth information under specular interference.

Abstract: Specular glare on reflective floors, glass boundaries, and glossy indoor surfaces frequently corrupts active-stereo RGB-D depth measurements, producing holes and spikes that accumulate as persistent phantom obstacles in occupancy-grid costmaps. This paper presents a glare-resilient costmap construction method based on explicit depth-reliability modeling. A lightweight Depth Reliability Map network (DRM-Net) predicts per-pixel measurement trustworthiness under specular interference, and a reliability-guided weighted-and-gated fusion (RGF) mechanism modulates occupancy updates before corrupted measurements are accumulated into the map. To support robust training and evaluation, the method uses pose-aligned multi-view reference-depth construction to reduce circular-supervision bias and is evaluated through fusion-variant ablations, parameter-sensitivity analysis, cross-condition tests, paired navigation comparisons, reliability-map metrics, and embedded runtime profiling. Experiments on a real mobile robotic platform equipped with an Intel RealSense D435 and a Jetson Orin Nano show that the proposed method reduces false obstacle insertion, improves free-space preservation, and maintains real-time throughput under reflective-floor, glass-wall, and natural-light glare conditions. These results support treating glare as a measurement-reliability problem rather than as a dense depth-completion problem for safety-critical indoor navigation.

</details>


### [158] [PerchRL: Vision-Based Agile Perching on Inclined Platforms under Rapid and Irregular Motion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03441)
*Zihong Lu,Zongzhuo Liu,Huaxu Li,Jinqiang Cui,Jie Mei,Youmin Gong,U Kei Cheang,Boyu Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PerchRL: a two-stage RL framework for vision-based perching on moving inclined platforms using state-based pre-training and vision-based fine-tuning, with randomized trajectories, temporal augmentation, visibility-aware augmentation, and active perception rewards; validated by sim-to-real experiments and cross-platform deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous perching on moving inclined platforms is essential for air-ground collaboration but is hard due to limited FOV and dynamic platform motion, leading to robustness and generalization challenges.

Method: Two-stage learning: (1) state-based pre-training to learn basic control; (2) vision-based fine-tuning with randomized platform trajectories, temporal augmentation to capture motion patterns. During fine-tuning, employ visibility-aware state augmentation and active perception rewards to handle intermittent visual loss. Real-time capable and tested across multiple quadrotor platforms; source code to be released.

Result: Demonstrates feasibility, stability, and real-time performance in extensive simulations and real-world experiments; successful multi-platform deployment indicates good generalization and adaptability.

Conclusion: PerchRL improves robustness and generalization for vision-based quadrotor perching on moving inclined platforms and is ready for broader community use via code release.

Abstract: Autonomous vision-based perching of quadrotors on moving inclined platforms is critical for air-ground collaboration but remains challenging due to the limited field of view (FOV). In this paper, we propose PerchRL, a reinforcement learning (RL) framework for vision-based agile perching on inclined platforms under rapid and irregular motion. Specifically, we employ a two-stage learning strategy consisting of state-based pre-training followed by vision-based fine-tuning. To improve generalization across diverse platform motions, we employ randomized platform trajectories to prevent overfitting and temporal augmentation methods to capture latent motion patterns from historical observations. During vision-based fine-tuning, a hybrid learning framework consisting of visibility-aware state augmentation and active perception rewards is presented to improve robustness under intermittent visual loss. Extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate the feasibility, stability, and real-time performance of PerchRL, while successful deployment across distinct quadrotor platforms further validates its adaptability. The source code will be released to benefit the community.

</details>


### [159] [Human2Humanoid: Physics-Aware Cross-Morphology Motion Retargeting for Humanoid Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03476)
*Tianchen Huang,Feiyang Yuan,Junchi Gu,Shurui Fang,Xiaohu Zhang,Yu Wang,Wei Gao,Shiwu Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Unsupervised retargeting of human motion to humanoid robots using a CycleGAN with a skeleton-aware GCN, morphology-invariant end-effector loss, and physics-aware feasibility constraints, enabling high-fidelity retargeting to Unitree G1 without paired data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge morphological discrepancies and lack of paired motion data to enable high-fidelity humanoid motion retargeting for teleoperation, imitation learning, and HRI.

Method: CycleGAN-based architecture with a skeleton-aware graph convolutional network to capture topology-dependent motion features; morphology-invariant end-effector consistency loss to align normalized end-effector trajectories; explicit physics-aware feasibility constraints to reproduce source contact patterns.

Result: Demonstrates successful retargeting of human motion to Unitree G1 without paired data and outperforms existing methods in downstream controllability and physical feasibility.

Conclusion: The framework achieves high-fidelity, physically plausible, domain-gap-robust motion retargeting across differing embodiments, enabling practical human-to-humanoid transfer with unsupervised data.

Abstract: Retargeting human motion to humanoid robots is critical for teleoperation, imitation learning and human-robot interaction. However, it remains challenging because of substantial morphological discrepancies between humans and robots, including differences in skeletal topology, limb proportions and degrees of freedom, as well as the scarcity of paired motion data. This paper presents Human2Humanoid, an unsupervised motion retargeting framework that transfers human motions to humanoid robot behaviors with high fidelity. To bridge the domain gap under unpaired data, we adopt a CycleGAN-based architecture equipped with a skeleton-aware graph convolutional network to capture topology-dependent motion features. To address cross-domain scale mismatches, we introduce a morphology-invariant end-effector consistency loss that aligns normalized end-effector trajectories to preserve motion semantics across embodiments. To improve physical plausibility and reduce contact artifacts, we impose explicit physics-aware feasibility constraints to encourage reproduction of the contact patterns in the source motion. Experimental results show that the proposed method successfully retargets human motion to the Unitree G1 humanoid robot without paired data, and outperforms existing methods in both downstream controllability and physical feasibility.

</details>


### [160] [SPADE: Sketch-guided Path Planning Augmented with Diffusion Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03512)
*Charbel Abi Hana,Tatiana Ghantous,Mikael Khalil,Anthony Rizk*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A diffusion-augmented behavioral cloning framework for AMR path planning, featuring an ROS 2 annotation tool and a demonstrations dataset. It yields substantial improvements in pose accuracy (APE) and distribution similarity (FID) with far fewer parameters, while preserving real-time, on-edge capability and diffusion-like generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limited generalization to unseen environments and robustness in demonstration collection for imitation-learning-based path planning by enhancing data quality and diversity using diffusion-based augmentation.

Method: An ROS 2-based annotation tool is introduced, and a diffusion-based augmentation strategy is integrated into a baseline behavioral cloning model. The approach uses a dataset of expert demonstrations and conducts ablation studies to evaluate robustness.

Result: The enhanced framework outperforms state-of-the-art methods with 39.1% lower Absolute Pose Error (APE) and 33.5% lower Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) while having 93.8% fewer trainable parameters. It achieves diffusion-level generalization and maintains real-time, on-edge performance.

Conclusion: The proposed system improves generalization and robustness in imitation-based path planning while remaining computationally efficient; diffusion-based augmentation appears to significantly enhance the quality and diversity of training data for AMR planners.

Abstract: Path planning is essential for Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs). Conventional methods for incorporating human preferences into planning typically rely on either complex reward engineering or hardware-intensive solutions. Recent state-of-the-art frameworks leverage imitation learning to train behavior-specific path planning models from expert demonstrations. However, these approaches face two key limitations: limited generalization to unseen environments and low robustness in demonstration collection. To address these challenges, this work introduces an enhanced framework that focuses on two main contributions: an overhauled annotation tool built on ROS 2, and a novel training strategy that integrates diffusion-based augmentation into baseline behavioral cloning models. A dataset of expert demonstrations is provided and evaluated through ablation studies to assess the robustness of the proposed solution. The enhanced approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods with 39.1% lower Absolute Pose Error (APE) and 33.5% lower Fr'echet Inception Distance (FID) while having 93.8% less trainable parameters. Moreover it attains diffusion-level generalization while preserving the real-time, on-edge properties of state-of-the-art models.

</details>


### [161] [Bionic Human-Motion Style Transfer for Physically Executable Whole-Body Control of Humanoid Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03536)
*Tianchen Huang,Mingkuan Zhao,Yang Gao,Feiyang Yuan,Junchi Gu,Xiaohu Zhang,Dongdong Zhao,Shi Yan,Yu Wang,Wei Gao,Shiwu Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Exemplar-driven style transfer for humanoid motion using a physics-aware diffusion model to convert short human style exemplars into executable robot trajectories, achieving high success in simulation and real-robot trials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need reusable, expressive, readable motion styles for humanoid robots in human environments. Fixed demonstrations limit reusability; desirable to transfer short human exemplars to diverse task content while preserving motion content.

Method: Develop a physics-aware multi-condition latent diffusion model that fuses style, content, and trajectory conditions; apply classifier-free guidance to adjust style intensity without retraining; enforce contact-consistency and temporal-smoothness regularization during training; convert generated references to G1-compatible robot trajectories and execute them via a preview-based whole-body tracking policy trained with a cluster-and-distill strategy.

Result: Simulation and Unitree G1 experiments show successful transfer of short human exemplars to diverse motion contents; reduced contact and jitter artifacts compared with animation-oriented baselines; 96.0% success rate over 125 real-robot trials.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of using short human motion exemplars as reusable bionic sources for physically executable expressive humanoid motion.

Abstract: Expressive whole-body motion is important for humanoid robots operating in human environments, where robots are expected to move stably while presenting readable and adjustable body behaviors. However, most expressive motions are still obtained from fixed demonstrations or manually designed scripts, making it difficult to reuse a demonstrated style across different motion contents. Inspired by the way human motion styles convey affective and intentional cues through gait rhythm, posture, arm swing and body sway, this paper proposes a bionic generation-to-control framework for exemplar-driven style transfer on humanoid robots. Given a short human style exemplar and a target content motion, the proposed framework generates a stylized whole-body reference that preserves the intended motion content while transferring the demonstrated style. A physics-aware multi-condition latent diffusion model is developed to fuse style, content and trajectory conditions, and classifier-free guidance is used to adjust the style intensity without retraining. To improve hardware executability, contact-consistency and temporal-smoothness regularization are imposed on decoded motions during training. The generated references are then converted into G1-compatible robot references and executed by a preview-based whole-body tracking policy trained with a cluster-and-distill strategy. Simulation and Unitree G1 experiments show that the proposed method can transfer short human style exemplars to diverse robot motion contents, reduce contact and jitter artifacts compared with animation-oriented style-transfer baselines, and achieve a 96.0% success rate over 125 reported real-robot trials. The results demonstrate the feasibility of using short human motion exemplars as reusable bionic sources for physically executable expressive humanoid motion.

</details>


### [162] [Static and Dynamic Representations for Tactile Contact-Angle Estimation with Event-Based Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03545)
*Yanhui Lu,Efi Psomopoulou,Benjamin Ward-Cherrier*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Static contour representation provides the most robust contact-angle estimation from event streams in NeuroTac, with sub-10 ms latency across scenarios and lowest MAE under scenario-specific training (0.160° rolling, 0.251° stop-phase). Dynamic and combined representations lag and show greater variability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable high-frequency, low-latency contact-angle estimation for contact-rich manipulation using event-based tactile sensing, by comparing how different spatial contour representations derived from event streams perform.

Method: Three event-derived contour representations (dynamic, static, and their combination) are computed from NeuroTac event streams. The study evaluates processing latency (P99 < 10 ms) across sampling intervals and compares MAE under continuous rolling and random stop-phase interruptions, with scenario-specific training.

Result: All representations achieve P99 latency below 10 ms. Static representation yields the best mean MAE (0.160° during continuous rolling; 0.251° during stop-phase interruptions) and exhibits smaller performance fluctuations across variations in speed and indentation depth compared to dynamic and combined representations.

Conclusion: Static spatial contour representation is the most robust for contact-angle estimation in the tested conditions, offering lower error and more consistent performance under scenario-specific training; dynamic and combined representations offer no clear advantage in the reported scenarios.

Abstract: Event-based tactile sensing offers low-latency signal acquisition for contact-rich robotic interaction. This paper investigates contact-angle estimation using event streams from an event-based tactile sensor (NeuroTac) and compares three event-derived spatial contour representations: a dynamic representation capturing recent event activity, a static representation recovering a more persistent contact state, and their combined representation. Across the evaluated motion scenarios, all representation pipelines exhibited P99 processing latency below 10 ms at all tested sampling intervals, demonstrating their potential for high-frequency event-based tactile angle estimation in robotic manipulation. The static representation consistently achieved marginally better performance than the dynamic and combined representations under scenario-specific training, yielding a mean overall MAE of 0.160° during continuous sensor rolling and a stop-phase mean MAE of 0.251° during randomly inserted motion interruptions. It also exhibited smaller performance fluctuations across speed and indentation depth variations than the other two representations.

</details>


### [163] [NVIDIA Isaac Sim: Enabling Scalable, GPU-Accelerated Simulation for Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03551)
*Sicong Gao,Maurice Pagnucco,Tomasz Bednarz,Yang Song*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Isaac Sim is a GPU-accelerated robotics simulator; the paper systematically analyzes its architecture, usage, and limitations, benchmarking it against other simulators and outlining key future directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond treating Isaac Sim as just another simulator by providing a systematic, architecture- and usage-focused survey that clarifies strengths, gaps, and practical usability.

Method: Survey methodology: describe architecture, compare with other simulators; analyze representative studies across five domains; identify usage patterns in data generation and high-fidelity simulation; discuss limitations and usability constraints; outline future directions.

Result: Key findings include GPU-accelerated parallelism and synthetic data pipelines, distinct architectural characteristics, typical usage patterns in data generation and high-fidelity simulation, identified limitations and usability constraints, applicability across five domains, and proposed future directions such as physics open-world learning and simulation-centric training.

Conclusion: The survey provides a structured understanding of Isaac Sim's role in robotics research, aiding researchers and practitioners in adoption and future work.

Abstract: Simulation has become a core infrastructure for robotics research. Unlike previous simulators, NVIDIA Isaac Sim leverages GPU acceleration to enable large-scale parallel training and physics-accurate modeling. Its synthetic data generation pipeline alleviates the scarcity of high-quality training data, supporting data-driven robot learning and large-scale simulation-centric experimentation. However, existing surveys often treat it as one simulator among many, without a systematic analysis of its architectural characteristics, usage patterns, and limitations. This survey reviews Isaac Sim from system and application perspectives, outlining its architecture and comparing it with widely used simulators. We analyze representative studies across five major domains and summarize common usage patterns, particularly in data generation and high-fidelity simulation. We also outline key future directions and challenges, including physics open-world learning, simulation-centric training and practical usability constraints.

</details>


### [164] [Partially Observable Adversarial Patch Attacks on Vision-Language-Action Models in Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03556)
*Xiaofei Wang,Mingliang Han,Tianyu Hao,Yi Yang,Yun-Bo Zhao,Keke Tang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Two-phase adversarial patch attack under partial observability sustains disruptions in VLA robotics by localizing patches via attention and degrading both perception and control, reducing task success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses realism gap: previous attacks assumed full trajectory access; study vulnerability of VLA agents to fixed patches when adversary only observes a short prefix.

Method: Phase 1: localize patch regions using model attention aligned with instruction. Phase 2: optimize patch to degrade semantic grounding of target objects and increase action trajectory curvature, causing perception and control failures.

Result: Attacks remain effective under partial observability in both simulation and real robots, inducing long-horizon disruptions and substantially lowering task success rates.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of partial-observability adversarial threats to VLA robotics and the need for defenses against patches that couple perception and control; future work includes detection and robust training.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models are gaining attention in robotics, yet their robustness to adversarial attacks remains largely unexplored. Existing work shows that adversarial patches can mislead VLA-based robots but assumes full access to the entire execution trajectory, an unrealistic requirement in practice. We address this limitation by formulating a partially observable threat model, where the adversary can exploit only a short prefix of the trajectory to generate a fixed patch applied to all subsequent frames. Under this setting, we propose a two-phase framework. First, we localize the patch using the model's attention maps to identify visually critical regions that correspond to the full instruction. Then, we optimize the patch to disrupt the semantic grounding of target objects and increase the curvature of action trajectories, thereby compounding failures in both perception and control. Extensive experiments in simulation and real-world robotic environments show that our method sustains adversarial effects under partial observability, inducing long-horizon disruptions and significantly reducing task success rates.

</details>


### [165] [CANMOT: Class-Aware Noise Modeling for Multi-Object Tracking in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03590)
*Timo Osterburg,Stefan Schütte,Torsten Bertram*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces CANMOT, a class-aware, object-aligned noise model for KF-based 3D MOT that uses class-specific diagonal process and measurement covariances, optionally in object frame to preserve longitudinal-lateral anisotropy; achieves improved tracking and fewer ID switches on nuScenes, but uncertainty calibration remains imperfect.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KF-based MOT typically uses global noise covariances shared across classes, assuming identical uncertainty; this ignores heterogeneity across traffic participants. A class-aware, object-aligned approach could better capture true uncertainty and improve tracking reliability.

Method: Incorporates class-specific diagonal process (Q) and measurement (R) covariance matrices into the KF-based MOT pipeline, with optional expression in the object coordinate frame to preserve anisotropy. Evaluates via nuScenes with ANEES and χ^2-based tests for uncertainty calibration; provides code: learned-3d-nms repository.

Result: Experiments on nuScenes show improved tracking performance and substantially reduced identity switches compared with state-of-the-art methods. Baselines exhibit overconfidence; CANMOT improves calibration but still demonstrates substantial inconsistency in uncertainty estimates.

Conclusion: Class-aware, object-aligned noise modeling improves KF-based 3D MOT performance without changing the filtering core, but residual inconsistency in uncertainty estimation indicates room for further research in calibration and reliability of EF (uncertainty) estimates.

Abstract: Kalman filter (KF)-based multi-object tracking (MOT) remains a strong baseline for autonomous driving due to its strong performance, computational efficiency and interpretability. In most practical systems, the process noise and measurement noise covariances are defined globally and shared across object classes, presuming identical uncertainty characteristics across heterogeneous traffic participants.
  This work revisits this assumption and proposes CANMOT, a class-aware and object-aligned noise modeling framework for KF-based 3D MOT. Class-specific diagonal process and measurement covariance matrices are introduced and optionally expressed in the object coordinate frame to preserve longitudinal-lateral anisotropy.
  Systematic experiments on the nuScenes benchmark show that class-aware and object-aligned noise modeling improves tracking performance and substantially reduces identity switches compared to state-of-the-art (SotA). In addition, the consistency of the estimated uncertainty is analyzed using the Average Normalized Estimation Error Squared (ANEES) and $χ^2$-based violation tests. The results reveal severe overconfidence in standard KF-based MOT baselines. While the proposed formulation improves calibration without modifying the underlying filtering framework, it still exhibits substantial inconsistency, highlighting the need for further research in this area.
  Code is available at https://github.com/rst-tu-dortmund/learned-3d-nms.

</details>


### [166] [PHASER: Phase-Aware and Semantic Experience Replay for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03598)
*Ziyang Chen,Shaoguang Wang,Weiyu Guo,Qianyi Cai,He Zhang,Pengteng Li,Yiren Zhao,Yandong Guo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PHASER is a framework that mitigates catastrophic forgetting in Vision-Language-Action robots during continual learning, using phase-centric memory allocation and adaptive interference routing, enhanced by Auto-PC for unsupervised phase boundary detection; it achieves significant ASR gains on LIBERO benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-ended skill acquisition with VLA models suffers catastrophic forgetting; uniform experience replay fails to align with the temporal structure of manipulation trajectories, and forgetting varies across tasks. A architecture-agnostic, autonomous continual-learning approach is needed to enable lifelong adaptation.

Method: PHASER introduces (1) phase-centric capacity allocation to ensure equal memory for all sub-skills, preventing phase starvation, and (2) a multi-modal interference routing strategy that prioritizes historical phases at high risk of forgetting. It also integrates Auto-PC, combining unsupervised action-change-point detection with VLM-based semantic verification to extract temporal boundaries without manual supervision.

Result: Evaluated on LIBERO continual learning suites across three VLA backbones. PHASER yields up to 31% improvement in Average Success Rate (ASR) over matched-budget experience replay and achieves an 87.8% final ASR on the LIBERO-Goal CL setting.

Conclusion: PHASER is architecture-agnostic and effectively mitigates forgetting in continual VLA learning by training-time memory allocation and history-aware interference routing, complemented by autonomous boundary detection via Auto-PC, enabling robust lifelong adaptation in robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have achieved remarkable success in language-conditioned robotic manipulation. However, deploying these models in open-ended environments requires continuously acquiring novel skills, a process that inevitably triggers severe catastrophic forgetting of previously learned behaviors. While experience replay (ER) serves as a standard mitigating strategy, naive uniform sampling fundamentally misaligns with the temporal characteristics of manipulation trajectories. It systematically under-samples brief but causally critical sub-skills, leading to phase starvation, and completely overlooks the varying degrees of forgetting across historical tasks. To overcome these limitations, we introduce PHASER, an architecture-agnostic continual learning framework. PHASER employs a phase-centric capacity allocation to guarantee equal memory support for all sub-skills, coupled with a multi-modal interference routing strategy that dynamically prioritizes historical phases at high risk of forgetting. Furthermore, to enable fully autonomous lifelong adaptation, we integrate Auto-PC, a lightweight pipeline combining unsupervised action-signal change-point detection with VLM-based semantic verification to extract temporal boundaries without intensive manual supervision. Evaluated across three VLA backbones on LIBERO continual learning suites, PHASER yields substantial empirical improvements, increasing Average Success Rate (ASR) by up to 31% over matched-budget ER and achieving an 87.8% final ASR on the LIBERO-Goal CL setting.

</details>


### [167] [A 3D Isovist World Model -- Revealing a City's Unseen Geometry and Its Emergent Cross-City Signature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03609)
*Xuhui Lin,Stephen Law,Nanjiang Chen,Kunyao Li,Tao Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes an embodied world model predicting 3D navigable geometry via isovists (spherical depth maps), not appearance. Predicts next isovist from past isovists and action using a depth residual decoder, with a persistent BEV map and self-rollout sampling. Finds a city-specific latent signature that generalizes across Manhattan and Paris, and releases dataset/pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current world models focus on appearance or flattened geometry, neglecting navigable 3D structure. A predictive target that captures open navigable space (negative space) without photometric entanglement is needed for robust navigation in urban environments.

Method: Model predicts the next 3D isovist (distance to nearest surface in all directions) from a short history of isovists and a movement action. Uses a depth residual decoder to preserve sharp geometry, trained with self-rollout scheduled sampling to mitigate error accumulation, and maintains a persistent bird's-eye-view latent map for cross-path consistency.

Result: The model learns a lightweight, interpretable geometric representation. Unexpected emergent property: a single city-blind model trained on two cities develops a linear-decodable cross-city spatial signature in its temporal latents, independent of appearance. An open dataset and pipeline are released.

Conclusion: This work provides a geometric substrate for spatial reasoning in embodied AI and urban analysis, offering a compact, interpretable model of navigable 3D space and demonstrating cross-city generalization via dynamics rather than appearance.

Abstract: Embodied agents that navigate cities rely on world models that predict how their surroundings will change as they move. But for navigation, what matters is not what the buildings look like; it is where the agent can go. Most world models nonetheless predict appearance, learning how a scene looks rather than the space an agent can move through. Those that do target geometry, such as bird's-eye-view occupancy grids, flatten the three-dimensional environment onto a ground plane, discarding the above-ground and multi-level structure that shapes real navigation. What is missing is a predictive target that captures the navigable geometry an agent actually traverses, without photometric entanglement and without collapsing the third dimension. Our key idea is to model the open volume between buildings, the negative space, encoded as a 3D isovist: a spherical visibility-depth map recording the distance to the nearest surface in every direction. We introduce an embodied world model that predicts the next isovist from a short history of past isovists and a movement action. The prediction is formulated as a depth residual so the decoder inherits sharp building edges, trained with self-rollout scheduled sampling to keep corrupted context on the geometry manifold, and equipped with a persistent latent bird's-eye-view spatial map for cross-path consistency. Our central finding is emergent and unexpected: a single city-blind model trained on Manhattan and Paris develops a cross-city spatial signature, with city identity linearly decodable from its temporal latents far above single-frame baselines, so the signature lives in the learned dynamics rather than in appearance. The representation is lightweight, interpretable, and reproducible, offering a geometric substrate for spatial reasoning in embodied AI, robotics, and urban analysis, released with an open dataset and pipeline.

</details>


### [168] [GN0: Toward a Unified Paradigm for Generation, Evaluation, and Policy Learning in Visual-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03682)
*Xinhai Li,Xiaotao Zhang,Yuehao Huang,Jiankun Dong,Tianhang Wang,Sunyao Zhou,Yunzi Wu,Chengnuo Sun,Yunfei Ge,Qizhen Weng,Chi Zhang,Chenjia Bai,Xuelong Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A unified data, simulation, and learning platform for embodied navigation: GN-Matrix, GN-Bench, and BAE; achieves state-of-the-art VLN performance via 3DGS-based data, BEV memory, and RL fine-tuning with DAgger.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLN tasks are hindered by limited, heterogeneous data and the need for long-horizon, reliable physical-world grounding. A high-fidelity, scalable data/simulation/learning pipeline with dynamic avatars and memory representations is needed to improve generalization and real-world applicability.

Method: Develop a 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) engine; create GN-Matrix by automated data generation in diverse 3D scenes; build GN-Bench, a BEV-based benchmark with dynamic 3DGS avatars for HRI; propose Break and Establish (BAE), an RL-driven navigation foundation model trained by supervised learning and DAgger, followed by RL exploration; unify map-based and map-free VLN tasks; GN-BAE encodes high-fidelity 3DGS-rendered Bird's Eye View as compact memory to enhance latent spatial reasoning in VLMs.

Result: GN0 achieves superior performance over state-of-the-art VLN methods on GN-Bench and VLN-CE; the framework demonstrates strong generalization and robust embodied navigation capabilities; GN-BAE's BEV memory improves spatial reasoning; overall, the data-simulation-learning pipeline enables scalable embodied navigation research and potential industrial deployment.

Conclusion: GN-Matrix provides a cohesive framework spanning data, simulation, and learning to advance embodied navigation, enabling research and industrial use-cases in embodied AI, human-robot interaction, and long-horizon navigation.

Abstract: Embodied navigation connects intelligent agents with the physical world and is fundamental for general robotic intelligence. Limited availability and quality of navigation data have constrained Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) systems' generalization and long-horizon capabilities. To address this, we curate diverse 3D scenes and develop an automated pipeline for large-scale navigation data, resulting in the GN-Matrix dataset. Building on a 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) engine, we introduce a high-fidelity simulation platform supporting interactive roaming and collision-aware navigation. We further propose GN-Bench, the first BEV-based benchmark incorporating dynamic 3DGS avatars for human-robot interaction evaluation. To leverage the simulator, we develop an RL-driven navigation foundation model, Break and Establish (BAE). After supervised learning, DAgger exposes the model to rollout-induced states, breaking narrow expert-centric distributions and enabling downstream RL exploration. This unified VLN paradigm integrates map-based and map-free tasks, including instruction following, human following, and goal navigation. GN-BAE formalizes high-fidelity 3DGS-rendered Bird's Eye View representations as compact memory, unlocking latent spatial reasoning in VLMs. Extensive evaluations on GN-Bench and VLN-CE show that GN0 outperforms state-of-the-art VLN methods. Overall, GN-Matrix offers a unified framework spanning data, simulation, and learning, advancing embodied navigation in research and industrial applications.

</details>


### [169] [Face versus Body Tracking for Human-Robot Interaction: An Egocentric Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03694)
*Jessica Wenninger,Gabriel Skantze*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An egocentric HRI study with Furhat robot develops a custom annotated dataset to examine face vs. body tracking, longer spatial memory, and re-identification (ReID) for reducing identity switches (IDSW). The optimized pipeline cuts IDSW by 49% but reveals trade-offs: memory helps occlusions but not complex events; ReID improves body tracking yet spikes facial IDSW. The work highlights the need for native social-dynamics benchmarks beyond standard datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable continuous engagement assessment in social robots by overcoming egocentric challenges (occlusions, occluded faces, identity switches) not well captured by surveillance/autonomous-driving benchmarks.

Method: Create a dense, egocentric dataset captured with Furhat; perform systematic evaluation separating detection vs tracking errors; compare face vs body tracking; evaluate extended spatial memory and appearance-based re-identification (ReID); measure ID switches and tracking stability.

Result: Spatial memory reduces prolonged occlusions but struggles with complex dynamic events; ReID stabilizes body tracking but increases facial ID switches due to profile-angle sensitivity; integration yields a 49% reduction in IDSW; standard benchmarks lack dense close-quarter occlusions, underscoring the need for native social-dynamics data.

Conclusion: Native social-dynamics data are essential to validate HRI perception models; developers must balance memory and ReID-related trade-offs; benchmarks should reflect real egocentric social interactions to properly assess HRI perception pipelines.

Abstract: To enable meaningful human-robot interaction (HRI), a robot must continuously assess engagement by consistently tracking users over time. State-of-the-art computer vision models, however, are heavily optimized for surveillance or autonomous driving. A social robot faces distinct egocentric challenges, such as humans bouncing, obstructing each other, or leaving the frame. Frequent identity switches (IDSW) cause the robot to lose its footing mid-conversation. To address this, we introduce a novel, custom-annotated egocentric dataset collected via the Furhat robot to capture complex social dynamics. We present a systematic evaluation isolating detection errors from tracking logic, comparing face versus body tracking, and assessing the impact of extended spatial memory and appearance re-identification (ReID). Results indicate that increasing spatial memory mitigates prolonged occlusions but fails on complex dynamic events. Integrating ReID resolves complex switches but exhibits opposing effects: it substantially improves body tracking stability, yet causes facial IDSW to spike due to profile angle sensitivity. Ultimately, our optimized pipeline reduces IDSW by 49\%, mitigating interaction breakdowns. Because standard benchmarks lack dense, close-quarter occlusions, this work highlights the critical need for natively captured social dynamics to truly validate HRI perception models.

</details>


### [170] [Neural Navigation Functions for Zero-Shot Generalizable Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03756)
*Benjamin D. Shaffer,Pei-An Hsieh,Brooks Kinch,Nathaniel Trask,M. Ani Hsieh*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Neural-NF: a neural reactive navigation function using elliptic PDE with learned coefficients enabling zero-shot transfer across unseen geometries; guarantees collision-free, monotone descent to a global minimum; linearly solvable optimal control; outperforms direct value-function predictors by up to 5x.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Develop a transferable navigation function that preserves the structure and guarantees of classical planning while leveraging data-driven learning to adapt to new geometries without retraining.

Method: Embed learned, data-driven Laplacian-derived features into local PDE coefficients of an elliptic boundary-value problem; solve to obtain a globally consistent value function per domain; the policy inherits collision-free safety, monotone descent, and a global minimum by construction; enables a linearly-solvable optimal-control interpretation for all parameter settings.

Result: Achieves strong zero-shot transfer across diverse geometries and outperforms learned planners that directly predict the value function by up to 5×.

Conclusion: Hybrid framework that couples learning with a structured PDE-planner to attain transferable, safe, and optimally-guided navigation across unseen environments, preserving planner properties while leveraging data-driven adaptation.

Abstract: We introduce Neural Navigation Functions (Neural-NF), a learned reactive navigation function capable of zero-shot transfer across unseen environment geometries. Neural-NF places data-driven adaptation within a structured elliptic planner, where the navigation objective is learned while planner structure is preserved by construction. Specifically, intrinsic Laplacian-derived features are mapped to local PDE coefficients, and solving the resulting boundary value problem produces a globally consistent value function on each target domain. For every admissible learned model, the resulting policy is collision-free, provides monotonic descent and a global minimum at the goal by construction. This admits a linearly-solvable optimal-control interpretation for any parameter setting. Empirically, Neural-NF achieves strong zero-shot transfer across diverse geometries and outperforms learned planners that directly predict the value function by up to a $5\times$ improvement.

</details>


### [171] [Revisiting Embodied Chain-of-Thought for Generalizable Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03784)
*Nan Sun,Yuan Zhang,Yongkun Yang,Wentao Zhao,Peiyan Li,Jun Guo,Wenxuan Song,Pengxiang Ding,Runze Suo,Yifei Su,Xin Xiao,Xinghang Li,Huaping Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ERVLA introduces a large-scale embodied CoT framework that uses reasoning traces as representation-shaping supervision rather than test-time reasoning, addressing scaling and instability of explicit CoT. It achieves state-of-the-art results on LIBERO-Plus and strong generalization on VLABench, with solid real-robot performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Embodied CoT can improve vision-language-action (VLA) models, but explicit CoT as autoregressive prompts causes error compounding and poor scalability. The goal is to enable robust, scalable reasoning-grounded action without test-time CoT decoding.

Method: Construct the largest embodied CoT corpus to date (978,743 trajectories; 226.3M samples; 2,592.5 hours). Propose ERVLA, a VLA model that uses embodied CoT as representation-shaping supervision during training and employs a reasoning-dropout strategy to predict actions directly during inference, avoiding CoT decoding. This design aims to improve data-scale scalability and stabilize learning-action coupling.

Result: ERVLA achieves 86.9% success on LIBERO-Plus and 53.2% on VLABench, with strong out-of-distribution generalization and superior real-robot performance on tasks requiring semantic disambiguation and long-horizon execution.

Conclusion: Leveraging embodied CoT as supervision with reasoning-dropout effectively decouples reasoning from action, improving scalability and real-world performance; results suggest strong potential for large-scale VLA models; code, data, and checkpoints will be released.

Abstract: Embodied chain-of-thought (CoT) aims to bridge linguistic reasoning and robotic control, but its effective form and integration strategy remain underexplored. In this paper, we revisit embodied CoT for vision-language-action (VLA) models at large scale. We construct the largest embodied CoT corpus to date, comprising 978,743 trajectories, 226.3M samples, and 2592.5 hours of robot data. Through extensive experiments, we find that effective embodied CoT should ground high-level semantic understanding into concrete action guidance, such as end-effector movement descriptions and image-space trajectories, while high-level reasoning alone brings only marginal gains. We further show that explicit CoT does not scale reliably when used as an autoregressive action prefix, as it suffers from compounding inference errors and unstable reasoning-action coupling. To address these limitations, we propose ERVLA, a VLA model that uses embodied CoT as representation-shaping supervision rather than mandatory test-time reasoning. ERVLA is trained with a reasoning-dropout strategy, enabling the model to absorb rich reasoning traces during training while predicting actions directly without CoT decoding during inference. This design improves scalability with increasing pre-training data and avoids autoregressive instability. ERVLA achieves state-of-the-art performance on LIBERO-Plus with an 86.9% success rate and reaches 53.2% success rate on VLABench, demonstrating strong out-of-distribution generalization. In real-robot experiments, ERVLA further outperforms competitive state-of-the-art baselines, especially on tasks requiring semantic disambiguation and long-horizon execution. Code, data, and model checkpoints will be released.

</details>


### [172] [Worth Remembering: Surprise-Gated Robot Episodic Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03787)
*Nicolas Gorlo,Derek K. Wise,Alberto Speranzon,Luca Carlone*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Bayesian surprise gates episodic memory in a deployment-agnostic, semantically rich latent space (V-JEPA-2) to store only high-utility past episodes, improving long-term robotic memory efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memory constraints prevent storing all past events; generalist robots require generic, task-agnostic memory with high future utility; selective memory formation aims to generalize beyond a priori tasks.

Method: Compute Bayesian surprise in the latent space from V-JEPA-2 to gate memory formation; integrate gated episodic memory with a 4D scene-graph based spatial memory; evaluate on robot question answering and event segmentation tasks against state-of-the-art and supervised/non-causal baselines, including an unsupervised causal approach.

Result: Consistent improvements over state-of-the-art in robot QA (≥12% gains for temporal, spatial, and binary questions). In event segmentation, surpasses supervised and non-causal methods with an unsupervised causal method.

Conclusion: Bayesian surprise-based memory gating in a deployment-agnostic semantic latent space enhances long-term memory utility for generalist robots, yielding stronger performance in memory-dependent tasks and suggesting practical viability for real-world deployments.

Abstract: Robots solving generalist tasks need to be able to ground instructions in their past experience, since humans may refer to notable past events when giving a task (e.g., ``Take me to where the chemical spill happened yesterday''). Since memory limits make storing all past events infeasible, long-term robot memory must be selective, ideally retaining only those episodes with high utility for future tasks. However, future tasks are not typically given a priori for generalist robots. To select generically useful memories, we propose Bayesian surprise as a gating mechanism for memory formation. We present an approach to compute surprise in a semantically rich deployment-agnostic latent space provided by V-JEPA-2. Using our gated episodic memory to augment 4D scene graph-based spatial memory, we show a consistent improvement over state-of-the-art benchmarks in robot question answering, outperforming prior robot memory methods by $\geq12\%$ for temporal, spatial, and binary questions, and surpassing the performance of supervised and non-causal methods with an unsupervised causal method in event segmentation tasks.

</details>


### [173] [Optimal Design and Analytical Modeling of a Soft Fin-Ray Effect Gripper Finger Using the Finite Rigid Elements Method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03798)
*Sara Adeli,Hassan Sayyaadi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Finite Ray (FR) inspired soft gripper finger designed for gentle handling of delicate agricultural products; FREM-based modeling with detailed ANSYS FEM; geometry optimization yields close agreement with experiments (3% FREM error, 2% ANSYS error).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for adaptable, force-controlled soft grippers capable of handling delicate, irregular agricultural items while overcoming soft-robotics modeling challenges (nonlinearity, infinite DOF, material variability).

Method: Develop a Finite Rigid Elements Method (FREM) framework to model the Fin Ray Effect finger. Create a detailed FEM in ANSYS, validate analytical results via simulation and experimental testing, and optimize finger geometry based on tip displacement, total deflection, stress distribution, and contact force. The optimal design uses a 30 mm length, 10 mm rib spacing, seven ribs at -15°, and 1 mm rib thickness.

Result: FREM predicted finger deformation with 3% error; ANSYS model achieved 2% error. The finger configuration above was identified as optimal under the four criteria.

Conclusion: The FRE-based approach provides accurate predictive capability for force-controlled soft gripper design, enabling reliable development of force controllers and effective handling of delicate produce in agricultural applications.

Abstract: Fin Ray-inspired soft grippers offer a promising solution for gently handling delicate, irregular objects, especially in agriculture. The objective of this research is to design, fabricate, and model a Fin Ray Effect (FRE) soft gripper finger to enable precise force control in future applications. This design aims to gently grasp delicate agricultural products, such as tomatoes, that require both adaptability and accurate force application. To address the inherent challenges of soft robotics, including nonlinear behavior, infinite degrees of freedom, and variable material properties, the Finite Rigid Elements Method (FREM) was employed for modeling. This method preserves analytical accuracy while providing a reliable foundation for the development of a force controller in later stages. A detailed Finite Element Model (FEM) was created using ANSYS, and the analytical results were validated through simulation and experimental testing. The gripper's fingers were optimized based on four key criteria: tip displacement, total deflection, stress distribution, and contact force. The optimal finger configuration includes a length of 30 mm, rib spacing of 10 mm, seven ribs angled at -15 deg, and a rib thickness of 1 mm. Theoretical modeling using the FREM predicted finger deformation with a 3% error, while the ANSYS numerical model achieved 2% error.

</details>


### [174] [Let the Dynamics Flow: Stable Flow Matching Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03834)
*Rodrigo Pérez-Dattari,Francisco Leiva,Andrea Testa,Leonel Rozo,Javier Ruiz del Solar,Noémie Jaquier*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces Stable Flow Matching Dynamical Systems (SFMDS), integrating flow matching with Lyapunov stability guarantees via soft (penalty) or hard (architectural) constraints, extended to Lie groups; demonstrates stable, multimodal motion generation in simulation and on a humanoid robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imitation learning with flow matching can produce expressive, multimodal policies but lacks formal stability guarantees, risking unsafe or unstable behavior. A framework that combines high-capacity generative modeling with Lyapunov-based stability biases is needed, especially for high- and low-dimensional state spaces and non-Euclidean manifolds (Lie groups).

Method: Parametrize dynamical systems using flow matching while enforcing stability through two variants: (i) soft constraint via a penalty term during learning, and (ii) hard structural constraint embedded in the model architecture. Extend both formulations to Lie groups. Evaluate on benchmarks, in simulation, and on a humanoid robot.

Result: SFMDS learns stable, scalable, and multimodal dynamical systems across low- and high-dimensional spaces, enabling safe and expressive motion generation in both simulated environments and on a humanoid robot.

Conclusion: SFMDS successfully unites high-capacity generative flow matching with formal Lyapunov stability guarantees, offering flexible soft and hard constraint approaches and broadening applicability via Lie-group extensions; a promising route for safe imitation learning in robotics.

Abstract: Flow matching has recently emerged as a powerful approach for imitation learning, enabling scalable, expressive, and multimodal motion policies. However, incorporating formal stability guarantees into these generative models, a prerequisite to ensure safe and generalizable robot behaviors, remains a significant challenge. While modeling robot motions as dynamical systems allows for such stability-based inductive biases, existing frameworks struggle to capture the rich action distributions inherent in complex robotic tasks. This paper introduces Stable Flow Matching Dynamical Systems (SFMDS), a novel framework that bridges the gap between high-capacity generative modeling and formal Lyapunov stability guarantees. SFMDS parametrizes dynamical systems via flow matching while simultaneously constraining the model to a family of stable solutions. We propose two variants: a soft constraint based on a penalty term, and a hard structural constraint embedded directly in the model architecture. We further extend both formulations to Lie groups. Experiments on benchmark datasets, in simulation, and on a humanoid robot show that SFMDS learns stable, scalable, and multimodal dynamical systems in low- and high-dimensional state spaces, enabling safe and expressive robot motion generation.

</details>


### [175] [Denoising Tells When to Replan: Denoising-Variance Adaptive Chunking for Flow-Based Robot Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03847)
*Xiangdong Feng,Yuxuan Cheng,Chen Shi,Boyao Han,Yuxuan Yan,Yitong Hong,Zhuotao Tian,Li Jiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DVAC adaptively sets action execution length in flow-based policies by measuring denoising-action variance, improving success and reducing replanning frequency across several robotic manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed action chunk horizons in flow-based policies fail to match the varying needs of predictable motion vs. contact/precision phases, leading to suboptimal replanning frequency and task performance.

Method: DVAC computes the variance of clean-action estimates across final denoising steps to identify a stable prefix of a predicted chunk; executes that prefix and replans before high-variance actions are taken. A rolling local-variance scale calibrates the threshold for transfer across tasks and rollouts.

Result: On LIBERO, RoboTwin, CALVIN, and real-world manipulation, DVAC improves task success (e.g., LIBERO from 94.75% to 98.00%) and reduces replanning by 43.0%, with aggregate gains across other tasks and improved real-world efficiency.

Conclusion: Adaptive denoising-variance chunking enhances action coherence and planning efficiency, enabling more reliable execution of flow-based policies across diverse manipulation scenarios.

Abstract: Action chunking has become a common inference strategy for flow-based robot policies, improving action coherence by modeling multi-step temporal dependencies in demonstrations. However, the execution horizon is still typically set as an empirical fixed value, overlooking that predictable free-space motions and precision-critical interaction phases often require different replanning frequencies. In this work, we first show that the denoising process of flow-based policies contains an intrinsic signal of task phases: clean-action estimates remain stable during predictable motion phases, but fluctuate more strongly around contact-rich or precision-sensitive operations. Motivated by this observation, we propose DVAC (Denoising-Variance Adaptive Chunking), a test-time method that adaptively determines how many actions to execute from each predicted chunk. DVAC measures the variance of clean-action estimates over the final denoising steps, executes the stable low-variance prefix, and replans before high-variance future actions are committed. To transfer across tasks and rollouts, DVAC further calibrates the threshold with a rolling estimate of the local variance scale. Experiments on LIBERO, RoboTwin, CALVIN, and real-world manipulation show that DVAC improves task success while reducing replanning frequency. With a $π_{0.5}$-based policy, DVAC improves LIBERO success from 94.75% to 98.00% and reduces replanning by 43.0%, while also yielding aggregate gains on RoboTwin and CALVIN and improving real-world execution efficiency.

</details>


### [176] [Semantic-weighted ICP for LiDAR Odometry: Class-Aware Residual Reweighting for Robust Scan Registration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03905)
*Vasco Carvalho,Tiago Barros,Urbano J. Nunes*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Semantic class-weighted ICP for LiDAR odometry assigns per-point weights based on semantic-driven stability priors, enabling informative but potentially unstable structures to contribute while mitigating dynamics; improves pose estimation in challenging scenes but effectiveness is scene-dependent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic and unstructured environments cause unreliable correspondences in geometric registration. Semantic cues can help, but not all scene elements are equally useful for registration; weighting semantic classes by expected geometric stability can balance robustness and informativeness.

Method: Integrate semantic class-based weighting into the ICP residuals instead of hard filtering. Points are assigned weights according to the predicted geometric stability of their semantic class, allowing dynamic objects to be down-weighted without entirely discarding useful structural elements; evaluated within standard LiDAR odometry pipelines.

Result: Experiments on SemanticKITTI and RELLIS-3D show improved pose estimation, particularly in off-road scenarios where rigid features are scarce. The weighting strategy's effectiveness is highly dependent on scene structure and semantic composition.

Conclusion: Semantic weighting improves registration robustness in LiDAR odometry but its benefits are environment-dependent. Choosing appropriate semantic priors and weights is crucial, suggesting avenues for adaptive or learned weighting schemes and extension to broader datasets.

Abstract: LiDAR odometry is a fundamental component of autonomous robotic systems, relying on geometric registration between consecutive point clouds to estimate ego-motion. However, traditional geometric approaches often degrade in dynamic or unstructured environments due to unreliable correspondences caused by moving objects, sparse geometric features, vegetation, and semantically ambiguous structures. Existing works have shown that, some of these limitations can be addressed by introducing semantic information from the environment in the registration process. In this work, we build on this, and show that not all elements in the environment are equally relevant for registration. Hence, we propose a semantic class-weighted ICP for LiDAR odometry. Instead of strictly filtering out points belonging to specific semantic classes, the proposed approach weights the residuals of points belonging to semantic categories based on their expected geometric stability. This strategy enables informative but potentially unstable structures, to contribute to the registration process while mitigating the influence of dynamic objects. The experimental evaluation was conducted on the SemanticKITTI and RELLIS-3D datasets, which include urban, highway, rural, and off-road environments. The empirical results show that the proposed Semantic-weighted ICP improves pose estimation, especially in challenging off-road scenarios where conventional rigid features are scarce. Furthermore, the analysis reveals that the effectiveness of this weighting strategy is highly environment-dependent, influenced by the structural and semantic composition of the scene.

</details>


### [177] [Multi-Robot Bearing-only Pose Estimation via Angle Rigidity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03931)
*J. Francisco Presenza,Leonardo J. Colombo,Ignacio Mas,Juan I. Giribet*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces a distributed bearing-based pose estimator for time-varying multi-robot systems in 3D.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimate 3D poses (positions) of robots without known orientations using only body-frame bearing measurements; enable distributed estimation with weaker topology requirements.

Method: Use bearings and their derivatives from body frames to infer inter-robot positions in R^3; recover full orientation in SO(3) from positions, bearings, and bearing derivatives; require angle-rigid directed sensing topology; design observer with local uniform exponential stability under persistently exciting motions for a subset of robots.

Result: The observer achieves local uniform exponential stability under PE conditions; simulations demonstrate effectiveness and practicality of the approach.

Conclusion: Angle-rigidity is a weaker and potentially more applicable condition than bearing rigidity for distributed pose estimation; the proposed method enables 3D pose estimation without direct orientation measurements and with directed sensing graphs.

Abstract: This letter proposes a novel distributed bearing-based pose estimator for time-varying multi-robot systems. The method uses angles computed from body-frame bearings to estimate the robots' positions in $\mathbb{R}^3$ without knowledge of their orientations. The orientations in $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ are recovered from the estimated positions, the bearings, and the bearing derivatives. The proposed observer only requires the (directed) sensing topology to be \textit{angle-rigid}, a weaker condition than the commonly used ones like bearing rigidity. Local uniform exponential stability of the proposed observer is established under the assumption of persistently exciting motions for a subset of robots. Simulations are presented and discussed to evaluate the scheme's effectiveness and practicality.

</details>


### [178] [PointAction: 3D Points as Universal Action Representations for Robot Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03943)
*Mutian Tong,Han Jiang,Qiao Feng,Lingjie Liu,Jiatao Gu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PointAction grounds video predictions into robot actions via joint RGB and dynamic 3D pointmaps, enabling 4D-consistent, embodiment-agnostic control with limited supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RGB-only video rollouts lack metric 3D motion, contact geometry, and fine-grained spatial constraints, making action grounding ambiguous; scalable action supervision across tasks/embodiments is costly.

Method: Fine-tune a foundation video generation model to jointly predict future RGB frames and dynamic 3D pointmaps, yielding temporally consistent 3D scene motion; use these point dynamics as a structured interface fed into a diffusion-based action decoder that outputs executable robot actions.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art 4D generation quality on robot scenes; outperforms baselines in simulation; generalizes to two real robot arms unseen during pretraining.

Conclusion: Metric 3D point dynamics provide a structured, embodiment-agnostic action interface, reducing RGB-grounding ambiguity and enabling transfer with limited action supervision.

Abstract: Video-Action Models (VAMs) leverage the broad visual dynamics captured by pre-trained video diffusion models, offering a promising path toward generalizable robot manipulation. However, RGB-only video rollouts are not directly actionable: they leave metric 3D motion, contact geometry, and fine-grained spatial constraints under-specified, making action grounding ambiguous. Meanwhile, scaling action supervision across diverse tasks and embodiments remains costly. We present PointAction, a framework that bridges video predictions to robot actions through explicit point-based 4D modeling. PointAction fine-tunes a foundation video generation model to jointly predict future RGB frames and dynamic 3D pointmaps, producing temporally consistent 3D motion of task-relevant scene geometry. These point dynamics serve as a structured, embodiment-agnostic action interface, which a diffusion-based action decoder maps to executable robot actions. By using metric 3D point dynamics as the interface between video prediction and control, PointAction reduces the ambiguity of RGB-only action grounding and supports transfer across tasks and embodiments with limited action supervision. Experiments show that PointAction achieves state-of-the-art 4D generation quality on robot scenes, outperforms existing baselines in simulation, and generalizes to two real robot arms unseen during pretraining.

</details>


### [179] [Preference-Calibrated Human-in-the-Loop Reinforcement Learning for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03949)
*Zeyi Liu,Guangyao Liu,Yinuo Qu,Yuquan Xue,Bofang Jia,Chunhua Yang,Weihua Gui,Keke Huang,Ziwei Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PACT introduces a preference-calibrated training framework for human-in-the-loop RL that reassigns credit away from suboptimal intervention segments using a progress model, and guides policy with human corrections, improving sample efficiency and success rate in real-robot manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Over-generalized credit assignment in HIL-RL causes overestimation of Q-values for suboptimal segments and misguides policy updates. Leverage implicit human preferences from interventions to reweight learning toward task-relevant actions.

Method: Train a progress model to detect suboptimal segments; create preference pairs from human vs. resampled policy actions at intervention points; derive a counterfactual advantage to penalize Bellman targets for identified suboptimal segments; perform policy updates aligned with human corrective actions in a bounded mean space to complement critic-guided updates.

Result: Evaluated on five real-robot manipulation tasks; average success rate up by 24.5%; convergence speed improved by ~1.3x; code provided.

Conclusion: Preference-calibrated actor-critic training effectively mitigates suboptimal-credit misassignment in HIL-RL and enhances sample efficiency and performance by integrating human preference signals into both the critic and actor updates.

Abstract: Human-in-the-loop reinforcement learning (HIL-RL) improves sample efficiency in real-robot manipulation through online human intervention. However, successful trajectories may include suboptimal actions that deviate from the desired task-execution path and force human intervention. Existing HIL-RL methods typically apply the consistent credit assignment principle to all transitions, uniformly propagating discounted terminal rewards through suboptimal segments, ignoring the actual contribution of each transition to task success. This overestimates Q-values for critic learning and indirectly misguides actor updates toward suboptimal behavior patterns. To this end, we propose PACT, a Preference-calibrated Actor-Critic Training framework that leverages the implicit preference signals induced by intervention to perform credit reassignment on identified suboptimal segments while directly guiding policy training for unbiased critic-actor learning. Specifically, we first design a progress model that learns from human demonstration and identifies suboptimal segments for credit correction. Then, from the human action and resampled policy action at the intervention state, we build preference pairs to define a counterfactual advantage that penalizes Bellman targets of the identified suboptimal segment, enabling directional credit calibration. Moreover, we directly align the policy with human corrective actions in the bounded mean space, providing an additional signal beyond critic-guided updates. Across five real-robot manipulation tasks, PACT improves the average success rate by 24.5% and achieves 1.3 times faster convergence, thereby improving both RL sample efficiency and performance. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/HILRL-A1X-BC05.

</details>


### [180] [Self-Refining Agentic Reinforcement Learning for Vision-Conditioned UAV Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03963)
*Roohan Ahmed Khan,Yasheerah Yaqoot,Muhammad Ahsan Mustafa,Dzmitry Tsetserukou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A multimodal GPT-powered agent autonomously designs rewards, refines policies via PPO, and diagnoses feedback to close the loop, enabling improved UAV navigation and sim-to-real transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward design and manual tuning bottlenecks hinder deep RL deployment for UAV navigation; autonomous reward shaping, policy refinement, and robust deployment are needed.

Method: A multimodal GPT agent interprets task information and visual scenes to generate task-specific reward functions, trains policies with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), and acts as a critic via diagnosis packets to provide feedback. This feedback guides closed-loop reward refinement. For inference, real-world images and language cues identify the active scenario and select the appropriate trained policy.

Result: 71% improvement in policy behavior compared with initial rewards; sim-to-real success rate of 91% and sim-to-real accuracy of 94%.

Conclusion: AgenticRL demonstrates increased autonomy in reward design, policy refinement, and real-world deployment for UAV navigation, with effective closed-loop self-improvement and strong sim-to-real transfer.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning has shown strong potential for enabling autonomous robots to learn complex navigational tasks. However, its practical use still depends heavily on human designed reward functions and repeated manual fine tuning, which is time consuming and does not guarantee high success in the desired task. This paper presents AgenticRL, agent guided reinforcement learning framework that increases autonomy in reward design, policy refinement, and real world deployment for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAV) navigation tasks. AgenticRL uses a multimodal generative pre-trained tansformer (GPT) agent to interpret task information and visual scene observations, generate task specific reward functions, train policies using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm, and then act as a critic by evaluating the trained policy through diagnosis packets to generate feedback. Based on this feedback, the agent identifies failure modes and refines the reward function in a closed loop self improvement process.
