To further leverage the multimodal GPT agent during inference, AgenticRL uses real world images and natural language task information to automatically identify the active scenario and select the appropriate trained policy for execution. The framework is evaluated on multiple navigational tasks, including gate traversal, obstacle avoidance, wall barrier crossing with landing, trajectory following, and motion behavior learning. Experimental results show that the closed loop refinement process improves policy behavior compared with initial rewards by 71%. We also demonstrate sim-to-real transfer of the proposed framework, achieving a real world success rate of 91% and a sim-to-real accuracy of 94%.

</details>


### [181] [Humanoid-GPT: Scaling Data and Structure for Zero-Shot Motion Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03985)
*Zekun Qi,Xuchuan Chen,Dairu Liu,Chenghuai Lin,Yunrui Lian,Sikai Liang,Zhikai Zhang,Yu Guan,Jilong Wang,Wenyao Zhang,Xinqiang Yu,He Wang,Li Yi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Humanoid-GPT is a GPT-style Transformer trained on a billion-scale motion dataset for unified, whole-body motion tracking with strong zero-shot generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior mocap-based trackers rely on shallow models and limited data, creating an agility-generalization trade-off. A scalable, unified model is needed to robustly track dynamic motions and generalize to unseen tasks.

Method: Pre-train a causal-attention, GPT-style Transformer on a 2B-frame retargeted mocap corpus that unifies major mocap datasets with in-house recordings, enabling a single model for rich motion tracking.

Result: The model achieves unprecedented zero-shot generalization to unseen motions and control tasks while tracking highly dynamic and complex motions; extensive scaling analyses show a new performance frontier.

Conclusion: A single generative Transformer can effectively track dynamic human motions and generalize to unseen control tasks, reducing reliance on task-specific trackers and curated datasets.

Abstract: We introduce Humanoid-GPT, a GPT-style Transformer with causal attention trained on a billion-scale motion corpus for whole-body control. Unlike prior shallow MLP trackers constrained by scarce data and an agility-generalization trade-off, Humanoid-GPT is pre-trained on a 2B-frame retargeted corpus that unifies all major mocap datasets with large-scale in-house recordings. Scaling both data and model capacity yields a single generative Transformer that tracks highly dynamic behaviors while achieving unprecedented zero-shot generalization to unseen motions and control tasks. Extensive experiments and scaling analyses show that our model establishes a new performance frontier, demonstrating robust zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks while simultaneously tracking highly dynamic and complex motions.

</details>


### [182] [CARVE: Certified Affordable Repair of Vetoed Maneuvers via Envelopes for Interactive Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02641)
*Yifan Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CARVE provides a prediction-free interactive repair certificate for multi-agent driving, enabling normatively admissible maneuvers with annotated repair, ownership, and fallback within a finite lattice; evaluation shows high repair coverage and preserved right-of-way on 589 replay episodes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rule-based vetoes and prediction-based planners lack runtime proof objects describing how to repair maneuvers, ownership, and fallback; a formal certificate is needed to guarantee bounded, attributable interactions under assumptions.

Method: Define CARVE as a certificate layer over a finite lattice of ego-owned and agent-owned tactical operators; introduce cooperation envelope B_j(s)=β(π_j)α_j^{max}(s); record binding rule, repair category, repair set, responsibility-weighted cost, and fallback; prove properties; evaluate on 589 INTERACTION episodes using Lanelet2 geometry-grounded replay; Greedy policy; measure acceptance, false veto recovery, right-of-way respect, etc.

Result: CARVE-Greedy accepts 98.64% of initially vetoed maneuvers; recovers 370/378 human-resolved false vetoes; preserves 589/589 right-of-way respect; zero priority-agent false positives; 400/400 negative-stress vetoes.

Conclusion: CARVE certifies whether a proposed interaction is bounded, attributable, and normatively admissible without predicting other driver's responses; provides a runtime, verifiable object for interaction repair under declared assumptions.

Abstract: Interactive driving exposes a failure mode that is easy to miss in rule-aware autonomous-driving stacks: a hard-rule margin can be negative for an ego candidate even though a small lawful accommodation by a non-priority agent would restore feasibility. Existing rulebooks, shields, and reachability filters are strong at vetoing unsafe actions, while prediction-based planners model likely responses. Neither returns a runtime proof object that states which bounded multi-agent edit repairs the maneuver, who owns the edit, whether the request is right-of-way affordable, and what ego fallback remains if the request is not observed. We formulate this missing object as *interactive repair certification* and introduce *CARVE*, a prediction-free certificate layer over a finite lattice of ego-owned and agent-owned tactical operators. Agent-owned requests are admissible only inside \(B_j(s) = β(π_j)α_j^{\max}(s)\), a cooperation envelope that separates kinematic reachability from normative priority. The resulting certificate records the binding rule, repair category, repair set, responsibility-weighted cost split, and fallback. On 589 Lanelet2-geometry-grounded INTERACTION replay episodes, CARVE-Greedy accepts 98.64% of initially vetoed maneuvers and recovers 370/378 human-resolved false vetoes, while preserving 589/589 right-of-way respect, zero priority-agent false positives, and 400/400 negative-stress vetoes. We prove certificate soundness, structural right-of-way respect, exact finite-lattice minimality, fallback contingency, and blame-consistency conditions. CARVE does not predict or require another driver's compliance; it certifies whether a proposed interaction is bounded, attributable, and normatively admissible under declared assumptions.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [183] [Visual Graph Scaffolds for Structural Reasoning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02673)
*Runlin Lei,Xiaokui Xiao,Zhewei Wei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Graphs can organize LLM reasoning; visual graph guidance beats flattened text formats for multi-hop QA, especially after fine-tuning and distillation; suggests graphs are valuable as internal, visual scaffolds rather than merely external knowledge sources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether graph-structured representations can serve as internal reasoning aids for LLMs, beyond simply supplying information at test time.

Method: Rewrite teacher-provided reasoning traces as graph mind maps and use them to guide a student model; compare modalities (flattened text vs visual graphs); evaluate on multi-hop QA; test under supervision, fine-tuning, and KL distillation.

Result: There is a modality gap: flattened text graphs lose benefits without direct answer hints, degrading reasoning efficiency and answer quality; visual graph guidance remains effective without answer cues and retains advantages after fine-tuning and KL distillation.

Conclusion: Graphs should be studied as visual scaffolds for organizing reasoning, not only as external knowledge structures for LLMs.

Abstract: Graphs have been used to enhance large language models (LLMs) for structured reasoning, mostly as external knowledge sources are provided to models at test time. In this paper, we take a different view: the value of graphs for LLMs lie not only in supplying information, but also in organizing reasoning. Inspired by how humans use graph-structured mind maps to organize branching and converging thoughts, we ask whether graphs can serve as an internal form of reasoning assistance. We study this question on multi-hop question answering tasks, where teacher-provided reasoning traces are rewritten as graph mind maps and used to guide a student model. Our experiments reveal a clear modality gap. When graph structures are flattened into text, their benefits become limited once direct answer hints are removed. Under this abstract guidance setting, both reasoning efficiency and answer quality degrade substantially. In contrast, visual graph guidance remains effective without direct answer clues, and its advantage persists after supervised fine-tuning and KL-based distillation. The above findings support the claim that graphs should be studied not only as external knowledge structures for LLMs, but also as visual scaffolds for organizing reasoning.

</details>


### [184] [AURA: Action-Gated Memory for Robot Policies at Constant VRAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02775)
*Josef Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AURA-Mem introduces a constant-size recurrent memory with a learned write gate that writes only when the action would change next step, reducing memory writes while maintaining accuracy and constant memory footprint, outperforming KV-cache on edge/robot tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV-cache is efficient for datacenter batching but is ill-suited for robots due to long-horizon, bandwidth-limited edge inference with scarce high-bandwidth memory and finite flash endurance; writes, not compute, constrain memory, so a non-volatile, low-write, constant-size memory with selective updates is desirable.

Method: Wraps a frozen vision-language-action backbone with a constant-size recurrent memory and a learned gate that writes only when the current observation would change the next action. Gate trained directly against action-error in closed-loop, not reconstruction. Inference state fixed at 4,224 bytes; KV-cache scales to 6,061x larger at 100,000 steps.

Result: On synthetic benchmark, matches best O(1) baseline in accuracy while reducing writes by 5.19–6.13x, up to 9.19x for easier configs. Schedule baselines do not recover gains, showing the benefit arises from action-surprise signal. On a trained closed-loop OpenVLA-OFT 7B panel (LIBERO-Long, 60 episodes per arm), AURA-Mem matches ungated policy (0.233) and slightly exceeds always-write KV (0.217) while achieving 7.0x fewer writes and constant memory. Also introduces an approximate-information-state value-loss bound; at this scale, bound is vacuous.

Conclusion: Selective write gating enables a constant, fixed-size memory to perform competitively with large KV-caches in long-horizon robotic inference, dramatically reducing write traffic without sacrificing, and sometimes improving, task success.

Abstract: The KV-cache is the right memory for datacenters but the wrong memory for robots. Datacenter inference batches many short requests and resets them, amortizing an attention cache across a crowd. Embodied agents instead run one long, non-resetting episode on bandwidth-limited edge hardware, where high-bandwidth memory and flash are scarce, flash has finite write endurance, and memory writes rather than compute can become the binding constraint.
  AURA-Mem (Action-Utility Recurrent Adaptive Memory) targets this regime. It wraps a frozen vision-language-action backbone with a constant-size recurrent memory and a learned gate that writes only when the current observation would change the next action: memory that knows when to stay silent. Unlike reconstruction-based memory, the gate is trained directly against a closed-loop action-error signal. Its inference state is fixed at 4,224 bytes regardless of horizon, while a KV-cache grows to 6,061 times larger at 100,000 steps.
  On a controlled synthetic benchmark, AURA-Mem matches the best O(1) baseline in accuracy while using 5.19-6.13 times fewer writes, and up to 9.19 times fewer writes on easier configurations. Budget-matched random and periodic schedules do not recover this gain, isolating the benefit to the action-surprise signal. On a trained closed-loop OpenVLA-OFT 7B panel on LIBERO-Long (n=60 episodes per arm), the gate does not hurt success: AURA-Mem matches the ungated base policy (0.233) and slightly exceeds an always-write KV arm (0.217), while using 7.0 times fewer writes and constant memory. We also instantiate an approximate-information-state value-loss bound as a methodology demonstration; at this scale, the bound is vacuous rather than a guarantee.

</details>


### [185] [Evaluating Transformer and LSTM Frameworks for Prediction in Ungauged Basins](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02791)
*Taye Akinrele,James Halgren,Noorbakhsh Amiri Golilarz,Sudip Mittal,Shahram Rahimi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LSTM outperforms an encoder-only Transformer for upstream streamflow inference in watershed networks; downstream hydrologic information boosts accuracy for all models, suggesting recurrent memory aligns better with upstream reconstruction and that downstream context provides a strong auxiliary constraint.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In ungauged basins with limited observations, it is uncertain which neural architecture best captures hydrologic sequence dynamics; this study tests encoder-only Transformers versus LSTM for upstream streamflow inference using retrospective NOAA NWM simulations.

Method: Compare encoder-only Transformer and LSTM across upstream-only and upstream+downstream configurations using retrospective NOAA NWM simulations; assess performance with median NNSE and evaluate inductive-bias implications rather than leaderboard standings.

Result: Across configurations, LSTM showed stronger overall performance than the encoder-only Transformer. Including downstream information boosted NNSE by >60% across all models. Downstream context serves as a strong auxiliary constraint, and recurrent memory aligns better with upstream reconstruction tasks.

Conclusion: Recurrent memory (LSTM) is better suited than an encoder-only Transformer for upstream hydrologic sequence inference. Downstream hydrologic context improves prediction across architectures, supporting interpretation of results as an inductive-bias test rather than a/b competition.

Abstract: Watershed networks exhibit convergent topologies in which multiple tributaries merge into downstream channels,integrating diverse upstream hydrological processes. In ungauged basins, the absence of direct observations increases uncertainty and limits the ability to anticipate extreme events. This study evaluates whether an encoder-only Transformer provides an advantage over an LSTM for upstream streamflow inference under limited hydrologic information, using retrospective simulations from the NOAA National Water Model (NWM). Across both upstream-only and combined configurations, the LSTM showed stronger overall performance than the Transformer model across the two configurations. Incorporating downstream information further boosted performance for all models, increasing median NNSE by more than 60%. Rather than treating this as a leaderboard-style comparison, we interpret the experiments as a test of architectural inductive bias for hydrologic sequence inference. The results indicate that recurrent memory remains better aligned with this upstream reconstruction task than an encoder-only Transformer, while downstream hydrologic context provides a strong auxiliary constraint that substantially improves prediction skill across architectures

</details>


### [186] [BehaviorBench: Modeling Real-World User Decisions from Behavioral Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02798)
*Liangwei Yang,Jielin Qiu,Zixiang Chen,Ming Zhu,Juntao Tan,Zhiwei Liu,Wenting Zhao,Zhujun Lan,Akshara Prabhakar,Silvio Savarese,Huan Wang,Shelby Heinecke*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces BehaviorBench, a benchmark for evaluating personalized decision modeling from real-world behavioral traces, using wallet-level decision histories to support two tasks: Belief prediction (final stance and confidence) and Trade prediction (direction and amount). Evaluates frontier and open-weight models under four history interfaces across 2,000 wallets; shows personalization benefits for Belief and varying failure modes across tasks and interfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of real-world data for evaluating personalized decision systems, as model-based simulations can diverge from human behavior; provide a benchmark grounded in authentic traces from prediction markets and on-chain records.

Method: Reconstructs wallet-level decision histories from public records and organizes them into two task layers. Evaluates models on 141,445 Belief instances and 1,485,972 Trade instances across disjoint support pools. Four history interfaces are tested: no personalization, direct recent history, generated user profiles, and retrieved support-wallet evidence. Uses retrieval-based evaluation with disjoint support pools across 2,000 wallets.

Result: Personalization improves Belief prediction more consistently than Trade prediction. Model rankings vary by task layer and metric. Different history interfaces reveal distinct failure modes and expose sensitivity to evidence type.

Conclusion: BehaviorBench offers an evaluation setting to study whether personalized methods can leverage real-world behavioral evidence rather than relying on simulated users alone; facilitates research into realistic personalization in decision-support systems.

Abstract: Many decision-support settings require systems that adapt to individual users, but evaluation data for this problem remain limited. Existing benchmarks for user understanding often rely on simulated users or model-generated behavior, even though recent work cautions that model-based simulations can diverge systematically from human behavior. We introduce \textsc{BehaviorBench}, a benchmark for evaluating personalized decision modeling from real-world behavioral traces. \textsc{BehaviorBench} reconstructs wallet-level decision histories from observed public prediction-market and on-chain records, and organizes them into two complementary task layers: \emph{Belief prediction}, which predicts a user's final revealed stance and confidence in a market, and \emph{Trade prediction}, which predicts the direction and amount of individual transactions. Across 2,000 evaluation wallets, the benchmark contains 141,445 Belief instances and 1,485,972 Trade instances, with disjoint support pools for retrieval-based evaluation. We evaluate frontier and open-weight generative models under four history interfaces: no personalization, direct recent history, generated user profiles, and retrieved support-wallet evidence. Personalization improves Belief prediction more consistently than Trade prediction, model rankings change across task layers and metrics, and different history interfaces expose different failure modes. \textsc{BehaviorBench} provides an evaluation setting for studying whether personalized methods can use real-world behavioral evidence rather than simulated users alone.

</details>


### [187] [ChatHealthAI: Aligning Electronic Health Record Representations with Large Language Models for Grounded Clinical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02802)
*Bo-Hong Wang,Baicheng Peng,Ruilin Wang,Jun Bai,Ziyang Song,Yue Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ChatHealthAI aligns structured EHR representations with a frozen LLM's semantic space via a task-aware resampler to enable clinically grounded natural-language reasoning alongside accurate patient prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between the strong language-based reasoning of LLMs and the structured, longitudinal nature of EHR data; enable interpretable, language-based clinical reasoning without sacrificing predictive performance.

Method: Propose ChatHealthAI, a multimodal framework: use a pretrained EHR foundation model to produce longitudinal patient representations; employ a task-aware resampler to align these representations with the LLM's embedding space; integrate refined clinical event descriptions with longitudinal features; keep the LLM frozen to preserve language capabilities; evaluate on three tasks from EHRSHOT.

Result: ChatHealthAI improves reasoning quality and interpretability while maintaining competitive predictive performance on three EHRSHOT tasks.

Conclusion: Integrating EHR foundation models with pretrained LLMs shows promise for interpretable, data-driven clinical prediction, enabling language-based reasoning grounded in structured medical histories.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit strong natural-language reasoning abilities for clinical decision support, but struggle to effectively model structured longitudinal electronic health records (EHRs). In contrast, EHR foundation models can learn predictive patient representations, yet lack interpretable language-based reasoning. To bridge this gap, we propose ChatHealthAI, a multimodal reasoning framework that aligns structured EHR representations from a pretrained EHR foundation model with the semantic space of a frozen LLM through a task-aware resampler. By integrating longitudinal patient representations with refined clinical event descriptions, ChatHealthAI enables clinically grounded natural-language reasoning while maintaining accurate patient prediction. We evaluated ChatHealthAI on three clinical predictive tasks from the EHRSHOT benchmark. Results show that ChatHealthAI improves reasoning quality and interpretability while preserving competitive predictive performance. These findings highlight the potential of integrating EHR foundation models with pretrained LLMs for interpretable clinical prediction.

</details>


### [188] [Traj-Evolve: A Self-Evolving Multi-Agent System for Patient Trajectory Modeling in Lung Cancer Early Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02812)
*Sihang Zeng,Matthew Thompson,Ruth Etzioni,Meliha Yetisgen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A self-evolving, retrieval-augmented multi-agent framework (Traj-Evolve) that combines a non-parametric Experience Pool with MARL to model long, multimodal EHR trajectories, achieving state-of-the-art results on lung cancer prediction and unveiling complementary dynamics between memory retrieval and collaboration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address sparse/noisy long-context EHRs and to move beyond isolated-case processing by leveraging learned experience from similar prior patients.

Method: ExPool: rejection-sampled traces for few-shot-like contexts; MARL: reward-ranked fine-tuning to optimize inter-agent and memory collaboration; leave-one-out cross-retrieval to align training/inference; evaluation on up to 5 years multimodal EHRs; compared to 9 baselines; analysis of dynamics.

Result: Outperforms 9 baselines on overall population and never-smoker subset; ExPool expands from diverse to specific samples; manager agent converges quickly; workers gain from more verified patients; ExPool improves specificity; MARL improves sensitivity; complementary effects on predicted risk.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective integration of non-parametric memory with parametric MARL in a retrieval-augmented, evolving framework for EHR trajectories; highlights importance of retrieval scope and agent coordination for clinical prediction; suggests avenues for further exploration in complex, long-horizon healthcare tasks.

Abstract: Modeling patient trajectories from longitudinal electronic health records (EHRs) requires reasoning over sparse, noisy, and long-context multimodal sequences. Existing LLM-based multi-agent systems address context length but process patients in isolation, failing to mirror how clinicians leverage accumulated experience from similar prior cases. We present Traj-Evolve, a self-evolving multi-agent system with two complementary evolving mechanisms. First, an Experience Pool (ExPool) acts as a non-parametric memory, indexing rejection-sampled reasoning traces to retrieve similar patients as few-shot contexts. Second, multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) via reward-ranked fine-tuning parametrically optimizes inter-agent and agent-memory collaboration. A leave-one-out cross-retrieval strategy unifies the two, aligning training- and inference-time behavior under retrieval augmentation. On a lung cancer prediction task utilizing up to five years of multimodal EHRs, Traj-Evolve outperforms 9 strong baselines on the overall population and a challenging never-smoker population. Analysis of the evolving dynamics highlights three key findings: (1) expanding the ExPool shifts optimal retrieval from diverse to specific samples; (2) under MARL, the manager agent's prediction loss converges quickly while the worker agents' temporal reasoning continues to benefit from more verified patients; and (3) the two mechanisms are complementary on the predicted risk, where ExPool improves specificity while MARL improves sensitivity.

</details>


### [189] [An Exploration of Collision-based Enemy Morphology Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02832)
*Johor Jara Gonzalez,Matthew Guzdial*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Three novel methods to generate enemy morphologies from player collision data, surpassing a robotics-inspired baseline; each method has distinct trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill the gap in PCG research on enemy body plans and collision geometry, leveraging insights from robotics morphology and using player collision information as guidance.

Method: Assess three morphology-generation approaches driven by player collision data; compare against an evolutionary baseline adapted from robotics morphology work; analyze strengths/weaknesses and performance.

Result: All three approaches show equal or superior performance relative to the baseline, with different methods offering complementary advantages and trade-offs.

Conclusion: Demonstrates viable morphology-generation strategies for PCG, highlighting trade-offs and potential for integration with gameplay data; invites further refinement and generalization.

Abstract: Despite a great deal of prior research into Procedural Content Generation (PCG), relatively little prior work has explored generating enemies for video games. In particular, there is almost no work on generating enemy morphologies, the basic body plan or collision information for in-game enemies, despite the existence of related morphology generation work in robotics. In this paper, we explore three different novel approaches to generate enemy morphologies based on player collision information. We found that each approach provides different strengths and weaknesses, but all had equivalent or better performance than an evolutionary baseline adapted from prior robotics morphology work.

</details>


### [190] [Thinking Past the Answer: Evaluating Harmful Overthinking in Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02835)
*Simone Caldarella,Davide Talon,Rahaf Aljundi,Elisa Ricci,Massimiliano Mancini*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Examines whether extra reasoning after a correct answer helps or hurts; proposes a prefix-level trajectory evaluation based on reasoning sufficiency to measure the minimal reasoning needed to reach correctness; finds that many tasks need little reasoning, stopping at the first correct prefix improves accuracy (up to ~21%), and early stopping reduces verbose overthinking but not all harmful overthinking; errors driven by logical drift and visual reinterpretation; findings extend to language-only tasks; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenge the assumption that longer reasoning always improves performance in Large Reasoning Models; distinguish verbose overthinking (harmless) from harmful overthinking (destabilizing correct solutions) and quantify the correct stopping point.

Method: Introduce a prefix-level trajectory evaluation protocol grounded in reasoning sufficiency. Define the minimal reasoning budget (prefix length) required for the model to first generate the correct answer. Apply this to multimodal benchmarks; assess the impact of stopping at the first correct prefix; analyze failure modes (logical drift, visual reinterpretation); test whether findings generalize to language-only reasoning benchmarks; provide code for replication.

Result: Many instances labeled as reasoning-intensive actually require little reasoning. Stopping at the first correct prefix yields up to 21% accuracy improvement over standard reasoning. Early stopping reduces verbose overthinking by up to ~50% but does not fully mitigate harmful overthinking. Correctness deviations are mainly due to logical drift and visual reinterpretation. Findings generalize to language-only reasoning benchmarks.

Conclusion: Harmful overthinking constitutes a reliability risk; optimal stopping points are task-dependent. Controlling the reasoning budget improves reliability and efficiency in both multimodal and language-only settings. The approach and code enable replication and further study.

Abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) improve performance by generating explicit intermediate reasoning traces through increased test-time compute, yet the assumption that longer reasoning is consistently beneficial remains under-examined. While recent evidence shows that additional reasoning can lead models to overthink, we ask: "Once a model has reached the correct answer, does further reasoning refine the solution, or deviate from it?" To study the dynamics after correctness, we introduce a prefix-level trajectory evaluation protocol grounded in reasoning sufficiency, defining the minimum reasoning budget required for a model to first generate the correct answer. This allows us to disentangle verbose overthinking, where additional reasoning is redundant but harmless, from harmful overthinking, where continued reasoning destabilizes an already-correct trajectory. Starting from multimodal benchmarks, we find that many instances considered reasoning-intensive require surprisingly little reasoning. Moreover, stopping at the first correct prefix improves accuracy over standard reasoning up to 21%, revealing that current models are limited not only by their ability to reason, but also by their inability to stop at the right time. Furthermore, while common efficiency strategies like early stopping substantially reduce verbose overthinking (up to 50%), they fail to mitigate harmful overthinking. Failure analysis reveals that correctness deviations are mainly driven by logical drift and visual reinterpretation. Finally, we show that our findings generalize to language-only reasoning benchmarks, highlighting harmful overthinking as a broader reliability risk. Code available at https://simonecaldarella.github.io/thinking-past-the-answer.

</details>


### [191] [Toward a Modular Architecture for Embedded AI Agent Systems at the Edge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02862)
*Marcus Rüb,Michael Gerhards*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a tiered Embedded Agent System architecture integrating On-Device Agents and Cloud-Augmented Agents with a Governance Layer to balance real-time control and LLM-based reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable agentic AI in resource-constrained embedded microcontrollers by surpassing the limitations of server-class resources and continuous connectivity.

Method: Proposes a modular reference architecture with decoupled On-Device Agents (compressed NN and rule-based logic) and Cloud-Augmented Agents (SLMs) plus a cross-cutting Governance Layer; analyzes architectural principles and trade-offs rather than relying on empirical benchmarks.

Result: Outlines architectural principles and trade-offs for latency, energy, and reliable execution in resource-constrained environments; introduces Governance Layer for observability, policy enforcement, and safety across device fleets.

Conclusion: Bridges deterministic real-time control and agentic intelligence in embedded systems by providing a scalable, governance-enabled tiered architecture.

Abstract: The rise of Large Language Models (LLMs) has enabled agentic AI capable of complex reasoning and tool use; however, deploying such autonomy in pervasive computing environments remains challenging due to the strict memory and energy constraints of embedded microcontrollers. Existing frameworks typically assume server-class resources or continuous connectivity, leaving a gap for deeply embedded systems. This paper proposes a modular reference architecture for Embedded Agent Systems that bridges the divide between deterministic real-time control and agentic intelligence.
  We introduce a tiered design that decouples On-Device Agents - executing highly compressed neural networks and rule-based logic for low-latency, privacy-critical tasks - from Cloud-Augmented Agents that leverage Small Language Models (SLMs) for higher-level reasoning and planning. A key contribution is the integration of a cross-cutting Governance Layer, ensuring observability, policy enforcement, and safety across distributed fleets of autonomous devices. Rather than presenting purely empirical benchmarks, we analyze architectural design principles and trade-offs regarding latency, energy, and reliable execution in resource-constrained environments.

</details>


### [192] [Don't Gamble, GAMBLe: An Analytical Framework for AI-Driven Research Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02863)
*Marquita Ellis,Paul Castro*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GAMBLe provides a lens to ADRS by decomposing the system into a generator, assessor, discovery mechanism, and budget, yielding an effective landscape that varies by component; there is no universal best choice among generators or mechanisms, and careful component selection significantly boosts performance and efficiency under budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ADRS performance hinges on interactions among components, which current convergence guarantees and analysis tools fail to capture; a formal framework is needed to study these dynamics.

Method: Introduce GAMBLe with four components (G, A, M, B) and the effective landscape Leff = A∘G; conduct extensive experiments (760+ runs, >46k iterations) across LLMs, ensemble strategies, greedy/co-evolution mechanisms, and varied assessors on three NP-hard problems; analyze per-problem landscapes.

Result: No total ordering between generators or mechanisms; frontier models can underperform open-source alternatives; simple mechanisms may outperform state-of-the-art meta-search; with budgets of 60 iterations, component choices can improve performance by 13–67% and search efficiency by 6–39×.

Conclusion: The framework clarifies how component choices sculpt the effective landscape; ADRS design should consider the interaction-driven landscape rather than seeking a universally best component; frontier models are not universally superior, and even simple component configurations can yield strong gains.

Abstract: AI-Driven Research Systems (ADRS) -- systems coupling LLMs with automated evaluation to discover algorithms, proofs, and designs -- are being optimized and adopted across domains, but the tools to analyze them have not kept pace. ADRS performance depends on component interactions that are poorly understood, expensive to explore, and (as we show) not well captured by standard convergence guarantees. These guarantees rely on structural assumptions that do not hold under the ADRS process we formalize. We introduce GAMBLe, a framework that decomposes ADRS behavior into four parameters (generator $G$, assessor $\mathcal{A}$, discovery mechanism $\mathcal{M}$, budget $B$) and one compositional object, the effective landscape $L_{\text{eff}} = \mathcal{A} \circ G$, which reveals that distinct generator-assessor pairs induce structurally different per-problem optimization landscapes. We exercise the framework on 760+ replicated runs (>46,000 iterations) spanning generators from single LLMs to dynamically-adaptive ensembles, mechanisms from greedy selection to co-evolutionary meta-search, and three NP-hard problems whose assessors range from continuous scoring to cliff functions. The experiments reveal no total ordering of generators or mechanisms: frontier models can underperform open-source alternatives and the simplest mechanism sometimes outperforms state-of-the-art meta-search. Results show that even under limited budgets (60 iterations per run), the right component choices can improve performance by 13-67% and search efficiency by 6-39x.

</details>


### [193] [When Helping Hurts and How to Fix It: Multi-Agent Debate for Data Cleaning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02866)
*Chirag Parmar,Akshat Mehta,Henglin Wu,Jagadish Ramamurthy,Shweta Medhekar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Multi-agent debate yields mixed effects: degrades generation due to critique-induced confusion but improves error detection; a robust benefit condition and a separate-critic configuration can surpass single-agent baselines in some tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand when debate helps data cleaning, given conflicting prior results; quantify conditions for benefit across domains.

Method: Across three benchmarks, four model families, 6k task-condition pairs; analyze debate's impact; factorial experiment; adversarial separation; code-execution grounding; evidence-gated generation; generalization tests to 19 published comparisons.

Result: Debate degrades generation across models (-1.6 to -15.5 pp) due to CIC; critic feedback is often hallucinated and uncritically accepted; however, debate improves error detection by +27.4 F1 (d=1.0); derive a benefit condition: debate helps when rescue probability times fixability exceeds destruction probability; adversarial separation is essential: self-verification with identical tools fails; separate Critic with code-execution grounding and evidence-gated generation yields first configuration to significantly exceed single-agent (+5.3pp, p<0.05); condition predicts all nine task types and generalizes with zero false positives across 19 comparisons in seven domains.

Conclusion: A simple, general criterion governs when debate helps: success requires a separation between generation and verification plus grounding; when satisfied, debate can beat solo baselines; the framework generalizes across tasks, offering practical guidelines for designing debate systems.

Abstract: When does multi-agent debate help data cleaning, and when does it hurt? Across three benchmarks, four model families, and over 6,000 task-condition pairs, we find debate's effect reverses sign: it degrades generation across all four models (-1.6 to -15.5pp) through critique-induced confusion (CIC), hallucinated Critic feedback that the Generator accepts uncritically, yet improves error detection (+27.4pp F1, d=1.0). We derive a debate benefit condition: debate helps when the probability of rescuing a wrong output (Critic verification odds weighted by fixability) exceeds the probability of destroying a correct one. A factorial experiment proves adversarial separation is essential: self-verification with identical tools fails, while a separate Critic with code-execution grounding and evidence-gated generation produces the first debate configuration to significantly exceed single-agent on a generative task (+5.3pp, p<0.05). The condition correctly predicts all nine task types and generalizes with zero false positives across 19 published comparisons in seven domains.

</details>


### [194] [Handoff Debt: The Rediscovery Cost When Coding Agents Take Over Interrupted Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02875)
*Dipesh KC,Anjila Budathoki*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduce the concept of handoff debt in coding-agent evaluation and propose a takeover protocol that tests successor agents under various handoff views; context-rich handoffs reduce resumption cost, suggesting evaluation should report both solvability and resumption effort.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real software work is interrupted and resumed by different agents; measuring the cost to resume work (handoff debt) is necessary for realistic assessment of coding agents.

Method: A takeover protocol that freezes the repository at deterministic handoff points and evaluates successor agents under four handoff views (repository state only, raw trace, summary notes, structured notes). The study uses 75 source tasks, generating 181 handoff-point tasks and 724 takeover runs per successor model across three successor models.

Result: Context-bearing handoffs reduce median agent events by 20–59% and cumulative prompt tokens by 42–63% relative to repository-only takeover. Solved-rate effects are smaller and model-dependent, but efficiency gains are consistent.

Conclusion: Coding-agent evaluation should report both whether a task is solved and how costly it is for another agent to resume; handoff-aware evaluation better reflects real-world use.

Abstract: Coding-agent benchmarks evaluate whether a single uninterrupted agent can resolve a repository issue. Real software work is messier: tasks are interrupted, reassigned, reviewed, and resumed from partial states left by another agent or engineer. We study this missing dimension through \emph{handoff debt}: the rediscovery cost imposed when a predecessor's work is opaque or incomplete. Our takeover protocol interrupts a coding agent at deterministic handoff points, freezes the repository, and evaluates successor agents under four handoff views: repository state only, raw trace, summary notes, and structured notes. Across 75 source tasks, the protocol generates 181 handoff-point tasks and 724 takeover runs per successor model. Across three successor models, context-bearing handoffs reduce median agent events by 20--59\% and cumulative prompt tokens by 42--63\% relative to repository-only takeover. Solved-rate effects are smaller and model-dependent, but efficiency gains are consistent. These findings suggest that coding-agent evaluation should report not only whether a task is solved, but also how costly that work is for another agent to resume.

</details>


### [195] [Large AI Models in Dental Healthcare: From General-Purpose Systems to Domain-Specific Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02914)
*Sema Helali,Lina Abu Nadab,Sausan Alqawas,Alaa Abd-Alrazaq,Faleh Tamimi,Rafat Damseh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A scoping review (PRISMA-ScR) of 97 studies (2020–2026) analyzes language-generative, vision foundation, and dental-specific models in dentistry, proposes a two-dimensional framework, and shows complementary strengths with integrated pipelines; highlights data asymmetry and deployment barriers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the comparative clinical potential of large-scale AI models in dentistry given the global burden of oral disease and the absence of a unified review.

Method: Systematic search across PubMed, Google Scholar, Scopus, and arXiv; independent screening by two reviewers; inclusion/exclusion criteria; 97 studies included; 2020–2026; proposes two-dimensional classification by architectural paradigm and dental specialization degree.

Result: LMs excel at text-based tasks (clinical reasoning, exams, patient communication) but are inconsistent for image-driven diagnostics; adapted SAM and CLIP variants boost tooth segmentation and lesion detection; dental-specific models (DentVFM, DentVLM, OralGPT) perform best on complex multimodal tasks; integrated pipelines outperform single-model approaches; dental pretraining is heavily skewed toward the vision domain due to limited dental text data.

Conclusion: General-purpose and dental-specific models are complementary; the strongest systems combine both in structured pipelines; three barriers to safe deployment: hallucination in generative models, scarce annotated dental data, and lack of standardized clinical evaluation benchmarks.

Abstract: Background: Oral diseases affect nearly 3.5 billion people worldwide, yet the comparative clinical potential of large-scale AI models in dentistry remains poorly understood. Three distinct model categories have emerged: language-generative models, discriminative vision foundation models, and dental-specific foundation models, with no unified review examining their relationships and collective limitations.
  Methods: Following PRISMA-ScR guidelines, we systematically searched four databases (PubMed, Google Scholar, Scopus, arXiv), screened independently by two reviewers. After applying inclusion/exclusion criteria, 97 studies (2020-2026) were included. We propose a two-dimensional classification framework organizing models by architectural paradigm and dental specialization degree.
  Results: Language-generative models excel at text-based tasks (clinical reasoning, licensing exams, patient communication) but show inconsistent performance on image-dependent diagnostics. Adapted SAM and CLIP variants achieve strong tooth segmentation and lesion detection results. Dental-specific models (DentVFM, DentVLM, OralGPT) demonstrate strongest performance on complex multimodal tasks. Integrated pipelines consistently outperform single-model approaches. A data asymmetry is observed: dental-specific pretraining concentrates almost entirely in the vision domain, reflecting scarce large-scale dental text corpora.
  Conclusions: General-purpose and dental-specific models play complementary roles; the most effective systems combine both within structured pipelines. Safe autonomous deployment requires resolving three persistent barriers: hallucination in generative models, limited annotated dental datasets, and absent standardized clinical evaluation benchmarks.

</details>


### [196] [What Benchmarks Don't Measure: The Case for Evaluating Abstention Competence in Autonomous Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02965)
*Victor Ojewale,Suresh Venkatasubramanian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces compliance bias in AI agents that tend to proceed even when inputs, verification, or authorization are missing; offers a three-gap abstention taxonomy and abstention evaluation protocols; provides preliminary empirical results showing adjustable safety–usability tradeoffs across models and a broad enterprise scenario evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmarks that reward continuing action obscure the need to abstain when conditions for safe action are not met, enabling unsafe behavior through reward hacking and misaligned incentives.

Method: Theoretically diagnose reward hacking in human-feedback pipelines; develop a three-gap taxonomy (specification, verification, authority gaps) for abstention-warranted scenarios; design abstention evaluation protocols (Safety Rate, Usability Rate, Informed Refusal Rate); empirically evaluate a runtime abstention mechanism across 144 enterprise scenarios and five model families.

Result: Abstention mechanisms blocked up to 89.2% of hazardous actions and achieved 87.5% usability on authorized tasks; results suggest the safety–usability tradeoff is tunable and model-dependent.

Conclusion: The proposed taxonomy and composite metrics provide a starting point for abstention-aware benchmarks; further work is needed to broaden validation and refine protocols, but the findings show promise for safer, more usable AI agents when abstention is incorporated.

Abstract: Benchmarks for autonomous agents measure whether agents complete tasks, yet this framing is systematically blind to whether an agent should have proceeded at all. Agents trained under human-feedback objectives develop a structural tendency to proceed even when they lack the inputs, evidence, or authorization to act safely, a disposition we term compliance bias, because both the reward signal and the benchmark scoring regime treat proceeding as the correct default regardless of whether the preconditions for safe action are present. We make three contributions. We first show that compliance bias originates in reward hacking within human-feedback pipelines and is entrenched by prominent agent benchmarks, which either penalize agents for pausing or are architecturally unable to distinguish a principled pause from a silent failure. We then introduce a three-gap taxonomy of abstention-warranted scenarios, covering specification gaps where required information is absent, verification gaps where world state cannot be confirmed, and authority gaps where explicit authorization has not been given, which together provide a principled basis for constructing abstention-aware agent benchmarks. Finally, we propose abstention evaluation protocols (Safety Rate, Usability Rate, and Informed Refusal Rate) and report preliminary results across 144 enterprise agent scenarios and five model families, in which a runtime-enforced abstention mechanism achieves up to 89.2% hazardous-action blocking and 87.5% usability on authorized scenarios, demonstrating that the safety--usability tradeoff is tunable rather than inherent and that its shape varies substantially across model families. We treat this as preliminary work and offer the taxonomy and composite metrics as a starting point for further conversations.

</details>


### [197] [WISE-HAR: A Generalizable Ensemble Deep Learning Framework for WiFi-Based Human Activity Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02974)
*Maheen Arshad,Qindeel E Zahra,Muhammad Khuram Shahzad*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An ensemble CNN-based WiFi HAR approach for three activities achieves high accuracy (94.87% LOS with Biquad) on Wallhack1.8k, with aggressive data augmentation and strong cross-scenario/ cross-antenna generalization; data augmentation notably boosted RF performance from 60% to 95%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address privacy and reliability limitations of vision-based HAR by leveraging non-intrusive WiFi sensing, enabling robust recognition under varying hardware and lighting, especially with limited data.

Method: Ensemble of five CNNs (Deep CNN, Wide CNN, MobileNetV2, ResNet50V2, EfficientNetB0) trained on Wallhack1.8k. Data augmentation includes time-warping, frequency masking, and noise. Evaluations include cross-scenario (LOS train / NLOS test) and cross-antenna (Biquad train / PIFA test).

Result: Test accuracy 94.87% on LOS with Biquad; ensemble outperforms best individual by 0.66%. Data augmentation raises RF from 60% to 95%. Cross-scenario accuracy drops small: 1.37% and 2.07%, indicating strong generalization.

Conclusion: Proposed ensemble with augmentation yields robust HAR performance across environments and hardware, suitable for real-world deployment; the approach mitigates small dataset limitations and demonstrates good transferability.

Abstract: Human Activity Recognition (HAR) using WiFi signals has emerged as a transformative technology for smart homes, healthcare monitoring, security systems, and ambient assisted living. Unlike traditional camera-based systems that raise significant privacy concerns and fail in low-light conditions, or wearable sensors that require user compliance, WiFi-based HAR is non-intrusive, privacy-preserving, cost-effective, and works seamlessly in any lighting condition. This paper presents a comprehensive approach to recognize three distinct human activities: "No Presence" (empty room), "Walking", and "Walking + Arm-waving" using the Wallhack1.8k WiFi spectrogram dataset. We propose three key improvements to address the main challenges in WiFi-based HAR. First, to address high performance variance, we implement ensemble learning with five different CNN architectures (Deep CNN, Wide CNN, MobileNetV2, ResNet50V2, and EfficientNetB0). Second, to address the small dataset size limitation, we apply aggressive data augmentation techniques including time-warping, frequency masking, and noise addition. Third, to evaluate real-world generalization capability, we perform cross-scenario evaluation (training on Line-of-Sight and testing on Non-Line-of-Sight) and cross-antenna evaluation (training on Biquad antenna and testing on PIFA antenna). Our ensemble model achieved a test accuracy of 94.87% on the LOS scenario with Biquad antenna, outperforming the best individual model by 0.66%. Data augmentation improved Random Forest performance from 60% to 95%. Cross-scenario evaluation showed minimal accuracy drops of only 1.37% and 2.07%, demonstrating strong generalization capabilities. The results indicate that the proposed approach is robust, reliable, and suitable for real-world deployment in diverse environments with different hardware configurations.

</details>


### [198] [Inducing Reasoning Primitives from Agent Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02994)
*Zhihan Lei,Jiarui Yan,Joshua Momo,William W. Cohen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Reasoning Primitive Induction (RPI) mines successful ReAct traces to extract frequent reasoning moves, builds a library of typed pseudo-tools, and uses a standard ReAct loop to compose them at test time. This single-pass approach yields robust improvements across multiple tasks, outperforming the original agent and zero-shot chain-of-thought while reducing inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ReAct-style LLM agents repeatedly rediscover similar reasoning routines but rely on transient scratchpads. RPI aims to convert recurring reasoning moves into reusable, interpretable primitives to improve generalization and efficiency.

Method: Mine successful ReAct traces, cluster recurrent reasoning moves, and convert top moves into a library of typed pseudo-tools each with a natural-language docstring interpreted by an LLM at invocation. At test time, a standard ReAct loop selects and composes these primitives.

Result: The induced libraries outperform the very agent that generated their traces: +44pp on RuleArena NBA (30→74), +30pp on MuSR team allocation (38→68), +22pp on NatPlan meeting planning (7→29). Across five subtasks spanning narrative deduction, rule application, and constraint-satisfaction planning, a single fixed configuration beats zero-shot Chain-of-Thought on every subtask, matches or surpasses expert decompositions, and outperforms AWM at lower average inference cost.

Conclusion: Inducing a compact library of reusable, typed pseudo-tools from successful traces yields robust, scalable improvements across diverse tasks and reduces inference cost, suggesting strong potential for generalizing reasoning strategies beyond the original agent.

Abstract: ReAct-style LLM agents often rediscover the same reasoning routines across problems, yet leave those routines trapped in transient scratchpads. We introduce Reasoning Primitive Induction, a single-pass method that mines successful ReAct traces, clusters recurrent reasoning moves, and converts the most frequent moves into a compact library of typed pseudo-tools. Each pseudo-tool is specified by a natural-language docstring interpreted by an LLM at invocation time, and a standard ReAct loop composes these primitives at test time. The central result is that induced libraries outperform the very agent that generated their traces: by +44pp on RuleArena NBA (30 -> 74), +30pp on MuSR team allocation (38 -> 68), and +22pp on NatPlan meeting planning (7 -> 29). Across five comparable subtasks spanning narrative deduction, rule application, and constraint-satisfaction planning, a single fixed configuration improves over zero-shot Chain-of-Thought on every subtask, matches or surpasses expert-authored decompositions, and outperforms AWM at lower average inference cost.

</details>


### [199] [AUDITFLOW: Executable Symbolic Environments for Structured Financial Reporting Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03031)
*Yan Wang,Xuguang Ai,Jaisal Patel,Xueqing Peng,Fengran Mo,Yupeng Cao,Haohang Li,Mingyu Cao,Lingfei Qian,Víctor Gutiérrez-Basulto*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AuditFlow introduces a graph-grounded, multi-agent framework that separates adaptive search from deterministic verification using a symbolic environment built from GAAP taxonomy and XBRL graphs to perform structured financial audits; it shows strong accuracy gains and dependence on deterministic checks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Correctness in financial audits requires linking reported facts to taxonomy concepts and performing reliable calculations, which pure text-based LLM reasoning struggles to do; a symbolic, graph-structured environment can enable verifiable verification.

Method: AuditFlow constructs a static GAAP taxonomy graph and a dynamic XBRL filing graph to form a symbolic environment accessed via typed tools (fact retrieval, taxonomy traversal, numerical checking, rule evaluation). Two junior auditors view cases from regulatory and evidentiary perspectives; a senior auditor arbitrates disagreements and can trigger further investigation. Outputs are fused evidentially to produce a verdict, expected value, evidence trail, and trustworthiness score.

Result: On a FinAuditing-derived FinMR dataset, AuditFlow achieves 82.09% joint audit accuracy with GPT-5.5, outperforming the strongest baseline by 14.93 points. An ablation removing deterministic checks drops accuracy to 17.91%, indicating the symbolic environment’s critical role in verification.

Conclusion: Separating adaptive search from deterministic verification in a graph-grounded framework yields robust audit verdicts and highlights the essential contribution of symbolic, rule-based checks to complement LLMs in structured financial auditing.

Abstract: Structured financial audit verification is difficult for language-model agents because correctness depends on structured evidence rather than text alone. A model must link reported facts to taxonomy concepts, traverse calculation or dimensional relations, and recompute expected values before applying an audit rule. We propose AuditFlow, a graph-grounded multi-agent framework that separates adaptive search from deterministic verification. AuditFlow builds a symbolic environment from a static US-GAAP taxonomy graph and a dynamic XBRL filing graph, and exposes it through typed tools for fact retrieval, taxonomy traversal, numerical checking, and rule evaluation. Two junior auditors inspect each case from regulatory and evidentiary views, while a senior auditor resolves disagreements and can request further investigation. The final reports are fused through evidential aggregation to produce an audit verdict, expected value, evidence trail, and trustworthiness score. On a FinAuditing-derived FinMR sample, AuditFlow reaches 82.09% joint audit accuracy under GPT-5.5, outperforming the strongest baseline by 14.93 points. Removing deterministic checks drops accuracy to 17.91%, showing that the symbolic environment performs the verification step that the model cannot reliably replace.

</details>


### [200] [TriEval: A Resource-Efficient Pipeline for LLM Bias, Toxicity, and Truthfulness Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03036)
*Akshatha Srikantha,Manpreet Singh,Yash Jajoo,Shyamal Lakhanpal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TriEval evaluates LLM outputs across bias, toxicity, and truthfulness with low compute, enabling multi-parameter benchmarking on standard laptops; finds notable toxicity/truthfulness gaps between open- and closed-source models; released as open source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for scalable, multi-parameter evaluation of LLM safety and fairness after deployment. Existing tools often test one parameter at a time or require heavy computation, limiting accessibility for researchers with limited resources.

Method: A low-resource evaluation pipeline that jointly assesses bias, toxicity, and truthfulness across LLM outputs, compatible with both open- and closed-source models and runnable on a standard laptop without a GPU cluster; validated on four models (Llama 3 8B, Mistral 7B, Gemma 2 9B, Claude Haiku); open-source release.

Result: Demonstrates clear differences between open-source and closed-source models in toxicity and truthfulness; confirms feasibility of multi-parameter evaluation under resource constraints; open-sourcing expands access for researchers with limited hardware.

Conclusion: TriEval makes robust, multidimensional LLM evaluation accessible to a broader research community and supports safer, fairer deployment by highlighting cross-model differences while reducing computational barriers.

Abstract: LLMs have evolved from basic chatbots to the backbone of the AI ecosystem, now widely used in healthcare, schools, and government services. The domain-wide adoption of LLMs necessitates continuous evaluation to ensure their safety and fairness. Common issues encountered after deploying LLMs include inconsistent outputs and hallucinations of incorrect information. Although numerous LLM evaluation tools exist, most are limited to testing a single parameter at a time or require massive computational resources that are not accessible to most researchers. TriEval addresses these challenges by evaluating LLM outputs across multiple parameters, including bias, toxicity, and truthfulness together, while minimizing computing resources. The pipeline is compatible with both open- and closed-source models and runs on a standard laptop without a GPU cluster. TriEval has been tested on four models: Llama 3 8B, Mistral 7B, Gemma 2 9B, and Claude Haiku. The results show clear differences between open-source and closed-source models, especially in terms of toxicity and truthfulness. TriEval is being released as open source to enable broader access for researchers with limited computational resources.

</details>


### [201] [RelGT-AC: A Relational Graph Transformer for Autocomplete Tasks in Relational Databases](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03040)
*Phillip Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RelGT-AC extends RelGT with (i) column masking to avoid trivial solutions, (ii) a unified autocomplete task head for classification/regression, and (iii) a TF-IDF text encoder for free-text columns; evaluated on RelBench v2 across 7 tasks and 3 datasets, achieving consistent gains over GraphSAGE and up to +10 AUROC on text-heavy tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automate intelligent form-filling in relational databases by leveraging heterogeneous graphs and GNNs; address leakage and heterogeneity in relational data; enable a single model to handle multiple autocomplete task types; exploit lexical signals from free-text columns to improve accuracy.

Method: Extend RelGT with a column masking strategy during subgraph encoding to prevent trivial target leakage; add a unified task head supporting binary, multiclass, and regression autocomplete within one model; incorporate a TF-IDF text encoder to detect and encode free-text columns, preserving lexical information; train/evaluate on 7 tasks across RelBench v2 datasets (rel-trial, rel-f1, rel-stack) and compare to GraphSAGE.

Result: RelGT-AC outperforms the GraphSAGE baseline on all three regression autocomplete tasks; achieves up to +10 AUROC on text-heavy eligibility tasks when using the TF-IDF encoder.

Conclusion: The proposed RelGT-AC architecture yields robust improvements for relational-graph-based autocomplete, with three targeted enhancements that address leakage, task heterogeneity, and lexical signal extraction; results indicate strong potential for practical intelligent form-filling in relational data systems.

Abstract: Relational databases underpin modern enterprise, scientific, and healthcare systems, yet predictive machine learning on such data remains challenging due to their multi-table, heterogeneous, and temporal structure. Relational Deep Learning (RDL) addresses this by representing databases as heterogeneous graphs and applying graph neural networks (GNNs) directly. RelBench v2 recently introduced autocomplete tasks -- a practically motivated task type where the goal is to predict an existing column value from relational context, analogous to an intelligent form-filling assistant. We propose RelGT-AC (Relational Graph Transformer for Autocomplete), extending the RelGT architecture with three targeted contributions: (1) a column masking strategy that prevents trivial solutions by masking the target column during subgraph encoding; (2) a unified task head supporting binary classification, multiclass classification, and regression autocomplete tasks within a single model; and (3) a TF-IDF text encoder that automatically detects and encodes free-text columns, recovering strong lexical signal that categorical encoders discard. Across 7 tasks spanning 3 RelBench v2 datasets (rel-trial, rel-f1, rel-stack), RelGT-AC outperforms the GraphSAGE baseline on all 3 regression autocomplete tasks and achieves up to +10 AUROC points on text-heavy eligibility tasks via the TF-IDF encoder.

</details>


### [202] [ToolGate: Token-Efficient Pre-Call Control for Tool-Augmented Vision-Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03054)
*Anjie Liu,Yan Song,Zhixun Chen,Ziqin Gong,Zhongwei Yu,Jun Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ToolGate introduces a lightweight pre-call controller for tool-augmented VLMs that predicts whether to execute or skip perceptual tool calls. It reduces token cost significantly while preserving accuracy across five benchmarks and two backbones, with additional gains from matched-domain trajectory training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce costs and improve efficiency of tool-augmented VLM agents by preventing unnecessary tool calls, addressing the poor local selectivity where both helpful and harmful calls occur at similar rates.

Method: Propose ToolGate, an external controller that uses trajectory text and simple structural features to decide execute/skip for proposed tool calls. Evaluated across five benchmarks and two Qwen3-VL backbones; includes matched-domain trajectory training on Qwen3-VL-30B.

Result: ToolGate reduces token cost to 64-69% of the unrestricted ReAct baseline while maintaining average accuracy in cross-domain settings. Matched-domain trajectory training yields a further accuracy gain of 1.65 points.

Conclusion: Explicit pre-call control over tool usage enhances efficiency of tool-augmented VLMs and complements tool quality; selective execution can deliver substantial cost savings with little to no loss in accuracy.

Abstract: Tool-augmented vision-language agents can acquire external perceptual evidence through OCR, detection, segmentation, and other tools, but executing every proposed tool call is costly and sometimes unnecessary. We study the pre-call control problem: after a ReAct-style VLM agent proposes a perceptual tool call, should the call be executed, or skipped before its output enters the context? Across five benchmarks, we find that the baseline agent exhibits poor local selectivity: helpful and harmful calls occur at similar rates (11.8% vs. 9.9%), while most calls do not change the immediate forced-answer prediction. We introduce ToolGate, a lightweight external controller that predicts execute/skip decisions from trajectory text and simple structural features. Across two Qwen3-VL backbones, ToolGate reduces token cost to 64-69% of the unrestricted ReAct baseline while preserving average accuracy in cross-domain settings. With matched-domain trajectory training on Qwen3-VL-30B, it further improves average accuracy by 1.65 points. These results show that tool-augmented VLM agents benefit not only from better perceptual tools, but also from explicit control over when tool outputs are worth paying for.

</details>


### [203] [SkillDAG: Self-Evolving Typed Skill Graphs for LLM Skill Selection at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03056)
*Tong Bai,Zhenglin Wan,Pengfei Zhou,Xingrui Yu,Wangbo Zhao,Yang You,Ivor W. Tsang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillDAG models inter-skill relationships as a typed directed graph and provides an inference-time, agent-callable retrieval interface for LLM agents. It supports accumulateable structure via propose-then-commit edits, improving skill selection beyond similarity-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM agents accrue large skill libraries, simple similarity-based retrieval fails to capture inter-skill structure (dependence, conflict, specialization, duplication). A structural representation enables more accurate, faithful retrieval.

Method: Introduce SkillDAG, a typed directed graph of skills with edges encoding relationships. Provide an inference-time interface for vector matches, typed-edge neighbors, and conflict signals. Use a propose-then-commit protocol to register execution-backed edges, allowing the graph to evolve across episodes.

Result: On ALFWorld and SkillsBench with MiniMax-M2.7, SkillDAG achieves 67.1% success and 27.3% reward, beating the Graph-of-Skills baseline by +12.8 and +8.6 points; gains transfer to gpt-5.2-codex, with intrinsic SkillsBench Ret@K increasing from 65.5 to 78.2 under matched queries.

Conclusion: Structural retrieval leveraging an evolving SkillDAG yields consistent performance gains, via robust candidate ranking as the pool grows and set-monotone online edits that maintain recall while expanding coverage.

Abstract: As LLM agents adopt large skill libraries, selecting the right subset becomes a structural problem rather than a similarity-matching one: skills depend on, conflict with, specialize, or duplicate one another, a structure invisible to both full enumeration and embedding similarity. We present SkillDAG, which models inter-skill relationships as a typed directed graph and exposes it to an LLM agent as an inference-time, agent-callable structural retrieval interface, queried and evolved during execution rather than baked into a fixed retrieval pipeline: each search returns vector matches, typed-edge neighbors, and conflict signals, and a propose-then-commit protocol lets the agent register execution-backed edges so the graph accumulates structure across episodes. On ALFWorld and SkillsBench with MiniMax-M2.7, SkillDAG reaches 67.1% success and 27.3% reward, exceeding the strongest reported Graph-of-Skills baseline by +12.8 and +8.6 points; the advantage ports to gpt-5.2-codex, and intrinsic SkillsBench Ret@K rises from 65.5 to 78.2 under matched queries. These gains trace to isolable mechanisms: candidate ranking that stays robust as the pool grows 10x where a fixed seeding-diffusion pipeline degrades, and set-monotone online edits that enlarge ground-truth recall without evicting prior hits.

</details>


### [204] [CORE: Conflict-Oriented Reasoning for General Multimodal Manipulation Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03066)
*Jinjie Shen,Yaxiong Wang,Yujiao Wu,Lechao Cheng,Tianrui Hui,Nan Pu,Zhihui Li,Zhun Zhong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CORE is a conflict-oriented reasoning framework that enhances multimodal fake-news detection by teaching LLMs to identify semantic/physical conflicts across modalities or with world knowledge. It introduces the Conflict Attribution Corpus (CAC) for fine-grained conflict annotations, uses conflict-focused representation and reasoning to achieve robust generalization to unseen manipulation types, including few-shot and zero-shot scenarios. Code and dataset released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current detectors rely on manipulation-specific models and large labeled datasets, leading to poor generalization to novel manipulation types. The authors hypothesize that detection can be improved by explicitly capturing intrinsic conflicts between modalities and knowledge.

Method: Construct Conflict Attribution Corpus (CAC) with fine-grained annotations of conflict factors and sources; train multimodal LLMs with conflict-oriented representation enhancement and reasoning on CAC; apply to detection of manipulated misinformation; evaluate generalization to unseen manipulation types with few-shot and zero-shot settings; release dataset and code.

Result: CORE outperforms state-of-the-art detectors; demonstrates robust generalization to unseen manipulation types with few- or zero-shot samples; effective conflict perception training; publicly available dataset and code.

Conclusion: Conflict-centric reasoning is an effective paradigm for multimodal misinformation detection, enabling rapid adaptation to new manipulation tactics and improving generalization compared to manipulation-specific detectors.

Abstract: The rapid rise of generative AI has made multimodal fake news increasingly realistic and pervasive, posing severe threats to public trust and social stability. Existing detection methods rely heavily on manipulation-specific models and large-scale labeled data, resulting in poor generalization to emerging manipulation types. We observed that the essence of manipulated misinformation lies in its intrinsic conflicts, \textbf{i.e.,} semantic or physical inconsistencies either across modalities or with common world knowledge. Inspired by this observation, we propose \textbf{C}onflict-\textbf{O}riented \textbf{RE}asoning (\textbf{CORE}) framework, an effective paradigm that learns to endows multimodal large language models (MLLMs) with explicit conflict-capturing capability. To this end, CORE first constructs the Conflict Attribution Corpus (CAC) with fine-grained annotations of conflict factors and sources, providing essential data support for subsequent conflict perception training. By performing conflict-oriented representation enhancement and reasoning based on CAC, CORE achieves robust and generalizable conflict detection, effectively and rapidly adapting to unseen manipulation types with a few samples or in even zero-shot settings. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CORE surpasses state-of-the-art models. The dataset and code are publicly available at https://github.com/shen8424/CORE.

</details>


### [205] [DELTAMEM: Incremental Experience Memory for LLM Agents via Residual Trees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03083)
*Haoran Tan,Zeyu Zhang,Zhicheng Cao,Rui Li,Xu Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DeltaMem introduces a residual-memory framework with two hierarchical trees (task skills and scene knowledge) using base-root and delta-nodes, enabling incremental knowledge growth. Retrieval uses a failure-penalized similarity scan with root-to-match reconstruction; an autonomous consolidation module expands frequent paths into new roots. Empirically, it outperforms baselines across diverse environments; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flat, independent memory representations in LLM-based agents cause redundancy and retrieval conflicts as experiences accumulate. An incremental, delta-based memory organization promises shared foundations and scalable specialization.

Method: Two independent residual trees: one for goal-conditioned task experiences (reusable skills) and one for scene/environment knowledge. Each tree has base root nodes and incremental delta nodes. Retrieval performs a failure-penalized similarity scan to find the best root-to-delta match, reconstructing the full experience via root-to-match chain. An autonomous consolidation mechanism distills high-frequency traversed paths into new root nodes, enabling self-organization from general heuristics to specialized variants.

Result: Empirical evaluation across diverse interactive environments shows DeltaMem consistently outperforms existing baselines.

Conclusion: DeltaMem provides a scalable, non-redundant memory organization for LLM-based agents, leveraging dual residual trees and automatic consolidation to improve continual learning. The accompanying code is released for future research.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents increasingly rely on memory to learn from experiences over continual interactions. However, storing experiences as independent, flat units leads to substantial redundancy and retrieval conflicts, as similar episodes repeat overlapping content and subtle scene variations cause retrieved memories to offer contradictory guidance. To address this, we introduce residual experience, positing that newly acquired experience is often an incremental variation of existing knowledge. We propose DeltaMem, a framework that organizes experience memory into two independent residual trees, one storing goal-conditioned task experience as reusable skills and another for scene-level environment knowledge. Each tree uses a root node for generalized base experiences and incremental delta nodes for subsequent variations, allowing related experiences to share a common foundation without duplication. For retrieval, a failure-penalized similarity scan locates the best match, reconstructing the full experience via root-to-match chain composition. An autonomous consolidation mechanism distills high-frequency paths into new root nodes, enabling the trees to self-organize from general heuristics to specialized variants. Experiments across diverse interactive environments show that DeltaMem consistently outperforms existing baselines. To facilitate future research, we release the code at https://github.com/import-myself/DeltaMem.

</details>


### [206] [The Shadow Price of Reasoning: Economic Perspective on Optimal Budget Allocation for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03092)
*Xu Wan,Speed Zhu,Jianwei Cai,Guang Chen,XiMing Huang,Wiggin Zhou,Mingyang Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Constrained Latent-utility Equilibrium Allocation for Reasoning (CLEAR) introduces a global constrained optimization framework to allocate inference-time resources for LLM reasoning tasks. It uses a shifted-surge utility model and a global shadow price to reallocate tokens from insolvent queries to solvable ones, improving the Pareto frontier of cost vs. accuracy, up to 3x accuracy gains in scarcity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inference budgets and latency constraints limit real-world LLM deployment. There is a need for principled, dynamic resource allocation that maximizes overall usefulness under a fixed budget.

Method: Formulate as a global constrained optimization with a shifted-surge per-query utility. Derive an optimal policy via a global shadow price that equilibrates marginal utility under scarcity. Propose CLEAR, which performs rational abandonment and reallocates resources from insolvent queries to near-threshold solvable ones, across varying traffic streams.

Result: Empirical results show improved Pareto frontier for total token cost vs mean accuracy. In scarce-resource regimes, CLEAR achieves up to 3x improvement in global accuracy compared to uniform allocation across several reasoning tasks and traffic patterns.

Conclusion: CLEAR provides a principled, scalable mechanism to optimize inference-time resource use under budgets. Its economic framing and empirical gains suggest practical value for deployment on constrained systems, with potential extensions to other resource types and utility models.

Abstract: Inference-time scaling has emerged as a critical avenue for enhancing Large Language Models' performance, yet real-world deployment is constrained by strict computational budgets. In this work, we formulate inference budget allocation as a global constrained optimization problem governed by economic principles.
