TL;DR: SAGE is proposed to compare social vs. self-evolution of agents. Surprisingly, peer history does not universally boost the strongest agents, but helps agents that plateau under self-improvement; gains depend on abstraction of shared traces, arena, and ability to extract transferable knowledge; in some tasks, filtered or summarized peer traces outperform raw logs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address how sharing experience among multiple agents, with public traces of history, impacts learning and performance beyond isolated self-improvement, and to determine when social learning yields transferable gains.

Method: Introduce SAGE framework with two compute-matched conditions: SocialEvo (agents co-evolve with access to all peers’ histories) and SelfEvo (agents see only their own history). Implemented in three arenas—open-ended ML research, long-horizon economic planning, and strategic multiplayer play—across multiple evolutionary rounds and model families; includes counterfactual controls to assess opponent-specific vs. general improvements; experiments compare forms of shared history (raw logs vs. filtered traces vs. reflective summaries).

Result: Group history is not a universal amplifier; the top-performing agent does not surpass its self-evolution ceiling. However, agents that stall under self-improvement can achieve significant breakthroughs with peer history. In competitive settings, improvements are generally not opponent-specific. Social gains vary with the form of shared history: filtered traces and reflective summaries often outperform raw logs, indicating gains arise from abstraction of public traces rather than exposure volume. Peer-history gains are agent-specific, arena-dependent, and hinge on the ability to abstract transferable knowledge from public traces.

Conclusion: Peer-history gains depend on agent characteristics, task domain, and the ability to extract transferable knowledge from public traces. Abstraction, not sheer exposure, mediates social improvement, suggesting that effective social-learning design should emphasize meaningful summaries and abstractions of peer experience.

Abstract: Self-improving language agents are typically evaluated in isolation: an agent attempts a task, receives feedback, and iteratively refines its own behavior. Yet agents increasingly operate alongside peers whose strategies and outcomes are publicly visible. This raises an under-studied question: when does shared experience produce improvements that self-improvement alone cannot achieve? We introduce SAGE (Social Agent Group Evolution),an evaluation framework that compares two compute-matched conditions: SocialEvo, where agents from five distinct model families co-evolve with access to all peers' histories; and SelfEvo, where each agent receives the same number of task attempts but sees only its own past, which is conventional in self-improving agent studies. We instantiate SAGE in three arenas: open-ended ML research, long-horizon economic planning, and strategic multiplayer play, evaluated across multiple evolutionary rounds. We find that group history is not a universal amplifier: the strongest agent does not exceed its self-evolution ceiling. However, agents that plateau under self-improvement can achieve significant breakthroughs when peer experience is available. In competitive settings, counterfactual controls reveal that agents improve generally rather than developing opponent-specific strategies. Across different forms of shared history, filtered peer traces and reflective summaries often outperform raw logs, indicating that social gains depend on abstraction rather than exposure volume. These findings reveal that peer-history gains are agent-specific, arena-dependent, and contingent on the capacity to abstract transferable knowledge from public traces.

</details>


### [234] [From Prompt to Service: An SLM-Based Agent Orchestration Gateway for AI-Driven Virtual Worlds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03557)
*Louis Nisiotis,Aimilios Hadjiliasi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Edge-deployed, lightweight SLM-based gateway that decouples virtual-world clients from heterogeneous AI backends via intent-driven routing, enabling scalable, extensible AI services in virtual worlds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the integration and orchestration challenge of multimodal AI capabilities in AI-driven virtual worlds by avoiding backend changes in clients and enabling edge-cloud coordination of diverse AI services.

Method: Introduce an edge-based SLM for semantic intent classification, a configurable service registry for routing validation, and a layered configuration (fine-tuned sub-billion-parameter model as router + larger SLM for response generation) to route requests to appropriate backends; implement and evaluate in the InterwovenXR virtual museum testbed.

Result: Compact SLMs can function as reliable edge intent routers; task-specific fine-tuning improves routing performance; layered router+generator config on mid-range edge hardware is more efficient than a single-model solution; demonstrates practical AI service orchestration in virtual worlds.

Conclusion: SLMs can support scalable, extensible AI interaction in virtual worlds by enabling virtual agents to act as access points to distributed generative AI services, with edge-supported orchestration validated by the study.

Abstract: As generative AI capabilities expand, AI-driven virtual worlds face a growing architectural challenge. Users interact through in-world interfaces in multimodal ways, yet their requests demand fundamentally different AI backend models and computational resources. Embedding these capabilities directly into virtual world systems reduces extensibility, complicates maintenance, and limits the ability to coordinate services distributed across edge and cloud infrastructure. This paper presents an SLM-based Agent Orchestration Gateway, a lightweight runtime coordination mechanism that decouples a virtual world client from heterogeneous AI backends through intent-driven service routing. An edge-deployed SLM classifies the semantic intent of each user prompt, a configurable service registry validates and resolves the routing decision, and the selected backend is invoked transparently, enabling new AI capabilities to be introduced in the virtual world without modifying the client application. The gateway is implemented and evaluated within the InterwovenXR virtual museum testbed. The evaluation shows that compact SLMs can serve as reliable intent routers on edge hardware, and that task-specific fine-tuning can transform sub-billion-parameter models into practical, low-latency routers. A layered configuration pairing a fine-tuned sub billion-parameter model as router with a larger SLM for conversational response generation is shown to be deployable on mid-range edge hardware and more efficient than delegating both responsibilities to a single model. The findings show that SLMs can support practical AI service orchestration in virtual worlds and the work contributes an evaluated architecture for scalable, extensible, and edge-supported AI interaction, enabling virtual agents become access points to distributed generative AI services.

</details>


### [235] [Cross-Lingual Token Arbitrage: Optimizing Code Agent Context Windows via Local LLM Preprocessing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03618)
*Mehmet Utku Colak*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Edge-side prompt rewriting using a local 3B Llama to translate non-English prompts to English, structurally rewrite prompts into compact task-oriented formats, and apply regex safeguards; reduces prompt and total tokens while preserving or improving accuracy across multiple LLM backends on OMH-Polyglot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI-assisted coding is bottlenecked by input-token costs caused by non-English tokenization inefficiency and high structural entropy in prompts; existing approaches compress after the fact or intervene only after failures.

Method: A pre-flight middleware deployed at the edge that uses a local Llama 3.2 (3B) model to perform cross-lingual translation to English, structural rewriting into a compact task-oriented format, and regex-validated rewrite-with-fallback safeguards to ensure the optimized prompt is never larger than the original; operates between the developer and cloud agent across three commercial LLM backends.

Result: Reduces prompt tokens by 34-47% and total tokens by up to 18.8%; maintains or improves task accuracy across backends; ablation shows gains mainly from rewriting stage rather than function-name extraction; at matched compression rates, outperforms LLMLingua-2 in OckScore across all evaluated backends.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that proactive, edge-side prompt optimization can substantially reduce inference costs without sacrificing coding quality.

Abstract: AI-assisted coding agents are bottlenecked by input-token cost. Two pathologies of raw human input drive much of this overhead: tokenization inefficiency for non-English text and structural entropy in conversational prompts. Existing approaches act reactively by compressing already-bloated contexts or intervening after failures occur.
  We introduce a pre-flight, edge-side prompt-rewriting middleware that operates between the developer and the cloud agent. A local Llama 3.2 (3B) model performs cross-lingual translation into English, structural rewriting into a compact task-oriented format, and regex-validated rewrite-with-fallback safeguards to ensure the optimized prompt is never larger than the original.
  We evaluate on OMH-Polyglot, a multilingual coding benchmark spanning Turkish, Arabic, Chinese, and code-switched specifications. Across three commercial LLM backends, the middleware reduces prompt tokens by 34-47 percent and total tokens by up to 18.8 percent while preserving or improving task accuracy. Ablation studies show that gains arise primarily from the rewriting stage rather than simple function-name extraction. Compared with LLMLingua-2 at matched compression rates, our method consistently achieves superior OckScore performance across all evaluated backends. These results demonstrate that proactive prompt optimization can substantially reduce inference costs without sacrificing coding quality.

</details>


### [236] [Bridging Auxiliary Constraints to Resolve Instruction Following in Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03624)
*Zhengyi Zhao,Shubo Zhang,Huimin Wang,Zezhong Wang,Yutian Zhao,Yefeng Zheng,Binyang Li,Yulan He,Kam-Fai Wong,Xian Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CRGC introduces a Constraint Relationship Graph Completion framework that models instructions as a knowledge-graph of constraints, adds bridge constraints to align and reconcile requirements, and reduces constraint violations by 39% on three instruction-following datasets while preserving reasoning abilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) often fail to reliably satisfy multiple, potentially competing constraints (the Constraint Adherence Problem, CAP). There is a need for a structured mechanism to model constraints and their relationships to improve adherence beyond generic instruction-following improvements.

Method: Encode instructions as a structured knowledge graph of constraints. CRGC explicitly models relationships between constraints, identifies adherence challenges, and discovers bridge constraints—auxiliary instructions that make primary constraints more salient and compatible. Bridge constraints guide generation by leveraging the model's own knowledge to create better pathways. Unlike general training approaches, CRGC targets constraint satisfaction via graph-based reasoning and constraint-aware prompting.

Result: Empirical evaluation on three instruction-following datasets shows CRGC reduces constraint violations by 39% compared to standard prompting, while maintaining the model's reasoning abilities.

Conclusion: CRGC provides a targeted, graph-based approach to CAP, using bridge constraints to reconcile competing requirements and leverage internal knowledge, achieving substantial reductions in constraint violations without sacrificing reasoning performance.

Abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in many tasks, yet they struggle with reliably following multiple instructions, either by failing to satisfy individual constraints or by struggling to balance competing constraints simultaneously. We formalize this challenge as the Constraint Adherence Problem (CAP). This paper introduces a novel framework that addresses CAP by representing instructions as a structured knowledge graph of constraints. Our approach, Constraint Relationship Graph Completion (CRGC), explicitly models relationships between constraints, identifies adherence challenges, and discovers ``bridge constraints'' that help the model better focus on and reconcile requirements. Bridge constraints act as auxiliary instructions that make primary constraints more salient and compatible. Unlike existing approaches that enhance instruction following through general training methods, CRGC specifically improves constraint satisfaction by leveraging the model's own knowledge to create better pathways for generation. Experiments across three popular instruction following datasets demonstrate that our approach reduces constraint violations by 39% compared to standard prompting while maintaining reasoning abilities of large reasoning models.

</details>


### [237] [TSQAgent: Rating Time Series Data Quality via Dedicated Agentic Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03629)
*Shunyu Wu,Dan Li,Haozheng Ye,Weibin Feng,Jian Lou,Bo Zhang,Wenjie Feng,Chenjuan Guo,See-Kiong Ng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A benchmark (TSQBench) and agentic framework (TSQAgent) for time-series quality assessment with LLMs. They tackle two capabilities: identifying relevant quality dimensions and performing dimensioned, evidence-grounded quality comparisons. Results show current LLMs struggle, while TSQAgent improves understanding and quantitative comparison, enabling better quality-aware data selection and downstream performance across 11 real datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quality assessment of time-series data is multi-dimensional and challenging. LLM-based approaches offer pairwise/text reasoning but may fail to identify truly relevant dimensions or to perform grounded, quantitative comparisons. A benchmark and an agentic framework are proposed to address these gaps.

Method: Introduce TSQBench to evaluate two capabilities: (i) discovering relevant quality dimensions, and (ii) conducting quality comparisons under given dimensions. Propose TSQAgent with three roles—Perceiver (selects relevant dimensions), Inspector (performs dimension-wise quantitative analysis), Adjudicator (aggregates/refines judgments). Leverage an agent workflow using external analytical tools to enable precise quantitative comparisons on selected dimensions.

Result: LLMs show consistent difficulty in dimension identification and evidence-grounded comparisons. TSQAgent substantially improves understanding and quantitative comparison, translating to better quality-aware data selection, improved downstream performance, and greater data efficiency on both the benchmark and 11 real-world datasets.

Conclusion: An agentic, tool-enabled framework (TSQAgent) effectively enhances LLM-based time-series quality assessment, enabling robust dimension identification and quantitative comparisons that lead to improved data selection and downstream outcomes.

Abstract: Assessing the quality of time series (TS) data is fundamental yet inherently challenging due to the multifaceted nature of quality dimensions. Recently, large language models (LLMs) have emerged as a promising paradigm for TS quality assessment via pairwise comparison and per-dimension evaluation. However, existing approaches rely on manually predefined quality dimensions and purely text-based reasoning, leaving it unknown whether LLMs can identify truly relevant quality dimensions or perform grounded and quantitative quality comparisons. To investigate this, we construct TSQBench, a dedicated benchmark for evaluating LLMs on two progressive capabilities: (i) understanding and identifying relevant quality dimensions, and (ii) performing quality comparison under specific dimensions. Our analysis reveals that current LLMs consistently struggle with both dimension identification and evidence-grounded quality comparison. To address these limitations, we propose TSQAgent, a novel agentic reasoning framework for TS quality rating consisting of three collaborative roles: Perceiver for focused dimension selection, Inspector for dimension-wise quantitative analysis, and Adjudicator that aggregates and refines the final judgment. In particular, we introduce an agentic reasoning strategy that instills the ability to identify and prioritize the most relevant quality dimensions, and further propose an agent workflow equipped with external analytical tools to enable precise quantitative comparisons over selected dimensions. Experiments on both the proposed benchmark and eleven real-world datasets demonstrate that our framework not only substantially improves LLMs' capabilities in quality understanding and quantitative comparison but also effectively translates these improvements into better quality-aware data selection, leading to enhanced downstream performance and data efficiency.

</details>


### [238] [Gender-Dependent Diagnostic Substitution in LLM Medical Triage: Same Symptoms, Unequal Urgency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03641)
*Qi Han Wong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs show gender- and age-based triage disparities for identical neurological symptoms; young women receive far fewer ER referrals than men, likely due to diagnostic anchoring on IIH; disparity vanishes by age 65; highlights need to decouple urgency from diagnostic priors; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether clinical LLMs replicate real-world epidemiological biases in triage and could propagate under-triage for certain demographics.

Method: Standardized symptom profile (persistent headache, blurred vision, morning nausea, visual disturbances) assessed across seven demographics (25, 38, 65 years × male, female, plus gender-unspecified). Three model families (Gemini 3.5 Flash, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.4-mini) with 30 trials per condition (630 total). Outcomes: triage urgency (ER referral vs outpatient) and diagnostic labels; analysis of diagnostic substitution (IIH vs space-occupying lesion).

Result: Consistent gender- and age-related disparities in ER referrals: Gemini 0%/23.3%, Claude 6.7%/96.7%, GPT 6.7%/66.7% for women vs men (all p<0.001). Age 65 eliminates disparity across models. Severity ratings similar (7–9/10). Diagnostic anchor on IIH in young women; men labeled with more urgent conditions. Authors provide code, prompts, and raw results.

Conclusion: AI triage systems should decouple urgency assessment from probabilistic diagnostic priors; current LLMs can reproduce human biases. Emphasizes need for debiasing, transparent priors, and evaluation of triage vs diagnosis. Reproducible research with released artifacts.

Abstract: We investigate whether large language models produce different medical triage recommendations for identical neurological symptoms when only the patient's stated gender and age vary. Using three model families--Gemini 3.5 Flash, Claude Sonnet 4.6, and GPT-5.4-mini--we present a standardized symptom profile (persistent headache, blurred vision, morning nausea, visual disturbances) across seven demographic conditions: three age groups (25, 38, 65) x two genders (male, female), plus a gender-unspecified baseline (n = 30 per condition per model, 630 total trials). We find a stark, systemic gender-dependent triage disparity: young women receive significantly lower emergency room (ER) referral rates than age-matched men (Gemini: 0% vs. 23.3%; Claude: 6.7% vs. 96.7%; GPT: 6.7% vs. 66.7%, all p < 0.001). The disparity disappears at age 65 for all models. The primary mechanism is diagnostic substitution: the models anchor on a gender-associated diagnosis, preferentially classifying young women with Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH)--a condition epidemiologically linked to women of childbearing age--while diagnosing men with generic increased intracranial pressure with space-occupying lesions in the differential. This diagnostic closure routes female patients to lower-urgency care (outpatient doctor appointments) despite comparable severity ratings (7-9/10). Our findings demonstrate that clinical LLMs replicate documented human clinical biases by using epidemiological priors to suppress triage urgency, suggesting that AI triage engines must decouple urgency assessment from probabilistic diagnostic priors. We release all code, prompts, and raw results.

</details>


### [239] [Towards Non-Monotonic Entailment in Propositional Defeasible Standpoint Logic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03655)
*Nicholas Leisegang,Thomas Meyer,Ivan Varzniczak*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A large fragment of propositional defeasible standpoint logic (PDSL) can be captured with situated standpoint conditionals, enabling transfer of ranking-based non-monotonic entailment (rational and lexicographic closures) from propositional logic into PDSL while preserving computational complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend defeasible reasoning with modal standpoint logics beyond satisfiability and monotonic entailment, by enriching PDSL with situated conditionals and lifting non-monotonic KLM-style semantics.

Method: Introduce situated standpoint conditionals and re-express PDSL syntax in terms of these conditionals. Show that a large PDSL fragment is expressible as a set of situated conditionals. Define a translation method that transports any ranking-based (non-monotonic) entailment relation from the propositional case into PDSL. Apply this to rational and lexicographic closures, providing faithful translations. Demonstrate that entailment checking in the fragment can largely reuse propositional algorithms while preserving complexity bounds.

Result: A faithful translation framework from propositional ranking-based entailment to PDSL via situated conditionals; the fragment captures diverse PDSL expressions; rational and lexicographic closures are instantiated within PDSL; complexity of entailment checks compatible with propositional methods.

Conclusion: The work provides a principled extension of PDSL to support non-monotonic reasoning using situated conditionals, enabling transporter of widely used propositional ranking-based entailments (e.g., rational/lexicographic) with preserved computational bounds, and highlighting practical entailment checking using propositional algorithms.

Abstract: Recent work in defeasible reasoning has seen notions of preferential semantics and entailment in the style of Kraus et al. applied to modal logics. However, work in this field has focussed primarily on satisfiability checking, and monotonic notions of entailment, which may be inferentially weak. One particular modal logic where this has been introduced is propositional standpoint logics, where modalities can express the views of different viewpoints. This has resulted in the formalisation of propositional defeasible standpoint logic (PDSL). In this paper, we propose a means of lifting the class of (non-monotonic) rational entailment relations from traditional KLM-style reasoning to a fragment of PDSL. In order to do so, we extend the expressivity of PDSL via situated standpoint conditionals, allowing us to talk about a defeasible conditional holding in the context of a given standpoint. This allows us to re-characterise the syntax of PDSL in terms of situated conditionals, and shows that a large fragment of PDSL is expressible as a set of situated conditionals. We then focus on characterising non-monotonic entailment in this fragment, defining a method to transport any ranking-based entailment relation from the propositional case into the PDSL case. This is first described in the general case and then considered in the specific cases of rational and lexicographic closures, providing a faithful translation of each inference into PDSL. We also show that entailment-checking in this fragment of PDSL can be done largely using algorithms from the propositional case, while preserving complexity bounds.

</details>


### [240] [Diagnosing Knowledge Gaps in LLM Tool Use: An Agentic Benchmark for Novel API Acquisition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03657)
*Jinnuo Liu,Yue Peng,Jinhan Niu,Hongyi Wen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Dynamic automated benchmark NovelAPIBench discovers novel APIs, extracts knowledge bundles, and evaluates retrieval vs tuning for LLM code generation, showing complementary roles and non-interchangeable knowledge components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing novel-API benchmarks are static, coarse, or synthetic and miss real library evolution; it's essential to study how models acquire and apply API knowledge in executable tasks.

Method: Automated pipeline: (1) discover novel APIs for a base model and target library; (2) extract decomposed knowledge bundles (signatures, module paths, IO contracts, semantics, usage patterns); (3) generate executable coding tasks; (4) assign failed samples to six diagnostic categories; (5) evaluate across ~1.9K tasks, four base models, five domains; (6) compare knowledge injected via retrieval versus parametric adaptation; also analyze the impact of added context.

Result: Knowledge components are not interchangeable: usage examples are the strongest standalone signal; the best two-component setting pairs signatures with either mechanisms or examples depending on domain/backbone; additional context (e.g., source code) can hurt due to import-path errors; parametric adaptation does not replace retrieval and mainly teaches how to use the provided bundles, with some transfer to held-out libraries; retrieval supplies volatile API content while tuning improves procedural integration; together they are complementary.

Conclusion: Dynamic, automated benchmarking reveals that retrieval and tuning play complementary roles for learning novel APIs: retrieval handles volatile API content, while tuning improves procedural integration; future benchmarks should embrace automated discovery, diagnostic categorization, and evaluation across diverse domains.

Abstract: Large language models for code generation often need to use APIs that are absent from their pretraining data. This requires more than recalling a function name: models must coordinate signatures, module paths, input-output contracts, semantics, and executable usage patterns. Existing novel-API benchmarks are typically static, rely on coarse pass/fail metrics, or use synthetic APIs that may not reflect real library evolution. We introduce NovelAPIBench, a fully automated dynamic benchmark that, for any base model and target library, discovers novel APIs, extracts decomposed knowledge bundles, generates executable coding tasks, and assigns failed samples to six diagnostic categories. Across about 1.9K tasks, four base models, and five domains, we compare knowledge injected through retrieval with knowledge internalized through parametric adaptation. We find that knowledge components are not interchangeable: usage examples are the strongest standalone signal, while the best two-component setting pairs signatures with either mechanisms or examples depending on the domain and backbone. Adding more context, especially source code, can hurt by increasing import-path errors. Parametric adaptation also does not replace retrieval once external knowledge is removed; rather, fine-tuning mainly teaches models how to use provided bundles, and this ability transfers to held-out libraries. These results suggest that retrieval and tuning play complementary roles: retrieval supplies volatile API content, while tuning improves procedural integration.

</details>


### [241] [From Answers to States: Verifiable Process-Level Evaluation of Chemical Reasoning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03660)
*Hongyu Guo,Hao Li,He Cao,Gongbo Zhang,Li Yuan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ChemCoTBench-V2 is a rule-verifiable benchmark for evaluating structured chemical reasoning traces in LLMs, enabling auditable, low-cost assessment across 5,620 samples and 18 tasks, reporting final-answer correctness, template adherence, and step-wise verifier correctness; reveals gap between final-answer accuracy and reasoning-state consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the critical failure mode where language models provide correct chemical outputs but violate chemical logic, and to overcome the cost and inconsistency of human step-level annotations and LLM judges by enabling verifier-addressable traces.

Method: Introduce ChemCoTBench-V2 with expert-designed templates requiring exposure of key intermediate steps; use deterministic chemistry rules to verify steps; for closed-answer tasks, compare to reference traces; evaluate open-ended molecular optimization with oracle-verifiable state constraints rather than strict trace matching; report three signals: final-answer correctness, template adherence, and step-wise verifier correctness across 5,620 samples in 18 tasks.

Result: Benchmark spans molecular understanding, editing, optimization, and reaction prediction; 5,620 evaluation samples across 18 reporting tasks; frontier models show a persistent gap between final-answer success and structured-reasoning-state consistency; models may follow the required format yet fail chemical-step checks or provide correct answers with weak reasoning; enables fine-grained comparison and identifies the first violating step.

Conclusion: ChemCoTBench-V2 offers scalable, auditable evaluation of chemical reasoning traces, enabling fine-grained diagnostics, identifying when traces fail verifier checks, and guiding model improvement by isolating the exact step where reasoning diverges from chemical rules.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used as chemistry assistants, yet most chemistry benchmarks still score only final answers. This masks a critical failure mode: a model may output the correct molecule, product, or option while its reasoning violates chemical logic. Existing process-level evaluators are hard to scale because LLM judges and human step-level process annotation are costly, inconsistent, and vulnerable to hallucination. We introduce ChemCoTBench-V2, a rule-verifiable diagnostic benchmark for low-cost, auditable evaluation of structured, verifier-addressable chemical reasoning traces. It spans molecular understanding, molecule editing, molecular optimization, and reaction prediction, with 5,620 evaluation samples across 18 reporting tasks. Models must expose key intermediate steps in expert-designed templates, and those steps are checked with deterministic chemistry rules and, for closed-answer tasks, reference traces rather than another LLM judge. Open-ended molecular optimization is evaluated with oracle-verifiable state constraints rather than strict trace matching. The benchmark reports three separate signals: final-answer correctness, template adherence, and step-wise verifier correctness over expert-refined intermediate commitments. Experiments on frontier models reveal a persistent gap between final-answer success and structured-reasoning-state consistency: models often follow the requested format while failing chemical-step checks, or answer correctly with weak supporting reasoning. ChemCoTBench-V2 enables fine-grained model comparison and identifies the concrete step at which the trace first violates the verifier.

</details>


### [242] [EvoDrive: Pareto Evolution for Safety-Critical Autonomous Driving via Self-Improving LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03678)
*Tong Nie,Yuewen Mei,Yihong Tang,Junlin He,Jie Deng,Jian Sun,Wei Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A novel automated, LLM-guided agentic evolution framework (EvoDrive) for multi-objective, simulator-grounded scenario generation in autonomous driving, expanding the Pareto frontier on MetaDrive and CARLA and yielding useful training scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need to generate safety-critical driving scenarios that balance realism and adversariality without relying on handcrafted heuristics or unconstrained agents that ignore simulator grounding.

Method: Simulator-grounded actor-critic architecture with memory-driven actor proposing generator improvements; critics filter implausible candidates; a self-evolving world evaluator routes promising proposals to optimize simulation budgets; maintains a Pareto archive to preserve attack-realism trade-offs; uses an LLM-based agentic evolution framework across multiple generators; evaluated on MetaDrive and CARLA.

Result: Significantly expands the Pareto frontier across various generators; produces valuable scenarios for policy training in autonomous driving.

Conclusion: EvoDrive demonstrates the feasibility and benefits of automated, LLM-guided, simulator-grounded multi-objective scenario generation, opening new possibilities for diverse, high-quality safety-critical scenario generation and policy validation.

Abstract: Generating safety-critical scenarios is essential for validating and improving autonomous driving systems, yet it inherently requires maximizing adversariality to expose failures while preserving realism. Existing methods usually manage this trade-off with handcrafted heuristics, confining generation to known priors and overlooking underexplored patterns. While recent open-ended agentic evolution can push this limit, unconstrained general agents lack strict simulator grounding and tend to collapse the multi-objective tension into single-scalar maximization. Here we present EvoDrive, the first automated, LLM-based agentic evolution framework for multi-objective scenario generation. EvoDrive employs a simulator-grounded actor-critic architecture where a memory-driven actor iteratively proposes improvements to the generators and critics filter out implausible candidates, and a self-evolving world evaluator routes promising proposals to optimize simulation budgets. EvoDrive further maintains a Pareto archive of evaluated candidates to preserve diverse attack-realism trade-offs and guide future evolution via simulation feedback. Benchmark results on MetaDrive and CARLA show that EvoDrive not only significantly expands the Pareto frontier across various generators, but also produces valuable scenarios for policy training.

</details>


### [243] [The DeepSpeak-Agentic Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03686)
*Sarah Barrington,Maty Bohacek,Hany Farid*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces DeepSpeak-Agentic: a 37+ hour dataset of semi-structured human–embodied AI conversations and a scalable capture system, to benchmark AI-generated media identification and study human–agent interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for benchmarks and methods to detect AI agents in audio/video/text, and to understand dynamics of human–agent interactions in embodied AI contexts.

Method: Collect and annotate 37+ hours of semi-structured conversations between humans and embodied AI agents; develop a scalable data-capture pipeline that creates agents, pairs them with crowd workers, records audiovisual exchanges, and automatically separates human and agent streams.

Result: A large, multi-modal dataset and accompanying data-capture infrastructure that enables forensic identification benchmarks and analysis of human–agent interactions; provides a foundation for evaluating advances in AI-generated voices/faces and LLMs.

Conclusion: The work delivers resources and benchmarks to propel research on AI-generated media detection and embodied AI interactions, supporting reproducible evaluation and future improvements in agent realism and identification.

Abstract: We present DeepSpeak-Agentic, a dataset of videos comprising over 37 hours of semi-structured conversations between a human and an embodied AI agent. We use this dataset to evaluate the automatic forensic identification (audio, video, or text) of AI agents, study the nature of human-agent interactions, and provide a benchmark for future advances in the large-language models and AI-generated voices and faces that power embodied AI agents. We also contribute a scalable data-capture system that creates agents, automatically pairs them with human crowd workers, records audiovisual conversations across specified scenarios, and identifies and separates the human and agent in the combined stream.

</details>


### [244] [SkillPyramid: A Hierarchical Skill Consolidation Framework for Self-Evolving Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03692)
*Yuan Xiong,Ziqi Miao,Qian Chen,Lijun Li,Yequan Wang,Shizhu He,Jun Zhao,Kang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillPyramid introduces a hierarchical skill consolidation framework with a self-evolution mechanism to reuse, compose, and validate skills for broader task generalization, achieving substantial performance gains across multiple environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI agents lack a unified, scalable approach to construct, accumulate, and transfer skills, leading to redundant capabilities and limited generalization to novel tasks.

Method: Proposes SkillPyramid with a hierarchical skill topology and a self-evolution process that composes, validates, and incorporates new skills during task execution; evaluated on ALFWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld with four backbone models.

Result: Average reward improved by 38.0% and execution steps reduced by 27.7% across tested tasks and backbones.

Conclusion: Transforms a static skill pool into a dynamic evolution system, enabling more efficient reuse and generalization of skills for long-term agent improvement.

Abstract: Recent AI agents can flexibly invoke skills to solve complex tasks, but their long-term improvement is fundamentally constrained by a lack of systematic skill construction, accumulation, and transfer. In particular, without a unified framework for skill consolidation, agents tend to redundantly construct similar capabilities across different tasks, are unable to effectively transform experience into reusable assets, and struggle to generalize task-specific skills to novel scenarios. To address this limitation, we propose SkillPyramid, a skill consolidation framework that reuses existing skill experience for broader task generalization. Operating on a hierarchical skill topology, SkillPyramid further introduces a self-evolution mechanism that enables agents to compose, validate, and incorporate new skills during task execution. Experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld across four backbone models show that SkillPyramid substantially increases the average reward by 38.0% and reduces execution steps by 27.7%. Overall, our method transforms a skill collection from a static resource pool into a dynamic evolution system.

</details>


### [245] [Dynamic Objective Selection with Safeguards and LLM Oversight for Financial Decision-Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03704)
*Keigo Sakurai,Takahiro Ogawa,Miki Haseyama,Anjyu Anan,Kei Nakagawa*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Dynamic objective selection (DOSS) chooses among candidate objectives for financial decision-making at each time point using recent returns, with confidence gating and LLM governance to prevent misselection and excessive switching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed objectives can be suboptimal across market regimes; regime-switching with latent estimates is noisy and reactive switching can destabilize portfolios. A real-time, safe objective selector is needed.

Method: Formulates objective selection as a classification problem over objective candidates; uses rolling window updates to avoid temporal leakage; outputs a per-proposal confidence score; applies confidence-aware gating with a fail-safe default to limit misselection and switching; enforces explicit switching controls; governance via an LLM limited to accepting proposed objective or overriding to a safe default with deterministic rule-based overrides.

Result: Abstract focuses on framework and mechanisms; no empirical results are stated in the provided text.

Conclusion: Introduces DOSS, a dynamic, governance-enabled objective selector with safeguards that aims to improve adaptability across regimes while reducing misselection and excessive switching.

Abstract: Financial decision-making tasks such as stock recommendation and portfolio allocation typically estimate future return and risk and then select trades or allocations for an investor, and the chosen optimization objective often determines realized performance. However, because market conditions evolve over time, a fixed objective can be suboptimal across regimes, while regime-switching pipelines that rely on latent regime estimates can be noisy or delayed and frequent switching can increase turnover and operational instability. In this paper, we propose DOSS (Dynamic Objective Selection with Safeguards), a learning-based selector that directly chooses the decision-relevant objective function at each time point from interpretable statistical summaries of recent returns, selecting among a small set of candidates (e.g., return-seeking, loss-averse, and risk-adjusted) without introducing intermediate regime variables. DOSS formulates objective selection as a classification problem over objectives and performs sequential updates with a rolling window to make forward-looking selections without temporal leakage, while also outputting a confidence score for each proposal. To mitigate misselection and excessive switching in deployment, DOSS applies confidence-aware gating with a fail-safe that overrides low-confidence proposals to a conservative default and enforces explicit controls tied to switching frequency. We further integrate governance by positioning a Large Language Model (LLM) as an oversight component rather than a generator of new objectives: the LLM is restricted to accept a proposed objective or override it to a predefined safe default, with deterministic rule-based constraints triggering overrides when needed.

</details>


### [246] [Code-on-Graph: Iterative Programmatic Reasoning via Large Language Models on Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03705)
*Weiwei Ding,Zixuan Li,Long Bai,Zhuo Chen,Kun Su,Fei Wang,Xiaolong Jin,Jin Zhang,Jiafeng Guo,Xueqi Cheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoG treats KG schemas as Python classes and generates executable code to perform reasoning with instantiated facts, enabling flexible, scalable LLM-KG integration without embedding large knowledge into prompts; achieves up to 10.5% gains on WebQSP, CWQ, GrailQA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predefined operators for LLM-KG access limit expressiveness and scalability; there is a need for a flexible, scalable mechanism to reason over factual knowledge without prompt-bloat.

Method: For each reasoning step, CoG retrieves facts, identifies the corresponding KG schemas, represents these schemas as Python classes acting as interfaces, and generates executable code using these classes. Retrieved facts are instantiated as objects during execution to drive reasoning.

Result: Empirical evaluation on WebQSP, CWQ, GrailQA shows up to 10.5% improvement over state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: Code-on-Graph provides a programmable, scalable framework that decouples factual knowledge from prompt content, enabling robust KG-informed reasoning in LLMs and showing strong gains on standard KG QA benchmarks.

Abstract: Knowledge Graphs (KGs) are widely used to mitigate the limitations of Large Language Models (LLMs), such as outdated knowledge and hallucinations. Existing LLM-KG integration frameworks typically rely on predefined operators to retrieve factual knowledge from KGs and inject it into prompts for answer generation. This paradigm faces two critical bottlenecks: 1) Inflexibility: The predefined operators are limited in scope and thus lack sufficient compositional expressiveness to fully capture the complex semantics required by KG questions. 2) Unscalability: Direct injection of factual knowledge into prompts limits scalability in handling large-scale factual knowledge. To address these two bottlenecks, we propose Code-on-Graph (CoG), a programmatic reasoning framework for LLM-KG integration. Specifically, given the factual knowledge retrieved at each reasoning step, CoG first identifies the corresponding KG schemas and represents these schemas as Python classes, which serve as abstract interfaces to the retrieved facts. It then generates executable code grounded in these classes, with the retrieved facts instantiated as objects of the corresponding classes during execution. This design enables flexible code-based reasoning while avoiding the direct injection of large-scale factual knowledge into prompts. Experiments on WebQSP, CWQ, and GrailQA demonstrate that CoG outperforms prior state-of-the-art models by up to 10.5%.

</details>


### [247] [Unveiling the Structure of Do-Calculus Reasoning via Derivation Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03719)
*Clément Yvernes,Emilie Devijver,Marianne Clausel,Eric Gaussier*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces derivation graphs for do-calculus to map equivalence classes of interventional expressions; shows a four-rule upper bound; reveals multiple valid estimands for the same causal quantity to improve estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Do-calculus creates many equivalent interventional expressions, but there is no compact representation or simple bound on rule application, hindering identification and efficient estimation.

Method: Define derivation graphs that encode sequences of do-calculus rule applications, analyze their structure to characterize the full space of observational and interventional probabilities equivalent under the do-calculus, prove a bound of at most four rule applications, and show that identification on equivalent queries yields multiple valid estimands.

Result: Characterization of the complete space of probabilities equivalent under do-calculus; a simple, four-rule bound for deriving interventional expressions; demonstration that equivalent queries admit multiple valid estimands, enabling more efficient estimators.

Conclusion: Derivation graphs offer a principled framework to study do-calculus equivalences, enable a compact derivation bound, and provide practical benefits for estimation by exposing alternative estimands with potential efficiency gains.

Abstract: The do-calculus defines a general system of inference for interventional queries, allowing causal quantities to be transformed through successive applications of its rules. This process induces a rich space of equivalent interventional expressions, but combining and ordering these rules remains challenging. In this work, we introduce derivation graphs, which represent how do-calculus rules are applied and combined, and characterize the full space of observational and interventional probabilities which are equivalent under the do-calculus. The structure of these graphs yields a simple procedure that uses at most four applications of do-calculus rules. Finally, we show how applying identification algorithms to equivalent causal queries produces multiple valid estimands for the same causal quantity, eventually yielding more efficient estimators.

</details>


### [248] [When to Re-Plan: Subgoal Persistence in Hierarchical Latent Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03741)
*Ayushi Chadha*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Feudal-style manager-worker latent HRM with subgoal persistence optimizes long-horizon planning; best performance at moderate persistence (P≈3) and intrinsic alignment weight (lambda≈0.05); persistence, not mere subgoal injection, drives compositional structure formation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate the stability-adaptivity tradeoff in latent multi-step reasoning where computation occurs inside hidden states, seeking a principled mechanism to balance re-planning and plan coherence.

Method: Extend Hierarchical Reasoning Model (HRM) with a slow high-level manager that periodically emits a normalized directional subgoal lasting for P low-level steps, biasing worker hidden-state updates and applying an intrinsic cosine alignment loss; evaluate on ARC and ConceptARC with ablations on P and lambda; replicate across seeds.

Result: Subgoal persistence is the central knob: moderate P in [3,6] outperforms very frequent (P=1) and long-horizon settings, with a clear minimum LM loss at P=3 (approx. 1.544 vs 1.674 at P=1; baseline 1.640; replicated mean 1.595, std 0.045). The alignment weight lambda shows a complementary narrow optimum near 0.05. Ablation at past-sweet-spot lambda isolates learned directional structure as the source of interference when alignment exceeds its optimum.

Conclusion: Medium-horizon intent must be coherent across enough computational steps for compositional structure to form; a design principle for latent reasoning systems is to maintain moderate persistence of high-level intent to enable stable, compositional planning.

Abstract: Long-horizon reasoning requires a system to commit to medium-horizon intent without becoming rigid: re-plan too often and computation never coheres into multi-step structure; commit too long and the plan goes stale. We study this stability-adaptivity tradeoff in the latent reasoning setting, where multi-step computation occurs inside hidden state rather than externalized token traces. We extend the Hierarchical Reasoning Model (HRM) with a feudal-style manager-worker interface: a slow high-level module periodically emits a normalized directional subgoal that persists for P low-level steps, biasing the worker's hidden-state updates and supplying an intrinsic cosine alignment loss. On ARC and ConceptARC, we find that subgoal persistence -- not subgoal injection alone -- is the central knob: moderate periods P in [3, 6] consistently outperform both very frequent (P=1) and very long horizons, with a clear minimum LM loss at P=3 (1.544 vs. 1.674 at P=1, 1.640 baseline; replicated over 5 seeds at mean 1.595, std 0.045). The intrinsic alignment weight lambda shows a complementary narrow optimum (lambda approximately 0.05). A controlled ablation at past-sweet-spot lambda isolates learned directional structure -- not architectural capacity or auxiliary loss alone -- as the source of interference when the alignment signal exceeds its optimum. Together these findings implicate a design principle for compositional planning in latent reasoning systems: medium-horizon intent must be coherent across enough computational steps for compositional structure to form.

</details>


### [249] [Proof-Refactor: Refactoring Generated Formal Proofs into Modular Artifacts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03743)
*Yiming Fu,Peixuan Liu,Zichen Wang,Kun yuan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Proof-Refactor, an agentic, four-phase framework for refactoring LLM-generated Lean proofs to improve readability/modularity/maintainability/reuse, evaluated on PutnamBench/Putnam2025, showing gains over Claude Code baseline, especially in signature quality and readability; argues process-guided refactoring is superior to length-based optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gaps between LLM-generated proofs and mature formal mathematics libraries due to compile-first objectives; readability, modularity, maintainability, and reusability are not well captured by length-based metrics; need a process-guided workflow inspired by human proof-refactoring.

Method: Four-phase Proof-Refactor: 1) extract candidate fragments, 2) design helper declarations, 3) formally prove extracted components, 4) repair original proof using verified components; evaluate on Lean proofs (PutnamBench, Putnam2025) against Claude Code baseline using rubric-based refactoring scores.

Result: Rubric-based refactoring scores improved; largest gains in signature quality and human readability; demonstrates process-guided refactoring enhances proof structure without optimizing for length.

Conclusion: Process-guided refactoring offers a viable path to library-quality proofs, shifting focus from length-based optimization to higher-level qualities like readability and modularity; potential for broader applicability beyond the current datasets.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown strong performance in generating formal proofs, their outputs often remain less readable, modular, maintainable, and reusable than proofs in mature formal mathematics libraries. We argue that this gap stems in part from the compile-first objective implicit in most proof-generation pipelines, which encourages monolithic or ad hoc proof scripts rather than library-quality artifacts. Existing approaches to proof-quality improvement often rely on explicit, computable optimization objectives. In practice, however, the most tractable and experimentally validated objectives are largely length-based, while higher-level qualities such as readability, modularity, maintainability, and reusability are difficult to reduce to reliable automatic metrics. Instead of optimizing proof improvement against a single proxy metric, we take a process-guided approach inspired by human proof-refactoring workflows. We propose an agentic framework $\textbf{Proof-Refactor}$ that decomposes proof refactoring into four phases: extracting candidate proof fragments, designing helper declarations, formally proving the extracted and designed components, and repairing the original proof using the verified components. On generated Lean proofs from PutnamBench and Putnam2025, Proof-Refactor improves rubric-based refactoring scores over a strong Claude Code refactoring baseline, with the largest gains in signature quality and human readability. These results suggest that process-guided refactoring can improve proof structure without treating proof length as the primary objective.

</details>


### [250] [LAP: An Agent-to-Instrument Protocol for Autonomous Science](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03755)
*Linwu Zhu,Liqiang Gao,Yan Chen,Dan Zhu,Jian Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LAP is a protocol that extends agent interoperability to the instrument edge, introducing four physical-world primitives (InstrumentCard, exclusive reservations, safety-fence with operator tokens, and a MeasurementResult schema with units, calibration, and uncertainty). It remains compatible with A2A/MCP and standard device interfaces (SiLA2, OPC-UA), specifies roles, a six-layer architecture, JSON-RPC, state machines, and cross-lab federation, and enables end-to-end autonomous experimental campaigns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent-interoperability standards cover agent-to-tool and agent-to-agent edges but neglect the instrument edge. Instruments are stateful, safety-critical, owned, and produce measurements with units and uncertainty; lacking a standard protocol hinders reliable, reproducible autonomous experiments across labs.

Method: Design of LAP that preserves A2A’s peer-to-peer, discovery-first lifecycle while adding four primitives: InstrumentCard (signed capability and limits), exclusive reservation for instrument/sample locking, a safety-fence handshake with operator-confirmation tokens cryptographically bound to a task, and a MeasurementResult schema with physically typed, calibrated, uncertainty-bearing, reproducible data. Defines roles, a six-layer architecture, JSON-RPC methods, task and safety state machines, an error model, and cross-lab federation; demonstrates end-to-end closed-loop workflow. It is transport-compatible with A2A/MCP and encapsulates existing device standards (SiLA2, OPC-UA).

Result: LAP fills the missing instrument edge in the agent ecosystem, enabling stateful, safe, and reproducible instrument control within autonomous campaigns while remaining interoperable with existing agent protocols and instrument standards.

Conclusion: LAP completes the triad of agent-to-instrument provisioning within autonomous laboratories by offering a complete, interoperable, safety-conscious protocol that can federate cross-lab deployments without displacing existing device standards.

Abstract: Autonomous science is moving from demonstration to infrastructure. Large language model agents now plan experiments, and self-driving laboratories execute them. Yet every such system rebuilds the link between the reasoning agent and the physical instrument from scratch, against fragmented vendor SDKs and standards built for deterministic software clients rather than probabilistic, goal-directed agents. Recent agent-interoperability protocols clarify two of the three edges of an agentic ecosystem (Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (MCP) standardizes the agent-to-tool edge, and Google's Agent2Agent (A2A) the agent-to-agent edge), but neither models the agent-to-instrument edge, where operations are stateful, safety-critical, exclusively owned, physically embodied, and produce measurements with units, calibration, and uncertainty. We present the Lab Agent Protocol (LAP), a protocol design that fills this gap. LAP retains A2A's peer-to-peer, discovery-first, task-lifecycle structure and adds four physical-world primitives: (i) the InstrumentCard, a signed capability and physical-limit description; (ii) first-class reservation for exclusive instrument and sample locking; (iii) a safety-fence handshake with operator-confirmation tokens cryptographically bound to a specific task and its parameters, gating hazardous and irreversible operations; and (iv) a MeasurementResult schema that makes every result physically typed (QUDT/UCUM), calibration-anchored, uncertainty-bearing, and reproducible by construction. We specify roles, a six-layer architecture, the JSON-RPC method set, the task and safety state machines, the error model, and cross-laboratory federation, and walk a closed-loop autonomous campaign through the protocol end-to-end. LAP is transport-compatible with the A2A/MCP ecosystem and encapsulates rather than replaces existing device standards such as SiLA 2 and OPC-UA.

</details>


### [251] [From Control Boundary to Insurance Claim: Reconstructing AI-Mediated Losses Through the CER Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03777)
*Alex Leung,Rex Zhang,Kentaroh Toyoda,SiewMei Loh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CER provides a use-case-level diagnostic for AI residual risk transfer, focusing on state reconstruction in AI-enabled losses rather than mere event reconstruction; it defines a three-part framework (C: control boundary, E: evidence reconstruction, R: insurance response) to assess enforceable controls, reconstructibility of system state, and insurance coverage. Public incidents illustrate applicability; aims to enable claim-grade evidence for AI-driven losses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI systems become capable of autonomous reasoning, tool use, and external interactions, losses may trace to the system's state and causal chain rather than isolated events. Traditional insurance/forensics struggle to reconstruct state-and-action histories for AI-driven incidents; CER aims to bridge this gap by formalizing reconstruction and insuranceability.

Method: Define a use-case-level diagnostic (CER) with three questions: C (control boundary) — did the system operate within an enforceable envelope? E (evidence reconstruction) — can the state and causal chain be reconstructed from retained artifacts? R (insurance response) — is the reconstructed loss insured, with verifiable proof for claims. Operationalizes this problem by outlining the AI-specific reconstruction needs and detailing claim-grade evidence; discusses real-world examples (PocketOS, Replit agentic DB deletion, Moffatt v. Air Canada) to ground the framework.

Result: A three-component framework (CER) that formalizes AI-specific loss reconstruction and outlines evidence requirements to support insurance claims; provides a structured approach to evaluate residual risk transfer for AI systems and connects theoretical concerns to adjudicated and public incidents.

Conclusion: CER offers a tractable, use-case-focused path to AI residual risk transfer by specifying control boundaries, reconstructible evidence, and insurance-facing proof. It lays groundwork for standardized claim-grade evidence and integration with AI/insurance practices, while highlighting practical considerations and areas for validation and standardization.

Abstract: AI losses that arise through an insured organization's generative or agentic AI system require state reconstruction, not merely event reconstruction, because the relevant state changes as the system reasons, retrieves, calls tools, and acts. The relevant question is not only what loss occurred, but what the system was allowed to do, what it actually did, and whether that reconstructed loss can support insurance claim recovery. This paper addresses losses in which the insured's AI system is in the causal chain, including externally triggered failures such as prompt injection, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) poisoning, malicious tool output, credential misuse, and data poisoning.
  Specifically, this paper introduces CER, a use-case-level diagnostic for AI residual risk transfer. C (control boundary) asks whether the system had an enforceable operating envelope. E (evidence reconstruction) asks whether the system state and causal chain can be reconstructed from retained artifacts. R (insurance response) asks whether the reconstructed loss is insured: whether insurance coverage is available in the market and placed for the insured, together with the proof needed to support insurance claim recovery.
  The paper makes three contributions: it defines the AI-specific reconstruction problem, operationalizes that problem through CER, and specifies claim-grade evidence for AI reconstruction. Public examples include the reported PocketOS and Replit agentic database-deletion incidents and Moffatt v. Air Canada as an adjudicated output/reliance case.
  Keywords: AI systems; CER framework; residual risk transfer; agentic AI; generative AI; AI insurance; evidence reconstruction.

</details>


### [252] [Enhancing Operational Safety via Agentic Dialogue Hazard Identification Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03812)
*Sanjay Das,Ran Elgedawy,Ethan Seefried,Ryan Burchfield,Tirthankar Ghosal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HAZDIAL evaluates whether multi-turn, multi-agent dialogue improves NLP-based hazard identification versus single-pass baselines, comparing adversarial debate and constructive discussion, with algorithmic interaction optimization, evaluated on a golden dataset using standard metrics and novel dialogue metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Operational safety in high-stakes domains requires reliable hazard identification. LLMs in safety analysis often rely on single-turn inference, which is brittle and lacks iterative self-correction and deliberation. The work proposes structured agentic dialogue (multi-agent, multi-turn) to enhance hazard analysis, bridging dialogue systems, multi-agent reasoning, and AI safety.

Method: Introduce HAZDIAL framework for structured agentic dialogue with multi-agent, multi-turn interactions. Systematically compare two dialogue modalities—adversarial debate and constructive discussion—and propose an algorithm-based agentic interaction optimization. Evaluate configurations against a curated golden dataset using standard classification metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1) and novel dialogue metrics.

Result: The study provides empirical evidence that dialogue-driven hazard analysis is beneficial over single-pass baselines. Performance is assessed across configurations and modalities using standard and novel dialogue metrics; results indicate detectable improvements and modality-dependent effects, as reported in the abstract (specific numbers are not provided).

Conclusion: Advances the integration of dialogue systems and multi-agent reasoning within AI safety. The results support using structured, agentic dialogue for hazard identification and point to effective modalities and optimization strategies for improved safety analysis.

Abstract: Operational safety in high-stakes domains such as industrial process control, autonomous, and safety-critical systems, demand reliable hazard identification. While large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in automating safety analysis tasks, single-turn, monolithic inference is brittle: it lacks the self-correction, deliberation, and contextual refinement that safety engineers apply iteratively. In this paper, we introduce HAZDIAL, a framework that investigates whether structured agentic dialogue-multi-agent, multi-turn interactions improves the quality of NLP- based hazard identification over single-pass baselines. We systematically compare two dialogue modalities: adversarial debate and constructive discussion, and propose an algorithm-based agentic interaction optimization. We evaluate all configurations against a curated golden dataset using standard classification metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1) and novel dialogue metrics. This work advances the intersection of dialogue systems, multi-agent reasoning, and AI safety, providing an empirical evidence for dialogue-driven hazard analysis.

</details>


### [253] [Calibrating Urban Traffic Simulation from Sparse Road Observations via Genetic Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03823)
*Hunter Sawyer,Jesse Roberts,Simon Matei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A GA-based calibration framework that learns sparse road data to produce realistic SUMO traffic simulations without detailed job-location inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome two data bottlenecks in urban traffic modeling: limited real-world traffic measurements and lack of high-resolution employment data, enabling scalable, data-light calibration for many cities.

Method: A genetic algorithm optimizes job distributions and gate-traffic parameters within the SUMO platform to align simulated traffic with observed flows on a small subset of roads, without using detailed job-location data; validated on Greensboro, NC.

Result: Simulated traffic shows strong correlation with real measurements, generalizes to unseen road segments, and yields job distributions qualitatively aligned with census employment data despite not training on that data.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that realistic, scalable urban traffic simulation can be achieved from minimal observations, reducing data barriers for deploying traffic models across cities.

Abstract: Urban traffic simulation is a critical tool for infrastructure planning, including the placement of electric vehicle charging stations. However, realistic traffic simulation across many cities is hindered by two fundamental data limitations: detailed real-world traffic measurements are available for only a small fraction of road segments in most cities, and employment distribution data critical for modeling commuter traffic is rarely available at the resolution needed for simulation. This paper presents a genetic algorithm-based framework that directly addresses both limitations, calibrating urban traffic simulations from sparse road observations without requiring detailed job location data. Using the SUMO traffic simulation platform for Greensboro, North Carolina, our approach optimizes job distributions and gate-traffic parameters to align simulated traffic with a small sample of roads with known traffic-flow rates. We demonstrate that this approach produces simulated traffic that correlates well with real-world measurements, generalizes to road segments withheld from training, and produces job distributions that show promising qualitative agreement with census employment data despite never directly training on that employment data. This work demonstrates that realistic urban traffic simulation can be achieved from minimal real-world observations, offering a scalable and data-light approach to simulation calibration that reduces the barrier to deploying traffic models across diverse cities.

</details>


### [254] [BigFinanceBench: A Workflow-Grounded Benchmark for Financial-Research Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03829)
*Alex Wang,Georg Meinhardt,Jacob Katz,Joseph H. Kim,Pratyush K. Chaudhary,Chase Blagden,Eric Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BigFinanceBench is a large, open-ended benchmark that links ground-truth answers with rubric-based, stepwise derivations to evaluate auditable financial analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Finance research outputs are decision-relevant only when their derivations can be audited; existing benchmarks miss derivation quality, hindering evaluation of reproducibility and reasoning.

Method: A 928-item expert-authored benchmark of open-ended financial-research tasks. Each item pairs a ground-truth reference answer with a point-weighted rubric that decomposes the derivation into checkable steps. The benchmark is workflow-grounded, with 36,241 rubric points enabling partial-credit evaluation and localization of failures across the analyst workflow. Evaluation spans ten frontier/open-weight agents.

Result: Best system achieves 58.8% rubric score; final-answer accuracy correlates with but is a lossy proxy for derivation quality; model capability varies non-uniformly across financial workflows.

Conclusion: Workflow-grounded evaluation reveals substantial headroom in current systems and underscores the need to evaluate derivations, not only final answers, to improve auditable financial reasoning.

Abstract: Financial-research answers are decision-relevant only when another analyst can audit how they were produced: which source was chosen, which period and accounting definition were used, which assumptions were made, and how the calculation was performed. Existing finance benchmarks largely evaluate isolated subskills or final answers, leaving the auditable derivation itself under-measured. We introduce BigFinanceBench, a 928-item expert-authored benchmark of open-ended financial-research tasks in which each item pairs a ground-truth reference answer with a point-weighted rubric that decomposes the derivation into independently checkable steps. BigFinanceBench is workflow-grounded in that it evaluates the full derivation rather than only the final output. Across 36,241 rubric points, the benchmark supports partial-credit evaluation and localization of failures across the analyst workflow. Evaluating ten current frontier and open-weight agents, we find substantial headroom: the best system reaches only 58.8% rubric score, final-answer accuracy is a useful but lossy proxy for derivation quality, and model capability varies non-uniformly across financial workflows.

</details>


### [255] [Reasoning Structure of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03883)
*Frédéric Berdoz,Luca A. Lanzendörfer,Fabian Farestam,Roger Wattenhofer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A scalable benchmark for LRMs that converts reasoning traces into verifiable graphs, introduces a reasoning efficiency metric, and shows structural metrics reveal differences hidden by accuracy or token counts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation metrics (accuracy, token count) can obscure internal reasoning structures. A scalable, graph-based analysis of reasoning traces enables verifiable, quantitative assessment of reasoning topology and efficiency.

Method: A pipeline converts unstructured traces into graphs of claims and dependencies, enabling quantitative topology analysis. Introduces a reasoning efficiency metric that captures how concentrated the model's logical flow is. Applies the framework to open-source reasoning models on logic puzzles to assess how reasoning scales with difficulty.

Result: Structural metrics separate behaviors that token counts and accuracy conflate, providing a practical diagnostic tool for failure modes and for comparing how reasoning scales with puzzle difficulty.

Conclusion: Graph-based reasoning analysis offers a scalable, verifiable framework for evaluating LRMs beyond surface metrics, enabling more nuanced diagnosis and comparison of reasoning strategies.

Abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) are often evaluated using metrics such as final-answer accuracy or token count. However, identical scores on these metrics can hide fundamentally different reasoning structures. To address this limitation, we introduce a scalable LRM benchmark of logic puzzles and a pipeline that converts unstructured traces into verifiable reasoning graphs of claims and dependencies. This turns reasoning into a structured, measurable object whose topology can be quantitatively analyzed. Building on this, we define a reasoning efficiency metric that quantifies how concentrated the model's logical flow is. Our analysis on open-source reasoning models shows that structural measurements separate behaviors that token count and accuracy conflate, providing a practical tool for diagnosing failure modes and comparing how reasoning scales with puzzle difficulty.

</details>


### [256] [scTranslation: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Single-Cell Multi-Omics Modality Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03906)
*Jiabei Cheng,Jingbo Zhou,Jun Xia,Changkai Li,Zhen Lei,Chang Yu,Stan Z. Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: scTranslation offers a comprehensive benchmark for single-cell multi-omics modality translation, aggregating diverse datasets, models, and metrics, and analyzes factors like feature selection, feature quality, and few-shot settings to standardize evaluation and guide future research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no systematic benchmark for evaluating modality translation in single-cell multi-omics, despite rapid methodological advances and varied data quality/cost constraints.

Method: Assemble diverse translation datasets; integrate current state-of-the-art models; define and apply comprehensive evaluation metrics; conduct ablation-like analyses across scenarios (feature selection, feature quality, few-shot). Release open-source benchmark and code for community use.

Result: A publicly available benchmark suite with datasets, models, and metrics, plus insights from large-scale evaluation showing how different factors influence translation performance.

Conclusion: The benchmark enables standardized, reproducible evaluation of single-cell multi-omics translation methods and should accelerate method development; the open-source release promotes community adoption and extension.

Abstract: Simultaneous measurement of multiple omics modalities in single cells enables researchers to gain a more comprehensive understanding of cellular states and regulatory mechanisms. However, due to high experimental costs, significant noise, and incomplete modality coverage, a variety of computational methods for modality translation have emerged in recent years. Despite the development of translation models, there is still a lack of systematic benchmark evaluation in terms of datasets, evaluation metrics, and influencing factors. To address this, we present scTranslation, a comprehensive benchmark for single-cell multi-omics modality translation tasks. It includes diverse translation datasets, integrates state-of-the-art models, and provides a comprehensive evaluation metrics. In addition, we assess model performance under different scenarios, such as feature selection, feature quality, and few-shot settings. These factors significantly affect model performance but have rarely been systematically studied before. Leveraging this benchmark, we conduct a large-scale study of current methods, report many insightful findings that open up new possibilities for future development. The benchmark is open-sourced to facilitate future research. The code is anonymously released at https://github.com/Bunnybeibei/scTranslation.

</details>


### [257] [Hedge-Bench: Benchmarking Agents on Hard, Realistic Tasks Pertaining to Financial Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03918)
*Eric Cho,Shawn Huang,Alice Lu,Andy Lyu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Hedge-Bench 1.0 is a deterministic, expert-trace-grounded benchmark of 102 on-the-job hedge fund reasoning tasks to evaluate open-ended financial analysis; frontier models score under 16%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks miss open-ended analyst reasoning and rely on model-judged outputs noise; there is a need for reproducible, expert-grounded evaluation in finance.

Method: Construct 102 real tasks reflecting on-the-job analytic steps of hedge fund analysts, using explicit reasoning traces and relevant information sources; provide a deterministic evaluation harness and dataset to enable grading against expert steps.

Result: Frontier models and agents score below 16% on Hedge-Bench 1.0; dataset and harness are published publicly at GitHub.

Conclusion: Hedge-Bench 1.0 addresses a gap in open-ended reasoning evaluation for finance; current models perform far below expert-level on real-world tasks; the benchmark enables reproducible, expert-aligned evaluation and will drive progress.

Abstract: AI agents can increasingly handle the mechanical tasks of financial analysis: retrieving documents, calculating formulas, updating spreadsheets. The harder, more valuable challenge is reasoning through the open-ended questions that define expert Analyst work. Existing benchmarks do not capture this class of problem, and those that attempt to evaluate open-ended reasoning rely on model-judged outputs that introduce noise and circularity. We present Hedge-Bench 1.0: a benchmark of 102 actual, on-the-job tasks grounded in the explicit reasoning traces of professional hedge fund analysts working with relevant information sources. This approach enables deterministic grading against verified expert steps. Frontier models and agents score below 16\% on the benchmark. We publish the dataset and evaluation harness at github.com/Trata-Inc/trata-hedge-bench.

</details>


### [258] [Entropy Is Not Enough: Unlocking Effective Reinforcement Learning for Visual Reasoning via Vision-Anchored Token Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03937)
*Senjie Jin,Peixin Wang,Boyang Liu,Xiaoran Fan,Shuo Li,Zhiheng Xi,Jiazheng Zhang,Yuhao Zhou,Tao Gui,Qi Zhang,Xuanjing Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: In visual reasoning, token-level entropy alone fails for credit assignment; VEPO couples vision sensitivity with entropy via a multiplicative rule to select tokens that are both visually grounded and informative, yielding notable gains over entropy-only baselines (≈2.28 points on 7B-scale and 3.15 on 3B-scale).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
