Motivation: Question whether entropy-based credit assignment used in text-only RLVR extends to multimodal visual reasoning; identify why vision-insensitive token entropy can misguide learning and how to jointly optimize perception and semantic exploration.

Method: Introduce VEPO: a policy optimization framework that multiplicatively couples visual sensitivity with token entropy to prioritize tokens that are both visually grounded and high-entropy informative; incorporate this token selection into gradient credit attribution.

Result: VEPO achieves leading performance, outperforming the entropy-only baseline by 2.28 points at 7B-scale and 3.15 points at 3B-scale; ablations validate the contribution of the visual-grounded, high-informative token selection and the multiplicative coupling.

Conclusion: Token entropy alone is insufficient for visual reasoning; integrating visual grounding with entropy via a principled multiplicative coupling yields better credit assignment and performance in multimodal RL, as demonstrated by VEPO across scales.

Abstract: While token-level entropy is commonly recognized as effective for credit assignment in text-only reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), it remains unclear whether this mechanism still holds in visual reasoning. Our controlled study shows that this mechanism collapses in visual reasoning due to the omission of vision-sensitive tokens with naturally low entropy. Although existing multimodal RL methods increasingly acknowledge the importance of visual perception, they struggle to satisfy the inherent demand for interleaving precise perceptual grounding with semantic reasoning, either lacking systematic visual measurements or overlooking that token entropy primarily drives semantic exploration. To address this, we introduce VEPO (Vision-Entropy token-selection for Policy Optimization), an effective RL framework explicitly integrating visual sensitivity with token entropy via a principled multiplicative coupling, where VEPO redirects gradient credit toward tokens which are simultaneously visually grounded and highly informative. Extensive experiments demonstrate VEPO's leading performance, significantly outperforming the entropy-only baseline by 2.28 points at 7B-scale and 3.15 points at 3B-scale. Ablations further substantiate the soundness of our method.

</details>


### [259] [Imaginative Perception Tokens Enhance Spatial Reasoning in Multimodal Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03988)
*Mahtab Bigverdi,Lindsey Li,Weikai Huang,Yiming Liu,Jaemin Cho,Jieyu Zhang,Tuhin Kundu,Chris Dangjoo Kim,Zelun Luo,Linda Shapiro,Ranjay Krishna*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IPT introduces intermediate perceptual tokens that externalize imagined spatial observations, improving VLM spatial reasoning on unseen configurations beyond plain prompts; achieves accuracy gains and competitive performance with limited inference-time generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the deficiency of vision-language models in reasoning about unobserved spatial information (e.g., unseen viewpoints, occluded spaces). Propose a structured, externalizable internal representation to guide reasoning.

Method: Introduce Imaginative Perception Tokens (IPT) as intermediate perceptual representations aligned with observed input. Build three tasks (Perspective Taking, Path Tracing, Multiview Counting) and ~20k examples with ground-truth imaginations. Use the BAGEL VLM backbone; apply IPT supervision, compare with label-only and textual chain-of-thought (CoT) baselines; evaluate on MVC, PT, PET datasets.

Result: IPT supervision consistently improves spatial reasoning; often outperforms textual CoT training, even without generating images at inference. MVC accuracy up by 3.4%; competitive with strong closed-source models on Path Tracing. Combining IPT with label-only supervision yields further gains; textual CoT degrades performance, indicating a modality mismatch when spatial computation is forced through language.

Conclusion: IPT provides a principled supervision signal for reasoning about unobserved spatial structure, enhancing generalization while yielding interpretable intermediate representations and competitive performance across tasks.

Abstract: Vision language models (VLMs) excel at many tasks but still struggle with spatial reasoning when critical information is not directly observable. Many such problems require imaginative perception: inferring what would be seen from an unseen viewpoint, tracing paths through occluded spaces, or integrating partial observations into a coherent spatial representation. We introduce Imaginative Perception Tokens (IPT), intermediate perceptual representations that externalize what a VLM would perceive under alternative spatial configurations while remaining consistent with the observed input.
  To study this capability, we formulate three tasks, Perspective Taking (PET), Path Tracing (PT), and Multiview Counting (MVC), and construct datasets of approximately 20K examples with ground truth imaginations, answers, and evaluation benchmarks. Using the unified VLM BAGEL as the backbone, IPT supervision consistently improves spatial reasoning and often outperforms textual chain of thought training, even without generating images at inference time. On MVC, IPT improves accuracy by 3.4% and achieves competitive performance with strong closed-source models on PT. We further find that combining IPT and label-only supervision yields additional gains, whereas textual chain of thought can substantially degrade performance, suggesting a modality mismatch when spatial computation is forced through language. Overall, IPT provides a principled supervision signal for reasoning about unobserved spatial structure, improving generalization while producing interpretable intermediate representations.

</details>


### [260] [VESTA: Visual Exploration with Statistical Tool Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00384)
*William Rudman,Abhishek Divekar,Kanishk Jain,Sebastian Joseph,Stella S. R. Offner,Matthew Lease,Kyle Mahowald,Greg Durrett,Junyi Jessy Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VESTA is a framework that enhances agent-based modeling for statistical fitting by dynamically creating and leveraging diagnostic tools, enabling data-driven model refinement via transformations, visualizations, and tests. Evaluated on the DAWN benchmark, it outperforms baselines, especially on complex tasks, with dynamically generated tools being richer and more aligned with VLM reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automate fitting of quantitative models, addressing limitations of existing agent-based systems that rely on critique but lack rich, reusable diagnostic tools and active data exploration.

Method: Introduce VESTA to equip language-vision models with a growing toolkit of data transformations, hypothesis-driven visualizations, and statistical tests. Compare three toolkit settings (no tools, static expert tools, dynamic model-written tools) on the DAWN benchmark (distribution fitting, time series, real-world astronomy tasks).

Result: Dynamic tool creation within VESTA yields the strongest performance gains, particularly on complex and domain-specific tasks. Dynamically generated tools are more sophisticated than those from existing visual tool-creation systems and tend to produce visual outputs that the VLM can reason about.

Conclusion: Dynamic, model-generated tool creation significantly improves automated statistical modeling workflows, with evidence from the DAWN benchmark and astronomy applications, suggesting a path toward more capable autonomous data-analysis agents.

Abstract: Fitting quantitative models to data is a central step in scientific workflows, yet it remains one of the least automated. Recent agent-based systems leverage language and vision-language models (VLMs) to iteratively propose and refine statistical models, but these systems struggle on more challenging modeling tasks. To address these limitations, we introduce VESTA: Visual Exploration with Statistical Tool Agents, a framework that equips VLMs with a dynamically growing exploration toolkit to guide model refinement through data transformations, hypothesis-driven visualizations, and robust statistical tests. Unlike prior systems that rely on iterative critique alone, VESTA actively explores data before and during refinement by selecting or creating diagnostic tools, which accumulate in the model's context and can be reused later. We evaluate VESTA against established baselines in three toolkit configurations: no tools, static expert-written tools, and dynamic model-written tools. To support this evaluation, we introduce DAWN (Dataset for Automated Workflows and Numerical Modeling), a benchmark targeting distribution fitting and time series modeling with varying difficulty tiers, and culminating in real-world astronomy tasks including modeling initial mass functions and gravitational-wave chirp signals. We find that VESTA's dynamic tool creation outperforms prior agentic pipelines, with the largest gains on complex and domain-specific tasks. We further show that dynamically generated tools are substantially more sophisticated than those produced by existing visual tool-creation systems, covering more diagnostic categories per function and strongly preferring visual outputs that the VLM critic can reason over directly.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [261] [Human-in-the-Loop Contextual Bandits for Short-Term Rental Dynamic Pricing: Structural Equivalence of Historical Warm-Up and Approval-Gated Live Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02595)
*Oleg Miroshnichenko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HITL-GB integrates human approval into contextual bandits for dynamic STR pricing; uses historical deterministic-policy data to warm-start HF-TS, reducing cold-start; claims domain-agnostic applicability in high-stakes, regulated settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address high financial risk, need for explainability, and sparse feedback in STR pricing; enable data-efficient online learning with human oversight.

Method: Propose Human-in-the-Loop Gated Bandit (HITL-GB). Formalize approval-gated reward, derive regularised ridge-regression warm-up from historical episodes, initialise HF-TS posteriors; validate on anonymised urban STR data (April 2022–April 2026; 1461 episodes).

Result: Warm-up reduces effective cold-start from ~150 to ~30 episodes; empirical results on real production data; claim domain-agnostic equivalence; suggests regulatory oversight as asset.

Conclusion: Human oversight in high-stakes domains can facilitate safe, data-efficient online learning; the warm-up approach generalizes to other regulated domains (clinical dosing, credit, content moderation, radiology).

Abstract: Dynamic pricing in short-term rental (STR) markets presents a distinctive challenge for online learning algorithms: pricing decisions carry significant financial risk, operators require explainability, and market feedback is sparse (one booking outcome per listed night). We introduce the Human-in-the-Loop Gated Bandit (HITL-GB) framework, in which a contextual bandit algorithm generates price recommendations but a human agent retains authority to accept, modify, or reject each recommendation before it is applied. We show that under this approval constraint, historical pricing data -- collected under a prior deterministic policy -- is structurally equivalent to on-policy warm-up data for initialising the bandit's posterior, bypassing the weeks-to-months cold-start period that renders pure online bandit learning impractical in sparse-feedback markets. We formalise the approval-gated reward signal, derive a regularised ridge-regression warm-up procedure from historical episodes, and validate the approach on real STR production data (anonymised urban market, 2 rooms, April 2022 -- April 2026, 1,461 nightly pricing episodes). Our warm-up procedure compresses effective cold-start from ~150 episodes to ~30 episodes when initialising agents from the Hierarchical Factored Thompson Sampling (HF-TS) family. We further argue that the structural equivalence result is domain-agnostic: any high-stakes domain where human approval is legally or operationally required -- including clinical drug dosing, credit origination, content moderation, and radiological diagnosis -- satisfies the same conditions and benefits from the same warm-up strategy. In regulated industries, mandatory human oversight is thus a statistical asset rather than a deployment constraint.

</details>


### [262] [Spectral Asymptotics of Neural Network Loss Landscapes: An Exact Decomposition of the Curvature Exponent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02596)
*Anherutowa Calvo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The curvature exponent α, linking Hessian eigenvalues to gradient scales, varies systematically by layer type and is explained by the Spectral Alignment Decomposition α = 2 + d log Φ_k / d log σ_k. Φ_k captures spectral alignment between Kronecker-factored eigenbases and gradient directions. This yields a spectral transfer identity s = α γ tying curvature, gradient-rank decay, and Hessian decay. Empirically, α and γ computed from independent data recover s within ~2% median error across 93 layers, 5 architectures, 3 datasets; a zeta-function bound shows curvature concentrates on a single direction per layer. A proof-of-concept architecture-adaptive preconditioner T(σ; α) with Spectral Newton outperforms AdamW on vision tasks when α ≈ 2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why α varies across layer types and what this implies for optimization and representation geometry. Provide a geometric decomposition that links curvature properties to spectral alignment, enabling prediction and improved optimization via an adaptive preconditioner.

Method: Derive Spectral Alignment Decomposition: α = 2 + d log Φ_k / d log σ_k. Define Φ_k as the alignment between Kronecker-factor eigenbases and gradient singular directions. Prove that the curvature-exponent variation reduces to a geometric question (LayerNorm, residual connections, softmax heads). Establish the spectral transfer identity s = α γ and show its empirical content by fitting α and γ on independent data (HVPs and SVD) to predict s with ~2% median error across 93 layers, 5 architectures, 3 datasets with no free parameters. Prove a zeta-function bound on participation ratio to show concentration of curvature onto one direction per layer. Develop architecture-adaptive preconditioner T(σ; α) and implement Spectral Newton in the gradient singular basis; evaluate against AdamW on vision benchmarks where α ≈ 2.

Result: Convergent empirical pattern: α ≈ 2 for convolutions, α ≈ 1 for transformer attention, α < 1 for MLP up-projections. The s = α γ relation recovers Hessian decay exponent s from α and γ with median error ~2% across many layers/architectures/datasets. Curvature concentrates onto effectively a single direction per layer (zeta-bound). The architecture-adaptive preconditioner with Spectral Newton improves performance over AdamW on vision tasks when α is near 2.

Conclusion: The variation of α is a geometric consequence of gradient–Hessian alignment, encoded by Φ_k and its log-derivative, and the s = α γ transfer identity provides a unifying link between curvature and gradient decay. The results enable architecture-aware optimization through a principled preconditioner, with practical gains in vision benchmarks.

Abstract: The curvature exponent $α$ in $h_k \propto σ_k^α$ -- governing how Hessian eigenvalues scale with gradient singular values -- varies systematically across layer types ($α\approx 2$ for convolutions, $\approx 1$ for transformer attention, $< 1$ for MLP up-projections). Why? We prove the Spectral Alignment Decomposition: $α= 2 + d\logΦ_k / d\logσ_k$, where $Φ_k$ measures alignment between Kronecker factor eigenbases and gradient singular directions. This reduces "why does $α$ vary?" to a geometric question we answer for LayerNorm, residual connections, and softmax heads. The decomposition implies a spectral transfer identity $s = αγ$ linking curvature exponent, effective gradient rank-decay $γ$, and Hessian decay exponent $s$. The identity is algebraic; its empirical content is that $α$ and $γ$, fit on independent data (HVPs vs. SVD), recover $s$ to ~2% median error across 93 layers, five architectures, and three datasets -- with no free parameters. A zeta-function bound on participation ratio shows curvature concentrates onto effectively one direction per layer. As a proof of concept, we derive the architecture-adaptive preconditioner $T(σ;α)$ and show that Spectral Newton -- implementing $T$ in the gradient singular basis -- outperforms AdamW on vision benchmarks where $α\approx 2$.

</details>


### [263] [Making Brain-Computer Interfaces More Secure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02597)
*Md Fahimul Kabir Chowdhury,Gahangir Hossain*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A lightweight CNN improves adversarial robustness in EEG-based BCIs compared to three established EEG-specific CNNs under gradient-based attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Secure and robust EEG-based BCIs are needed due to susceptibility to adversarial perturbations that can cause misdiagnosis or incorrect decisions.

Method: Develop a lightweight custom CNN architecture and evaluate its robustness against gradient-based adversarial attacks on two EEG datasets, comparing against EEGNet, DeepConvNet, and SleepEEGNet.

Result: The proposed lightweight CNN maintains higher classification accuracy under adversarial perturbations than the baseline EEG-specific models across evaluated datasets and attacks, indicating enhanced robustness.

Conclusion: Lightweight architectures can improve the reliability of EEG-based BCIs under adversarial conditions, offering a potential design principle for secure BCI systems.

Abstract: The development of brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) based on electroencephalograms (EEGs) has advanced significantly mainly to machine learning. Although the majority of earlier research has been on increasing classification accuracy, relatively little focus has been placed on security and robustness. According to recent research, EEG-based BCIs are susceptible to adversarial attacks, which can cause misdiagnosis due to minute, well-crafted disturbances. Evaluating model robustness against such perturbations is therefore critical for ensuring reliable deployment. In this study, we propose a lightweight custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture to investigate adversarial robustness in EEG-based BCIs. The suggested method is assessed using two EEG datasets and contrasted with three novel CNN models tailored to EEG, namely EEGNet, DeepConvNet, and SleepEEGNet, under gradient-based adversarial attack scenarios. According to experimental findings, the suggested model continuously performs better in classification under adversarial perturbations compared to baseline models, indicating improved robustness. These findings highlight the potential of lightweight architectures for enhancing the reliability of EEG-based BCI systems under adversarial conditions.

</details>


### [264] [Assessing Region-Level EEG Contributions to Cognitive Workload Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02598)
*Jacob Wong,Sohan Singh,Prannaya Gupta,Jin Xing Ang,Kritika Johari,U-Xuan Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Region-level EEG-based workload prediction shows frontal/fronto-central regions provide robust, generalizable cues across tasks and subjects, outperforming full-scalp baselines in subject-independent settings while using fewer electrodes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how consistently region-level EEG contributions support workload prediction across different tasks, datasets, and subjects, enabling efficient and generalizable monitoring systems.

Method: Train and evaluate workload prediction models using features restricted to anatomically defined scalp regions. Conduct a large-scale analysis across four public EEG workload datasets with varied tasks, hardware, and electrode montages. Use a model-agnostic, performance-based approach to estimate region importance under mixed-subject and subject-independent protocols, with results aggregated via rank-based statistics.

Result: Frontal electrode groups yield ~15-20% improvement in relative rank position over full-scalp baselines in subject-independent evaluations, while using substantially fewer electrodes. Fronto-central regions show the most stable predictive utility; posterior and occipital regions are less consistent across conditions.

Conclusion: Workload-relevant EEG information is most consistently encoded in frontal and fronto-central regions, supporting development of efficient, generalizable EEG-based workload monitoring systems with reduced electrode counts.

Abstract: Accurate and generalizable estimation of cognitive workload from electroencephalography (EEG) is critical for human-centered and safety-critical systems. Although EEG is widely used for workload assessment, the consistency of region-level EEG contributions across tasks, datasets, and subjects remains unclear. This paper presents a region-level evaluation framework for EEG-based workload prediction in which models are trained and evaluated using features extracted exclusively from electrodes belonging to anatomically defined scalp regions. We perform a large-scale analysis across four publicly available EEG workload datasets spanning diverse task demands, recording hardware, and electrode montages. Region importance is quantified using a model-agnostic, performance-based approach under both mixed-subject and subject-independent evaluation protocols, with results aggregated using a rank-based strategy to ensure robustness across experimental configurations. Across all datasets and subject-independent evaluations, frontal electrode groups outperform the full-scalp baseline by approximately 15-20% in relative rank position while using substantially fewer electrodes. Fronto-central regions exhibit the most stable predictive utility, whereas posterior and occipital regions contribute less consistently across experimental conditions. These findings indicate that workload-relevant EEG information is most consistently retained within frontal and fronto-central electrode groups, supporting the design of efficient and generalizable EEG-based workload monitoring systems.

</details>


### [265] [Testing the Test: Score-Direction Instability in Class-Split Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02601)
*Alejandro Ascarate,Leo Lebrat,Rodrigo Santa Cruz,Clinton Fookes,Olivier Salvado*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Within-dataset splits for anomaly detection can be unreliable when the held-out anomaly overlaps the normal distribution in representation space; a training-free diagnostic (neighborhood class leakage) predicts score-direction instability across datasets and spaces; benchmarks should be viewed as geometry-dependent stress tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify the reliability of class-split anomaly detection benchmarks and reveal geometry-induced instabilities in anomaly scores.

Method: Propose and validate a training-free diagnostic, neighborhood class leakage; evaluate score-direction stability on Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, Imagenette; test in both pixel space and VAE latent space.

Result: Observed score-direction instability; possible collapse to chance or inversion; leakage diagnostic predicts instability across datasets and spaces.

Conclusion: Class-split AD benchmarks are geometry-dependent and should be treated as stress tests, not unconditional evidence of anomaly-detection ability.

Abstract: Within-dataset class-split evaluation is widely used as a proxy for fully unconditional out-of-distribution anomaly detection. We show that this protocol can become ill-posed when the held-out anomaly class overlaps the normal mixture in representation space. In this regime, anomaly scores may collapse toward chance or even invert, and the preferred score direction can depend on the unknown anomaly class. We introduce a simple training-free diagnostic, neighborhood class leakage, and show that it predicts score-direction instability across Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and Imagenette, in both pixel and VAE latent spaces. Our results suggest that class-split AD benchmarks should be treated as geometry-dependent stress tests rather than unconditional evidence of anomaly-detection ability.

</details>


### [266] [Graph Mamba Survival Analysis Based on Topology-Aware ordering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02602)
*Yuanfang Chen,Peiqiang Yan,Yuntao Shou,Qian Zhao,Xiangyong Cao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TopoMamSurv: a topology-aware ordering framework for Graph Mamba in WSI survival analysis, integrating a bidirectional Mamba module and GCN for bidirectional spatial context, achieving hierarchical local-to-global feature learning and improved performance on five TCGA datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers incur O(N^2) complexity on large WSIs; Mamba lowers computation but is highly sensitive to input order and lacks bidirectional spatial modeling. A topology-aware ordering is needed to preserve topological connectivity and enable effective sequential processing; a bidirectional fusion with GCN can capture spatial relations.

Method: Introduce topology-aware ordering (TAO) to guide Graph Mamba's sequential processing (TopoMamSurv). Add a bidirectional Mamba module and integrate a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) to model bidirectional spatial context. Employ hierarchical feature learning: local aggregation via graph operations, global capture via Mamba. Validate on five TCGA WSIs datasets, with visualization confirming higher node similarity under TAO.

Result: TAO-extracted nodes show higher similarity; the framework demonstrates comprehensive performance advantages over baselines on five TCGA datasets, indicating better survival analysis performance with efficient computation and improved spatial modeling.

Conclusion: Topology-aware ordering enables Mamba to leverage topological structure and bidirectional spatial cues while maintaining linear-time complexity. The hierarchical fusion of TAO, bidirectional Mamba, and GCN reconciles long-range dependency modeling, computational efficiency, and spatial structure utilization, yielding robust performance in WSIs survival analysis.

Abstract: In computational pathology, Whole Slide Images (WSIs) survival analysis is crucial for patient prognosis assessment, but it faces multiple technical challenges. Although the Transformer captures long-range dependencies through its self-attention mechanism, its $O(N^2)$ time complexity causes a severe computational bottleneck in large-scale WSIs graph structures. The Mamba model breaks through the Transformer's computational bottleneck with linear complexity. But, owing to Mamba's high sensitivity to the order of input data, traditional node sorting methods in Graph Mamba, such as those based on node degree or subgraph size, fail to adequately account for the topological connectivity of graph data. This inadequacy consequently restricts the performance of Mamba's sequential modeling. Moreover, its unidirectional architecture cannot leverage the bidirectional spatial structure of images. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel Graph Mamba survival analysis framework based on topology-aware ordering (TopoMamSurv) to adapt to the sequential sensitivity of Mamba. Our visualization experiments further confirmed that the nodes extracted through the topology-aware ordering (TAO) strategy indeed exhibit higher similarity. Furthermore, we designed a bidirectional Mamba module and integrated a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) to achieve bidirectional spatial context modeling of images, forming a hierarchical feature learning architecture for "local aggregation - global capture." This framework effectively reconciles the contradiction between long-range dependency modeling, computational efficiency, and spatial structure utilization in WSIs analysis through its systematic design of TAO, bidirectional semantic modeling, and hierarchical feature fusion. This framework has been validated for its comprehensive performance advantage on five TCGA datasets.

</details>


### [267] [Auditable Climate Risk Intelligence from Fragmented ESG Data: Deterministic Orchestration and Imbalance-Aware Learning for Scope 1-3 Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02604)
*Karan Sehgal,Khawar Naveed Bhatti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a deterministic, auditable ESG validation framework with provenance-aware orchestration, drift detection, and anomaly handling, plus a synthetic benchmark for reproducibility; evaluated with multiple metrics and cross-validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ESG/climate data are fragmented across Scope 1-3; current validation pipelines lack provenance, drift detection, reproducibility governance; need auditable, transparent validation infrastructure.

Method: Deterministic climate risk intelligence framework: single source of truth orchestration; temporal anomaly detection; SMOTE-based rare event optimization; ensemble learning; TreeSHAP interpretability; provenance-aware governance; synthetic ESG validation benchmark calibrated to GHG Protocol, PCAF, ISSB; temporal drift analysis; calibration; evaluation against classifiers, detectors, and forecasting baselines; cross-validation with paired significance testing.

Result: Performance measured using recall, F1, ROC AUC, ECE, Brier score; governance audit trace completeness metric; results averaged across stratified five-fold CV with paired significance tests.

Conclusion: Framework anchors ESG reporting in deterministic governance infrastructure, enhancing reproducibility, explainability, and auditability for climate risk validation.

Abstract: ESG and climate risk data remain fragmented across heterogeneous Scope 1, Scope 2, and Scope 3 reporting environments, while conventional validation pipelines lack provenance aware auditability, hidden drift detection, and reproducibility oriented governance. This paper proposes a deterministic climate risk intelligence framework integrating single source of truth orchestration, temporal anomaly detection, imbalance aware ensemble learning, and explainability oriented governance for auditable ESG validation. To support open reproducibility, we construct and release a synthetic ESG validation benchmark calibrated against publicly reported characteristics of the GHG Protocol, PCAF, and ISSB standards. The methodology incorporates temporal drift analysis, SMOTE based rare event optimization, ensemble learning, provenance aware orchestration, and TreeSHAP based interpretability for governance inspection and audit reconstruction. We evaluate the framework against statistical classifiers, anomaly detection methods, temporal forecasting baselines, and a threshold based system using classification metrics (recall, F1, ROC AUC), calibration metrics (ECE, Brier score), and a governance oriented audit trace completeness metric measuring the fraction of flagged anomalies for which a deterministic source to escalation provenance chain can be reconstructed. Results are reported as mean and standard deviation across stratified five fold cross validation with paired significance testing. The framework reframes ESG reporting toward deterministic climate risk governance infrastructure supporting reproducibility, explainability, and operational auditability.

</details>


### [268] [Cross-Modal Contrastive Learning of ECG and Angiography Representations for Severe Stenosis Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02605)
*Nikola Cenikj,Özgün Turgut,Alexander Müller,Alexander Steger,Jan Kehrer,Marcus Brugger,Daniel Rueckert,Philip Müller*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: StenCE pretraining framework uses ECG-derived representations to stratify stenosis risk, enabling non-invasive detection from ECGs and improving performance across severities and tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ECGs are fast, cheap, and routinely collected; invasive coronary angiography is the standard but carries risk and resource costs. Many patients, including asymptomatic ones, may be undiagnosed. No reliable stenosis-specific ECG signal exists, limiting ECG-based risk stratification. A pretraining approach could transfer information from general ECG tasks to stenosis detection.

Method: Propose StenCE, a pretraining framework that learns ECG representations enabling patient stratification for stenosis from ECG data. Evaluated across varying stenosis severity thresholds and additional ECG disease classification tasks, using multiple ECG encoders to demonstrate transfer and robustness.

Result: StenCE yields consistent performance gains across different encoders and outperforms prior work. It enables detection signals for stenosis diagnosis in ECGs and achieves high performance in severe stenosis classification.

Conclusion: ECG-based stenosis risk stratification is viable with StenCE, enabling earlier, non-invasive screening. The approach generalizes across encoders and severities, and the authors release source code for replication.

Abstract: Coronary artery stenosis is a common cardiovascular disease, with severe, untreated cases posing significant risks of heart attack. Although coronary (X-ray) angiograms remain the standard for stenosis diagnosis, they are invasive, time- and resource-intensive, and therefore only performed on patients with a high probability of disease based on symptoms and prior clinical tests. However, a subset of patients, especially those without symptoms, may remain undiagnosed. Detecting indications of stenosis from ECGs, which are fast, cheap, non-invasive, and thus routinely acquired even in asymptomatic patients, would support early diagnosis. However, as no reliable stenosis-specific signal has been identified in ECGs, they can not currently be used for stenosis risk stratification. To address this, we introduce StenCE, a pretraining framework, allowing stratification of patients based on features derived directly from ECGs. Evaluations across varying stenosis severity thresholds and additional ECG disease classification tasks demonstrate consistent performance improvements across different ECG encoders, outperforming previous work. The obtained models successfully detect signals for stenosis diagnosis in ECGs and are the first to achieve high performance in severe stenosis classification. The source code is available at https://github.com/NikolaCenic/ecg-stenosis-cls.

</details>


### [269] [ReLoRA: Knowledge-Reusing Adaptation for Fast Rollout of Evolving LLM Services](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02606)
*Yang Xu,Zihuai Xu,Hongli Xu,Yunming Liao,Zhiwei Yao,Xitong Fu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReLoRA offers a knowledge-reusing re-adaptation framework to efficiently restore service-ready LoRA adapters for evolving LLM services, achieving faster readiness and better accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Frequent base-model updates can invalidate task-specific LoRA adapters. Retraining each adapter from scratch is costly, and naive transfer harms performance due to adapter-backbone mismatch. A compatibility-aware, efficient re-adaptation method is needed.

Method: Two-step optimization: (1) Adaptive LoRA initialization via Bayesian optimization that fuses information from the previously deployed adapter and base-model evolution to obtain a compatibility-aware starting point. (2) Fine-tuning with scheduled regularization: strong regularization initially to rapidly enter a good region, followed by relaxed regularization for task-specific refinement.

Result: Experiments show up to 8.9x reduction in time-to-readiness and up to 4.6% improvements in accuracy compared with baselines.

Conclusion: ReLoRA enables rapid service-quality recovery for evolving LLM services with reduced re-adaptation overhead while preserving or improving task performance.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as continuously evolving services, where frequent base-model updates may invalidate previously deployed task-specific Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapters. For service providers managing numerous downstream model services, retraining each LoRA adapter from scratch for every updated base model is computationally prohibitive and delays service rollout. Meanwhile, the simpler alternative, i.e., naively applying the original LoRA adapter to the updated base model, often leads to degraded service quality due to adapter-backbone incompatibility. To address this problem, we propose ReLoRA, a knowledge-reusing re-adaptation framework that efficiently restores service-ready LoRA adapters for evolving LLM services while preserving or improving task performance. Specifically, ReLoRA comprises two key optimization steps: 1) Adaptive LoRA initialization leverages Bayesian optimization to construct a compatibility-aware starting point by fusing information from both the previously deployed task adapter and the base model's evolution; 2) Fine-tuning with scheduled regularization first rapidly steers the adapter to a high-quality region via strong regularization, followed by relaxed regularization for task-specific refinement. This design enables rapid service-quality recovery with reduced re-adaptation overhead. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ReLoRA reduces time-to-readiness by up to 8.9$\times$ and improves accuracy by up to 4.6\% compared to baselines.

</details>


### [270] [Geometry-Aware Tabular Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02607)
*David Turtora Zagardo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Geometry-Aware Tabular Diffusion (GATD) injects pairwise geometric relations (angles and lengths derived from column value differences) into tabular diffusion denoisers, yielding state-of-the-art performance with substantially fewer parameters across multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models for tabular data struggle to explicitly model inter-column relationships; explicit geometric supervision provides a portable inductive bias to improve data utility.

Method: Compute pairwise angles and lengths from column value differences; use them as additional inputs and as auxiliary targets to denoisers. Implement with an MLP that achieves state-of-the-art with 3.5x fewer parameters (up to 25x on classification). Transfer default loss weights to GNN and Transformer denoisers. Conduct ablations showing supervision (not extra inputs or capacity) drives gains.

Result: On ten datasets, GATD-based models win 8/10 in Shape, 7/10 in Trend, and 9/10 in downstream utility (F1/RMSE). Shape and Trend errors reduced by 27% and 20%. Average parameter count reduced by 3.5x (up to 25x for classification). Loss-weight transfer improves Shape on 27/30 and Trend on 25/30 architecture-dataset cells. Ablation confirms supervision is key to the gains.

Conclusion: Explicit relational supervision is a portable inductive bias for tabular diffusion, enabling efficient, architecture-agnostic improvements in tabular data synthesis and downstream tasks.

Abstract: Tabular synthesis is critical for privacy-preserving sharing and augmentation, yet diffusion models rely on implicit mechanisms to capture inter-column relationships. We introduce Geometry-Aware Tabular Diffusion (GATD), which augments tabular diffusion denoisers with pairwise angles and lengths computed from column value differences and used as inputs and auxiliary targets. Our MLP instantiation achieves state-of-the-art benchmark performance while using 3.5x fewer parameters on average (up to 25x for classification tasks): on ten datasets, it wins 8/10 Shape, 7/10 Trend, and 9/10 downstream utility (F1/RMSE), reducing Shape and Trend error by 27% and 20%. Default loss weights transfer to GNN and Transformer denoisers, improving Shape on 27/30 and Trend on 25/30 architecture-dataset cells. A matched ablation shows supervision (not extra inputs or capacity) drives the gain. This shows explicit relational supervision is a portable inductive bias for tabular diffusion.

</details>


### [271] [Pruning Deep Neural Networks via the Marchenko--Pastur Distribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02608)
*Leonid Berlyand,Theo Bourdais,Houman Owhad,Yitzchak Shmalo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MP-random-matrix theory enables pruning with minimal fine-tuning budgets, providing data-path certificates and strong accuracy retention across ViT and CNN on ImageNet-1k, with notable sparsity and speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce post-pruning training and calibration budgets while delivering reliable pruning; derive deterministic certificates linking removed components to margins and logits.

Method: Employ a Marchenko–Pastur random-matrix model to characterize pruning; define removed component R with small propagated logit effect L_s ||R ψ_1(s)||_∞; show pruning reduces an elastic-net objective; zero-budget pruning; prune–restore extension; additive L2-regularized model; analyze MP edge σ_+ under iid-Gaussian; apply to ViT and CNN with distillation; report structured sparsity and permutation patterns; measure sparse-execution MAC and backend speedups.

Result: Zero-budget pruning yields perfect pruning; on ImageNet-1k, with three distillation epochs, ViT-B/16 2:4+ ToMe achieves 83.41% top-1 on 59.81% sparse-execution MAC, with 1.388× best-observed A40 native-2:4 speedup; separate A100 endpoint yields 2.705×. Structured sparsity results: ViT-B/16 6:12 -> 83.74%; ViT-L/16 8:16 dense+permutation -> 85.33%; ConvNeXtV2-Base 12:16 -> 86.35%; CNNs: ResNet50 8:16 -> 75.87%; ResNet152d CAST-conv+permutation -> 81.33% (≈50% MAC, 1.62× A40 im2col+2:4 sparse-GEMM).

Conclusion: MP-based pruning can preserve accuracy under very tight budgets, with deterministic data-path certificates and broad applicability across vision transformers and CNNs; results suggest practical viability for sparse deployment and speedups, though depend on model type, pruning patterns, and training setup.

Abstract: We study a Marchenko--Pastur (MP) random-matrix approach to pruning deep neural networks with very small post-pruning fine-tuning budgets. The main practical contribution is accuracy retention under short calibration and fine-tuning schedules, rather than a long post-pruning reoptimization pipeline. The theory gives deterministic data-path certificates: if the removed component $R$ has small propagated logit effect $L_s \| R ψ_1(s) \|_\infty$, pruning decreases an elastic-net objective and preserves samples whose dense margin exceeds twice the perturbation. The zero-budget case gives perfect pruning; a prune--restore extension models weight restoration inside a fixed sparse-execution pattern; and an additive $L_2$-regularized model shows admissible random-like components vanish at the training limit, with persistent spikes stabilizing as the MP bulk collapses. Under iid-Gaussian sufficient conditions, the fitted MP edge $σ_+$ gives a high-probability layerwise budget signal.
  On ImageNet-1k, after only three distillation epochs, ViT-B/16 $2{:}4{+}$ToMe reaches $83.41\%$ top-1 ($-1.70$ pp from dense) at $59.81\%$ sparse-execution MAC reduction, with $1.388\times$ best-observed A40 native-$2{:}4$ backend speedup for the same checkpoint and ToMe graph; a separate no-ToMe A100 endpoint gives $2.705\times$. At structured sparsity, ViT-B/16 $6{:}12$ reaches $83.74\%$, ViT-L/16 $8{:}16$ dense+permutation reaches $85.33\%$ ($-0.51$ pp), and ConvNeXtV2-Base $12{:}16$ reaches $86.35\%$ ($-0.37$ pp). For CNNs, ResNet50 $8{:}16$ dense+permutation reaches $75.87\%$ ($-0.26$ pp), and ResNet152d CAST-conv+permutation reaches $81.33\%$ ($-1.53$ pp) at ${\sim}50\%$ MAC accounting with a $1.62\times$ A40 im2col$+2{:}4$ sparse-GEMM audit.

</details>


### [272] [Building Better Activation Oracles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02609)
*Jan Bauer,Celeste De Schamphelaere,Adam Karvonen,Niclas Luick,Neel Nanda*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Enhances Activation Oracles via four training improvements and releases AObench; modest capability gains but substantial QoL improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address hallucinations, vagueness, and evaluation confounds in residual-activation interpretability methods to advance scalable end-to-end interpretability.

Method: Four training enhancements: on-policy rollouts, improved conversational dataset, more layers, and injection-formula tweak; plus open-source AObench evaluation suite for AO quality.

Result: Capability gains are marginal; quality-of-life improvements are substantial; AObench is the first comprehensive AO evaluation suite.

Conclusion: Foundational step toward improved AOs and scalable end-to-end interpretability; open-source evaluation framework will facilitate future improvements.

Abstract: Activation Oracles (AOs) are promising methods for interpreting residual stream activations. However, current AOs face important issues, such as hallucinations and vagueness. Additionally, text-inversion confounds make them hard to evaluate. To this end, we improve the Activation Oracle (AO) training regime in four ways: training on on-policy rollouts, improving the conversational dataset, feeding more layers and an improvement to the injection formula. The capability improvements are marginal, but quality of life improvements are quite substantial. In addition, we open source the first comprehensive evaluation suite for AO quality, which we call AObench. Overall, we hope that our work sets a foundation that helps improve AOs and other models in the paradigm of scalable, end-to-end interpretability.

</details>


### [273] [Hallucination Is Linearly Decodable from Mid-Layer Hidden States in Quantized LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02628)
*Aizierjiang Aiersilan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A linearly separable truthfulness signal exists in hidden states of open-source LLMs and is strongest in mid-layers; linear probes outperform sampling detectors; peak layers are model-specific but within middle blocks; first-block attention entropy provides complementary signal in knowledge-grounded tasks; code/data released for reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether truthfulness is encoded as a linearly separable signal in hidden representations of open-source LLMs, identify the network depth where this signal is strongest, and compare detection methods across multiple models and benchmarks.

Method: Per-layer hidden states were extracted from four 7–8B instruction-tuned models (Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B, Qwen2.5-7B) quantized to 4-bit NF4. On four hallucination benchmarks (TruthfulQA, HaluEval-QA, FEVER, and a synthetic set), four detection approaches were evaluated: linear probes, MLP probes, INSIDE EigenScore, self-consistency, and attention entropy. Probes were trained on held-out splits to assess AUROC as a measure of truthfulness signal detectability across layers.

Result: A linear probe on a single mid-network layer achieved 0.904–1.000 AUROC on held-out splits, while sampling-based detectors remained <=0.541 AUROC under the same protocol. MLP probes rarely beat linear probes by more than 0.01 AUROC. Peak probing layers occurred consistently in blocks 13–18 of 32 for Llama and Mistral, and blocks 19–25 of 28 for Qwen. First-block attention entropy provided a complementary signal (0.866–0.941 AUROC on HaluEval-QA) without extra inference cost. The low discriminability of sampling methods is attributed to a protocol mismatch rather than a limitation of the methods. Code and data are released for reproducibility on a single 8 GB GPU.

Conclusion: Truthfulness signals in hidden states are approximately linear and concentrated in mid-layers across model families, allowing simple linear probes to detect them effectively. This supports the view that truthfulness can be extracted with low-complexity linear readouts, while certain non-probe detectors may underperform in this evaluation setup. The findings are robust across models/benchmarks and include a complementary attention-entropy signal for knowledge-grounded settings.

Abstract: We investigate whether open-source LLMs encode a linearly separable truthfulness signal in their hidden states, and at which network depth this signal is strongest. Across three $7$B--$8$B instruction-tuned models (Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B, Qwen2.5-7B) loaded in $4$-bit NF4 quantization, we extract per-layer hidden states on four hallucination benchmarks (TruthfulQA, HaluEval-QA, FEVER, and a controlled synthetic set) and compare four detection approaches: linear and MLP probes, INSIDE EigenScore, self-consistency, and attention entropy. A linear probe on a single mid-network layer achieves $0.904$--$1.000$ AUROC on held-out splits, while sampling-based detectors do not exceed $0.541$ AUROC under the same protocol. The truthfulness signal is approximately linear: MLP probes rarely surpass linear probes by more than $0.01$ AUROC. Peak probing layers fall in a consistent band across model families on natural-language benchmarks -- blocks~$13$--$18$ of~$32$ for Llama and Mistral, and blocks~$19$--$25$ of~$28$ for Qwen. First-block attention entropy provides a complementary signal in knowledge-grounded settings ($0.866$--$0.941$ AUROC on HaluEval-QA) at no additional inference cost. The low discriminability of sampling methods under this protocol reflects a structural mismatch between paired-label evaluation and the information these methods access, rather than an inherent limitation of those methods. Code and data are released for full reproducibility on a single $8$\,GB GPU.

</details>


### [274] [Regime-Arrival Uncertainty in Generalization Bounds under Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02657)
*Prince Poudel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A regime-aware generalization framework for Markov-switching distributions: exact decomposition of regime mismatch and sensitivity, extension to beta-mixing data via spectral-gap-corrected effective sample size, and minimax lower bounds; introduces an ex post realized generalization gap as a penalty; empirical validation on synthetic data and 25 years of global equity indices; not a forecast tool, with forecasting future regime composition remaining open.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard generalization bounds assume static or well-aligned train/test distributions. In regime-switching environments, the composition of regimes (calm vs crisis) can differ between training and deployment, creating extra risk. The work seeks to quantify and bound this regime-mismatch risk under Markov-switching and dependent data.

Method: 1) Derive an exact decomposition of generalization error into regime-mismatch and regime-sensitivity components. 2) Extend the bound to beta-mixing processes by using an effective sample size that accounts for the spectral gap. 3) Establish minimax lower bounds for synthetic data and for 25 years of global equity indices. 4) Propose an ex post realized generalization gap as a training-free penalty; compare against a training-only estimator. 5) Empirically validate and illustrate that crisis-related feature geometry can be detected, but temporal arrival cannot be reliably forecasted.

Result: An exact decomposition of risk due to regime composition mismatch is obtained. The generalization bound is extended to beta-mixing data with a spectral-gap-corrected effective sample size. Minimax lower bounds are established on synthetic data and on long-span equity data. The proposed penalty (ex post realized generalization gap) correlates with true generalization gaps, while a training-only estimator does not capture temporal arrival. The framework provides a principled non-forecasting penalty for regime-mismatch risk and highlights limitations in forecasting future regime composition.

Conclusion: The framework offers a principled way to account for regime-mismatch risk in generalization under Markov-switching, but it is not a forecasting tool. Forecasting future regime composition remains an open question, especially in rare regime-change instances.

Abstract: The standard generalization bounds assume that the training and deployment distributions are the same, or are static, and don't consider regime switching environments where the ratio of calm vs crisis states is different. This paper proposes a framework that generalizes regime-aware models by quantifying the extra risk due to regime composition mismatch, when distribution shifts are Markov-switching. We obtain an exact decomposition, separating regime mismatch from regime sensitivity; we extend the bound to beta-mixing data using the effective sample size corrected for the spectral gap; and we show a minimax lower bound for synthetic data and on 25 years of global equity indices. The proposed penalty is an ex post realized generalization gap, whereas the training-only estimator does not show significant correlation: the feature geometry of crises can be detected, but not the temporal arrival. Thus, the framework is not a forecast machine. Forecasting the composition of the future regime is an open question in the rare cases of regime change.

</details>


### [275] [CL-DMDF:Dynamic Multimodal Data Fusion Model Based on Contrastive Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02659)
*Dong Li,Lingling Zhang,Binghao Han,Linlin Ding,Yue Kou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Dynamic Multimodal Data Fusion model with contrastive learning (CL-DMDF) uses cross-feature and cross-modality attention, an entity-centroid contrastive module, and adaptive fusion to Robustly fuse multimodal inputs with missing modalities, validated on three datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal fusion methods assume fully observed modalities and often focus on local interactions, underutilizing global complementary cues. Real-world data frequently have missing modalities, necessitating robust, dynamic fusion that leverages cross-modal information.

Method: Introduce CL-DMDF with (1) an attention mechanism operating over feature and modality dimensions to produce reliable attention scores; (2) an entity-centroid contrastive learning module that forms centroid-based positives from entity features to enhance discriminative power; (3) an adaptive fusion module to improve efficiency and accuracy of dynamic fusion under missing modalities.

Result: Extensive experiments on three datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of CL-DMDF across diverse multimodal fusion tasks.

Conclusion: CL-DMDF improves handling of missing modalities by leveraging global cross-level cues via a novel attention mechanism and centroid-based contrastive learning, with an adaptive fusion component boosting efficiency; validated on multiple datasets.

Abstract: Multimodal data fusion involves integrating and analyzing information from multiple modalities to uncover latent correlations and complementary patterns, thereby enhancing data processing and decision-making. While existing methods for structured multimodal inputs are typically designed around specific tasks and assume fully observed modalities, real-world applications often suffer from uncertain or missing modality inputs due to various factors. Some traditional models overly emphasize local interactions within missing modalities, neglecting the global complementary cues embedded in multimodal representations. To overcome these limitations, we propose a Dynamic Multimodal Data Fusion model based on Contrastive Learning (CL-DMDF). CL-DMDF introduces a novel attention mechanism that operates across both feature and modality dimensions to compute reliable attention scores, effectively reflecting importance at each level. The CL-DMDF further incorporates an entity-centroid contrastive learning module that constructs centroid-based positive samples from entity features to enhance discriminative learning. Additionally, an adaptive fusion module is employed to improve the efficiency and accuracy of dynamic fusion strategies. Extensive experiments conducted on three datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the CL-DMDF across diverse multimodal fusion tasks.

</details>


### [276] [Improvise, Adapt, Overcome: An On-The-Fly Multifidelity Algorithm for Efficient Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02662)
*Vivin Vinod,Peter Zaspel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An adaptive on-the-fly multifidelity ML (adaptive-MFML) framework that autonomously selects training data across fidelities to saturate accuracy at low-cost levels, reducing data costs substantially relative to single-fidelity and standard MFML approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High cost of high-fidelity quantum-chemical training data; standard MFML uses fixed fidelity ratios and causes data redundancy; need an adaptive, data-efficient approach.

Method: Introduce an adaptive on-the-fly MFML algorithm that queries samples at each fidelity during training, saturating accuracy at cheaper fidelities before ascending to expensive reference calculations; evaluated on CC energies and excitation energies.

Result: Data generation costs reduced up to 30× compared to single-fidelity methods; up to 5× improvement over standard MFML; effective across diverse chemical properties, including CC energies and excitations; reduced data redundancy.

Conclusion: Adaptive MFML enables sustainable, cost-aware ML in quantum chemistry by achieving high-accuracy models with substantially less high-fidelity data.

Abstract: Machine learning has accelerated quantum chemistry but is hindered by the prohibitive cost of generating high fidelity training data. Multifidelity machine learning (MFML) mitigates this overhead by systematically combining abundant low fidelity data with sparse high fidelity data. In spite of its success, standard MFML schemes rely on pre-defined scaling factors to determine sparse data ratio across fidelities, often generating redundant multifidelity data resulting in a loss of efficiency. Here, we introduce an adaptive on-the-fly multifidelity framework for machine learning that autonomously determines training dataset composition. By dynamically querying training samples at each fidelity, the algorithm saturates model accuracy at lower fidelities before moving up to more expensive reference calculations. We benchmark the novel adaptive-MFML across diverse chemical properties including the computational chemistry gold standard coupled cluster energies, and the more chemically challenging excitation energies. In our numerical experiments we show that our adaptive algorithm reduces data generation costs by up to a factor of 30 compared to single fidelity methods and improves upon standard MFML by up to a factor of 5. The mitigation of data redundancy establishes a high-accuracy low-cost pathway for sustainable cost-aware machine learning in quantum chemistry.

</details>


### [277] [AdaWeather: Adaptively Mixing Probabilistic Weather Forecasts with Logarithmic Regret](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02663)
*Saptarishi Dhanuka,Sarvesh Iyer,Manmeet Singh,Mihir More,Rushil Gupta,Dhruman Gupta,Parthasarathi Mukhopadhyay,Sandeep Juneja*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AdaWeather blends a large set of probabilistic forecasts via machine learning and mixture-of-experts to produce a unified probabilistic forecast, with a logarithmic regret bound relative to the best static mixture in hindsight and empirical improvements in temperature forecasting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forecast accuracy varies across contexts and no single model consistently dominates; adaptive, robust forecast combining is needed.

Method: An adaptive framework that combines multiple probabilistic forecasts using machine learning and mixture-of-experts techniques; extends regret analysis to compare against the best static mixture in hindsight.

Result: The approach achieves improved probabilistic temperature forecasts and provides a theoretical guarantee: logarithmic regret relative to the best static mixture of experts in hindsight.

Conclusion: Adaptive forecast fusion via AdaWeather is effective for probabilistic weather prediction, offering both theoretical guarantees and empirical gains; future work could extend to other variables and settings.

Abstract: Recent advances in machine learning have produced probabilistic weather forecasting models comparable to state-of-the-art numerical weather predictors. But no model consistently dominates spatio-temporally, and relative performance is highly context-dependent. This motivates adaptive methods for combining multiple forecasts to obtain improvements and robustness. While combined forecasts have been proposed in the literature, these are achieved either through supervised learning or through prediction with expert advice methods. We introduce AdaWeather, an adaptive framework that combines many probabilistic forecasts using both machine learning as well as mixture of experts to arrive at a unified improved probabilistic forecast. While traditional expert methods develop the regret bounds with respect to the best single expert in hindsight, we extend the algorithm and analysis to show our method has logarithmic regret compared to the best static mixture of experts in hindsight. Empirically, we focus on forecasting temperature, and observe improvements over existing methods.

</details>


### [278] [Anomalies in Multivariate Time Series Benchmarks Are Mostly Univariate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02670)
*Marc Pinet,Julien Cumin,Samuel Berlemont,Dominique Vaufreydaz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cross-channel modeling in multivariate time series anomaly detection (MT-SAD) often yields little to no benefit on standard benchmarks; most labeled anomalies are univariate. The study questions the value of cross-channel approaches and calls for more structurally diverse benchmarks; code is released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test the common assumption that anomalies manifest via cross-channel dependencies in MT-SAD and to assess whether cross-channel modeling provides practical gains on public benchmarks.

Method: Introduce a per-segment diagnostic framework that classifies each labeled anomaly as univariate deviation, cross-channel correlation change, or both. Apply this to eight public MT-SAD benchmarks. Supplement with synthetic data of phase-shifted sinusoidal channels with shared noise to create channelwise corruptions that preserve marginal distributions but break cross-channel structure. Compare channel-dependent (CD) and channel-independent (CI) models, and evaluate a state-of-the-art detector on real benchmarks. Provide public code.

Result: Cross-channel rupture (i.e., cross-channel structural changes) does not occur without a concomitant univariate deviation in the analyzed segments. On six of eight benchmarks, at least half of labeled anomaly segments show univariate deviation across 79%–100% of timesteps, with 100% on three datasets. Synthetic data show CD models leverage cross-channel signals, while CI models fail. On real benchmarks, CD modeling offers no measurable gains over CI. 

Conclusion: Current MTSAD benchmarks are insufficient for validating cross-channel modeling capabilities. There is a need for more structurally diverse evaluation sets that can reveal cross-channel benefits when present. The authors provide public code for replication and further study.

Abstract: Many recent multivariate time series anomaly detection (MT-SAD) models incorporate cross-channel modeling, under the implicit assumption that the structure of anomalies may be spread across multiple channels. We evaluate this assumption on eight widely used public benchmarks by introducing a per-segment diagnostic framework that flags, for each labeled anomaly, whether at least one channel deviates individually from its normal history, whether the cross-channel correlation structure changes, or both. The framework shows that no crosschannel rupture occurs without an accompanying univariate deviation across a range of reasonable thresholds. A complementary metric also reveals that on six of the eight benchmarks, at least half of the labeled anomaly segments deviate univariately on 79% to 100% of their timesteps, reaching 100% on three of these datasets. To verify that our framework captures cross-channel structure when present, we construct synthetic data of phase-shifted sinusoidal channels with shared noise. Each anomalous segment is altered through one of two channelwise corruptions that preserve the per-channel marginal distribution while breaking cross-channel structure, and our framework correctly characterizes these segments as cross-channel-only. On these data, channel-dependent (CD) models successfully exploit the cross-channel signal whereas channel-independent (CI) ones fail. The CI/CD comparison of a recent SOTA detector on real benchmarks further confirms that CD modeling brings no measurable gain. We conclude that current MTSAD benchmarks are unsuitable for validating cross-channel modeling capabilities, and we call for the development of more structurally diverse evaluation sets. The code for this study is publicly available.

</details>


### [279] [Aligning Data-Driven Predictors with Allocation: A Decision-Focused Approach to Survival Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02671)
*Itai Zilberstein,Ioannis Anagnostides,Tuomas Sandholm*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Demonstrates that survival predictors optimized for standard metrics (e.g., C-index) can underperform in allocation tasks, and proposes decision-focused learning with NDCG to align predictions with policy outcomes, including handling censoring; shows large NDCG gains on US heart transplant data and significant projected life-years gained.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Align predictive model objectives with downstream decision tasks (organ allocation) to guarantee utility, addressing the misalignment between traditional survival metrics and practical allocation performance; address right-censoring in ranking evaluation.

Method: Propose a decision-focused learning approach that optimizes normalized discounted cumulative gain (NDCG) for survival-based ranking; introduce a bootstrapping method to optimize NDCG for existing survival models; address right censoring during evaluation of rankings.

Result: Empirical evaluation on historical US heart transplant data shows NDCG improvements of 50–100% over baseline survival models, translating to tens of thousands of life-years gained annually if deployed for transplant allocation.

Conclusion: The framework bridges survival analysis and policy optimization, yielding practical gains in allocation decisions; the approach is general and applicable to other decision-making problems that rely on predictive models.

Abstract: Machine learning predictors have become essential tools for guiding automated decision making. However, a major misalignment persists: predictive models are typically optimized in terms of standard statistical metrics in isolation from the algorithmic tasks they inform. We highlight this incongruity in the high-stakes domain of organ allocation by demonstrating that any algorithm relying on (even highly accurate) survival predictors optimized for standard metrics -- such as the Concordance index (C-index) -- can yield arbitrarily poor outcomes when used for allocation, failing to guarantee utility better than a uniform random selection. To bridge the gap between survival analysis and policy optimization, we introduce a decision-focused learning approach based on optimizing normalized discounted cumulative gain (NDCG), a mainstay metric in information retrieval. We establish the utility of NDCG in survival analysis by proving that it translates to guarantees on the performance of allocation. Empirically, we propose a bootstrapping approach to optimize the NDCG of existing survival models. Unlike prior work, we also address the challenge of right censorship when evaluating ranking. On historical heart transplant data from the US, our method dramatically boosts the NDCG of baseline models by 50-100%, which translates to tens of thousands of additional life years gained annually when deployed for transplant allocation. We anticipate that our framework will find broader applications in decision making with predictions.

</details>


### [280] [Before Fusion, Ask What to Keep: Contextual Calibration of Multimodal Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02679)
*Jiyuan Liu,Liangwei Nathan Zheng,Wei Emma Zhang,Xinpei Wang,Weitong Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a pre-fusion calibration module for multimodal fusion that modulates each modality using cross-modal cues at the summary level, improving robustness and performance across diverse backbones and tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal systems can be degraded by unreliable or conflicting modality signals; calibrating before fusion can suppress noise and preserve informative evidence.

Method: A compact calibration module compares modalities at the summary level to extract cues of cross-source support and conflict, producing instance-wise and dimension-wise modulation signals applied to original modality features before fusion; plug-in to various fusion backbones.

Result: Empirical gains on five benchmarks spanning sentiment understanding, action recognition, audio-visual event detection, and audio-visual emotion classification, under both sequence-based and convolutional fusion; analyses show reduced interference, more stable training, and clearer feature-level effects.

Conclusion: Pre-combination calibration is an effective, versatile strategy for improving multimodal fusion, enabling better utilization of cross-modal evidence with minimal changes to prediction heads.

Abstract: Multimodal systems often benefit from combining information across language, sound, and visual streams, but this benefit is not guaranteed. A modality that is useful for one input may become distracting for another, and local feature responses within the same modality can disagree with evidence from other sources. This work investigates how to adjust multimodal representations before they are merged by a downstream predictor. We develop a compact calibration module that compares each modality with the others at the summary level, extracts cues of cross-source support and conflict, and converts these cues into instance-wise and dimension-wise modulation signals. The calibration is applied to the original modality features rather than to already fused representations, enabling the model to suppress misleading components, preserve weak but useful evidence, and emphasize responses that are better supported by the current multimodal context. The module is designed as a plug-in component and can be attached to different fusion backbones without changing their prediction heads. Across five benchmarks covering sentiment understanding, action recognition, audio-visual event detection, and audio-visual emotion classification, the proposed pre-combination calibration strategy improves performance under both sequence-based and convolutional fusion settings. Additional analyses under modality removal, synthetic corruption, training dynamics, and feature-level visualization show that calibrating signals before fusion can reduce interference from unreliable modalities and produce more stable multimodal optimization.

</details>


### [281] [Locality Does Not Imply Reachability: Boundary Repair in Block-Sparse Causal Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02680)
*Zhibo Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Defines a locality-reachability mismatch in fixed-block causal attention, formalizes structural boundary artifacts, and proposes Boundary Bridge Attention with a minimal repair; provides a theory-guided diagnostic framework and phase-conditioned coverage analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address an architecture-level boundary artifact in block-sparse causal attention where fixed block masks can render tokens within a block prefix inaccessible from certain sources. Seek a diagnostic framework and repair strategy that reconciles locality with reachability.

Method: Formalize reachability via structural dependency sets under fixed blocking; derive phase-conditioned coverage functions that depend on source-target distance and target offset; introduce Boundary Bridge Attention (BBA) with zero-parameter auxiliary edges near block boundaries using shared projections; validate with controlled 1024-token experiments and a fixed-checkpoint 8K-token Qwen2.5-7B probe.

Result: Demonstrates boundary-copy separation with top-1 accuracy upper bound 1/K and cross-entropy lower bound log K under a K-way boundary-copy distribution; coverage laws predict failure and repair opportunities, and show non-interchangeability between sliding-window and boundary repair. BBA preserves fixed block path while adding minimal connections. Empirical gains concentrate on coverage-aligned diagnostics and replicate on external data.

Conclusion: Provides a theory-guided diagnostic framework for locality-reachability mismatch in block-sparse causal attention, along with phase-conditioned coverage analysis and a minimal constructive repair (Boundary Bridge Attention) that clarifies when sparse patterns fail and how to repair them.

Abstract: Sparse causal attention is usually described by sequence locality: nearby tokens should remain easy to access, while distant tokens may be dropped to reduce cost. This paper studies a mismatch between sequence locality and attention-graph reachability. In fixed block causal attention, two adjacent tokens can be disconnected in the attention graph at every depth. We formalize this boundary artifact through structural dependency sets: if every attention layer uses the same fixed block causal mask and all remaining operations are positionwise, a target representation can depend only on tokens in its own block prefix. This yields an architecture-level boundary-copy separation for a constructed K-way boundary-copy distribution, with top-1 accuracy upper bound 1/K and expected cross-entropy lower bound log K. We then derive phase-conditioned coverage functions showing that reachability depends on both source-target distance and the target's offset within its block. These coverage laws predict when a sparse pattern should fail, when a repair can help, and why sliding-window attention and boundary repair are not interchangeable. Boundary Bridge Attention is treated as a constructive witness: it preserves the fixed block path and adds zero-additional-parameter auxiliary causal edges near block boundaries using shared projections. Controlled 1024-token experiments show that gains concentrate in coverage-aligned diagnostics. As secondary external-validity evidence, a fixed-checkpoint 8K-token Qwen2.5-7B probe shows the same coverage-incomparability pattern. The contribution is a theory-guided diagnostic framework for locality-reachability mismatch in block-sparse causal attention, together with phase-conditioned coverage analysis and a minimal constructive repair.

</details>


### [282] [Filter, Then Reweight: Rethinking Optimization Granularity in On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02684)
*Yuying Li,Leqi Zheng,Yongzi Yu,Wenrui Zhou,Xuchang Zhong,Xing Hu,Jing Jin,Huangjie Yuan,Tao Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FiRe-OPD (Filter, then Reweight) introduces a two-stage optimization granularity for On-Policy Distillation: filter trajectories to drop low-quality rollouts and apply soft, token-level reweighting within the retained data, yielding finer-grained supervision and improved stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the shift in OPD toward selective, trajectory- and token-level supervision, reducing information loss from hard selections and improving optimization stability.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) filter trajectories to remove low-quality rollouts; (2) within retained trajectories, apply soft reweighting to tokens to emphasize informative ones rather than hard token selection.

Result: Outperforms recent token-level OPD methods, reporting gains such as +6.25 on AIME 2024 (strong-to-weak) and +18.81 on Miner (multi-teacher); demonstrates robustness across strong-to-weak, single-teacher, and multi-teacher settings; code released at GitHub.

Conclusion: FiRe-OPD provides a finer-grained, stable OPD optimization by combining trajectory filtering with soft token reweighting, with empirical improvements across diverse teacher settings.

Abstract: On-Policy distillation (OPD) in large language models is shifting from full-trace KL supervision toward more selective training paradigms. Recent OPD methods increasingly focus on selecting which trajectories to learn from, which tokens are most informative, and which supervision signals are most reliable. Motivated by this trend, we rethink optimization granularity of OPD and propose \fireicon\ FiRe-OPD (Filter, then Reweight), which jointly adjusts supervision signals at both trajectory and token levels. In details, FiRe-OPD first filters trajectories to remove low-quality rollout samples, and then applies soft reweighting within the retained trajectories to emphasize informative tokens. Compared with hard token selection, FiRe-OPD leverages a soft-weighting mechanism to effectively mitigate information loss and enhance optimization stability, thereby achieving finer-grained OPD optimization. We validate the effectiveness of FiRe-OPD across strong-to-weak, single-teacher, and multi-teacher settings, and demonstrate its superiority over recent token-level OPD methods ( (e.g., +6.25 on AIME 2024 in strong-to-weak, +18.81 on Miner in multi-teacher). Our code is available at https://github.com/YuYingLi0/FiRe-OPD.

</details>


### [283] [$Ψ$-Bench: Evaluating Persona-Sensitive Influencing in Persuasive Dialogues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02754)
*Peixuan Han,Hongyi Du,Jiayu Liu,Yihang Sun,Yutong Liu,Jiaxuan You*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Ψ-Bench benchmarks proactive, persona-sensitive persuasion in LLMs by simulating realistic client profiles and scenarios; 10 frontier models show coherent reasoning but limited persuasion; access to profiles boosts performance by ~18.24%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current work treats personalized agents as passive responders; there is a need to systematically evaluate proactive personalization and influencing capabilities in realistic, interactive settings.

Method: Three real-world interaction scenarios involving persuasion are designed. Simulated clients are endowed with explicit user profiles derived from dialogue histories. Ten frontier LLMs are evaluated on Ψ-Bench, with performance measured on their ability to persuade users.

Result: Models generally produce coherent, reasonable arguments, but show substantial room for improvement in persuasion effectiveness. Providing access to client profiles yields an average performance gain of 18.24%.
