Conclusion: Persona-sensitive influencing is a challenging yet practical direction for evaluating and developing proactive personalized LLM agents; Ψ-Bench offers a scalable framework. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: Personalization is a crucial capability of modern language agents. However, current research primarily positions personalized agents as passive responders to user preferences, limiting their ability to interact with users and provide suggestions or guidance proactively. To systematically evaluate such proactive personalization in realistic interactions, we propose $Ψ$-Bench, a benchmark for assessing LLMs' ability to influence realistic users through conversation. We design three real-world interaction scenarios that involve persuasion in $Ψ$-Bench, and endow simulated clients with personal characteristics through explicit user profiles derived from dialogue histories. We evaluate 10 frontier LLMs on $Ψ$-Bench and find that while most models can produce coherent and reasonable arguments, even state-of-the-art models still leave considerable room for improvement in persuasion. We also find that providing access to client profiles yields an average performance gain of 18.24\%, highlighting the importance of user-specific information for effective persuasion. Overall, our work highlights persona-sensitive influencing as a challenging yet practical direction for evaluating and developing more proactive personalized LLM agents. Codes are available at: https://github.com/Hanpx20/Psi-Bench.

</details>


### [284] [Binary Road Surface Classification Using Machine Learning on Production Vehicle Signals During Cruising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02762)
*Vishal Hariharan,Salar Basiri,Kanwar Bharat Singh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A data-driven, sliding-window ML framework correlates vehicle dynamics with road surface state to classify grip vs. slip, enabling surface condition estimation during cruising when traditional friction estimators fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Friction estimation via dynamics-based recursive estimators relies on detectable slip; during cruising there is little to no slip, so micro-slip signals are hard to measure with standard sensors, degrading surface discrimination. ML can exploit correlations between readily measured vehicle signals and road conditions to improve estimation.

Method: Two approaches: a feature-based framework and an end-to-end data-driven framework. A sliding-window buffer of wheel speeds, torques, longitudinal acceleration, steering angle, and yaw rate is fed into a machine learning module to predict road state (binary class: grip/dry-or-damp vs. slip/snow-or-ice). Validation uses public-road data.

Result: The data-driven method identifies road surface correctly even during cruising scenarios, demonstrating potential for accurate data-driven friction-related state estimators in tire and vehicle dynamics.

Conclusion: The study shows promise for ML-based road-surface and friction state estimation, with potential to enhance tire–vehicle dynamics control and warning/intervention systems in real-world driving.

Abstract: Knowledge of real-time road slipperiness, or even better, a refined estimate of peak grip potential, is a critical input for vehicle warning and intervention control systems. Typically, friction is estimated through dynamics-based recursive estimators by calculating the slip slope; however, its efficacy is heavily constrained by the vehicle dynamic scenario. When the vehicle is cruising and there is little to no slip, these methods become ineffective due to the inability of present-day production-grade sensors, such as wheel speed sensors, and methods to either measure or accurately estimate micro slip, which is crucial for distinguishing different surfaces. To address this challenge, the correlation between vehicle signals and road surface condition during cruising needs to be uncovered using machine learning. In this paper, a feature-based framework and an end-to-end data-driven framework are used to correlate the statistics of vehicle dynamics behavior with the condition of the road surface and perform binary classification into grip, dry or damp, and slip, snow or ice, conditions. A sliding-window approach is adopted to batch a short buffered window of wheel speeds, wheel torques, longitudinal acceleration, steering angle, and yaw rate, which are fed into a machine learning module for predicting the road state. Validation results on public-road data show scenarios where the data-driven method identifies the road surface correctly even during cruising, showing promise for accurate data-driven friction-related state estimators in the field of tire and vehicle dynamics.

</details>


### [285] [Representational Capacity: Geometric Limits on Feature Representation in Transformer Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02765)
*Alexander Guha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework to quantify transformer representational capacity by near-orthogonal feature directions. It defines an epsilon-orthogonality measure via embedding matrices, reveals two model classes (high vs low epsilon) across open-source models, and derives an adjusted capacity bound where capacity scales with k/d rather than k, showing capacity is exponentially sensitive to epsilon and that larger models favor tighter orthogonality constraints. The work questions the sufficiency of raw dimensionality for capacity and highlights scale-related trade-offs with potential ties to stability or concept ceilings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how much information richness a model's latent space can support by measuring the geometry of representations, bridging empirical embedding structure with a formal capacity bound that depends on model scale.

Method: Quantify near-orthogonality via the embedding matrix by analyzing the boundary in the cosine-similarity distribution between meaningful token relationships and incidental similarity to estimate the permissible deviation epsilon. Apply this metric to dozens of open-source models to classify them into high- and low-epsilon groups. Revisit the Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma to derive an adjusted capacity formula where the number of near-orthogonal directions depends on k/d rather than k, and validate that this reduces prediction error without extra parameters. Define representational capacity as an upper bound on the number of distinguishable featuredirections and embeddings in latent space.

Result: Two model classes emerged: high-epsilon models with embeddings lacking near-orthogonal structure, and low-epsilon models that preserve near-orthogonality. The standard JL lemma underestimates packing efficiency; the revised capacity formula shows dependence on k/d. Representational capacity is exponentially sensitive to epsilon, and larger models tend to favor tighter orthogonality constraints over raw capacity, suggesting a stability-capacity trade-off or a ceiling on usable concepts.

Conclusion: Representational capacity is an upper bound on distinguishable latent directions. epsilon controls capacity exponentially; model scale pushes toward tighter orthogonality constraints, implying trade-offs between stability and capacity. Future work to explain these patterns (scale effects, stability vs. capacity) and validate across broader architectures.

Abstract: Model dimension ($d_{model}$) is a fundamental hyperparameter in transformer language models, yet its role in setting the geometric limits of feature representation remains under-explored. Grounded in the Linear Representation and Superposition Hypotheses - which propose that models encode features as near-orthogonal directions in latent space - we develop a framework for estimating how many such directions a model can support. We first establish the embedding matrix as a measurable proxy for near-orthogonality constraints across the latent space: the boundary between meaningful token relationships and incidental similarity in the pairwise cosine similarity distribution gives a concrete estimate of the model's accepted deviation $\varepsilon$ from perfect orthogonality. Applying this metric across dozens of open-source models reveals two classes: models with high $\varepsilon$ whose embeddings lack near-orthogonal structure, and models with low $\varepsilon$ that maintain it. We then show that the standard Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma greatly underestimates the packing efficiency of trained representations, and derive an adjusted capacity formula in which the number of near-orthogonal directions depends on the ratio of vectors to dimensions ($k/d$) rather than the raw count - a single modification that cuts prediction error by two orders of magnitude with no extra parameters. Combining these results, we define representational capacity as an upper bound on the number of distinguishable directions available for features and embeddings in a model's latent space. Capacity is exponentially sensitive to $\varepsilon$, and larger models favor tighter orthogonality constraints over maximizing raw capacity - a pattern compatible with several explanations (a stability-capacity trade-off, a ceiling on usable concepts, or confounds with model scale) that we leave to future work.

</details>


### [286] [QUIVER: Quantum-Informed Views for Enhanced Representations in Large ML Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02785)
*Aritra Bal,Michael Binder,Markus Klute,Benedikt Maier,Michael Spannowsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QUIVER adds a quantum Fisher information (QFI) view from a variational quantum circuit to classical features, boosting performance on QM9 and JetClass, in a domain-agnostic integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leverage quantum information geometry to capture intrinsic higher-order correlations that are difficult to learn with classical data alone, providing a complementary feature stream.

Method: Train a variational quantum circuit (VQC) on the same task, compute the quantum Fisher information matrix as a feature map, and fuse this quantum-geometric view with standard classical architectures via architectural modifications that encode quantum geometry.

Result: Demonstrated improved performance on two benchmarks (QM9 molecule properties; JetClass jet flavor at the LHC). The approach is domain-agnostic and can be integrated into a broad class of models.

Conclusion: Quantum-geometric features derived from simulated circuits can enhance classical ML tasks prior to fault-tolerant quantum hardware, indicating a broadly applicable, complementary modality for representation learning.

Abstract: Large machine learning models benefit substantially from multimodal inputs that provide a complementary view of the same example. We introduce QUIVER (QUantum-Informed Views for Enhanced Representations, a paradigm that enriches classical data-driven features with a quantum Fisher view: a geometrically motivated, basis-independent summary of higher-order correlations captured by a variational quantum circuit (VQC) trained to perform the same task. Unlike classical feature augmentation, the quantum Fisher information matrix encodes the intrinsic geometry of the learned quantum state manifold. While this feature map, motivated by quantum information theory, is ordinarily non-trivial to model classically, it can surface statistical structure that additional classical data or model capacity finds difficult to learn. This makes the quantum Fisher view a genuinely complementary modality rather than a redundant one. We demonstrate that QUIVER improves standard performance metrics on two benchmark datasets from very different fields: QM9 for predicting molecule properties, and JetClass for predicting jet flavor at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC). The core contribution, however, is domain-agnostic: the quantum Fisher view can be fused into a broad class of model architectures via targeted modifications to the base architecture, to incorporate information about the quantum geometry of the problem. These results demonstrate that quantum-geometric features, extracted from simulated variational circuits, can deliver measurable value for standard machine learning tasks, well before the advent of fault-tolerant quantum hardware.

</details>


### [287] [Qift: Shift-Friendly No-Zero W2 Post-Training Quantization for Rotated W2A4/KV4 LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02823)
*Chi-Wei Huang,Chia-Chi Tsai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Qift: a fixed no-zero two-bit level set for rotated W2A4/KV4 quantization, addressing level-set collapse in aggressive two-bit quantization by Hadamard rotation Gaussianizing weights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce memory footprint and maintain accuracy in LLM inference with two-bit quantization; standard W2 level sets fail under W2A4/KV4; investigate level-set geometry and reconstruction-level issues.

Method: Analyze scalar level-set geometry of two-bit weights in a Hadamard-rotated pipeline; study distributional properties of pretrained LLaMA weights; introduce Hadamard rotation, zero-centered Gaussian-like source model, and Qift with fixed level sets; perform scale-preserving mapping, ratio analysis to explain performance; evaluate across LLaMA-2-7B and LLaMA-3.1-8B.

Result: Qift improves W2A4 perplexity, L-layer mixed W2/W4 perplexity, downstream accuracy, and GPTQ residuals vs standard W2; achieves competitive gap to W3A4 at L=16 while using two-bit only on half layers; faster and simpler than learned W2 codebooks; preserves per-channel scale.

Conclusion: A simple, training-free, deployment-friendly fixed two-bit level set (Qift) for rotated W2A4/KV4 quantization effectively mitigates reconstruction-level issues and narrows the W2 vs W3 gap, offering a practical alternative to learned codebooks.

Abstract: Two-bit weight quantization is attractive for memory-efficient LLM inference, but the standard W2 level set {-2,-1,0,+1} often collapses under aggressive W2A4/KV4 settings. We study the scalar level-set geometry of two-bit weights in a Hadamard-rotated quantization pipeline. Conventional asymmetric W2 substantially improves over the standard level set, indicating that W2A4 failure is not only a bit-width problem but also a reconstruction-level problem. Across all 224 linear modules in each of LLaMA-2-7B and LLaMA-3.1-8B, pretrained weights are already nearly zero-centered, while Hadamard rotation primarily Gaussianizes their standardized shape: excess kurtosis and Q-Q error drop by orders of magnitude. Based on this approximate zero-centered Gaussian-like source model, we propose Qift, a fixed no-zero W2 level set for rotated W2A4/KV4 inference. The main level set is {+/-0.5, +/-1.5}, equivalently {+/-1, +/-3} under a half-scale reparameterization; a power-of-two variant uses {+/-1, +/-4} for sign-and-shift decoded weight application. Qift redesigns the fixed two-bit code-to-level mapping and is training-free, learned-codebook-free, group-grid-free, and zero-point-free, retaining the standard per-channel scale. A scale-invariant ratio analysis identifies an effective inner/outer centroid ratio range of 0.25 to 0.33, explaining why mirror no-zero (MNZ), Lloyd, NF2, and PoT-MNZ perform well while {+/-1, +/-2} does not. On both models, the no-zero level sets consistently improve pure W2A4 perplexity, L-layer mixed W2/W4 perplexity, downstream accuracy, and GPTQ residual behavior over the standard W2 level set. At L=16 mixed precision, they substantially narrow the gap to W3A4 while keeping half of the transformer layers at two-bit precision, giving a simple, source-aware, and deployment-friendly alternative to more complex learned W2 codebooks.

</details>


### [288] [Mitigating Spurious Correlations with Memorization-Guided Dataset De-Biasing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02830)
*Arda Fazla,Abolfazl Hashemi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-stage sample scoring disentangles core (causal) vs spurious features to select informative samples, enabling ERM with much less data and outperforming debiasing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world data contain spurious correlations causing models to rely on them; minority samples lack these patterns. Group labels are often unavailable, and existing sample-scoring methods rely on spurious cues, failing to capture core feature difficulty.

Method: Develop a two-stage sample scoring function that separately models the learning dynamics of core and spurious features and evaluates their difficulty. Use this metric to build an algorithm that prioritizes informative samples, both with and without spurious correlations.

Result: An ERM model trained on the selected samples outperforms state-of-the-art debiasing approaches and requires only about 10% of the original training data.

Conclusion: Disentangling core and spurious feature learning to guide sample selection effectively mitigates spurious correlations, reducing data requirements while maintaining or improving accuracy.

Abstract: Real-world datasets often contain spurious correlations that are not causally related to the target label. When such correlations dominate the majority of training samples, models tend to rely on them, leading to misclassification of minority samples that do not exhibit the same spurious patterns. While a potential approach is to select subsets of data to better represent the minority samples, this may require access to group labels, which are typically unknown. Furthermore, as we demonstrate, widely used sample scoring functions in the invariant subset or coreset selection literature largely depend on spurious features and therefore fail to accurately capture the importance or difficulty of core, causally relevant features. Accordingly, we propose to mitigate spurious correlations by developing a two-stage sample scoring function that disentangles the learning dynamics of core and spurious features and evaluates their difficulty separately. Based on our proposed metric, we introduce a new algorithm to find and prioritize informative samples both with and without spurious correlations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that a standard ERM model trained on our selected samples achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art debiasing techniques, while requiring as little as 10\% of the original training data.

</details>


### [289] [Learning Coherent Representations: A Topological Approach to Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02841)
*Sigurd Gaukstad,Melvin Vaupel,Valdemar Kargård Olsen,Erik Hermansen,Benjamin Dunn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Defines and enforces a geometric coherence property for non-negative matrices to align samples and features via topological interleaving, improving interpretability; introduces a differentiable Coh objective and validates on autoencoders and BERT embeddings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interpretability of neural representations: individual neurons often activate for unrelated inputs. A geometric constraint promises interpretable, contiguously activated feature regions and a feature space with meaningful topology.

Method: Introduce coherence: rows (samples) attend to geometrically clustered columns (features) and vice versa; require both directions and that every sample and feature are used. Prove bounded interleaving between Vietoris-Rips filtrations of samples and features. Propose Coh, a differentiable objective based on Fréchet variance to enforce coherence during training. Compare with sparsity (which limits activations) by enforcing geometric connectivity. Validate in an auto-encoder with synthetic and rotated MNIST data and in BERT token embeddings.

Result: Coherence imposes that features tile geometric state spaces (e.g., a circle would be partitioned into contiguous arcs). The bounded interleaving ensures compatible topological structure between sample and feature spaces, aiding interpretability. The Coh objective guides training toward coherent representations, yielding interpretable feature spaces beyond sparse activations. Empirical validation on auto-encoders (synthetic and rotated MNIST) and language data (BERT embeddings) demonstrates practical applicability.

Conclusion: Geometric coherence offers a principled path to interpretable representations by constraining how samples relate to features and by aligning the topological structure of their spaces; Coh provides a differentiable means to enforce this during learning, with demonstrated benefits in vision and language contexts.

Abstract: Deep neural networks learn representations where individual features often lack interpretable meaning; a single neuron may activate for scattered, unrelated inputs. We introduce coherence, a geometric property inspired by neural coding in the brain, where neurons like grid cells and head direction cells respond to contiguous regions of state space. A non-negative matrix is coherent if each row (sample) attends to geometrically clustered columns (features) and vice versa, and in addition every sample is well described by some feature and every feature is needed by some sample. We prove that coherent matrices induce a bounded interleaving between the Vietoris-Rips filtrations of samples and features, guaranteeing that both spaces share compatible topological structure. This geometric constraint facilitates interpretability. For example, if data lies on a circle, coherent features must tile that circle into contiguous arcs. We introduce Coh, a differentiable objective function based on Fréchet variance that enforces coherence during training. Unlike sparsity, which bounds how many samples a feature activates on, coherence bounds which samples, requiring geometric connectivity rather than only rarity. This yields not just interpretable features but an interpretable feature space. We validate Coh in an auto-encoder using synthetic and rotated MNIST datasets and in a token embedding of BERT using language data.

</details>


### [290] [Spectral-Progressive Thought Flow for Lightweight Multimodal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02842)
*Yixian Shen,Zhiheng Yang,Qi Bi,Changshuo Wang,Shuai Wang,Jia-Hong Huang,George Floros,Prayag Tiwari,Anuj Pathania*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SpecFlow represents intermediate visual thoughts in a fixed-size discrete cosine space to enable low-latency, memory-efficient multimodal spatial reasoning, guided by autoregressive textual thoughts, achieving competitive performance with up to 2.1x reductions in compute and KV cache costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long chains of intermediate textual and visual thoughts in multimodal spatial reasoning incur substantial computation and memory overhead due to accumulating visual tokens and dense cross-modal attention. A bounded, scalable approach is needed for long-horizon inference with stable latency.

Method: Introduce Spectral-Progressive Thought Flow (SpecFlow) that encodes the visual state in a fixed-size discrete cosine space, exploiting energy compaction to preserve global layout while adding high-frequency details only when higher spatial precision is necessary. Use classifier-free guidance to align autoregressive textual thoughts with flow-based updates of the visual workspace. The visual state updates depend only on the current state and accumulated textual trace, yielding a bounded workspace and depth-independent latency/memory.

Result: SpecFlow achieves competitive or superior reasoning performance while reducing computation and KV cache costs by up to 2.1x, with stable latency and memory usage independent of reasoning depth.

Conclusion: A spectral, flow-based, bounded visual state representation with guidance from textual thoughts enables efficient long-horizon multimodal spatial reasoning without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: Multimodal spatial reasoning often relies on long chains of intermediate textual and visual thoughts, where accumulating visual tokens and dense cross-modal attention incur substantial computation and memory overhead. To address this challenge, we propose Spectral-Progressive Thought Flow (SpecFlow), a novel lightweight multimodal spatial reasoning framework that represents intermediate visual thoughts in a fixed-size discrete cosine space. By exploiting strong energy compaction, SpecFlow preserves global layout and relational structure while introducing high-frequency details only when increased spatial precision is required. To align visual state evolution with linguistic intent, classifier-free guidance enables autoregressive textual thoughts to steer flow-based updates of the visual workspace/state without expanding the context. As a result, SpecFlow maintains a bounded visual workspace whose updates depend only on the current visual state and accumulated textual trace, enabling long-horizon inference with stable latency and memory usage independent of reasoning depth. Empirical results show that SpecFlow achieves competitive or superior reasoning performance while reducing computation and KV cache costs by up to 2.1 times.

</details>


### [291] [A Systematic Evaluation of Current Architectures in Wind Power Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02849)
*Vinicius Bortolini,Gilson Adamczuk Oliveira,Erick Oliveira Rodrigues,Matheus Henrique Dal Molin Ribeiro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid interval forecasting for wind generation using decomposition (VMD/EEMD) and deep learning (LSTM/ELM) with a dual-bound approach; guide selection via LDA-based topic modeling; improved interval accuracy and reliability, though standard evaluation, computational cost, and real-world validation remain challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address uncertainty in wind power forecasts by producing reliable interval forecasts and to improve decision-making for wind energy operations through hybrid models and decomposition techniques.

Method: Systematic literature review of hybrid interval forecasting approaches; topic modeling with Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) to identify trends; analysis of interval construction using dual-bound models (separate lower/upper bound forecasts) and data decomposition (EMD, EEMD, VMD) feeding models like LSTM/ELM.

Result: Hybrid-decomposition methods (VMD/EEMD with DL models) tend to narrow prediction intervals while maintaining coverage, improving both accuracy and reliability. Most studies employ a dual-model strategy for bounds and use decomposition to extract frequency components for model inputs. Evaluation metrics balance interval coverage with width; challenges include inconsistent metrics, high computational load, and limited real-world validation.

Conclusion: Interval forecasting is valuable for wind energy operations. The literature supports hybrid-decomposition approaches for more robust interval forecasts, but standardization of evaluation, computational efficiency, and real-world testing are needed to advance robustness and applicability.

Abstract: Interval wind speed forecasting is essential for the efficient integration of wind energy into power systems, as it accounts for the inherent uncertainty of wind resources. This study presents a systematic literature review focused on hybrid approaches to interval forecasting of wind generation, exploring the combination of deep learning, modal decomposition, and statistical methods. To guide the paper selection, Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) was applied for topic modeling, enabling the identification of patterns and research trends. The findings emphasize that integrating hybrid models with decomposition techniques-such as Variational Mode Decomposition (VMD) and Ensemble Empirical Mode Decomposition (EEMD)-enhances forecast accuracy and reliability by narrowing prediction intervals without compromising coverage. Regarding interval construction, most studies adopt a dual-model strategy, independently forecasting the lower and upper bounds. Input data are commonly decomposed using techniques like EMD, EEMD, or VMD, which extract frequency-based components. These components serve as inputs to models such as LSTM or ELM, trained separately for each bound. This approach allows for targeted modeling of uncertainty, improving flexibility and precision, Interval quality is typically evaluated through metrics that balance coverage and interval width. The review also highlights challenges, including the lack of standardized evaluation metrics, computational complexity, and limited real-world validation. Overall, the study reinforces the value of interval forecasting for wind energy operations and offers insights for advancing model robustness and decision-making.

</details>


### [292] [RESCAST-100K: A Comprehensive Dataset for Cross-Domain Residential Load and Indoor Temperature Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02852)
*Jainam Dhruva,Yousaf Raza,A. B. Siddique,Simone Silvestri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RESCAST-100K is a large, configuration-driven residential forecasting benchmark for cross-domain generalization, enabling systematic evaluation of transfer learning and domain adaptation across geography, climate, construction, and heating, using ~100k EnergyPlus-simulated homes plus five real-world datasets; cross-attention and MLP-mixer models outperform baselines under domain shift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of comprehensive, cross-domain residential forecasting datasets and standardized evaluation protocols, which hinder progress in transfer learning and domain adaptation for home, community, and grid-scale forecasting.

Method: A config-driven interface instantiates source and target domains along axes like geography, climate zone, wall construction, and heating equipment. The benchmark uses ~100k EnergyPlus-simulated U.S. homes (ResStock-derived) with 15-minute time series for total load, HVAC load, and indoor temperature, plus weather channels, HVAC setpoints, and 40+ static covariates. Five real-world residential datasets are integrated under a unified schema for sim-to-real evaluation. Multiple architectures (recurrent, attention-based, MLP-mixer) are benchmarked for zero-shot, domain-shifted forecasting, including missing-input scenarios.

Result: Cross-attention and MLP-mixer architectures consistently outperform recurrent and classical transformer baselines under domain shift; the benchmark supports sim-to-real evaluation and systematic transfer learning/domain adaptation studies across home, community, and grid scales.

Conclusion: RESCAST-100K is a practical benchmark to advance cross-domain residential forecasting research, enabling standardized evaluation of transfer learning, domain adaptation, and zero-shot generalization across diverse domain shifts.

Abstract: Accurate short-term forecasting of residential energy load and indoor temperature is essential for home energy management systems, grid-level demand response, and community energy efficiency efforts. Domain adaptation and transfer learning have shown promise for improving forecasting accuracy under data heterogeneity and scarcity commonly seen in residential settings. However, progress is limited by the lack of comprehensive residential datasets: existing benchmarks are narrow in target coverage and rarely support structured cross-domain evaluation. We introduce RESCAST-100K, a large-scale residential forecasting benchmark for studying cross-domain generalization. It provides a configuration-driven interface that instantiates source and target domains along interpretable axes, including geography, climate zone, wall construction, and heating equipment, enabling systematic evaluation of transfer learning, domain adaptation, and zero-shot generalization under controlled domain shifts. The benchmark covers approximately 100,000 EnergyPlus-simulated U.S. homes derived from ResStock, with 15-minute time series for three coupled targets per home: total load, HVAC load, and indoor temperature. These are paired with weather channels, HVAC setpoints, and over 40 static building covariates. RESCAST-100K also integrates five real-world residential datasets under a unified schema, supporting sim-to-real evaluation on the same tasks. We benchmark recurrent, attention-based, and MLP-mixer architectures for zero-shot performance across domains, missing-input conditions, and forecasting tasks. Cross-attention and MLP-mixer models consistently outperform recurrent and classical transformer baselines under domain shift. RESCAST-100K is intended to aid the machine learning and building analytics communities advance cross-domain residential forecasting at home, community, and grid scale.

</details>


### [293] [GRZO: Group-Relative Zeroth-Order Optimization for Large Language Model Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02857)
*Liyan Tan,Yequan Zhao,Yifan Yang,Ruijie Zhang,Xinling Yu,Zheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRZO reduces zeroth-order gradient variance for LLM fine-tuning by per-example perturbations and group-relative normalization, achieving batch-size gradient info at no extra forward cost, with memory savings and improved accuracy compared to MeZO and other ZO variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ZO optimization suffers from high variance in gradient estimation; backpropagation is memory-intensive for fine-tuning large language models; there is a need for memory-efficient, scalable ZO methods with stronger convergence guarantees.

Method: For each mini-batch example, GRZO draws one pseudo-independent perturbation and computes per-example losses. It then aggregates these losses using group-relative normalization, effectively increasing the gradient-direction count from 1 to the batch size without additional forward passes, preserving inference-time memory. The estimator is directionally unbiased and its variance shrinks proportionally to batch size, yielding a tighter nonconvex convergence bound than MeZO.

Result: Empirical evaluation on RoBERTa-large, Llama3-8B, and OPT-13B across multiple tasks shows GRZO improves average accuracy on Llama3-8B by +3.0 over MeZO at 23% lower peak GPU memory. As a drop-in replacement for the MeZO core, GRZO also lifts performance of sparse, low-rank, and quantized ZO variants by +6.0 on average.

Conclusion: GRZO provides a memory-efficient zeroth-order optimization with reduced gradient variance and a stronger convergence guarantee, demonstrated across multiple large-model paradigms and ZO variants; it serves as a practical, scalable improvement over MeZO for ZO-based fine-tuning of large language models.

Abstract: Zeroth-order (ZO) optimization is a memory-efficient alternative to backpropagation for fine-tuning large language models, but its deployment is limited by the high variance of gradient estimation. We propose GRZO, a Group-Relative Zeroth-Order optimizer that draws one pseudo-independent perturbation per mini-batch example and aggregates the per-example losses through group-relative normalization, raising the effective gradient-direction count from one to the batch size at no additional forward cost while preserving inference-level memory. We prove that GRZO is directionally unbiased with variance shrinking proportionally to the batch size, yielding a tighter nonconvex convergence bound than MeZO. Across RoBERTa-large, Llama3-8B, and OPT-13B over multiple tasks, GRZO improves average accuracy on Llama3-8B by $+3.0$ over MeZO at $23\%$ lower peak GPU memory; as a drop-in replacement for the MeZO core, it lifts sparse, low-rank, and quantized ZO variants by $+6.0$ on average.

</details>


### [294] [RRISE: Robust Radius Inference via a Surrogate Estimator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02876)
*Jong-Ik Park,Shreyas Chaudhari,Carlee Joe-Wong,José M. F. Moura*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RRISE replaces per-input Monte Carlo sampling in randomized smoothing with a learned surrogate that, after offline precomputation, provides provably conservative certified radii in a single forward pass, enabling real-time deployment with comparable certified accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the real-time cost of randomized smoothing (RS) certificates, which currently relies on expensive per-input MC sampling, by sharing information across the deployment stream and eliminating repeated base-model evaluations.

Method: Train a surrogate network to imitate MC-based class-count targets using soft-label cross-entropy, based on precomputed MC results. Apply a one-time conformal calibration to convert surrogate outputs into conservative certified radii. During deployment, use the surrogate to predict radii; if the radius is positive, the prediction provably matches the smoothed classifier and is constant within that radius.

Result: On image-classification benchmarks, RRISE matches fixed-budget MC certified accuracy within 0.84 percentage points while replacing up to 10^4 noisy base-model evaluations per query with a single surrogate forward pass. It recoups MC training cost after ~10^5 deployment queries. On CIFAR-100 and Tiny ImageNet, where prior offline-surrogate methods fail, RRISE achieves 1.23× to 1.91× higher certified accuracy, enabling practical repeated-deployment RS.

Conclusion: RRISE demonstrates that efficient, deployment-friendly randomized smoothing is feasible, delivering near-parity with MC-based guarantees while dramatically reducing online computation and enabling robust, repeated-deployment certification.

Abstract: Randomized smoothing (RS) uses a smoothed classifier to provide architecture-agnostic certificates of $\ell_2$ classification robustness, but its dependence on per-input Monte Carlo (MC) sampling undermines its use in real-time systems. We argue that this cost is structural rather than fundamental, such that it can be significantly reduced by sharing information across the deployment stream. We introduce RRISE, an RS framework that compresses certification into a single forward pass through a learned surrogate. RRISE trains the surrogate against precomputed MC class-count targets via a soft-label cross-entropy loss and converts surrogate predictions into provably conservative certified radii through a one-time conformal calibration step. The resulting certificate is deployment-verifiable: whenever the calibrated radius is positive, the surrogate's prediction provably matches the smoothed classifier's and the smoothed classifier is constant on a ball of that radius around the input. Across image classification benchmarks, RRISE matches fixed-budget MC certified accuracy within $0.84$ percentage points while replacing up to $10^4$ noisy base-model evaluations per query with a single surrogate forward pass, recouping MC training cost after $\approx 10^5$ deployment queries. On CIFAR-100 and Tiny ImageNet, where the only prior offline-surrogate method collapses, RRISE achieves $1.23$ to $1.91\times$ higher certified accuracy, establishing efficient randomized smoothing as a practical path to certified robustness in repeated-deployment settings.

</details>


### [295] [Are we really tilting? The mechanics of reward guidance in flow and diffusion models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02884)
*Sanjit Dandapanthula,Nicholas M. Boffi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Finite-particle Doob h-function plug-in estimation causes reward hacking in reward-guided diffusion; two distinct failure modes are identified (within-mode bias and poor mode selection); a closed-form damping schedule fixes within-mode bias without extra compute, and best-of-n sampling helps with mode selection; validated on Gaussian mixtures, 2D checkerboard, and FLUX.1 text-to-image generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reveal the fundamental origin of reward hacking in reward-guided diffusion beyond empirical biases, by analyzing the common Doob h-function estimator used in practice.

Method: Theoretically derive the finite-particle plug-in estimator for the Doob h-function in reward-guided diffusion; isolate two failure modes (within-mode bias and mode-selection failure); propose a closed-form damping schedule; analyze how best-of-n sampling compensates for mode selection; validate findings on synthetic targets (Gaussian mixtures, 2D checkerboard) and a real-world-like task (FLUX.1 text-to-image generation).

Result: Identification of two distinct failure modes of the plug-in Doob h-function estimator in reward-guided diffusion. A closed-form reward damping schedule is proposed to correct the within-mode bias without additional computation, and the role of best-of-n sampling in addressing mode-selection failure is clarified. Empirical evidence on Gaussian mixtures, a 2D checkerboard, and FLUX.1 supports the theoretical insights, showing correspondence between theory and practice.

Conclusion: Reward hacking in reward-guided diffusion originates from finite-particle Doob h-function estimation. The proposed damping remedy and the clarified role of best-of-n sampling offer practical mitigation with theoretical grounding, and the results generalize from synthetic targets to realistic generative tasks.

Abstract: Reward guidance algorithms steer a learned generative process toward the reward-tilted measure at inference time. While empirically powerful, these methods are prone to reward hacking: the guided model over-optimizes the reward at the cost of fidelity to the learned distribution. Prior work has attributed this to the complexity of neural reward functions or implicit biases in diffusion training, but its fundamental origins remain poorly understood. We show that reward hacking arises from an approximation made in most practical implementations of reward-guided diffusion -- finite-particle plug-in estimation of the Doob h-function -- even in the simplest non-trivial settings of Gaussian and Gaussian mixture targets with quadratic rewards. In closed form, we isolate two distinct failure modes of the plug-in estimator: it leads to reward hacking within each mode and it cannot select high-reward modes. We propose a closed-form reward damping schedule that corrects the within-mode bias with no additional compute, and clarify the role of best-of-n sampling in compensating for the mode selection failure. Experiments on Gaussian mixture targets, a 2D checkerboard, and FLUX.1 text-to-image generation confirm that our theoretical insights carry over to practical settings.

</details>


### [296] [Scalable Uncertainty Quantification for Extreme Weather Forecasting via Empirical Neural Tangent Kernels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02886)
*Jose Marie Antonio Miñoza,Rex Gregor Laylo,Sebastian C. Ibañez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NTK-UQ provides uncertainty quantification for deep-learning weather models using last-layer empirical features, with two architecture- dependent mechanisms: (i) variance collapse due to eigenvalue truncation; (ii) heavy-tailed extreme-event structure exploited via ICA vs SVD. A data-driven rule selects ICA or SVD for feature decomposition. It yields 31-37% sharper 90% coverage intervals vs split conformal prediction and adaptive intervals, with no retraining and inference requiring only one matvec per sample.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To supply reliable, scalable, and architecture-aware uncertainty estimates for deep-learning weather forecasts, addressing the lack of predictive uncertainty in deterministic neural models, particularly for extreme weather events.

Method: Develop a Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK)–based uncertainty quantification (UQ) framework using last-layer empirical features. Theoretical analysis reveals two mechanisms: (1) variance collapse tied to eigenvalue truncation and effective feature-space rank across architectures; (2) decomposition performance influenced by non-Gaussian, heavy-tailed extreme-weather statistics, with ICA leveraging higher-order moments vs SVD's second-order capture. A data-driven rule selects between ICA and SVD based on eigenspectrum concentration. Empirical evaluation across four architectures compares NTK-UQ to split conformal prediction, focusing on interval sharpness and adaptivity. Computational cost: no retraining; inference-time requires a single matrix-vector product per sample.

Result: NTK-UQ achieves 31–37% sharper 90% prediction intervals versus split conformal prediction; it yields adaptive intervals that scale with extreme-event severity; a data-driven ICA/SVD selector correctly prescribes the superior decomposition for all four architectures; inference is efficient (one matvec per sample) with no retraining.

Conclusion: NTK-UQ provides an architecture-sensitive, efficient, and adaptive uncertainty quantification framework for deep learning weather models, leveraging last-layer features and higher-order statistics to better differentiate extreme-event conditions from routine forecasts—outperforming post-hoc baselines and compatible with real-time deployment.

Abstract: Deep learning weather models now match numerical weather prediction accuracy while running orders of magnitude faster, but produce deterministic forecasts without uncertainty estimates, a critical gap for high-stakes decisions during extreme weather events. This paper proposes Neural Tangent Kernel-based uncertainty quantification (NTK-UQ) using last-layer empirical features. Theoretical analysis predicts that UQ quality is architecture-dependent through two mechanisms. First, a variance collapse mechanism explains when UQ fails: when the eigenvalue truncation rank approaches the effective rank of the feature space, the GP correction term consumes nearly all prior variance, destroying discrimination between tropical cyclones and routine conditions; architectures with concentrated spectra (spectral operators) require aggressive truncation ($k \leq 10$), while attention-based models tolerate full-rank computation. Second, decomposition performance depends on the non-Gaussian, heavy-tailed structure of extreme weather: Independent Component Analysis exploits higher-order statistics (kurtosis, negentropy) to isolate heavy-tailed extreme-event features, achieving higher discrimination than singular value decomposition, which captures only second-order variance. A data-driven selection rule chooses ICA or SVD from the feature eigenspectrum concentration ratio, correctly prescribing the superior decomposition for all four evaluated architectures. Compared to split conformal prediction (the natural post-hoc baseline), NTK-UQ achieves 31--37\% sharper prediction intervals at 90\% coverage, and uniquely produces \emph{adaptive} intervals that scale with extreme event severity, which conformal prediction cannot achieve by construction. The framework requires no retraining; inference-time uncertainty requires only a single matrix-vector product per sample.

</details>


### [297] [Hierarchical RBF-KAN and RBF-SKAN Architectures for Multidimensional Function Approximation and Random Field Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02936)
*Mingtao Xia,Qijing Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: We propose hierarchical RBF-KAN and hierarchical RBF-SKAN for high-dimensional function and random-field learning, proving universal approximation, obtaining quantitative error estimates, and showing Wasserstein-2 convergence with empirical validation; the framework partially mitigates the curse of dimensionality via effective dimensionality reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the curse of dimensionality in learning high-dimensional deterministic functions and random fields by integrating radial basis function activation into a hierarchical Kolmogorov–Arnold network (KAN) framework.

Method: Introduce hierarchical radial-basis-function KAN for multidimensional deterministic function approximation and hierarchical radial-basis-function SKAN (stochastic KAN) for random-field learning; establish universal approximation theorems for both; derive quantitative approximation estimates for the deterministic case; prove approximation under Wasserstein-2 metric for random fields; provide empirical demonstrations.

Result: Both architectures are universal approximators. The hierarchical approach yields quantitative error estimates suggesting reduced effective dimensionality and partial mitigation of the curse of dimensionality; the stochastic variant achieves Wasserstein-2 convergence to target random-field models; empirical results confirm capability to learn multivariate functions and random fields.

Conclusion: The proposed hierarchical RBF-KAN/SKAN provide a theoretically grounded and practically effective framework for high-dimensional function and random-field learning, with potential to alleviate dimensionality-related challenges and to guide future empirical and theoretical work.

Abstract: In this manuscript, we propose and analyze hierarchical Kolmogorov--Arnold neural network architectures employing radial basis functions as activation functions for approximating deterministic functions and random field models. Specifically, we develop a hierarchical radial-basis-function Kolmogorov--Arnold network (hierarchical RBF-KAN) for multidimensional deterministic function approximation and a hierarchical radial-basis-function stochastic Kolmogorov--Arnold network (hierarchical RBF-SKAN) for random field learning. From a theoretical perspective, we establish universal approximation results for both architectures. In particular, we derive quantitative approximation estimates for the hierarchical RBF-KAN, showing that the proposed framework has the potential to partially alleviate the curse of dimensionality in learning high-dimensional functions by reducing the effective dimensionality of the approximation problem. Furthermore, we show that the hierarchical RBF-SKAN can approximate random field models under the Wasserstein-2 metric. Empirically, we show that our proposed radial-basis-function-based neural network structure could effectively learn multivariate functions and random field models.

</details>


### [298] [ERP-XTTN: Interpretable Prototype-Guided Cross-Attention for Cross-Subject ERP Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02939)
*Charlotte Genevier Wyman,Leanne Hirshfield*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ERP-XTTN: a cross-attention, prototype-based ERP classifier enabling calibration-free, interpretable cross-subject ERP decoding; small AUROC gaps vs baselines across multiple ERP components and datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for interpretable, calibration-free brain-computer interface classifiers that generalize across subjects, sustaining performance in real deployment with minimal montage.

Method: Propose ERP-XTTN, a cross-attention architecture routing input EEG patches to fixed, data-derived difference-wave prototypes via query-key-only cross-attention (no value projection). Prototypes derived from extrema in training-fold difference wave. Evaluation with LOSO across BNCI Horizon 2020, HRI Cursor, ERP CORE; 3-channel and full montage; baselines EEGNet and xDAWN with Riemannian geometry (xDAWN+RG).

Result: ERP-XTTN achieves mean AUROC gaps of 0.018 at 3 channels and 0.034 at full montage versus best baselines, with two main cost components: temporal-flexibility vs EEGNet and spatial-exploitation vs xDAWN+RG, the latter due to SNR at full montage. The model provides interpretable cross-subject signal routing; errors resemble true positives neurophysiologically, indicating explainable misclassifications. First epoch-level LOSO benchmark on ERP CORE; generalizes across diverse ERP components.

Conclusion: ERP-XTTN offers cross-subject, calibration-free ERP decoding with a transparent, prototype-guided cross-attention mechanism and manageable interpretability cost, demonstrating generalization across diverse ERP components under causal conditions.

Abstract: Interpretable brain-computer interface classifiers that generalize across subjects without calibration remain an open challenge. We test whether prototype-based cross-attention can provide competitive, interpretable event-related potential (ERP) classification under deployment-compatible conditions. We propose ERP-XTTN, a cross-attention architecture that routes input EEG patches to fixed difference-wave prototypes via query-key-only cross-attention with no value projection, so classification depends entirely on attention routing and attention faithfulness is structural rather than post-hoc. Prototypes are derived automatically from extrema in the training-fold difference wave. We evaluate across three public sources (BNCI Horizon 2020, HRI Cursor, and ERP CORE) spanning eight ERP components (ERN, LRP, ErrP, N170, P300, N2pc, MMN, N400), using leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) evaluation with causal filtering at two channel counts (3-channel and full montage), against EEGNet and xDAWN with Riemannian geometry (xDAWN+RG). The mean gap between the best baseline and ERP-XTTN was .018 AUROC at 3 channels and .034 at full montage, arising from two largely distinct sources: a temporal-flexibility cost relative to EEGNet and a spatial-exploitation cost relative to xDAWN+RG, the latter driven by signal-to-noise ratio at full montage. Beyond accuracy, the transparent routing reveals cross-subject signal structure that black-box models cannot: false positives resembled true positives more than true negatives did, indicating that classification errors are neurophysiologically explicable. ERP-XTTN generalizes across diverse ERPs under causal, calibration-free conditions with a small interpretability cost at minimal montages. To our knowledge, this is the first epoch-level LOSO benchmark on ERP CORE.

</details>


### [299] [Outsmarting the Chameleon: Counterfactual Decoupling for Tactical OOD Shifts in Live Streaming Risk Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02946)
*Yiran Qiao,Jing Chen,Jiaqi Xu,Yang Liu,Qiwei Zhong,Xiang Ao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LPCD provides latent-counterfactual decoupling for robust risk assessment under adversarial, tactically evolving OOD shifts in live streaming.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current OOD generalization fails when malicious actors continuously repackage narratives while maintaining stable objectives, making strict assumptions about invariants untenable. A latent causal view with counterfactual reasoning can separate stable intent from shifting tactics to improve robustness.

Method: Propose Latent-Predictive Counterfactual Decoupling (LPCD) as a plug-in framework. It models intent and narrative variation at the latent level, enforces latent counterfactual consistency to align risk with causally stable malicious intent, and applies a lightweight, parameter-free calibration at inference to mitigate tactic-induced shifts.

Result: Empirical evaluation on large-scale industrial datasets and online production traffic shows LPCD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in live streaming risk assessment.

Conclusion: A latent-causal, counterfactual decoupling approach yields robust risk predictions under evolving tactical shifts; LPCD’s latent modeling and inference-time calibration anchor predictions to stable intent, with strong practical performance in real-world settings.

Abstract: Live streaming has emerged as a primary medium for social interaction and digital commerce, yet it is increasingly plagued by sophisticated risks. A fundamental challenge in this domain is \emph{tactical out-of-distribution (OOD) shift}: while malicious actors maintain stable underlying objectives, they continuously redesign narrative packaging to evade detection. Such adversarial shifts expose critical limitations of existing OOD generalization paradigms, whose assumptions are difficult to satisfy in the presence of tightly coupled intent-tactic evolution and ill-defined raw-level counterfactuals.
  In this paper, we tackle this issue from a \emph{latent causal} perspective and propose \underline{L}atent-\underline{P}redictive \underline{C}ounterfactual \underline{D}ecoupling~(LPCD), a plug-in framework for robust live streaming risk assessment. LPCD enables counterfactual reasoning under adversarial tactical re-packaging by modeling intent and narrative variation at the latent level, and enforces \emph{latent counterfactual consistency} to anchor risk prediction on causally stable malicious intent. At inference time, LPCD applies a lightweight, parameter-free calibration to further mitigate tactic-induced distribution shifts. Extensive experiments on large-scale industrial datasets and online production traffic demonstrate that LPCD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, validating its effectiveness in moderating evolving adversarial risks in real-world live streaming. The project page is available at https://qiaoyran.github.io/LiveStreamingRiskAssessment/.

</details>


### [300] [BYORn: Bootstrap Your Own Responses to Defend Large Vision-Language Models Against Backdoor Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02947)
*Ivan Sabolić,Marin Oršić,Josip Šarić,Sven Lončarić*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BYORn is a backdoor-robust fine-tuning framework for autoregressive vision-language models that detects semantically implausible poisoned responses and replaces them with model-generated alternatives, breaking trigger-output correlations; it preserves clean-task performance and resists adaptive attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Supervised fine-tuning for autoregressive V-L models is highly vulnerable to backdoor attacks, with current defenses failing in open-ended generation. A robust defense is needed that can handle generative settings and adaptive adversaries.

Method: Identify poisoned, semantically implausible target responses given image-text inputs and a pretrained model. Dynamically replace such responses with alternative responses generated by the model, thereby decoupling triggers from targets. The training objective approximates the gradient of the empirical risk upper bound over the clean distribution.

Result: Empirically improves robustness to backdoor attacks while preserving clean-task performance, establishing a new trade-off frontier between generalization and attack success. Effective against adaptive attacks designed to bypass the defense.

Conclusion: BYORn offers a practical defense for backdoor robustness in open-ended vision-language generation, maintaining performance on clean data while limiting backdoor efficacy and with resilience to adaptive attack strategies.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning is the predominant approach for adapting autoregressive vision-language models to downstream tasks. Recent work has shown that this paradigm is highly vulnerable to backdoor attacks, and that existing defenses are ineffective in open-ended generation settings. In response, we propose BYORn, a backdoor-robust fine-tuning framework motivated by the observation that poisoned target responses are often semantically implausible given the corresponding image-text inputs and a pretrained model. BYORn identifies such misaligned responses and dynamically replaces them with alternative responses generated by the model, thereby breaking the correlation between triggers and target outputs. The resulting objective gradient corresponds to the gradient of the empirical estimate of the population risk upper bound over the clean data distribution. Empirically, BYORn consistently improves robustness to backdoor attacks while preserving clean-task performance, establishing a new trade-off frontier between generalization and attack success rate. Finally, we demonstrate that BYORn remains effective against adaptive attacks specifically designed to circumvent the proposed defense.

</details>


### [301] [From Non-Convex to Strongly Convex: Curvature-Adaptive FTPL for Online Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02948)
*Moses Charikar,Chirag Pabbaraju,Ambuj Tewari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FTPL can be curvature-adaptive in online non-convex Lipschitz settings by using a data-driven, time-varying perturbation scale. It retains the O(√T) regret in general and achieves O(log T) regret when cumulative curvature grows linearly, with matching lower bounds for prescribed curvature sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Curvature adaptivity in online optimization: bridge between worst-case non-convex regret and fast rates under curvature, leveraging approximate offline optimization.

Method: Replace fixed FTPL perturbation with a TV-scale chosen by past information via a simple tuning rule for the scale. Use an approximate offline-optimization oracle to enable the non-convex setting; event-driven scale updates aim to track cumulative curvature without prior knowledge.

Result: Upper bounds: O(√T) regret for arbitrary non-convex Lipschitz losses; faster rates as cumulative curvature grows; O(log T) regret under linear growth of cumulative curvature with accurate oracle calls. Matching lower bounds established for one-dimensional convex losses, showing intrinsic tradeoff between worst-case non-convex regret and curvature-driven rates.

Conclusion: Curvature-adaptive FTPL extends online learning to non-convex Lipschitz losses, achieving near-optimal regret across regimes with a simple, data-driven perturbation scheme and fundamental lower bounds confirming the tradeoffs.

Abstract: Curvature adaptivity is a classical theme in online optimization: for convex Lipschitz losses, adaptive methods interpolate between the optimal $O(\sqrt{T})$ regret for general convex losses and $O(\log T)$ regret under strong convexity. Recent work has shown that Follow-the-Perturbed-Leader (FTPL) achieves optimal $O(\sqrt{T})$ regret even for online non-convex Lipschitz losses, assuming access to an approximate offline-optimization oracle, but these guarantees do not exploit curvature. We show that FTPL can be made curvature-adaptive in the non-convex setting, without knowing in advance how curvature will accumulate over time. Our algorithm replaces the fixed perturbation scale of standard FTPL with a time-varying scale chosen using only past information. We give a simple follow-the-leader tuning rule for this scale and show that it competes, up to constants, with the best choice in hindsight. The resulting method achieves $O(\sqrt{T})$ regret for arbitrary non-convex Lipschitz losses and improves as cumulative curvature grows; with sufficiently accurate oracle calls, it achieves $O(\log T)$ regret when cumulative curvature grows linearly, which includes the classical strongly convex regime. We complement these upper bounds with matching lower bounds for prescribed cumulative-curvature sequences, already for one-dimensional convex losses, showing that the tradeoff between worst-case non-convex regret and curvature-driven fast rates is intrinsic.

</details>


### [302] [KForge: LLM-Driven Cross-Platform Kernel Generation for AI Accelerators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02963)
*Taras Sereda,Burak Bartan,Ankita Nayak,Tom St. John,Natalie Serrino,Zain Asgar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KForge is a cross-platform LLM-driven framework that uses two collaborating agents to iteratively synthesize and optimize kernels for heterogeneous accelerators. It demonstrates modest gains on NVIDIA and substantial speedups on Intel backends by leveraging functional correctness and performance-driven refinement, including operator fusion and mixed-precision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Heterogeneous accelerator environments demand high-performance kernels across multiple backends and programming models. Manual kernel development is time-consuming and scalable poor; while LLM-based kernel generation shows promise, cross-backend generalization and low-level fidelity remain challenges. A collaborative, iterative framework could automate and accelerate kernel production while adapting to backend-specific constraints.

Method: KForge employs an iterative refinement loop with two LLM agents: a generation agent that creates and refines kernels using compilation and correctness feedback, and a performance-analysis agent that interprets profiling data and emits recommendations to steer subsequent synthesis. The loop alternates functional passes (ensuring correctness) and optimization passes (closing performance gaps). Evaluation targets two backends: NVIDIA B200 (2.12% end-to-end throughput gain vs TensorRT-LLM on gpt-oss-20b) and Intel Arc B580 (5.13x geometric mean speedup over PyTorch eager/torch.compile on 37 GEMM + tail-ops workloads from KernelBench Level 2), driven by operator fusion and mixed-precision execution.

Result: NVIDIA B200: 2.12% improvement in end-to-end throughput over TensorRT-LLM on gpt-oss-20b. Intel Arc B580: 5.13x geometric mean speedup over the faster of PyTorch eager and torch.compile on KernelBench Level 2 workloads (37 GEMM + tail-ops).

Conclusion: The two-agent, iterative refinement framework effectively generates cross-backend kernels that yield measurable performance gains, illustrating the viability of LLM-assisted, automated kernel synthesis for heterogeneous accelerator stacks and highlighting optimization strategies such as operator fusion and mixed-precision.

Abstract: Production inference increasingly targets a heterogeneous mix of accelerators. Agentic pipelines interleave reasoning, tool calls, and multi-agent coordination, each with distinct compute and memory profiles. For optimal efficiency, each stage should run on the accelerator best suited to it. This creates a systems challenge: each pipeline now requires high-performance kernels across a growing set of hardware backends and programming models. Writing these kernels by hand is time-consuming, demands deep low-level expertise, and does not scale as kernel complexity grows. Recently, Large Language Models (LLMs) have been leveraged for automatic kernel generation, but challenges in low-level code generation and cross-backend generalization persist. We present KForge, a cross-platform framework built around an iterative refinement loop driven by two collaborating LLM-based agents: a generation agent that produces and progressively refines kernels using compilation and correctness feedback, and a performance-analysis agent that interprets profiling data, from programmatic APIs to GUI-based tools, and emits recommendations that steer the next round of synthesis. The loop alternates between functional passes, which drive a candidate to correctness, and optimization passes, which close the performance gap to hand-tuned baselines. We evaluate KForge on two backends with very different baseline reference availability. On NVIDIA B200, KForge achieves a 2.12$\%$ improvement in end-to-end throughput compared to TensorRT-LLM on the gpt-oss-20b inference speed benchmark. On Intel Arc B580, KForge generates Triton kernels achieving a 5.13$\times$ geometric mean speedup over the faster of PyTorch eager and torch.compile on 37 GEMM + tail-ops workloads from KernelBench Level 2, primarily via operator fusion and mixed-precision execution.

</details>


### [303] [Neural Networks Provably Learn Spectral Representations for Group Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02993)
*Jianliang He,Leda Wang,Fengzhuo Zhang,Siyu Chen,Zhuoran Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-layer nets solving a group-product task reveal structured Fourier-domain representations. Gradient flow becomes Riemannian gradient ascent on a representation-theoretic energy, driving neurons to a single irreducible representation with cross-layer Fourier alignment. For Abelian groups, random initialization yields uniform diversification across nontrivial reps and Haar-uniform phases, approximating a majority-vote indicator. Both phase alignment and representation competition converge exponentially.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain how structured internal representations emerge during training through group representation theory, and reveal low-rank compression phenomena in matrix-valued group representations.

Method: Lift projected gradient flow to the Fourier domain and analyze it as a Riemannian gradient ascent on a representation-theoretic energy functional. Prove almost-sure convergence to a single irreducible representation per neuron, rank-one alignment of cross-layer Fourier coefficients, and exponential convergence rates. Provide a complete population-level description for Abelian groups.

Result: Offers a representation-theoretic account of feature learning and uncovers a novel low-rank compression in matrix-valued group representations. For Abelian groups, gives a full population-level picture: random initialization promotes uniform diversification across nontrivial representations and induces Haar-uniform phases, approximating the indicator via majority voting.

Conclusion: Links neural-network training dynamics to representation theory, explaining emergence of structured, low-rank features and phase alignment; highlights exponential convergence and a clear mechanism for representation competition, with a complete Abelian-group characterization.

Abstract: Understanding how structured internal structure emerges during neural network training is central to the study of deep learning. We investigate this phenomenon through the group composition task, where a two-layer neural network is trained to predict $g_1 \star g_2$ for elements of a finite group $G$. By lifting the projected gradient flow to the Fourier domain, we demonstrate that the training dynamics are governed by a Riemannian gradient ascent on a representation-theoretic energy functional. We prove that, under random initialization, this flow drives each neuron to converge almost surely toward a single irreducible representation, while the cross-layer Fourier coefficients achieve a rotational rank-one alignment. This framework provides a representation-theoretic account of feature learning and characterizes a novel low-rank compression phenomenon for matrix-valued group representations. Moreover, for Abelian groups, we provide a complete population-level description: random initialization promotes uniform diversification across nontrivial representations and induces Haar-uniform phases, jointly approximating the indicator via a majority-vote mechanism. We further prove that both phase alignment and representation competition emerge with exponential convergence rates.

</details>


### [304] [CoughSense: Five-Class Respiratory Disease Classification via Whisper Encoder Fine-Tuning and Dual-Encoder Cross-Attention Fusion with Balanced Contrastive Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02998)
*Nikhil Vincent*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoughSense uses a Whisper-based backbone and active-frame QKV attention pooling to classify coughs into five classes on a smartphone, achieving macro-F1 ~0.817, AUC 0.941, and 5-class balanced accuracy ~82.3%; a dual-encoder extension with OPERA-CT improves to 85.4%. It addresses class imbalance and domain shift with multiple training strategies; active-frame pooling is the key contributor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for multi-class, smartphone-based cough screening beyond binary COVID detection; handle short audio segments and cross-dataset domain shifts.

Method: Backbone: OpenAI Whisper encoder; active-frame QKV attention pooling restricting to first 200 of 1500 tokens; training strategies: WeightedRandomSampler, SpecAugment, Balanced Mixup with forced minority pairing, supervised contrastive auxiliary loss, FiLM conditioning, gradient-reversal domain adaptation. Architectures: dual-encoder fusing Whisper with OPERA-CT via cross-attention.

Result: CoughSense (Whisper-tiny, 8.6M) achieved 82.3% balanced accuracy (macro-F1 0.817, AUC 0.941) on five-class classification; all classes had 74% recall, four of five had ≥80% recall; dual-encoder reached 85.4% balanced accuracy. Ablation shows active-frame pooling contributes 5.1 points.

Conclusion: Active-frame QKV pooling enables effective short-audio classification using a Whisper backbone; multi-dataset training and dual-encoder fusion yield robust five-class cough classification, suitable for smartphone-based respiratory screening.

Abstract: Automated cough analysis offers a path to low-cost respiratory screening, but most existing work stops at binary COVID-19 detection. A practical tool needs to tell apart several respiratory conditions from one cough recording on a consumer smartphone. We present CoughSense, a system that sorts cough recordings into five classes. These are healthy, COVID-19, asthma or respiratory condition, bronchitis, and pneumonia. We aggregated 18,301 recordings from four public datasets (Coswara, CoughVID, Virufy, and the West China Hospital Pediatric Cough Dataset) and used the OpenAI Whisper encoder as a pretrained backbone for cough disease classification. The main contribution is active-frame QKV attention pooling, which restricts attention to the first 200 of 1500 encoder tokens. This avoids the silence-dilution problem that arises because a 3-second cough fills only 150 tokens of Whisper's 30-second input window. Other training parts handle the 19 to 1 class imbalance and the four-dataset domain shift. These include WeightedRandomSampler, SpecAugment, Balanced Mixup with forced minority pairing, a supervised contrastive auxiliary loss, FiLM symptom conditioning, and gradient-reversal domain adaptation. A dual-encoder model fuses Whisper with the OPERA-CT respiratory foundation model through cross-attention. CoughSense (Whisper-tiny, 8.6M parameters) reached 82.3 percent balanced accuracy on five-fold cross-validation (macro-F1 of 0.817, AUC of 0.941). It beat an ImageNet-pretrained EfficientNet-B2 by 11.1 points and a ViT trained from scratch by 29.6 points. All five classes passed 74 percent recall and four of five passed 80 percent. The dual-encoder model reached 85.4 percent balanced accuracy. Active-frame pooling is the largest single contributor across all ablation components at 5.1 points, which should help any short-audio task using Whisper as a backbone.

</details>


### [305] [How Quantization Changes Interpretable Features: A Sparse Autoencoder Analysis of Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03002)
*Evan Duan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Feature-level interpretability breaks under quantization: many SAE features survive quantization in a graded manner, while others degrade or blur; task metrics can hide this damage; a large share of features overlapped with pruning damage, suggesting a shared vulnerability and need for feature-level audits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Given growing reliance on interpretable features for safety, steering, and audits, it is essential to test whether interpretability transfers when full-precision models are quantized for deployment.

Method: Use a frozen sparse autoencoder (SAE) as a fixed measurement basis. Encode full-precision and round-to-nearest (RTN) quantized activations for identical tokens and quantify per-feature survival via Pearson correlation. Sweep bit-widths from INT8 to INT4 on Pythia-70M and Gemma-2-2B.

Result: Feature survival is graded rather than binary: 62.4% of active SAE features survive at INT6 on Pythia-70M; 51.3% survive at INT6 on Gemma-2-2B, with most non-survivors blurred. Survival is predictable from full-precision statistics (cross-validated AUCs 0.92–0.97); peak activation is the strongest marginal predictor. Task metrics can miss damage (INT7 can improve perplexity while degrading 18.7% of features). Quantization and magnitude pruning damage show strong overlap (Jaccard 0.79–0.86; Spearman 0.98).

Conclusion: Behavioral parity alone does not guarantee transfer of interpretability to quantized deployments; compression can induce feature-level damage, motivating feature-level audits during compression.

Abstract: Quantization is a standard path to deploying large language models, and a quantized model is typically judged acceptable when its perplexity or downstream accuracy stays close to the full-precision original. Whether the model still computes in the same way, or whether the interpretable features identified in the full-precision model survive weight rounding, is rarely tested, even as safety audits and steering interventions increasingly rely on those features. We ask whether sparse autoencoder (SAE) features extracted from a dense full-precision model remain faithful once that model is quantized. Using a frozen SAE as a fixed measurement basis, we encode full-precision and round-to-nearest (RTN) quantized activations on identical tokens and quantify per-feature survival by Pearson correlation, sweeping bit-widths from INT8 to INT4 on Pythia-70M and Gemma-2-2B. We find that feature survival is graded: features degrade systematically rather than failing all at once, with 62.4 percent of active features surviving at INT6 on Pythia-70M and 51.3 percent surviving at INT6 on Gemma-2-2B, and with most non-survivors blurred rather than destroyed. Survival is predictable from full-precision statistics alone, with cross-validated AUCs of 0.92 to 0.97 and peak activation as the strongest marginal predictor. Critically, task metrics can miss this damage: on Gemma-2-2B, INT7 improves perplexity while degrading 18.7 percent of features. Finally, quantization and matched-perplexity magnitude pruning damage strongly overlapping feature sets, with Jaccard overlap of 0.79 to 0.86 and damage-score Spearman correlation of 0.98, suggesting a shared mode of compression-induced vulnerability. These results show that behavioral parity is insufficient evidence that interpretability findings transfer to quantized deployments, motivating feature-level audits of compression.

</details>


### [306] [Exact equivariance, kept through training, buys zero-shot generalisation across the symmetry group](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03003)
*Hongbo Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An equivariant latent world model with an orthogonal group action yields exact invariance of one-step prediction error across the group and invariant closed-loop trajectories, enabling accurate generalization from a limited orientation slice and outperforming non-equivariant baselines in 2D/3D tasks with robust scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether incorporating group-equivariant encoders and predictors into latent world models confers symmetry-preserving dynamics, leading to loss and planning invariances, improved generalization, and robustness to common training heuristics.

Method: Use an equivariant encoder E and an equivariant predictor f implementing an orthogonal representation ρ(g) of group G. Train with Muon/AdamW + EMA + VICReg on tasks that incorporate SE(2)/SE(3) symmetry; evaluate one-step relMSE across orientations, cross-horizon H-fold rollouts, and closed-loop planning. Compare against a non-equivariant baseline. Stress-test invariance against augmentation, brute-force scaling, and soft-equivariance.

Result: One-step prediction error remains flat across the full group; end-to-end training preserves exact invariance even with optimizers and during real runs. Non-equivariant baselines fit only a slice and exhibit severe out-of-distribution errors (VN×1.00 vs baseline×13.8 in 2D, ×17.2 in 3D, ×157 over SE(3)); equivariant model shows 4.5–7.4× smaller errors.
