Closed-loop trajectories under a matching equivariant planner are exactly transformed by ρ(g); invariance holds in 2D SO(2) and is statistically flat in 3D SE(3). H-fold rollouts remain flat (×1.00, ≤2e-7).

Conclusion: The paper provides strong empirical and theoretical support that equivariant latent models preserve symmetries of the world dynamics, yielding exact loss and trajectory invariances, robust to standard training techniques and scalable to higher horizons; however, task-success metrics and planner-free invariance are left for future work.

Abstract: A latent world model built from an equivariant encoder $E$ and an equivariant predictor $f$ inherits a provable symmetry of its training loss: when the world's dynamics genuinely carries a group $G$ acting on latents by an orthogonal representation $ρ(g)$, the one-step prediction relMSE is exactly invariant across the whole group, so fitting the dynamics on a restricted slice of orientations mathematically determines it on the entire orbit (jǔ yī fǎn sān). We verify this end-to-end at laptop scale (CPU/MPS, fully seeded). [A] The symmetry survives a real Muon/AdamW + EMA + VICReg run -- composed encode-then-predict residual $\sim 10^{-6}$ after optimisation, not just at initialisation, and under any optimiser. [B] One-step error is flat to five digits across the group, while a same-hypothesis-class non-equivariant baseline fits the slice but breaks out-of-distribution (VN $\times 1.00$ vs baseline $\times 13.8$ in 2D, $\times 17.2$ in 3D, $\times 157$ over the full $\mathrm{SE}(3)$ ladder), with the equivariant model $4.5$-$7.4\times$ smaller. [C] The same isometry argument lifts to closed loop: under a matching equivariant planner the control trajectory at orientation $g$ is exactly $ρ(g)$ applied to the seen one, so closed-loop error is invariant across the group -- float-floor-exact in 2D/$\mathrm{SO}(2)$ on real PushT and statistically flat in 3D/$\mathrm{SE}(3)$ (disjoint 95% CIs). We stress-test the prior against Sutton's Bitter Lesson: augmentation, brute-force scale, and soft-equivariance each close at most the across-group task metric, never the float-floor exactness. Because equivariance is closed under composition, the $H$-fold rollout stays flat ($\times 1.00$, $\le 2\times 10^{-7}$) at every horizon, while the baseline's residual compounds with $H$. Out of scope: task-success sweeps, planner-free invariance, and scaling.

</details>


### [307] [MOSAIC: Efficient Mixture-of-Agent Scheduling via Adaptive Aggregation and Inference Concurrency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03014)
*Saptarshi Mitra,Yifan Zhang,Rachid Karami,Phyo Pyae Moe Aung,Nazmul Takbir,Sreetama Sarkar,Souvik Kundu,Sitao Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MOSAIC introduces an ILP-based expert-placement and per-worker prompt-assignment scheduler for mixture-of-agents (MoA) workloads, coupled with a confidence-aware adaptive aggregation to skip heavy final LLMs when consensus can be reached from inter-expert agreement. It achieves substantial speedups with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoA systems on limited GPU resources suffer from skewed expert demand due to skill-based routing, extreme variability in generation lengths when combining instruction-tuned LLMs with long-reasoning models, and heavy load imbalances that degrade throughput. A scalable, efficient scheduler is needed to reduce GPU idling and balance workloads.

Method: Two main components: (1) An ILP-based scheduler that jointly optimizes expert placement and per-worker prompt assignment using offline-profiled costs; it replicates heavyweight reasoning experts across workers while pinning lightweight ones. (2) Confidence-aware adaptive aggregation that leverages inter-expert agreement to decide when the final aggregator LLM can be bypassed for consensus queries.

Result: On a 4-GPU system, MOSAIC yields up to 2.5x speedup at the expert-stage, 4.23x at the aggregator-stage, and 1.7–2.3x end-to-end, with accuracy within 0.1 percentage point of the baseline.

Conclusion: MOSAIC demonstrates effective acceleration of MoA workloads with minimal accuracy loss by combining ILP-based scheduling with adaptive aggregation. The approach hinges on offline profiling and may need extensions to dynamic workloads and larger-scale deployments; potential future directions include reducing profiling overhead, handling more heterogeneous hardware, and exploring robustness under varying query mixes.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Agents (MoA) systems improve reasoning accuracy by routing each query to multiple expert LLMs and aggregating their outputs. Efficiently executing this workload on limited GPU resources has bottlenecks. Skill-based routing creates skewed expert demand, and combining instruction-tuned LLMs with long-reasoning models results in extreme variability in generation lengths. Consequently, traditional scheduling strategies suffer from significant GPU idling and throughput collapse due to load imbalances. We present MOSAIC, a scheduling framework to accelerate MoA workloads. First, we formulate an Integer Linear Program (ILP) based scheduler that jointly optimizes expert placement and per-worker prompt assignment from offline-profiled costs, replicating reasoning experts across workers while pinning lightweight ones. Second, MOSAIC uses confidence-aware adaptive aggregation, leveraging inter-expert agreement to bypass the heavy final aggregator LLM for consensus queries. In our 4-GPU system, MOSAIC achieves up to 2.5x expert-stage, 4.23x aggregator-stage and 1.7~2.3x end-to-end speedups over the baseline scheduler, while matching accuracy within 0.1pp.

</details>


### [308] [ConTraIRL: Factorized Contrastive Abstractions for Transferable IRL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03017)
*Yikang Gui,Bikramjit Banerjee,Prashant Doshi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ConTraIRL introduces Factorized Contrastive Abstractions for Transferable IRL, learning separated latent representations for environment dynamics and task goals via dual encoders and contrastive losses with temporal alignment, enabling compositional reward transfer to unseen dynamics-goal combinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IRL reward transfer is unreliable when generalizing to unseen combinations of environment dynamics and task goals, necessitating representations that decouple these factors.

Method: Train a dual-encoder architecture that maps observations into separate dynamics and goal latent spaces. Use a dual contrastive objective with temporal alignment: the dynamics encoder learns goal-invariant structure, while the goal encoder captures dynamics-invariant features.

Result: In continuous control benchmarks, ConTraIRL achieves effective few-shot transfer to unseen dynamics-goal pairings, improving sample efficiency and reward recovery compared to transfer IRL baselines.

Conclusion: Factorized latent representations for dynamics and goals enable compositional reward transfer, addressing generalization gaps in IRL across unseen dynamics-goal combinations.

Abstract: Reward transfer in Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) is unreliable when policies must generalize to unseen combinations of environment dynamics and task goals. We propose Factorized Contrastive Abstractions for Transferable IRL (ConTraIRL), a framework that enables compositional reward transfer by learning decoupled latent representations of these two factors. ConTraIRL uses a dual-encoder architecture that maps observations into separate dynamics and goal latent spaces, trained with a dual contrastive objective. Temporal alignment encourages the dynamics encoder to learn goal-invariant structure, while the goal encoder captures dynamics-invariant features. This factorization supports reward inference under recombined dynamics-goal settings. Experiments on continuous control benchmarks demonstrate effective few-shot transfer to unseen dynamics-goal pairings, improving sample efficiency and reward recovery over transfer IRL baselines.

</details>


### [309] [Will Accurate Fields Mislead Photonic Design? FromGlobal Accuracy to Port Readout](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03038)
*Yitian Zhang,Yonghong chen,Youming Chen,Yiyang Li,Xing Zhe,Renhe Lu,Shaolin Liao,Yuzhe Ma,Zhong Guan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Propagation-aligned neural operator PaNO improves surrogate fidelity for photonic design by aligning latent representations with local boundary and propagation structure, resolving mismatches between global field accuracy and localized port readouts; PaNO and its output-aware variant PaNO-R2 outperform a global-field surrogate on a 3x3 MMI benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global complex-field error does not necessarily correlate with design-relevant port metrics; field-to-design chain involves propagation profiles and output-window aggregation that can misrank candidates.

Method: Introduce Field/Mediator/Readout view; propose PaNO, a propagation-aligned neural operator with latent state organization around local boundary structure, transverse modal content, axial propagation, and cross-mode interaction; add PaNO-R2 as an output-aware residual feedback variant focusing on port region.

Result: On a 15-wavelength tunable 3x3 MMI benchmark with 4608 held-out fields, PaNO reduces port-power error from 0.2018 to 0.0739 (slightly higher cMAE); PaNO-R2 achieves the best cMAE, propagation-profile error, output-profile error, and port-power error, reducing NeurOLight's port-power and output-profile errors by 72.7% and 72.5%.

Conclusion: Global field accuracy alone is insufficient for design-relevant readout fidelity; propagation-aligned surrogates PaNO and PaNO-R2 yield superior port-level performance for MMI-based devices.

Abstract: Neural field surrogates can accelerate photonic design loops, but a surrogate that looks accurate in global field error can still mis-rank candidate devices when the final decision depends on localized output-port readouts. This risk is acute in propagation-dominated MMI splitters and couplers, where port power, splitting, phase, and coupling are determined by accumulated modal interference and output-window aggregation rather than by average field similarity alone. We study this field-to-design mismatch through a Field/Mediator/Readout view that separates dense complex-field error from propagation-profile and output-window errors before port aggregation. To align the surrogate with this chain, we propose PaNO, a propagation-aligned neural operator that keeps the full-field prediction interface while organizing latent states around local boundary structure, transverse modal content, axial propagation, and cross-mode interaction. We also evaluate PaNO-R2, an output-aware feedback variant for residual field components near the port region. On a 15-wavelength tunable $3{\times}3$ MMI benchmark with 4608 held-out fields, PaNO lowers NeurOLight's port-power error from 0.2018 to 0.0739 despite slightly higher cMAE, showing that global field accuracy alone is not sufficient for design-relevant readout fidelity. PaNO-R2 attains the best cMAE, propagation-profile error, output-profile error, and port-power error, reducing NeurOLight's port-power and output-profile errors by 72.7\% and 72.5\%.

</details>


### [310] [What Do Students Learn? A Feature-Level Analysis of Dark Knowledge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03052)
*Seungu Kang,Songkuk Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KD acts as a regularizer that prunes low-frequency, sample-specific features; dataset-level confusion encodes structural knowledge akin to teacher's dark knowledge; Confusion Distillation (CD) is a teacher-free self-distillation using evolving confusion as soft targets; competitive on CIFAR-100 with ResNet-34/50, surpassing CS-KD and PS-KD by 1.2%, with lower computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how student representations emerge under KD and whether dataset-level information can substitute teacher signals; to develop a computationally efficient self-distillation technique leveraging the model's own confusion patterns.

Method: Use Interaction Tensor framework to analyze student feature learning; observe regularization effect and feature pruning; reveal confusion matrix structure; design CD to use evolving confusion patterns as dynamic soft targets; evaluate on CIFAR-100 with ResNet-34/50; compare to CS-KD and PS-KD.

Result: KD acts as a regularizer pruning low-frequency, sample-specific features; dataset confusion matrix contains structural information analogous to dark knowledge; CD matches or exceeds self-distillation baselines by about 1.2% and is more efficient than standard KD.

Conclusion: Dataset-level confusion information is valuable for self-distillation; CD offers a competitive, computationally efficient alternative to KD; potential applicability beyond CIFAR-100 and standard CNNs; invites further study of confusion-based targets.

Abstract: Knowledge Distillation (KD) is a powerful tool for model compression, yet the precise mechanisms by which student models acquire feature representations remain underexplored. In this work, we analyze student feature learning using the Interaction Tensor framework. Our analysis reveals that effective KD acts as a regularizer that prunes low-frequency, sample-specific features, encouraging the student to rely on a compact set of highly reusable features. Crucially, we observe that the dataset-level confusion matrix contains structural information analogous to the teacher's "Dark Knowledge." Leveraging this insight, we propose Confusion Distillation (CD), a teacher-free self-distillation method that utilizes the model's own evolving confusion patterns as dynamic soft targets. CD achieves competitive performance on ResNet-34 and ResNet-50 for CIFAR-100, outperforming existing self-distillation methods like CS-KD and PS-KD by 1.2% while offering a computationally efficient alternative to standard KD.

</details>


### [311] [Rethinking Molecular Text Representations for LLMs: An Empirical Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03057)
*Arun Raja,Garrett M. Morris,Kian Ming A. Chai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A benchmark shows representation choice critically shapes LLM performance in chemistry tasks; no universal winner. CML is best overall, followed by MolJSON, InChI, then canonical SMILES. Representations dominate task outcomes: structured text for structural tasks; IUPAC for semantic tasks and molecule retrieval; SMILES often suboptimal despite pretraining prevalence. Chemistry-specialized models lean on SMILES, losing on structured representations. Using LLM-as-judge, IUPAC yields most correct generations. Tokenization, probes, and attention analyses reveal representations are encoded differently, with structured forms needing broader attention. The study argues against representation-invariant evaluation and advocates task-aware representation routing for LLM-based chemistry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine which molecular representations best enable LLMs to perform a suite of chemistry tasks, testing whether representation choices impact performance and whether evaluation should be invariant to representation.

Method: Systematic benchmark across nine representations and eight chemical tasks, evaluating 16 LLMs from five model families (including reasoning/non-reasoning variants, chemistry-specialized LLMs, and closed frontier models). Assess performance across tasks, use LLM-as-judge for generation quality, and conduct mechanistic analyses (tokenization audits, linear probes, attention) to understand encoding of representations.

Result: Performance is strongly representation-dependent with no single winner. CML is the best overall, MolJSON and InChI follow, then canonical SMILES. Explicit structured text representations dominate structural tasks; IUPAC wins semantic tasks and molecule retrieval for all models; SMILES variants are rarely optimal. Chemistry-specialized models perform well with SMILES but degrade with structured representations, indicating SMILES-only evaluation may overfit. LLM-as-judge finds IUPAC yields the highest correct generations. Mechanistic analyses show representations are encoded differently; structured representations demand higher molecular-span attention. Overall, results argue against representation-invariant evaluation and support task-aware representation routing.

Conclusion: Evaluation of LLM-based chemistry should be task-aware and representation-routing-driven rather than assuming a universal representation; selecting representations per task yields better performance and generalization.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for molecular tasks, but it remains unclear which molecular representation to use. We present a systematic benchmark evaluating LLM molecular competence across nine representations and eight chemical tasks. We benchmark 16 LLMs across five model families, including reasoning and non-reasoning variants, chemistry-specialized LLMs, and closed frontier models. Performance is strongly representation-dependent and no single representation wins across tasks, though CML is the best, followed by MolJSON, InChI, and then canonical SMILES. Explicit structured text representations (CML and MolJSON) dominate structural tasks; IUPAC dominates semantic tasks, winning molecule retrieval for all 16 LLMs; and SMILES variants are rarely optimal despite their prevalence in pretraining. Chemistry-specialized models perform well with SMILES at the cost of large degradations with structured text representations, suggesting SMILES-only evaluation rewards specialization that does not generalize. Using LLM-as-a-judge, we find that IUPAC produces the highest fraction of correct molecule generations. A mechanistic study via tokenization audits, linear probes and attention shows that representations are encoded differently inside the model; for example, structured representations require higher attention across the molecular span. Our results argue against representation-invariant evaluation and motivate task-aware representation routing for LLM-based chemistry.

</details>


### [312] [Learn When and Where to Connect: Adaptive Virtual Nodes for Dynamic Message Passing on Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03068)
*Jaejun Lee,Joyce Jiyoung Whang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive on-demand virtual nodes for MPNNs (MAVN) with a dual-scoring mechanism, enabling flexible, layer-wise connectivity and end-to-end learning; achieves strong empirical gains and can simulate arbitrary node–VN patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VN-based MPNNs impose rigid constraints (same number of VNs per node, fixed pre-MPNN connections, and independent node–VN edges), limiting expressiveness and hindering learning of message passing.

Method: MAVN introduces a pool of candidate VNs and, at each layer, selects a subset to connect to nonempty node subsets. Connections are determined by a dual-perspective scoring mechanism that captures both node preferences for VNs and VN preferences for nodes. VNs can be introduced on demand as node representations evolve, enabling end-to-end training. A theoretical result shows MAVN can simulate any node–VN connectivity pattern.

Result: Empirical evaluation on nine real-world datasets shows MAVN consistently improves backbone MPNNs, with gains up to 46.5% over backbones and outperforms baselines.

Conclusion: MAVN provides a universal, dynamic VN framework for MPNNs, achieving flexible connectivity, end-to-end trainability, theoretical universality, and strong empirical performance across tasks.

Abstract: While Virtual Nodes (VNs) are often utilized in Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) to facilitate effective message passing, existing VN-based methods have limitations, such as constraining all nodes to connect to the same number of VNs, fixing the connections before applying MPNNs, and connecting a node to a VN independently of the other nodes that connect to the same VN. We propose MAVN, an end-to-end differentiable MPNN framework that allows non-constrained connections between nodes and VNs and dynamically introduces VNs on demand in response to evolving node representations across layers. Specifically, MAVN learns to adaptively determine when (at which layer) and where (to which nodes) to introduce and connect VNs based on the relative importance of connections. From a pool of candidate VNs, MAVN selects the necessary VNs in each layer, where each selected VN is connected to a nonempty subset of nodes, guided by a dual-perspective scoring mechanism that jointly captures the nodes' preferences for VNs and the VNs' preferences for nodes. We theoretically prove that for any node-VN connectivity pattern, there exists a set of MAVN's parameters that can simulate the pattern. Experiments on nine real-world datasets demonstrate that MAVN consistently improves the performance of backbone MPNNs, achieving up to 46.5% improvement over the backbones and outperforms the baselines.

</details>


### [313] [ASymPO: Asymmetric-Scale Policy Optimization for Asynchronous LLM Post-Training Without Behavior Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03070)
*Zehua Liu,Yuxuan Yao,Xiaojin Fu,Tao Zhong,Mingxuan Yuan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ASymPO and SPO offer current-policy-only objectives for asynchronous RL in language-model post-training, normalizing token losses by current-average NLL to stabilize training without behavior-policy logs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate distribution drift caused by stale responses in asynchronous RL post-training without requiring token-aligned, versioned behavior log-probabilities.

Method: ASymPO normalizes each response's token loss by its current-average token negative log-probability, restoring zero-sum balance and preserving a learning signal; SPO is a fixed negative-scaling baseline. Both are evaluated on asynchronous mathematical reasoning post-training using current-policy-only objectives.

Result: Empirical evaluation on asynchronous mathematical reasoning tasks shows that current-policy-only objectives can stabilize learning, restore balance across samples, and preserve a nonzero learning signal without behavior-policy probabilities; ASymPO provides better balance and signal; SPO serves as a baseline.

Conclusion: ASymPO and SPO enable current-policy-only stabilization for asynchronous RL in language-model post-training. ASymPO achieves normalized token losses to restore zero-sum balance and preserve learning signal; efficiency and applicability discussed with potential extension to broader LM post-training settings.

Abstract: Asynchronous reinforcement learning can improve language-model post-training throughput by decoupling response generation from policy optimization, but stale responses introduce distribution drift. Standard behavior-corrected methods control this drift with behavior-policy probabilities, importance ratios, or clipping, which requires token-aligned, versioned, and numerically consistent behavior log-probabilities across rollout and learner systems. We ask whether asynchronous group-relative RL can instead be stabilized using only current-policy probabilities. We identify a scale-imbalance failure mode: when stale responses are evaluated under the current policy, positive and negative loss terms can appear at different negative log-probability scales, so zero-sum advantages no longer imply balanced loss contributions. We propose Asymmetric-Scale Policy Optimization (ASymPO), which normalizes each response's token loss by its current average token negative log-probability. ASymPO requires no behavior-policy probabilities, restores response-level zero-sum balance, and preserves a nonzero learning signal. We also introduce Scaled Policy Optimization (SPO), a fixed negative-scaling baseline, and evaluate both current-policy-only objectives in asynchronous mathematical reasoning post-training.

</details>


### [314] [Efficient Hyperparameter Optimization for LLM Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03073)
*Minping Chen,Bowen Xiao,Du Liang,Chuxuan Zeng,Zeyi Wen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Joint Fidelity HPO (JF-HPO) optimizes both model size and training budget for RL with LLMs, using a proxy model, early stopping, and checkpointing to speed up trials; reports up to 14.9x per-trial speedups and improved accuracy vs VeRL Recipe.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hyperparameter optimization for RL with large language models is highly sensitive and computationally expensive; existing multi-fidelity HPO methods are inefficient at scale.

Method: Jointly treat model size and training budget as fidelity variables; train/evaluate using a small proxy model of the target LLM; integrate early-stopping based on training dynamics; implement checkpointing to avoid redundant work.

Result: Per-trial efficiency up to 14.9x improvement; better or competitive predictive accuracy under the same time budget; improvement over VeRL Recipe ranging 5.8% to 111.6%.

Conclusion: Joint fidelity HPO is an effective approach for efficient HPO in RL for LLMs, offering substantial speedups while maintaining or improving predictive performance.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for large language models (LLMs) is highly sensitive to hyperparameter configurations, making hyperparameter optimization (HPO) essential yet computationally expensive. Existing multi-fidelity HPO methods remain inefficient for LLM RL due to the massive model scale and resource-intensive training cycles. In this paper, we propose Joint Fidelity Hyperparameter Optimization (JF-HPO), which simultaneously adapts both model size and training budget as fidelity. JF-HPO is empowered by: (i) it leverages a small proxy model of the target LLM for efficient training and evaluation in each HPO trial; (ii) it integrates carefully designed early-stopping strategies based on training dynamics; (iii) it introduces an efficient checkpointing mechanism to eliminate redundant computations. Compared with existing HPO methods, JF-HPO significantly improves the computational efficiency of each trial (up to 14.9 times), while achieving better or competitive predictive accuracy under the same time budget. Notably, compared with utilizing hyperparameter configurations from the VeRL Recipe, JF-HPO delivers performance improvements ranging from 5.8% to 111.6%.

</details>


### [315] [RMPrior: Bridging Propagation Priors and Diffusion Refinement for Efficient Radio Map Construction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03074)
*Zixuan Guo,Xiucheng Wang,Nan Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mid-start diffusion with propagation priors speeds up radio map reconstruction by ~2x while boosting quality, with theory linking initialization gap, truncation, and prior quality; enables ranking of scene fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models yield high-fidelity radio maps but are computationally heavy for dynamic wireless systems. Classical propagation priors contain scene-level information that standard diffusion starts from noise ignore; integrating priors can reduce computation while maintaining or improving accuracy.

Method: Perturb a matched propagation prior to an intermediate diffusion timestep (mid-start). The pretrained diffusion network performs the remaining reverse steps, focusing on multipath-aware refinement. The work provides a theoretical bound on the initialization gap, a sufficient condition under which truncation improves fidelity, and a prior-quality sensitivity analysis. Empirical evaluation includes an ablation across three propagation models.

Result: At P_start=0.5, achieves a 2.01x speedup and improved NMSE, RMSE, SSIM, and PSNR over the full-step baseline on IRT4HighRes. Prior-quality ablations show reconstruction quality tracks prior fidelity; sensitivity is amplified with shorter reverse trajectories. Mid-start quality can serve as a proxy for ranking scene-level fidelity of propagation models.

Conclusion: The proposed mid-start sampling method effectively leverages propagation priors to accelerate diffusion-based radio map reconstruction without sacrificing—and often improving—quality. The approach provides insight into prior quality's role and offers a practical mechanism to rank propagation models in dynamic scenarios.

Abstract: Diffusion models achieve high-fidelity radio map construction through iterative denoising, yet their sampling cost limits practicality in dynamic wireless systems where radio maps must be refreshed repeatedly. Meanwhile, classical propagation models encode valuable scene-level knowledge that standard diffusion inference discards entirely by initializing from pure Gaussian noise. This paper bridges propagation priors and diffusion refinement through a mid-start sampling strategy. A matched propagation prior is perturbed to an intermediate diffusion timestep, and the pretrained diffusion backbone executes only the remaining reverse steps, focusing computation on multipath-aware refinement rather than full reconstruction from noise. We provide theoretical analysis establishing an upper bound on the initialization gap, a sufficient condition under which truncation improves reconstruction fidelity, and a formal characterization of prior-quality sensitivity under aggressive truncation. Experiments on IRT4HighRes show that, at $P_{\text{start}}=0.5$, the proposed method achieves a $2.01\times$ speedup while simultaneously improving NMSE, RMSE, SSIM, and PSNR over the full-step baseline. A prior-quality ablation across three propagation models of different fidelity confirms that reconstruction quality tracks prior quality, with the sensitivity amplified under shorter reverse trajectories, consistent with the theoretical predictions. These results also suggest that mid-start reconstruction quality can serve as a proxy for ranking the scene-level fidelity of different propagation models.

</details>


### [316] [Libra: Efficient Resource Management for Agentic RL Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03077)
*Kaiwen Chen,Xin Tan,Jingzong Li,Hong Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Libra enables efficient resource management for agentic RL by (1) a periodic global resource planner that jointly allocates GPUs across rollout and training using an elastic hybrid pool for non-blocking reallocation, and (2) a causality-driven multi-level feedback queue (C-MLFQ) that routes requests to rollout buckets based on tool-return signals rather than length predictions. This yields up to 3x throughput and up to 2.5x faster reward convergence on 48 A800 GPUs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic RL workloads exhibit long-tailed trajectory distributions, strong asymmetry between rollout and training compute/memory patterns, and distribution drift as policies evolve. Static resource splits become suboptimal under such dynamics, leading to inefficiency and underutilization.

Method: Two mechanisms: (i) periodic global resource planner that jointly optimizes GPU allocation across rollout and training clusters with an elastic hybrid pool enabling lightweight, non-blocking reallocation; (ii) C-MLFQ scheduler that routes requests to heterogeneous rollout buckets using causal signals from tool-return outcomes, avoiding fragile length-prediction methods.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 48 A800 GPUs shows Libra achieving up to 3.0× higher throughput and up to 2.5× faster reward convergence compared to baselines.

Conclusion: Libra effectively addresses long-tail, asymmetry, and drift in agentic RL workloads by dynamic resource planning and causality-driven scheduling, delivering substantial improvements in throughput and convergence speed.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a standard post-training paradigm for large language models (LLMs), extending beyond preference alignment to complex reasoning and multi-turn agentic behaviors. In agentic RL, the rollout stage generates trajectories while invoking tools, producing long-tailed and non-stationary workloads that challenge conventional resource-management assumptions. Three fundamental challenges arise. First, due to the long-tail distribution, a small fraction of trajectories dominates rollout makespan. Second, rollout and training exhibit strong asymmetry in compute patterns, memory demands, and sensitivity to sequence length. Third, as the RL policy evolves, the trajectory-length distribution drifts over time, rendering any static resource split progressively suboptimal.
  We present Libra, which introduces two core mechanisms. The first is a periodic global resource planner that jointly optimizes GPU allocation across rollout and training clusters. It leverages an elastic hybrid pool to enable lightweight, non-blocking worker reallocation between stages. The second is a causality-driven multi-level feedback queue (C-MLFQ) scheduler, which routes requests to heterogeneous rollout buckets based on causal signals derived from tool-return outcomes, rather than relying on fragile length predictions. Evaluated on 48 A800 GPUs, Libra achieves up to 3.0$\times$ higher throughput and converges up to 2.5$\times$ faster in reward compared to the baselines.

</details>


### [317] [Multi-component Causal Tracing in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03085)
*Zirui Yan,Dennis Wei,Dmitriy A. Katz,Prasanna Sattigeri,Ali Tajer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified, scalable causal-tracing framework for identifying multiple model components (e.g., attention heads, MLP neurons) that causally influence a target metric, using soft interventions and a continuous optimization transformation to handle combinatorial complexity; empirically outperforms baselines and code is released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend causal tracing from single-component analyses to multi-component settings to understand how various components jointly influence target metrics (e.g., accuracy, fairness) in LLMs, addressing the combinatorial search challenge.

Method: A unified framework that applies interventions to many components (heads, neurons) for a chosen metric, employs soft interventions and a tailored metric transformation to convert the combinatorial search into a continuous optimization problem, yielding binary decisions for component selection under constraints.

Result: Experiments show the method efficiently identifies high-impact component subsets and outperforms existing baselines in affecting the target metric.

Conclusion: The framework enables scalable, multi-component causal tracing in LLMs, facilitating targeted model analysis and optimization; code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: Causal tracing systematically intervenes on a large language model's (LLM's) internal representations to uncover and quantify the causal pathways linking specific inputs or computations to specific metrics of interest, quantifying the LLM's behavior. Building on previous single-component or single-layer studies, this paper presents a unified framework for causally tracing multiple components simultaneously. This framework systematically identifies the subsets of components (e.g., attention heads and multi-layer perceptron neurons) most critical to a desired target performance metric (e.g., accuracy and fairness). This is achieved by incorporating flexible interventions applied to a wide range of desired metrics. To address the combinatorial complexity of the multi-component problem, an efficient algorithm is designed that leverages soft interventions and a carefully designed metric transformation, converting the combinatorial search problem into a continuous one that can be solved efficiently under proper constraints, thereby generating proper binary decisions for selecting components. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method efficiently identifies subsets of the model's components that have a high impact on the target metric, outperforming existing baseline approaches. Our code is available at https://github.com/ZiruiYan/multi-component-causal-tracing.

</details>


### [318] [Learning to Solve, Forgetting to Retain: Correct-Set Turnover in RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03087)
*Chuanyu Qin,Chenxu Yang,Qingyi Si,Naibin Gu,Peng Fu,Zheng Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLVR improves LLM abilities but gains can regress due to correct-set turnover; introduce retention-aware review method (the proposed \texttt{METHOD}) with pre-rollout batch replacement to remind prior solutions, achieving zero extra rollout overhead and improved performance across 20 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address retention/regression dynamics in RLVR; optimize retention as an explicit objective and exploit the repair-window to minimize cost of restoring regressed prompts.

Method: Propose \texttt{METHOD}, a retention-aware review mechanism that tracks mastered prompts and periodically reintroduces them via pre-rollout batch replacement; incurs zero rollout overhead; uses recall-based prompts to prevent forgetting.

Result: On 20 benchmarks across image-text, video, and text-only tasks using Qwen3-VL and Qwen2.5-Math, \texttt{METHOD} consistently outperforms GRPO, DAPO, and replay baselines, demonstrating cross-modal and cross-algorithm generalization.

Conclusion: Retention-aware RLVR can mitigate correct-set turnover by combining acquisition with retention; the repair-window principle quantifies the cost of delayed repair and the proposed method exploits a low-cost window to sustain mastered prompts.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) improves the ability of large language model, yet headline accuracy gains often conceal a hidden cost: previously solved problems quietly become unsolvable as training proceeds. We frame this phenomenon as \emph{correct-set turnover}, representing the coupled dynamics of solution acquisition and regression over the mastered set. Under this view, retention becomes an explicit optimization target alongside acquisition. We analytically and empirically establish the \emph{repair-window principle}: the cost of restoring a regressed prompt grows sharply with review delay, defining a low-cost window that standard RLVR pipelines fail to exploit. To address this, we propose \textbf{\method{}}, a retention-aware review mechanism that tracks mastered prompts and periodically reintroduces them to \textbf{remind} the model of previous solutions. By utilizing pre-rollout batch replacement, \method{} incurs zero additional rollout overhead. Evaluated across 20 benchmarks spanning image-text, video, and text-only tasks with Qwen3-VL and Qwen2.5-Math, \method{} consistently improves performance over GRPO, DAPO, and replay baselines, demonstrating robust generalizability across modalities and algorithms.

</details>


### [319] [HARVE: Hacking-Aware Reward-Head Vector Editing for Robust Reward Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03131)
*Shuang Liu,Yuxuan Bo,Qiuyang Zhao,Caiyue Huang,Xiaorong Chen,Yanguang Liu,Mengnan Du*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RewardHackBench reveals vulnerabilities of reward models to hacking; HARVE provides a training-free remedy by editing the reward head in a residual-subspace to reduce hacking sensitivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure robust alignment of LLM reward models against reward hacking, addressing gaps in evaluation and defense.

Method: Introduce RewardHackBench with 13 hacking patterns; propose HARVE that identifies a multi-directional hacking subspace from residual stream directions linked to hacking subcategories, and remove the reward-head component aligned with this subspace using a small set of gold-hacked examples; no gradient updates or fine-tuning.

Result: HARVE improves hacking robustness across eight reward models, outperforms fine-tuning baselines, and preserves general capabilities; analyses show hacking is multidimensional in residual-space rather than isolated cues.

Conclusion: Training-free reward-head editing in a multidimensional residual space offers an effective defense against reward hacking and highlights the complex structure of hacking signals.

Abstract: Reward models are central to large language model (LLM) alignment, but they remain vulnerable to reward hacking. To evaluate reward-model robustness, we introduce RewardHackBench containing 13 reward-hacking patterns covering real life high-stakes domains and general settings, and we find severe failures on specific subcategories across eight reward models. To mitigate these failures, we propose HARVE, a training-free reward-head editing method for scalar reward models. Instead of fine-tuning the reward model, HARVE identifies a multi-directional hacking subspace from residual stream directions associated with selected hacking subcategories, and removes the component of the reward-head vector aligned with that subspace. This directly reduces the reward head's sensitivity to hacking-related features using only a small set of contrastive gold-hacked examples, without gradient updates or fine-tuning. Comprehensive experiments across eight reward models indicates that \model improves hacking robustness, outperforms fine-tuning baselines, and preserves reward-models' general capability. Further analyses suggest that reward hacking is better captured as a multidimensional residual-space structure than by isolated surface cues.

</details>


### [320] [When RLHF Fails: A Mechanistic Taxonomy of Reward Hacking, Collapse, and Evaluator Gaming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03238)
*Zelalem Abahana*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLHF failure modes are dynamic, localized, and partially predictable; aggressive PPO correlates with higher reward hacking; a pre-transition logistic model can forecast hacking; training dynamics create failure surfaces that can be diagnosed and anticipated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how RLHF failures emerge during training, not just at convergence, by examining mechanics with a compact pipeline and multiple evaluators to map failure pathways and predictability.

Method: Empirical study on a compact RLHF pipeline using PPO, DPO, UP-PPO, and diagnostics (reward-model uncertainty, policy drift, diversity/repetition), two external LLM judges; analysis across 61 checkpoint rows and 1920 transitions; matched transitions classified by reward/judge signals; logistic regression for pre-transition prediction; bootstrap CIs.

Result: Aggressive PPO shows highest localized reward hacking rate (14.45%, bootstrap 95% CI: 10.16–18.75); UP-PPO lower in the same regime (≈11.3%, CI around 10.94–11.33%); pre-transition logistic model achieves ROC-AUC 0.821; row-level analysis detects localized reward hacking missed by checkpoint averages in 3 of 12 settings; study spans 61 checkpoints and 1920 transitions.

Conclusion: RLHF failures are training-dynamics phenomena that can be categorized, localized, and partially anticipated; methodology emphasizes moving beyond final-model evaluation to a dynamics-based failure taxonomy.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) makes large-scale post-training possible by replacing an underspecified human objective with learned and scalable proxies. The same substitution creates a structured failure surface: optimization can raise the learned reward while external quality falls, degrade both proxy and judge scores, reveal proxy under-alignment, or produce evaluator-specific disagreement. We present an empirical failure-mode study of a compact RLHF pipeline with proximal policy optimization (PPO), direct preference optimization (DPO), uncertainty-penalized PPO (UP-PPO), reward-model uncertainty, approximate policy drift, diversity and repetition diagnostics, and two external LLM judges. Rather than treating reward hacking as a single terminal event, we classify matched transitions between checkpoints using the directions of the learned reward, judge scores, and average judge score. Across 61 checkpoint rows and 1920 row-level transitions, aggressive PPO has the highest localized reward-hacking rate (14.45%; bootstrap 95% CI: 10.16-18.75), while UP-PPO yields lower rates in the same aggressive regime (11.33-10.94%). A pre-transition logistic model predicts future row-level reward hacking with ROC-AUC 0.821, and row-level analysis finds localized reward hacking that checkpoint averages miss in 3 of 12 settings. The central conclusion is methodological: RLHF failures are not only final-model pathologies, but training dynamics that can be classified, localized, and partially anticipated.

</details>


### [321] [Message Tuning Outshines Graph Prompt Tuning: A Prismatic Space Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03290)
*Yancheng Chen,Dun Ma,Shuai Zhang,Yang Liu,Xixun Lin,Xiangyu Zhao,Wenguo Yang,Wei Chen,Chuan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MTG uses a small set of learnable message prototypes to adapt GNNs without updating pre-trained weights, and, guided by PS-Theory, can exceed the theoretical upper bound of graph prompt tuning, with MTG outperforming baselines across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of rigorous metrics to quantify the adaptation capacity of graph prompt tuning in graph foundation models, hindering understanding of its limits and the development of stronger adaptation methods.

Method: Introduce Prismatic Space Theory (PS-Theory) to quantify and bound the adaptation capacity of graph prompt tuning; propose Message Tuning for GFMs (MTG) by injecting learnable message prototypes into each GNN layer to guide message fusion without updating pre-trained weights.

Result: PS-Theory provides an upper bound for the adaptation capacity of graph prompt tuning; MTG’s adaptation capacity can exceed this bound; experimental results show MTG consistently outperforms graph prompt baselines across diverse datasets.

Conclusion: PS-Theory offers a framework to analyze adaptation capacity; MTG demonstrates improved adaptation under tight parameter budgets, suggesting potential for more efficient GFM adaptation beyond traditional prompt-tuning limits.

Abstract: Graph Foundation Models (GFMs), built upon the Pre-training and Adaptation paradigm, have emerged as a research hotspot in graph learning. For GNN-based GFMs, graph prompt tuning has become the prevailing adaptation method for downstream tasks. Although recent methods explain why graph prompt tuning works, how to rigorously measure its adaptation capacity remains an open problem. Addressing this problem is critical for understanding the capability limits of graph prompt tuning and for developing more powerful adaptation methods. In this paper, we propose Prismatic Space Theory (PS-Theory), a novel mathematical framework to quantify the capacity of adaptation methods, while focusing on establishing the upper bound for the adaptation capacity of graph prompt tuning. Building upon the proposed PS-Theory, we further introduce Message Tuning for GFMs (MTG), a lightweight approach that injects a small set of learnable message prototypes into each layer of the GNN backbone to adaptively guide message fusion without updating pre-trained weights. Through our PS-Theory, we prove that the adaptation capacity of MTG can exceed the theoretical upper bound of graph prompt tuning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MTG consistently outperforms graph prompt baselines across diverse benchmark datasets, providing strong empirical support for our theoretical findings.

</details>


### [322] [Learning Multi-Scale Hypergraph for High-Order Brain Connectivity Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03310)
*Jaeyoon Sim,Soojin Hwang,Seunghun Baek,Guorong Wu,Won Hwa Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MuHL: an adaptive, multi-scale hyperedge learning framework for brain networks that learns high-order interactions through dynamic hyperedge construction on multi-resolution graph signals, improving disease classification and enabling interpretable ROI interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph methods focus on pairwise connections and fixed hyperedges, missing higher-order, multi-resolution dependencies crucial for neurodegenerative disease progression.

Method: MuHL builds hierarchical node features and continuously constructs hyperedges across multi-resolution graphs to capture high-order, adaptive interactions.

Result: Empirical results on brain-network benchmarks show consistent improvements in disease classification across stages; the learned hyperedges reveal key ROIs and group-wise interactions linked to disease progression.

Conclusion: MuHL offers a flexible, scalable approach for brain-network analysis, enabling adaptive high-order modeling and interpretable biomarker discovery in neurodegenerative disorders.

Abstract: Understanding complex interactions between brain regions is critical for early neurodegenerative disease classification such as Alzheimer's Disease (AD) and Parkinson's Disease (PD). While graph-based models are widely used to analyze brain networks, most existing approaches primarily focus on pairwise interactions between directly connected nodes, limiting their ability to capture higher-order dependencies across multiple regions. Although hypergraph-based methods have been proposed to model higher-order relations, many rely on predefined hyperedges or restrict learning to hyperedge weights, reducing flexibility and limiting their capacity to capture multi-resolution structural patterns. In this regard, we introduce an adaptive multi-scale hyperedge learning framework, i.e., MuHL, which constructs hierarchical node features and dynamically learns high-order interactions through continuous hyperedge construction over multi-resolution graph signals. Extensive experiments on multiple brain network benchmarks demonstrate that MuHL consistently improves disease classification performance across different stages, and further identifies key regions of interest (ROIs) and their group-wise interactions from the learned hyperedges that are associated with disease progression, highlighting its potential as a powerful tool for brain network analysis in neurodegenerative disorders.

</details>


### [323] [Calibration Data Trade-offs Across Capability Dimensions: Why Multi-Source Mixing Matters for High-Sparsity LLM Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03328)
*Hu Xu,Zhaolong Xing,Congcong Liu,Jiaxing Wang,Zhida Jiang,Junshi Huang,Zhen Chen,Jianfeng Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-training pruning with multi-source calibration improves overall retention across capabilities; single-source calibration cannot preserve all capabilities due to conflicting trade-offs across General, Commonsense, Code, and Math. Introduces IGSP (information-guided self-calibration) to construct multi-source mixes by minimizing 4-gram perplexity and balancing across dimensions, yielding substantial gains over single sources on LLaMA-3.1-8B at 60% sparsity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether the impact of calibration sources on post-pruning accuracy depends on evaluating capability-specific retention rather than aggregated accuracy, and to explore whether multi-source calibration can better preserve diverse abilities.

Method: Decompose post-pruning capability into General, Commonsense, Code, and Math. Analyze n=15 calibration sources via Spearman correlations between OIT information metrics and per-dimension retention. Identify an opposite-sign trade-off: calibration perplexity correlates positively with General retention (rho=+0.71) but negatively with Math (-0.53) and Code (-0.59). Propose IGSP to automate multi-source construction without capability-aligned corpora by minimizing 4-gram aggregation and balancing perplexity across dimensions.

Result: Found an opposite-sign trade-off, meaning no single source preserves all capabilities. A uniform multi-source mix attains 58.8% total retention at 60% sparsity, beating the best single source (MetaMath, 50.0%) by 8.8 points and the C4 default (40.0%) by 18.8 points. IGSP improves over Self-Cal by 2.4 and over SGS by 4.8.

Conclusion: Multi-source calibration with information-guided self-calibration can better preserve multi-dimensional post-pruning capabilities, overcoming capability-specific trade-offs inherent to single-source calibration.

Abstract: Post-training pruning compresses large language models to high sparsity using a small unlabelled calibration set, and recent work has concluded that the choice of calibration source has only modest impact on averaged post-pruning accuracy. We ask whether this conclusion survives once calibration impact is evaluated separately across distinct capability dimensions rather than aggregated. Decomposing post-pruning capability into General, Commonsense, Code, and Math, and analysing $n{=}15$ calibration sources via Spearman correlations between OIT information metrics and per-dimension retention, we uncover an opposite-sign trade-off: calibration perplexity correlates positively with General retention ($ρ{=}{+}0.71$) but negatively with Math and Code retention ($ρ{=}{-}0.53,\,{-}0.59$; $p{<}0.05$), so no single source can preserve all capabilities. We respond with multi-source calibration mixing, and propose IGSP, an information-guided self-calibration protocol that automates multi-source construction without capability-aligned corpora by minimising 4-gram aggregation and balancing perplexity across dimensions. On LLaMA-3.1-8B at SparseGPT 60% sparsity, a uniform multi-source mix reaches 58.8% total retention, outperforming the best single source (MetaMath, 50.0%) by $+8.8$ and the C4 default (40.0%) by $+18.8$; IGSP improves over Self-Cal by $+2.4$ and SGS by $+4.8$.

</details>


### [324] [FLIPS: Instance-Fingerprinting for LLMs via Pseudo-random Sequences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03330)
*Gurvan Richardeau,Gohar Dashyan,Erwan Le Merrer,Gilles Tredan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes instance-level fingerprinting (FLIPS) to identify deployed configurations of the same LLM, enabling regulator-oriented assessments of actual behavior rather than provenance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Regulators need to verify deployed model behavior across configurations. Traditional fingerprinting protects IP and is robust to configuration changes, but fails for regulatory accountability. This work seeks a method to distinguish configurations of the same LLM.

Method: FLIPS analyzes biases in generated binary random sequences to fingerprint instance-level configuration. It reports high identification accuracy (96% closed-set, 90% open-set) across 237 model instances, outperforming an adapted LLMmap baseline (35%). Code is released.

Result: Demonstrates that instance-level fingerprinting is feasible and effective for regulator-oriented verification; identifies configurations of the same LLM with high accuracy across a large instance set.

Conclusion: Instance-level fingerprinting is necessary for regulation and practically feasible, enabling verification of deployed behavior rather than just model provenance.

Abstract: Literature reveals that a Large Language Model's (LLM) behavior is not only conditioned by its original weights but also its instance-level parameters, such as instructional prompt, sampling configuration or quantization. A model that generates safe outputs under one configuration may produce toxic content under another. However, current LLM identification techniques (such as fingerprinting) focus on intellectual property protection, and their design favors robustness to changes in these instance-level parameters. This poses a critical challenge for AI regulation in which compliance assessments target actual deployed behaviors, not model provenance. In this paper, we introduce instance-level fingerprinting, a regulator-oriented paradigm that distinguishes configurations of the same LLM. Our method FLIPS, exploits biases in generated binary random sequences to reach 96% (closed-set) and 90% (open-set, where some targets are unknown) identification accuracy across 237 model instances, versus 35% for the adapted LLMmap baseline. This shows that instance-level fingerprinting is both necessary for regulation and practically feasible. Code available at https://github.com/GurvanR/FLIPS-LLM-Instance-Fingerprinting.

</details>


### [325] [Tailoring Strictly Proper Scoring Rules for Downstream Tasks: An Application to Causal Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03332)
*Roman Plaud,Alexandre Perez-Lebel,Antoine Saillenfest,Thomas Bonald,Marine Le Morvan,Gaël Varoquaux,Matthieu Labeau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework to derive task-specific strictly proper scoring rules by matching the curvature of the downstream error, applied to ATE via IPW, yielding a closed-form loss and mapping that improves performance over standard objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard training objectives (e.g., log-loss) may misalign with downstream causal estimation goals, especially when propensity scores near 0/1 induce high bias/variance in IPW; a task-specific objective is needed to minimize downstream error.

Method: Propose a principled framework to derive strictly proper scoring rules by matching the local curvature of the downstream error metric. Apply this framework to Average Treatment Effect (ATE) estimation to obtain a closed-form loss and a canonical probability mapping, compatible with neural networks or gradient-boosting models.

Result: Empirical evaluations on causal inference benchmarks show that the tailored objective consistently outperforms standard likelihood-based and covariate-balancing approaches.

Conclusion: Task-specific scoring rules that align with downstream estimation error can substantially improve IPW/ATE performance; the framework delivers a plug-in, closed-form loss and mapping with broad applicability.

Abstract: Probabilistic models are typically trained using task-agnostic objectives like log-loss, which can lead to significant errors in downstream estimation. This disconnect is especially critical in Inverse Probability Weighting (IPW) for causal inference, where propensity score errors near $0$ and $1$ often lead to high bias and variance. We propose a principled framework for deriving task-specific strictly proper scoring rules by matching the local curvature of the downstream error metric. We apply this to the Average Treatment Effect (ATE) estimation, deriving a closed-form loss and its corresponding canonical probability mapping that can be readily integrated with any model like a neural network or a gradient boosting algorithm. Extensive evaluations on causal inference benchmarks demonstrate that our tailored objective consistently outperforms standard likelihood-based and covariate-balancing approaches.

</details>


### [326] [IdEst: Assessing Self-Supervised Learning Representations via Intrinsic Dimension](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03338)
*Julie Mordacq,Vicky Kalogeiton,Steve Oudot*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IdEst uses the MST-based intrinsic dimension estimator to quantify SSL representations' intrinsic dimensionality (ID). It strongly correlates with downstream linear probe performance and enables efficient hyperparameter selection, providing a geometric proxy for SSL quality that complements standard probing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To connect generalization of neural networks with intrinsic dimensionality and to provide a computationally efficient, geometry-based proxy for evaluating SSL representations beyond costly linear probing.

Method: Apply the Minimum Spanning Tree dimension estimator (dim_MST) to SSL representations across diverse datasets, architectures, and pretraining objectives to estimate their intrinsic dimensionality (ID). Then assess the relationship between ID and downstream linear probe performance and use ID as a hyperparameter selection signal.

Result: IdEst shows a strong correlation between estimated ID and downstream linear probe accuracy across various settings, and can guide hyperparameter choices with substantially reduced computational cost compared to supervised baselines.

Conclusion: Intrinsic dimensionality is a principled geometric proxy for assessing SSL representations, complementing traditional linear probing and enabling more efficient hyperparameter tuning.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for learning meaningful representations from unlabeled data. However, the standard protocol for evaluating these representations, linear probing, is computationally expensive, sensitive to hyperparameters, and provides limited insight into the geometric structure of the representation space. In this work, motivated by connections between neural network generalization and intrinsic dimension (ID) we propose IdEst, a method for estimating the ID of SSL representations via the Minimum Spanning Tree dimension estimator ($\mathrm{dim}_\mathrm{MST}$). Across diverse datasets, architectures, and SSL pretraining objectives, we show that IdEst strongly correlates with downstream linear probe performances. Furthermore, we demonstrate that IdEst enables efficient hyperparameter selection, significantly reducing the computational cost compared to supervised alternatives. Our results highlight intrinsic dimensionality as a principled geometric proxy for assessing SSL representations, complementing standard supervised probing protocols.

</details>


### [327] [AugMask: Training Diffusion Models on Incomplete Tabular Data via Stochastic Augmentation and Masking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03347)
*Jungkyu Kim,Taeyoung Park,Kibok Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AugMask enables diffusion-based tabular data generation with missing values by separating conditioning from supervision: using conditional stochastic augmentation to construct inputs and applying denoising supervision only to observed coordinates, connected to a Rao–Blackwellized objective that yields a variance-weighted penalty to curb over-reliance on imputed entries; empirically outperforms missing-aware baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tabular data often contain missing values and standard diffusion backbones assume complete inputs; there is a need for a plug-and-play method to adapt existing backbones to incomplete data without redesign.

Method: 1) Construct numeric inputs via conditional stochastic augmentation with lightweight auxiliary models; 2) Apply denoising supervision only to observed coordinates; augmented missing entries serve as uncertain conditioning context; the training rule corresponds to a Rao–Blackwellized objective; marginalizing missing entries yields a variance-weighted sensitivity penalty that discourages over-reliance on uncertain completions.

Result: Empirically across diverse datasets and missingness regimes, AugMask enables standard diffusion-based tabular generators to outperform specialized missing-aware baselines.

Conclusion: AugMask provides a simple, plug-and-play training rule to adapt diffusion models to incomplete tabular data, separating conditioning from supervision to reduce reliance on imputed values while maintaining competitive performance.

Abstract: Score-based diffusion models have emerged as prominent deep generative models; however, their application to tabular data remains challenging because their backbones assume fully specified inputs, whereas real-world tabular data often contain missing values. We propose AugMask, a plug-and-play training framework that adapts missing-unaware backbones to incomplete data by separating conditioning from supervision. AugMask 1) constructs numeric inputs via conditional stochastic augmentation using lightweight auxiliary models, and 2) applies denoising supervision only to observed coordinates. In effect, augmented missing entries serve as uncertain conditioning context rather than training targets. We connect this training rule to a Rao--Blackwellized objective and show that marginalizing missing entries yields a variance-weighted sensitivity penalty, discouraging over-reliance on uncertain completions. Across diverse datasets and missingness regimes, AugMask enables standard diffusion-based tabular generators to outperform specialized missing-aware baselines.

</details>


### [328] [APIC: Amortized Physics-Informed Calibration using Neural Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03355)
*Aishwarya Venkataramanan,Sai Karthikeya Vemuri,Joachim Denzler*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: APIC extends Kennedy-O'Hagan with amortized, neural-process-based calibration to enable scalable, uncertainty-aware calibration across related physical systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physics models are inherently imperfect and standard KOH calibration is non-amortized and non-scalable across families of related systems; APIC aims to enable population-level inference across realizations with uncertainty quantification.

Method: Two-branch latent architecture to separate instance-specific physical parameters from shared structural discrepancy; neural-process-based amortization; integration of differentiable physics into the inference backbone; capable of calibrating unseen realizations from sparse observations while quantifying uncertainty.

Result: Demonstrates improved parameter recovery and consistent identification of the systemic discrepancy structure across multiple systems (damped spring oscillator, Lotka-Volterra, advection-diffusion PDE) and outperforms other calibration approaches.

Conclusion: APIC enables rapid, scalable calibration with uncertainty quantification across related systems, combining differentiable physics with amortized inference for robust discrepancy-aware calibration.

Abstract: Physics models are inherently imperfect due to misspecified or missing mechanisms, resulting in systematic discrepancies between model predictions and real-world observations. The Kennedy-O'Hagan (KOH) framework addresses this issue through explicit discrepancy modeling. However, its non-amortized, per-instance formulation limits scalability across families of related systems. We introduce Amortized Physics-Informed Calibration (APIC), a population-level extension of KOH that leverages Neural Processes to perform scalable Bayesian inference across realizations. Our framework employs a two-branch latent architecture to disentangle instance-specific physical parameters from shared, state-dependent structural discrepancies. By integrating differentiable physics into an amortized inference backbone, APIC enables rapid calibration of unseen realizations from sparse observations while quantifying uncertainty. Experiments on the damped spring oscillator, the Lotka-Volterra system, and the advection-diffusion PDE with misspecified physics demonstrate improved parameter recovery and consistent identification of the systemic discrepancy structure compared to other calibration approaches.

</details>


### [329] [The Impact of Temporal Granularity on Socio-Demographic Inference from Household Load Profiles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03358)
*Dejan Radovanovic,Maximilian Schirl,Andreas Unterweger,Günther Eibl*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Coarsening temporal granularity in smart-meter data yields two stability plateaus (15 min–1 h and 1–7 days); data minimization can occur without losing utility. XGBoost with handcrafted/tsfresh features outperforms alternatives; static attributes are inferable from coarse data while some dynamic attributes require fine resolution. Overall, temporal resolution, features, and classifier choice jointly affect privacy-utility trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how temporal resolution in smart-meter data affects the ability to infer socio-demographic attributes and to identify how to balance privacy and utility.

Method: Train-year-round classifiers and test on arbitrary weeks to force generalization; vary data granularity from 15 minutes to 7 days on a dataset of 1,589 households over one year; compare handcrafted features, tsfresh features, CNN-based autoencoder embeddings; evaluate classifiers (notably XGBoost).

Result: Predictive accuracy decreases with coarser granularity but stabilizes on two plateaus: (i) 15 minutes to 1 hour, and (ii) 1 day to 7 days. Handcrafted and tsfresh features rival CNN embeddings; XGBoost consistently outperforms others. Dwelling size can be inferred even from coarse data; swimming-pool usage requires fine-grained temporal signals. Overall, a privacy-utility trade-off emerges, highlighting the joint influence of temporal resolution, feature extraction, and classifier choice on socio-demographic inference.

Conclusion: Temporal resolution, feature engineering, and classifier selection jointly shape inference risk in smart-meter data; there are meaningful opportunities for data minimization without sacrificing core utility, guiding privacy-preserving data sharing and analysis strategies.

Abstract: Smart meter data can reveal sensitive socio-demographic characteristics of households, raising privacy concerns. While this risk has been demonstrated at fixed granularities, the role of temporal resolution in shaping inference performance remains insufficiently explored. This paper addresses this gap by analyzing how load profiles with granularities from 15 minutes to 7 days affect the predictability of eight socio-demographic attributes in a dataset of 1,589 households over one year. We introduce an evaluation framework where classifiers are trained on year-round data but tested on arbitrary weeks, forcing generalization across seasonal and weekly variations. Our results show three main findings. First, while coarsening granularity reduces predictive accuracy, two plateaus emerge: performance is stable between 15 minutes and 1 hour, and again between 1 and 7 days. This reveals opportunities for data minimization without sacrificing utility. Second, interpretable handcrafted and tsfresh features remain competitive with CNN-based autoencoder embeddings, while XGBoost consistently outperforms alternative classifiers. Third, feature importance analysis highlights differences between static and dynamic attributes: dwelling size can be inferred even from coarse data, whereas swimming pool usage requires fine-grained temporal signals. Overall, our study provides new insights into the privacy-utility trade-off in smart metering, showing how temporal resolution, feature extraction, and classifier choice jointly influence socio-demographic inference.

</details>


### [330] [Mitigating False Credit Propagation: Probabilistic Graphical Reward Aggregation for Rubric-Based Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03361)
*Can Lv,Mingju Chen,Heng Chang,Shiji Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A probabilistic graphical framework named GEAR for dependency-aware rubric aggregation to fix false credit propagation in rubric-based RL. It treats each rubric criterion as latent Bernoulli events on a typed rubric graph, uses soft suppression from unsupported parents to children, and outputs a normalized expected signed utility in linear time. It outperforms flat aggregation and deterministic gating (up to 15.5% gains; 96.5% leakage reduction) across HealthBench, WritingBench, and PLawBench with two policy backbones; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rubric-based rewards are frequently aggregated as independent utilities, ignoring prerequisite/activation relationships among criteria, causing False Credit Propagation (FCP) where rewards are counted without licenses. A dependency-aware aggregation is needed.

Method: Introduce GEAR: a probabilistic graphical framework where each criterion outcome is a latent Bernoulli variable on a typed rubric graph. Propagate soft suppression from disabled/unsupported parent events to children, and aggregate into a normalized expected signed utility. Computation is linear-time and can be plugged into existing rubric-based RL pipelines without changing the outer optimization.

Result: Empirical evaluation on HealthBench, WritingBench, and PLawBench with two policy backbones shows consistent improvement over flat aggregation and deterministic gating, with relative gains up to 15.5% over flat aggregation. FCP diagnostics indicate leakage reduction of 96.5% compared to flat aggregation while preserving more licensed downstream utility than deterministic gating.

Conclusion: GEAR provides dependency-aware, efficient rubric aggregation that mitigates false credit propagation while maintaining downstream utility, and its code is publicly available.

Abstract: Rubric-based rewards are increasingly used for open-ended language model post-training, but criterion-level scores are often aggregated as independent utilities. This flat scalarization ignores rubric-specified prerequisite and activation relations among criteria, allowing reward or penalty to be counted even when the condition that licenses it is absent. We call this structural reward-aggregation failure \textbf{False Credit Propagation} (FCP). To address this limitation, we propose \ourname (\textbf{G}raphical \textbf{E}vent \textbf{A}ggregation for \textbf{R}ubric rewards), a probabilistic graphical framework for dependency-aware rubric aggregation. \ourname models each criterion outcome as a latent Bernoulli event in a typed rubric graph, propagates soft suppression from unsupported parent events to their children, and aggregates the resulting event probabilities into a normalized expected signed utility. This yields a linear-time reward computation that can be plugged into standard rubric-based RL pipelines without changing the outer optimization algorithm. Experiments on HealthBench, WritingBench, and PLawBench with two policy backbones show that \ourname consistently improves over flat aggregation and deterministic gating, achieving relative gains of up to 15.5\% over flat aggregation. FCP diagnostics further show that \ourname reduces leakage by 96.5\% relative to flat aggregation while preserving more licensed downstream utility than deterministic gating. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/LvCan926/GEAR.

</details>


### [331] [Link Prediction or Perdition: the Seeds of Instability in Knowledge Graph Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03365)
*Guillaume Méroué,Fabien Gandon,Pierre Monnin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KG embedding models show substantial stability issues: high MRR does not imply stable or reproducible predictions or embedding spaces; predictions diverge at the triple level and embeddings vary across runs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CurrentKGEM evaluation relies on rank-based metrics (e.g., MRR, Hits@K) and often ignores seed- and stochastic-factor-induced result instability, raising concerns about reliability and reproducibility in knowledge graph completion.

Method: Systematic stability analysis across multiple KGEMs and datasets. Isolate stochastic factors (initialization, triple ordering, negative sampling, dropout, hardware) to quantify their independent contributions to instability. Assess triple-level prediction stability, embedding-space variation, hyperparameter–stability relationship, and the effect of voting as a remediation.

Result: High-performance models exhibit divergent triple-level predictions and highly variable embedding spaces. Each stochastic factor contributes instability of comparable magnitude. Better MRR configurations are not guaranteed to be more stable. Voting provides only limited improvements to stability.

Conclusion: Benchmarking protocols are limited; the reliability of KGEMs for knowledge graph completion is questionable. Stability-aware evaluation is needed to ensure reproducibility and trustworthy deployment.

Abstract: Embedding models (KGEMs) constitute the main link prediction approach to complete knowledge graphs. Standard evaluation protocols emphasize rank-based metrics such as MRR or Hits@$K$, but usually overlook the influence of random seeds on result stability. Moreover, these metrics conceal potential instabilities in individual predictions and in the organization of embedding spaces. In this work, we conduct a systematic stability analysis of multiple KGEMs across several datasets. We find that high-performance models actually produce divergent predictions at the triple level and highly variable embedding spaces. By isolating stochastic factors (i.e., initialization, triple ordering, negative sampling, dropout, hardware), we show that each independently induces instability of comparable magnitude. Furthermore, for a given model, hyperparameter configurations with better MRR are not guaranteed to be more stable. Moreover, voting, albeit a known remediation mechanism, only provides a limited enhancement of stability. These findings highlight critical limitations of current benchmarking protocols, and raise concerns about the reliability of KGEMs for knowledge graph completion.

</details>


### [332] [Local Guidance, Global Impact: Gaussian-Reshaped Trust Region Unlocks Behavior Transitions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03382)
*Bingxu Liu,Jiashun Liu,Johan Obando-Ceron,Hao Wang,Runze Liu,Pablo Samuel Castro,Aaron Courville,Ling Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PPO struggles in continual/non-stationary RL; introduce Gaussian Trust Region Policy Optimization (GTR) with Mixture Gaussian Anchor to achieve geometry-aware, bounded, non-monotonic updates and improved robustness across domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-stationary environments require geometry-aware policy updates. Standard PPO's local, directionally inefficient updates fail to converge to new behaviors; divergence-based regularization is monotonic and hinders necessary shifts.

Method: Introduce Gaussian kernel-based trust region (GTR) that yields a bounded, non-monotonic constraint; add Mixture Gaussian Anchor adapting to recent trajectories to reduce variance due to stale references; architecture-agnostic.

Result: Empirically strong performance across games, simulated robotics, open-world exploration, and language-model post-training, indicating robustness in non-stationary settings.

Conclusion: Geometry-aware trust-region design is a promising direction for robust RL in complex non-stationary environments; GTR and Mixture Gaussian Anchor provide practical improvements and broad applicability; code available.

Abstract: While Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) demonstrates strong performance in stationary settings, we show that its standard optimization paradigm struggles in continual and non-stationary environments. The failure does not stem from insufficient model capacity or overly restrictive clipping. Instead, PPO performs persistent, directionally inefficient local updates, which indicates a lack of geometry-aware guidance for accumulating meaningful behavioral change and ultimately hindering transitions toward new behavior patterns. Although divergence-based regularization introduces partial geometric awareness, its monotonically increasing penalties implicitly discourage large policy deviations, even when such shifts are necessary for effective adaptation.
