To address this limitation, we propose Gaussian Trust Region Policy Optimization (GTR), which reshapes the trust region using a Gaussian kernel. The resulting constraint is bounded and non-monotonic, providing strong local stability while progressively relaxing under sustained high-advantage updates. To further improve robustness, we introduce a Mixture Gaussian Anchor that adapts to recent policy trajectories, reducing variance induced by stale references. GTR is architecture-agnostic and achieves strong performance across games, simulated robotic control, open-world exploration, and language model post-training. These results demonstrate that geometry-aware trust-region design can be a promising direction for robust reinforcement learning in complex non-stationary environments. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/GTR_demo/README.md.

</details>


### [333] [When Model Merging Breaks Routing: Training-Free Calibration for MoE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03391)
*Canbin Huang,Tianyuan Shi,Xiaojun Quan,Jingang Wang,Jianfei Zhang,Qifan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Training-free Hessian-aware router calibration (HARC) realigns merged MoE routers to prevent routing breakdown, yielding notable performance gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model merging of Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) is cost-effective but fragile: small perturbations from merging disrupt non-linear softmax/Top-k routing and load-balancing, causing misrouting that degrades performance especially due to expert specializations.

Method: HARC uses second-order curvature information (Hessian) to calibrate the merged router without training. It yields a closed-form solution solvable via a matrix-free conjugate gradient method.

Result: Empirical results on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks show HARC mitigates routing breakdown across diverse MoE merging baselines and yields substantial performance improvements; code available at the provided URL.

Conclusion: HARC provides an effective, training-free mechanism to realign routers in merged MoE models, addressing routing breakdown and boosting performance across tasks.

Abstract: Model merging has emerged as a cost-effective approach for consolidating the capabilities of multiple LLMs without retraining. However, existing merging techniques, largely based on linear parameter arithmetic or optimization, struggle when applied to Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures. We identify a critical failure mode in MoE merging, termed routing breakdown, in which the merged router fails to dispatch tokens to suitable experts. Routing breakdown stems from the sensitivity of the non-linear softmax and discrete Top-k routing mechanisms to parameter perturbations from merging, a sensitivity further amplified by load-balancing constraints imposed during MoE pretraining. Because fine-tuned experts exhibit distinct specializations, even modest misrouting can cause severe performance degradation. To address this issue, we propose Hessian-Aware Router Calibration (HARC), a training-free framework that leverages second-order curvature information to realign the merged router. This approach admits a closed-form solution that can be efficiently solved using a matrix-free conjugate gradient method. Experiments on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks show that HARC effectively mitigates routing breakdown across diverse MoE merging baselines and leads to substantial performance improvements. Our code is available at https://github.com/huangcb01/HARC.

</details>


### [334] [Flicker-DDPM: Accelerating Denoising Diffusion via 1/f Colored Noise Injection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03393)
*Kexiang Mao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Flicker-DDPM introduces 1/f^α colored noise into the forward diffusion process via a spatial correlation kernel, enabling faster sampling and better alignment with natural image statistics by tuning η to control α; backed by a frequency-domain theory that explains sampling acceleration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address mismatch between isotropic white noise in standard DDPMs and the 1/f spectral statistics of natural images, aiming to improve sample quality and reduce sampling steps.

Method: Incorporate colored noise using a spatial kernel σ(d) = (d+1)^(-η); derive the mapping between η and the resulting spectral exponent α; develop a frequency-domain linear theory showing reverse trajectory linearization; validate on CIFAR-10 with ~3.33x fewer steps and negligible per-step cost.

Result: Achieves comparable or superior sample quality to standard DDPMs on CIFAR-10 using 3.33x fewer sampling steps; computational cost per step remains similar; theoretical framework explains the acceleration via spectral matching of the forward noise.

Conclusion: Matching the spectral statistics of natural images with colored 1/f^α noise in diffusion models can accelerate sampling and improve efficiency, with a principled theoretical explanation and potential applicability to datasets with diverse spectra.

Abstract: We propose a novel diffusion model, Flicker-DDPM, which incorporates flicker (1/f) noise inspired by self-organized criticality (SOC), a widely observed phenomenon in natural systems. Unlike denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs), which employ isotropic white noise in the forward process, Flicker-DDPM adopts colored noise with power-law spectra to better match the spectral statistics of natural images, whose power spectra typically follow P(k) proportional to 1/k^α. To this end, we develop a colored-noise module based on a spatial correlation kernel, σ(d) = (d + 1)^{-η}, and theoretically establish that adjusting η controls the spectral exponent α of the generated 1/fα noise, enabling adaptation to datasets with diverse spectral characteristics. On CIFAR-10, Flicker DDPM matches or surpasses the generation quality of a standard DDPM baseline using 3.33 times fewer sampling steps, with negligible additional computational cost per step. We further develop a frequency-domain linear theory demonstrating that spectrally matched colored noise linearizes the reverse trajectory, theoretically explaining the observed sampling acceleration.

</details>


### [335] [KVarN: Variance-Normalized KV-Cache Quantization Mitigates Error Accumulation in Reasoning Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03458)
*Lorenz K. Muller,Philippe Bich,Chiara Boretti,Hyun-Min Chang,Jiawei Zhuang,Lukas Cavigelli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KVarN introduces a calibration-free KV-cache quantizer using Hadamard rotation and dual-scaling variance normalization to mitigate cumulative errors in 2-bit KV-cache during autoregressive decoding, achieving state-of-the-art results on MATH500, AIME24, and HumanEval; includes an implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test-time KV-cache quantization is memory-bottlenecked during long-horizon decoding and suffers from error accumulation, especially under autoregressive decoding; existing methods lack calibration-free robustness.

Method: Apply a Hadamard rotation to the K and V matrices, followed by a dual-axis variance normalization across both K and V. This calibration-free quantization operates at 2-bit precision, evaluated on generative benchmarks (MATH500, AIME24, HumanEval); VLLM implementation provided.

Result: The approach substantially reduces error accumulation and corrects outlying token-scale errors, achieving state-of-the-art KV-cache quantization performance at 2-bit on the reported benchmarks.

Conclusion: KVarN effectively mitigates quantization-induced errors in autoregressive KV-cache, enabling robust, low-bit quantization without calibration and enabling efficient test-time scaling for LLMs.

Abstract: Test-time scaling is a powerful approach to obtain better reasoning in large language models, but it becomes memory-bottlenecked during long-horizon decoding, as the KV-cache grows. KV-cache quantization can help improve this, but current methods are evaluated under prefill-like settings and errors behave differently under autoregressive decoding. We show that in the latter regime, quantization errors accumulate across timesteps, driven primarily by incorrect token scales. We introduce KVarN, a calibration-free KV-cache quantizer that applies a Hadamard rotation followed by a dual-scaling variance normalization across both axes of the K and V matrices. We find that this combination fixes outlying token-scale errors and substantially reduces error accumulation over existing baselines. KVarN establishes a new state-of-theart for KV-cache quantization on generative benchmarks, including MATH500, AIME24 and HumanEval, at 2-bit precision. A vLLM implementation of the KVarN method is available at https://github.com/huawei-csl/KVarN

</details>


### [336] [Topology-Aware Gaussian Graph Repair for Robust Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03462)
*Anubha Goel,Juho Kanniainen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TAGR proposes a sparse, topology-aware graph repair framework that augments the observed graph with a feature-neighborhood Gaussian repair and a residual correction, enabling robust GNNs without changing architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world graphs are noisy or incomplete; edge removal or dense graph learning either loses information or adds optimization complexity. A lightweight, sparse repair that preserves topology while adding meaningful connections is desirable.

Method: Construct a sparse feature-neighborhood graph using an adaptive Gaussian kernel to connect feature-similar nodes. Apply a topology-aware residual correction to reweight/preserve the original observed graph. Combine repaired graph with standard GNNs without architectural changes.

Result: Empirical evaluation on benchmark citation networks shows improved robustness of GNNs under noisy- and missing-edge scenarios. The Gaussian repair provides most robustness gains; residual correction aids stability when the graph is incomplete.

Conclusion: Lightweight sparse graph repair can achieve robustness comparable to or better than dense graph structure learning, with reduced optimization overhead and easy integration into existing GNN pipelines.

Abstract: Graph neural networks have achieved strong performance on graph-structured data, but their effectiveness depends heavily on the quality of the observed graph. In real applications, graph topology is often imperfect: noisy edges may connect unrelated nodes, while missing edges may prevent useful information from being propagated. Existing robust graph learning methods mainly address this problem by removing suspicious edges or by learning a new graph structure during training. However, edge removal alone cannot recover missing connections, and graph structure learning may introduce additional optimization complexity. In this paper, we propose Topology-Aware Gaussian Repair (TAGR), a simple graph repair framework for robust message passing in graph neural networks. Instead of learning a dense adjacency matrix, TAGR constructs a sparse feature-neighborhood graph using an adaptive Gaussian kernel and combines it with a topology-aware residual correction of the observed graph. The Gaussian repair component introduces auxiliary edges between feature-similar nodes, while the residual correction preserves and reweights the original topology according to local feature and structural consistency. The repaired graph can be used directly with standard graph neural networks without changing their architectures. Extensive experiments on benchmark citation networks show that TAGR improves the robustness of GNNs under both noisy-edge and missing-edge settings. The analysis further show that Gaussian feature-neighborhood repair provides the main robustness gain, while topology-aware residual correction improves stability when the observed graph is incomplete. These results suggest that effective graph robustness can be achieved through lightweight sparse graph repair rather than dense graph structure learning.

</details>


### [337] [Forgetting is Not Erasure: Recovering Latent Knowledge via Transport Keys](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02860)
*Archie Chaudhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes transport keys to mitigate catastrophic forgetting by correcting interface drift between model stages via interface-alignment operators; shows near-complete recovery of Task A after training on Task B with model stitching on ResNet and ViT; suggests indexing latent computations is key in continual learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the strong claim that forgetting is permanent due to erasure; show that a substantial portion stems from interface drift and can be recovered with proper reaccess to latent computations.

Method: Introduce a stitched evaluation protocol that uses early computation from the post-update network and late computation from its predecessor, mediated by compact transport keys estimated from paired activations; define transport keys as interface-alignment operators; evaluate on split CIFAR-100 with ResNet-style and compact ViT.

Result: Transport keys recover most of original Task A performance after sequential Task B training; similar recovery with ViT; results imply that indexing and re-accessing latent computations is important.

Conclusion: Continual learning requires mechanisms to index/re-access latent computations (interface alignment) in addition to preventing weight change.

Abstract: Catastrophic forgetting is often framed as a representational problem: after sequential training, a model appears to lose the features that supported performance on earlier tasks. We challenge the stronger form of this view. Across controlled continual-learning settings, we find that a significant portion of apparent forgetting can be attributed to interface drift between internal stages rather than permanent erasure of task-relevant computation. We study this phenomenon through a stitched evaluation protocol that combines early computation from a post-update network with late computation from its predecessor, optionally mediated by a compact, task-specific transport key. We describe transport keys at a systems level as compact interface-alignment operators estimated from a small set of paired anchor activations and evaluated through model stitching. On split CIFAR-100 with a ResNet-style network, transport keys recover most of the original Task A performance after sequential training on Task B. On a compact vision transformer, we observe a similar recovery pattern. These results suggest that continual learning may require better mechanisms for indexing and re-accessing latent computations, not only methods that prevent weight change.

</details>


### [338] [Rethinking the Role of Tensor Decompositions in Post-Training LLM Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03465)
*Artur Zagitov,Alexander Miasnikov,Maxim Krutikov,Vladimir Aletov,Gleb Molodtsov,Nail Bashirov,Artem Tsedenov,Aleksandr Beznosikov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Systematic evaluation of tensor decompositions for post-training compression across dense and MoE LLMs reveals a mismatch between assumed shared subspaces and heterogeneous representations, highlighting limits and practical roles for large-scale deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify whether tensorization remains effective at scale and in heterogeneous architectures, addressing gaps left by prior narrow evaluations.

Method: Empirical and theoretical analysis across dense and mixture-of-experts architectures; large-scale experiments; analysis of subspace sharing; evaluation of compression performance and deployment implications.

Result: Identified a fundamental mismatch: the shared subspaces assumed by tensor decompositions do not align with the heterogeneous representations learned by modern LLMs; delineated practical limits and viable roles for deployment; provided code for reproducibility (GitHub link).

Conclusion: Tensor compression via tensor decompositions has limited universal applicability for large-scale LLM deployment due to representation heterogeneity, but may be viable in specific components or under particular constraints with careful design.

Abstract: Post-training compression is essential for deploying large language models (LLMs) under tight resource constraints. Tensor decompositions have emerged as a promising direction, offering compact parameterizations well suited to Transformer weight structures. However, existing studies evaluate these methods in narrow settings, leaving unclear whether tensorization is effective at large-scale deployment. We systematically evaluate tensor compression across dense and MoE architectures, establishing performance trade-offs grounded in both empirical analysis and theoretical analysis. We identify a fundamental mismatch between the shared subspaces assumed by tensor decompositions and the heterogeneous representations learned by modern LLMs, thereby delineating their practical limits and clarifying their viable role in large-scale deployment. The code is available at https://github.com/brain-lab-research/TT-LLM.

</details>


### [339] [Analyzing Stream Collapse in Hyper-Connections: From Diagnosis to Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03483)
*Ekaterina Alimaskina,Gleb Molodtsov,Aleksandr Beznosikov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hyper-Connections (HC) with multiple residual streams often behaves like a single-stream system due to near-identity residual mixing and dominant-stream usage; initializing with symmetry breaking improves performance across HC variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how permutation symmetry among HC residual streams is resolved in practice and whether HC enables balanced cross-stream information exchange or tends to collapse to a dominant stream.

Method: Fine-grained diagnostics on HC-based language models to trace multi-stream usage, analyze residual mixing, assess signal distribution across streams, and test symmetry-breaking initializations across mHC variants.

Result: Residual mixing after an early seeding stage remains close to identity; a single dominant stream captures most signal and features, causing underutilization of multi-stream capacity. Breaking symmetry at initialization reduces dominant behavior and improves performance across mHC variants.

Conclusion: Symmetry breaking at initialization is beneficial for HC to realize its multi-stream potential; future design should encourage early symmetry disruption to avoid dominant-stream collapse.

Abstract: Hyper-Connections (HC) replace the single Transformer residual stream with multiple streams, introducing a permutation symmetry over stream indices. We study how this symmetry is resolved in practice: whether streams specialize in a balanced way or exhibit dominant-stream usage. Using fine-grained diagnostics for HC-based language models, we trace how multi-stream representations are actually used. We find that after an early seeding stage, residual mixing often remains close to identity, limiting a core HC mechanism for exchanging information between streams. Moreover, both signal and interpretable features concentrate in a dominant stream, and the nominally multi-stream residual connection can underutilize its capacity, behaving closer to a single-stream residual pathway. Finally, we show that breaking symmetry at stream initialization reduces dominant behavior and improves performance across \textit{m}HC variants. Our code is publicly available.

</details>


### [340] [HiSE: A Lightweight Hierarchical Semantic Explainer for Heterogeneous Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03495)
*Zongrui Li,Yuhang Zhao,Ying Zhao,Yuanzhao Guo,Qiang Huang,Yuan Tian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HiSE is a lightweight, semantically aware explanation framework for HGNNs that uses LASSO-based local surrogates per semantic view and KL-divergence-based cross-semantic fusion to yield faithful and efficient explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interpretability in heterogeneous graph neural networks (HGNNs) is challenging due to lack of fidelity to the model's semantic hierarchy and high computational costs of feature explanations; a semantically principled, efficient explanation method is needed for high-stakes applications.

Method: HiSE introduces hierarchical semantic modeling: (1) semantic level — local surrogate models built with LASSO to learn sparse feature representations under each semantic view; (2) cross-semantic level — adaptively weighting the contributions of different semantic views via KL divergence to produce a unified explanation.

Result: Extensive experiments show HiSE achieves higher fidelity, robustness, and cross-semantic explanation capability than existing methods, while its lightweight design reduces computational overhead, suitable for large-scale heterogeneous graphs.

Conclusion: HiSE provides semantically aware, faithful, and efficient explanations for HGNNs, addressing both fidelity to the model's semantic decisions and computational efficiency, enabling scalable deployment in real-world heterogeneous graphs.

Abstract: Heterogeneous graph neural networks (HGNNs) have demonstrated remarkable performance in modeling complex relational data, however their interpretability in high-stakes applications remains a critical challenge. Existing explanation methods suffer from two major limitations: on the one hand, the generated explanations fail to reflect the inherent semantic hierarchy of HGNNs, resulting in a lack of fidelity to the model's internal decision-making mechanism; on the other hand, feature explanations often rely on complex search or perturbation mechanisms, leading to excessive computational complexity and poor efficiency. To address these issues, we propose HiSE, a lightweight feature-oriented interpretable model for HGNNs. HiSE achieves semantically aware feature explanations through hierarchical semantic modeling: at the semantic level, local surrogate models based on the Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (LASSO) are employed to learn sparse feature representations under each semantic view; at the cross-semantic level, the contributions of different semantic views are adaptively characterized via KL divergence to produce a unified explanation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HiSE outperforms existing methods in terms of fidelity, robustness, and cross-semantic explanation capability, while its lightweight framework incurs low computational overhead, enabling efficient application to large-scale, complex real-world heterogeneous graphs.

</details>


### [341] [Demystifying Pipeline Parallelism: First Theory for PipeDream](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03498)
*Ivan Ilin,Peter Richtárik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Randomized PipeDream (RPD) for pipeline model parallelism, providing the first clean nonconvex convergence guarantee for a PD-style method; derives a delay scaling of PD with S stages and compares PD to LocalSGD; empirically PD beats LocalSGD on quadratic and small LM tasks, while LocalSGD can win with more stages on logistic regression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand convergence properties and scalability of pipeline model parallelism (PipeDream) and to benchmark against LocalSGD, addressing how staleness from pipelined updates affects optimization in nonconvex settings.

Method: Proposes Randomized PipeDream (RPD) as a stale block-SGD abstraction; develops a nonconvex convergence guarantee for a PD-style method; derives an explicit delay growth with S stages (≈ S^2 - S/2 + O(1)) and shows the stale-read term in the convergence bound scales as Θ(γ^2 S^4) (or Θ(S^4/K) in a tuned-rate form); compares PD with LocalSGD; supports findings with simulated-time experiments across a quadratic objective, a small language-modeling loss task, and logistic regression.

Result: First clean nonconvex convergence guarantee for a PD-style method via RPD; detailed scaling law indicating large-staleness for PD as stages grow; empirical results showing PD can outperform LocalSGD on quadratic and small LM tasks, while LocalSGD may be superior for logistic regression as the number of stages increases.

Conclusion: RPD provides a rigorous theoretical foundation for PD-style pipeline parallelism, quantifying how staleness scales with the number of stages and guiding method choice (PD vs LocalSGD) depending on the objective and stage count; practical implication is that PD is favorable for certain objectives but may deteriorate with many stages, where LocalSGD becomes advantageous.

Abstract: Training modern machine learning models increasingly requires computation to be distributed across many accelerators. Data parallelism remains the default choice and is often paired with tensor-parallel sharding, but model parallelism becomes unavoidable once parameters, activations, or optimizer states no longer fit on a single device. This paper studies pipeline model parallelism through the lens of PipeDream (PD) (Harlap et al., 2018). Our first contribution is theoretical: we introduce Randomized PipeDream (RPD), a stale block-SGD abstraction that yields, to our knowledge, the first clean nonconvex convergence guarantee for a PD-style method. Our second contribution is a scaling diagnosis: we prove that the delay induced by steady-state PD grows as $S^2 - S/2 + O(1)$ for $S$ stages, so the stale-read contribution in the convergence theorem scales as $Θ(γ^2 S^4)$, equivalently as $Θ(S^4/K)$ in the tuned-rate form. Our third contribution is a comparison with LocalSGD, whose periodic model averaging trades weight staleness for synchronization bubbles. In our reported simulated-time experiments, the better-performing method depends on the objective: PD performs better on the quadratic objective and on a small language-modeling training-loss task, while for logistic regression LocalSGD becomes superior as the number of stages increases.

</details>


### [342] [Post-Hoc Robustness for Model-Based Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03521)
*Siemen Herremans,Ali Anwar,Siegfried Mercelis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-hoc robustness enhancement for deep RL using model-based MPC with adversarial rollouts, improving robustness without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve real-world applicability of RL by defending against adversarial/environment perturbations and avoid costly retraining by performing robustness at inference time.

Method: Use a learned transition model with a nominal policy and perform a robust policy improvement step via model-predictive control under adversarial rollouts. Adversarial rollouts are approximated with projected gradient descent within a bounded uncertainty set, and offline rollouts are mitigated for out-of-distribution issues.

Result: Significant robustness improvements in perturbed Gymnasium MuJoCo environments under post-hoc inference constraints, balancing robustness with computational limits.

Conclusion: Post-hoc robustification can meaningfully enhance robustness of deep RL without additional training, by leveraging a learned model and nominal policy within an MPC framework.

Abstract: To improve the real-world applicability of reinforcement learning (RL), the field of adversarially robust RL studies how to train agents under adversarial environment perturbations. In this setting, a protagonist agent optimizes a policy under environmental perturbations from an adversary, resulting in a zero-sum Markov game. When adversarially robust RL is combined with model-based RL, the adversary can target a learned transition model instead of the training environment. Extending this idea, this work introduces post-hoc robustification of deep RL agents at inference time. By using the learned model in combination with a trained nominal policy, our approach performs a robust policy improvement step. The goal is to improve robustness without any additional training of neural networks. Specifically, we utilize model-predictive control under adversarial rollouts, which are approximated via projected gradient descent within a bounded uncertainty set. Furthermore, these offline rollouts are performed while considering and mitigating out-of-distribution issues. The proposed methodology is validated by demonstrating significant improvements in robustness when the algorithm is evaluated in perturbed Gymnasium MuJoCo environments, while considering the computational limitations of the post-hoc inference setting.

</details>


### [343] [When Should the Teacher Move? Temporal Coupling and Stability in Self On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03532)
*Haowei Guo,Baolong Bi,Ruicheng Zhang,Bingqian Sun,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Isolation-period teacher updates stabilize self on-policy distillation; a diagnostic framework reveals state-oblivious collapse under clocked refresh; CGTR gating honors consolidation to achieve zero collapse and state-of-the-art results across four domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify how the teacher update schedule affects stability in self-distillation and move beyond teacher age as a stability variable; develop diagnostics and a robust refresh mechanism.

Method: Empirical schedule sweeps on Qwen3-8B; define isolation periods; introduce temporal KL structure, refresh shock, length-tail risk; analyze collapse modes; propose Consolidation-Gated Teacher Refresh (CGTR); evaluate on four tasks with a single shared parameter set.

Result: Isolation periods are key to stability; short-horizon clocked refresh can cause irreversible copying of a drifting student (state-oblivious collapse); CGTR yields zero collapse and best final scores across Chemistry, Biology, Physics, ToolUse with adaptive refresh.

Conclusion: Effective stability in self on-policy distillation can be achieved by enforcing isolation periods and gating teacher refresh on consolidation signals; CGTR offers robust, task-adaptive updates without per-task retuning.

Abstract: Self on-policy distillation trains a student policy against a teacher derived from its own parameter history, yet the teacher's update schedule -- which governs the \emph{temporal coupling} between teacher and student -- has not been systematically studied as a stability variable. Through a controlled schedule sweep on Qwen3-8B, we establish that \emph{isolation periods}, defined as complete teacher freezing between updates, are the key structural property enabling stable learning, not teacher age. To characterize these underlying training dynamics, we introduce a diagnostic framework of temporal KL structure, refresh shock, and length-tail risk. This framework further uncovers \emph{state-oblivious collapse}: optimal short-horizon fixed schedules catastrophically fail under long-horizon training because a clock-driven refresh can copy a transiently drifting student into the teacher in a single, irreversible step. This failure mode is invisible under short-horizon evaluation and mechanistically distinct from EMA's chronic contamination. To address this, we propose \emph{Consolidation-Gated Teacher Refresh} (CGTR), which preserves isolation periods while gating each refresh on joint evidence of reward improvement and length-tail safety, ensuring every teacher movement responds to genuine student consolidation rather than a clock signal. With a single shared parameter set and no per-dataset retuning, CGTR achieves \textbf{zero collapse} and the best final score on all four tasks (Chemistry, Biology, Physics, ToolUse), self-regulating its refresh frequency to each task's learning dynamics.

</details>


### [344] [How Many Trees in a Random Forest? A Revisited Approach with Plateau Search and Optuna Integration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03549)
*Vadim Porvatov,Andrey Dukhovny,Andrey Lange*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a triplet-based plateau-search for RF hyperparameter optimization that removes the number-of-trees from TPE space, using relative OOB-score changes across triplets to adaptively identify a near-minimal ensemble size; includes theoretical analysis and empirical validation with publicly available code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard HPO for Random Forests benefits from increasing ensemble size, but the optimal tree count is data-dependent and introduces a need to avoid fixed ranges or premature stopping. Existing methods (TPE, Hyperband) either require predefined search bounds or are sensitive to noise; a robust, automated approach that determines near-minimal ensemble size is desirable.

Method: An integrated triplet-based plateau-search that excludes the number of trees from the direct TPE search space. It tracks a near-minimal ensemble size by monitoring relative changes in the out-of-bag score across a triplet of forest sizes and shifts the triplet as sufficient improvement ceases. The approach is parameterized by a tolerance and leverages information accumulated over HPO trials.

Result: Empirical results show that the selected number of trees can differ substantially from common heuristics: generally smaller on classical benchmarks but larger on high-dimensional bioinformatics datasets such as Arcene and Dorothea. The authors provide source code and reproducible experiments.

Conclusion: The paper presents an automated, interpretable procedure for selecting tree counts in RF HPO, backed by theoretical links between the relative OOB-score criterion and the score gap, plus an asymptotic variance estimate. This method enables robust, data-adaptive pruning of the search space and can yield dataset-dependent improvements.

Abstract: Hyperparameter optimization (HPO) for Random Forest faces a specific difficulty in tuning the number of trees: the predictive score typically improves monotonically with ensemble size, so standard methods such as Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE) and Hyperband require a predefined search range and often drive the estimate toward its right boundary. Early-stopping strategies avoid fixing such a range, but can be sensitive to score noise and prone to premature stopping. To address this, we propose an integrated triplet-based plateau-search algorithm that removes the number of trees from the direct TPE search space and still exploits information accumulated across HPO trials. The method adaptively tracks a near-minimal sufficient ensemble size by monitoring relative changes in the out-of-bag (OOB) score across a triplet of forest sizes and shifting this triplet accordingly. This yields an automated and user-interpretable procedure based on a tolerance parameter. We also provide a theoretical analysis: we relate the proposed relative OOB-score criterion to the gap between the current and limiting scores, and derive an asymptotic variance estimate for the corresponding OOB-based absolute relative difference. Experiments show that the selected number of trees can differ substantially from the common heuristic: for most classical benchmark datasets it is smaller, whereas for some high-dimensional bioinformatics datasets, such as Arcene and Dorothea, it is larger. The source code and reproducible experiments are available at https://github.com/lange-am/rf_plateau_hpo.

</details>


### [345] [Analytical Evaluation of DCA Convergence Properties for Minimizing Prediction Functions of Gaussian RBF Support Vector Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03559)
*Yohei Kakimoto,Yuto Omae,Hirotaka Takahashi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DC programming for RBF-SVR nonconvex optimization yields explicit μ and L bounds, depending on Cα, γ, ρ; a single scalar Cαρ dominates convergence and initialization, revealing two independent pathways (C→Cα, γ→ρ) and enabling pre/post-training convergence assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable tractable convergence analysis and practical guidance for applying the DC algorithm to nonconvex optimization problems arising from training Gaussian-kernel SVR models, by exploiting the RBF structure to obtain explicit bounds.

Method: Derive closed-form lower bound μ of strong convexity and upper bound L of gradient Lipschitz for the DC subproblem; express μ and L in terms of post-training dual-sum Cα, kernel parameter γ, and DC parameter ρ; identify the leading term Cαρ and analyze its role in convergence and initialization; decompose its variation into C→Cα and γ→ρ pathways; validate with experiments on six benchmark functions.

Result: μ and L are determined solely by Cα, γ, and ρ; both share the leading term Cαρ; Cαρ governs convergence properties and initial-point dependence; two independent pathways (C→Cα, γ→ρ) explain how SVR hyperparameters (C, γ) influence convergence; convergence assessment can be done in closed form, pre- and post-training, using the scalar Cαρ.

Conclusion: A practical DC-decomposition framework for RBF-SVR is established, enabling a priori and a posteriori convergence assessment through the single scalar Cαρ; this links SVR hyperparameters to optimization convergence, guiding hyperparameter selection and initialization for improved DCA performance.

Abstract: For nonconvex optimization problems whose objective is the prediction function of a trained Support Vector Regression (SVR) model with the Gaussian radial basis function (RBF) kernel (RBF-SVR), we present a framework that applies the difference of convex functions (DC) algorithm (DCA) by exploiting the analytical structure of the RBF kernel to construct an explicit DC decomposition. Specifically, we derive in closed form both the lower bound $μ$ of the strong convexity parameter of the DC components and the upper bound $L$ of the gradient Lipschitz constant of the subproblem. Both $μ$ and $L$ are determined solely by the post-training dual-coefficient sum $C_α$ and the RBF kernel parameter $γ$, together with the DC decomposition parameter $ρ$, and they share a common leading term $C_αρ$. Through numerical experiments on six benchmark functions, we show that $C_αρ$ is the primary single quantity characterizing both the convergence properties and the initial-point dependence of DCA, and further demonstrate that it decomposes into two independent pathways, $C \to C_α$ and $γ\to ρ$, with its primary variation governed by the SVR hyperparameters $(C, γ)$. Together, these results allow the convergence properties of DCA on RBF-SVR to be assessed in advance through the single scalar quantity $C_αρ$: approximately from $(C, γ)$ before training, and exactly in closed form after training.

</details>


### [346] [Training a Predictive Coding Network on ImageNet using Equilibrium Propagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03584)
*Tugdual Kerjan,Rasmus Høier,Benjamin Scellier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EP-based training for predictive coding networks scaled to ImageNet, achieving 13.23% top-5 on VGG10—close to 12.2% BP—and first ImageNet-scale demonstration of PCNs and EP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scale EP and predictive coding networks (PCNs) beyond small problems; test whether scaling challenges are due to the EP framework or computational properties of physical systems.

Method: Combine the centered variant of Equilibrium Propagation with a novel equilibration scheme for PCNs; train a 10-layer convolutional PCN (VGG10) on full ImageNet.

Result: Achieved 13.23% top-5 error on ImageNet for VGG10 PCN using EP-based training; performance is close to 12.2% BP baseline; first demonstration of PCNs and EP-based training at ImageNet scale.

Conclusion: EP-based training and PCNs can scale to ImageNet, suggesting scaling challenges may lie more in system computational properties than in the EP framework itself.

Abstract: Equilibrium Propagation (EP) is a physics-based training framework that has primarily been employed in energy-based models, including continuous Hopfield networks, nonlinear resistive networks and coupled phase oscillators. However, EP's practical applications have so far remained limited to relatively small-scale problems. Predictive coding networks (PCNs), another class of energy-based models rooted in computational neuroscience, are typically trained with a specialized algorithm and have likewise not yet been demonstrated at large scale. In this work, we develop an EP-based training method for PCNs which combines the centered variant of EP with a novel equilibration scheme for PCNs. Using this approach, we train a 10-layer convolutional PCN (VGG10) on full-size ImageNet, achieving 13.23\% test error rate on the top-5 classification task, close to the 12.2\% backpropagation baseline. To our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of both PCNs and EP-based training at ImageNet scale. These results significantly extend the scalability of both approaches and suggest that the primary challenges in scaling EP in other physical systems may come more from the computational properties of these systems than from inherent limitations of the EP framework.

</details>


### [347] [CauTion: Knowing When to Trust LLMs for Ensemble Causal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03602)
*Bo Peng,Kaiwen Wu,Sirui Chen,Zhiheng Wang,Yu Qiao,Chaochao Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CauTion is a framework that robustly integrates LLM domain knowledge with an ensemble of statistical causal-discovery algorithms via consensus filtering, trust-calibrated arbitration, and cycle repair, achieving high accuracy and robustness across six datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal discovery from observational data is limited by statistical indistinguishability, finite-sample sensitivity, and biases of single algorithms. LLMs offer domain knowledge to aid inference but suffer from errors and high token costs; a single data-centric method inherits its biases.

Method: Three-stage framework: (1) Algorithm ensemble forms a consensus that resolves up to 96% of edges, with near-perfect accuracy on the filtered set. (2) Trust-calibrated arbitration estimates the relative reliability of LLM and algorithms using annotation-free calibration, guiding a trust-weighted voting that limits LLM arbitration to edges with unreliable algorithmic evidence. (3) Cycle repair ensures final causal graph is acyclic. 

Result: CauTion consistently outperforms both data-centric and LLM-augmented baselines across six datasets, with larger gains on larger graphs and demonstrated robustness to LLM errors.

Conclusion: CauTion provides a reliable, scalable approach to integrating LLM knowledge with an ensemble of causal-discovery algorithms, improving accuracy and robustness. The authors release code at: https://github.com/OpenCausaLab/CauTion.

Abstract: Causal discovery from observational data remains challenging due to the fundamental limitations of purely statistical methods, such as statistical distinguishability within equivalence classes and sensitivity to finite sample sizes. While large language models (LLMs) offer a promising source of domain knowledge to complement statistical inference, existing LLM-augmented methods are vulnerable to LLM errors and incur high token costs. Moreover, reliance on a single data-centric algorithm can make results sensitive to algorithm-specific biases. To address these limitations, we propose CauTion, a framework that reliably integrates LLM domain knowledge into an ensemble of statistical causal discovery algorithms through consensus filtering and LLM reliability estimation. CauTion proceeds in three stages. First, an algorithm ensemble utilizes a consensus voting to resolve up to 96% of edges on which algorithms agree, achieving near-perfect accuracy on the filtered consensus edges. Second, a trust-calibrated arbitration mechanism estimates the relative reliability of the LLM and the algorithms via an annotation-free trust calibration procedure, which is then utilized to govern a trust-weighted voting process that restricts LLM arbitration exclusively to edges with unreliable algorithmic evidence. Third, a cycle repair step is applied to guarantee the final causal graph is validly acyclic. Experiments on six datasets demonstrate that CauTion consistently outperforms both data-centric and LLM-augmented baselines, with larger gains on larger graphs and strong robustness to LLM errors. Code is available at https://github.com/OpenCausaLab/CauTion.

</details>


### [348] [Exploiting Verification-Generation Gap: Test-Time Reinforcement Learning with Confidence-Conditioned Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03608)
*Jiahui Li,Jianfeng Shan,Wenpei Chen,Shunyu Wu,Jian Lou,Wenjie Feng,Dan Li,See-Kiong Ng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: We analyze why optimizing Pass@k in label-free test-time RL for LLMs is challenging and present TTRL-CoCoV, a confidence-conditioned verification framework that adapts verification to sample confidence to improve Pass@k, achieving notable gains across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pass@k optimization is essential for assessing generation coverage in label-free test-time RL of LLMs, but existing Pass@1–focused methods underperform for higher k due to labeling noise on low-confidence samples and diversity collapse on high-confidence samples.

Method: Introduce TTRL-CoCoV (Test-Time Reinforcement Learning with Confidence-Conditioned Verification). It uses a three-tier confidence-conditioned strategy: (1) high-confidence samples bootstrap a verifier and receive an exploration-enhancing reward to preserve diversity; (2) low-confidence samples have the pseudo-labels filtered by the verifier; (3) medium-confidence samples bypass verification. This grows Pass@k coverage while improving Pass@1.

Result: Empirical evaluation across six benchmarks shows TTRL-CoCoV surpasses the best competing methods, with average gains of +9.8% in Pass@1 and +18.7% in Pass@16 over TTRL; achieves up to +5.0% absolute Pass@1 improvements over fully supervised RL methods; code available at the provided repository.

Conclusion: Confidence-adaptive verification effectively expands Pass@k coverage and boosts Pass@1 performance in label-free test-time RL for LLMs, with strong empirical gains and practical potential for integration with related RL approaches.

Abstract: Test-time reinforcement learning has emerged as a promising paradigm for enhancing the complex reasoning abilities of large language models in a completely label-free manner. Despite existing studies focusing on Pass@1 performance, optimizing Pass@k remains under-explored yet critical in label-free settings, which measures generation coverage for sustained exploration. Optimizing Pass@k in label-free setting is highly non-trivial, as directly applying the Pass@k advantage designs effective for RLVR yields unsatisfactory performance. Through in-depth empirical analysis, we discover the root causes hindering performance: pseudo-label estimations for low-confidence samples have a high probability of being incorrect, while candidate answers for high-confidence samples suffer from severe diversity collapse. To overcome these hurdles, we propose TTRL-CoCoV (Test-Time Reinforcement Learning with Confidence-Conditioned Verification), a novel confidence-adaptive framework that expands Pass@k coverage and improves Pass@1 performance. Based on our key insight that verification capability generally leads generation capability, TTRL-CoCoV employs a confidence-conditioned mechanism: for high-confidence samples, it bootstraps verifier and applies an exploration-enhancing reward to prevent diversity collapse; for low-confidence samples, it delegates pseudo-label selection to the verifier to filter incorrect pseudo-labels; and for medium-confidence samples, it bypasses verification entirely. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TTRL-CoCoV outperforms the best competing methods across 6 widely-recognized benchmarks, achieves average absolute gains of +9.8% in Pass@1 and +18.7% in Pass@16 over TTRL, and even achieves absolute Pass@1 improvements of up to +5.0% across multiple reasoning benchmarks when compared against fully supervised RL methods. Our code repository: https://github.com/shanjf666/CoCoV.

</details>


### [349] [Physics-Guided Policy Optimization with Self-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03620)
*Ke Wang,Yuning Wu,Haoran Liu,Chaoqun Jia,Devin Chen,Kai Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PGPO introduces a mutual-information–driven, physics-inspired, information-modulated step-size in self-distilled policy optimization to improve stability and accuracy; it preserves SGD guarantees and yields gains over SDPO on Science-QA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SDPO corrections can be highly informative on some batches and misleading on others; a fixed step-size can destabilize training. Need a method to adapt trust in self-generated feedback.

Method: Introduce an information-modulated step-size multiplier based on mutual information between the student's predictions and the feedback-conditioned teacher; formalize via viscous-fluid dynamics analogy and SDE-level analysis; maintain order-1 weak-approximation guarantees; negligible per-iteration overhead.

Result: Empirical evaluation on Science-QA shows PGPO outperforms SDPO on 3 of 4 domains with gains up to 4.5 points; PGPO remains stable in scenarios where SDPO collapses late in training.

Conclusion: PGPO provides a theoretically grounded, stable, and efficient improvement to SDPO with practical gains; relies on MI estimation and an SDE-based framework; further validation across domains would strengthen generality.

Abstract: Self-distilled policy optimization (SDPO) has become a popular paradigm for LLM post-training, where a model learns from its own predictions conditioned on privileged information. SDPO, however, is sensitive to how much each update step should be trusted: corrections from a self-teacher can be highly informative on some batches and misleading on others, and applying them uniformly with a fixed step size can destabilize training. Drawing inspiration from viscous-fluid dynamics and formalizing the analogy at the SDE level, we propose Physics-Guided Policy Optimization (PGPO), which introduces an information-modulated step-size multiplier derived from a mutual-information estimate between the student's predictions and the feedback-conditioned teacher. We show that this modulation preserves the order-1 weak-approximation guarantees of vanilla SGD, and incurs negligible overhead per iteration. We evaluate PGPO on the Science-QA dataset, where it outperforms SDPO on 3 of the 4 domains with gains of up to +4.5 points, while remaining stable in a setting where SDPO collapses late in training.

</details>


### [350] [AnchorMoE: Interpretable Time Series Classification via Anchor-Routed MoE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03631)
*Tao Xie,Zexi Tan,Haoyi Xiao,Mengke Li,Yiqun Zhang,Yang Lu,Cuie Yang,Yiu-ming Cheung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AnchorMoE provides an interpretable-by-construction MTSC framework that uses a Mixture-of-Experts to decompose predictions additively over input segments, with a geometric orthogonality constraint to diversify expert specializations and a reliability gate to suppress background noise, achieving competitive performance while grounding decisions in raw time series.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In high-stakes MTSC tasks, explanations must be ante-hoc and faithful, but real-world signals are sparse, heterogeneous, and noisy. A transparent decomposition of predictions over input segments can improve trust and safety.

Method: AnchorMoE builds a multi-view representation of local patches, routes them to specialized experts within a Mixture-of-Experts architecture, and enforces an exact additive decomposition of the final prediction over input segments. A geometric orthogonality constraint reduces redundancy among experts, promoting diverse, heterogeneous predictive patterns. An uncertainty-aware reliability gate dynamically calibrates each segment's contribution, suppressing background noise.

Result: Experiments on real-world and synthetic benchmarks show that AnchorMoE achieves competitive classification performance while providing faithful, interpretable grounding of decisions in the raw time series.

Conclusion: AnchorMoE demonstrates that interpretable-by-construction MTSC is feasible: through additive decomposition, orthogonalized expert specialization, and a reliability gate, one can obtain competitive accuracy with ante-hoc transparency and robust handling of sparse, noisy signals.

Abstract: Multivariate time series classification (MTSC) is pivotal in high-stakes domains, such as clinical diagnosis and industrial fault detection, where safe deployment necessitates transparent decision-making. However, isolating the temporal segments that drive model predictions is challenging because discriminative signals in real-world time series are typically sparse, heterogeneous, and heavily obscured by background noise. This paper, therefore, proposes AnchorMoE, an interpretable-by-construction classification framework. Built upon a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, AnchorMoE encodes multi-view representations of local patches and routes them to specialized experts, ensuring that the final prediction is formulated as an exact additive decomposition over the input segments, facilitating ante-hoc transparency rather than relying on post-hoc estimations. To maintain the reliability of this decomposition under sparse signal distributions, we introduce a geometric orthogonality constraint that penalizes representational redundancy, compelling distinct experts to specialize in heterogeneous predictive patterns. Furthermore, an uncertainty-aware reliability gate is designed to dynamically calibrate the contribution of each segment, effectively suppressing residual background noise. Extensive experiments on real-world and synthetic benchmarks demonstrate that AnchorMoE achieves highly competitive classification performance while faithfully grounding its decisions in the raw time series.

</details>


### [351] [Spatial Transcriptomics-Guided Alignment Enhances Molecular Profiling in Pathology Foundation Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03644)
*Fengtao Zhou,Yingxue Xu,Zhengyu Zhang,Yihui Wang,Zhengrui Guo,Ling Liang,Jiabo Ma,Cheng Jin,Ziyi Liu,Huajun Zhou,Hongyi Wang,Du Cai,Chenglong Zhao,Xi Wang,Can Yang,Yu Wang,Wenbin Li,Feng Gao,Zhe Wang,Zhenhui Li,Xiuming Zhang,Li Liang,Hao Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STAMP integrates spatial transcriptomics-guided supervision into pathology foundation models to imbue molecular awareness, via pathway-level alignment and efficient fine-tuning, supported by a new HumanST-1k dataset of 1.8M H&E patch–transcriptomic pairs; validated through multi-tier clinical evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome cost, tissue exhaustion, and long turnaround in molecular profiling by enabling PFMs to infer molecular phenotypes from histology, leveraging spatially resolved transcriptomic data.

Method: Develop Spatial Transcriptomics-guided Alignment for Molecular Profiling (STAMP); curate HumanST-1k ST dataset; perform pathway-informed aggregation of transcriptomic data; integrate into PFMs using parameter-efficient fine-tuning; evaluate across multi-tier clinical tasks.

Result: Demonstrates enrichment of PFMs with molecularly aware representations, enabling resolution of sub-visual molecular signatures; validation via a multi-tier evaluation framework shows clinical utility of the augmented models.

Conclusion: STAMP and the HumanST-1k resource advance molecularly aware histology models, linking histological structures to functional molecular states and potentially improving precision oncology workflows with efficient, spatially informed supervision.

Abstract: Comprehensive molecular profiling is essential for modern precision oncology but remains hindered by prohibitive costs, specimen exhaustion, and protracted turnaround times. While pathology foundation models (PFMs) have demonstrated potential for inferring molecular phenotypes from routine hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) whole-slide images (WSIs), current architectures primarily rely on vision-centric self-supervised learning or vision-language alignment, lacking the spatially resolved molecular supervision required to connect subtle morphological features with underlying genomic alterations. Spatial transcriptomics (ST) emerges as a transformative technology that enables transcriptomic quantification within intact tissue sections, thereby preserving the precise spatial link between histology and molecular profiles. In this study, we present a Spatial Transcriptomics-guided Alignment framework for Molecular Profiling (STAMP), which endows PFMs with intrinsic molecular awareness. To support this paradigm, we curated HumanST-1k, a human ST dataset spanning diverse anatomical organs and sequencing platforms. This atlas yields 1.8 million pairs of H&E patches and corresponding transcriptomic profiles, providing a corpus that links histological structures with their molecular states. To mitigate the technical noise inherent to raw transcriptomics, STAMP applies a pathway-informed alignment strategy that aggregates transcriptomic data into biologically functional pathways, which are subsequently integrated into PFMs via parameter-efficient fine-tuning. This alignment enriches the representation space of PFMs and unlocks their capacity to resolve sub-visual molecular signatures. The clinical utility of these augmented representations was validated through a multi-tier evaluation framework.

</details>


### [352] [The Shape of Addition: Geometric Structures of Arithmetic in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03645)
*Liuyuan Wen,Xun Zhu,Lihao Huang,Wenbin Li,Yang Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Iso-Raw-Sum Trajectory (IRST): a geometric structure in residual streams underlying multi-operand addition; explains arithmetic errors via Noisy Quantization Model and geometric slippages due to internal noise; introduces Probe Versatility to disentangle latent signals; provides a geometric consistency check to detect and correct quantization failures; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain the paradoxical fragility of LLM arithmetic by linking internal continuous representations to discrete output behavior and to develop diagnostic/probing tools for reliable numeric reasoning.

Method: Analyze residual-stream geometry during multi-operand addition to define IRST, formulate the Noisy Quantization Model (carry potentials with noise crossing quantization thresholds), and demonstrate Probe Versatility by using lightweight probes to separate coexisting latent signals; validate with a geometric consistency check for inference-time correction.

Result: Characterization of a geometric structure governing addition in LLMs, identification of geometric slippages causing errors, demonstration that probes can disentangle latent signals, and a practical geometric consistency check for error detection/correction. Code released at the provided URL.

Conclusion: A geometric framework ties internal representations to discrete arithmetic outputs, enabling diagnostic and corrective tools for more reliable numeric reasoning in LLMs.

Abstract: Large Language Models exhibit paradoxical fragility in fundamental arithmetic, implying a disconnect between internal computation and discrete output. By analyzing the residual stream geometry during multi-operand addition, we identify the Iso-Raw-Sum Trajectory (IRST), a geometric structure where representations are anchored by semantic digits and modulated by continuous carry fibers. We propose the Noisy Quantization Model to explain this geometry, framing arithmetic errors as Geometric Slippages caused by internal neural noise pushing a continuous, latent Carry Potential across quantization thresholds. This geometric framework further elucidates Probe Versatility, explaining how lightweight probes can disentangle coexisting latent signals (such as ground truth versus hallucination) from a single activation vector. Finally, we validate these insights through a geometric consistency check method that effectively detects and corrects these quantization failures during inference. Our code is available at https://github.com/RL-MIND/Shape-of-Addition.

</details>


### [353] [Speedrunning Tabular Foundation Model Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03681)
*Salih Bora Ozturk,Alexander Pfefferle,Frank Hutter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A community 'speedrun' protocol standardizes pretraining speedups for nanoTabPFN on TabArena, achieving a very fast target time (0.92 min) and large speedups with fewer synthetic datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pretraining cost is a major bottleneck for tabular foundation models; a simple, reproducible method is needed to compare, verify, and accumulate speedups across contributions.

Method: Introduce a 'community speedrun' where contributors modify a single-file training script and aim to reach a fixed downstream ROC AUC target on subsampled TabArena using one NVIDIA L40S GPU; maintain a public leaderboard and provide code at the referenced repository.

Result: Current best reaches the target in 0.92 minutes, an 81x speedup over the 74.32-minute baseline, while using 22x fewer synthetic datasets.

Conclusion: The speedrun format offers a simple, verifiable protocol for the community to add and stack pretraining improvements, with an open leaderboard; code and records are publicly available to foster ongoing collaboration.

Abstract: Pretraining cost is a major bottleneck for research on tabular foundation models, slowing the iteration cycle for new architectures, priors, and optimization ideas. Yet the community lacks a simple way to compare and accumulate pretraining speedups. We introduce a community speedrun for nanoTabPFN: contributors modify a single-file training script and compete to reach a fixed downstream ROC AUC target on subsampled TabArena using one NVIDIA L40S GPU. The current best record reaches the target in 0.92 minutes, an 81x speedup over the 74.32 minute baseline while using 22x fewer synthetic datasets. The speedrun format provides a simple protocol for the community to add, verify, and stack pretraining improvements, with the leaderboard open to contributions. Code and records are available at https://github.com/borawhocodess/modded-nanotabpfn.

</details>


### [354] [Constitutional On-Policy Safe Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03089)
*Ming Wen,Yuxuan Liu,Kun Yang,Yunhao Feng,Zhuoer Xu,Yuhao Sun,Shiwen Cui,Xiang Zheng,Xingjun Ma,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: COPSD addresses safety OPSD contraction by diagnosing geometric leakage under safety boundaries in a non-orthogonal semantic space and introduces a cross-SFT cold-start plus constitution-conditioned on-policy distillation to improve safety–helpfulness trade-off with less tax on general reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve safety alignment in on-policy self-distillation by mitigating distribution contraction and expressiveness loss caused by constitutional conditioning and reverse KL, while preserving or enhancing helpfulness.

Method: 1) Diagnose: identify geometric leakage where safety pressure contracts the teacher distribution toward conservative responses. 2) Propose COPSD: a two-stage pipeline—Cross-SFT cold-start to calibrate the teacher, followed by constitution-conditioned on-policy distillation.

Result: Empirical validation on 12 benchmarks showing COPSD yields a stronger safety–helpfulness trade-off than baselines, with reduced safety tax on general reasoning ability.

Conclusion: Constitution-conditioned on-policy distillation, when preceded by a cross-SFT calibration, mitigates safety-induced expressiveness loss and improves overall task performance on safety–generalization axes.

Abstract: On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) has emerged as an efficient post-training paradigm by using a teacher conditioned on privileged information to provide dense token-level supervision. Prior work has shown that OPSD can collapse in verifiable reasoning tasks, but safety alignment differs in that it is guided by high-level constitutions rather than explicit target answers, making it a natural setting to revisit dense distillation. However, our pilot study show that safety OPSD still suffers from severe collapse: constitutional conditioning contracts the teacher distribution toward short and overly conservative responses, and Reverse KL further amplifies this contraction into reduced expressiveness. We formalize this effect as geometric leakage under safety boundaries in a non-orthogonal semantic space, where safety pressure transfers into the expressiveness dimension. Based on this analysis, we propose Constitutional On-Policy Safe Distillation (COPSD), which first calibrates the teacher through a Cross-SFT cold-start and then performs constitution-conditioned on-policy distillation. Experiments on 12 benchmarks show that COPSD achieves a consistently stronger safety--helpfulness trade-off than baselines while substantially reducing the safety tax on general reasoning ability.

</details>


### [355] [A Close Look At World Model Recovery In Supervised Fine-Tuned LLM Planners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03685)
*Patrick Emami,Nan Qiang,Peter Graf*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SFT enables linear encoding of action validity and some state predicates in planning LLMs; internal representations can separate valid/invalid actions even when output probabilities fail; broader fine-tuning data improves world-model recovery; the work provides an interpretability recipe and insights into knowledge representation in LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether LLMs trained on planning tasks acquire internal world-model representations, not just end-to-end planning ability, and to develop interpretability methods for planning LLMs.

Method: A series of interpretability experiments combining analysis of internal representations and generative capabilities. The authors fine-tune LLMs with supervised action-sequence data, assess linear separability of action validity and state predicates in hidden representations, compare reliance on output probabilities for classification versus latent representations, and vary training data coverage (e.g., random-walk trajectories) to evaluate impact on world-model recovery.

Result: a) After SFT, action validity and some state predicates are linearly encodable in the model's internal representations. b) Models that do not effectively use output probabilities for action validity classification can still maintain internal representations that separate valid from invalid actions. c) Greater state-space coverage during fine-tuning (e.g., random walk data) yields more accurate recovery of the underlying world model.

Conclusion: The work provides a practical interpretability recipe for planning LLMs and offers insights into how world-model knowledge is represented in LLMs, addressing open questions about representation in planning contexts.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) improves end-to-end classical planning in large language models (LLMs), but do these models also learn to represent and reason about the planning problems they are solving? Due to the relative complexity of classical planning problems and the challenge that end-to-end plan generation poses for LLMs, it has been difficult to explore this question. In our work, we devise and perform a series of interpretability experiments that holistically interrogate world model recovery by examining both internal representations and generative capabilities of fine-tuned LLMs. We find that: a) Supervised fine-tuning on valid action sequences enables LLMs to linearly encode action validity and some state predicates. b) Models that struggle to use output probabilities for classifying action validity may still learn internal representations that separate valid from invalid actions. c) Broader state space coverage during fine-tuning, such as from random walk data, yields more accurate recovery of the underlying world model. In summary, this work contributes a recipe for applying interpretability techniques to planning LLMs and generates insights that shed light on open questions about how knowledge is represented in LLMs.

</details>


### [356] [Staying Alive: Uncensored Survival Analysis with Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03689)
*Mariana Vargas Vieyra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A training-free survival regression framework using Tabular Foundation Models (TFMs) to construct an Accelerated Failure Time (AFT) model with a single scalar parameter, complemented by a Buckley-James-based non-parametric imputation for right-censored data, yielding competitive performance against trained survival models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Right-censoring complicates survival analysis and many effective methods require training; leveraging powerful Tabular Foundation Models in a training-free setup could enable accurate time-to-event predictions without dataset-specific fitting.

Method: Use a TFM to instantiate an Accelerated Failure Time model and estimate a single scalar parameter; apply Buckley-James non-parametric estimator to iteratively impute right-censored observations in-context.

Result: The proposed method achieves competitive performance on standard survival analysis benchmarks compared with trained models such as Cox regression and parametric AFT models, despite requiring no training beyond the scalar parameter.

Conclusion:  demonstrates that TFMs can enable effective training-free survival regression by combining AFT modeling with Buckley-James-based imputation for censored data.

Abstract: Survival Analysis (SA) is a statistical framework that models the time span until some event of interest occurs. Widely used in several domains, including healthcare and churn prediction, a central challenge in its applicability stems from the time of the event being partially observed or \emph{right-censoring}.
  Tabular Foundation Models (TFM) have attracted significant interest in recent years due to their ability to perform prediction tasks in a single forward pass, requiring no dataset-specific parameter fitting. Despite their success, their application to prediction tasks on time-to-event data remains difficult due to right censoring. In this work, we present a training-free method to survival regression by leveraging TFMs to both predict the time of the event and iteratively impute right-censored data.
  Our method uses a TFM to construct an Accelerated Failure Time (AFT) model requiring no training beyond fitting a single scalar parameter. Subsequently, by building on the Buckley-James estimator, we introduce a non-parametric in-context estimator for right-censored data. Our experiments on standard survival analysis benchmarks show that our method is competitive with several parametric and semi-parametric survival regression models that require training, including Cox regression and parametric AFT models.

</details>


### [357] [Multi$^2$: Hierarchical Multi-Agent Decision-Making with LLM-Based Agents in Interactive Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03698)
*Sangeun Park,Minhae Kwon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Multi^2, a hierarchical two-system framework for LLM agents: System 1 generates context-aware sub-goals via supervised fine-tuning; System 2 executes actions via offline-to-online RL, improving long-horizon stability and reducing objective drift, with new hierarchical benchmarks showing robust multi-turn performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle objective drift and fragility in long-horizon decision-making of LLM-based agents by introducing explicit hierarchical roles and separation of planning and action Execution.

Method: Decompose agent behavior into System 1 (high-level, context-aware sub-goal generation via SFT) and System 2 (low-level, atomic action execution via offline-to-online RL in interactive environments); train and evaluate on newly released hierarchical benchmark datasets for hierarchical decision-making in LLM agents.

Result: Multi^2 consistently outperform strong agentic baselines across diverse interactive environments, showing improved robustness and coordination in multi-turn interactions.

Conclusion: Hierarchical decomposition into distinct planning and execution modules mitigates objective drift and yields stable long-horizon control, with benchmark datasets addressing a prior gap in evaluating hierarchical decision-making for LLM agents.

Abstract: A central goal of large language model (LLM) research is to build agentic systems that can plan, act, and adapt through sustained interaction with dynamic environments. While recent LLM-based agents exhibit impressive contextual reasoning, their long-horizon decision-making remains fragile, often suffering from objective drift, where goals and plans drift over extended interactions. We introduce Multi$^2$, a hierarchical multi-agent decision-making framework that explicitly decomposes agent behavior into complementary roles. A high-level agent (System 1) focuses on context-aware sub-goal generation using supervised fine-tuning (SFT), while a low-level agent (System 2) executes atomic actions through offline-to-online reinforcement learning (RL) in interactive environments. This separation enables stable long-horizon control, mitigates objective drift, and allows efficient adaptation. Across diverse interactive environments, Multi$^2$ consistently outperforms strong agentic baselines, demonstrating improved robustness and coordination in multi-turn interaction. Beyond performance, we introduce and release three hierarchical benchmark datasets, filling a long-standing gap in training and evaluating hierarchical decision-making for LLM-based agents.

</details>


### [358] [When Graph Tokens Sink: A Mechanistic Analysis of Graph Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03712)
*Ding Zhang,Runtao Zhou,Wenqing Zheng,Rizal Fathony,Bayan Bruss,Chirag Agarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GLMs show activation-level saliency of graph tokens does not correspond to graph-information usage; graph sink tokens are activation outliers but not the main carriers of structure, revealing a decoupling between saliency and semantic utility in current graph-token representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how LLMs internally interpret graph tokens and whether graph tokens meaningfully encode topology in GLMs.

Method: Analyze GLM architectures by examining graph-token activations and attention; identify graph sink tokens as activation outliers; perform pruning, repositioning, and swapping interventions to assess token importance for downstream predictions.

Result: Graph sink tokens exhibit large activation values and bias toward early positions but do not consistently carry graph information; they are not the dominant tokens attracting attention from queries; pruning/repositioning/swapping show sink tokens are not the most semantically or structurally important tokens for the model’s output.

Conclusion: There is a decoupling between activation-level saliency and graph-semantic utility in GLMs, indicating limitations in current graph-token construction/placement/alignment; graph structure is not naturally preserved as a topology-aware internal representation after tokenization.

Abstract: Graph Language Models (GLMs) have become a promising direction for adapting Large Language Models (LLMs) to graph learning tasks. By transforming graph topology and node information into graph tokens, GLMs allow LLMs to jointly process structured graph inputs and textual instructions. Yet, it remains unclear how LLMs internally interpret these graph tokens and whether graph tokens act as meaningful carriers of graph structure. In this work, we analyze how LLMs process graph information through graph-token behavior in representative GLM architectures.
  Findings. We find that the internal saliency of graph tokens in GLMs is not equivalent to graph information utilization. Graph sink tokens consistently emerge as activation-level outliers: they can be identified by massive activation values along a small set of hidden-state dimensions and are biased toward early graph-token positions. However, this activation-level saliency does not imply that these tokens are the main carriers of graph information. Unlike classical attention sinks in language and vision-language models, graph sink tokens do not necessarily attract the largest attention weights from query tokens. Through pruning, repositioning, and swapping interventions, we show that graph sink tokens are not the most important semantic or structural tokens for downstream prediction.
  Implications. Together, these results suggest that after current GLMs map graph structure into the LLM token space, the resulting graph-token representations do not naturally form a fully usable topology-aware internal representation; instead, they exhibit a decoupling between activation-level saliency and graph-semantic utility. This decoupling points to limitations in existing graph-token construction, placement, and alignment mechanisms.

</details>


### [359] [Compress then Merge: From Multiple LoRAs into One Low-Rank Adapter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03723)
*Zhengbao He,Ruiqi Ding,Zhehao Huang,Ruikai Yang,Tao Li,Xiaolin Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Compress-then-Merge (CtM) enforces a rank-r bottleneck before merging multiple LoRAs, guaranteeing a rank-r LoRA, enabling efficient core-space merging, and outperforming single-LoRA baselines while approaching full-parameter merging performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA adapters are proliferating across tasks, fragmenting capabilities and complicating reuse/deployment. There is a need to merge multiple task-specific LoRAs into a single, rank-constrained LoRA without destroying low-rank structure.

Method: CtM computes shared r-dimensional subspaces from the LoRA weights alone, projects each adapter into these subspaces to obtain r×r coordinates, and then merges in this reduced space using standard rules. This ensures a rank-r LoRA by construction and reduces computation to the core subspace spanned by the concatenated LoRA factors.

Result: Across multiple models and tasks, CtM consistently outperforms single-LoRA-output baselines and narrows the performance gap to full-parameter merging methods.

Conclusion: CtM provides a rank-r guaranteed, efficient merging pipeline for multiple LoRAs by performing the merge in a shared core subspace, improving practical deployment and performance relative to existing approaches.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) enables parameter-efficient specialization of foundation models, but the proliferation of task-specific adapters fragments capabilities across many adapters, complicating reuse and deployment. We study the problem of merging $T$ LoRAs into a single rank-$r$ LoRA, thereby preserving the benefits of low-rank structure.
