Existing Merge-then-Compress pipelines treat the rank constraint as an afterthought: they merge adapters in the full parameter space, then compress the merged result to rank $r$ via truncated SVD. However, full-parameter merging may destroy the low-rank structure, making it difficult for subsequent compression to recover an effective rank-$r$ LoRA. We propose Compress-then-Merge (CtM), a reversed pipeline that enforces the rank-$r$ bottleneck before merging: CtM computes shared $r$-dimensional subspaces using only the LoRA weights to capture cross-adapter common structure, projects each adapter into the shared subspaces to obtain $r\times r$ coordinates, and then applies standard merging rules in this reduced space. CtM guarantees a rank-$r$ LoRA by construction, avoiding post-hoc truncation, and enables efficient computation in the core space spanned by concatenated LoRA factors. Experiments across multiple models and tasks show that CtM consistently outperforms existing single-LoRA-output baselines while narrowing the performance gap to full-parameter merging methods.

</details>


### [360] [GFFMERGE: Efficient Merging of Graph Neural Force Fields and Beyond](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03232)
*Parth Verma,Parv P. Singh,Vipul Garg,Ishita Thakre,N. M. Anoop Krishnan,Sayan Ranu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A principled, closed-form GNN model-merging framework (GFFMERGE/GNNMERGE) that aligns embeddings to merge specialized GNNs for neural force fields, achieving large speedups and data-efficient convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient adaptation of GNN-based neural force fields to new chemical systems is costly; retraining foundation models is expensive. A modular, fast merging approach is needed to compose specialized models without full retraining.

Method: Leverage the linear structure of message-passing layers to cast model merging as a convex embedding-alignment problem with an analytical (closed-form) solution; introduce GFFMERGE for force-field GNNs and GNNMERGE as a generic variant; perform systematic benchmarking against vision-language baselines on force-field regression tasks.

Result: GFFMERGE/GNNMERGE recover performance close to gold-standard joint training; vision-language methods catastrophically fail in this domain; achieve 5–27× speedups across molecular (MD17, MD22), solid-state (LiPS20), and large-scale graph benchmarks; closed-form solution provides a strong initialization leading to faster data-efficient convergence.

Conclusion: Closed-form model merging is viable and advantageous for GNNs in neural force fields, enabling modular composition and significant computational gains; the approach relies on the linearity of MP layers and embedding alignment, with strong initialization benefits, though potential limitations include applicability to non-linear or non-convex merging scenarios and domain shifts.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have revolutionized Neural Force Fields for atomistic simulations, achieving near-quantum accuracy at reduced cost, yet adapting these models to new chemical systems requires expensive retraining of foundation models. Inspired by model merging in vision and language processing, we introduce GFFMERGE, the first principled framework for closed-form model merging in GNNs. We exploit the linear structure of message-passing layers and formulate merging as a convex embedding-alignment problem with an analytical solution. Through the first systematic benchmarking of model merging for GNNs, we show that existing methods designed for vision and language catastrophically fail on force field regression, while GFFMERGE recovers performance approaching gold standard joint training. Across molecular (MD17, MD22), solid-state (LiPS20), and large-scale graph benchmarks, GFFMERGE and GNNMERGE (its generic GNN counterpart) achieve 5-27$\times$ speedups while enabling modular composition of specialized models. Remarkably, our closed-form solution alone outperforms all baseline methods before fine-tuning and provides superior initialization for faster, data-efficient convergence.

</details>


### [361] [Conformal Language Modeling via Posterior Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03731)
*Nicolas Emmenegger,Theo X. Olausson,Armando Solar-Lezama,Chara Podimata*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Calibrate generation by sampling from an LLM posterior conditioned on a high-scoring region, matching statistical guarantees of conformal post-hoc methods but with higher downstream utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-hoc conformal filtering can degrade coherence and fails to redistribute probability mass toward useful outputs; need generation-time calibration for risk control.

Method: Develop a calibration procedure for conditional sequential generation that identifies a calibrated region; sample from approximations to the LLM posterior conditioned on this region; ensures target risk control.

Result: Empirical case studies on open-ended biography generation and math problem solving show same statistical guarantees as prior work while achieving higher downstream utility.

Conclusion: Posterior-conditional sampling with calibrated regions provides better risk-utility trade-offs than post-hoc filtering, addressing limitations of conformal post-hoc methods.

Abstract: Large Language Models remain plagued by hallucinations. Recent work has sought to tame their prevalence using statistical techniques based on conformal prediction, with both theoretical and empirical success. However, these methods operate in a post-hoc fashion, treating the sampling procedure itself as atomic and then surgically altering samples to remove hallucinated claims. This disconnect between filtering and generation can result in samples that are incoherent, inconsistent, or simply unlikely under the model itself. Moreover, post-hoc surgery is unable to shift probability mass towards more useful and helpful responses. To address these issues, we propose to instead sample from approximations to an LLM posterior, where the conditioning event corresponds to a calibrated, high-scoring region. We develop a calibration procedure tailored to the setting of conditional sequential generation that effectively identifies this region and achieves target risk control. Empirically, we apply our method to case studies focused on open-ended biography generation and mathematical problem solving; compared to prior work, we obtain the same statistical guarantees, with higher downstream utility.

</details>


### [362] [Tool-Aware Optimization with Entropy Guidance for Efficient Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03762)
*Hongye Cao,Nuo Yan,Haoyuan Deng,Ziwei Wang,Tianpei Yang,Jing Huo,Yuyao Zhang,Yang Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TAO-RL introduces a unified framework that stabilizes and improves tool-enabled RL for LLMs by combining tool-aware trajectory filtering with entropy-guided exploration, achieving superior reasoning performance on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Integrating external tools into agentic RL often destabilizes training due to input distribution shift from tool use and excessive conservatism that limits exploration; a more selective, informative data and exploration strategy is needed.

Method: Data-level: filter rollout trajectories by discarding tool-invocation failures and removing trajectories with uniformly correct or incorrect outcomes, preserving tool-capable and informative data. Algorithm-level: apply a tool-aware entropy-guided bonus that reshapes the advantage at post-tool-call tokens to encourage diverse reasoning paths at crucial decision points.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 7 challenging reasoning benchmarks across 3 model scales shows TAO-RL outperforms existing methods.

Conclusion: Joint trajectory filtering and entropy-guided exploration form a mutually reinforcing framework that provides a cleaner training distribution and stronger reasoning behavior for tool-enabled RL in LLMs.

Abstract: Agentic reinforcement learning (RL) equips large language models (LLMs) with tool-use capabilities that substantially improve reasoning on complex tasks. However, integrating external tools often destabilizes training: over-reliance on tools can induce input distribution shift, while overly conservative tool use limits effective exploration. To address this issue, we propose a unified framework TAO-RL that couples tool-aware trajectory filtering with entropy-guided exploration for efficient policy optimization. Specifically, at the data level, TAO-RL filters rollout trajectories along two criteria: discarding those where all tool invocations fail to execute, and removing those where all rollouts are either correct or incorrect, as both cases yield degenerate advantage estimates that contribute no discriminative learning signal. This joint filtering retains data that are both tool-capable and informative, establishing a high-quality training distribution. At the algorithmic level, we introduce a tool-aware entropy-guided bonus that reshapes the advantage function at post-tool-call tokens, encouraging the policy to explore more diverse reasoning paths at critical decision points. These two components are mutually reinforcing: trajectory filtering establishes a clean and informative training foundation, while entropy-guided exploration drives stronger reasoning behaviors at critical tool-interaction junctures. Extensive experiments on 7 challenging reasoning benchmarks across 3 model scales demonstrate the superiority of TAO-RL over existing methods.

</details>


### [363] [Limit Analysis of Graph Neural Networks with Wireless Conflict Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03794)
*Romina Garcia Camargo,Zhiyang Wang,Alejandro Ribeiro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper provides theoretical transferability bounds for GNN-based wireless resource allocation on sparse random geometric graphs (RGGs) by relating RGGs to deterministic grid graphs (DGGs) and analyzing conflict graphs for link scheduling; it validates the bounds empirically, showing learned policies outperform benchmarks at scale and examines sensitivity to modeling assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To obtain theoretical guarantees for transferring GNN policies trained on small-scale wireless graphs to larger, sparse networks, leveraging structural similarities between random geometric graphs and grid graphs.

Method: Model interference via conflict graphs derived from sparse RGGs. Establish closeness between RGGs and deterministic grid graphs to derive bounds on performance loss when transferring GNN policies across scales. Validate with link scheduling experiments and analyze how empirical performance aligns with theoretical assumptions.

Result: The work derives bounds on transferability/performance loss when scaling GNN-based resource allocation from small to large graphs. Empirically, learned link-scheduling policies outperform existing benchmarks at scale, and the study examines how violations or variations of the assumptions affect performance.

Conclusion: Under specified graph-model closeness assumptions (RGGs to DGGs), GNN transferability in wireless scheduling is theoretically supported, with practical policy gains at scale; results justify cross-scale deployment and highlight the importance of assumption robustness for real-world networks.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as a powerful tool for wireless resource allocation that leverages the underlying graph structure of communication networks. Their transferability property enables models trained on small-scale graphs to generalize to large-scale deployments with little performance deterioration, a desirable property for currently growing networks. Wireless networks are sparse regimes, where a single node is connected to a small number of other users. This work establishes theoretical results for transferability of GNNs over graphs derived from sparse Random Geometric Graphs (RGGs). In particular, we focus on conflict graphs of RGGs used to model interference among links. Our approach considers the closeness between RGGs and Deterministic Grid Graphs (DGG) to establish bounds in the performance loss when a model is transferred across scales. We validate our theoretical findings through the problem of link scheduling, demonstrating that our learned policies consistently outperform existing benchmarks at scale. Finally, we examine the impact of our theoretical assumptions on empirical performance.

</details>


### [364] [EqGINO: Equivariant Geometry-Informed Fourier Neural Operators for 3D PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03260)
*Sungwon Kim,Juho Song,Seungmin Shin,Guimok Cho,Sangkook Kim,Chanyoung Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EqGINO is a spectral-domain operator that enforces isotropy to achieve exact discrete equivariance for 3D PDE surrogates, while generalizing to continuous orientations with limited SE(3) training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning surrogates for 3D PDEs struggle to generalize across geometric transformations due to reliance on specific coordinates; local equivariant methods have limited global receptive fields, while FNOs lack practical 3D equivariance.

Method: Introduce EqGINO, a Fourier-based operator that imposes an isotropy prior in the spectral domain to guarantee exact equivariance to the discrete symmetries of the discretized domain. The framework further enables generalization to arbitrary continuous orientations with a small number of SE(3)-transformed training samples.

Result: Robust modeling of coordinate-invariant physical laws on complex irregular 3D geometries; code available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: EqGINO bridges the gap between equivariant networks and Fourier Neural Operators by enforcing spectral isotropy, achieving discrete equivariance and improved generalization to unseen orientations on irregular 3D domains.

Abstract: Deep learning surrogates for 3D Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) often fail to generalize across geometric transformations because they depend heavily on specific coordinate systems. While equivariant networks offer a solution, they typically rely on local operations in the spatial domain, making the global receptive field, which is essential for PDE dynamics, computationally expensive. Conversely, Fourier Neural Operators (FNOs) efficiently capture global interactions, yet establishing 3D equivariance within them remains impractical due to the prohibitive cost of spectral group convolutions. To bridge this gap, we introduce EqGINO, a geometrically robust framework that enforces isotropy in the spectral domain. By design, EqGINO guarantees exact equivariance to the discrete symmetries inherent to the discretized computational domain. Beyond this discrete guarantee, our structural prior enables effective generalization to arbitrary continuous orientations even with a limited number of SE(3)-transformed training samples. Consequently, our method robustly models coordinate-invariant physical laws on complex irregular 3D geometries. Our code is available at https://github.com/sung-won-kim/EqGINO

</details>


### [365] [Trading Human Curation for Synthetic Augmentation in RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03800)
*Akshansh <last>,Leonardo Rosa Rodrigues,Michael Korostelev,Youssef Hassan,Mark E. Whiting*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gate-filtered augmentations of a small hand-authored base can substitute for additional human-authored RLVR tasks, preserving generalization while reducing labeling effort; economic substitutability quantified by ρ_cost ranges from 1.4× to 11.6× across plausible cost ratios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality RLVR task supply is a bottleneck; scalable alternatives to hand-curated tasks are needed to scale RL on agentic language models.

Method: Formalize the cost-adjusted trade rate ρ_cost between augmented and human-authored RLVR tasks, perform controlled ablations with varying augmentation shares across training corpora, and analyze end-to-end economics and generalization on a ten-benchmark suite.

Result: Substituting augmented content for additional human-authored RLVR tasks preserves aggregate held-out generalization across the benchmark suite; ρ_cost remains in [1.4×, 11.6×] over plausible cost ratios (c_human/c_aug).

Conclusion: Gate-filtered synthetic augmentation can economically substitute a portion of human-authored RLVR tasks without loss in generalization, with substitutability bounded by relative task costs; implications include more scalable RLVR pipelines and guidance on cost-aware augmentation strategies.

Abstract: The supply of high-quality training tasks is a central bottleneck for reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) on agentic language models. Each task requires a sandboxed setup, a prompt, and a hand-authored reward function, and only tasks that pass a quality bar produce useful training signal. Hand-curation at this quality bar does not scale economically to the task counts effective RL training requires, and the substitution rate between automatically generated task variants and human-authored ones is not yet established. We investigate using pre-specified, gate-filtered augmentations of a small hand-authored base as a substitute for additional human curation during RLVR. We formalize the cost-adjusted trade rate $ρ_{\text{cost}}$ between augmented and human-authored tasks, measure it through a controlled ablation across training corpora with varying augmentation share, and characterize the end-to-end economics of the augmentation pipeline. Substituting augmented content for additional human-authored tasks retains aggregate held-out generalization on a ten-benchmark suite spanning code, instruction following, reasoning, and multi-turn agentic function-calling. The cost-adjusted trade rate $ρ_{\text{cost}}$ between gated synthetic and human-authored RLVR tasks stays in $[1.4\times, 11.6\times]$ across the plausible $c_{\text{human}}/c_{\text{aug}}$ range.

</details>


### [366] [Are Common Substructures Transferable? Riemannian Graph Foundation Model with Neural Vector Bundles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03270)
*Li Sun,Zhenhao Huang,Yiding Wang,Qin Chen,Pietro Lio,Philip S. Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a geometry-aware framework for transferring substructures in graphs by learning intrinsic geometry via a vector bundle on graphs; shows that functional geometry aligns with transferability, with a pretrainable GAUGE architecture and a Dirichlet loss to measure transfer effort, and validates on zero-shot link prediction and graph isomorphism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph structure contains rich, transferable patterns that are not captured by discrete substructure transfer alone. Understanding transferability requires characterizing the intrinsic geometry of graph representations, leveraging Riemannian geometry to explain how substructures transfer.

Method: Propose Neural Vector Bundle to model intrinsic graph geometry via local coordinates; develop GAUGE, a pretrainable architecture that constructs the vector bundle and aligns local coordinates; introduce a Dirichlet loss to quantify transfer effort and geometry compatibility.

Result: Empirical experiments show superior expressiveness and transferability, achieving strong performance in zero-shot link prediction and graph isomorphism tasks.

Conclusion: Building geometry-aware representations through neural vector bundles enables transferable substructures in graphs, with GAUGE offering a practical pretraining paradigm and Dirichlet loss serving as a transfer-effort metric.

Abstract: Foundation models have sparked a revolution via a pretraining-adaptation paradigm, with recent efforts extending this success to graphs. Unlike other modalities, graphs contain rich structural patterns, yet their structural transferability remains poorly understood. Prior studies consider common substructures in the discrete realm, and we are motivated by a fundamental question: Are common substructures transferable? The underlying theory is largely underexplored. In this work, we shift toward learning transferable structures through the lens of functional behavior. Theoretically, we connect transferable substructures to intrinsic geometry of the representation space. However, characterizing such intrinsic geometry has rarely been touched. Grounded in Riemannian geometry, we develop a graph intrinsic geometry learning framework called Neural Vector Bundle, which enables parsing intrinsic geometry with local coordinates. Building on this, we design GAUGE, a pretrainable neural architecture that constructs the vector bundle, flattening geometrically compatible local coordinates, and a new Dirichlet loss, which also measures the transfer effort. We empirically validate its superior expressiveness in challenging tasks including zero-shot link prediction and graph isomorphism.

</details>


### [367] [Easy-to-Use Shielding for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03804)
*Stefan Pranger,Bettina Könighofer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends a formal shield synthesis tool (Tempest) into a practical Python backend for safe RL (tempestpy), integrates with Gymnasium, adds stochastic shield support, and provides MiniGridSafe environments to study safe exploration with explicit domain models and probabilistic transitions, enabling end-to-end evaluation of shielded RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lower the barrier to applying formal safety guarantees in RL by providing end-to-end tooling that connects shield synthesis with standard RL pipelines, and enable safe exploration in stochastic multi-agent settings.

Method: Develop tempestpy to wrap Tempest shield synthesis and expose it via the Gymnasium API; extend algorithmic support to compute sound shields for stochastic multiplayer games; provide symbolic MiniGrid models and the MiniGridSafe suite to facilitate safety-oriented exploration with probabilistic transitions and additional agents.

Result: A practical backend for shielded RL, end-to-end demonstration pipeline, an extended shield solver for stochastic settings, and a new MiniGridSafe benchmark suite to study safety in a transparent, accessible environment.

Conclusion: Bringing formal shielding into routine RL workflows lowers entry barriers and enables reproducible safety experiments; the work opens paths for broader adoption, richer benchmarks, and further optimization of shielded RL pipelines.

Abstract: Safe exploration is a key challenge in Reinforcement Learning (RL) that aims to prevent agents from making harmful decisions while exploring their environment. Safe exploration is a key challenge in Reinforcement Learning (RL) that aims to prevent agents from making harmful decisions while exploring their environment. Shielding is one such technique that assumes domain knowledge in the form of an environment model to decide upon action safety. Although well-established, shielding has seen limited adoption in RL due to the lack of accessible end-to-end infrastructure connecting formal shield synthesis with standard RL frameworks. Applying shielding typically requires expertise in formal methods and substantial engineering effort, keeping it outside the typical RL workflow. We address this by extending our shield synthesis tool Tempest into a practical backend for safe RL. Our core contribution is tempestpy, a Python library that integrates Tempest-based shield synthesis directly into the Gymnasium API, allowing shields to be synthesized and deployed within existing RL pipelines. This lowers the barrier to entry for shielding and turns formal safe-exploration methods into a usable component for RL practitioners. We also extend Tempest's algorithmic support to compute sound shields for stochastic multiplayer games, preserving formal safety guarantees. We demonstrate the resulting workflow end to end and evaluate shielded and unshielded RL across multiple environments. To facilitate modeling, we provide symbolic models for MiniGrid and introduce MiniGridSafe, a collection of playground environments designed to make shielding easily accessible and experimentally transparent. MiniGridSafe extends MiniGrid with safety-oriented scenarios featuring probabilistic transitions and additional agents, enabling the study of challenging safety aspects in a simple and intuitive setting.

</details>


### [368] [Learning to See via Epiretinal Implant Stimulation in silico with Model-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03118)
*Jacob Lavoie,Marwan Besrour,William Lemaire,Jean Rouat,Réjean Fontaine,Eric Plourde*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reinforcement-learning-based stroke rendering for epiretinal stimulation using isotropic/anisotropic phosphenes to improve perceptual intelligibility, validated in a model-based retina environment (rlretina) with axon-map perceptual models; outperforms naive baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address loss of visual acuity in degenerative retinal diseases by shaping electrical stimulation to produce intelligible, pixel-like phosphenes while minimizing axon-fascicle stimulation artifacts.

Method: Formulate image rendering as a brushstroke-based, stroke-by-stroke assembly task in a deep RL environment (rlretina). Use both isotropic and anisotropic phosphene shapes. Train agents in a model-based data generation setup using a psychophysically validated axon map model to render percepts for virtual patients. Compare error-based vs perception-based reward metrics.

Result: The RL agent learns to compose isotropic/anisotropic shapes to generate more intelligible images than a naive approach across multiple virtual patients, demonstrating improved perceptual intelligibility.

Conclusion: A first-step demonstration that controlled phosphene shaping via reinforcement learning can enhance the intelligibility of artificially-restored vision; suggests a viable path toward improved visual acuity with anisotropic/phosphene-aware stimulation and stroke-based rendering frameworks.

Abstract: Objective: Diseases such as age-related macular degeneration and retinitis pigmentosa cause the degradation of the photoreceptor layer. One approach to restore vision is to electrically stimulate the surviving retinal ganglion cells with a microelectrode array such as epiretinal implants. Epiretinal implants are known to generate visible anisotropic shapes elongated along the axon fascicles of neighboring retinal ganglion cells. Recent work has demonstrated that to obtain isotropic pixel-like shapes, it is possible to map axon fascicles and avoid stimulating them by inactivating electrodes or lowering stimulation current levels. Avoiding axon fascicle stimulation aims to remove brushstroke-like shapes in favor of a more reduced set of pixel-like shapes. Approach: In this study, we propose the use of isotropic and anisotropic shapes to render intelligible images on the retina of a virtual patient in a reinforcement learning environment named rlretina. The environment formalizes the task as using brushstrokes in a stroke-based rendering task. Main Results: We train a deep reinforcement learning agent that learns to assemble isotropic and anisotropic shapes to form an image. We investigate which error-based or perception-based metrics is adequate to reward the agent. The agent is trained in a model-based data generation fashion using the psychophysically validated axon map model to render images as perceived by different virtual patients. We show that the agent can generate more intelligible images compared to the naive method in different virtual patients. Significance: This work shares a new way to address epiretinal stimulation that constitutes a first step towards improving visual acuity in artificially-restored vision using anisotropic phosphenes.

</details>


### [369] [PURGE: Projected Unlearning via Retain-Guided Erasure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03808)
*Vedant Jawandhia,Daksh Ahuja,Ghufran Alam Siddiqui,Prashant Trivedi,Yash Sinha,Pratik Narang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PURGE is a machine unlearning method that leverages a duality with continual learning. It uses gradient projection (A-GEM) to constrain unlearning updates, applies multi-layer representation erasure, and uses a retain-confusion target plus two self-regulating stopping criteria. Empirically, it preserves retain accuracy (>96%) and achieves near-ideal MIA AUROC (≈0.5) across 22 class-forgetting tasks on five datasets, outperforming several baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the duality between continual learning and machine unlearning: remove specific data (forget-set) while preserving performance on retained data. Improve privacy (mitigating membership inference) and utility without manual tuning.

Method: Adapt gradient projection from A-GEM to ensure unlearning steps do not increase retain-set loss. Perform multi-layer representation erasure by pushing forget-set activations toward the retain distribution. Use a retain-confusion target (instead of uniform) to conceal forgetting, aligning with the model’s natural confusion on retained data. Employ two self-regulating stopping criteria: a retain-loss budget and a forget-accuracy target to decide when to stop automatically.

Result: Empirical evaluation on CIFAR-10, MNIST, SVHN, STL10, PathMNIST across 22 class-level forgetting tasks shows retain accuracy >96% and MIA AUROC close to 0.5 (ideal). PURGE outperforms gradient ascent, KL-uniform, and several baselines on the privacy-utility frontier.

Conclusion:  PURGE provides a robust, auto-stopping, multi-layer unlearning approach that leverages the CL-MU duality to maintain high retained performance while making the forgotten data hard to identify via membership inference. The design choices yield strong empirical privacy-utility outcomes across diverse datasets and tasks.

Abstract: We propose PURGE, a machine unlearning algorithm built on a simple but an under-exploited observation: continual learning (CL) and machine unlearning (MU) which are fundamentally dual problems. CL tries to learn new tasks without forgetting old ones; MU tries to erase specific data without hurting retained performance representing the same underlying tension in opposite directions. PURGE leverages this duality by adapting gradient projection from A-GEM (Chaudhry et al., 2019) so that every unlearning step is constrained to not increase the retain-set loss. On top of this, it performs multi-layer representation erasure, pushing forget-set activations in intermediate layers towards the retain distribution to remove information from hidden representations rather than just suppressing it at the output. A key design choice is the retain-confusion target: rather than pushing forget outputs toward the uniform distribution, which we found to be surprisingly easy for membership inference attacks to detect, we instead target the model's natural confusion pattern on retain data. This makes the unlearned model hard to distinguish from one retrained from scratch. Two self-regulating stopping criteria (a retain-loss budget and a forget-accuracy target) let the algorithm decide on its own when to stop, removing the need for manual epoch tuning. In experiments on five datasets (CIFAR-10, MNIST, SVHN, STL10, PathMNIST) across 22 class-level forgetting tasks, PURGE consistently keeps retain accuracy above 96% while achieving MIA AUROC close to 0.5 (the ideal), outperforming gradient ascent, KL-uniform, and several published baselines on the privacy-utility frontier.

</details>


### [370] [TreeFlash: Parallel AR-Approximation for Faster Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03819)
*Peer Rheinboldt,Frédéric Berdoz,Roger Wattenhofer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TreeFlash introduces a two-stage autoregressive-approximation for one-shot block drafting in speculative decoding, addressing distribution drift between the drafter and verifier in non-autoregressive tree drafting, and achieves state-of-the-art results with 12% block efficiency gains and 9% speedup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing one-shot block drafters condition on the prefix only, causing the distribution to diverge from the true autoregressive distribution as draft depth grows; the divergence is worse in tree-based drafting where branches share the same marginal distribution.

Method: Incorporate an MLP layer conditioned on the drafter's hidden state and the previous token to approximate an autoregressive distribution; employ a two-stage approximation to preserve O(1) decoding time.

Result: State-of-the-art performance across multiple tasks and models; improves over marginal tree drafting by 12% in block efficiency and 9% in speedup.

Conclusion: TreeFlash demonstrates that autoregressive-consistent one-shot drafting can be achieved with minimal runtime overhead, enabling efficient draft generation in speculative decoding frameworks.

Abstract: One-shot block drafters for speculative decoding generate the full draft in a single forward pass, achieving strong throughput by eliminating sequential token generation. However, they predict each draft token conditioned only on the prefix context, with no dependence on previously drafted tokens. This non-autoregressive conditioning causes the drafter's distribution to diverge from the verifier's true autoregressive distribution as draft depth grows. This problem becomes more severe in tree-based drafting, where distinct branches are forced to share the same marginal distribution for subsequent tokens. We propose TreeFlash, which addresses this by incorporating an MLP layer conditioned on the drafter's hidden state and the previous token to approximate an autoregressive distribution. TreeFlash retains the $\mathcal{O}(1)$ decoding time complexity of one-shot drafters by employing a two-stage approximation mechanism. TreeFlash achieves state-of-the-art performance across a variety of tasks and models, improving over marginal tree drafting by $12\%$ higher block efficiency and $9\%$ higher speedup.

</details>


### [371] [Finding Needles in the Haystack: Transductive Active Labeling in Ecology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03821)
*Rupa Kurinchi-Vendhan,Sara Beery*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Active learning in ecology should be evaluated on a transductive objective focused on discovering rare classes within a pool, using a sampling-difficulty metric and a rarefaction-inspired stopping rule to reduce premature stopping and improve long-tail recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ecological labeling aims to label an entire data pool efficiently, making discovery of rare or long-tail classes critical; inductive test-set accuracy underestimates the value of continued labeling and human-in-the-loop involvement.

Method: Formalizes a transductive objective for active learning in ecology, introduces a latent-space sampling-difficulty metric to quantify discovery challenge, and proposes a conservative hybrid stopping criterion inspired by rarefaction curves that combines predictive performance with discovery signals.

Result: Shows that long-tail performance is governed by discovery rather than prediction; a hybrid stopping rule reduces premature stopping and improves recovery of rare classes when discovery is the limiting factor.

Conclusion: To align active learning with ecological goals, evaluation and stopping criteria should emphasize discovery of rare classes in the pool, leveraging latent-space geometry and rarefaction-inspired strategies to guide labeling until the entire pool is labeled or the discovery objective is saturated.

Abstract: Active learning is now standard practice in labeling ecological data, enabling ecologists to quickly process large volumes of field data to understand and monitor natural environments. Current practices evaluate active learning inductively, estimating predictive performance on a held-out test set. We argue that this evaluation is misaligned with most ecological tasks, where the goal is to transductively label an entire pool of data as efficiently as possible. We demonstrate that ignoring the human-in-the-loop underestimates the importance of continuing to label, particularly for classes in the long tail which may be of disproportionate ecological importance (rare species, uncommon behaviors, etc.). Our analysis shows that, for this long tail, the transductive objective shifts importance from prediction to discovery: the true challenge becomes finding "needles in the haystack," examples of rare classes that are embedded within dense regions of abundant classes in the latent geometry, which we quantify with a novel metric of sampling difficulty. Finally, to translate these insights to practical ecological workflows, we propose a conservative hybrid stopping criterion inspired by ecological rarefaction curves, and show that combining predictive performance with discovery criteria reduces premature stopping on long-tailed pools, improving rare-class recovery when discovery, not classification, is the limiting factor.

</details>


### [372] [Dynamic Short Convolutions Improve Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03825)
*Oliver Sieberling,Bharat Runwal,Rameswar Panda,Yoon Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dynamic short convolutions introduce input-dependent filters to short convolution blocks in Transformers, preserving locality while increasing expressivity. Applied to Q/K/V (and after each linear layer) they outperform standard Transformers and static conv variants across language-modeling tasks (150M–2B params). They offer compute advantages (≈1.33× when applied to K/Q/V; ≈1.60× when added after every linear layer) and remain hardware-friendly via custom Triton kernels, with improvements also seen in linear RNNs and MoEs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limited expressivity of fixed convolutional priors and the static nature of short convolutions in Transformers by introducing input-dependent, dynamic filters that retain locality bias, potentially improving associative recall and overall LM performance while maintaining hardware efficiency.

Method: Introduce dynamic short convolutions as a neural primitive and apply them to the Q/K/V representations (and optionally after every linear layer). Evaluate on language-modeling tasks across 150M–2B parameter ranges, including associative recall tasks. Compare against standard Transformers and static short convolutions. Use custom Triton kernels to enable efficient training and quantify compute scaling (scaling laws). Explore applicability to linear RNNs (Mamba-2/Gated DeltaNet) and mixture-of-experts architectures.

Result: Dynamic short convolutions consistently outperform standard Transformers and static short-convolution variants across LM scales (150M–2B params). Scaling laws indicate ≈1.33× compute advantage when applied to Q/K/V, and ≈1.60× when added after every linear layer, compared to compute-matched baselines. Gains extend to linear RNNs and MoEs. Implemented with custom Triton kernels to maintain practical training speed with manageable slowdown.

Conclusion: Dynamic short convolutions are a scalable, hardware-efficient, and expressive primitive that enhances Transformer-based language models, offering notable performance gains and favorable compute-density characteristics, with demonstrated applicability beyond standard Transformers.

Abstract: Transformers have become the dominant architecture for large language models, largely due to the scalability and flexibility of attention, feed-forward layers, residual connections, and normalization. This paper introduces dynamic short convolutions as an additional neural network primitive for improving Transformers. Unlike static short convolutions, dynamic convolutions use input-dependent filters, which preserves the locality bias of convolution while increasing expressivity. Motivating experiments show that applying dynamic short convolutions to key, query, and value representations improves performance on challenging associative recall tasks compared with static convolutional variants. Across language-modeling experiments ranging from 150M to 2B parameters, dynamic convolutions consistently outperform standard Transformers and Transformers augmented with static short convolutions. Fitting scaling laws indicates a 1.33$\times$ compute advantage over compute-matched Transformers when dynamic convolutions are applied to the key, query, and value vectors, and a 1.60$\times$ advantage when adding dynamic convolutions after every linear layer. Dynamic convolutions also offer improvements on linear RNNs (Mamba-2/Gated DeltaNet) and mixture-of-experts architectures. We make these gains practical with custom Triton kernels that enable efficient training with a manageable end-to-end slowdown. These results suggest that dynamic short convolutions are a scalable, hardware-efficient, and expressive primitive for advancing Transformer-based language models.

</details>


### [373] [Multi-Modal Graph Neural Network with Transformer-Guided Adaptive Diffusion for Preclinical Alzheimer Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03322)
*Jaeyoon Sim,Minjae Lee,Guorong Wu,Won Hwa Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An integrated diffusion-guided transformer framework on brain graphs improves preclinical Alzheimer’s classification by combining diffusion-based local aggregation and multi-head attention-based global aggregation, yielding interpretable key ROIs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNNs for brain networks face two main limitations: (i) convolutional methods struggle to aggregate information from distant neighborhoods, reducing sensitivity to long-range dependencies; (ii) attention-based methods may underretain critical node-centric information from pivotal nodes, hindering interpretability and disease-specific feature capture. There is a need to leverage multimodal features to better diagnose/prognose neurodegenerative disease, especially in preclinical stages.

Method: Introduce a diffusion process at each node guided by a downstream transformer. Short-range information is aggregated via a diffusion kernel; long-range dependencies are captured via multi-head attention. The model operates on brain ROI graphs with multimodal features, combining local diffusion-based aggregation with transformer-based global aggregation to improve representation for disease prediction and facilitate ROI-level interpretability.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows improved performance in preclinical Alzheimer's disease classification across modalities. The model also identifies key ROIs strongly associated with preclinical stages, suggesting potential for early diagnosis and prognosis.

Conclusion: The diffusion-guided transformer framework offers improved accuracy and interpretability for brain network analysis, highlighting its potential to enhance early detection of neurodegenerative disease by effectively leveraging local and global graph structure and multimodal data.

Abstract: The graphical representation of the brain offers critical insights into diagnosing and prognosing neurodegenerative disease via relationships between regions of interest (ROIs). Despite recent emergence of various Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to effectively capture the relational information, there remain inherent limitations in interpreting the brain networks. Specifically, convolutional approaches ineffectively aggregate information from distant neighborhoods, while attention-based methods exhibit deficiencies in capturing node-centric information, particularly in retaining critical characteristics from pivotal nodes. These shortcomings reveal challenges for identifying disease-specific variation from diverse features from different modalities. In this regard, we propose an integrated framework guiding diffusion process at each node by a downstream transformer where both short- and long-range properties of graphs are aggregated via diffusion-kernel and multi-head attention respectively. We demonstrate the superiority of our model by improving performance of pre-clinical Alzheimer's disease (AD) classification with various modalities. Also, our model adeptly identifies key ROIs that are closely associated with the preclinical stages of AD, marking a significant potential for early diagnosis and prevision of the disease.

</details>


### [374] [Online Learning with Gradient-Variation Interval Regret](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03831)
*Yan-Feng Xie,Shuche Wang,Peng Zhao,Zhi-Hua Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First online learning algorithm achieving interval regret bounds that adapt to gradient variation, introducing a two-layer online ensemble with Lipschitz- and smoothness-adaptive meta-algorithm. Extends to interval dynamic regret and stochastic extended adversarial optimization; validated empirically.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-stationary online learning requires good performance over all time intervals. Interval regret captures this; gradient variation measures cumulative gradient change, linking to problem-dependent quantities and stochastic optimization. Aims to obtain adaptive, near-minimax guarantees without strong hyperparameter tuning.

Method: A two-layer online ensemble framework. Core regret bound scales with gradient variation. A Lipschitz- and smoothness-agnostic variant uses a novel Lipschitz-adaptive meta algorithm to automatically adapt unknown constants.

Result: Regret bounds adapt to problem-dependent quantities while preserving minimax optimal rate in worst case. Provides interval dynamic regret bounds and the first piecewise characterization for stochastic extended adversarial optimization. Experimental validation supports theoretical claims.

Conclusion: Introduces a robust, adaptive online learning algorithm with strong theoretical guarantees across interval regret and dynamic/adversarial settings. The Lipschitz-adaptive meta algorithm adds practical adaptability, and the approach opens avenues for broader interval-based metrics.

Abstract: This paper investigates non-stationary online learning using the metric of interval regret, which requires an online algorithm to perform well over every time interval. We propose the first online learning algorithm that achieves an interval regret bound scaling with gradient variation, a fundamental measure of the cumulative change in online function gradients, which relates to various problem-dependent quantities and is closely connected to stochastic optimization and other problems. Our method employs a simple and efficient two-layer online ensemble structure that achieves strong theoretical guarantees. Specifically, it enjoys a regret bound that simultaneously adapts to various problem-dependent quantities while also preserving the minimax-optimal rate in the worst case. Moreover, recognizing the challenge of hyperparameter tuning, we introduce a Lipschitz- and smoothness-agnostic variant that automatically adapts to these potentially unknown constants. This is primarily enabled by a novel Lipschitz-adaptive meta algorithm, which may be of independent interest. Beyond interval regret, our method also yields broader implications: it provides versatile bounds for interval dynamic regret, a stronger measure that competes with changing comparators over any interval, and yields the first piecewise characterization for stochastic extended adversarial optimization. Theoretical findings are validated by experiments.

</details>


### [375] [Text-attributed Graph Condensation via Text Selection and Attribute Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03839)
*Haowei Han,Yuxiang Wang,Guojia Wan,Hao Wang,Shanshan Feng,Hao Huang,Jiawei Jiang,Xiao Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TAGSAM compresses Text-Attributed Graphs by jointly condensing text and topology, preserving accuracy at small scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-attributed graphs (TAGs) require joint GNN+LM training, which is resource-intensive; a compacted TAG with preserved accuracy can dramatically reduce space/time costs on large datasets.

Method: Two design components: (1) subgraph text Selection — merges representative text chunks from related descriptions by maximizing mutual information; (2) Attribute similarity Matching — stabilizes graph-topology condensation by aligning robust similarity matrices to reduce training-trajectory variance.

Result: Compared to six state-of-the-art baselines, TAGSAM achieves higher accuracy at equal compressed sizes, averaging a 4.9% improvement over the best baseline. It also retains competitive training accuracy when compressed to 1% of the original size.

Conclusion: TAGSAM provides effective and scalable condensation for TAGs, achieving superior accuracy preservation under aggressive compression and offering practical gains for large-scale graph-language learning."

Abstract: Text-Attributed Graph (TAG) is an important type of graph structured data, where each node has a text description. TAG models usually train a Graph Neural Network (GNN) and language model jointly, which leads to high space and time consumption, especially on large datasets. To mitigate this, we propose TAGSAM, a condensation method that compresses TAGs while preserving training accuracy. TAGSAM comes with two key designs, i.e., subgraph text Selection and Attribute similarity Matching, which compress the text description and graph topology of TAG, respectively. For the texts, subgraph text selection selects and merges representative text chunks from multiple related text descriptions by maximizing mutual information. For the graph topology, popular condensation methods based on Matching Training Trajectories (MTT) suffer from high variance, which hinders accuracy. Our attribute similarity matching mitigates this issue by aligning stable similarity matrices. We evaluate TAGSAM against six state-of-the-art baselines, where it showcases superior performance. For the same compressed size, TAGSAM improves upon the best-performing baseline by an average of 4.9% in accuracy. Furthermore, it maintains competitive training accuracy even when the TAG is condensed to just 1% size. Our code is available at https://github.com/SundayVHan/TAGSAM

</details>


### [376] [MAdam: Metric-Aware Multi-Objective Adam](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03904)
*Fengbei Liu,Rachit Saluja,Sunwoo Kwak,Ruibo Wang,Ruining Deng,Heejong Kim,Johannes C. Paetzold,Mert R. Sabuncu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MAdam is a metric-aware wrapper for multi-objective optimization that remedies weighting and geometric mismatches when using Adam, yielding improved Pareto optimization across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard MOO solvers that couple with Adam suffer two systematic gaps: a weighting mismatch where Adam's second-moment denominator entangles time-varying preferences with gradient statistics, and a geometric mismatch where Adam's adaptive metric distorts the Euclidean geometry of MOO, misaligning objective trade-offs.

Method: Introduce MAdam as a drop-in wrapper that preconditions the reconciled direction using the curvature of the scalarized objective conditioned on the preference. This whitening makes Adam's second moment collapse to identity, so the update follows a preference-conditioned metric without altering the solver or optimizer.

Result: Across domains (multi-task learning, Pareto-front recovery, physics-informed neural networks, medical imaging), MAdam consistently outperforms Adam for every solver family.

Conclusion: MAdam resolves both weighting and geometric mismatches in MOO, providing a plug--in enhancement that yields better Pareto optimization without changing existing solvers or optimizers.

Abstract: Multi-objective optimization (MOO) underlies many machine learning problems, yet MOO solvers across the loss-balancing, gradient-balancing, and Pareto-based families almost universally hand their reconciled directions to Adam~\cite{kingma2015adam}. We show this coupling introduces two systematic gaps between the solver's intent and the optimizer's execution. The first is a \emph{weighting mismatch}: Adam's second-moment denominator entangles the time-varying preference vector with gradient statistics, marginalizing the preference into a history average and collapsing distinct Pareto trade-offs toward a near-uniform mixture. The second is a \emph{geometric mismatch}: Adam's adaptive metric distorts the Euclidean geometry MOO solvers assume, turning aligned objectives into apparent conflicts. To resolve both jointly, we introduce \textbf{MAdam} (Metric-Aware Multi-Objective Adam), a drop-in wrapper that leaves both solver and optimizer unchanged. MAdam preconditions the reconciled direction by the preference-conditioned curvature of the scalarized objective; on this whitened input, Adam's second moment collapses to identity, so the realized update is governed by the preference-conditioned metric. Across multi-task learning, Pareto-front recovery, physics-informed neural networks, and medical imaging, MAdam consistently improves over Adam for every solver family.

</details>


### [377] [Re-Evaluating Continual Learning with Few-Shot Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03843)
*Amogh Inamdar,Matthew So,Vici Milenia,Richard Zemel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Few-shot evaluation is proposed as a more comprehensive measure of stability and plasticity in continual learning, introducing per-shot plasticity and a foresight meta-learning approach that yields learning-to-learn behavior; evaluated on continual image classification, revealing insights beyond 0-shot metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 0-shot forgetting and limited assessment of retention/adaptation; need a more granular evaluation to capture retention and rapid adaptation across task sequences.

Method: Propose few-shot evaluation framework; introduce per-shot plasticity metric; conduct fine-grained analysis on task sequences for continual image classification; implement meta-learning of a short sequence of future tasks to induce learning-to-learn.

Result: Few-shot evaluation reveals novel insights into popular CL strategies; per-shot plasticity provides a nuanced view; foresight/meta-learning yields L2L behavior across tasks.

Conclusion: Few-shot evaluation and foresight meta-learning offer more comprehensive assessment and potential improvements for CL systems; suggests future work on standardized protocols and broader domains.

Abstract: Continual learning methods aim to maximize the stability and plasticity of machine learning models that are trained on a sequence of tasks. The standard measure of stability (i.e., forgetting) is the 0-shot performance of a model on previously learned tasks, and plasticity, the performance on the most recently learned task. However, 0-shot evaluation does not fully measure a model or method's ability to retain learned information or adapt quickly to new information, as it requires perfect recall across multiple tasks. In this paper, we propose few-shot evaluation as a more comprehensive assessment of the stability and plasticity of a continual learning system. We conduct a fine-grained assessment on task sequences for continual image classification and find that this paradigm produces novel insights into the performance of popular continual learning strategies. Through few-shot evaluation with a novel metric -- per-shot plasticity -- we show that adding `foresight' to continual learning methods via the meta-learning of a short sequence of future tasks induces learning-to-learn behavior over the task sequence.

</details>


### [378] [Two-Action Apple Tasting with Switching Costs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03851)
*Tommaso Cesari,Roberto Colomboni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes a two-action apple-tasting problem with switching costs under an oblivious adversary, showing the minimax expected regret is Θ(√T) rather than the conjectured Ω(T^{2/3}) obstruction. It demonstrates that the apple-tasting graph does not create a harder regime for switching costs, yielding tight √T bounds with explicit constants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve whether the two-action apple-tasting feedback graph constitutes the elusive Ω(T^{2/3}) obstruction in switching-cost online learning. If such an obstruction existed, it would extend to a broad class of feedback graphs; this work closes the gap by showing a √T minimax regret.

Method: Normalize the problem so that at each round the learner chooses between a revealing action (gives 0 reward and reveals x_t∈[-1,1] for the blind action) and a blind action (gives x_t but reveals nothing). The learner incurs a switching cost of 1 per switch. Analyze the oblivious minimax regret against the best fixed action in hindsight. Use minimax techniques to derive lower and upper bounds, constructing hard adversaries for the lower bound and designing an algorithm achieving the upper bound.

Result: The oblivious minimax expected regret satisfies (1/(2√3))·√T ≤ R_T^* ≤ 2√3·√T. This yields a tight Θ(√T) regret for the problem, refuting the presence of an Ω(T^{2/3}) obstruction for this graph and implying that the switching-cost classification does not generalize a barrier from this specific case.

Conclusion: For the two-action apple-tasting with switching costs, the minimax regret scales as Θ(√T) rather than Θ(T^{2/3}). The two-action apple-tasting graph is not a source of the previously hypothesized obstruction, informing the broader understanding of feedback-graph complexity in switching-cost settings.

Abstract: We study the two-action apple-tasting problem with switching costs against an oblivious adversary. In an equivalent normalized formulation, at each round the learner chooses between a revealing action and a blind action: the revealing action gives reward $0$ and reveals the hidden value $x_t\in[-1,1]$ of the blind action; the blind action gives reward $x_t$ but reveals nothing. The learner pays one unit whenever they switches actions, and regret is measured against the best fixed action in hindsight.
  General feedback-graph algorithms with switching costs give $\widetilde O(T^{2/3})$ regret guarantees for this problem. The two-action apple-tasting graph was the natural candidate for the missing $Ω(T^{2/3})$ obstruction in the switching-cost classification: such a lower bound would have transferred to a large family of still-unclassified feedback graphs. We prove that this obstruction is not there: the oblivious minimax expected regret for this problem satisfies \[
  \frac{1}{2\sqrt3}\cdot\sqrt T
  \le
  R_T^\star
  \le
  2\sqrt{3}\cdot \sqrt{T}. \]

</details>


### [379] [Neuron Populations Exhibit Divergent Selectivity with Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03990)
*Amil Dravid,Yasaman Bahri,Alexei A. Efros,Yossi Gandelsman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Rosetta Neurons exhibit sublinear growth with model size and polarization, indicating increasing selectivity and specialization at neuron level; a simple analytic model accounts for these trends and suggests scalable, interpretable neuron-level structure across domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test whether neuron populations in neural networks scale predictably with model size, extending scaling laws from macroscopic metrics to neuron-level interpretability.

Method: Empirical analysis of Rosetta Neurons across language models up to 30B and vision models up to 5B; identify Rosetta Neurons, quantify their counts relative to model size, assess selectivity/monosemanty, develop an analytic model, and perform a targeted data-filtering case study for continued pretraining (domain specialization).

Result: Rosetta Neurons scale sublinearly with model size (absolute count grows, fraction shrinks); a Neuron Polarization Effect arises as Rosetta Neurons become more selective and monosemantic with scale, separating from a growing non-Rosetta population. An analytic model balancing feature utility against neuron capacity explains the sublinear scaling and polarization. Rosetta Neurons also become more domain-specialized with scale, demonstrated via a targeted data-filtering case for continued pretraining.

Conclusion: A scaling law for interpretable, shared neuron-level structure exists: model size modulates neuron universality, selectivity, and specialization across language and vision models.

Abstract: We investigate whether neuron populations within neural networks evolve predictably with scale, extending scaling laws beyond macroscopic observables such as loss. To probe this question, we study Rosetta Neurons, a previously characterized class of neurons whose activation patterns are similar across independently trained models (Dravid et al., 2023). In separate analyses of language models up to 30B parameters and vision models up to 5B parameters, we observe that the population of Rosetta Neurons follows a sublinear power law in model size, growing in absolute number but occupying a shrinking fraction of the total neuron count. We further observe a Neuron Polarization Effect: Rosetta Neurons become more selective and increasingly monosemantic with scale, separating from a growing non-Rosetta population that remains less selective. An analytical model balancing feature utility against limited neuron capacity explains the sublinear power-law scaling and this polarization effect. Finally, we find that Rosetta Neurons become more domain-specialized with scale and illustrate their selectivity through a targeted data-filtering case study for continued pretraining. Our results point to a scaling law for interpretable, shared neuron-level structure, linking model size to systematic changes in neuron universality, selectivity, and specialization.

</details>


### [380] [Attribution via Distributional Paths for Information Revelation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03885)
*Kieran A. Murphy,Shameen Shrestha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reveal-IG is a distributional path attribution method that preserves completeness with respect to the model's expected response, mitigating input-space path artifacts by progressively revealing information via probe distributions around the instance, enabling multiscale probes and uncertainty handling in tabular data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Path-based attributions in input space can introduce artifacts and fixed-resolution biases. There is a need for a framework that controls when/how features are queried and accounts for uncertainty, while maintaining completeness.

Method: Replace input-space trajectories with a distributional path of probes around the example. At each step, progressively reveal information about the input and measure the change in the model's expected output along this distributional path, yielding a complete, multiscale attribution.

Result: Preserves completeness w.r.t. the expected model response. Reduces path artifacts seen in input-space methods. Produces stable, signed attributions on ImageNet and tabular regression and remains competitive on various metrics; supports multiscale probes and feature-wise uncertainty.

Conclusion: Reveal-IG provides a flexible, distribution-based attribution framework that generalizes path methods to distributions around the instance, enabling robust, multiscale, and uncertainty-aware explanations for vision and tabular data.

Abstract: Feature attribution methods explain predictions by assigning importance scores to input features. Path-based methods such as Integrated Gradients are especially appealing because they satisfy \textit{completeness}: attributions sum to the change in model output between a reference state and the input. Yet most path methods define this trajectory in input space, explaining a model through pointwise perturbed inputs along a chosen path. An input-space path integrates the model's raw response at each point it passes through, with no control over the resolution at which a feature is queried; the early, baseline-adjacent part of the trajectory contributes to the explanation on equal footing with the input itself. Here, we lift path attribution from input space to a space of structured probe distributions around the example of interest, and call our method Reveal-IG. Rather than traversing raw input values, Reveal-IG progressively reveals information about the input and attributes changes in the model's expected output along this distributional path. The result is a path-attribution framework that retains completeness with respect to the expected model response, and naturally accommodates multiscale image probes and feature-wise uncertainty in tabular data. Synthetic diagnostics show that Reveal-IG avoids path artifacts that affect input-space methods, and across ImageNet classification and tabular regression it produces stable, signed attributions -- leading on metrics that use attribution sign while remaining competitive on the rest.

</details>


### [381] [Denoise First, Orthogonalize Later: Understanding Momentum in Muon via Spectral Filtering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03899)
*Xianliang Li,Zihan Zhang,Weiyang Liu,Han Bao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Momentum in Muon acts as a spectral filter: it suppresses perturbations while preserving the dominant signal, enlarging the spectral gap and stabilizing the orthogonalization step; applying momentum before orthogonalization yields stronger signal alignment; empirical results on LLM pretraining support the theory; the perspective may generalize to other matrix-based optimizers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a theoretical gap in understanding momentum's role in Muon, as prior work either removes momentum or does not explain its empirical benefits. A spectral perspective on momentum can explain stability and performance in large-scale training.

Method: Under a structured signal-plus-perturbation gradient model, analyze how momentum affects perturbations vs. signal. Prove that momentum enlarges the spectral gap by suppressing perturbations and preserving the signal, thereby stabilizing the singular subspaces passed to Muon's orthogonalization. Show that applying momentum before orthogonalization yields stronger alignment with the signal component than other orderings.

Result: The theory shows momentum reduces perturbations and preserves the signal, enlarging the spectral gap and stabilizing subspaces; ordering matters: momentum-before-orthogonalization yields better signal alignment. Empirical experiments across tasks including LLM pretraining corroborate the theory.

Conclusion: This work provides a foundational spectral-filtering interpretation of momentum in matrix-based optimizers and explains its empirical advantages in Muon; the approach may extend to other optimizers with similar matrix-based dynamics.

Abstract: Muon has recently demonstrated strong empirical performance in large language model training, but the theoretical role of momentum in Muon remains unclear. Existing analyses of Muon either remove momentum to study spectral updates in isolation, or retain momentum without explaining why it improves empirical performance. Our work bridges this gap by showing momentum in Muon acts as a spectral filter. Under a structured signal-plus-perturbation gradient model, we prove that momentum suppresses perturbations while preserving the dominant signal, thereby enlarging the spectral gap between them. This enlarged gap stabilizes the singular subspaces of the matrix passed to Muon's orthogonalization step, making the resulting update more reliable. We further show that applying momentum before orthogonalization achieves provably stronger alignment with the signal component of the gradient than either reversing this order or simply removing momentum. Experiments across diverse tasks, including LLM pretraining, support our theoretical analysis. More broadly, our theory offers a starting point for understanding the benefits of momentum in other matrix-based optimizers.

</details>


### [382] [Contrastive Neural Algorithmic Reasoning for Graph Coloring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03923)
*Thien Le,Tianyu Zhao,Melanie Weber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A transferable contrastive GNN for approximate k-coloring learns color-aware embeddings; unit-norm embeddings exhibit a line-prototype geometry with color subspaces, while unnormalized variants show max-margin bias; empirically, the method generalizes across graphs and achieves low monochromatic edges comparable to greedy baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address lack of generalization in instance-wise unsupervised GNNs for approximate graph coloring. Develop a transferable framework that learns color geometry across graphs of varying sizes/distributions to minimize monochromatic edges.

Method: Contrastive learning framework: align embeddings of nodes of the same color and push apart adjacent colors. Analyze population objective on bounded-size graphs. For unit-norm embeddings, prove a line-prototype structure where same-color nodes collapse to a 1D subspace and edges connect orthogonal subspaces. Under projected subgradient dynamics, this structure remains (balanced-coloring assumption). In the unnormalized variant, show gradient descent has a max-margin bias tied to a quotient-graph hard-margin problem.

Result: The line-prototype geometry yields stationarity conditions in the supervised setting and is preserved under dynamics. The unnormalized variant exhibits max-margin bias. Empirically, contrastive GNN encoders generalize to new graphs and produce low-conflict colorings, performing as well as or better than greedy methods on synthetic and real-world graphs.

Conclusion: A transferable contrastive GNN approach can learn color-aware embeddings that generalize across graph distributions. The unit-norm regime reveals a clean line-prototype structure facilitating color separation, while the unnormalized regime aligns with max-margin principles. Empirical results support effectiveness and competitiveness with greedy coloring.

Abstract: Graph coloring seeks to assigns colors to a graph's nodes so that adjacent nodes receive different colors, using as few colors as possible. Here, we study approximate $k$-coloring, where the goal is to use at most $k$ colors while minimizing the number of monochromatic edges. This problem is central to graph theory and has applications in areas such as scheduling and resource allocation. Recent unsupervised GNN approaches optimize each instance directly, precluding generalization across graph sizes and distributions. We instead propose a contrastive learning framework that learns transferable coloring geometry where the embeddings of same-color nodes align, while adjacent nodes' representations are pushed toward distinct directions. We analyze the resulting population objective over bounded-size graphs. For unit-norm embeddings, we show that its optima have a line-prototype structure: Representations of nodes of the same color collapse to a shared one-dimensional subspace, and edges connect orthogonal subspaces. This geometry yields stationarity conditions in the supervised setting and is preserved by projected subgradient dynamics under a balanced-coloring assumption. In an unnormalized variant, gradient descent has a max-margin bias governed by a quotient-graph hard-margin problem. Experiments on synthetic and real-world graphs show that contrastive GNN encoders generalize effectively and produce low-conflict colorings, matching and sometimes improving on greedy approaches.

</details>


### [383] [FFR: Forward-Forward Learning for Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03927)
*Xinyang Liu,Xuanyu Liang,Shiqi Ding,Boyang Li,Zhiqiang Que,Jiayang Li,Guosheng Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends Forward-Forward to regression (FFR): ordinal competitive learning, stratified ladder architecture, and hierarchical uncertainty-aware predictions; achieves near BP performance with substantial memory and time efficiency across real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address FF's limitation for regression due to lack of natural positive/negative contrast for continuous targets and absence of target magnitude/order information, enabling biologically plausible, local, layer-wise learning for real-valued targets.

Method: 1) Replace contrastive pairs with distance-aware ordinal supervision via an ordinal competitive goodness function among partitioned neuron groups. 2) Propose a stratified ladder architecture: shallow layers learn coarse ordinal discrimination, deeper layers refine into fine-grained regression, with multi-scale feature aggregation for inter-layer collaboration. 3) Implement hierarchical prediction with uncertainty estimation by multi-scale predictors providing joint predictions and confidence estimates with no extra cost (free-lunch).

Result: Empirically, FFR recovers on average 98.6% of backpropagation's accuracy on five real-world regression benchmarks; reduces peak training memory to 27% of BP at depth 8 and 8% at depth 32; per-iteration time is ~72% of BP; outperforms all BP-free competitors.

Conclusion: FFR demonstrates practical viability of FF in regression, delivering competitive performance with substantial efficiency gains and robust uncertainty-aware predictions, enabling broader adoption of FF-based learning for real-valued targets.

Abstract: The Forward-Forward (FF) algorithm offers a computationally efficient and biologically plausible alternative to backpropagation (BP) by training neural networks through purely local, layer-wise optimization. However, FF is inherently designed for classification via contrastive positive-negative sample pairs, and extending it to regression poses fundamental challenges: continuous target space lack natural "opposites" for contrastive learning, and the standard goodness function carries no information about target magnitude or ordering. We propose FFR (Forward-Forward for Regression), to our knowledge, the first framework to extend FF to real-world regression and demonstrate competitive performance across diverse real-world datasets. FFR introduces three key innovations: (1) an ordinal competitive goodness function that replaces contrastive pairs with competitive learning between partitioned neuron groups under distance-aware ordinal supervision; (2) a stratified ladder architecture where shallow layers learn coarse ordinal discrimination and deeper layers refine into fine-grained regression, with multi-scale feature aggregation for inter-layer collaboration; and (3) hierarchical prediction with uncertainty estimation, where multi-scale predictors jointly provide robust predictions and prediction confidence as a free-lunch. Extensive experimental results show FFR recovers on average 98.6% of BP's accuracy across five real-world regression benchmarks while reducing peak training memory to only 27% of BP's at depth 8 and 8% at depth 32, with per-iteration time around 72% of BP's, and substantially outperforms all BP-free competitors.

</details>


### [384] [Value-Aware Stochastic KV Cache Eviction for Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03928)
*Ting-Yun Chang,Harvey Yiyun Fu,Deqing Fu,Chenghao Yang,Jesse Thomason,Robin Jia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VaSE: a training-free, value-aware, stochastic KV cache eviction method that protects large-magnitude value states and promotes cache diversity, achieving higher accuracy at 4x KV cache compression than selection-based and eviction baselines on six reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the accuracy-efficiency trade-off in long-context reasoning with KV cache eviction, which can degrade accuracy when evicting important states and cause repetitive reasoning loops.

Method: Introduce Value-aware Stochastic KV Cache Eviction (VaSE): a training-free eviction policy that guards large-magnitude value states and injects stochasticity to diversify eviction decisions; compatible with FlashAttention2; yields static memory footprint.

Result: Across six tasks on Qwen3 models, VaSE with 4x compression attains higher average accuracy than state-of-the-art selection at same sparsity and outperforms the strongest eviction method by more than 4%.

Conclusion: VaSE reduces the efficiency-accuracy gap in reasoning with KV caches, enabling static memory usage and improved robustness, bridging eviction-based and selection-based approaches.

Abstract: Reasoning models improve accuracy through extended chains of thought, but their long outputs create a memory and compute bottleneck. KV cache eviction methods reduce this cost by evicting unimportant key-value pairs from the cache, yet they often yield worse accuracy than selection-based sparse attention alternatives, which keep the full KV cache. We identify key factors crucial to KV cache eviction accuracy. First, a small fraction of value states have abnormally large magnitudes, and evicting them causes catastrophic failure where models enter repetitive reasoning loops. Second, introducing stochasticity during eviction improves accuracy by increasing cache diversity. Based on these findings, we propose Value-aware Stochastic KV Cache Eviction (VaSE), a training-free recipe that protects large-magnitude value states and promotes diverse eviction decisions. Across six reasoning tasks, Qwen3 models using VaSE with 4x KV cache compression yield higher average accuracies than SOTA selection method at the same sparsity, while outperforming the strongest eviction method by more than 4%. Overall, VaSE bridges the gap between efficiency and accuracy, supporting FlashAttention2 and enabling a static memory footprint for reasoning models.

</details>


### [385] [Correcting Neural Operator Spectral Bias via Diffusion Posterior Sampling with Sparse Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.03936)
*Niccolò Perrone,Fanny Lehmann,Stefania Fresca,Filippo Gatti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FreqNO-DPS fuses a score-based diffusion prior with diffusion posterior sampling conditioned on sparse observations and a frozen neural operator to reduce spectral bias in neural operator surrogates for PDEs, achieving near-zero spectral bias at low sensor coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural operator surrogates are fast but suffer spectral bias; sparse sensor data provides pointwise accuracy but covers limited domain. A method is needed to combine both without reintroducing frequency-dependent distortion.

Method: Train an unconditional score-based diffusion prior on high-fidelity data; perform diffusion posterior sampling (DPS) conditioned on sparse measurements, guided by a frozen neural operator. Use a closed-form, spectrally shaped guidance score that weights the surrogate by its frequency-dependent accuracy; avoid denoiser backpropagation. Provide a distribution-free analysis bounding error across frequency-time and introduce a coherence diagnostic to verify diagonality of residuals. Demonstrate on 3D elastic wavefield prediction at 5% and 2% sensor coverage.
